===== PAGE 8 =====

PREFACE
It is perfectly logical that an encyclopaedia t of the knowledge and activity of man should devote a volume to techniques, which is concerned both with activities and knowledge. However, neither would it have been surprising if one had not been included, since the material world is still not considered to be part of the more noble preoccupations of mankind. Which writer today would prepare a discourse on the ball-point pen? Workers do not refer to the machines they work, they prefer to talk about alienation, fatigue and the class struggle, and this in spite of the long chapter which Marx devoted to machines. In everyday conversation this topic is limited to the strictly domestic: to the performance of a car or the qualities of a self-cleaning oven, or to the comparative effectiveness of washing powders. Diderot's
Encyclopedie may be admired; it is rarely emulated.
Any attempts to reconcile man and everday objects, those which are used, and those of which one uses oneself would probably be futile. So too would an attempt to decipher the true relationships between man and everyday objects, and their long history of quarrels and reconciliations.
The main difficulty was to write a work which did not duplicate others.
There are three major histories of techniques already in existence, not including innumerable popularised works, and works limited in historical time, space and subject. The first of these works was in English, published in five large volumes. It was followed by a Russian, and then a French work, which is being completed at the very moment these lines are being written. Three histories were devoted almost exclusively to pure techniques, and are in a sense technical histories of techniques. Was it a good idea to add a fourth one, even if there are still unexplored fields and obscurities?
It seemed to us that it would be more fruitful to tackle this problem from quite a different angle, using a new method. This implied a new work hypothesis, which is explained at length at the beginning of the book. The idea was based on two major principles.
tThe French edition is part of the Encyclopedie de la Pteiade. (Translator's Note.) vii

===== PAGE 9 =====

viii
PREFACE
The first of these led to a plan almost diametrically opposed to that of preceding works. These in effect presented the evolution of techniques in a fragmentary manner, each technique isolated one from the other, and furthermore, divided into somewhat dubious chronological divisions. This fragmentation seemed regrettable to us on more than one account: it inevitably lead to a sort of mosaic where colour and design were confused, creating juxtapositions without formal clarity. The authors only knew their own period or speciality, ignoring the works of others. As a result no general
.
overview, no global explanation could emerge. The documentation was worthwhile and interesting, but one was left feeling somewhat lost at the end of it. One found the Parthenon and Descartes in this history mosaic, but one would certainly never find Trafalgar and the Pont des Arts together in the same paragraph.
The notion of a technical system: a coherent ensemble of compatible structures, seemed to us to be necessary to render in a more interesting way the material world of mankind since its origins. This was a more sensitive approach to the problem, even if its formulation seemed to some readers a little excessive. By exaggerating contrasts, the painter or the photographer permits one to perceive more clearly the reality of certain things. Daily life is a close mixture of constraint and material acts, even if one does not intuitively perceive the multiple relationships which undoubtedly exist between the elements which surround us in this technical environment, a world sometimes strange, always ineluctable. This environment only has meaning or value in so far as it has a specific order, and our manuals are more than reticent on the order of technical knowledge, even in the narrowest sense of the concept. Those who do not care about this problem are led directly to complete misapprehensions: to say that Heron of Alexandria, with his eolipyle, came close to inventing the steam engine is striking proof of this.
This way of tackling the technical phenomena has another advantage: that of starting a dialogue with specialists of other disciplines, with economists, linguists, sociologists, politicians, lawyers, scientists, and philosphers, for example. This is a dialogue which is difficult to organise these days, but in this way it would be possible to highlight the indispensable coordination of human activities, and the evident concordance of results which are its products.
Such a proposition implies at its very least a dividing into chronological periods. Technical systems succeed one another. The dynamics of such systems thus conceived, gave a new value to what is sometimes called in a vague and ambiguous way 'industrial revolutions'. From system to system,

===== PAGE 10 =====

PREFACE
ix it was now possible to scan the centuries, indeed millenia, according to rhythms quite other than those to which we have been introduced by a more conventional history. We can already hear critical voices: but in writing a history, is it not inevitable that some aspects will be emphasised at the expense of others? We are aware of a certain viewpoint somewhat scornfully known as battle-history, this being the history of a long period, where events and humanity were submerged beneath descriptions of secular movements. What we propose here is a history which is in a sense bound in by the material world.
Once we took this position, the work plan fell into place of its own accord.
The first part of the book was conceived according to a plan which was almost completely chronological. The stages of technical progress needed to be examined, and put into the context of the other great movements of history.
Of course, several compromises were made, but this did not unduly affect the chosen plan. It was the vestiges of a classical education which made us separate the Greeks and the Romans (who apart from a few exceptions possessed almost the same technical system) and made us link the Byzantines with Rome. Vitruvius was only the last of the architects and engineers of the School of Alexandria, and the Latin agronomists that have so often been highly praised are in fact only the descendants of the agronomists from the whole of the Mediterranean Basin, less well known because their works have disappeared. In the same way we have grouped together in a somewhat arbitrary fashion three independent civilizations, both in terms of time and space. Situated outside the Western domain, their only characteristic in common was that they all found themselves limited at various levels. The chapter on the t1rst great technical civilizations (as far as the great empires are concerned) presents a juxtaposition of these civilizations, which were similar to one another but had major differences.
In our descriptions there are gaps, which the extent of the subject made inevitable. India is not mentioned, neither are some belated civilizations of Africa and Asia. Is it really necessary to explain that we are perfectly conscious of these faults?
The most important objective from our point of view was to understand, if not explain completely, the transition from one technical system to another, because it is there that the problem of technical progress truly lies. What might seem complex but clear and strictly ordered in the field of scientific progress, will appear infinitely less clear in the field of technical progress.
The gap which exists between what has been conventionally called theory and practice is such that invention assumes a singular importance, and this

===== PAGE 11 =====

x
PREFACE
has often led to a collection of inventors' biographies. However, there is something else to consider: the development of the stages of scientific progress is logical and autonomous. And above all a certain coherence is necessary (which we have mentioned above) because a technique does not exist in isolation, and is always linked to other techniques. 'In 1814, Collier, the great engineer from Paris, began his attempts to make shears with helicoidal blades for linen, the basic principle of which came from the United
States, but where no attempts at actually making such an implement had been made. Everything remained to be done, and it took him five years.
Collier had to create alloys, smelting and machining processes, anti-friction bearings, a transmission chain, a control system using a mitrometric screw, in fact al the elements required to make a machine of unprecedented precision.' In the field of invention this coherence of techniques is an inescapable necessity. And last but not least, remains the economic viewpoint. Recently a writer quite correctly made clear the difference between the progress of techniques, and technical progress, the first being of a purely technical order, in the sense that there are some inventions which are not immediately useful, but which can lead to 'innovations'. The second represents exactly the point at which the invention enters everyday or industrial life. It is thus possible to put invention on two planes: that of technical thought representing technique in its pure form, and that of economic need in the wider sense of the term, in order to respond to technical problems or to follow purely economic needs. The latter may be in the form of increased demand, in the need for reduction in production costs, etc, all factors more or less linked with each other. Lastly we note (and we shall return to this point throughout the book) the compatibilities which exist between a technical system which is being developed and the con temporaneous social system.
Internal technical problems, the necessary relationships between various techniques, and compatibilities with other systems, appear to be the principal dynamic elements existing in the field of technical systems. The concept of a technique developed to its limits, and the resulting impasse followed quite naturally from this approach. There has been very little research into the mechanisms of technical progress, and it seemed to us that research would eventually have to develop in this direction so we have consistently promoted this method of study. Certainly we have dealt with what is known as growth: the causes of technical progress and the acceleration of the latter according to universally known lines of development. The works of Rostow, although sometimes vague and ambiguous, provided us with a model to

===== PAGE 12 =====

PREFACE
xi follow, develop and refine. Despite some surprising gaps, it fills a large vacuum in historical thought. It is far from our intention to fill these gaps;
what was more important was the line of research to be taken.
The second volume of this work was to our mind just as fundamental, and complementary to the first part. Our aim here was to introduce technical progress in a certain number of disciplines representing other human activities: science and law, politics and geography, sociology and economics.
The language of technology was substituted for the "language of the arts", examined by Diderot in the Encyclopedie, and an essay on technical knowledge was the final chapter in this part of the book.
Of course there was no question of giving techniques a privileged position, and of giving it pre-eminence in the formation of all types of structures' in the dynamics of global evolution. It was only necessary to show that techniques as well as influencing the external world were also affected by external influences. We thus hoped to integrate techniques with disciplines which have to date not given techniques much notice.
Generally this work was written for people with an enquiring mind, the peculiar characteristic of all encyclopaedias, and in particular for those who come into contact daily with techniques without ever realising it, such as the office worker, or the teacher. We also believe that technicians of al kinds should be aware of the history of their techniques. They are rarely taught this, and that is why in most cases, they have false ideas about them. It would no doubt be interesting for such people to become aware of all the various links between techniques, and to discover in the final analysis their implications for a world which is becoming more and more complex. We must take care to avoid however the self-satisfaction of the author of the
Prospectus of Diderot's Encyclopedie 'too much has been written on the sciences, not enough has been written on the liberal arts: how little of worth we see in the authors' contributions compared with the extent and diversity of the subject'.
Having established the broad lines of the general idea, the next step was to find some collaborators, and collaborators who would accept that way in which the problem was to be posed. This proved to be the most difficult task, since the association between sciences and techniques is a difficult one to make, and requires a particular kind of effort. Of course there are many people today who are interested in technical progress, and in its implications and consequences. Since the last war an abundant literature proves this fact, even though it is of very uneven quality. But the principal aim of the work was historical.

===== PAGE 13 =====

xii
PREFACE
Meetings between historians and technicians at the end of the 19th century were frequent and fruitful. A large number of highly skilled technicians had ben provided with a good classical education which enabled them to consider the origins of their 'art' with more objectivity. Such relationships, apart from a few very notable exceptions, have become all too rare today. The technician of today is totally cut off from ancient techniques, so great are the differences between the two. Only a few decades ago, retiring technicians could easily throw themselves into historical research: Quenedey, an engineering officer, studied the construction of wooden houses during the Middle Ages; Lefebvre des Noettes, a cavalry officer, researched the problem of harnessing horses;
Masson, an engineer of the Pants et Chaussees, studied the gothic vault.
Today's generation has burnt its bridges and treats history with the fleeting attention of the dilettante - this can be a dangerous exercise where techniques are concerned.
For their part, historians tend to feel ill at ease in a field where they hardly ever dare venture knowingly for fear of ridicule. History manuals reveal this only to clearly. The unfortunate result of this attitude is that the historian often neglects this aspect of history, and consequently this affects their interpretation of events, especially in the field of economic history. The writing of history throughout the world is riddled with misunderstandings and simple mistakes of this kind.
Too often the technician is ignorant of historical methods, the tracing of source material, and the critical commentary accompanying them, which can result in the wildest anachronisms. Historians, on the other hand, shy away from such a task, or at best, they have already written and published what they know, and have no desire to start all over again.
For these reasons, the editor of this work has had to take the unusual step of writing the whole of the first part himself. Since the main outline was perfectly systematic, this made the work more homogeneous, and a mosaic of more or less divergent opinions was replaced by a coherent, unified discourse. But this advantage was accompanied by numerous difficulties and some dangerous stumbling blocks. One person could not possibly be a specialist on all periods and all techniques discussed. As with Diderot (if one can make such an immodest comparison) when he wrote most of the technical articles in his Encyclopedie, much had to be learnt in order to be able to describe the techniques, and complete success would have been too much to expect. In the final analysis, the reader will have to judge whether the trouble that was taken was worth it.
The second volume, which consisted essentially of the work of specialists,

===== PAGE 14 =====

PREFACE
xiii posed the same problems. The economist and the geographer, the scientist and the lexicographer responded to the call, and understood the project perfectly. All the others made themselves scarce, some of them because they were too busy, others because they were somewhat nervous and felt ill prepared to venture into foreign territory, something to which they admitted in all honesty. We were left with the option of leaving the second half incomplete with only some of the aspects covered. After a great deal of hesitation, and not without misgivings which still persist, the same solution was adopted, and the editor of the work had to force himself into another exercise of mental contortion and assume the role of sociologist, lawyer and politician. Again the reader will have to judge for himself whether this solutions works. All this explains the slow pace at which this work was written. One can imagine how many new books have been published in the last ten years dealing with the periods and questions that have already been written about in this book. Short of constantly revising the text, it was practically impossible to take all these new works into account. The same difficulty occurred with the covering of the newest techniques and their implications. The rapidity of progress in certain techniques is often greater than the speed of writing and publishing a book. On the other hand, there are other areas of development, such as maritime law for example, which develop very slowly. We hope that readers will take this into account when they pass their final judgement on this book.
Nothing evokes the spirit in which this work was written better than this text by Raymond Queneau: "There should be no question of considering the volumes of this encyclopaedia as treatises from which one could learn a science. It goes without saying, however, that the reader stands to learn much, since it consists after all of a complete course of study. This work aims to educate, to assess, and to provide an opening into the future. Nowhere does this undertaking conceal the extent of our uncertainty, or the immensity of our ignorance. The reader will learn to ignore, to doubt, and that, too, is a critical enterprise."
Raymond Queneau, who entrusted us with this work, is no longer with us. When things became difficult his sometimes sardonic but always gentle smiles proved to be the most effective form of encouragement. We should like this work, or at least the very best parts of it, to be considered as a homage to his memory.
BERTRAND GILLE

PUBLISHERS' NOTES
NITE TO THE ORIGINAL EDITION
To help further research, the reader will find at the end of the book the following:
1. Bibliography 2. A table of the major contemporaneous technical events from the earliest times to the present.
3. A detailed summary of each chapter.
4. A list of illustrations.
5. A comprehensive index of names of individuals mentioned in ,the book's chapters.
6. A title index of books quoted.
7. A subject index.
NITE TO THE ENGLISH EDITION
The reader already familiar with the original French edition will notice that the contribution by Bernard Quemada, "Technique et langage" has not been included here. This decision was approved by the original publisher: the content was devoted to the French language only, which made it unapplicable to a wider context, and indeed untranslatable.
The Bibliography has also been modified. Its sections have been regrouped toward the end of the book. Each has been extended and updated by Dr Eda
F. Kranakis of Yale University, whose contributions will appear under the headings ''Additional Bibliography".

===== PAGE 18 =====

PROLEGOMENA
TO A HISTORY
OF TECHNIQUES

===== PAGE 19 =====

===== PAGE 20 =====

In 1935, Lucien Febvre was still able to write: "The History of techniques, one of those numerous disciplines which has still yet to be created." A few years before a work was published which proved to be a milestone in history - it raised a particular issue, that of harnessing and the saddle horse, and its relationship with one of the great watersheds in history, the disappearance of slavery. However much
Captain Lefebvre des Noettes' ideas might have been disputed later, there was no arguing the fact that his work had opened up new channels, and original explanations and perspectives.
In the Annal es of 1935, Lucien Febvre and Marc Bloch do not appear to have just discovered the history of techniques, but rather they were emphasising the interest of the subject, and the little attention paid to it by historians. This does not mean that it had been completely neglected until this point, but by its very nature it was placed outside the main body of history. It was as difficult to integrate technical history into history as a whole as it was to integrate the techniques themselves into a general economic theory, to take but one example. Lucien Febvre clearly discerned that there was a conflict of spheres: "A technical history of techniques, necessarily the work of technicians, risks serious errors, confusion, and a complete misunderstanding of the general conditions of how such a task should be executed." But he qualified this: "a work by technicians who look beyond their period or sphere of interest and are thus capable not only of understanding and describing, but also of reconstituting the tools in the manner of exacting and ingenious archaeologists, and also of interpreting the texts as wise historians." This was the problem: to link these different types of knowledge, and to use different methodologies.

===== PAGE 21 =====

4
PROLEGOMENA
It is not surprising, therefore, that historians were hesitant to enter such unfamiliar territory. The technicians, for their part, held little interest for techniques which were no longer in existence, and when they did broach the subject, they did so with an attitude of mind which had only tenuous links with history. Some wrote a history which did not include techniques at all, while others concentrated on research concerned solely with this aspect, such that history became mere chronology. "Technical activity cannot be isolated from other human activities" noted Lucien Febvre, and synthesis was less necessary than concordance. In any global historical explanation, it was essential to include techniques. It is interesting to note that when economics finally found its way into global explanations, techniques still remained apart, by the very fact of its slow and difficult integration into general economic theory which we have just mentioned.
There were numerous traps, not least within the history of tech niques itself. We had to avoid too many divisions, which were essential however for the initial exposition of the facts, but which could rapidly lead to every history of each particular technique closing in on itself. Beyond this, it was vital to reintegrate each history of techniques into a larger historical grouping, which could itself be easily linked to economic or demographic history and the history of science or ideas, and finally also factual history the importance of which is often underestimated. This was our aim, then. But before we approach the very heart of this subject, and because this attempt is a relatively original one, certain precautions should be taken. It is the discussion of these precautions which constitutes the essence of this long introduction.
It would be useful to sketch a brief picture of the history of techniques, thereby enabling us to see the development, with all its wealth and indeed with all its problems, of a discipline which can already lay claim to being established.
The oldest history of techniques is that by the German Beckmann, Beitriige zur Geschichte der Erfindungen, published in Leipzig bet ween 1780 and 1805. As the title indicates, this is a history of inventions, that is the very type of fragmented history which I spoke of above. It is very similar to a work which is almost contemporary, the
Geschichte der Technologie seit der Wiederherstellung der Wissenschaf ten bis an das Ende des 18 Jahrhunderts, the three volumes of which were published in Gottingen, between 1807 and 1811 . However this

===== PAGE 22 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
5 work did at least take into account some major historical events and the notion, albeit still a vague one, of technical systems.
It was not before the middle of the 19th century that the history of techniques underwent a marked development as attempts were finally made to integrate it with other disciplines. It was at this time, during the period corresponding to the Second Empire, that techniques and technology were demanding more attention. Several viewpoints emerged from this.
The first viewpoint aimed at providing a response to the growing public interest in techniques. This meant writing popular works, which entailed not only popularising existing techniques, but also demon strating the amount of progress which had been made. Two works are particularly worth mentioning in this category: L. Figuier's Les Mer veilles de l'industrie, and Merveilles de la science, which are still useful even today. Each industrial sector is dealt with separately but they are nonetheless placed in some kind of historical context.
The second viewpoint responded to the need felt by certain techni cians to know the history of their own technique. Some authors of technical manuals were quite happy to devote several pages to the history of a single technique. Percy's long work, A Manual of Metal lurgy, not only provides information about the history of metallurgical techniques, but also about the techniques used in some exotic countries.
The third viewpoint reveals concerns which are already more truly historical, and was propounded by archaeologists and technicians who combined their efforts to reconstruct ancient techniques. Two types of work are distinguishable within this sizeable amount of research. The first deals with the reconstruction of ancient military techniques. It is well known that Napoleon III himself was possibly its instigator: he began research on this subject before 1848, when he was imprisoned in
Ham castle. At the emperor's request, Colonel Fave began work on the history of artillery. He also reconstructed certain weapons which were used in trials. This method was devised and developed and after a while, proved to be quite efficient. The second type of work was born of necessity. The restoration of ancient monuments had been undertaken on a large scale, and it was felt that the techniques actually used to build the original monuments were the only ones which could make the restored monuments look authentic. The efforts made to understand these techniques by Viollet-le-Duc are well known and his

===== PAGE 23 =====

6
PROLEGOMENA
dictionaries of architecture and furniture are still an interesting source for historians of techniques today.
Those adhering to the third viewpoint went even further to say that techniques should be integrated into global explanations. Most people are familiar with the importance that Marx attached to techniques as part of his general theory and it is not surprising that for the historical part of his works he used histories of techniques, such as he was able to find at that time. It was also around this time that some economists began to include technical progress in their general theory.
This proved to be the launch-pad for the history of techniques, and during the last decades of the 19th century, some works were written which are still useful today. Usually they deal with particular tech niques: Ludwig Beck's work on techniques used in the iron and steel industry, Thurston's on the history of the steam engine, and Theodor
Beck's work on the construction of machines are all examples of this.
Similarly, many technical treatises, especially those from Antiquity, were studied, published and translated, and Berthelot's research on alchemists and other technicians, and the numerous works on the
Greek engineers from the Alexandria school bear witness to this. T.
Henri Martin's work on the life and works of Hero of Alexandria were in fact written in 1854. Sometimes, the expositions were even more general, thus, in 1897, A. Espinas published his work on Les Origines de la technologie.
The movement was consolidated during the first years of the 20th century, when the inestimable importance of original sources and reconstructions finally began to be appreciated, and when the first museums devoted to the history of techniques were opened. This was not without a certain amount of chauvinism - the Science Museum opened in London in 1857 was created in honour of British science and techniques. The Deutsches Museum in Munich was established in
1906. The traditional history of techniques by sector and histories of inventions continued to be written. The first historical dictionaries of techniques appeared at this time with works such as Bliimner's dic tionary of the techniques of classical Antiquity, and Feldhaus' dic tionary of the techniques of Antiquity, the Middle Ages and the modern period.
The most important development was certainly that of the first attempts to integrate the history of techniques into global historical explanations. The publication in 1906 of the thesis by Mantoux on the

===== PAGE 24 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
7 English industrial revolution of the 18th century was an important stage in the historiography of techniques. This was followed by Ballot's research on the introduction of mechanisation in French industry, which sadly was interrupted by the Great War, with the result that it was not published until 1922. The French and the English were to become the specialists on the techniques of the Renaissance period, while the Germans concentrated on the techniques of the Ancient and
Medieval eras.
During the period after the First World War, the progress of the history of techniques was temporarily halted, its vigour only renewed with the arrival of the 1930's. Usher's work on mechanical inventions published in 1929, and Captain Lefebvre des Noettes' work on harnes sing and the saddle horse breathed new life into the discipline and strengthened it. It was now clear that writing history without including techniques was impossible. The Anna/es of Bloch and Febvre in 1935, which devoted an entire volume to the history of techniques, demon strated just how important this discipline had become. By expounding the aims and assessing the scale of the enterprise, Anna/es encouraged historians to continue on the path they had already traced. Before the
Second World War, the history of techniques had already assumed the character which it still has today. Dickinson's history of the steam engine, published in 1939, and Captain Quenedey's history of wood construction in Rouen are evidence of two different types of methodo logy, each adapted to its own particular field. At the same time, new museums were being founded and centres of research were being established, in Vienna in 1931 and at the university of Paris in 1932.
However, we should note that this research was badly coordinated, partial and sometimes narrow in outlook. Some technicians did show a true historical sense, but historians were reluctant to begin studying techniques, fearing problems in an area which was relatively new and uncertain. The links made between the various fields were unsatisfac tory, and sometimes they were not attempted at all.
After the Second World War, the history of techniques became an established discipline. It was not yet perfectly coherent: there were still conceptual differences. But one factor was immediately obvious - the museums and centres specialising in the history of techniques were springing up everywhere.
The history of techniques remains an isolated discipline, however, still considered as the domain of the "scientists." It has still not

===== PAGE 25 =====

8
PROLEGOMENA
managed to find its way into the internation11l history congresses, and it was not included in the recent international congress of economic history [Transl. Note: although B . Gille himself delivered the General
Report: "Progres scientifique, progres technique et progres economi que" for the Section on "Technology and Economic Growth" at the
4th International Congress on Economic History, Bloomington, Indiana, 1968; there was also a Theme devoted to "Science and
Technology" at the 6th International Congress on Economic History, Copenhagen, 1974]. It is quite significant, however, that the Comite
Franais des travaux historiques et scientifiques has established a joint commission for the history of science and techniques. The same indispensable collaboration exists in some other countries, although usually it is limited to specific areas of research. A work on medieval metallurgy published recently in Hungary was written with the colla boration of an engineer, a metallurgist, an archaeologist and an historian. It would not be impossible to create such cooperation on an institutional level.
The latest general histories of techniques reveal other gaps which in fact havΔ already been mentioned. But this is a difficult problem to resolve. As Lucien Febvre noted, we do need a technical history of techniques, and it is difficult to deny the interest afforded by the small technical monographs which deal with a single process, or the birth of an invention. It is from an accumulation of such works that we can gain a true knowledge of the history of techniques. However, this knowl edge needs to be extended, and this first of all within the technical domain itself. Both the personality of the inventor and the genesis of the idea are integral parts of the birth of an invention. A successful invention is often preceded by a set of objectives which presuppose that they are technically possible, and also by various needs, which we shall consider later. One should consider next the moment when the invention is made, and the person who makes it. And finally, the success of the invention - which thus becomes innovation - is an important factor, because of course the true significance of techniques is in their concrete application in the social, economic, institutional and political structures, without which it is almost impossible to understand them. In our opinion, all these recent works were too insular: they are useful, but still incomplete.
This was our aim, then. We did not simply want to write another technical history of techniques, because of the attraction afforded by

===== PAGE 26 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
9 any research which is slightly esoteric. Essentially, we wished to include the technical world in general history. Our main obstacles were the gaps in our own knowledge, and our reliance on histories which have tended to be descriptive rather than interpretative. We found it useful to construct what economists call a model, but which we would prefer to be known as an explanatory schema. To do this, we had to clarify and agree on our concepts, allow for all the variables - which could be either extrinsic or intrinsic - and be conscious of all the links within this structure. The discussion that follows is a major exami nation of this possible explanatory schema. Of course, this is only a provisional schema and it is certainly not immutable.
Since we were approaching this problem from a different standpoint, we felt it would be useful to devote some of the space to a discussion and critique of the nature of the various sources available to us. In order that we should not have to quote the same titles again and again, there is a general directive bibliography for this chapter, to be found at the beginning of the Bibliography.
CONCEPTS AND METHODOLOGY
No science or discipline would merit these names if they did not possess the conceptual and methodological means necessary to any analysis. The reader will not be surprised, therefore, that a large part of this long introduction will be concerned with these aspects of the problem.
Techniques are best analysed as a scientific entity. This is only possible on a global level if one can work with an appropriate lan guage, and also with models based on precise concepts. These models should reflect two levels of reality, the static structures and systems, and the dynamic factor which we have termed "technical progress."
Working on the contributions of our precursors, we shall try to construct such a method of analysis.
There is so much literature about the notions of systems and structures that there seems little point in discussing them again. It is true that there is still some uncertainty about the substance of these two notions which are often applied within very different areas. It seemed necessary to highlight them here, however, since a

===== PAGE 27 =====

10
PROLEGOMENA
comprehensive treatment of this angle of research has not yet been done in this field. As for the study of political economy, the use of these notions "seems to be the only method which science has found to date capable of bridging the two spheres of research which are separated all too often, historical research and theoretical analysis." This seems all the more desirable since the history of techniques is still a young discipline, which should be provided right from the beginning with clearly defined concepts. Some of these, incidentally, are already controversial and have been the object of rigourous methods of research. However, the lack of specialised research means that we must confine ourselves to general themes and only occasionally pro vide examples.
The task seems beset with difficulties right from the start. The term "techniques" itself is usually used in the plural - there are textile techniques and iron and steel making techniques, for example. Even at the simplest level, take for example, the technique of the clog-maker, it is soon obvious that this "technique" is in fact a group of operations which often require different tools. So what about the "technique" of the locksmith, as it was described by Mathurin Jousse at the beginning of the 17th century or by Duhamel du Monceau in the middle of the
18th century? It is thus almost impossible to clearly define the object of research. It is very rare that a technique involves only a single operation, and even if it does, this simple action still entails a necessary coupling of matter and energy. These two elements are combined precisely by the technical act, which very often requires some sort of support. Even at the most elementary level, and even for the most primitive techniques, there is a technical combination which, as far as more complex techniques are concerned, can be called a technical ensemble. The support, at the simplest level, is a tool or a process.
Cutting down a tree presupposes the following: the existence of the raw material; the materials to be used; for a specific purpose, the finality of the technical act, energy, and what is known as an instru ment or instruments, an axe, saw, rope, wedge and sledgehammer, etc.
We can distinguish several important notions from these observa tions, namely that there are several types of technical combinations which can be studied from several different points of view.
At the bottom of the scale, we can effectively talk of structures, even though this word is still somewhat ambiguous. A structure is a unitary

===== PAGE 28 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
11 combination. There are elementary structures, such as the tool, for instance, and assembly structures, such as the machine. Some examples will help to explain this point.
A. Leroi-Gourhan has clearly demonstrated that it is possible to define structures even within elementary operations. He used the example of cutting by percussion, of which there are three different methods:
A) Static percussion - the knife applied to the wood - which gives a precise but low evergy cut.
B) Dynamic percussion - the bill hook, the woodcutter's axe, the carpenter's adze - which gives an imprecise but strong energy cut.
C) Static percussion with the aid of a striker -scissors, hammer or sledgehammer - which combines the advantages of both of these processes, and which Bachelard called "administered force."
The saw should be added to this, because it is slightly different to the three other processes. It is a more complex instrument, consisting of a series of knives arranged in such a way to give a precise cut, and to enable greater force to be applied than with one knife alone.
The term "structure" is also used to denote other combinations, which in spite of their complexity, in fact represent a single act. Take
J .-L. Maunoury's example: "Heat engines have characteristics which can be defined on two levels. As motors, their function is to create work, as heat engines, they have in common the functioning principle of using heat derived from the fuel of certain bodies. " Using this definition, the author attempts to demonstrate the corresponding elementary structures "the combinations of which explains the dif ferent types of heat engines." Firstly, he distinguishes two different series of structures: work structures and heat structures. The table below will explain this better than a long discussion.
The table is a perfect example of an assembly structure. Maunoury was led to distinguish between structural groups and sub-groups, but one could almost certainly improve this model: the type of fuel and its conditions of use, which require annexed elements (carburettor, electric spark). Certain formulas could also be clarified, and the actual structure of the energy converter could be constructed: the cylinder and piston which with the crank-shaft is able to provide rotary motion, paddle-wheels, etc.

===== PAGE 29 =====

External (Table taken from Maunoury, La Genese des innovations, Paris, 1968) This still leaves a great deal to be said about the structure of tools, depending on their function, the material which they are used to work, the material of which they are made, and also on the traditions of size and shape. We will examine two very basic examples.
The number of different types of saw is clearly evident in Charles Fremont's study of this tool. There is no need to discuss the differences between saws made of bronze, iron or steel, they are self-explanatory.
But Fremont distinguished several other types: (A) the hand saw; (B) the cross-cut saw for pit sawyers; (C) the bow saw; (D) the frame saw;
( E) the circular saw; ( F) the band saw.
Fig. 1 shows some pictures of bill hooks which appear in a tool maker's catalogue. There are one hundred and six different types of this single tool, the names of which are toponymic, related, naturally only to France. These varieties correspond to local traditions and usage, and also according to the work and the main type of vegetation.
Systematic research is needed in order to achieve as fine an analysis as possible. The same catalogue has a similar collection of pictures of axes, scythes and even anvils. Listings of tools would certainly be very

===== PAGE 31 =====

14
PROLEGOMENA
useful as would a collection of ancient catalogues and inventories of tools kept in museums. Some such studies at the most primitive level, i.e. into the types of tools or instruments used for a given operation, have already been conducted. Most notable are those by Leroi
Gourhan.
The second notion is that of the technical ensemble, which is at a quite different level to the former. Some complex techniques require not what could be called a unitary technique, but a group of tributary techniques, the combination or ensemble of which is designed to perform a clearly defined technical act. Take the manufacture of cast iron, for example, the complexity of which is illustrated in the adjoining diagram: there are problems concerned with energy, with the components, the ores and fuels, the blast, and finally with the instrument itself-the frame, the refractory lining, and the shape. This is a good example of the ensemble whose every part is essential to the required result (see fig. 3). There are other examples of this in the chemical industry, all of which are different combinations.
There is a greater understanding of the technical ensemble, because technological research is far more interested in this aspect, with the result that all technological manuals have diagrams which are very useful to the historian.
The final notion is possibly that of the technical concatenation. This is a combination of technical ensembles which is designed to produce the final product. The manufacture of this final product is usually done in several successive stages.
One of the simplest examples of this is clog-making. Figure 2 illustrates the breakdown of this process as conceived by researchers from the Musee des Arts et Traditions populaires. This diagram in fact represents only a part of the process, but here six different operations are apparent, which require three different types of tool. The process continues beyond this, however. From the initial ·rough casting to the scythe, only one instrument is used, the water-powered tilt hammer, which is adapted using a series of panels to tools as varied as hammers and anvils.
This schema is more complicated where a more elaborate product is concerned (fig. 3). Cast-iron can be used to produce iron or steel, and the latter can be used to provide the power needed to give the product its final form. There is thus a.n entire hierarchy of diverse techniques, which contribute to the working of the technical complex. The same is

===== PAGE 32 =====

A
B
HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
15 c
D
E
F
Figure 2. - The different stages in the clog-making process at Cuse (Doubs, France).
true of the textile industry: the first ensemble is the production of the raw material (animal, vegetable or synthetic) ; the second is to prepare this material for use (washing, retting, degreasing). After this comes the spinning, the weaving, and finally the sucessive finishings, all of which are integrated at different stages of the manufacturing process
(fulling, shearing, bleaching and dyeing).
These structures, except for the technical ensembles, are best considered as vertical chains. However it is possible to conceive of them on a horizontal level as well i.e. a technical structure can be used in several different series. This is true of course of tools, which although having a given structure, can take on different forms or aspects. Two simple examples are the hammer or hammers and the pincer or pincers. The same is true at a more complicated level. The cylinder-piston structure is used in heat engines, but also in suction pumps and even in cigarette lighters.
A technical ensemble or series could not function normally without certain conditions being fulfilled. Usually these conditions are those concerning quality and quantity (if we confine ourselves to the techni cal domain, a restriction that we shall discuss further on). The interac-

tions between quality and quantity are many. For example, in terms of quality, the working of a given type of material requires tools of a given type. But the product of given quantities can also require precise qualities in the means of production. The influence of quantity on quality is less marked, but it is nevertheless apparent in a great number of cases.
Beyond this, the links are not only within the linear process, but also with more indirect actions. Each part of a technical ensemble in order

===== PAGE 34 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
17 to function properly may need one or more of the products of the same ensemble. This relationship is obvious where materials are concerned:
iron production requires a steam engine, which in turn needs to be made of a metal strong enough to withstand high pressure and superheating. This relationship exists, although in more diluted forms, in many other areas. In the diagram illustrating iron production, it is immediately apparent that both cast and wrought iron are necessary parts of the sub-ensemble. Many other examples could be brought into play and the schema made more complex by including the techniques of transport and the associated problem of energy for example. In some cases, the links between each of the sub-ensembles and the latter and the global ensemble are very close; such is the case for the chemical industry, for example. In other cases, these links are far less apparent, less precise, and in fact relatively weak -the textile industry is an example of this.
This means therefore, that in general, all techniques are dependent upon the others, and this necessarily requires a certain coherence: the coherence within the structures, ensembles and series constitute what could be called a technical system (fig.4). The internal links so vital to these technical systems become greater in number as the systems evolve and become more complex. These links are only effective if the technical ensembles have reached a common level, save perhaps for a few techniques which remain above or below the general level. The second state is naturally more favourable than the first.
Once such an equilibrium has been obtained, the technical system is viable. Those enamoured with chronological divisions are thus able to assess history in terms of a series of different successive technical systems. These can be analysed beyond the extent of the monographs which deal only with one technique: the links between the techniques can be examined, and the nature and conditions of such links.
Research still has many gaps to fill and uncertainties to resolve: most of the studies available to us were hardly adequate. In Technics and
Civilisation, Lewis Mumford, like many others before him, did not deal with technical systems but with ill-defined and very long periods, such as the eotechnic and paleotechnic phases. However, he does seem to have grasped the problem: "Each phase represents a period of human history, but they are better characterised by the fact that they each constitute a separate technological complex . . . Each phase has its origins in certain well-defined regions, and tends to use certain particu-

===== PAGE 35 =====

18
PROLEGOMENA
lar resources and raw materials; each one has its own specific means of creating and utilizing energy, and its own particular forms of produc tion. Almost every part of a technical complex (this term is used in a different way to our use of it above) tends to accentuate and symbolize, even within the complex itself, entire series of interconnections." In
MARITIME TRANSPOR T
Steam 1 736-1772 Iron 1 787
TRANSPORT ON LAND
Cugnot 1 769 Trewithick 1 792 / / locomotives // Railways Fuel Kay 1 733 Hargreaves 1 765 Arkwright 1 767
Crompton 1 782 Cartwright 1 789 Figure 4. - Simplified scheme of the technical system of the first half of the 1 9th century.

===== PAGE 36 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
19 order to distinguish between these various phases, Mumford isolated what could be called the dominant techniques which exercised - precisely because of their general importance - a commanding influ ence on all the others. "The eotechnic phases is a complex of water and wood, the paleotechnic is one of coal and iron, and the neotechnic phase is a complex of electricity and alloys." The idea itself was an interesting one, but the treatment of what we call technical systems was unsatisfactory. Even though Mumford did talk about the overlap ping of one system into another, the overall treatment of these systems was vague and arbitrary.
A more refined analysis taking into account a greater number of elements would create a tighter chronology, so that the history of techniques would not simply represent three very long periods. This is all the more necessary if technical history is to be related to other areas of human activity, without which it cannot be understood. It is vital, therefore, that technical and non-technical systems should be compared, and the aspects which unite them outlined.
It is only natural that economists, insofar as they also think in terms of structures and systems, should have found this problem interesting:
we shall return frequently to this point. However, this does not seem to have done much to advance the present state of research. Following in part the line taken by A. Lewis in The Theory of Economic Growth,
Andre Marchal divided up the phases of technical evolution, not in technical terms, but in terms of the ideas held about technics at various times. This greatly facilitated the introduction of technics into econo mic thought, but it also created a mixture of varied notions rather than general concepts, which would have enabled him to define a technical system. In pre-literate societies, technical achievements remained roughly on the same level: "the same instruments, the same techniques for cutting and polishing, the same metallurgical processes, the same methods of cultivation and irrigation, and the same hunting tricks were used by populations which were separated by different continents and even by different milleniums." It was the knowledge of writing, the development of mathematics and the accumulation of knowledge which was to "distinguish the technically archaic societies from the technically primitive ones." Marchal places the Middle Ages in the technically archaic division: "This period is distinguished from the modern age by the fact that a small group of privileged men were at leisure to indulge in abstract thought and some experimentation, but

===== PAGE 37 =====

20
PROLEGOMENA
with no practical aim in mind. Any technical achievements came from skilful but nearly illiterate artisans who improved their methods by a process of empirical trial and error." The Renaissance is merely said tQ have brought with it curious mind and the experimental spirit, and to have filtered these through to other social layers. From the 17th to the beginning of the 19th century, most revolutionary inventions were due to professional artisans, or expert amateurs. "The 20th century, however, marked the change to the concept of full-time experts and professional technicians working in teams, either for large enterprises, but most often for governments." The emergence of new ideas is not enough to compensate for the insufficient analysis, laced with those cliches so dear to economists, and some unfortunate areas of confusion. Consequently, the divisions are still unsatisfac tory.
In fact, economic analysis, even when .it is structural, completely neutralises the technical phenomenon, or else deals with it only from certain angles - take Adam Smith's concept of the division of labour for example. Perhaps the only economist to rank it highly was Marx, who quite rightly felt it was extremely important and dealt with it at length. Most economists have included a "technical variable" only when dealing with the dynamics of systems and structures. The founders of the English classical school and their successors, including
Marx, perhaps intuitively sensed the necessary relationships of com patibility and coherence between the various systems, but it has long been, and still is, difficult to integrate techniques into global economic theory - or at least to close the gap between the two areas of activity.
If the ultimate aim of every discipline is to be of central importance, it is up to the disciples of political economy to incorporate technics into their theory. Similarly, historians of techniques must not neglect neighbouring "forces. "
All economists refer to technical structures, but they do not ususally discuss them explicitly. Marchal writes, in a rather vague fashion, that
"the economic system is characterised by the particular arrangement of various types of structures," some of which naturally are technical.
Fran<$ois Perroux is more precise: "the economic system is the combi nation of a technical apparatus, an apparatus of juridical and social relations, together with fundamental economic forces." Technical structures were thus considered to be a fundamental element of the
"economic system".

===== PAGE 38 =====

HIS TOR Y OF TECHNIQUES
21 Detailed and comprehensive research is required into the system of relations between technics and the economy; current research still concentrates more on the dynamics of these two types of system. Johan
Akerman was well aware of the importance of techniques in "autono mous, or motor forces,'' and even though his successors reduced the number of these, technics was still considered to be an important part of the most dynamic ones.
The effect of technical systems on economic ones is clearly evident today, but it needs to be properly understood. Akerman's concept of
"autonomous forces" helps us to define the problem more clearly, but it is obvious that there is interaction between the two systems, and that no force is ever completely autonomous or completely induced. The size of an enterprise, the costs of production and the rate of investment are closely dependent on the current level of technology. In other words, the main objective is to determine how economic and technical systems interact, not which one is more important than the other, even without determining whether or not there are obligatory routes. It would also be very useful to compare the economic and technical systems of various different periods. Like many others before him,
Payen symbolised modern capitalism by the steam engine (this is clear from the title of his work, Machine a vapeur et capitalisme): this does not provide us with an entirely accurate picture - there are different forms of capitalism - but at least it paved the way for more specialised and fruitful research which had not yet been undertaken. The issue of the reciprocal requirements of technical and economic systems would be an interesting topic of research; many think that technical systems have always been more constricting and demanding than economic ones.
On the other hand, techniques must also be incorporated into a price system, and into the organisation of production, without which they would have no economic interest, which is after all their final purpose.
We know that ultimately, artisan techniques only survived because of a particular demand, and that techniques in general were accepted or rejected according to how they affected the market and profit margins.
On a worldwide or at least an international scale, legal measures can maintain the use of techniques which would not survive in a free market.
A system can evolve in several different ways; consequently the perspective of dynamics is paramount. If a technical system appears to

===== PAGE 39 =====

22
PROLEGOMENA
be imposing on the economy a course which it is unable to support, even temporarily, then the latter can be changed. It was possible to substitute for the liberal capitalism of Western Europe, a form of state capitalism, even collectivism. Colbert, for example, conceived of several formulas to introduce as yet unknown techniques into France.
Then, in the 19th century, the governments in France and elsewhere found it in their interests to fund the building of several railway networks. Perhaps even more significant here are the protectionist policies pursued by European powers from around 1820-1821 , desig ned not only to avoid competition from the technically superior
English industries, but also to give their own industries a chance to undergo this technological mutation.
Even if his claims are somewhat arguable, by linking the harnessing of horses with the disappearance of slavery, Captain Lefebvre des
Noettes showed he understood of the close relationship between technical and social systems.
One question in particular has been the subject of many discussions:
the relationship between the general level of technology and the optimum population. Leon Buquet has made a detailed analysis of the various interpretations of this problem since the time of John Stuart
Mill, dealing in particular with the issue of dynamics which we shall consider later. It seems that there is a correlation between the two factors: any progress in the level of technology necessarily alters the curve of the population optimum. With regard to income per capita, the form of the curve itself remains unchanged, but its position within the graph is raised.
The close links between social and technical systems are obvious.
The adoption of a particular technical system requires the adoption of a corresponding social system to preserve coherence. But does this mean that technical systems have a dominant influence over social ones? As with the economic system, the answer is two-fold. Yes, in the sense that the technical system imposes itself from the outside. But there are numerous cases where new techniques have been refused.
The imposition of a technical system requires some adaptations in the social system. Since the Middle Ages, for example, corporate bodies have refused to include in their regulations which usually consist of lists of prohibitions, rather than collections of positive instructions, many new techniques which would have destroyed their organisations - they refused to accept new preparation and dyeing products, the spinning

===== PAGE 40 =====

HIS TOR Y OF TECHNIQUES
23 wheel, mechanical fulling and carding. This continued until the 17th century. In the late Middle Ages, the coastal communities resisted the new technique of preserving herring in barrels because it threatened to disrupt their established social order. Another case which has been closely studied is the mechanisation of shoe-making in Yankee City during the second half of the 19th century. The nature of technical change - substituting a traditional activity with a completely different one, for example - greatly affects the degree of social change neces sary.
The factors which must be considered here are not only social habits, community traditions and ways of thinking, but also the distribution of labour, qualifications - and hence standards of education. And if one considers the rigidity of some social structures, it is easier to understand the difficulty of reconciling the two systems. The fear of technological unemployment in more industrialised and advanced countries and the incidences of luddism of which there have been several examples since the Middle Ages - has existed for centuries. It is no wonder therefore, that technology has progressed so little in underdeveloped countries, where social structures are sometimes completely inadaptable.
The structures of consumption are certainly more flexible, but even here there have been some surprising cases of resistance. In 1829,
French "forge masters" replying to a government inquiry stressed that their customers refused to accept the new iron made with coal, and remained steadfast to charcoal iron. But as we move towards the 20th century, so we find less such consumer resistance. At any rate, there is still a great deal of research to be done on the relations between technical and other non-technical systems.
Technical systems succeed one another both in time and in space, and studying the history of these systems' dynamics would greatly help to solve the problem of static factors, and static analysis. The dynamic analysis raises several important questions, one of which concerns the limits of a technical system, and the effect this has on technical progress.
The notion of structural limits is now a familiar one, and is naturally concerned with movement. These structural limits usually become apparent at the end of a period of expansion and are evident in the difficulties of increasing the diversity and quantity of production, or lowering production costs. These various elements are all concerned

===== PAGE 41 =====

24
PROLEGOMENA
primarily with economics, with questions of quantity, quality and cost, and there are many interactions between them.
It is most unfortunate that so little research has been done on this issue, because it would have been very instructive. It has been sugges ted that the major crises of the 14th century were precipitated by the failure of existing techniques to meet the demands of population growth as well as the increased demands of the individual consumer.
But it would help to study in more detail the limits of what we have termed as "classical techniques," that is those of the Renaissance. The rise in prices as a result of the influx of precious metals from the New
World, must have been followed by a far greater demand for luxury goods. It is possible that the problems experienced from the mid 16th to the mid 17th centuries were as a result of this, as was the population decline at the end of the 16th century. Such phenomena could reflect the inability of classical technology to respond to such a demand and we shall see that these techniques were prevalent until the first third of the 18th century. The population decline towards the mid 17th century would also have made technical innovation less necessary. It is also true that this period was still marked by famine and plague, and these seem to accompany periods of technical stagnation: the plague of
1629-1631 , the famine of 1709, and the plague of 1720 are examples of this. It is noteworthy that it was not until the middle of the 18th century that both technical progress and demographic growth were resumed.
It appears that a new technical system emerged during the middle of the 19th century. The technical limits of the periods preceding the 18th century caused a halting of growth and several crises, more or less serious, but when growth was resumed, this led to the formation of a new technical system. It is probable that without this new system, the economically most advanced countries could not have continued to develop. Furthermore, those countries where industrialisation was just beginning were able to benefit from the new system without having to face the difficulties of abandoning the old one. It is not impossible that one of the reasons for the great depression of 1929 was a technical blockage.
In the report Limits to Growth, D.H. Meadows and other resear chers at M.l.T. gave us a gloomy picture of the future, using a model based on five different factors: the state of non-renewable resources
(e.g. iron and other ores, oil, etc.); the world population; the amount of food available per head; industrial production per head; the level of

===== PAGE 42 =====

HIS TOR Y OF TECHNIQUES
25 pollution. They then examined the relationships between these factors (using the loop process), and the resultant model was analysed using the method of systems dynamics (fig. 5) . The result was a standard model comprised of a network of curves representing the period from
1900 to 2100. In the first phase, a large part of the technical progress is neutralised. In the second phase, the authors of the report do take into account some cumulative elements of technological progress, but they are not seen to constitute a system, or to have any relationship with the other systems. We shall return to this point later. At any rate, it seems that one could just as easily have produced a model between 1850 and
1855 for the next two centuries, and come to exactly the same pessimistic conclusions. Let us consider why.
This problem needs to be considered not only at the general level, but also at the level of each technique. For example, land can only become more fertile and productive if plant selection is practised, and more effective fertilisers are used - apart from the increase of land cultivated, which was a major factor after the discovery of America, and particularly so in the 19th century. It is conceivable, therefore, that limits do exist as long as botany does not lend its support to seed selection or chemistry to the creation of artificial fertilisers. All these elements have a part to play: extensive and intensive culture, methods of cultivation and methods of fertilisation. It should be possible to create schemas which would assess technological limits, those which historical facts relate. The increase in meat consumption was blocked for a long time by the difficulty of keeping animals alive over the winter period: the adoption of artificial fodder went a long way to removing this block, and was more significant than the breed selection. Simi larly, when it finally became possible to conserve grain, the effects of bad harvests were not nearly so devastating.
The industrial sector also affords examples of such limits, particu larly in the field of energy. As machines became more and more powerful, they also became more and more cumbersome, which posed many problems for steam transport, both on land and sea (for a ship, the power necessary as a function of the speed increases rapidly, approximately as the third power of the speed) . Another difficulty was the relative inflexibility of the steam engine in relation to the variable demand for power. Then, finally there is the delicate problem of transporting that energy over distances. Apart from all these prob lems, the design, the construction and the productive capacity of the

===== PAGE 43 =====

26
PROLEGOMENA
A
classical steam engine - i.e. , the old alternating engine - imposed several limits. Its power capacity did not go above 5000 horse power.
Below this, it was not profitable, but it was technically impossible to go beyond it. Consider the curve in figure 6. It shows that at a certain level, the increase in power, Pl to P2, can be achieved at a reasonable cost, Cl to C2. But beyond this, a proportionately lesser increase, P2

HIS TOR Y OF TECHNIQUES

(A] and behaviour of the world model [BJ. (From D.H. Meadows and Others, Limits to Growth, 1972) to P3, can be achieved only with a proportionately much greater increase in cost, C2 to C3. In other words, beyond a certain threshold of power, no increase is economically feasible: the dimensions, the efficiency and the costs all imposed limits which were impossible to overcome.

===== PAGE 45 =====

28
PROLEGOMENA
p3 - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - --=--.-- P t Cost Figure 6.
There were other kinds of limits, and some of these are the subject of a Harvard report. Raw materials are not in everlasting supply, and the growing shortage of charcoal threatened to strangle the English iron industry at the end of the 15th century. It was the discovery of smelting iron ore with coke that not only saved the industry, but also enabled it to expand. We might have been in the same position today, if other sources of power to compensate for a growing shortage of coal had not been discovered, such as petrol, hydraulic energy, electricity and nuclear power.
There are many examples of technological limits which can block an entire system, and create the kind of imbalances which generate major crises. There are the integral limitations of a given technological lineage and the limitations of raw materials. Then there are the limitations within a technical sequence imposed by the distortions within a manufacturing process - this is evident in the English textile industry at different stages of the 18th century - and finally there are economic limitations, such as those affecting the building of the railways during the 19th century. Here again, we need precise and

===== PAGE 46 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
29 detailed analyses and inventories if we are to be able to study technical progress effectively and universally.
Since all techniques are interdependent in any one system, it only needs one sector to reach its limit for the whole system to be blocked.
The railway is a good example of this. Around 1850-1855, rails were having to be replaced so frequently, that they threatened to become a major financial liability if trains continued to increase in weight and speed. Only the steel rail made it possible to solve this problem. Steel had been too costly to use in quantity until after 1860 when cheap steel began to be produced by the Bessemer process -which itself was to be superseded by the Thomas-Gilchrist process twenty years later.
The very concept of technical synthesis shows the importance of structural limits in this field, but their importance varies according to whether or not the problems they create can be solved using substi tutes. One example of the latter is the intensive culture of land, as long as this was politically feasible. Some countries are able to do without some technical innovations because their particular social and econo mic circumstances are such that the limits of certain techniques have yet to be reached. This is a case of external factors being used to correct, on an external level, obvious imbalances. The imposition of tariffs as a national defence against a more advanced technology is an example of this. Thus, in 1821-22, the French placed a tariff on British iron - this was to enable France to maintain her traditional structures in that sector. It is true that there were other reasons for adopting such a policy, such as the inaccessibility of natural deposits, the narrow outlook of the industry's leaders, and the influence of pressure groups.
It was for similar reasons that France took so long to adopt the steam engine: coal deposits were difficult to mine, and transport was also beset with problems. In fact, it was not until 1881 that more energy was produced by steam than by water. The nature of France natural deposits, coal and iron ore, meant that they were obliged to continue with traditional methods of smelting - it was only in 1864 that smelting with coke became more widespread than smelting with charcoal. Thus, as long as France and other countries in the same position kept to a certain level of production, and as long as they did not form an important part of international trade, they were able to keep within the limits of their technical system. This would explain the small role played by France for so long in the international market.

===== PAGE 47 =====

30
PROLEGOMENA
A study of this subject would be of invaluable importance. There are several courses open to a country whose technical system has reached its limits in the face of more advanced techniques in other countries.
Some countries rich in raw materials can survive on the export of these materials, and still maintain the structures of an older system. Elsew here, if these materials are vital to the economy, the problem is solved by technical means, and from this stems the complex history of replacement products or "ersatz," of which coke was perhaps the first example, followed by beet sugar and artificial rubber and a whole host of other products.
Dynamic analysis is perhaps the best method of studying this relatively new area of research, because it throws into sharp relief technical structures and systems, and clearly shows the structural limits which encourage invention and precipitate the mutation of systems.
These are the two essential poles of "technical progress": the technolo gical lineage on the one hand, and technical blockages, on the other.
The study of the dynamics of technical structures is still in its infancy, even though several major works have been written on the subject. It will only be possible for us to deal with it on a fairly superficial level therefore, especially since we will have to consider exterior systems and structures as well if we are to understand the complexity of the problem. There are four distinct concepts which enable us to explain the dynamics of the technical system in relation to other systems. We shall discuss these briefly.
Scientific progress is perhaps the best known and most widely studied of these, but its relationship with technical progress still needs to be considered in more detail: an entire chapter has been devoted to this problem. Nevertheless, a few points warrant a mention here.
Scientific progress is not always autonomous: it is often precipitated by attempts to solve a technical problem. And it is not impossible that some forms of mathematics and modern physics were also the fruit of technical preoccupations. Geometry, for example, was a technique used in land-surveying before it became a "pure" science. It is the same with the instruments used in science itself which depend largely on technical progress.
Two points of convergence between science and techniques should be emphasised here, each of which appears to be the inverse of the other. Science can solve some technical problems, and technics can help the development of science.

===== PAGE 48 =====

HIS TOR Y OF TECHNIQUES
31 It is clear that up to a certain level, there is hardly any link between techniques and science. However, the more complicated techniques become, and the more they need to be rationalised, the more science becomes necessary, and even indispensable. This concerns not only the so-called manufacturing processes (tools, machines and the processes used), but also the exact definition of the required product.
For a long while, the textile industry did not need the support of science at all. But there are some cases more doubtful than this. It is not true, for example, that the steam engine was devised completely outside the scientific context. It is often said that it was developed and in widespread use before Carnot elaborated his theory of the heat engine in 1824, but in fact, it could not have been devised without the scientific discoveries made in the 17th and 18th centuries. The discovery of atmospheric pressure, the Magdeburg experiments with hemispheres, coupled with the increased understanding of conden sation and latent heat finally enabled engineers to translate what had only been an idea into reality. In the same vein, siderurgy became a precise method only in 1788, when Berthollet, Monge and Vander monde determined "the different states of iron."
The two curves in the following diagram represent scientific and technical progress. Curve "a" represents the scientific knowledge required at the different chronological stages of a given technique.
Curve "b" represents the level of scientific knowledge which is at the disposal of a given population. The technique can be adopted at any moment before the point Tl , but point T2 must necessarily precede level C2. Obviously, all types of curves can be used.
This leads us directly to an important point. In the past, inventors are considered not to have had any great knowedge of science, but to have been gifted with a particular type of genius, and they themselves have often perpetuated this idea. Leonardo called himself an "unedu cated man," and Lenoir, who invented the internal combustion engine, and Gramme, the inventor of the dynamo, were no doubt self-taught. But at this time, much of the scientific knowledge they required was easily popularised and readily available. From public lectures, their own reading, conversations and consultations, these self-taught inventors acquired all the science they needed. In fact what needs to be studied further is precisely the level of convergence between science and technology on the one hand, and on the other, the general level of scientific knowledge in a given population. The curves

===== PAGE 49 =====

32
PROLEGOMENA
Q)
