C 2
b ] ....
C t 0 0) ;.
Q)
..-l Time Figure 7.
in figure 7 reveal that there is no chronological determinism, but they also reveal that certain inventions would have been impossible before a clearly determined period. The analysis could be further refined by allowing for the body of specialists whose level of knowledge would be above that of the general level and also by defining the scientific bases of a given population according to its elementary education. It would be very interesting to compare the latter in 18th centuty England, with what it was in other countries during the same period.
The opposite problem to this can also arise, whereby scientific development has reached such a point that a certain invention seems possible, and yet it occurs only after a considerable lapse of time.
Historians still wonder at the fact that the Greeks were unable to develop their technology, despite having the required amount of scientific knowledge. They have even criticised the Greeks for failing to invent the steam engine. But this only shows that we are still unsure as to the extent of scientific knowledge required for such an invention, and for many other inventions besides. Furthermore, technical con struction is a complex process, and technical contexts can be very restrictive. It may be that there is no suitable material, or else tha.t a suitable mechanism is not available. It was an entire century after the physical phenomenon used in photography was discovered that the first actual photograph was produced (1727-1829): this is evidence of the technical problems which can delay the realisation of a scientific

===== PAGE 50 =====

HIS TOR Y OF TECHNIQUES
33 idea. Here again, it is not enough simply to consider this one conver gence, there is a whole ensemble of convergences which should be dealt with. The discrepancies between scientific discovery and techni cal invention became less significant as science became better equip ped to explain more phenomena, and as technical progress placed more extensive means at the disposal of the inventor: this discrepancy was 56 years for the telephone (1820-1876); 35 years for radio (18671902); 12 years for television (1922-1934) ; 14 years for the radar
(1926-1940); 6 years for the uranium bomb (1939-1945); and 5 years for the transistor ( 1948-1953).
The phenomenon of obsolescence is relevant here, but we shall discuss this later. It concerns many areas, from manufactured products to industrial installations.
The inventive process has its own rules, its own logic, and conse quently, it has its own history. In Theorie generate de /'invention, Rene
Boirel has attempted to systematise the concepts and language used to analyse this subject.
Boirel speaks of a "diffuse rationality" to be found in technical progress, but he does not thereby believe that the latter is determined.
He feels that is is necessary to "retrace the steps of the inventor in order to discover the dialogue between his intellectual intentions, and the technical structures which he is manipulating." Rather than
"expounding the necessary thought processes involved in passing from one structure to another," it is necessary to "demonstrate the possible courses of action available to anyone who clearly understands the potential of the structures they are dealing with." Boirel points out that it would be interesting to draw up a table of all the possible transitions from one technical form to another: for example, the advance from wooden planks to metal rails. The "creation of machines through the combination of pre-existing mechanical structures" should also be studied in this way. One example of such a machine is the steam engine which used the principle of the suction pump. There were three ways in which the inventor could use steam: its direct action, i.e. the force of a jet of steam; its pressure; or the difference in pressure created by the vacuum produced by condensation. Boirel calls this the "operative project." It is implemented by means of a structure, either a steam turbine or a piston and cylinder. Finally, they could also use the force of atmospheric pressure, either directly, as in Savery's machine, or indirectly as in the engine invented by Newcomen. Thus it is possible to

===== PAGE 51 =====

34
PROLEGOMENA
establish a kind of genealogy of techniques, which determines the nature of inventions made in successive generations. Of course, a technical structure used in a given type of machine can be used in another type of machine: when Lenoir created the first internal combustion engine, he used the basic parts of the classic steam engine.
Generally, a technological transfer represents the use of a given structure for a technique other than the one for which it was originally conceived. Coke was originally used in malthouses, which means that
Darby's use of it in blast furnaces really represents a technical transfer.
We are certainly better placed now to discuss "diffuse rationality." Invention and progress are conditional upon pre-existing elements. A technological lineage of course has a certain logic, but it is diffuse in that choices can be made and different combinations of these elements can be constructed. What historians need to establish is how wide this area of choice actually is, for it seems to be much more restricted than has been supposed.
Boirel and Usher's work on the history of techniques, the latter of which is incomplete since it deals only with mechanical techniques, have both been assessed by J. -L. Maunoury, and he concludes that the theory is not yet complete.
According to this theory, technical progress consists of a succession of strategic inventions which synthesise disparate and relatively new elements. There is a clearly pronounced rhythm of technical evolution, but this periodic discontinuity does not supress the fact that this process is a social one , and that need plays a particularly dynamic role. Perhaps W. James' term "soft determinism" is a more useful one , since it preserves the idea of individual liberty and initiative , whilst leaving room for an important margin of uncertainty and chance . But this chance is not the contingency of a shapeless and arbitrary universe , but rather the kind of chance which manifests itself through a system of relations and interdependencies. The succession of events is an ordered and logical one , therefore , such that it is possible to trace quite closely, a posteriori, the genesis of innovations through their technical filiation. However, it is still impossible to predict a priori what the nature of these inventions will be , and when they will appear.
To illustrate this, Maunoury traced the genesis and evolution of heat engines from the 18th century to the present day, the schema of which is produced here.
This simplified schema incorporates only two types of structure as variables, and seems to support the concept of "soft determinism," in that the inventor does seem to have had a certain number of choices.
Of course, if more real and constraining variables were incorporated,

===== PAGE 52 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
35 then there would be fewer choices. These variables range from the types of fuel and energy sources - for the engine is after all only an energy convertor - to the quality of the materials used (high speed turbines can only be made with certain specified materials) . Fur thermore, the heat engine could be combined with other types of convertors. The electric motor is either connected directly to the energy source, as in hydraulic turbines, for instance, or it is connected indirectly, as in the case of diesel, which is the intermediary between the energy source, and the energy produced.
Certain points can be made on the basis of these texts and our above discussion. Both of these concepts, diffuse rationality and determi nism, suggest a reluctance on the part of their authors to compromise the liberty of the inventor. There is rationality because any invention is necessarily situated in one technical line, even though lines may be broken and new ones may appear. Such was the case with the steam engine and hydraulic energy, for the classic steam engine was in fact only a more refined suction pump. The development of the idea is not difficult to understand: the action of steam had been known since
Ancient Times, but only as a force, and the action of a vacuum and atmospheric pressure had been demonstrated by scholars, but whose use at this time was not necessarily a logical one. In fact the real invention was the use of atmospheric pressure simultaneously with the vacuum created by the condensation of steam. The object of this was not the raising of water directly, as was the case with Savery's machine, but rather the indirect action of the pressure on the other side of the piston. In other words, the actual pump was finally separate from the engine producing the energy, the latter using the structure of the former. So from this stage onwards, from Watt's machines to the most recent ones, the technical chain followed a quite logical process. "Any analysis of a filiation of inventions, must therefore be conducted in terms of structural development."
But the concept of "soft determinism" invoked by Maunoury to avoid choosing between free-will or determinism is not so easily justifiable. We shall take issue once more with Maunoury, the origina tor of the formula:
It is easy to understand how the inventor changed from one structural combination to another; what is not so easy to understand is why this precise combination should have been chosen and realised, because an invention is not simply a logical progression from antecedent structures. The inventor must have a problem to solve and certain objectives

===== PAGE 54 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
37 to fulfil. It is from the interaction of these objectives with the technical structures available to the inventor that invention is borne.
There are various factors, technical, economic, social and others, too (armament techniques for example), which should be considered in relation to the concepts of rationality and determinism. Neither
Boirel or Maunoury are perhaps sufficiently aware of this, because they are both approaching this issue from a relatively narrow standpoint. It is only natural for the philosopher to support the notion of rationality and reject determinism, and similarly, the economist speaking in terms of means and ends is bound to come down in favour of determinism at the expense of rationality. Furthermore, they draw their examples only from the domain of mechanical techniques, and especially from the heat engine, which has become the almost mythical symbol of technical progress and invention. If this problem is to be dealt with properly, the analysis needs to be refined still further.
Firstly, the invention needs to be placed in its technical context.
Some inventions could be termed "simple" when the progress is autonomous - take John Kay's flying shuttle for example. But in fact the flying shuttle is related laterally to another much older technique, the weaving loom. The invention of the hot blast for the blast furnace was an identical case. The term "development" should be used, moreover, to denote the successive improvements of a technique of which the fundamental principles have remained unchanged. The introduction of automation in many industries, for example, left intact the basic principles of existing techniques.
Invention can also be an assembly operation, as in the case of the internal combustion engine, for example. The essential idea of this invention was to place the heat source actually inside the apparatus, and this depended on a combination of innovations: the carburettor for the fuel mixture; the dynamo or the battery; the coil, and the spark plug to produce sufficient electricity for the spark; and finally all the components of the classic steam engine. We are deliberately simplify ing this description. This does not represent a single technical line, but a whole series of lines combined together. The structure of the piston and cylinder did not change - what did change was the type of fuel, the source of heat (coal, gas, and oil). The next step was the choice of heat conductor which was either steam, or an explosive mixture of air and petrol, or air and gas. This in turn determined the fuel-type used.

===== PAGE 55 =====

38
PROLEGOMENA
This enables us to move onto other levels. The first of these undoubtedly concerns scientific knowledge: we have dealt with this above. Scientific rationality may often be superimposed on technical rationality, as in the case of chemical techniques for example, and some branches of physics.
At the second level, beyond the structures, lie the systems. In other words, insofar as technical invention is not only the product of intellectual speculation but also its practical realisation, it is necessa rily part of a system since the interdependence of techniques is one of the greatest constraints on technical progress. There is no better proof of this than the problem of materials. The complete mechanisation of the textile industry was inconceivable as long as machines were made of wood. Similarly, high pressures, superheating, and high-speed turbines are only possible with suitable materials. And there are many more examples of this.
The last two cases are examples of a kind of "negative determinism" in the sense that invention is conditional upon certain diverse factors.
Positive determinism is quite different, and involves the consciousness of a problem to be solved. There is more than one variable contained in this concept.
Most needs are purely technical, although they do have economic consequences, particularly in terms of cost. This occurs in technical filiations, where an invention at a given level causes imbalances within the whole series. The English textile industry - especially weaving and spinning - affords many examples of this throughout the 18th century.
The discoveries of Bessemer, Martin, and Thomas and Gilchrist also belong in this group. And the same happens when an element of a given technique is lacking: this was the case just before coke was used in smelting, only coke coal (which was already being used in forging), could make up for the insufficient production of charcoal. The numerous patents taken out between 1570 and 1710 are evidence of the fact that this was the only possible solution at the time. Darby's discovery of using coke in blast furnaces was made possible by borrowing from other techniques which used coal prepared in exactly the same way (probably malting and the refining of copper). This is an example of a technical transfer.
Economic needs - expressed in problems of quantity and cost - are just as important, since the problems of quality are of a technical order. Most of these needs are usually divided roughly into three

===== PAGE 56 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
39 categories: to produce the same quantity at a lower cost, or a greater quantity at the same cost, or to produce a greater quantity at a lower cost. These objectives are usually achieved only through technical invention. We shall discuss the necessary links between techniques and the economy further on. The idea of invention can only be analysed correctly if both technical and economic factors are taken into account.
No matter what else has been said of it, the history of inventions is still largely built on myth and idealisation, which in turn are often prompted by nationalist tendencies. The myth arises from the incor poration of vague autonomous and impersonal forces, and the ideal isation quite simply from the endowment of the inventor with superna tural powers. The two are closely linked because any person who figures in such a mythology must necessarily have the qualities of a saint, or at least be in direct contact with the divine. Recent writers on the subject have tried to demystify the subject by concentrating only on the technical aspects of invention, since the economic analysis of technical progress, or at least the incorporation of the technical variable in economic theory was only coming into practice, and not without some reluctance.
Our aim, then, is to tighten the definitions of the expressions used in this type of analysis. We cannot avoid the concept of rationality, since the number of technical combinations is definitive and since any inventor must rely on existing structures and therefore follow largely pre-determined paths. The extent to which these paths are pre determined needs to be analysed. This rationality is actually evident on many occasions when it seems to be unnoticed by the inventor. The explanations for example, put forward by Bessemer to rationalise his invention do not warrant serious consideration. The logic exists, certainly, but it is quite different from Bessemer's notion of it. The successive patents taken out by Bessemer enable us to follow his steps quite closely, and it appears that he was trying to produce large quantities of steel at low cost, using existing techniques. The injection of air into a bath of molten iron was already practised during the first stage of refining cast iron (mazeage). The essential factor was to produce a convertor and a suitable apparatus, and to create all the conditions necessary for its constant function (there were problems with the blast of air, the refractory materials, the addition of manga nese, etc). Bessemer relied on pre-existing structures, so the main problem is to establish how he solved the problem of the apparatus, for

===== PAGE 57 =====

40
PROLEGOMENA
the final development of the process was in fact the work of several people. It is possible to conceive of another chain of logic, which was not the result of sometimes misleading research into works on ancient technology, or patents, but which arose - because of the demands of the time - from the beginnings of a true spirit of scientific, or quasi scientific speculation.
The notion of determinism is no less evident, in all kinds of forms, technical, scientific, economic, and even social and political. The latter two have not been discussed, but we have already mentioned the necessary links which exist between their systems. Social determinism is evidenced in the English textile inventions of the 18th century, which, rightly or wrongly, have been attributed to inventors who wanted to reduce the misery of the working classes. The "Jenny" symbolises this by its very name. Political determinism is far more obvious, especially in the field of military techniques: there is no need to insist on this, but Nef's Technical progress and War is worth reading again. As far as Bessemer's discovery is concerned, one could study the patents to establish the range of "needs" felt most strongly during this period. These needs were mainly technical and were imposed by the development of certain activities mainly concerned with defence and communication: shells and steel cannons, rails and tyre wheels for railways, and finally cheap, plentiful steel.
In fact, whatever the period, and at whatever level one chooses to place the liberty of the inventor, it is always severely circumscribed by the needs that the invention is designed to meet. The inventor is constrained by choice, then and this range of choice is limited (the choice of possible channels for nuclear power, or of processes which can be used in colour television, for example), and furthermore, the timing of inventions depends largely upon scientific and technical progress, and on economic necessity, etc.
It would also be useful to study the institutional context within which the invention is created and developed. The main function of these institutions is to encourage invention and its application.
In earlier times, this institutional context was vital - and this is proof of more than one type of determinism - since it was felt necessary to publicise the need for inventions, and point out the most promising approaches, which represent incidentally, various different types of determinism and rationalism. An example of the latter is the transfor mation of the nature of technical knowledge. Symbolised by the

===== PAGE 58 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
41 change from the exhibitions of machines in the 17th century, to the collections of models and experiments in the 18th, technical instruc tion no longer consisted of the diffusion of facts, instructions on the better use of existing techniques and the techniques of invention, but of instilling a more rational scientific approach. The same institutions sought to increase an awareness of technical and economic need, but without always distinguishing between the two. The foundation of the great science academies, whose field was technical as much as scienti fic, and the creation of the industrial or agricultural societies as well as the offering of prizes, such as those given in the early 19th century for the discovery of sugar beet or for the development of mechanisation in spinning, were all forms of encouragement which have remained important ever since.
Another aspect to consider was the fact that the inventor would naturally want to reap the financial benefits of his invention without giving away any secrets. It was on account of this that whole system of patenting arose. The government in Venice is said to have promul gated a law in 1474 which granted certain privileges to the inventors of
"new techniques or machines." The first real patent rights were introduced in England after the statute of monopolies was promul gated by James II in 1623. In France these privileges, which in fact rarely resulted in monopolies, were granted only to those who were importing foreign techniques: this gave rise to a new method of diffusion, the patent for the importation of new techniques, which clearly had nothing to do with invention. Then in 1699, the Academie royale des sciences was authorised to examine and approve new inventions, introducing firstly control of the invention, then gradually, the idea of priority. Legislation in France developed gradually, from the declaration of 24 December 1762, to the law passed on 7 January
1791 , which protected both technical innovations and the importation of foreign techniques. From this point, the law on invention was extremely rigourous. (The first patent laws in the United States were passed in 1792.)
In fact, the only element of freedom left is that of the inventors themselves. But this is only interesting insofar as it enables us to study more precisely the conditions of invention. i.e. in Maunoury's words,
"the encounter between the objectives and the possibilities offered by the technical structures available." Individual motive, be it selfish or altruistic, is of little importance.

===== PAGE 59 =====

42
PROLEGOMENA
We have examined the framework of invention, we should now examine the idea of invention itself in a little more detail. We have defined simple and complex inventions, but the phenomenon itself still needs to be clearly defined. Many writers have tried this, but few have succeeded. This may not be very important to the engineer, but to the historian it is vital. We shall leave the question of indispensable scientific discoveries aside for the time being, since this appears only at a certain level.
A concrete definition of invention, writes Maunoury, should help us resolve a number of delicate problems. An invention is one event among many which leads to the launching of a new product on the market, it is not simply an activity or a type of intellectual process. It is distinguished, therefore, by three other events, which generally precede, accompany and follow it, and which tend to "smother" the invention itself:
a) technical discoveries which affect invention directly, by summation, accumulation, etc., or by improving tests and insufficient outlines;
b) simple improvements;
c) the operations of commercial development.
According to Maunoury, the harmony is based on three sets of criteria: "an invention is a technical device or schema which either resolves a technical problem in a new way, or resolves a new technical problem; next, it may possibly be useful in production; and finally, it is the result of creative processes and above-average intellectual effort. "
Similarly, S. Kuznets defines technical inventions as "new combi nations of existing knowledge in the shape of devices potentially useful for economic production and which are the result of a mental act above the average."
But these formulas are too simple and too general to accurately reflect what is doubtless a complex reality. One can hardly talk of the invention of the car or television, except in terms of a combination of a number of older inventions that had appeared separately for use in other combinations. So in fact, the only "invention" is in the originality of the combination, which is mainly due to a single new element completing an assembly of older structures. Furthermore, the expres sion "it may possibly be useful in production" is not sufficiently precise: the terms "possibly" and "useful" are both difficult to apply.
However, there are some terms which warrant more precise defi nition, such as the term "development" for example. Development is generally accepted as meaning: "the creation, testing and perfecting of

===== PAGE 60 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
43 prototypes; the creation of models on different scales; trials; the construction of pilot installations; research based on these pilot schemes with a view to mass production; the attempts to find solutions to all the various problems raised during all these stages; new trials, etc. , until finally a temporarily definitive solution is found which enables the product to be launched into the market. The problem is certainly an interesting one, if only because it raises the question of when the term "invention" actually becomes meaningful. In fact, it seems to be at the stage which was formerly known as "improvement."
It is true that Bessemer's invention dates from 1855, but it is equally important to note the date when the first true Bessemer convertor was actually used in industry, which was 1862. It is noteworthy that very few historians have concerned themselves with this aspect of inven tion; we read everywhere that Bessemer's invention dates from 1855, but nothing is ever said about its development. Similarly, the steps taken to perfect the invention, taking it to its full potential, are also worthy of study. The discovery of Thomas and Gilchrist, for example, which was followed by a "development" process making its practical application possible, could also be considered a development. We have decided to use the term "improvement" to denote the phase which leads to the practical application of the invention, and the term
"development" for the phase which takes the invention to its limits.
Some historians have talked of "associated inventions" with regard to the latter stage.
Innovation is quite different to invention, but the two are necessarily linked. "The dynamic entrepreneur," writes F. Perroux, "is an econo mic innovator, in that he takes a technical invention, or more broadly, a new combination of techniques, and places it in the real world of the market place."
The principles of innovation are better understood thanks to the interest of economists in this subject. It would still be useful to clarify certain concepts at this point, however. Innovation is chiefly an economic factor, so we are dealing here with a different type of link from those discussed above: the relation between technical progress and economic need, of which some examples have already been given.
In general, and as we have already stated several times, this rela tionship between techniques and economics is based on quantity and cost. Thus, for example, economic calculation can assess the useful ness of a new technique as opposed to an old one. There are several

===== PAGE 61 =====

44
PROLEGOMENA
new problems involved with the notion of cost, as opposed to that of quantities, however, all of which obviously influence the solutions adopted.
Innovation, like invention and for the same reasons, responds to certain technical needs. This may be the need to reestablish the balance of a technical concatenation, or the coherence of a technical system, etc. But unlike invention which is in a sense a gratuitous act, there are limits to innovation. In effect, there is one major factor which cuts short the period of profitability of a technical structure. This is obsolescence, which according to A. Hayek, "can be applied to any element of capital whose physical durability outlives its usefulness. "
The appearance of new techniques cuts short the economic life of techniques in an older system.
The threat of innovation can be avoided in certain cases, as it was in Spain by monopolies, for example. Twenty years ago, Spanish railways still used some of their first locomotives, and some of these were therefore almost a hundred years old. Again, protectionist policies are useful for protecting outdated techniques rendered obso lete by international competition. Beyond this, there is the problem of newly-industrialised countries equipped with the latest techniques who find themselves in advance of countries whose industrialisation is long-established. This may have been the case with England at the end of the 19th century - this point is made in Andre Siegfried's work on the subject. In countries ravaged by war, the differences between those regions affected, and those left untouched are obvious.
All this inevitably weakens the spirit of the entrepreneur, understandably, because there might seem little point in changing something which can still be used. Furthermore this attitude is sustained by tariff protection and the rising costs of manufacturing.
Mimerel, the renowned spinner of Roubaix (France), noticed this in the textile industry in northern France after 1834. This attitude can quickly turn into a fear of technical progress to the extent that if an invention threatens to overthrow a manufacturing process, some entrepreneurs may refuse that innovation in case a new invention might appear which would render their changes obsolete. There were some striking examples of this just after the invention of the Bessemer process. Apart from the iron-masters' usual hesitation and scepticism, there were others who really feared that this invention might lead to others which would soon overthrow current practises. Studies of

===== PAGE 62 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
45 reports from the boards of directors to general meetings of companies in existence at this time reveal that this fear was widespread. The 1834 survey of the French textile industry also revealed clear proof of this. A
Parisian spinner, Griolet, declared: "when no advance is made in industry, it declines." Vayson, a carpet-manufacturer in Abbeville changed his industrial equipment twice in under ten years, while
Dablaing, a spinner from Douai changed his three times within the same period. Yet Mimerel, whom we have just quoted, expressed that fear of seeing his latest equipment rendered obsolete by yet another round of inventions: "we are reluctant to replace our old machines with new ones, preferring instead to patch up the old ones." Quite recently, the large French firm Alsthom expressed exactly the same fear in a letter to the press: "if we get orders for two nuclear plants per year, we hope to make sufficient profit on our investment (in equipment), based on the hope that the present level of technology will remain stable for the next few years."
Similar reactions are produced when having gone beyond the level of a given technique, one tries to integrate it into the entire technical system. Some entrepreneurs have been quick to see the birth of a new technical system and adopt its structures within their own particular industry. For example in 1822-23, Franc;ois de Wendel sought to introduce new English processes into his iron works in anticipation of the growing market for iron created by innovations in other areas such as canals, steam engines, railways, etc. Such innovation on the part of de Wendel was justified, therefore, in the light of innovations made in other areas. By contrast, Pechiney from Salindres, was doubtful about the market for aluminium, which he was beginning to produce: "The market for aluminium is limited: it is used to make tubes for opera glasses, and whether you sell it for ten francs or a hundred, you will not sell a kilogram more or less. "
The economic requirements of innovation are no less important, and they have been well exploited. Economists have constructed abstract models which do not necessarily coincide with reality, but they do at least provide us with some precious elements of analysis. A given technique is expressed by a production function of a general type:
P = f(a, b, . . . n) P expressing the quantity produced and a, b, . . . n the quantities of production factors A, B, . . . N which have been necessary to make this

===== PAGE 63 =====

46
PROLEGOMENA
amount of merchandise. As G. Hosmalin noted, whose presentation of the model we have drawn from here, the technical level of a branch defines itself by its "technical horizon," its "average production fun ction" and a series of particular production functions, each one of which is characterised by a given enterprise or a "sector" in the branch.
Each particular function describes a type of relations between factors and production. It allows for the evolution of proportions according to which different factors are combined in correlation with the variations of the volume of production. For each function, these proportions are represented by independent coefficients or parameters, all of which modify the terms a, b, . . . n. There are two categories of technical coefficients, the flux coefficients, which relate to variable factors of production, and the capital coefficients which relate to fixed factors of production. Their values constitute the specific aspect of each fun ction.
The production function of an industrial branch is a "series of technical relations between its factors and its products." It resembles the totality of particular functions which can be used at any one given moment, in any one given society. Two graphical representations can be drawn from this. In Figure 9, the two axes of coordinates serve to measure the quantities of production factors necessary for the fabri cation of product M. To simplify this, the number of combined factors has been reduced to two, I and k. The lines A, B, C and D represent the different techniques used by particular enterprises or groups of enterprises which constitute the branch manufacturing the product M.
Each line expresses, therefore, a particular function of production, i.e. , the "law" of a combination of factors according to the variations in the amount being produced. Any point on one of these lines corre sponds to a volume of the production of M. Va represents 1000 objects,
V' A 1200. The points I and k have been associated for the first value, /' and k' for the second. One can see that Ilk = I' /k' . No matter what the volume of production, for each technique the factors are always combined according to the same proportion.
Figure 10 introduces a new method of production into the branch.
This enables us to define the conditions on which the newly introduced technique can qualify as technical progress. Points VA, VB, VC and
VD have been joined by line segments. These points each correspond to the techniques used in producing 1000 objects. In this way, an
"indifferent production curve" or "isoquant curve" is obtained. Line

===== PAGE 64 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
A
D
k k' Figure 9. - Production function in an economic branch. (From G. Hosmalin, Investissements, rentabilite et progres technique, Paris, 1 956)
47 C' (in dots) represents a new technique which associates I and k according to new proportions. It therefore expresses a new particular production function. It is presumed that it lies between lines B and C.
Point VC' on line C' represents the combination of factors required for producing 1000 objects. In this way, the isoquant curve can be completed.
The estimated volume of production (1000 objects in this case) is obtained for each technique by bringing a number of factors into play which are expressed by the following indices:
method A 11 + kl method B 12 + k2 method C 13 + k3 method D 14 + k4 method C' Ix + kx Consequently, method C requires the quantity Ix in factor I to be smaller than l1 and 12 , and greater than 13 and 14.
In order that the newly introduced method can be unequivocally considered as technical progress, it must enable the same volume of

===== PAGE 65 =====

48
PROLEGOMENA
Figure 1 0. - Technical progress and production function in an economic branch.
(From G. Hosmalin) merchandise to be produced, incurring on average in each of the factors I and k a smaller expense than the other known techniques.
This is not the case with this example. We need to specify, therefore, if the use made by method C' of one of the factors is greater than the subsequent increase in expense regarding the other factor. Method C' only represents real progress if:
l1 - Ix greater than kx - kl in relationship to A 12 - lx greater than kx - k2 in relationship to B
Ix - 13 smaller than k3 - kx in relationship to C Ix - 14 smaller than k4 - kx in relationship to D
If the four conditions thus expressed are not met, then method C' does not represent true technological progress, but rather technologi cal change. It is possible that a discovery of this type could be effectively adopted, but it would not be in order to increase actual productivity, because it does not carry this advantage. The exploi tation of a new method is sometimes used for other reasons: to make the best use of a country's resources in one of the production factors, in

===== PAGE 66 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
49 order to actually use national resources, or there are sometimes commercial considerations linked to price changes in factors according to the fluctuations in the market. Every model has its limits, and this is clearly evident in this case. First of all, the actual number of production factors is much greater than two, and furthermore, these are of a varied nature; Bessemer's convertor needed pure cast-iron for example. There is no perfect identity between production factors for all the available methods, and the possibilities of substitution entail changes in the proportions according to which they are combined.
Besides this, clearly defined units of measurement are needed to make comparisons. In the preceding model, progress was described in terms of the increase in the productivity of labour or global productivity, or in the framework of a production function, by measuring the quantities of factors within the same group of coordinates: one would think, therefore, that the problem of the unity of measure would be resolved.
There are some well-founded objections to this however, which extend even to price measures. Some have attempted, with little success, to solve this problem, with productivity per man and per hour, or the
National Bureau of Economic Research index "man-hour." It is up to the historian to make the transition from theory to reality and to examine all the factors of innovation, even if only to produce a catalogue. This means studying the formal analysis of the economists, but also outlining the other factors involved. These may be economic or technical, but they are also sometimes part of exterior systems, which may be important. It is in this sense that the history of technical progress contributes as much to general theory as to the means of analysis, in that it offers a more refined method. It is hardly necessary by now to state that research has only just begun on this subject, so that anyone reading this work is bound to be left with some unanswered questions.
Shortly after the Imperial period, a French iron-master named Rambourg revealed his awareness of all these variables when he replied to the criticism directed against his industry for not adopting the more advanced English processes:
Those people who propose so lightly that we should substitute coal for charcoal, seem to be unaware of the number of changes that this would require, in the furnaces, the refineries, the machines, and the workshops. They seem to forget as well that it is necessary to be close to the right type of coal mine, to have the ore close by the fuel, and to train all the workers for this new type of work.

===== PAGE 67 =====

50
PROLEGOMENA
Thus, for a given date, 1815, we know all the variables that were considered important in this particular sector: well-placed raw materi als, the other elements in the technical complex, and a trained workforce. Rambourg also considered the situation in England:
We would be deluding ourselves if we thought that most of our forges could compete with the English in the price of iron. Mother Nature has favoured them with a close proximity of fuel and ore. They convert the former into coke, and with steam powered machines they are able to produce enough power to stretch the iron into bars. They are then able to transport the iron easily down the many canals to the sea. All these advantages, which we so sorely lack, are for them an immense source of economy.
An iron-master from Bourbonnais, also a contemporary of Ram bourg's, included the lack of invested capital among these problems.
Of course, it would be useless to apply models which are too rigid.
The variables which represent production factors are not necessarily identical, be it from one sector to another, or, where the same technique is concerned, from one concatenation to another. In the latter case, they cannot be reduced to a single common denominator
(of price, for example) and nor are they interchangeable. In the case of the changeover from iron smelting with charcoal to iron smelting with coke, besides other problems of price (and transport may be included in this), there was also the question of the different properties of the two fuels. Coke was not as easily crushed and could furnish higher temperatures than charcoal.
We must also consider the notion of time in this discussion. The graphs which have been reproduced suppose that the problem exists at one particular date or during a specific period of time. If the passage of time is taken into account the production function particular to each technique can very well change and no longer remain the same.
According to the descriptions made, especially by the National Bureau of Economic Research, one may notice that the rate of technical progress affects: (a) a rapid advance in the middle of a period of expansion; (b) a slowing down at the end of this phase; (c) an acceleration at the beginning of the cyclical contraction; (d) a new slowing down in the middle of the period of depression. Yet once again, a more detailed analysis demonstrates the flaws besetting these measures when they try to explain reality.
Consequently, a list should be drawn up illustrating the whole typology of innovation, both a typology with relation to purely techni-

===== PAGE 68 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
51 cal elements, which would correspond fairly well with a typology of invention, and a typology according to production factors which are not of a technical nature, and finally a typology according to a chronology yet to be determined.
Disagreement arises at the decision-making level. While the individ ual entrepreneur was still in command, he was not subjected to the pressure of a technical framework. Indeed, the appearance of these technical frameworks within a business literally creates the problem.
The problem is further complicated on the management level of the firm, that is to say, when not only technicians but also financiers intervene. History often cites financiers' fears in the face of inno vations proposed by technicians. Take the example of Fourneyron developing the hydraulic turbine despite the misgivings of the bankers in charge of the business. Yet the position of the financiers is understandable. Had Pechiney been refused by the bankers before whom he presented his projects, the liaison between the enterprise and the financiers would have ceased, for the banker must consider not only the profitability of a proposed innovation, but he must choose among the various profitable uses to which the capital he manages might be put. One need hardly multiply examples of this. A curve
(fig.11) has been drawn from the observations made by Shelby Cullom Davis regarding the case of the U. S. Steel Corporation. It concerns the evolution of the relationship between the investments recom mended by the engineers, on the basis of scientific and technical considerations, and those adopted which were . advocated by the
"financial commission" who were enlisted to assess the eventual profitability of the projects. This curve is very similar in nature to the one illustrating the applications for patents put before the Department of Commerce in the United States between 1924 and 1939 (fig. 12).
As innovations exist on the individual level, there is little need for explanation or numerous examples. Obviously innovations also exist on the collective and national level. There is also the problem of what might be called the channels of innovation, which may affect the individual, collective and national levels. We will shortly return to the purely technical problems of these channels. The most important problems are those of a financial nature, as they affect both capital structures and financial mechanisms. The notions of structures and convergence must also be included. A coherence of structures must necessarily exist between technical innovation on a certain level of

===== PAGE 69 =====

52
PROLEGOMENA

1 937. (From G. Hosmalin) investment and available capital structures, that is to say the mobile capital available for investment in new types of business. It is then necessary to create other forms of societies. This is indeed the inno vation which led to the creation of individual companies in France in the middle of the 18th century, which had nothing to do with the legal structures imposed by the Ordonnance sur le Commerce (ordinance on trade) taken in 1673 but still in force. This is why the evolution towards the modern limited liability corporation began to take place, which was regularized in France by the Code de commerce of 1807. This action made possible the mobility of investment, while maintaining the permanence of capital. Yet the capital for industrial innovation still had to be found, but for most of the first half of the 19th century, the structure of French banking made it incapable of meeting this growing demand for industrial investment capital. It was in 1825 that the
Parisian banker Jacques Laffitte realized the necessity for the conver gence between available capital and the innovative industrialist who needed it. "This tendency for available capital to be productively invested comes together naturally with another tendency, that being to

"Societe commanditaire de l'industrie" (an industrial investment bank) whose object would be "to contribute to and participate in the success of any enterprise, any invention, and any improvement con cerning agriculture, industry, or commerce." This investment com pany was also to be advised by a commission of applied scientists, on matters of mathematics, physics, chemistry, and mechanics, but for political reasons Laffitte's project for an investment bank never saw the light of day.
Group innovation is relatively recent. It only took place with the aid of state intervention. Colbert's many efforts to introduce and establish various foreign techniques in France have already been mentioned.
The invervention of the state was on the level of promoting the search and importation of the best foreign techniques and craftsmen, of encouraging qualified workers and entrepreneurs with subsidies and tax exemptions, and to put pressure on capitalists to invest. This policy

===== PAGE 71 =====

54
PROLEGOMENA
was no doubt less in effect in the 18th century. One must also cite the Caisse du demi pour cent (the half-percent fund) of the 18th century, which was supplied by a supplementary tax on imported goods from
America, and was intended precisely to support any business adopting new techniques. More indirect than direct, this state intervention attempted to gather and circulate technical information; it sent mis sions abroad, inspected manufactures, set up collections of models of technical devices (the purchase of the Vaucanson practice took place in 1783), and it tried to develop technical instruction when the larger schools opened their doors to both entreprΑneurs and future state officials. As Turgot noted in 1772, next to tax incentives and tariff protection, "what the state can do to favour and promote commerce and industry is to provide technical education and encourage research by scientists and technicians which would lead to the improvement of techniques, and especially it can spread knowledge of the devices and processes that certain greedy groups seek to keep secret." And one must emphasise that this was to be the policy of numerous gov ernments in France up until the end of the 19th century.
Innovation thus necessarily takes place only at a point where many factors converge. The foremost of these convergences is purely techni cal. Indeed it is essential that, on the level of production itself, innovation must fit in a technical system that is in equilibrium. It is useless to innovate at a given stage of a technical procedure, if the network of techniques upstream as well as downstream are not soon developed and coordinated at the same level. Although having said this, it is true that some old techniques can maintain a satisfactory equilibrium with innovations for a certain time. Such was the case on the European continent for ironmaking for a while after the English technical revolution: the most advanced techniques were imported and applied for puddling and rolling iron, while pig iron continued to be produced to a large extent by traditional techniques. But sooner or later the coexistence of old techniques and advanced techniques, for which there are various reasons, will eventually have the effect of checking innovation. This phenomenon is particularly true of some exploitation techniques, agriculture being the main one.
Beyond that, such convergences must exist within the principal systems themselves. We have already mentioned above the problem of equilibrium between the technical system and the economic one, but we should also point out that harmony must similarly be achieved

===== PAGE 72 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
55 between the technical and the social system, and between the political and the institutional system. There are consequently many sorts of constraints which inevitably weigh upon innovation, and the power of judgment which the successful entrepreneur must exercise before embarking upon innovation should also be considered. And conver sely, the evolution of the other systems may call for technical inno vation. History, as we will see, is teeming with examples of both:
obstacles and resistance to technical innovation as well as stimulants, indeed even pressure, for the adoption of a new technique, or a whole new technical system. From the prohibitions, in the name of quality, imposed by medieval guilds on the spinning wheel, or certain dyeing and other textile processes in the Middle Ages, to the first printers' strikes against the introduction of new presses, to the first machine smashing, as in the case of Lee's machine for knitting stockings in the
16th century, one encounters obstacles of all sorts to innovation. The more a society is rigidly structured, the more difficult it is to initiate innovation. It has recently been noted that African tribal societies without an urban base, as is frequently the case in Africa, offer less resistance to Western industrial influence than the traditional Asian societies founded upon an urban network. In extreme cases of social rigidity, only an authoritarian state can bring about innovation.
It is in this way that the historian can explain certain delays in innovation, certain blockages of technical progress which may appear astonishing, and rightly so, such as those in Ancient Greece or in China in the 15th and 16th centuries. Once again, we require a catalogue of well-defined innovations, the study of which would have to be carried out on all levels. Unfortunately all we can do is to bewail the absence of such a catalogue.
Today economic growth is a problem much studied by economic theorists, and historians have begun to analyse it with some success.
But both economic theory and historical analysis have as yet produced an inadequate account of technical progress. They come up against the same problem of the difficulty of measuring it. According to some authorities, "technical progress is the relative variation of overall productivity in a given sphere, between two given epochs." The overall productivity is defined by the following ratio:
R
= production
Q
factors of production
F

===== PAGE 73 =====

56
PROLEGOMENA
If one calls RI the productivity at the period Tl R2 the productivity at the period T2 technical progress is measured by the expression:
R2 - RI
RI
But in actual production technical progress manifests itself according to much more varied modalities than would be supposed by the concepts of real productivity growth, or the concept of the fun ctional relation between the factors and the products:
I. - From the micro-economic point of view.
A) Increase in the dimensions of the firm in order to produce the same product by means of the same factors of production. The productivity of certain factors increases and then decreases. This is the well-known phenomenon of "economies or diseconomies of scale."
B) The substitution among factors within each of the two large categories of capital and labour, in order to produce the same product.
C) The introduction of new factors to produce the same objects (new machines, modifications in the composition of the work force, intro duction of certain specialists, etc.).
D) A change in the quality or even in the nature of the products made by the firm (for example, the substitution of steel for iron at the end of the 19th century) .
II. - From the macro-economic point of view.
A) Variation of the volume of investment necessary both within the branch and in relations between branches of an economy. This is where calculations of profitability are involved.
B) In a more general manner, "the technical evolution is principally an indogenous variable oriented in directions determined by economic forces."
But in fact even the most recent analyses show a certain confusion, which arises precisely from the way they define a technical variable:
technical variables defined only in terms of productivity cannot account for the overall reality. This is where historical analysis sheds

===== PAGE 74 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
57 much light on the subject although for the most part this analysis remains to be done.
The first point to establish concerns growth or growths. Attention has mainly been attracted to the periods of growth in the 19th century, beginning in England about 1780 - 1800, then gradually spreading through the 19th century to most other Western countries. The many studies which have been done on this subject have emphasized the role of technical progress in the take-off stage, and it is in this perspective that the notion of an "industrial revolution" has taken on its definitive image. Using the terminology of the systems' approach, we may say that the formation of the new system was Βne of the causes of the beginning of growth, and that this beginning could not have taken place without a new and viable technical system whose diverse tech niques had attained a certain coherence. It is well known that this equilibrium was only established during the years 1780 - 1800.
What must be explained is the role of technology in the continuation of economic growth, whatever the shape of the curve the latter may take. To be sure, the balanced developn:ient of the various techniques in an existing system constitutes technical progress that contributes to the maintenance of economic growth. But at some point the moment must arrive when the technical system reaches its limits. It may even be enough for one technique to attain its limits for disequilibrium to be brought about within the whole system. This disequilibrium may then lead to one of two results: either economic growth will cease, which can of course happen for other reasons, and which will simultaneously stop technical progress, or a new technical system will substitute the old, which will permit economic growth to continue.
Some authors attribute the great crisis of the beginning of the 14th century to tensions provoked in this way when the medieval technical system reached its limits. At the end of the 16th century another period of growth also came to an end for various reasons accompanied by a stagnation of technical progress. So what seemed to ensure, both the growth of the Middle Ages and the Renaissance, and the growth of the
English technical system at the end of the 18th century, was the organization, each time, of a new technical system which, to a certain degree, enabled new economic growth to take place.
We have already noted the fact that economic growth in the 19th century, particularly in England and France, would have faced a similar stagnation had not a new technical system completely different

===== PAGE 75 =====

58
PROLEGOMENA
from the one prevalent in the first half of the century taken its place in the second half of the century. It is possible to answer some of the questions asked in the recent works by Fram:;ois Crouzet and Maurice
Levy-Le boyer. These two historians, following many other studies and observations, in particular the works of the Institut des Sciences
Economiques Appliquees, emphasize the slow rate of French econo mic growth between 1815 and 1914, and the absence of any real take-off. It is precisely owing to reasons ranging from the location of natural resources to social and political preoccupations, that the technical progress developing in England since the end of the 18th century was introduced extremely slowly into France under very strong protection from customs. We have already mentioned tradi tional iron smelting which continued until after 1864. Similarly, in order to avoid social conflicts and to maintain wages at the lowest possible level, a large part of weaving was not mechanized, and according to the declarations of an Alsatian industrialist of the first half of the 19th century, weaving continued to be practiced for a long time in the countryside on more or less primitive looms. Such a policy meant that the conquest of foreign markets was impossible. It was completely different in England where innovators had been required to respond rapidly to certain production demands such as the shortage of wood for iron smelting. There was also a large and relatively cheap labour force and the general political evolution made available immense markets for English industry. This explains why the English take-off into growth was so much faster.
But one should be cautious of the growth curves described by these authors, for while they do represent the overall movement very well, they do not provide all the explanations. For example, in one of his curves Crouzet puts together iron and steel, saying that "steel was one of the substitutes for iron." This is precisely the kind of thing the historian of technology cannot accept. There is a very clear division between the properties of the two metals, as steel has very different qualities from iron, and as a result permitted technical developments and hence economic growth which would not have been possible otherwise.
In fact, in urder to get back to it in terms of theoretical analyses of growth, technical progress must result both in productivity and in capital stock. In the case of capital stock, analysis is difficult. "We escape from this difficulty," an economist wrote recently, "by suppos-

===== PAGE 76 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
59 ing that several types of capital goods always exist: each type belongs to a different generation, a generation being defined by the date at which the corresponding equipment was built. So a unit of capital of a given generation represents a certain production capacity, which requires the use of a given quantity of workers in order that it may be exploited." This is in effect much like the notion of the technical system which we have tried to clarify a few pages above.
Linked to technical progress, growth causes distortions to appear at very diverse levels of the economy. Firstly, it affects the individual level, that is the level of the individual enterprise. Not only does technical progress cause the disappearance of individual enterprises it has rendered marginal, but it also tends to reduce the total number of enterprises. In France in 1815, there were approximately 600 iron making enterprises; today there are only two, and in order to create a new unit of production, these two have to come to an agreement in order to make it a reality. In most cases, it is indeed technical progress that makes necessary the concentration of enterprises. Between 1837 and 1845, the problem of pumping water out of the mines forced all the coal mines in the Loire basin to merge into one company.
The higher level involves the nation. Here again, technical progress causes distortions between regions. For as an industry advances technically, it moves closer to its natural resources and the best transport facilities. Natural resources are imperative, even if transport is less important for some sectors (such as the transport of energy), so the tendency to concentrate geographically is none the less very restricting. The same problem appears on the global level. This is why economic growth and technical progress have always left depressed zones, in both agriculture and industry. The phenomenon of depressed zones was evident during the 19th century in England and France, and can be seen all over the world today. Just as technical progress leaves depressed geographical zones in its wake, so it also leaves depressed groups in the structures of societies and sectors of the economies.
We have thus emphasized the importance of these four principal factors: scientific progress, invention, innovation, and economic growth, to put it as generally as possible. In each section we have discussed two by two the connections that can exist between these various factors. We have stressed that the influences among them are reciprocal, and that these influences vary by sector and by epoch, and that in the end they must arrive at points of convergence with each

===== PAGE 77 =====

60
PROLEGOMENA
other. It is from this ensemble of convergences that the final conver gence results: technical progress-economic progress.
We should also study the links which are established in three of the factors thus defined. These may be categorized into two groups:
scientific progress-invention-innovation.
invention-innovation-economic growth.
But of course here again multiple combinations occur. In order to explain how this phenomenon is produced, one may, depending on the case, start at one side or at the other, or in the middle, and follow the effects in both directions. Let us take the most famous English example from the 18th century, the steam engine. It was partly under the influence of scientific progress that Watt made his first discoveries.
But subsequently it was the industrialist, Boulton, who offered him a partnership that enabled him to develop his second series of inven tions. Similarly, in the 19th century, the great firm of Saint-Gobain secured as its director a talented chemist, Clement-Desormes. On the other hand, it was a professor of chemistry, Kuhlmann, who founded in the north of France the important enterprise that was to bear his name.
In the second group of factors, invention-innovation-economic growth, there is no clear relationship between scientific progress and invention. Here it is rather the entrepreneur himself who is encou raged to innovate by his own perception of economic need, whereupon he seeks the appropriate invention with which to innovate. Clearly, in the first group the median term is invention, while in the second it is innovation. In other words, in the first type of connection technology is the driving force, while in the second it is the economy.
Let us recapitulate our aim: invention is the median term. That is to say that invention cannot be realized without pressure from one side and a point of convergence on the other. More precisely, whenever scientific progress has advanced far enough to render a new invention possible, science may be said to exert pressure on technology. But an invention is only feasible if it accords with an economic, social or other need, which is to say, when innovation is desirable. Conversely, the pressure of a particular requirement, in other words the need for an innovation, can stimulate invention only if there is a convergence between technology and science.
This is the most simple case. In reality, these diverse conditions rarely occur except in individual cases, such as inventions and inno-

===== PAGE 78 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
61 vations which bear upon one specific aspect of technology. One might even suggest that it is the pattern of a type of developing technical system which is progressing towards a definitive result by the accumu lation of small improvements. The history of English technology during the 18th century is teeming with examples where the mechan isms of invention were of this individual type: the search for additional net profit, fresh capital taken out of turnover, and costs transferred to third parties. The absence of risks, or to put it a better way, the minimization of certain risks were compensated by a temporary monopoly. Here the advance of technical progress appears to be more rapid vertically than horizontally.
The connections between the last three elements, invention innovation-growth, are more difficult to imagine. In this group, inno vation is the median term. The direction of pressures and the level of convergences are more difficult to situate. It would seem, although our analyses are still insufficient, that the pressure comes above all from the necessities of economic growth, whether in order to accelerate the development and completion of a new technical system or to correct distorsions in technical progress, and in the economic or social system.
In all these domains collective efforts are involved. The inventor now counts less than the entrepreneur, who decides and initiates the junction between families of innovations. The time of the struggling inventor such as Watt is past, for henceforth it is Boulton who creates not only an improved steam engine, but also the whole industry of machine building associated with it. Watt who was at the beginning the central element, at least the Watt of the patents of 1769, is no longer anything more than one cog among many others, and now it is only as a part that he can put his patents of 1787 into use. With Watt and
Boulton, clearly, the median element has passed from invention to innovation. An enterprise is founded for the diffusion of inventions and seeks to encourage technical progress. By hiring Watt, it subord inates invention to innovation. The role of science is minimal in the sense that it is above all a matter of employing a pre-existing stock of inventions. Coordination of the whole is the important factor now, as well as the whole coherence of all the innovations which often create yet more new inventions. On the whole, at least during the ancient epochs, this necessity for new inventions was never really appreciated.
Strictly speaking then, innovation becomes less important than diffusion, and particular and partial inventions become less important

===== PAGE 79 =====

62
PROLEGOMENA
than global innovation. Collective efforts take on more importance, and temporary monopolies disappear. The influence of small groups also increases in importance. State intervention manifests itself; pri vate investment must be accompanied by public funds (vital both for the constitution of an economic infrastructure, above all in the domain of communication and transport means, and for the development of education to raise the level of general scientific culture). Here the horizontal propagation is more rapid than the vertical spread of technology. This is illustrated by those countries which do not initiate technical progress themselves but try to adopt a more advanced foreign technical system in an overall manner. There are excellent examples of European countries in the first half of the 19th century seeking to borrow other foreign technical systems.
The general adjustment has passed from the simple technical stage as demonstrated by the preceding pattern. Indeed, it is at this level that coherence is most difficult to achieve between the other economic, social, institutional and political systems.
Thus, one may notice that the two models of technical progress (a.
scientific progress-invention-innovation, and b. invention-innovation economic growth) are clearly different from each other, but without being mutually exclusive. In fact, the most intense pressures for technical progress come from the two extremes common to both models, science on the one side and economic growth on the other. So the multiple relationships may be explained in terms of a certain scientific level on one side and by the needs of economic growth on the other.
Above all these two models apply to different stages of devel opment. The first applies to the preparatory stage where the major components of the new technical system appear independently of each other. Against this, the second applies to the period of take-off, after the system has already attained a minimum of coherence, and when the search for coherence has been extended to other systems.
History is obviously concerned with the progess of evolutions. As one comes closer to contemporary epochs, one observes that there are important changes in the concepts which have been defined above, and some seem to progressively disappear altogether. Two phenomena seem rather evident:
A) First of all, and this fact needs little explanation, scientific progress and technical progress henceforth become more closely

===== PAGE 80 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
63 linked. Today there is no more possibility of major invention without extensive scientific knowledge. This raises fundamental problems which are continually echoed in the daily press, that is, should the goal of science be purely scientific progress or should it encourage more and more improved techniques? The question still stands.
B) Similarly, innovation and economic progress can no longer advance one without the other. Innovation has become one of the essential components of economic progress. And, to the same extent, collective action becomes more important than individual effort. The take-off of technical research, which requires ever greater fixed costs, has curbed individual research.
So the distinctions which we have established between the different concepts grow increasingly fainter.
Invention itself is disappearing as a distinct entity: it pales before the growing importance of the two elements on either side of it. When the need for a new technique arises and is designed for immediate use, it can no longer be invented without the aid of science. The barriers between science and technology are thus crossed, and just as scientific progress takes place in laboratories, so technical progress also stems from work in laboratories, a fact which implies identical intellectual structures for science and technology. It would be fascinating to study the history of the development of factory laboratories, their origin, advance, the way in which research was carried out, and on another level, the exact politics of business research. Although we know something of their main policies today, very little of their beginnings is known. For the iron and steel industry, we known that it was the
Holtzer firm, which, in 1869, with Boussingault and Brustlein, created the first factory laboratory for reasearch on specialized steel. In 1880,
Fayol hired scientists to study the same problem of specialized steel in a laboratory at his factory at Imphy. Successive monographs have enabled us to understand the precise way in which these factory laboratories dealt with such problems. With the aid of these mono graphs we may finally reach a more general and more global analysis.
Formerly, invention had to wait for the favourable technical, econo mic and social conditions to arise in order to be applied. Innovation follows invention. Henceforth it will be the desire for innovation that brings forth invention: the schema is completely reversed. An enterprise can begin research in its own labs "as soon as it discerns the possibility of profitable application." Dr. Holst, creator and first

===== PAGE 81 =====

64
PROLEGOMENA
director of the Philips laboratories, launched a study into electrical discharges through gases just after the discoveries of Franck and
Hertz, into solid state research after the fundamental work of Peierls, Bloch, Brillouin, and Heisenberg, and into nuclear physics and instru mentation after the discoveries of Joliot-Curie and Fermi. Applied research even sometimes advances ahead of fundamental research, in which case industrial laboratories establish scientific results from empirical data. But as the economy grows more impatient with the hazards of scientific progress, it tends increasingly to institutionalize fundamental research in its own laboratories. A direct link is thus established between economic and scientific progress.
So an evolution has gradually taken place. In the first metallurgical laboratories, there was a great deal of trial and error. Using scientific methods of observation, successive proportions of alloys were tried in order to determine the best one. For a long time industrial research remained at this stage. Two stages can be seen in the history of the
Philips laboratories. The first consisted of using the results from fundamental research in an industrial and systematic way: or to put it another way, the method of experimentation was reversed as resear chers began with basic scientific knowledge and sought to apply it industrially. The method reversed itself again in the last stage. In order to invent something new for which the entrepreneur discerned a future market, they needed to solve a given technical problem, whereupon they carried out the fundamental research necessary to achieve that level and solve the problem. This was the way in which fundamental research was undertaken on the properties of tungsten in the Philips laboratory.
But after this stage, the distinctions in these methods become increasingly vague. Invention can no longer be situated in relation to innovation. In a word, only the two extreme elements of scientific progress and economic growth remain.
The new relation among the elements of technical progress have raised several difficulties. Some remain strictly on the level which we are concerned with here. The high cost of research, either basic or applied, necessarily creates a choice among many alternatives. Conse quently, the first choice is whether basic research should be given priority in the hopes that technical progress will result from it sooner or later. Or, on the other hand, since it is becoming more and more possible, should technical research be given priority while fundamen-

===== PAGE 82 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
65 tal research receives only the funds necessary for it to support technical research? Apart from this, there is another series of questions which are as important. If today we are moving towards a new technical system, as we will try to demonstrate in the rest of this work, both a stable internal coherence and a coherence with other systems must be established. Although one is generally aware of this fact, the notion does not appear to have been seriously applied. The authors of the
Fifth Plan of the National Planning Commission of France justified its work as follows:
Since research by its very nature involves a fundamental risk, it is the duty of a government in formulating a science policy to assess this risk in the interest of the public and assume explicit responsibility for attaining the cultural, social, economic and military objectives of the nation . . . The first reflex of a nation which wishes to preserve its independence is to seek to understand the relations between scientific research and the economy, and to determine the criteria that make some kinds of research more fruitful for economic growth than others.
This exemplifies just how important this first question is, but other considerations must be taken into account.
Obviously the increasing cost of research makes any individual initiative more and more impossible. So it has been necessary to construct either private or public collective institutions. The factory laboratory is consequently relegated to the simple role of controlling the production process. It has not, it can no longer have, the means of inventive research. We need hardly stress this aspect of the question as it is well known of everyone.
The integration of technical progress into a prospective aspect is more vague. Henceforth the term planning must be used. It is no longer acceptable for us to submit to the hazards of technical progress in which we have no choice, and we can no longer accept willy nilly what may happen in the field of technology and then try to make the necessary adaptions as best we can. In every field, the economic as well as the military, we must organize the future at the national level or at the level of enterprise, and accordingly we must also organize technical progress. In other words, to use the terminology which we have employed above, invention, in so far as it exists as a distinct entity, is necessarily specified and rationalized: it cannot be otherwise. The only difficulty is in what way this should be implemented.
Let us forget about the hopes of the modern counterparts of Jules Verne. We will return to the Delphi programme, the inadequacies of

===== PAGE 83 =====

66
PROLEGOMENA
which will be emphasized. This only involves determining the actions made posssible by technical progress in a given time. The domesti cation of intelligent animals destined to perform basic tasks, to take but one example among many, does not constitute technological expectation, but rather one of the great hopes similar to the imaginary designs of some Renaissance men, and which was only possible when all the necessary conditions for the existence of a technical complex were realized.
Nevertheless, technological forecasting is conceivable, but it must be the result of precise and valid analyses, and today such analyses are largely lacking.
