New institutions of which we spoke earlier, diverse agencies, and even ministries of research and technology, as they exist in certain countries, have been created to meet this end. But one must not be mislead into thinking that the problem is solved because such institutions have been set up and charged with solving it.
As technical progress accelerates and grows increasingly more complicated, we should remember one last but vital question. As technical progress develops via uncertain, or seemingly uncertain, means, the adjustments of the new technical systems with the other systems have always taken place in a makeshift fashion, by the play of a number of freely acting forces, and with all the errors and reversals that were necessary until a satisfactory equilibrium came about. But henceforth, if technical progress is to become something rationally planned, that is to say the result of a methodical programme, simulta neously in reality, space and time, then programming must extend to all the necessary compatibilities in every field of society, in the economy (which is most often mentioned), society and culture, etc. In the absence of such research, it would be useless to try to impose any technical progress which did not meet the indispensable conditions required for general equilibrium and harmony.
DOCUMENTARY SOURCES
Where history is concerned, the importance of sources cannot be denied. But an idea of the form in which they appear is as important for their study as for their use. Lastly, it is impossible to assess the way in

===== PAGE 84 =====

HISTORY OF 1ECHNIQUES
67 which methodology varies according to the type of sources to which it corresponds. Like any other subject, the history of techniques applies quite naturally to various different sources. Each period in this subject has its own characteristics. These remarks are just so many obvious truths.
Texts Texts have long formed and still form the most important part of historical documentation, so particular attention will be given to these.
Emphasis should also be given to the fact that these texts are often very diverse in nature, and a great deal of cross references should be made between these and other sources.
Among such texts, there are some which directly and others which indirectly, inform us of the techniques used at different epochs. We shall review them very briefly.
Technical literature Technical literature comes immediately to mind, as it probably provides the most direct source of knowledge about earlier techniques. It is a truism to say that it has evolved as much in its conception as in its presentation. The history of this technical litera ture, which has yet to be completed, presents numerous problems.
Very recent studies have tackled this particular aspect and one hopes that a whole work on this subject will soon be undertaken.
When the subject of technology has finally lost the magical and religious character which it had originally, we may begin to a certain extent to codify and teach it, although how far this may be done has still to be determined. While the helping hand plays an essential role in the operation of a technique, it is impossible to give a written description of the work it does. All one can do is to list, on the one hand, the quality of the raw materials, and on the other, describe the instruments and the tools required for a particular product. If, however, the techniques in practice demand any kind of thought process, be it even partial, the so-called "technical treatise" becomes impossible to compile. But for a long time the technical treatise must have remained a compromise, even within some techniques, between passages which demonstrated reasoning and those which were the product of unor ganised, empirical knowledge, in other words, they did not reveal any real methods of experimentation.

===== PAGE 85 =====

68
PROLEGOMENA
The Greeks seem to have been the first to try and create a form of technical literature. We will study this in context in more detail later.
For the moment we will just point out that this literature was strictly limited to a description of the techniques defined above, that is techniques involving machines, certain elements of which could pro vide material for the basis of a theory, in fact the very theory that affects the five kinematic chains. Treatises on lifting apparatus were of this nature, as were those on war machines, and machines for lifting heavy weights. Techniques from which certain principles were drawn were similar, and treatises on fortification are good examples of this.
Finally, accounts of certain combinations and the existence of a programme in a specific time led to the development of treatises on automation. Although our knowledge of Greek literature is incomplete, we do have almost everything produced by this civili zation. There do not, however, seem to have been any treatises on architecture or agriculture.
The Roman period does not add much to our present knowledge.
Their greatest contribution lies precisely in architecture and agricul ture, which will be discussed later. They barely changed the concept of the technical treatise and were most probably content with what the
Greeks had achieved in these well-defined areas. The only point which should be made here, is that some works exist which demonstrate the extent of the Roman contribution to the field of organization: Vege tius' treatise on military art and Frontinus' tract on aquaducts are not so much technical treatises, in the strict sense of the term, as adminis trative manuals which were themselves important. The late epoch, and here we are thinking of the period from the Byzantine age to the beginning of the Middle Ages, was content to accept all the classical literature. In the 5th century, the Byzantines may have tried to take up antique "syntaxes," but still using the same model into which they would integrate the few improvements realized since.
The Middle Ages undoubtedly marks a step back in the field of technical literature. In the same way that science was divided into a limited number of precise problems, technology was confined to works, the bulk of which were merely compendiums of unorganized facts. One might call this medieval literature a recipe book, usually containing disparate elements which were never systematized. We have numerous examples of various types of these. Many of these recipe books do not concentrate on a defined technology, but cover a

===== PAGE 86 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
69 wide number of subjects. The best example is that of the monk, Theophilus, who wrote on the subject of artistic techniques, or the chemical formulae recently studied by Pierre Cezard. Similarly another excellent example is provided by Guy de Vigevano's treatise on machines of war, compiled for the French King's departure for the
Crussades. This was rapidly to lead to what the Renaissance termed "theatres of machines." These so-called "theatres of machines" took their definitive form from engineers' notebooks which were originally not intended for wide circulation. Each engineer noted down anything of interest or curiosity about his craft in a book. Although Leonardo da
Vinci's notebooks are generally known, we forget that these were preceded by a great number of others, which various historians have tried to list.
The Middle Ages, however, did not ignore treatises centred around a given activity. Once again, these contain accumulated formulae but they are better organized and facts are linked together. Many examples of various subjects have been preserved. This is the case of agricultural treatises which increase in number from the works of the
13th century Anglo-Normans, notably Walter of Henley, to the great treatise by Pietro dei Crescenti of the 14th century. The treatises on the art of the farriery should also be mentioned, as well as those on small animals or hunting which were written in much the same vein. The treatise by Jean de Brie on shepherding is also similar.
All this literature was to inspire the work of the Renaissance, for it was to follow the example offered by the preceding period, although several modifications were introduced. It is thanks to Leonardo da
Vinci that we know that engineers' notebooks were to enjoy the same success as they had done earlier. Similarly we know that the "theatres of machines" were very popular. The tradition remains the same from the manuscripts of the treatise by the German writer, Kyeser, to the magnificent editions by Ramelli (1588) , Della Porta (1601) , Zonca
(1607), and Branca (1629). Such works were to continue throughout the 18th century with Leupold (1724) and Konig (1752). The latter is in fact a presentation of machines, based on engraving, frequently accompanied by brief descriptions. Undoubtedly the "descriptions" written in the 18th century are the most elaborate and organized form of this category.
But at the same time, another formula appeared which had already been introduced in the Middle Ages, but which was now more

===== PAGE 87 =====

70
PROLEGOMENA
developed. These works always concentrated on a given technique.
Writers tried to collect all the information concerning a particular production process, but this time the approach to the subject was more scientific, or at least more critical, which it had not been before. The subject of agriculture was certainly to profit greatly from this improved method, while the subjects of small animal hunting or big game hunting were to become rather vague. But books were to appear on almost every subject, some of which remained great classics for some time. Mining and metallurgy were particularly favoured, perhaps only because we know more of these: Agricola and Biringuccio, among others, are always remembered. Treatises were also written on the subject of guns, revealing the first stumbling attempts made in bal listics; there were also tracts on distilling, dyeing, and we could go on to cite many other examples. Leonardo da Vinci had envisaged several such treatises, among them a treatise on hydraulics. Need we mention the numerous tracts on architecture and town planning written during this period? In certain sectors therefore, technology was well-ordered in this way.
The whole state of mind or attitude had changed. This can be seen in the very titles of works, as from the end of the 16th century the formulae gave way to "reasons. " In reality the scientific level reached at this epoch and the weak links between science and techniques, despite some hopes and successful results, blocked the development of a perfectly reasoned form of technology.
Written "descriptions" were developed at the end of the 17th century. We know that Colbert, probably more concerned with a stationary economy than progress itself, had enlisted the Academie des sciences to draw up some sort of balance sheet illustrating all the techniques in use. The best methods were to be chosen, improved upon if necessary - as the title of the collection was to be Description et
Perfection -, so that they would achieve recognition in themselves. It was therefore a work compiled by technicians and checked by the highest scientific authority of the age. Atthe same time, moreover, the
Academie des sciences began to publish a work entitled Machines approuvees, in other words a work on new inventions which had been submitted. Although some of these works were ready at the end of the
17th century, the first publication did not appear until 1762, which perfectly reflects the picture of classical techniques.
The Encyclopedie compiled by Diderot and d' Alembert followed

===== PAGE 88 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
71 exactly the same path. Some authors were involved with the Ency clopedie and the Descriptions, and both publications have stolen plates. But the greatest value of the Encyclopedie lies in its attempt to systematize enterprise and link techniques with other knowledge. But d' Alembert's Prospectus shows that the ideas on the constitution of a system of technology had hardly progressed.
On the whole, it is the workhand which creates the artist and there is nothing in these works which instructs one of the operations involved. The artist will only find in our work views which he may not have had before and observations made after many years of research. We offer to the studious reader imformation which he could have learnt from watching a craftsman at work out of curiosity, and to the artist we offer information which he could have learnt from philosophy in order to achieve perfection.
By making the volumes of the Encyclopedie methodique more specific, Panckoucke gave his work greater depth, but in doing so he dissociated techniques from the general culture.
The technical treatises, which as we have seen were reintroduced at the end of the 15th century, continued to be produced in moderation during the 17th century, but were more abundant in most countries during the 18th century. The first great treatises on agriculture appeared, succeeding the books on "the country estate" which had been such a success from the mid 16th century: Jethro Tull provides a very good example. There was hardly any technical field which did not have one if not several works written on it, and virtually every country in Western Europe participated in this movement.
The 19th century finally abandoned the theatres of machines and descriptions in favour of the technical treatise. This resulted from progress made in the sciences, and the increasingly marked alliance between science and techniques. Naturally it is impossible to make a complete catalogue of these and we will return to this aspect of the question later.
From the end of the 18th century this technical literature was to be completed by technical journals. In this way, the public could be given an exact idea of the technical progress being made without having to recourse to technical treatises. Moreover, these journals are inter esting as they reveal a number of important facts, such as definitive clarification, and the adaptation of different natural resources. In
France some of these journals were originally published by the gov ernment, but in England they were produced by purely private sectors.

===== PAGE 89 =====

72
PROLEGOMENA
The Transactions of the Society for Encouragement of Arts was first published in London in 1783. France began to produce the Journal, in
1794, then the Anna/es des Mines in 1795, and the Journal des Arts et Manufactures. From 1801 the publication of the Bulletin de la Societe d' encouragement pour l'industrie nationale marked the beginning of
"industrial societies" which were to increase after this. Systematic perusal of these 19th century journals certainly provides the history of techniques with some valuable elements. Notice also that most of these journals did not strictly limit their curiosity to techniques, but econo mic problems which arose from technical progress were often men tioned.
A great effort must be made with regard to the whole of this technical literature. Naturally the first step would be to compile a critical list of all the publications, examining both the presentation and the content of these books and journals. What has been attempted on boat building, iron and steel metallurgy, and locksmithing illustrate all that may be learnt. Similarly, an attempt to publish or republish these works should be made, an idea which has been undertaken for some manuscript treatises. Apart from Leonardo da Vinci's notebooks, published in the last few years, works by Francesco di Giorgio, Kyeser and Taccola have also been published. The republishing of old works, which are often difficult to find, proves to be equally useful: from the end of the 19th century some works were reproduced in this way.
Republishing still continue but more cautiously: the Italians have reproduced a work by Branca. The critical reeditions are far more interesting, as they provided notes on the original work which often contained passages which were difficult to understand or interpret.
The United States began to undertake this task for some important works. Between the two world wars, President Hoover published an
English translation of De re metallica by Agricola. More recently more systematic works have been undertaken. Publications concerned with metallurgy, some produced by C.S.Smith in America (from Biringuc cio to Reaumur, thanks to The American Institute of Mining), and some by the Academy of Freiberg, which similarly deals with mining techniques, are also worth mentioning. There is still much to be done.
Some Greek technical treatises have also been published, often in the original text, and occasionally with the necessary translations. A large number are still unpublished but medieval manuscripts and those of the first Renaissance are now better known.

===== PAGE 90 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
73 Direct sources Sources which are directly useful are generally recent ones. They only actually date from the point at which administrative archives were really set up and organized. There are two types of direct sources: public administrative archives and company archives.
When large administrations were really formed, a certain type of document was introduced, and from this period onwards, that is the end of the 17th century, this documentation becomes more and more rich with the development of the mercantile age in which technology was the primary consideration of government concerns. The docu mentation is often important, diverse, and of varying value but no less negligible. It stems from three principle roles of the state:
information, supervision, and protection of the rights of the indi vidual.
Information and supervision are closely linked. From the end of the 17th century the state was interested in almost every sector of technical progress made both at home and abroad where techniques may be different or more improved. This interest in the latter was both for the smooth operation of its own services and the encouragement of the economic development of the country. A whole set of technical documentation was therefore created on the governmental level. In
France, to take but one example, this effort was made in several directions. Inside the country itself, quality controls and extensive regulation was observed, which stimulated inquiries mainly concerned with technical problems. Similarly, missions were sent abroad, notably to England, but also Germany to observe in particular improvements and new ideas wrought in mining and metallurgical techniques. The archives of economic departments such as Agriculture, Industry and
Public Works, which at the beginning of the 19th century replaced the old Controle general, contain many interesting files regarding this subject. The archives of departments which by their very nature used techniques, we are thinking here of the Ministry of War and the Navy, are also rich in information.
The state was also intended to serve official institutions which were charged with the task of advising and circulating technical knowledge.
The Academies des Sciences, created at the end of the 17th and at the beginning of the 18th century, were technical as well as scientific in inspiration. Societies of agriculture, created from 1758 onwards in
France, were similar as they were instructed to promote technical reform in farming.

===== PAGE 91 =====

74
PROLEGOMENA
The patent, to give it its modern name, had a dual aim. On the one hand, it was a necessity, for it seemed essential to protect the interests of the inventor, if only to allow inventive minds to develop. On the other hand, it was important that the invention should not become a factory secret, as the economists of the early 19th century suggested, primarily because the monopoly of a product is always a dangerous thing, and secondly, there was a natural agreement between the two standpoints, as the diffusion of inventions is beneficial for the eco nomy. Interesting research has been done on the history of the patent, which varied in form according to the country and the epoch. The patent was first employed in the mercantile period, and it seemed to be general economic policy rather than intended for the protection of the individual. For a long time it did not protect inventions strictly speaking, but rather the importation of foreign techniques. It favoured less technical progress than the take-off of new industries within a country, using techniques already in practice in other countries.
An older text exists which also illustrates the age of the mercantile spirit, at least on the subject of techniques. In 1236, the King of
England gave an inhabitant of Bordeaux the exclusive right for fifteen years to manufacture woollen cloth in the Flemish, French or English tradition. From this period onwards, some characteristics of the patent were established, including limited monopoly, and the guarantee of public power. The institution seems to have been systematized in
Venice from the end of the 15th century, and it was already perfectly laid down in the English edict of 1623. Henceforth the composition and the law pertaining to the patent certainly evolved. Although the
English or Dutch patent resembles the modern form, the French "privilege" has departed from the earlier form. It was often both limited in time and place, as it was under Colbert. Almost everywhere, research for precedence, closely linked to the present day patent did not exist. France inaugurated a new notion, with the control of the
Academie des sciences, which has not been preserved, concerning the usefulness of a new process and a new machine: this fact was well noted in the statutes of the Academie of 1699.
In the 19th century, the institution was defined and improved. Public bodies were formed in almost every sector. While they were charged to protect the rights of the inventor, they were also appointed to circulate technical knowledge. Patents fairly rapidly became regular publi cations: in France in 1811 , with recall of those granted since 1791, in

===== PAGE 92 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
75 England in 1853, with recall of those granted since 1671. These publications thus formed an important source: a recent Parisian exhibition demonstrated the significance of these sources. A publi cation in Holland has retraced the country's "patents" from the 16th to the 18th century, but it would be useful to extend this research to other countries.
This documentation gradually becomes less abundant and more vague, apart from that which deals with the industrial functions of the
State. Henceforth this technical knowledge was to spread by other means, in particular by the introduction of technical literature. Mis sions abroad and written works were thus less useful, although after the Second World War, many missions went to the United States to observe the technical progress initiated by the war. Larger enterprises also created their own documentation.
Company archives provide an indispensable source for the history of techniques from a certain period onwards. One would hope that this documentation does not disappear because it is thought out-dated. It is all the more interesting because it concerns both invention and inno vation in their contexts.
Once again we will end, as we did for the subject of technical literature, by expressing a desire. In some countries research guides have been published about various fields of historical research. It would be useful, in some sectors this is even an urgent requirement, if such guides were given over to the history of techniques. It would not be impossible to complete this work in the field of direct sources. We believe that international coordination would be rather easy to formu late on this question.
Indirect sources It is difficult to present indirect sources in a logical manner as they are very varied. But these images and objects are all that remain for the reconstruction of the techniques in practice during early epochs.
Historical accounts, often literature of the time, may be useful sources, although they are frequently tricky to interpret. Need one quote Archimedes' technical experiments as recounted by Plutarch?
The first example of the hydraulic saw appears in the poem Mosel/a by Ausonius, but we could cite many other examples. The greatest obstacle in any research of this nature is the time required to study these sources. Historical accounts may also provide us with some facts.

===== PAGE 93 =====

76
PROLEGOMENA
Sometimes the exceptional events, wonderful adventures, and aston ishing occurrences portrayed by chroniclers and historians may offer descriptions and explanations from which the historian of techniques may benefit. There is one particular field where this literature is of exceptional value, and that is military art. Here again, the use of this documentary material requires close attention.
Diplomatic medieval sources form an important source of material for which Antiquity unfortunately has no equivalent. Once again, some kind of classification would be useful, even if it were to oversim plify the problem.
Acts of donation, in general to monastic establishments which are rather vaguely known as charters, represent, up to a certain epoch, a semi unique documentation. It is true that this documentation is often imprecise, although sometimes important facts may be revealed, particularly for the introduction and diffusion of some instruments. By studying these, we may certainly trace the spread and diversification of hydraulic energy before the 13th century. The publication of cartula ries or even a simple bibliography of these could provide the first step in this research. It would be useful if editions of such documents included an index of subjects as this would considerably facilitate research: even a few tables show the advantage to be gained from this.
It would also be interesting if these documents' inventories concen trated on one industry, or a type of industry, thus assembling the whole of the documentation on a given subject. Some essays have been attempted on the fulling mills in England, and in France a compen dium on the subject of the iron and steel industry has been started. All these documents assembled in this way provide diverse but partial elements, the accumulation of which permits the basis for hypotheses which no other form of documentation allows.
To be honest, statutory sources may be considered direct sources.
They are incomplete in the sense that instructions in some cases, and bans in others, only provide half the picture of the techniques used.
Corporation regulations are by far the best known. In some towns they were united and codified: Etienne Boileau's book on the crafts of
Paris is such an example which is often cited. Some collections have been formed on particular products in well-defined regions, offering invaluable variations to the history of techniques. Collections on the production of cloth in the north of France and Belgium provide examples of this. Similarly, many collections have been published in

===== PAGE 94 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
77 Italy, often in topographical order, and remarkable monographs on the history of techniques have resulted from these texts. On the whole, the regulations were lists of bans rather than positive instructions. This proves, moreover, that these bans were imposed upon techniques which were considered inferior, but already in use. As more regu lations were imposed, so the spinning wheel, certain ingredients used in dyeing, and methods of preparing textile materials became wide spread.
Mining codes were much the same, the oldest of which undoubtedly represents the Roman law used in Spanish mines, formerly studied by
Ardaillon. Unlike guild regulations, these mining codes were con structive, in the sense that a number of rules were applied for different reasons, such as relationships with neighbours, and safety measures.
Some of the documents from these notebooks have been studied in detail, and we will mention in particular the studies undertaken on the codes dealing with mining methods in present day Yugoslavia.
The successive drafting of regulations and mining codes clearly conveys the evolution of techniques. The lifting of certain bans, such as those previously imposed on the spinning wheel, on carding, or on the use of certain materials employed in the process of dyeing, are symptomatic of an evolution which many considered during the period to constitute a decline in technical progress rather than an advance.
Finally, notarized acts may co'nstitute a last important source. We should emphasize the fact that notaries were only first conceived in the north of France in the 15th century, but existed in the south in the 12th century. We need not define in detail at this point the types of acts passed by notaries, which may be interesting for the historian of techniques, such as inventories, estimates, and building contracts.
Inventories often provide whole lists of tool types. Similarly, we possess very detailed contracts for the construction of ships, but this has rarely been consulted, apart from in some exceptional instances. It is also true that these sources demand long and careful perusal.
The interpretation of such texts raises serious difficulties, for literary texts in particular and some administrative documents are frequently inaccurate. One of the main difficulties results from the ambiguity of the language employed. Apart from a few rare exceptions, technical language has hardly been studied, although the development of some words would certainly be very revealing. A study of the mining codes in operation in the Italian mines of Massa, and research in the mining

===== PAGE 95 =====

78
PROLEGOMENA
codes of present day Yugoslavia, have demonstrated that most mining terms in use in Europe were of German origin. In England, almost all the vocabulary used in iron and steel techniques are French, and in
France a good number of naval terms are derived from Holland. The birth of a technical vocabulary presents some awkward problems: it is natural that in Greek the wheel and pulley should be associated, but surprisingly many Greek machines are associated with animals and so bear their names; while this might be natural for war machines, it is perhaps more surprising that lifting and building equipment should take their names from animals. (While this can be seen in the English language, for example, monkey wrench and crane, it is more obvious in the French where "chevre" means both a goat and a hoist, 'mouton' a sheep and a ram, and "grue" means a crane, both the bird and the machine). Consequently there is a double problem, that of neologisms as techniques become more improved, and translations as tools and machines appear. All sorts of situations are possible, for a new instrument can be given an old name, or the tool and the name may be borrowed from abroad. We will return to this important question later.
Any appreciation of these texts naturally raises the problem of the methods of historical criticism common to all texts, such as dating, origin, influences, etc. The historian of techniques must add anything which he may deduce from his own subject to complete the analyses, and make comparisons with other texts. He must judge if the tech nique quoted corresponds to the norm, if dates match with what we know of the history of techniques, and in brief, he must add any supplementary information provided by specific study to the tradi tional critique.
Iconographic Sources The inaccuracy of some texts, and the absence of old objects, render any image, whatever form it may take, of prime importance for the history of techniques. Bliimmer, writing on the techniques of Anti quity, and Jal, writing on ship building, have both demonstrated the importance of iconographic sources. More recently, Captain Lefebvre des Noettes based his study of the harness mainly on iconographic documentation.
The problem is extensive in many respects, and varies depending upon the period and the technical sectors involved. We must therefore

===== PAGE 96 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
79 place the information at our disposal into some sort of order. Firstly, iconographic sources must be distinguished, for there are "images," in the widest sense of the term, in which the historian of techniques may find interesting elements, although this was not the artist's main objective. The cart and the ship in Breughel's Fall of Icarus furnishes such an example. On the other hand, we should consider the technical drawing proper, which began to appear with the introduction of the first technical treatises.
What one might call general iconography covers a very wide area of limitless variety. Hardly a single technique has not been portrayed by an artist, in one form or another. This type of documentation naturally evolved as the style of art changed.
Paintings, sculptures, vases, and the whole graphic decor have been used for the study of classical Antiquity, one of the most abundant and extensive sources of the history of techniques being found in pictorial representation. The corpus of Greek vases has doubtlessly been thoroughly examined, and the mosaics of Ostia provide remarkable images of Roman ships.
The Middle Ages used the same art forms, replacing vases with more paintings, manuscript miniatures, stained glass windows and seals.
The documentation for this period is therefore extremely rich but very dispersed.
Documentation for the following centuries certainly diminishes in quantity but gains in quality and precision. There are yet more paintings and engravings, some of which are still very useful, despite the fact that technical drawing had become more advanced. Many views of factories and workshops were depicted by English, French and German painters of the Realist school, and numerous examples may be cited.
This iconographic documentation must be interpreted, and any attempt to do so should be made in several ways: collecting documen tation and its critique, interpretation and use. In all these cases, especially in the first, research should be collectively organized on the international level.
The most important, but most demanding task lies in assembling all these images. Should one classify the images in sectors, or should one engage in a general but geographically limited perusal? In a systematic framework, the corpus of Greek vases already provides us with a type of pictorial representation which constitutes extremely important

===== PAGE 97 =====

80
PROLEGOMENA
documentary material. This is the only general index we possess for Antiquity. Some photographic collections are particularly useful for the Middle Ages, such as the Marburg and Princeton collections, but less so for Antiquity. The Bibliotheque Nationale in Paris already possesses a sizeable photographic collection of manuscripts' minia tures. It would be fairly easy to draw up a list of the tools generally used, starting with centres which already exist and by using the work in progress, the result of which could only be known in a few years time:
we know, for example, that France has also begun to assemble the corpus of the country's stained glass windows.
Another, less logical method, as it would require several systematic perusals of certain collections, would involve gathering all the infor mation available on one particular technique. In certain fields, this would be facilitated by the existence of themes which have already been studied elsewhere on artistic and religious levels; such as the mystic press, the mystic mill, and the workshop of Saint Joseph for the examination of the carpenter's tools, etc. We have already mentioned
Captain Lefebvre des Noettes' work on the problem of the harness. A similar study has recently been attempted by Mrs. Van Tyghem on the construction of buildings. Another series has already been started on watermills, windmills, working equipment, and tools used by the blacksmith, particularly for the medieval period. There is an important collection of seals depicting types of ships, providing one of the principle sources for the Middle Ages, now at Greenwich, in London.
Any critique of iconographic documentation is therefore very prob lematic. Datings, origins and influences all make the · use of this material very difficult. First of all, one must be selective, for there are absurd representations which are a waste of time. Many other contem poraries have warned historians to take great care over these. Images of the plough which appear in the Bible moralisee of the 13th century, formerly published by Laborde, are purely fanciful. Similarly, a 14th century miniaturist, whose manuscript is conserved at the Bodleian
Library places the ploughshare in front of the axle unit. Elsewhere oxen are illustrated pushtng the plough rather than pulling it.
We shall only briefly touch upon the problem of dating: it is up to specialists in the history of art to provide all the elements necessary in this respect. The problems of localization are similar. Although, in some instances, the localization of a fresco or a mosaic is relatively easy, in other cases, particularly for manuscripts, the problem is more

===== PAGE 98 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
81 complex. It is useful for the historian of techniques to know if the so-called Tours' Pentateuque comes from North Africa. Note that the technician is often liable in these two cases to enlist the aid of the art historian.
Analysis of content must often remain hypothetical. If the true identity of the artist is known, then the problem is simplified but not completely solved. But many vital questions arise concerning the image itself which are frequently impossible to answer.
A) The first concerns the fidelity of the image. In many cases the artist was not quite sure what he wanted to depict. The object was sometimes relatively simple to represent, such as spinning with a distaff. The situation was rapidly complicated, however, when the technical instrument to be represented became more complex itself, as in the case of the plough and the ship. It was much later that artists took the trouble to depict everything down to the last detail, which is good news for historians of techniques.
B) Artists travelled far and wide. An artist from Italy travelling in Flanders, may have wished to depict an object from his native country.
It is therefore necessary to establish this point, as in some cases the historian of techniques can discover, better than the art historian, what one could call an anomaly.
C) Lastly, we should consider reciprocating influences, which may be perfectly observed from one manuscript to another, where elements have been borrowed resulting in various deformations. In some instances one may even establish concrete relationships between them.
Iconographic documentation is therefore very rich and valuable because it provides information often absent in texts, but it always requires very delicate handling for all the reasons stated above. We should also look at the so-called technical drawing, of which we only have examples from after the Middle Ages. The earliest examples are provided by Greek Byzantine manuscripts of the 10th century, which contain certain treatises by mechanics of the school of Alexandria and technical Byzantine compilations. In Europe, we have the notebook by Villart de Honnecourt, an architect of Picardy in the second half of the 13th century. Following this, technical treatises such as engineers' notebooks were covered in drawings. The technical drawing was really introduced at this point and continued to develop to the present day.

===== PAGE 99 =====

82
PROLEGOMENA
Thus the need was soon felt to bypass the necessarily formal explanations of a written text by providing a graphic representation which could give the idea of a tool or a machine at first glance. In order to arrive at a satisfactory result, a number of difficulties had to be overcome. The first was the problem of conceiving a type of drawing which was capable of reconstructing the object, and not just making it part of the background decor, as had been the case in general iconographic works. We know that a simple picture, the reflection of a view, is necessarily almost always incomplete, as parts will inevitably be obscured. The second difficulty is identical to the first. If rapid notes could not be taken, as they were in the engineer's notebook, then a rough sketch could suffice, but when it came to the construction of the object by another person, all the surfaces had to be depicted, and accompanied by the relevant dimensions and measurements.
The technical drawing evolved in this way. At the beginning, and for a considerable time afterwards, only one drawing was provided, but in order to fulfil the requirements which we have pointed out, all the parts of a machine, to take the most complex example, had to be repre sented in a plan where they could be best seen. Carrara's seal, though it is not strictly speaking a technical drawing, shows a four-wheeled chariot, each wheel being represented by a circle. The war machines in
Guy de Vigevano's military treatise, of the beginning of the 14th century, are represented in this way.
The discovery of perspective fairly rapidly corrected this somewhat primitive method of drawing and design. Thus the wheel could be ellipsed, and one needed only to place the object in a position so that the whole could be reproduced in a drawing. This method was to be used until the end of the 18th century, although it was gradually improved. The most important modification consisted of completing the drawing of the whole machine, to use the same example, with drawings of its various parts, which were assembled in a small compo sition, indeed even accompanied by a little setting. One of the most perfect examples of this system of drawing is provided by Agricola's
De re metallica of the mid 16th century. The Description des arts by the Academie des Sciences, and the Encyclopedie which appeared in the middle of the 18th century, refined and completed this method of representation: in particular we find for a given loom all the necessary tools which accompany it, and if a machine is depicted, the represen tation is on different plans, cross-sections and profiles, and as in

===== PAGE 100 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
83 Agricola's work, certain parts are drawn in detail. After the first technical plates of the 18th century, it became possible to construct models.
Another technique of graphic representation began to appear around the same time which was to fulfil the second requirement, that being quotation. In the middle of the 15th century, Ghiberti had already provided drawings illustrating bell casting, and these to some extent replaced the table. Similar drawings for boat frames were conceived by the English builder, Matthew Baker. From this point on, as far as the techiques of drawing lent themselves, technical designs were to be developed.
From the 18th century onwards, in some cases even from the end of the 17th century, the real industrial drawing was to emerge. Architects had already arrived at this stage. Cross sections, plans and profiles provided an exact method of estimating the amount of materials needed, and subsequently the way in which they could be reproduced materially.
Objects Of all the testaments to the evolution of techniques, objects which have survived to the present day must constitute the most accurate and valuable sources. It is annoying that these have only recently been used to retrace the history of techniques, as many losses have resulted. In a good number of countries, these souvenirs of a material civilization are still rarely considered, and some historians and curators are often angrily obliged to relinquish frequently prestigious remains. In 1949,
France refused England the chance of salvaging one of the old ships constructed in 1800 and captured in the Battle of Trafalgar.
Two types of objects must obviously be distinguished. On the one hand, there are the tools or machines used in production, and on the other hand, there are the manufactured products. For a long time an attempt has been made to trace the history of techniques by examining the development of production instruments, and research has been undertaken, at least in certain sectors, in the products made, in order to try to determine the techniques employed. Studies of these material sources, invaluable lists, and the analyses of which they must be the subject, are, to tell the truth, not available in most cases, and are only wishful thinking. Fortunately, exemplary attempts exist in various fields which promise hope for the future.

===== PAGE 101 =====

84
PROLEGOMENA
There are many more examples of the products made than the machines used in production. Naturally they are scattered far and wide and in many cases their artistic character renders them inaccessible to certain analyses. Or, to put it a better way, curators of archaeological museums or art schools have not yet grasped that some analyses could be done without incurring any damage to the object, and could provide irreplaceable information about the riches which they preserve.
As the material to be considered is extensive, we should first mention a few major difficulties, two of which seem indisputable, before approaching certain areas of this research: origin and dating.
These difficulties increase by virtue of the fact that even for relatively recent periods, the objects which we possess are often exceptional works, and this is precisely the reason why they have been preserved.
Leaving aside the Vix vase (the origin of which is still the object of hypotheses and suppositions), the existence of Byzantine or Byzantine type materials found in a tomb from the high Middle Ages, suggests two theories: it may be the pure and simple importation of a commo dity, or an importation of Byzantine techniques and therefore an imitation. The numerous distant influences in art have been abundan tly discussed, but few historians have considered the technical influ ences which may similarly be produced. Let us look at a recent example: the great chancel gates which exist in so many pilgrimage churches bear striking similarities. This is true, in particular, of the chancel gates in central France (Conques, Billom) and of those in
Spain (Iguacel, Pampeluna etc.). Here again, several hypotheses may be put forward regarding the similarity of the techniques used in casting and assembling the gates: were the works themselves, the workers or the actual techniques spread abroad? In some cases we have the answers to these questions. It would be interesting, for example, to study Cistercian techniques in certain areas in order to discover how the diffusion of highly advanced techniques and their modification came about, and how these techniques themselves came about.
The problems involved in dating are no less awkward, but here we are joining the preoccupations of numerous other disciplines. Let us return to the previous example: was the simply constructed Romanes que gate a translation of an old product, a certain archaism, or the unskilled work of the village smith? Historians still argue over the introduction of the horseshoe, but it has been demonstrated that in

===== PAGE 102 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
85 some places the iron shoe could sink down into the earth over the years and appear on archaeological levels which do not correspond in reality to their true period. We can only arrive at a more or less approximate dating by making continual cross-checks, either with other objects or with texts or images.
The couple "instrument of production-object produced" is certainly the most interesting, but unfortunately also the most rare. Only a few elements may be associated with it. The Poles and Czechs have found traces of primitive reducing furnaces in recent excavations. By comparing these furnaces, the minerals used, the slag, and charcoal remains, archaeologists have been able to attempt to reconstruct an idea of what elements were employed, thus providing a rich harvest of information for the history of iron and steel making techniques. If these results are compared to ancient texts on the subject of mining in these estates and to the mining regulations which we have already mentioned, it is possible to suggest a social explanation for production.
Objects are therefore extremely important sources for the history of techniques, and research in this subject should be undertaken in two directions. On the one hand, it must deal with the study of techniques which have been preserved, and on the other, it should try to discover new evidence of techniques which have disappeared.
Two types of manufacturing instruments have been preserved. The first are almost always old tools, generally discovered in excavation sites. Such as the tools found at Pompeii, the ploughshares or coulters which can be seen in many museums, and the tools discovered in old mine galleries, such as those of Massa, in Italy, or those of Wieliezka, in Poland. As for machines, the earliest have undoubtedly disappeared for ever. The mills at Pompeii are probably the oldest known machines. The hand mill of Valere, at Sion, in Switzerland, dates from the 14th century, and lathes dating from the 16th century are conser ved at the Brewers' House at Antwerp and in the Munich museum.
More instruments, dating from the 18th century, used in the textile industry have been preserved. From this period onwards, moreover, public collections were better supplied, as is attested by the impressive collection of old steam-driven machines preserved in the Science
Museum in London, and the very old rolling mill which appears in a private collection in Liege. Some period buildings can give us an impression of what old industrial plants were like, although this is often difficult to assess. We need only look at examples of certain

===== PAGE 103 =====

86
PROLEGOMENA
Cistercian buildings, such as the ironworks at Fontenay, and the works building of Royaumont, in France.
A few years ago, it might well have been possible to save from destruction material dating back to the 19th century, which has too almost completely disappeared. During the last few years, however, several countries have endeavoured to preserve old workshops and their tools. In Poland, an old nail factory containing a very interesting rolling mill was transformed into a museum. Similarly, in Sweden, an old lamination workshop was entirely preserved. A movement seems to have grown up today concerned with safeguarding the rare evidence of techniques which are not particularly old. [Transl. Note: the
Ironbridge Museum at Telford (Great Britain) is also a case in point.] The second area of study involves old techniques, modified to a greater or lesser degree, which have remained in use up until the present day. This material is undoubtedly both better preserved and studied due to the development of ethnological research. Thus collec tions of so-called "traditional" tools, have been assembled, indeed a few period workshops have been classified as historic monuments. But even more important are the precise studies made of certain crafts, such as the research undertaken by the French Musee des Arts et
Traditions populaires, on iron tilt hammers, on the work of the clog maker or the stove maker of Villedieu-les-Poeles, in Normandy.
Collected works on instruments, tools and machines have been compiled virtually everywhere. The historian of techniques only needs to be able to distinguish from this important contribution, old tech niques and those which may have resulted from relatively recent technical improvements.
Apart from the tools and machinery themselves, small-scale models are also interesting. Renaissance architecture had already discovered the use of the scale model for the construction of buildings. Similarly, the notion of making scale models naturally evolved, particularly for machines, as did the fashion for setting up exhibitions or collections with the aim of demonstrating and spreading technical progress.
Descartes first had this idea. In 1683, the first exhibition of model machines was organized in Paris, a catalogue of which was made and which fortunately still exists today. This idea was taken up again by the great Swedish engineer, Christoffer Polhem, who made a great num ber of models of machines used in the mines: this collection may still be seen today. In the second half of the 18th century, model collections

===== PAGE 104 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
87 really became popular. Under the direction of the Perier brothers, acting on the instructions of Madame de Genlis, the mechanic, Calla, made small models from some of the illustrations from the Encyclope die, for the future Louis-Philippe. At almost the same period, Vaucan son began to assemble a vast collection of machines and models at the hotel de Mortagne, bought by the king in 1783, which was to be the beginning of the Conservatoire des Arts et Metiers. There were other famous collections, such as that of the great Duhamel du Monceau, which was broken up only very recently.
The use of the model as an educational instrument declined with the development of the technical press, and the introduction of industrial design, but they retained their popularity when technical progress entered certain fields. In many instances models were complementary to patents, and it is in this way that the collections of the Smithsonian
Institution, in Washington, came into being. In 1881, the Science Museum in London similarly took over the collections of the patent service. But private collections also existed: in 1900, the Science
Museum was entrusted with the Maudslay Collection, originally started by this great English mechanic, and in 1903, the museum also acquired the Bennet Woodcroft Collection.
There is certainly a greater number of objects manufactured, from architectural remains upon which we will not dwell, to objects in the literal meaning of the word. Some are preserved in situ, and others are assembled in public or private collections. Numerous points should be clarified here, even with regard to architecture. In this domain, Roland
Martin has studied the marks made on the stones of some Greek temples which prove that levering and lifting apparatus was used. This encompasses the whole problem of erecting Gothic cathedrals and the techniques which were employed. The history of art has often neglected to consider the history of techniques in this field.
Apart from objects found in archaeological digs, many museums have some quite exceptional pieces, rich or magnificent objects which are better identified on the chronological level regarding their origins or localizations, and are therefore equally valuable from the technical point of view.
Archaeologists were not interested in techniques for quite some time. It seems that it was only after the First World War that exca vations were made specifically to bring to light manufacturing instru ments and standard objects. Such efforts have usually been directed in

===== PAGE 105 =====

88
PROLEGOMENA
particular areas. Research was first undertaken in Germany on reduc ing furnaces. Since the end of the Second World War, these digs have been systematized throughout Germany, England, Czechoslovakia,
Hungary, Poland, and the U.S.S.R. These excavations have contri buted extremely important information to our knowledge of iron and steel making techniques.
Ships discovered in Viking tombs have enabled us to partly reconstruct an evolution which otherwise would not have been known.
The Nydam ship in Denmark, probably dating from the 3rd century AD, the 7th century Sutton Hoo ship in England, and the Gokstad and
Oseberg ships in Norway, dating around the 9th or 10th centuries, perfectly mark out stages in the history of the ship. All these discover ies were made beteween 1863 and 1903. From 1950 onwards, other important finds have been made. Under-water research has contri buted to our knowledge of antique vessels, as cargo ships have been located in the Mediterranean, brought to the surface, and even reconstructed. In Bremen, a 14th century ship was brought up from the mud, and in Stockholm a 17th century ship was reftoated. (A more recent English example is provided by the successful attempts to raise the Mary Rose, now in Portsmouth.) Such prestigious remains are well worth the expense involved, as they provide accurate information about periods which other types of documentation cannot.
Although these chance discoveries have contributed many inter esting facts to our knowledge of material life, no systematic research has yet been undertaken. Excavations of buried villages in Poland, and more recently in other countries, have enabled an accumulation of first-rate documentary material. In Czechoslovakia, systematic exca vations have revealed numerous plough shares, with which it is possible to date the change from symetrical to asymetrical ploughing using the heavy plough.
Thus our knowledge of the material world and of techniques is gradually increasing. But it is still true to say that greater efforts must be made both in exploiting the existing material now preserved, and in gathering other evidence. An international collaboration should be organized to this effect, as international boundaries are of no concern in the study of the history of techniques. Links certainly already exist, even on the international level, such as the association of agricultural museums, regular symposiums of maritime history have been organized, and an international group has been formed for the study of

===== PAGE 106 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
89 ancient metals. We must, however, emphasize two other aspects of research concerned with objects.
The first concerns manufactured objects which are often dispersed throughout numerous collections. It would be useful if detailed list of analysis were established for each type of object. A recent example of this was proposed by the Centre d' etude des textiles anciens, recently established in Lyons. The example index included a large number of questions, the answers to which are vital for the history of techniques.
In another field analyses of the old process of casting metal, undertaken by the Societe generale des conduites d'eau, at Liege, has similarly demonstrated the interest brought forth by detailed research.
The second point is equally important. It would be interesting to organize research on the international level. Research was recently conducted on iron tilt hammers, not only in France, but also within countries which still use such tools, and there are many other examples of this sort.
AVAILABLE RESOURCES
We do not intend to make a complete list of all the various resources available at this point, but to provide some guidelines on the avenues already explored and those for future exploration.
Centres of Research Research centres have been set up virtually all over the world, the sole or principal aim of which is to study techniques. It will often be necessary to establish a certain harmonization between them. Note also that the links established between these centres are still too much on a personal level.
Germany: the Union of German engineers, the seat of which is at Diisseldorf, has created a research group on the history of techniques.
Austria: the Austrian Institute of the history of techniques, created in 1931, was the first organization of its type.
France: several centres exist, some of which are strictly specialized.
The Institut d'histoire des sciences et des techniques is one such centre,

===== PAGE 107 =====

90
PROLEGOMENA
attached to the University of Paris, and created in 1932. The Centre de recherches d'histoire des sciences et des techniques is dependent upon the Ecole des Hautes Etudes en Sciences Socia/es. The Conser vatoire national des arts et metiers houses a documentation centre on the history of techniques. The Centre de recherches d'histoire de la siderurgie is attached to the Musee du fer at Jarville, near Nancy. The
Centre international d' etude des textiles anciens, founded in 1954, is associated with the Musee historique des tissus at Lyons.
Great Britain: the Newcomen Society for the Study of the History of Engineering is a well established centre and has many publications to its credit.
Italy: the Italian Institute for the history of techniques was created in 1961 and was established at Milan in the Museum of the history of sciences and techniques.
Eastern countries: there are Institutes of the history of sciences and techniques in almost every country (we shall cite those in Poland and the U.S.S.R.). Centres of research on material culture have similarly been established.
We have mentioned the Committee for the ancient iron industry, a product of the international Union of Prehistonc and Protohistoric sciences whose seat is at the Institute of Archeology at Prague. We can not list here all the centres of ethnographical research which are also of primary concern for historians of techniques: we have also already cited the French laboratory of ethonography which is attached to the
Musee des arts et traditions populaires.
Two conclusions may be drawn from this brief survey. In the field of the history of techniques, it would be more judicious if research centres and museums were associated, as the material upon which a large part of the research rests should be at hand. This short list above, proves, moreover, that these .. centres were created rather haphazardly.
Some centres have a dual function and other centres are totally lacking, such as those on farming and marine techniques. Each important technique should, indeed must have, a reseach centre.
International cooperation could fulfil this necessity, and the regretful dispersal and uneven distribution of financial means could be avoided.
Museums We have discussed the importance of objects and images as sources for the history of techniques. Objects and images are brought together, at

===== PAGE 108 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
91 least in part, in museums and collections, and the historian of tech niques should not neglect this highly important aspect of documen tation.
It is difficult to classify museums according to the information which they provide for the history of techniques. Let us say merely that there are some which are of secondary use in this field, and others whose contribution is more important. Other museums concentrate exclusively on just one technique. We will not discuss the first museums mentioned, as we would have to go into too much detail. A few concrete examples should be enough to provide some generally valuable information.
Art museums have large collections of images and objects.
Drawings or paintings, from the very earliest to the Realist work of the 19th and 20th centuries, often form an important source of documen tation, as Rene Evrard's book on Les Artistes et Les usines a fer proves.
Here again catalogues have yet to be compiled and reproductions collected together, which has been done in some technical fields.
The same remarks may be made with regard to archaeological museums, both for their iconographic resources which are akin to preceding preoccupations, and for all the objects found in excavations
- the tools and the manufactured objects. Some museum collections form genuine technological collections. Thus their catalogues become first-rate reference works: one need only look at some of the catalo gues of the museum of Saint-Germain, near Paris. It is now certainly possible to make lists of equipment, the value of which is unquestio nable.
Local museums are often extremely rich, be they museums which cover virtually every aspect of local life in art, history and archaeology, as is often the case in France, or purely historical museums, as is the case in Germany. Their collections range from archaeological finds to more recent objects collected with the aim of studying local ethno graphy.
Among the more specialized museums, we should first point out ethnographical museums. The importance of techniques in ethnogra phical research is well known. Georges-Henri Riviere has perfectly defined these museums and their possible roles in a number of domains in a volume in the PLeiade Series. This is also true of museums of general ethnography, of which the Musee de /'Homme, in Paris, is one of the most illustrious representatives. Even heterogeneous collec tions can sometimes contribute valuable information: here we are

===== PAGE 109 =====

92
PROLEGOMENA
thinking particularly of the Missions Museum of Saint John Lateran in Rome, whose riches are quite remarkable.
At this point we can only provide a very brief list of all these museums. A comprehensive guide would, however, be most useful in directing research in areas which have not yet been examined, due probably to the absence of a vital thread. We must direct our attention to museums which concentrate principally on techniques. Our list does not claim to be exhaustive, but we will cite the most important ones and give a selection which may be of use to the researcher. General museums often possess more comprehensive lists, and inventories have already been undertaken which will be added to progressively.
Some museums concentrate on the whole technical ensemble. They are often associated with science museums, but here, we will indicate some of the most important centres.
Germany: Deutsches Museum von Meisterwerken der Naturwissens chaft und der Technik. This was founded in 1903, in Munich.
Austria: Technisches Museum fur Industrie und Gewerbe. Created in 1908 and established in Vienna.
Denmark: Tekniske Museum.
United States of America: Smithsonian Institution, in Washington.
France: Musee du Conservatoire national des arts et metiers. We have already mentioned that the origin of this museum goes back to the model collection set up by Vaucanson. These objects, formerly instru ments of documentation and demonstration, have gradually become museum pieces.
Great Britain: Science Museum, in London.
Italy: Museo Nazionale della Scienza e della Tecnica, in Milan.
Norway: Museum of techniques established in Oslo.
Sweden: Tekniska Museet, in Stockholm.
Czechoslovakia: Museum of techniques, in Prague.
U.S.S.R. : Museum of technology, in Moscow.
Most of these various museums have published catalogues, which often represent well-defined techniques. The catalogues published by the Science Museum, in London, do not just provide descriptions of the collections but also include information on the historical background of the techniques considered. Founded in 1857, it is undoubtedly the most experienced museum.
Many museums are given over to a single technique or a technical ensemble. These are without doubt the richest and consequently the

===== PAGE 110 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
93 most interesting museums. Because they have restricted themselves to one subject of study, the presentation of that technique is far more comprehensive. Larger museums, on the other hand, are often composed of collections or objects placed side by side without any general theme of presentation. Similarly, large museums often try to gather together the most exceptional pieces, but more specialized museums have another advantage, as the techniques are generally presented in their historical, social and economic context. We will give just a few examples, mentioning only the best stocked museums, and leaving aside the specialized sections in general museums.
Agriculture: apart from the museum of agriculture in Budapest, there are no large museums on the history of farming so a lamentable gap exists in this subject.
Mining: a mining museum exists at Bochum, in Germany, and another is presently in formation at Saint-Etienne, in France. Old mining sites are often converted into museums, such as those in
Sweden, the most important of which is in Falun, and we should again mention the marvellous salt mines at Wieliezka in Poland, and there are several in Wales and in the North of England.
Military art: Army and War museums have been created almost everywhere. Firmly centred on the history of the army, these museums frequently preserve rich military material, at least from the 15th century onwards, which enables much work to be done on military techniques. The art of fortification has not been covered in so much depth although in truth this is considerably more difficult to present, but there are certainly some excellent examples of military archi tecture available for use.
Transport: virtually nothing exists on the transport system as a whole, although some sectors of it are very well represented. Almost every country which enjoyed maritime vocations now have maritime museums. Sometimes there are several such museums in one country.
Although these museums are generally given over to the history of the military navy, the merchant navy is also given a noticeable place, such as the museum of Albenga which concentrates on under-water archaeological finds. Some museums, in particular those of Paris and
Greenwich, are very rich in models and documentation of all kinds.
Land transports are less well covered. There are railway museums at Utrecht, in Holland, and at Storhamar, in Norway, which was founded in 1896. Others are planned, as in Mulhouse, France. There is no one

===== PAGE 111 =====

94
PROLEGOMENA
large automobile museum, but private collections exist, such as those of Ford or Fiat which are the richest, and there are many small, limited collections which together could form a very large and useful museum.
Britain, however, boasts the London Transport Museum. Similarly, no large aviation museum exists as one might have expected, although the Battle of Britain Museum covers aircraft and weapons of that period. Rich Parisian collections are still waiting to be housed before they can be presented.
Agricultural Industries: museums on particular farming activities exist, although they are somewhat unequally represented. The most famous activity represented by museums is of course wine making, and many such museums exist within a single country. The most famous are at Beaune, in France, at Speyer, in Germany, and
Krems, in Austria. Other fields of agriculture are less well repre sented by specialized museums, but in France there is a Tobacco museum at Bergerac, and an Olive museum at Cagnes-sur-Mer.
Recently a cheese museum has been established in Auvergne. There is almost nothing, however, on fishing, forestry, and animal hus bandry.
Diverse industries: these are perhaps better, but again only partially represented, and those that are do not have enough material.
The textile industry is represented by the Textile museum at Lyons, one of the most important in this field, the Hoisery museum at Troyes,
France, and a Knitting museum in Denmark.
The iron industry is represented by the Iron museum at J arville, near Nancy, which has clearly tried to show techniques in their human, historical and economic context. We should also mention the Cutlery museums at Sollingen, in Germany and at Langres, France.
Clock making is well represented at Besanc;on, France, and natu rally in Switzerland.
For the glass industry we must cite the Glass museum at Murano, in Italy, and the large collection at the Corning Glass Centre in the state of New York.
The shoe industry is exhibited in several rooms in Fougeres and Romans, in France.
For photography, the large collections of Eastmann-Kodak in Rochester in the United States are available, and a rather more modest museum exists in the suburbs of Paris, as well as one in Chalon-sur
Sa6ne.

===== PAGE 112 =====

HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
95 We have only given a brief selection here, and the list could easily be completed. As we see, there are many industries which are not represented, among them the most important. Although some infor mation on industries, such as the chemical industry, is difficult to exhibit in museum collections, it is regretful that other industries do not have their own museums. Must we await their arrival?
Libraries and Archives Similar comments and distinctions may be applied to libraries and archives. General institutions, national libraries and national archives exist which are naturally the first places of reference. But here, specialized centres are far less numerous.
For libraries, we must first point out those brought together by research centres, which have been discussed above. This is true of libraries which form specialized museums, some of which are a rich source of material. Specialized libraries concentrating on a particular subject are very rare. The Iron library at Schaffhausen, in Switzerland, is an exception, however, as it has a well-defined aim and extensive documentation.
Technical libraries exist, generally annexed to technical teaching establishments: we could put forward the library of the Conservatoire national des arts et metiers, in Paris, as a model example. The older these libraries are, the greater the chronological limits of their collec tions. Libraries belonging to certain associations could equally be cited, such as those of the association of engineers, and the association of manufacturers. In France there is the rich library of the Societe des ingenieurs civils (Society of civil engineers) and that of the Association nationale pour le developpement de l'industrie (National Association for the development of industry), a wealthy collection which unfortu nately has suffered the consequences of regretful neglect. Those responsible for these institutions should not dispose of these invalua ble documents under the pretext of getting rid of out-dated material.
The same observations may be made for archive material. We should first of all distinguish public and private archives. The former are naturally fascinating, in so far as public establishments are concer ned with questions of techniques, because they use these techniques themselves, either with the aim of supervision, or to try to initiate innovation in order to sustain economic expansion. Military archives,

===== PAGE 113 =====

96
PROLEGOMENA
those of Bridges and Roads, and institutions whose aim is the issue of patents, may be cited in the first category. The second category may be represented by ministry archives in the Departments of Mining,
Industry, and Agriculture. The last category includes almost all the administrations of the state. We need not worry about the preservation of these archives, which have provided a large part of the documen tation for historians of techniques, but an indexing system is required, however, as are study guides, which have been compiled in other domains.
The problem of private archives is more difficult to solve, and some cases are agonizing. By private archives we mean mainly business archives. Company technological archives may even relate to docu ments which do not concern technology in the first place: such is the case of accounting archives. But this documentation may concern application, adaptation, and the improvement of techniques used by the enterprise. Equally important, and more recent, are the archives for research purposes which appeared at the end of 19th century.
Here, technology is less isolated than elsewhere as in these archives one finds the whole human environment with the labour force which commands it, and in the complete commercial, economic, and finan cial context etc. It would be a grave error to reduce the conservation of these technical archives. Herein lies the whole problem of company archives which must be resolved. Since the end of the Second World
War interesting attempts in this direction have been made in several countries, but they have yet to be generalized and systematized.
Similarly archives belonging to the association of manufacturers must be considered, some of which are very old. Thus technical problems are occasionally considerable.

===== PAGE 114 =====

TECHNIQUES
AND CIVILIZATIONS

===== PAGE 115 =====

===== PAGE 116 =====

THE ORIGINS
OF TECHNIQUES
There are innumerable works, scholarly and popular, on the origins of techniques. Prehistorians, philosophers and technicians have all con sidered this problem; their conclusions are varied and sometimes even contradict one another, which can leave the reader confused and uncertain. I would therefore like to reassure readers that this chapter will provide no new explanations. My aim here has been simply to classify and establish a certain order with regard to the facts, in an attempt to discover the first outlines of an evolutionary logic of techniques. This aim seems all the more worthwhile now that some very recent discoveries have completely shaken our ideas about the origins of humanity.
SOLUTIONS BORN OF IGNORANCE
This is not a question of so-called false solutions. To the extent that those who have dealt with the question of the origins of techniques were unable to detect any main theme, they fell back on explanations which placed these origins outside of the human domain. We should not be mistaken into thinking that the knowledge of different technical civilisations lasts for ever. Take for example the basic instrument of all technical activity, the tool. Certainly, tools evolve according to tendencies which can be easily studied. But there are tools which disappeared: the double-faced cut flint no longer exists in modern civilisation, the bow drill was removed about twenty years ago from the

===== PAGE 117 =====

100
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
catalogue of this classic of mail-order companies, the Manufacture d'Armes et de Cycles de Saint-Etienne. Certain trades have almost completely disappeared, for instance there are no more blacksmiths in our villages.
The restoration of techniques used in the early ages of manking began only a century ago. One can understand, therefore, that the lack of a coherent body of knowledge should have led the first of those to write about the history of techniques to look for the earliest forms of evidence in exterior worlds. Within all these words, there were two privileged areas: the gift from the gods was the first of these, and this was succeeded much later by the imitation of nature.
Gifts from the Gods There is a rich mythology of techniques, but it is unequally distributed.
It exists in polytheistic religions, but rarely in monotheistic ones. The Greeks actually have such a mythology, the Romans far less so, and the Hebrews have virtually no mythology of techniques at all. The
Greeks attributed the working of metals to a god, Hephaestus; the equivalent in Hebrew mythology was a man descended from Cain, known as Tubal Cain. We shall deal only with Greek mythology in this short part. It is the richest of its kind and the most widely studied.
I would like to point out first, as others have done, the use and profound significance of certain words, some of them close in meaning.
Tekhne designates above all, practical activity, both material and manual. In Homer, this work evokes the idea of a gift from the heavens. P.-H. Michel even arrives at the conclusion that before the
6th century BC, science was indistinguishable from techniques and that the latter (a divine entity) bore witness to its religious origins. A second example is the word metis, which designates a form of practical intelligence. Remember, too, that Metis is the mother of Athene.
Thus we have the outline of a filiation which permits the intervention of gods, demi-gods and heroes and heroines who are almost human.
