In the first group, two closely-linked gods, Athene and Hephaestus, play a major role in the transmission of certain techniques to men.
We have just mentioned that Athene was the daughter of Metis; this connects her with practical activities which require a certain type of intelligence. Jealous of Demeter, who is reputed to have invented wheat, Athene is believed to have invented the swing plough which she

===== PAGE 118 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
101 gave to Atticus. But according to others, it was a woman, Myrmex, who stole the idea - one of the numerous examples of "stealing" from the gods. According to J.-P. Vernant, Athene is seen as a divine power devoted to work on the land, or more precisely, to plowing and its fecundating action. In other words, one could say that, from Demeter to Athene there has been a movement from gathering to agriculture.
"Athene intervenes as a power endowed with sollertia, or manual dexterity and practical intelligence: she makes the instrument, the technical object which allows Demeter's wheat to be harvested more easily." Athene is certainly a technical power.
She is also the goddess of textiles. She not only made her beautiful peplos herself, but she also taught Pandora, the creature borne of
Haphaestus and Athene, the art of making cloth. It should be easy to describe the different types of Athene's activities, all of which make her a polyvalent power, and indeed, this has already been done. For in fact she is also a warrior goddess, and familiar with everything concerned with weapons. The same goes for the art of carpentry, which along with cloth-making and the working of wood, leads us naturally to ship-building, from which Ulysses was to profit. Finally, was it not she who invented the bit, and thus enabled Bellerophon to tame Pegasus? Throughout all this a pattern emerges, techniques link together and become complementary. Take the horse, for example, which was used for agricultural purposes, but was equally important as a creature of war. Athene is well on the way to have conceived of and entrusted to men, whether voluntarily or not, a real technical system.
And now we come to Hephaestus, the technical god par excellence, who invented the working of metal by using fire. It is well-known that for a long time forging seemed a mysterious activity, verging on sorcery. Even in the 13th century, Biscournet, the locksmith who is reputed to have forged the hinges of Paris Notre-Dame is said to have been ready to sell his soul to the devil in order to acquire the techniques necessary for his work. Primitive, ancient and traditional civilisations have always attributed the smith with having supernatural powers: fire and the transubstantiation of all kinds of materials from stone to metal, remained incomprehensible for a Jong time.
Hephaestus worked all metals, and the works attributed to him are firm proof of this. Hera's earrings and Pandora's crown reveal his expertise as a goldsmith. Achilles' shield, Diomedes' and Heracles' breastplates prove him to be an effective maker of weapons and a

===== PAGE 119 =====

102
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
skilful worker of bronze. He also worked with wood and leather: he made both chariots of war and harnesses for horses.
In a given technical system, therefore, Hephaestus appears to be the complementary power to Athene. These two represent virtually all the technical activities of the early era. But there is still more to say about
Hephaestus which brings us almost to the time of Archytas. Hephaes tus created veritable automata which were in fact more than mere robots, since they were actually alive. These creations were often made with Athene's help, and included the three-legged tables which could make their own way (automatoi) to an assembly of the gods, or the golden mechanical women who helped the crippled god to move.
And then there was the creation of Pandora. Hephaestus was a true demiurge, therefore, giving life to everything he made. He also · created Talas, the man of bronze who was appointed by Minos as the guard of Crete which he regularly patrolled at an unerring pace. This was the supreme warrior robot. Hephaestus was also familiar with pottery: Pandora was made with terra cotta before being brought to life. Similarly, there is a vase in the Berlin Museum which depicts
Athene modelling a horse out of clay. Forging, smelting and casting bronze, working with precious metals and using the art of the potter:
these were the main elements of Hephaestus' technical power, which to a certain extent overlaps with that of Athene's. Together, these two created woman, just as Hephaestus hepled Prometheus to create man.
With a little effort, it would be possible to establish when this mythology began to evolve. There are some techniques which actually go back as far as the VI millenium BC (agriculture and pottery for example), others are slightly more recent (the metallurgy of copper and bronze), and these are followed by even later ones (chariots of war) . But generally speaking, they were all in existence by the beginning of the III millenium. During the 6th century BC, the new upsurge in technical development was in a sense the continuation of a long historical process, but adapted to an era when techniques were of prime importance.
With Prometheus we are breaking new ground, in that the first signs of a humanisation of techniques are becoming apparent. P.-H. Michel notes:
(It) remains a gift from the gods, but there is a poem by Homer which glorifies Hermes for it, who is not so much an inferior god, as a more human one in that he does not possess all skills, but rather he discovers them by intelligent means. Like a man of

===== PAGE 120 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
103 genius, he invents them and then passes the knowledge onto man. The guardian of the "secret" then teaches himself and he too acquires a knowledge which he is capable of passing on. Better still, skills are no longer considered as gifts from gods, but as the result of a theft at the expense of the gods. The myth of Prometheus is the most legendary expression of this belief which in the works of Hesiod and Pindar especially, has given new meaning to the word tekhne. Hesiod believes, for example, that alongside the skills of divine origin, there are purely human skills, such as the art of the caster.
In fact the conflict which broke out between Prometheus and Zeus first appeared in a late text by Diodorus. According to J.-P. Vernant, the promethean myth as represented by Hesiod is quite coherent, and already raises the problem of the function of techniques: labour appears to be consequence of the conflict between Zeus and Prome theus. The stealing of fire must be paid for and henceforth, labour was the condition of all wealth. From this stemmed the opposition between fertility and labour, between that which was supplied by nature through the intermediary of the gods, and techniques, which given to the accursed, or stolen by him, or which simply issued forth from his imagination. Demeter gave us wheat, but that was not enough; the gods gave us the wind, but that was not enough either. Athene gave us the plough, and ships and sails, and Prometheus gave us fire. In Plato,
Prometheus represents the elaborate idea of techniques as a social function.
This, according to Vernant; was how the myth of Prometheus evolved. Labour and techniques were activities imposed upon us, but they were linked to religious thought. But in Plato, labour and techniques fulfil a social function which is entirely human and material, and they are therefore considered as human activities. Similarly,
Aeschylus attempted to integrate labour and technical activities into the human world.
We must note, too, that the techniques associated with Prometheus are those which involve using fire: they concern metallurgists, potters and no doubt cooks as well. He is not connected with any of the other techniques. But these connections are relatively vague if one considers them from a purely technical point of view. In the latter sense,
Prometheus emulated, if not competed with Hephaestus. For this hero, there was no question of weapons or metal implements. He was the symbol, quite simply, of all sources of heat and by virtues of this, he represented all those descendants of the devil and the sorcerors, the select guilds concerned with skills using fire. Furthermore, these were the first artisan guilds which developed outside of the domestic envir-

===== PAGE 121 =====

104
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
onment and whose members were part of the first specialised trades.
Spinning and weaving could be done in the home, as could the working of leather and wood, but forging and pottery-firing was impossible.
And of course the main unit of those working the land was the family.
But activities concerned with fire, except cooking and baking, had to be done outside the house, and they were not practised within the family community, but by outsiders.
From our point of view, which is concerned strictly with the origins of techniques, the role of Prometheus, like that of his slow-witted brother
Epimetheus, is particularly confusing. The creation of man and the stealing of fire are complementary, as man cannot live without fire, and this distinguishes him from animals. This seems to show a necessary link between the appearance of man and the birth of techniques. But henceforth there was to be an indispensable intermediary between the gods and men. Unable to accept the human origin of techniques, or at least of the most essential, man invented Prometheus.
Daedalus leads us to a third definitive stage, which is a kind of secularisation of techniques. But there is an important point to be made beforehand. Until this point, we were concerned only with broadly surveying the basic techniques which appear to have been given to man, such as metals, pottery, textiles, and ships. With
Daedalus, we shall enter into the details of techniques, and in particu lar, its most important aspect, the tool.
But there is more. We are leaving behind divine origins. Daedalus is a man; he even has the makings of an historical context. He can be situated in time and space, and genealogically. He no longer receives, he "invents."
Chronologically, Daedalus was a Cretan sculptor in the 7th century BC. Many people, including Diodorus, believe he was an Athenian and of royal descent. His father was probably Metion, which would connect him with the metis, which we have discussed above. According to Pausanias, "Daedalus was famous throughout the world for his skills, as well as for his wanderings and misfortunes." But it is possible to be more precise about this. The Daedalus legends, or at least the oldest of them were brought to us by Diodorus which would place them in the first century BC. Prometheus was much older than this. During the period between Prometheus .. and Daedalus, the first major devel opment of Greek techniques (whichever century one chooses to place him in - although it was most likely between the 6th and 4th centuries

===== PAGE 122 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
105 BC) was an exclusively human achievement and was the first to be acknowledged as such. From here onwards, the secularisation of techniques was inevitable. No gods will feature in the following discussion, therefore, only men, and these shall have an historical reality. This includes Daedalus and his legend. We are no longer dealing with the hazy memories of the beginnings of agriculture, metallurgy and pottery where only gods and demi-gods played a part.
Frarn;oise Frontisi-Ducroux has provided us with an excellent study of Daedalus and one of the things she deals with is the hero's name.
"Daidalon" denotes anything which can be done in the material sense, anything that can be made: weapons and armour, jewels, cloths and fabrics, all wooden objects, chariots and the framework of ships.
Moreover, the gods of daidalon are in fact Athene and Hephaestus.
When a list was made of the objects coming under this generic term, it was noticed that out of forty-nine quotations, forty were concerned with metal objects, five with textiles and four with wood.
It is possible to see the formation of a system at this point. We have a hero whose name in everyday vocabulary denotes every object of craftsmanship, a hero who belongs to the royal dynasty of Athens, which itself goes back to Hephaestus and Athene. Daedalus seems to be the prototype of the artist and the artisan, and indeed, of the artist and artisan fused within the same person. He created the first divine images, but he also invented essential technical implements. Fur thermore, he was also an architect and an engineer. He is the very embodiment of inventive genius and artistic talent. He is the true representative of tekhne, a term which denotes the skill of the doctor, the dexterity of the cobbler, the knowledge of the architect and the artistry of the musician, indeed everything which concerns material creation.
Daedalus was also a sculptor. He is reputed to have invented statues of gods made out of wood or wood covered with a layer of metal. The dedalic style of sculpting is characteristic: with their eyes open, legs apart, and arms stretched out in from away from the body, the statues seem almost alive. and like Athene and Hephaestus, Daedalus is supposed to have succeeded in giving them life, hence the legends of chained statues. This is an example of tekhne, the creation of illusion and the imitation of life which is such a passionate desire of all men from Daedalus to Descartes to Vaucanson. We are also nearing the
Archaic period of Athenian art, with all the suggestions that these

===== PAGE 123 =====

106
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
early statues could present to the eyes and minds if the inhabitants of Attica and Crete. We can see these statues in our museums. It seems then, that there has been some kind of a metamorphosis. That which was attributed to the gods Athene and Hephaestus in the ancient myths is now seen as the work of man. It is not impossible that
Archytas' flying dove is also a symbol of the humanisation of the most advanced techniques.
Daedalus was a craftsman and a metallurgist, which means that he is not easily distinguished from the gods we have already mentioned who also worked metal. Daedalus, however, is important because he is supposed to have invented a large number of implements. These attributions vary from one author to another, and the differences depend largely on how much credit is awarded to Daedalus' nephew,
Talos, his sister's son, who was his apprentice. Daedalus was so jealous of his success that he killed him by hurling him off the top of the
Acropolis. This form of murder can be found time and time agan in Greek religious legends.
According to Pliny, Daedalus invented the saw, the hatchet, the plumbline, birdlime and fishglue. It is noteworthy that all these instruments are used for working wood. Birdlime and fishglue are both used in assembling. The plumbline is used by carpenters and architects. The hatchet, or adze, is used to trim wood and is extremely useful to carpenters, joiners and sculptors alike. There is no need to explain the saw. Also cited are the gimlet or drill, which Homer includes in the category of carpenters' tools. All these implements can be seen on a dish in the Berlin Museum which represents the workshop of a sculptor. An Etruscan golden bull, hammered and engraved anew during the 54th century BC, depicts Daedalus holding a saw in one hand and a broad axe in the other. This association is still apparent on a much later piece of engraved Roman glass, which depicts a ship being constructed apparently under the guidance of
Athene.
Other legends involve Talos in all these "inventions," as a preco ciously gifted young man who is thereby doomed to die. As Frontisi
Ducroux has noted, the emphasis, as far as Talos' inventions are concerned is on the notion of circularity. According to Diodorus, we have him to thank for the wheel, the potter's wheel, and the compass, all of which are used for making or marking out circles. It is possible that a fishbone or a snake's jaw gave Talos the idea for the metal saw

===== PAGE 124 =====

TIIE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
107 used for working with wood, and it was this saw which was to arouse Daedalus' anger.
Following the murder, Daedalus sought refuge in Crete, with Minos. Daedalus appears as a sculptor and woodworker in the legend of Pasiphae, but there is perhaps more, because tekhne, the creator of illusion and the imitator of life appears to be at work once more, just as it was with the living statues. In this sense, techniques were becoming a substitute for nature. On the other hand, Daedalus is not presented as being the creator of the labyrinth. It seems that he stayed in Egypt and was prompted by seeing King Mese's tomb which in fact was a converted fortified palace. It is not an architectural work but an enclosed space, with no ornamentation. However, it is as an architect that Daedalus went to Sicily after his escape from Crete.
Diodorus writes of the construction of a dam near Megara, and fortifications at the Camicos citadel. This then, is the real hero: the architect and the "engineer." He also installed a water-cure estab lishment at Selinonte, and the temple of Aphrodite at Erice on an almost inaccessible rock. "He built", notes Diodorus, "a wall on the actual precipice, enlarging the vertically overhanging platform in an extraordinary fashion." It has been demonstrated that these works raised all the problems of controlling rivers and draining marshes
(and it was the same in Greece) that were encountered by Sicilian engineers during the historical and archaic periods. And many of these works are built in suspension between the land and the heavens, as if they are somehow a link between the artistry of the gods, and the ingenuity of man.
The escape form Crete poses two problems. According to some, Daedalus escaped by sea, while Icarus was drowned and washed up onto the island which now bears his name. Daedalus is supposed to have been a carpenter and a ship-builder, and is reputed to have invented the sail: we can see again here the transfer of activities from the gods to man. According to the majority of writers, however,
Daedalus escaped from Minos, the King of Crete, with his son Icarus, by sticking wings to his body with wax and flying away. It is well known that Icarus flew too close to the sun, which caused the wax to melt and thence his downfall.
Daedalus best represents the technical developments which began during the 6th century BC. For those writing much later - Diodorus was around in the 1st century BC and Pausanias in the 2nd century -

===== PAGE 125 =====

108
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
the history of the origins of more developed techniques is completely unknown. It was necessary to conceive of Daedalus in order to understand it. This demonΏtrates, with all due respect to Plato, the serious consideration given to techniques and the "consciousness of the role of technical development in the cultural progress of humanity."
Other characters can be found in this fantastic world. They are of a lesser calibre, but they still stand out in a group within which it is so difficult to tell the legendary beings from the historical ones. Let us take two examples.
Palamedes was a prolific inventor. He, too was a representative of metis. Acording to some interpretations, he invented certain letters of the alphabet, dice games, draughts and knucklebones, etc. Palamedes' aims seems rather surprising, and they are the object of a certain amount of ridicule in Plato's Republic.
Rhoicos of Samos and his son Theodoros have also been mentioned - both of whom were living during the 6th century BC. They are reputed to have created the Hera temple at Samos, and the labyrinth at
Lemnos. They were also goldsmiths and sculptors and may have invented the wax casting method for making statues. Finally, we might have to thank them for the level and the compass. They are clearly still part of the same lineage.
Technical knowledge, and especially the notion of invention, con siderably aroused the imagination of the Greeks. There had to be an explanation for it, its origins had to be told. And even if some have managed to provide a worthwhile analysis to explain the appearance of fire, be it either lightning striking a tree, or rubbing two very dry sticks together, they still found it necessary to place the responsibility for technical activity on a select few, who came first from the heavens, then from an intermediary position halfway between the heavens and earth, and finally, from Earth itself.
Nature's Example This area is less well-defined. Ever since Antiquity, men have looked for the origins of tools and techniques in nature. There are examples of this from all periods.
We noticed above that Daedalus or Talos arΐ supposed to have invented the saw by imitating either a fishbone or a snake's jaw. This is

===== PAGE 126 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
109 an example of the direct influence of observing nature on the making of the tools. The number of animals' names given to machines by the
Greeks which have remained in use is surprising - the ram, the sawhorse, the crane, the grab [Transl. Note: louve in the french, meaning she-wolf] , the crayfish and the slug (which is the name given to the archimedian screw) - and of course the same goes for weaponry:
the battering ram, the onager and the scorpion, etc.
Of course, this is no doubt due to a certain resemblance, but of what sort? Did the animal inspire man? Or did the machine remind man of the animal simply on account of its general shape? Note that cochlea, like the Greek cochlias, is used in technology to designate the snail's shell, the tower with spiral staircase, the screw-press and the archime dian screw, all of which terms are derived from the word cochlos, which means snail. We shall return to this discussion later.
We hardly need dwell on Leonardo da Vinci's research into the flight of birds when working on his flying machine.
In a more contemporary vein, it is worth citing Charles Fremont, a notable historian of implements. Prompted by the sort of vocabulary we have just discussed, Fremont, like many others, sought the origins of the screw in natural objects, in particular helical shells, such as the snail. Reuleaux rejected this explanation, as did Fremont for the basic type of screw, however both of them found it acceptable to explain the screw and nut. Moreover, Fremont attributed different origins to the spiral staircase, the archimedian screw and the screw press. For
Fremont, the relative movement created by the snail when it is taken out of its shell to be eaten is an exact and absolute representation of the screw and its nut - not to tighten adjoining parts, but to create a thrust, as in the case of the beam of a screw press. In order to extract the whole snail, one had to perform a giratory movement. Readers may think of this explanation as they wish.
There is a third type of explanation, which concerns the pebble tools found some time ago in East Africa. In the words of Leroi
Gourhan:
A collection of eoliths taken from random samples has one striking characteristic: its is impossible to detect any kind of morphological constancy, and the distribution of forms seems to be entirely arbitrary. The only form of constancy which could be said to exist is of a purely mechanical order. This relates to the accidental thinness of a flint kidney, it affects the protruding parts indiscriminately, or result, on rather long flint kidneys, from fractures by bending.

===== PAGE 127 =====

110
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
And the conclusion which precedes this quotation:
Which means that if the percussion bulb indicates the likelihood of human intervention, it is also possible to detect a certain number of fragments which are the result of natural causes.
A flint which fell off a cliff and then shattered might well have been the first tool.
We should try to classify the above: the facts are diverse and they are the result of very different types of observation. They also presuppose, however, an imaginative effort which is relatively easy to define. When faced with a technical need, man has only to look to nature to find the solution, either by the simple discovery of a suitable object - a shattered flint - or by observing animal organs such as a fishbone or a snake's jaw. So it is no longer a question of a simple transposition from the natural object to the tool. And here we can distinguish between process.es which are possible, and those which are not. To reproduce the shattered flint presupposes the notion of percussion. And the passage from a fishbone to a saw is much more complex. It seems to us, therefore, that within the natural universe, which represented for early man an enormous field of experiment, three different types of observa tion are discernible: (a) the effects of nature, which are clearly the most perceptible, even by primitive mae; (b) the general shape of beings, which certainly require more sustained attention and which could only date from relatively recent periods as we shall see; (c) the activities of animals, which are associated in part with conformation, but which are in fact an extension of it, the conformation being associated with a specific "task."
We shall not stay long with the first category. We have already mentioned the case of pebble tools which could have been the product of natural causes. There are other examp'.:s, one of the most typical of which is the tree trunk floating down a water course which probably provided the inspiration for the first one-man craft. Another perfect example of this is the case of Newton and the apple. Newton
"understood" the fall of the apple because he was already thinking in those terms. The transition from a floating trunk to a craft is of the same nature, if not of the same degree. In other words, and this is the same in other cases, the problem necessarily preceded observation.
Certainly, monkeys use sticks to bring down fruit, or as levers. In which case, it is best look for the differences between them and man:

===== PAGE 128 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
1 1 1 the conservation of the tool for example. The transition from the wheel which turns according to a water current, to a mill is infinitely more difficult to explain. Initially, they must have looked for materials which were already prepared by nature, and which could be used immediately, on their own or with other objects.
Nature is unquestionably the mother of energy, and it has supplied the three essential forces on which man has survived for thousands of years, running water, wind and fire. But none of these forces leads directly to utilisation: we have indicated this already with regard to a piece of floating wood and sails, and lightning and rubbing together two pieces of wood. We saw above that these tranpositions were difficult to understand for the Ancients, who made up for this by appealing to the gods or heroes. A technical act is one which places man outside of natural phenomena, and which sometimes even goes against them.
The shape of objects demands long and pertinent observation, which, as we have already stated, must necessarily be preceded by the recognition of a problem to be solved: efficient observation is born of a need to be fulfilled. And this observation must be conducted according to certain rules which have gradually been put into place.
The first example is relatively unimportant. Around 1881 , a glove manufacturer invented the press stud, which was to replace the impractical button and buttonhole. By relying on mechanical princi ples, he attempted to find the most suitable forms for a solid fastening.
He gradually conceived of this object, outlined a design and made a prototype which he then tried out. Now the press stud exists naturally within certain animals: crabs, cephalopods, decapods and aquatic insects, etc. It is exactly the same shape and performs exactly the same function, holding together two seperate regions of the body. But it is certain that there is no link between the man-made and the natural object.
There is another, morΓ significant example. Towards the end of 1789, Volta built his first battery, creating electricity by forcing contact between two different metals. On the 20th March 1800, he wrote an explanatory letter to Sir Joseph Banks, vice-president of the Royal
Society of London. "As I shall demonstrate, this apparatus is fundamentally similar, in substance and in form, to the electrical organ of the torpedo, and of the eel, much more so than the Leyden jar and other known electric batteries, and I would therefore like to call it the

===== PAGE 129 =====

112
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
artificial electric organ." The ancient voltaic battery was composed of three vertical rods, supported by a wooden platform; there is a raised column between the three rods formed by superposing in the same order a disc of zinc, then one of copper, and one of felt soaked in slightly acidic water. This structure is similar to the organ found in the torpedo. The liquid used in the Volta element is similar to that found in the intralamelliara, the metallic conducters being represented by the intermediary tissues.
In the first case, there is no possible link between invention and observation of nature. In the case of the snail and the screw, the link is very doubtful. For Leonardo da Vinci, the relationship is real, but nothing concrete emerged from his ideas. In the last case, was it a matter of genuine influence or retrospective comparison? It is very likely that it was the latter. There is a certan resemblance but no similarity, and it would have been difficult to conceive of the metal based battery from the organ found in the torpedo. Besides, those types of observation which provide ideas or proof of similarity, are relatively recent and imply a specialised knowledge of the natural world.
Some people have spoken of the "tools of living beings" and have cited examples which appear somewhat disquieting. "In spite of their diversity, cleaning apparatuses in animals are always composed of bristles or spines arranged in either one or several rows and suggesting thus the comb or brush used by man; these are either used together or separately." It is the same with coupling or fixing structures: hooks, fishhooks and suction pads. Whatever the function of the latter, it must always keep the subject firmly fixed to its support. This common feature requires two processes: on the one hand the isolation of a certain surface on which the edges of the suction pad are hermetically sealed, and on the other, a break in the balance of pressure surround ing and within the suction pad. Even before rubber was available, which was to enable man to create the perfect suction pad, he was familiar with the moistened washer secured with a cord. I am forced to admit my ignorance here, because we do not know when the suction pad was first used, or the nature of its original function. Consequently, we have no means of comparison.
The same goes for all types of pliers. These grasping devices are built from two main models, scissor pliers or pincers composed of two parts which are joined or crossed, or the sharp-edged, folding pliers, formed

===== PAGE 130 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
113 by the withdrawal of one or more of the terminal parts (jaws) into a thicker part immediately behind it (handle). The former are most often found in crustaceans, and the latter in insects. Again, it is difficult to know whether nature was a genuine influence. For it is undeniable that man has created a much more varied range of pliers than can be found in nature. These pliers have a commmon characteristic: the jaws or rough surfaces found on the contacting segments which serve to hold the object.
A. Tetry has noted the resemblances between nature and diving equipment. Man uses the supra-aquatic atmosphere for making such equipment, which makes oxygen available in greater or lesser amounts. This means that they are restricted in terms of time ( appara tuses containing oxygen) or in space (feeding by a tube). Carbonic gas is eliminated either by the expulsion of the vitiated air or by chemical absorbtion. Human diving apparatuses correspond with the most specialised types which have been observed in nature. Tetry seems to indicate that one day it will be possible to create a device which will enable us to breath under water, a device which so far has been found only in nature. In any case, human exploits under water, real or legendary, have been taking place for centuries. Similarly, man made floats rest on the same principle, the structures are analogous - cork holds an important place - and they fulfil the same function as animal or vegetable floats.
I shall end on a more precise n()te. We are not yet at the stage of the luminous, cold organs of animals. Nature created the prototypes of certain stringed instruments (violin, guitar), and percussions instru ments (tambourine, cymbals, xylophone) and even noise instruments,
(grasshopper). But their aim is completely different to the artificial instruments used by man. Animals' instruments are used to call, to frighten, to attract the male or female, and perhaps to communicate.
Man's musical instruments are altogether different and are used to supply ordered sounds, arranged according to a scale. The difference between the musical organs of animals and man-made instruments is so fundamental that it is impossible that any links should exist between them. "The grasshopper which rubs the rasp of its thigh on a rib from its outer wing is comparable to the violinist who slips his bow accross the strings of his violin." The two techniques are perhaps similar in terms of their overall structure, that is, they both make a suitable material vibrate by rubbing it with some kind of rasp. But the violin,

===== PAGE 131 =====

1 14
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
even without including its organisation on the scale, is another matter:
the different types of string, the bow, the resonance chamber, the range of possible sounds, etc. , render it a completely different type of object.
Certainly, there are certain parallels to be drawn between animal and human implements. It could hardly be otherwise, since our needs are often the same, and there are not a thousand and one interchangeable means of action in any given circumstance. Yet resemblances, analogies or comparisons are not absolute. Certain animal tools are inefficient. Crustaceans' claws are an example of this:
the arrangement of the contracting muscle, its length, and finally, the size of the pincer's two jaws reveal that the basic principle of all human pincers, the reduction ratio of levers, is absent. An animal must expend considerably more energy to produce the same result. But on the other hand, humans are familiar with only one type of elementary suction pad, while the same device found in animals can be very complex.
A fascinating film has been made about birds' beaks. Of course, we know that there are a great variety of these, from pointed beaks to spoonbills, whose differences depend on the nature of the bird's food and habitat. But again, man would have needed a large selection of samples and observation techniques which have only recently evolved.
Let us consider a few points.
First of all, the transformation from the animal tool, which is an integral part of the animal's physiology, to the human tool, can only take place after correct observation: of the purpose of the animal tool, its mode of operation, and its structure. In other words, they would have needed equipment, like magnifying glasses and microscopes, to observe. They would also have had to understand the meaning of this observation and its link with an eventual need, and finally, a fairly advanced knowledge of physiology would have been necessary.
Research is being conducted today into certain types of animal beha viour, such as the dolphins' for example, in order to discover new techniques, but this would still have been out of the question not so very long ago.
Next, we should bring together both the animal tool and the need to be met by the human tool, and also the necessary change from a natural conformation to something which is very probably made of other materials, requiring further mechanical tranformations. The

===== PAGE 132 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
1 15 passage from a crab's claws to pincers completely transforms nature.
Does taking a snail out of its shell really suggest to us the idea of the screw and the nut? This is hardly likely. Only Archimedes perhaps, in his bath (according to the legend) might have related a natural phenomenon with a scientific demonstration, the technical effects of which are well-known.
Ultimately, one can hardly assert that nature has contributed signifi cantly to the development of techniques. Certainly, eating snails could bring to mind wine and the wine-press, but the relationship is a tenuous one. A crab's claws could not possibly have given rise to the fulcrum of pincers. It seems that the analogies were only discovered a posteriori.
The study of animal activities should certainly lead us to a similar conclusion. From the integrated tool, one can move on to the work it performs. Here again, there are numerous examples, most of which have been taken from Tetry's work.
The first example is rather a curious one, which concerns packing down the earth covering certain fossorial hymenopteras' burrow holes. This is a common enough practice. Sometimes this is done with a part of the body, but there are several European and American species of the Ammophila type which use a small pebble held in the mandibles and handled like a rammer to conceal the burrow's hole. This seems to be evidence of exterior tool being used. Weaver ants are another example. They build their nests by sewing leaves together (often from mango trees) using threads secreted by ant larvae. Thus, they are not only using a tool, but they are working together as a logically organised team. It appears that octopuses use their suction pads to deposit small stones between open valves of mollusc shells in order to prevent the shell closing. The animal can then be easily extracted and eaten.
The utilisation of one animal by another occurs fairly frequently, especially in hunting, as is the case with humans. Parasites are evi dence of this, but there are more complex examples: certain types of crab brandish a small sea-anenome between their claws, whose poiso nous sting paralyses their prey.
"Constructions" have altogether a different meaning. Generally, they are used for habitation. It is known that certain types of birds are thereby able to make perfectly constructed nests, because their young are born still in the embryonic stage, and they remain in the nest for a long time. Sparrows' nests are remarkable for their intricate network of interwoven twigs which surround a deep bowl lined with the most

===== PAGE 133 =====

116
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
delicate of materials. Some have even managed to make closed nests.
As above in the case of the ant, there is a warbler which makes its nest by sewing leaves together. We have included some drawings which show the different forms of knots and ties used by the weavers to make their nests (fig. 1).
We need only remind readers of the number of burrows which often contain many long galleries.
No animal has perhaps affected man more by its work than the beaver. There are beavers which live in burrows whose opening is above the water level, and there are others which build lodges out of branches and mud, which are completely surrounded and immersed in water. If a brook does not provide a sufficiently big stretch of water, they build their famous dams, thereby creating artificial lakes. Two beavers were once observed over two months, during which time they used dead wood and boughs from freshly-cut aspen and alder trees to build a dam thirty metres long and one metre high. The upper side was covered with a mixture of mud and stones. The leaf-fall in Autumn served to seal off the numerous holes. The animals also accumulated stocks of winter food. In their surroundings, beavers dig canals measuring up to hundreds of metres so that they can float the logs they have cut down to their lakes. Does this represent a series of associated instincts or foresighted intelligence? In fact, there is no intelligence involved. Beavers have been observed looking after man-made stone dams for an entire summer, as if they thought repairs were needed.
Furthermore, although they live in colonies, beavers work individ ually. When they cut trees down, some of which are very large, the beavers are forced to leave large parts behind, because the trees have fallen in such a way that they cannot be removed.
Also relevant here are the devices used by a number of animals.
Monkeys, and especially chimpanzees use tools in a way most similar to man. These animals have prehensile hands and are thus able to throw stones and tree-branches which they have broken off, either in order to defend themselves or to get fruit down from trees. In captivity, they use a stick to obtain an object which is out of reach.
Does this not represent a kind of training?
At first sight, the imitation of nature appears more logical than the idea of gifts from gods. In fact, however, it seems that at no time in history has nature inspired man in this way. When methods of observa tion were gradually developed, some curious minds were there to find

===== PAGE 134 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
117 Figure 1 . - Different types of knots and bows used by the weavers to build their nests.
(From J. Dorst, La Vie des oiseaux, Lausanne: Rencontre, 1971)

===== PAGE 135 =====

118
1ECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
analogies and resemblances. But when the latter are studied more closely, it appears that they are the result of amusement rather than of explanation. The only technique which is truly derived from nature is man's use of certain animals' instincts: the racing dog, the falcon and the homing pigeon are perfect examples among many others.
THE INGENIOUSNESS OF MAN
This is the third and vital part of the trilogy. The gods have no part to play in this, and nature has only a small role. Only man remains, faced with his task, confronted with his labour. And now we are left to scrutinise the remains, which are almost as difficult to interpret as nature, the texts and the legends. The arguments exist to prove this.
And immediately the first question arises: how are we to define the first human among all those beings, each of which has the same characteristics? They are all upright and they are all prehensile thanks to the placing of the thumb opposite the other fingers. Measuring their brain capacity is not a sufficient test and in effect it is less that than the organisation of the brain which counts, and we know nothing about the brains of the so-called first humans. One is tempted to adopt what is perhaps the most rational solution, namely, that the tool, no matter how primitive, seems to be the earliest evidence of humanity. The tool is no longer a hereditary phenomenon, like the chimpanzee's stick which is abandoned after use, but a heritage, which is preserved. Both the materials, and the knowledge of the way in which it is made, are handed down to the next generation. It represents in fact the birth of a culture. The tool is not simply some suitable matter found here and there and used in its natural form, but matter which is prepared for a specific use and according to a rational form.
Formerly, it was believed that the first humans evolved around 500,000 years ago. Today, however, thanks to fairly recent discover ies, specialists are talking in terms of millions of years, so that the notions of homo sapien and homo faber are now no longer so clearly defined.

===== PAGE 136 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
119 Lucy, or the First Outlines Certainly, "human" remains do exist. The borders of Kenya and
Abyssinia are valuable areas for research. From Olduvai in Tanzania to the river Omo in Ethiopia which supplies Lake Rudolph, an impressive number of sites have been providing us for the fast twenty five years or so with evidence which has completely changed the human genealogical table. This area seems to have been the cradle of the human race: the bones of hominidae have been found, and some of the world's oldest fossils providing evidence of abundant wildlife, and sometimes even of primitive forms of industry. These remains come from the entire Pleistocene period, which ranges according to inter pretation from 2.6 to 1.5 millions of years. It was in the Omo valley that the earliest, almost complete skeleton was found, a young girl baptised by pre historians as Lucy.
East Africa thus appears to have been the cradle from which the human species gradually emerged as a race distinct from that of the common "trunk" shared by primates and hominids. A tooth, discovered at N'Gorora, near Lake Baringo and thought to be 11.5 million years old, heralds quite clearly the hominid line. The discover ies made at Zothagam, which are about 5.5 to 5 million years old, help to mark out this evolution. Then, at the end of 1972, discoveries made near Lake Rudolph revealed that hominids and Australopithecines were living alongside one another 2.6 million years ago. The hominid's brain had an interior capacity of 880 cubic centimetres compared to that of 500 for a robust Australopithecine, 620 for a gracile Australopi thecine and 750 for a Pithecanthrope a million years ago.
The numerous sites excavated in this region have provided us with some important information. It is now thought that there was a
"clumsy" Australopithecine, which was herbivorous. It was unable to walk on its hind legs and no doubt moved around with the help of its finger joints (in the same way as gorillas and orangutans). The other type of hominid, much more gracile, was omniverous and bipedal, and thus more highly evolved.
The oldest implements known today were found on several sites in the Omo valley and on the eastern banks of Lake Rudolph. From about 2.5 million years ago, hominids were using shattered stones, sharpened bones and naturally pointed teeth (mainly hippopotamus' and pigs' canines) . By the middle of 1972, the remains of26 individuals and more than 300 tools had been excavated.

===== PAGE 137 =====

120
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Until recently, prehistorians were agreed that the first implements were pebble tools or choppers as they are sometimes known. One or two fragments taken from a stone smaller than a fist was sufficient to give the latter a cutting edge which was fairly rough but sufficient nonetheless to be suitable for cutting or scraping. The double-faced flint was derived from this extremely primitive tool, and was characteristic of numerous paleolithic industries, with its precise cutting edge giving a triangular shape. Such was the classical theory, almost universally accepted until
1972 when all this was suddenly called into question (fig. 2).
Figure 2. - Australanthropian stereotype. (From A. Leroi-Grouhan, Le Gaste et la parole, Paris: Albin Michel, 1964)

===== PAGE 138 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
121 Many of these primitive tools were not actually found in situ, but rolled about and carried along by streams and rivers they had been broken up by the process of erosion. It is difficult to date such fragments, but a quartz pebble tool, discovered in 1969 by the French expedition to the Omo valley, has been dated by the gangue of concretions which covered it as being some 2.2 to 2.1 million years old:
until new finds are made, this would appear to be in all likelihood the oldest known tool in existence. In the absence of tools made of any other materials, some authorities have even suggested that this must have been the only tool in use, that is to say, the universal tool. Flakes have also been found in place, in layers of gravel over 2 million years old, with some rare pebble tools and teeth belonging to Australopi thecine man. Nicks and furrows on the stones suggest that the larger flakes were used as cutting edges. One must therefore re-evaluate everything that has been put forward. Such pebble tools could in fact be the remains of the raw material used to obtain the flakes, rather than tools themselves. Thus the history of the most primitive tool has been completely turned about, but is this not a simple hypothesis of work?
In 1959, Mary Leakey discovered pebble tools linked with the very first hominids in the Olduvai Gorge, thus dating them between 1 .8 million and 800,000 years old in this region. Before this discovel,"y only a few pebble tools in Morocco and South Africa, thought to be about a million years old, had been found. The pebble tools "made" in the
Omo Valley date back as far as 2.2 to 2.1 million years, and recently discovered flakes go back nearly 3 million years in time.
A provisionary genealogy was thus possible, and we have sche matized this below.
clumsy Homo habilis Pithecanthropus Australopithecine gracile Australopithecine extinct Homo sapiens extinct
To quote Andre Leroi-Gourhan, writing before the latest discover ies in the Omo valley:
The African pebble industry really corresponds to what one may imagine as the first distinct shape of the rough stone. Identification of these first products of human industry is not easy, and has been the preoccupation of prehistorians from the beginning of the

===== PAGE 139 =====

122
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
60's of the last century onwards. Although it is easy to recognize tools from the point at which secondary adjustments gave them a definite shape, it is more difficult to do so for cut stones which may only be untreated fragments.
Let us continue to look at this quotation, as it presents precisely the problem of conscious human intervention. "The blow required to determine the shape of the flake tool must be applied with a direction and force which presupposes conscious intervention, but of the thousands of blows produced on pebbles by undertow or water falls, chance determines a certain number of flakes which appear to have been retouched by humans." Apart from the possibility of natural causes, the tool is thus a real product of the body and the brain of the
Anthropiens. "It is logical in this case to give to an artificial instrument the norms of natural instruments: it must correspond with these constant forms, and to a genuine stereotype." And for the prehisto rians, the pebble tool corresponds to a stereotype as attested by millions of objects.
Obviously two pebbles were necessary, as one was used for striking the other.
(the blow is) delivered to one of the sides, perpendicular to the surface, and removes a flake from the pebble leaving a sharp edge; two or three such blows would make a longer and more sinuous edge. Delivered to one side, the operation gave birth to the
"chopper," and applied to two sides it became a "chopping tool." One must state that the operation implied only one type of action, that being the most simple: hitting the side of the pebble at an angle of 90 degrees. No further identification other than that of one action producing one cutting edge is possible.
To make his tool the Australopithecine employed the simplest action used when he wished to break up bones (and there are many examples of broken bones), crack nuts or kill animals. This technical nature corresponds to the little that we know about his brain. It is human and "appears coherent with the organism of the being which it completed. It implies a genuine state of technical awareness."
It is true that archaeology is still only at the start of its investigation.
"It would be difficult to go further back than the Australopithecine in the study of the origins of the tool. Moreover I regret this deficiency," admits Leroi-Gourhan, "as the Australopithecine is certainly not the initiator of manual operations. Obviously we have not yet found Adam and Eve, and we cannot say if we are even close. It is only recently by going further still, about 600 kilometres north of Addis Ababa, that

===== PAGE 140 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
123 other discoveries have upset hypotheses already made. And here again we see that the Australopithecines and man lived together.
Was this first mankind limited to the zones of East Africa which have been defined above? It appears that some remains, previously ignored in other countries, now attract attention. At Chilhac, not far from Le
Puy, in France, three or four pebble tools have been found in torrential deposits combined with lakeside sediment and volcanic material.
Research undertaken to date them has suggested that they are 1.8 million years old. Around 1 million years ago, hominids lived on the
Cote d'Azur as they did on the banks of the Somme river, in the north of France, and pebble tools have also been found in Spain, Italy,
Germany, and Yugoslavia.
Subsequently, this wealth of human remains and the "tools" which accompany them have enabled the prehistorians to date the first man as far back as 3 million years. Life was difficult for our ancestors living in the Vallonnet cave, near Roquebrune-Cap-d'Ail, on the French
Mediterranean coast. The cold transformed the poplar forests into grasslands, or even into steppes. The cave was not so much a dwelling as a lair. Food consisted of land animals, such as rhinoceros, deer or cattle, most of them old, and even whales and seals, whose vertebrae and jaws have been discovered. They were mostly old animals which were easier to hunt, and half-rotting beached whales which were easier to cut up with the pebble tools. Naturally, all this did not constitute any great advance.
Stages of Progress Progress was most certainly slow in the beginning. An interesting exerise is to scale down the progress of techniques to the duration of a single year. By this time-scale the first tool would have appeared on the
1st January, fire arrived between the 1st and the 16th October, the first funeral rites on 22nd December, the birth of art on the 28th December, agriculture and the Neolithic revolution on the 30th December at 5 pm, the steam driven engine on the 31st December at 11 .20 pm, and nuclear energy at 11.54 and 35 seconds. Thus we can see the enormous temporal distance which separates the discovery of the first technique from the Neolithic revolution of the next chapter.
All too frequently, at least until a few years ago, the work of prehistorians lacked any systematization. Everything was diluted in

===== PAGE 141 =====

124
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
terms which were difficult to relate to each other. And this problem was compounded by discrepancies in studies of various regions. The most precise effort of clarification to date is to be found in the various works of Leroi-Gourhan. So the reader will not be surprised if we follow him step by step.
Classifications In order to trace the stages of progress in the domain of the tools cut from stone, one must first establish as strict a classifi cation as possible, which is exactly what the pioneers of natural sciences have tried to do. In the subject which concerns us here, it is not sufficient just to classify, or, in other words, identify clearly defined characteristics, but also to deduce the actions involved, both in the actual manufacture of the stone tool, and if possible its use. Once this list has been made, the researcher has a solid foundation on which to base his definitions of the technical civilization which he is studying.
The problem should be considered in terms of progress and systems.
It will be difficult, as far as this period is concerned, to distinguish saturations and imbalances, given that only a limited number of tools are available, mainly of stone - wood and bone tools having disap peared - and partly because there are few remaining examples of the objects made, which are necessary counterparts to the tools. Having said all this, it is nevertheless essential to provide descriptions and classifications, and even more important to possess an overall view of the morphology of these tools. Once we have acquired the necessary vocabulary, and we cannot overemphasize the importance of this problem, we may then determine the typology of a period, place it upstream or downstream in the global technical concatenation and also to relate it to the technical civilizations which exist side by side.
Most interesting comparisons may be made between technical civili zations which are very distant from each other: the overall time-lags, concomitants and the purely general laws of technical progress of each category should be noted, as well as the the relevance of the grouping within types.
And immediately, essential differences may be observed. In a word, the earliest form of tool was an instrument for cutting, albeit a very basic one. This may have been a pebble already formed by nature which needed only to be adapted to perform a certain type of work, or it could have been a rough, unworked block prior to shaping used by man for the appropriate cutting job, known as the core, and which had

===== PAGE 142 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
125 to be given the shape and the proportions suitable for certain types of cutting. In the first instance, the raw material, or the original pebble, was prepared for use, and in the second, the raw material itself was first worked in order to provide, technically speaking, usable flakes.
It is obvious that the nature of the materials used was an important consideration in the methods employed for the manufacture of tools.
But whatever the case, the method of preparation determined the exact shape of the quarried product. In other words, man knew the type of tool he required and from the start began to take the steps necessary to obtain it.
The shaping of the core-produced flakes may be explained in various ways. One may regard them as mistakes or scraps, and sites reveal many hundreds of flakes discarded in this way. Similarly, these flakes could be used to make new tools. This question has already arisen elsewhere with reference to the first human tools. These middens, or large amounts of discards, indicate either abandonment or the begin ning of a new industry, depending upon particular cultures and local resources. This leads to the problem of assessing how it was that these two ideas could eventually be combined.
One quickly arrives at the question of modules for cutting, whose form was strictly dictated by a given material either because of its resistance or because of its size (fig. 3). It is quite likely that men, at different stages of their civilizations had always been aware of reasons for saving raw materials. And here we find one of man's character istics: we might even say, up to a point, that in primitive civilizations, economy, such as the use of flakes produced by cutting, marks progress.
Apart from cutting, the fashioning or flaking of the stone tool should also be examined. Flaking was performed by the removal of very small parts from the core, a process known as retouching, work which demanded delicacy and precision. There are many types of retouching, unifacial or bifacial, or sucessive retouching by a sort of slow approach to the definitive form, which suggests the extent of the retouches made
(fig. 4).
Thus the stages in the production of stone tools may be defined. It will always be difficult, if not quite impossible, to explain the devel opment of the methods of producing these tools. And here the question arises as to how a tool is born. Of course a tool never comes into being by itself but was designed to fulfil an inevitably pre-existing

===== PAGE 143 =====

126
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
need. Thus one must start with an existing tool and transform it in order to obtain a suitable one; it might even in a sense be necessary to invent one. It is essential to study, for example, the progress from the stone tool to the metal tool, which presupposes totally different techniques. Some authorities have noted that a copy of a stone tool made in metal and vice versa works equally well. We will return to this later.
The tables reproduced here, which are the work of Leroi-Gourhan, aptly illustrate the evolution of techniques (fig. 5). The first concerns the subject discussed above. The length of the cutting-edge obtained in relationship to a given weight of material used provides important data. Clearly the use of waste flakes, once they were put to use, considerably increased the proportions of the length of the cutting edge, right up to the fashioning of microliths. The curve expresses an apparently characteristic movement. After a relatively slow take-off period, indicated by the flatness of the line at the level of the biface (a phenomenon of saturation), the use of the waste flakes permits a redress, in the form of a parabola until the appearance of the micro liths. At this point there should be a new flattening of the curve: the knapped stone industry is in its turn saturated and apart from a few rare improvements, it cannot be developed any further.
The second diagram is equally revealing (fig.6) . The number of types increases more rapidly than the length of the cutting edge obtained from a given mass of raw material. The two curves increase less rapidly at the beginning than the curve illustrating the growth of cranial capacity. The latter slows down at the same time as the two other curves rise. At this period, cranial capacity reaches its peak, although this is not the only point in question, as the texture of the brain is certainly far more important.
Apart from techniques involved in manufacture, the other important element is constituted by tool types, the two being indis solubly linked. Here, morphology and typology merge. One may note that in general the number of tool types does not cease to rise, and that tool systems are developed, revealing by that very fact the same chronological stages. On the other hand we witness some disappea rances which may in fact be no more than transformations: the
"chopper" thus becomes the biface. But tools rapidly become inte grated, the transformed tool, intended for some other use, does not eliminate the earlier tool which had its own use. We should finally

point out that the genealogy of tools is still very vague. The "chopper" is similar to the biface, while axes and knives are closely related to axes, adzes and hoes. Having said this, prehistorians seem to agree on distinguishing the following three main types:

\ < < 0 0 :l 1) tools with distal cutting edges, from the "chopper," thus the most primitive tool, with unifacial then bifacial retouching ("chopping tool"), to the axe, the bevel of which forms the cutting edge and exists prior to flaking, and finally the knife which also possessed a pre existing bevel which is either retouched or fashioned;

===== PAGE 148 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
131 2) axes, adzes, and hoes: objects of polished stone, of animal or vegetable material, comprising important morphological distinctions
(fig. 7) ;
3) chisels, tools with straight cutting edges obtained by the removal of a blade known as "chisel cut." Here again there is a considerable number of morphological varieties (fig. 8);
4) bifaces, scrapers and points "together with the "chopper" consti tute one single family, characterized by a generally oval form, the sides of which are most often dissymmetrical in outline, or by the nature of the retouching." The variability of these tools is significant (fig. 9) ;
5) objects with worn and jagged edges, geometric tools: "a large morphological family is formed by blades or bladelets, or more rarely flake tools, on which sheer or vertical, direct or inverse retouching ensures the creation of varied dissymmetrical forms following their outline or the nature of the alteration." Geometric implements are most often represented by microliths;
6) foliated pieces, either bi- or unifacial, made from stone or bone material, are almost limitless in the variety of forms;
7) scrapers in particular have been the object of studies: their purpose is to grate bone or wood material. "Technical determinism thus plays a large part in the form and function of the cutting-edge."
Scrapers are classified into three main large categories, according to their use: those for the removal of thin material, those for the removal of whole strips, and "ridged" scrapers (fig. 10);
8) perforating implements and borers: there are innumerable varie ties of these. The borers either had a straight or slanting axis, or were angled. The notches were either on an oblique or double axial end
(fig. 11);
9) points made of boΌ material display pronounced similarities in every civilization. Although they were often used to serve as spear or arrow-heads, they could also have been used for other purposes which are difficult to determine.
Thus we possess a long list which indicates relations, families and varieties. Only by studying such instruments designed for work may we define the civilizations involved, and then draw up chronologies. It would, however, be going too far to base any system of techniques upon descriptions of groups of tools alone. Two considerations must

Figure 1 1 . - Points. (From A. Leroi-Gourhan, La Prehistoire, Paris: P.U.F., 1 968) not be overlooked. The first concerns tools, which are naturally inseparable from the objects, or, in the case of weapons, from the effects which they were designed to produce. Information is not sufficient in either case, and a whole gap exists between the tool and the object which is extremely difficult to fill. Although, to a certain degree, an abundance of tools suggests an abundance of objects, this hypothesis is not immediately valid, for civilizations which are very rich in objects and relatively poor in tools are on record, and the

===== PAGE 152 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
135 reverse is equally true. This question has certainly not been neglected, but a general absence of objects produced does not permit broad conclusions to be made. Insofar as a system of tools and a system of objects are indeed closely interdependent, we lack here an essential explanation.
The second consideration is no less important. Tools, or systems of tools, only represent one aspect of techniques but there are many others of which we are almost totally ignorant, such as fire, which should easily be detected in prehistoric deposits. According to our present knowledge, apparently the oldest trace of fire in the world is that found in the Escale cave, at Saint-Esteve-Janson, near Aix-en
Provence, France: this has been dated back some 700,000 or 600,000 years. More recent are the fires of Terra-Amata (near Nice, France),
Versteszollos (Hungary) and those of Chou Kou-Tien (China). And we could cite many other techniques here: clothing, gathering, habita tion, hunting or fishing, and trapping . . . We often imagine them by a far-from-convincing analogy with the technical practices of so-called primitive peoples. All that remains today is the stone or bone tool and, once again, we are not really sure how or for what purpose it was used.
Stages of development We will continue to plunder shamelessly Leroi-Gourhan's exemplary works. Indeed his books provide a global view of the evolution of techniques, which pleases and reassures the mind in a field in which the accumulation of details often leads to confusion. He distinguishes several chronological stages which may, moreover, be placed in the context of temporal space at levels often removed from each other.
Archanthropian From the very beginning of the 20th century, the hominids who are known today as Archanthropines, were hailed as being the first of the human race. Now it seems that they are merely one link in an extensive chain of history which goes back long before their appearance. "In their time, the Archanthropines already had very distant human predecessors."
The Pithecanthropines and Sinanthropines are virtually the same.
The Sinanthropines knew how to make fire, the Atlanthropines had already developed tools, and the Pithecanthropines had a femur similar to ours. Their face is enormous and their cerebral capacity is about two thirds the size of modern man. Their forehead is like that of the Australanthropines, with a very sizeable orbital circle. They

===== PAGE 153 =====

136
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
evolved during the ancient Quaternary period. There was, however, some progress, as the cerebral capacity of the Archanthropines is almost double that of their predecessors.
All of these stages in man's development come in what is usually known as the Lower Paleolithic period, the best known of which is the
Acheulean culture. Let us locate them geographically: Pithecanthro pines were discovered in Java, Sinanthropines in China, Atlanthro pines in North Africa, and Australanthropines in East Africa.
Only the Sinanthropines have been found in their habitat, and this area· has revealed millions of tools worked in a material which was hardly suitable for the functions they were designed to perform.
The Atlanthropines display an industry which may be attributed to a very primitive Acheulean tradition. From their bifaces and axes one can estimate that the others had formed a similar technical civili zation.
Using the tools found in the area inhabited by the Atlanthropines, it is possible to reconstruct the features of the industrial stereotype of these populations which are distributed over a large part of the earth
(fig. 12).
Primitive methods remained in use, in particular the method of flaking by perpendicular percussion. Bifaces tended to replace
"choppers," but a second series of actions seems to have been intro duced "by which the core of the stone is struck tangentially, rather than perpendicularly to its axis, producing flakes which are much longer, sharper, and already very similar to the flakes used by the Paleanthro pines." Although the type of equipment remained very limited, flakes were used directly and "core tools" continued to be fashioned, so we must therefore emphasize the number of additional new actions. But as Leroi-Gourhan notes, "this acquisition corresponds to something more than just a simple addition, as it already implies on the individual level, a higher rate of planning in the progress of technical operations."
This differs fundamentally from the process used by the Australanth ropine. The latter conceived his tool well and chose the pebble accordingly: the possibilities were enormous and the imagination of the creator of the tool very limited. In the case of the Archanthropine, the processes were virtually the opposite. He possessed the raw material and had to choose, if for example he wished to make an axe, the point at which a large fragment would break off, the cutting edge of which would constitute the working edge of a future tool, as well as

===== PAGE 154 =====

() #-fJ n.
