===== PAGE 155 =====

138
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
deciding upon any secondary improvements to be made. A similar but rather less deliberate method was used for making the biface. "Thus the technical intelligence of the Archanthropine already reveals itself to be highly complex and his industry shows him able to combine two series of actions in obtaining a stereotyped form from one consciously isolated block."
This important effort had no immediate consequence. Industries only developed very gradually over a period of 300,000 to 400,000 years. The tool hardly changed from the Abbevillian to the final
Acheulean tradition, the one difference being that there was an increase in rare additional forms and improvements in the precision of the work. One cannot even suggest that there was a blockage of technical thought as that certainly did not exist over this long period of time. A comparison of the available tools, either by dating or locating their origin, proves that if there was any progress at the outset, it was only a flash in the pan.
Attempts have been made to trace this slow evolution of tools. From the outset therefore - and this is the most important new idea - tool parts were trimmed with a hammer and finished with a wooden tool.
Some bifaces, however, were entirely fashioned with a stone hammer struck obliquely at the piece. Using the finds from the Somme valley, the Abbe Breuil distinguished seven cultural stages in one glacial and two interglacial epochs. The whole civilization of the biface may be traced from this viewpoint. Thus it was that chisels made their appea rance in the Middle Acheulean period.
It is quite evident that civilizations overlap, early in some regions and later in others, and this is also true of the techniques used for making flake tools. The Clactonian flaking industry perfected its methods from the Abbevillian to the Acheulean tradition. Abbe
Breuil considered that the sudden progress in the Levallois technique could have occurred at the end of the Acheulean period. This history of technical progress offers many such discrepancies sometimes occurring in very important fields.
Obviously even summary explanations for these changes and devel opments are out of the question. Indeed, we can only ask questions and our knowledge may need to be redirected in order to answer them.
How and why did one pass from the primitive stage (the Australanth ropines are still known as Chelleans or Abbevillians) to the more developed techniques of the Acheulean tradition? Can one talk of a

===== PAGE 156 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
139 revolution, or is it merely a question of a very gradual evolution by slow stages? Works on prehistory are rather misleading in this respect, as they swamp the reader with avalanches of discoveries and evidence, mainly tools, without (apart from exceptional cases) supplying us with diagrams in a chronological framework, albeit hypothetical, which could provide us with the exact direction of the evolution, even though we may never know the reasons to explain it. Consequently, seeing where technical "progress" came and trying to comprehend this phenomenon becomes an almost impossible task.
Paleanthropian This stage represents a period of transition between the Lower Paleolithic and the Middle Paleolithic. It is therefore a relatively vague and imprecise notion, "which is normal if one con ceives evolution as a progressive phenomenon." Beings from this period have been discovered virtually all over the world, together with their tools and often in their habitat. Their age corresponds to the second part of the penultimate interglacial period and to the first part of the last glacial period, one which is relatively short in comparison to the preceding one, and would provide evidence of the acceleration of the progress of techniques (fig. 13).
The final culmination in human evolution would be Neanderthal man. Despite the eye socket which remains the same, the cerebral capacity of this man approaches that of our own. "One must admit that apart from a detail, the exiguity of the prefrontal areas, but none theless an important one, the brain of Neanderthal man corresponds approximately to ours, in that he possesses the same cell endowment, for the middle cortex area in particular."
We will now witness what might be called the Levallois "revolu tion. " In the words of Leroi-Gourhan:
During the Middle Paleolithic age, a very important evolution came about in the Iithic tool. The Archanthropines of the preceding period for a large part still followed primitive tradition, and their tools, bifaces and hand-axes, were still hewed from a block in the same way that the "chopper" had been by the Australopithecines. From this block came by-products in the form of flakes, the cutting edge of which could or could not be used. During the Acheulean period, the thinning of the edges of the biface by tangential percussion detached many wide and thin fragments from the matrix, which were consequently used as cutting tools. From this development of cutting the biface arose the technique described by prehistorians as the Levallois technique. The block which was originally destined to become one amygdaloid shaped tool thus became the material for predetermined shaped fragments which were to serve as tools themselves. In order to arrive at this result, the core was first worked like a rough biface, then prepared for the extraction of a flake, and sharpened for successive extractions until it was used up. The

===== PAGE 157 =====

140
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
preparation might reach the point at which the pin came out of the nucleus with a single blow, either in a triangular point, a subcircular-shaped fragment, or a long, narrow blade.
And in conclusion: "the Levallois technique represents the most advanced method for the making of flint tools created by man." Our scientists are aided by the considerable number of tools at their disposal, and by the discovery of real workshops where evidence of every stage of tool making is available.
It is difficult, if not impossible, to define the period and the place where this new civilization was born. One notes, moreover, that it presents two aspects. The Moustiers (in the Dordogne, France) was still a cave civilization while Levallois was an open-air one. The profound similarities are so numerous that many have suggested that there was a winter and summer civilization. The Levallois encircles the
Mediterranean in a ring more than a thousand kilometres wide: in Africa, it extends as far down as Kenya. One assumes that these techniques were established over a period of some 5000 years.
Although this might seem rapid in comparison with others, the spread of more developed techniques was still very slow.
The diversification of the tool is now more pronounced than before, but even so one can still detect a certain sameness. The original block, which was previously made into the tool, is henceforth the source from which tools are made. Production was mainly in cutting tools, and apart from the old tools, knives, scrapers, points and notches could be obtained. This is clear evidence of an already advanced technical intelligence. A bone and wood industry certainly also existed, but unfortunately very few relics remain, the rest having disappeared.
"For the bone industry," notes Leroi-Gourhan, "it seems that the situation had hardly changed since the Australopithecines." The absence of any tools made out of bone and the remarkable prolifer ation of flint flake tools on which marks indicate that they were used either to work bone or wood, suggest that working in wood was already a very important occupation.
So far we have been concerned with the tool and ignored everything else, in particular indications of habitat. Without doubt the idea of the winter cave dwelling must be abandoned. But man had always inha bited caves during this period: can we not learn something from their existence? We have not yet reached the period when man began to settle: it is therefore more accurate to speak of provisional camps, all

===== PAGE 158 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
141 the more destructible precisely because of their temporary nature.
There are only a few rare examples, two or three in the West and in the U.S.S.R. Let us look in particular at the deer hunters' camp found at
Pincevent (near Montereau in Seine-et-Marne, France). "An open air settlement was found, with more or less circular shaped areas indicat ing the remains of ancient huts. Domestic organisation did not appear to be very structured. Man lived in a circle, a few metres in diametre, outside of which he threw the debris of comsumption." Here again, there seems to have been little progress of any note from the
Archanthropine period.
We are reduced to making hypothetical observations about other techniques. Traces observed on animal bones seem to indicate that they were skinned, so that their fur or skin could provide clothing, or could be used for some sort of bedding. The climates in which the
Neanderthal man lived were fairly varied so that it is absolutely impossible to generalize on this subject.
Once more we return to the same question: how and why did this new civilization arise? Imagination alone is not sufficient to explain this. Research has been undertaken in one direction, the only one which might have provided the material for a valid explanation. As has been pointed out above, we are now approaching the last stage in the development of the brain, at least in its capacity, if not in its structure.
Cerebral development would therefore have been the factor which led man to seek the greater improvement of techniques. We have still to ascertain why the human brain, whose capacity had barely evolved from that of the Chapelle-au-Saint man, could henceforth achieve such incredible progress in the field of techniques. But by limiting ourselves to our remote period, the logical step would be to search for other traces of intelligence in Neanderthal man which did not exist in his predecessors. Are there any burial places, for example? And has any evidence of graphic signs been preserved?
Replies to these questions are hesitant. Excavations, particularly those made in earlier times, have overturned sites before accurate observations could be made, and the few remaining undisturbed examples are too slight, too fragile to allow any useful conclusions to be made for our purpose. The area in which skeletons have been discovered is not convincing. A few rare cases might suggest that cannibalism was practised: above all there are abandoned bodies, whose limbs were probably dispersed by animals in search of food. The

===== PAGE 159 =====

142
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
discovery of the Neanderthalian skull at Mont Circe in 1939 is very feeble evidence. It was found lying on the floor of the cave, surrounded by some stones and a pile of animal bones, which seemed to have been placed there intentionally. The lack of tools proves that it was not a habitat which had been lived in for long. Was it really a burial place?
The notion of a cult of skulls, bones, bears and foxes appears to be the result of misinterpretation. Consequently there is very little more one can safely say on this subject.
At several sites, indications of ochre have been found in Mousterian strata. To deduce from this that these people practised a form of art, would be pure speculation. Various discussions and interpretations have surrounded the discovery made in 1968, in the Pech-de-Laze cave, near Sarlat, in the Dordogne, of an animal's bone, on which engravings made by a flint were observed, but it is very difficult to date this bovine rib. These engravings would seem to have been deliberate, as the traces are parallel and the V-shaped drawing are so clear that they cannot just be marks from scraping, resulting for example from cutting off meat. The author of this find, Franc;ois Bordes, "did not dare suggest the significance of these engravings." He simply recog nized that this engraved bone is by far the oldest known to date, the oldest until then having been discovered in Aurignacien or Per igourdin deposits of the late Paleolithic period. An American resear cher, Alexander Marshack, on the other hand, has attempted to distinguish the earliest engraved marks and the tools used to incise them. He has even gone as far as to suggest that he had recognized a lunar calendar. So, on the one hand, Mr. Bordes estimates that it is the work of "an idle hunter amusing himself", and on the other, Marshack detects "the mark of a prescriptive stage made with the aim of using and reusing this image in a ritual or ceremonial way." He goes on to conclude that the Acheulean hunters or their immediate predecessors already commanded a complex language. The historian subsequently finds himself in the awkward position of having to choose between these theories.
Neanthropian We enter a very different and considerably more advanced world during the Upper Paleolithic period. "The devel opment of habitation structures, the importance of bone material used to create weapons, tools, or jewellery, the use of colouring materials, and the appearance of the first works of descriptive art" bear witness to the profound evolution of mankind. From now on we are in Homo

===== PAGE 160 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
143 sapiens' age, one of the most famous representatives of which is Cro-Magnon man. This civilization covered the period from 30,000 BC to 8000 BC.
There are some quite considerable changes. Man generally no longer lived in caves, but instead built huts and tents. He made clothes out of skins, skillfully sewn together, and jewellery was introduced. As a furrier, he was a hunter and an assegai user at that, and as a basket maker, he would undoubtedly have used wood and bark.
The "chopper" had been replaced by the biface, a tool kept in use for thousands of years without undergoing any radical development.
During the Levalloisian era, the biface itself became the nucleus for the creation of flakes which in tum were worked. "The Mousterians brought about the most important technical revolution, perhaps the greatest in the whole history of mankind, by providing the solution to the flaked core tool with a pre-determined shape. " Subsequently, and in the period which concerns us here, "the evolution continued through minor improvements in quarrying devices." They succeeded in making narrower and thinner blades. But the flakes were also used according to their shape: henceforth the tool certainly became more and more specified and, at the same time, less was wasted. It has been possible to calculate that towards 12,000 BC, during the Magdalenian period, man could make several hundred tools out of two or three kilos of broken flint. This trend towards microlithism led to an extremely widespread use of tools, even in areas where no raw material was available.
Figure 14 represents a table, borrowed once again from Leroi Gourhan, which clearly demonstrates the development of tools, and thus the diversification of techniques (fig. 14).
The first three stages proceed following a single order, by the accumulation of new forms derived from old ones, but without totally abandoning the latter ( . . . ) The third stage is already continuing in a new direction ( . . . ) There is a complete contrast by the fourth stage. Due to a transition which rapidly developed over the years between 35,00 and
30,00 BC, in Western Europe, one is faced with not only three times as many tool types, but also tools and objects which are reflected in present primitive cultures.
And in conclusion: "thus yet another technical world is in the making, ours." The making of bone objects, both for tools and jewellery, seems like a real achievement, so much so that in some places attempts have been made to imitate the bone points in stone.
