===== PAGE 162 =====

THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
145 It may therefore be possible to distinguish types of civilizations, even though certain tools, such as Solutrian points, are widely distri buted. The historian can now make distinctions and establish classifi cations for approximately twenty tools and identify more than two hundred variants. And we can already observe the extent to which the notion of a technical system may enable greater undertanding of the subject. "The development of the bone industry is directly linked to the increase of scrapers and chisels." It is not only a matter of multiplication but, above all, of diversification. The knife and the point appear as tools, and as major acquisitions.
Little more remains to be said on the subject. Today, detailed studies are being made which, with the discovery of new sites, will certainly contribute important elements to an understanding of this evolution of techniques.
These marked stages are undeniably revealed and defined by dif ferent technical systems. From the birth of the tool, which probably soon ceased to be unique to the Levallois "revolution," and then to the
"revolution" of the Upper Paleolithic age. Of course evolution is not so clear-cut, or so chronologically distinct. At the end of each period, transformations are difficult to detect, preparations are slow, and technical civilizations overlap.
We lack, and will most probably continue to do so, models of explanation. They certainly exist, for instance the capacity of the brain, and the evolution of climates which influence the whole envir onment. But the brain and the hand, and successive glaciations cannot explain everything. Societies, beliefs, and many other factors must also have played an important part, the significance of which we are almost incapable of assessing.

===== PAGE 163 =====

THE FIRST GREAT
TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
Some facts are self explanatory. It took Homo Sapiens some thirty thousand years to develop farming. In three thousand years, man acquired agricultural know-how, animal domestication, pottery and metals, and two thousand years later he developed the written word.
In the words of Andre Leroi-Gourhan:
Farming was barely consolidated around 6000 BC, pottery appeared to be already very advanced, and in approximately 3500 BC, metal and writing began to dawn; all this must lead one to consider that 2500 years of farming were sufficient for Western society to acquire the technical and economical foundations upon which the whole strm:ture of mankind is based.
The reasons for the sudden acceleration of this evolution are still rather obscure and were, in any case, all suggested by specialists:
evolution of the human race, and climatic changes. Firstly, we should note that the cerebral capacity of Neolithic man was no greater than that of the Paleanthropines of La Chapelle-Aux-Saints or of La Quina:
we are now dealing with a brain volume of between 1 ,300 and 1 ,500 cubic centimetres, the same as modern man. As for the question of the climate, a,t this moment in time, research is still at an early stage of its investigation.
One may discourse interminably about the "inventions" of this period. Their genesis will always remain difficult to explain. Two examples will serve to demonstrate the limits of our understanding.
Throughout the world there was a variety of clays which made possible the development of pottery, and man had known the secret of fire for some time. Despite this, however, it is not until the dawn of the sixth

===== PAGE 164 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
147 millennium BC that one sees the beginnings of ceramics. The second example is almost the reverse of the first. For any type or any nature of metallury to appear, atleast the reduction of a mineral was required.
One had to use the necessary apparatus, and an adequate temperature had to be maintained: this might have been how ceramics were produced, but the genesis of the potter's kiln still remains to be explained. Furthermore, one must recognize the mineral types and imagine the melting agents required, and subsequently, the method of working metal, which is fundamentally different from that of stone.
Unfortunately we cannot provide a satisfactory explanation for all this.
The historian of techniques is condemned to suggesting only chronolo gical landmarks which have been broadly clarified in the last few decades.
Documentary material is thus now abundant, although there are still many areas of obscurity. Only explanations are lacking, and we must assume that they will always be so. Although it is relatively easy, when all is said and done, to find out how man at this epoch proceeded in obtaining well-defined products, it is still difficult to establish the means by which he arrived at these methods. Moreover, the Ancients had clearly understood the problem, as we have seen in the preceding chapter. Attributing various inventions to the gods or heros was not only an easy solution, but also demonstrated the recognition of their ignorance, and at the same time, emphasized their astonishment at having created these processes.
The stages are clearly defined. At the beginning, there was the "Neolithic revolution," a term which should be clarified. This took place between the eighth millennium BC and the beginning of the third, and it was during this period that the first great technical civilizations appeared in Mesopotamia and Egypt. Progress then spread to neighbouring Mycenaean and Hittite regions, and step by step, but visibly slower, to successive concentric areas. Both devel oped and underdeveloped countries existed at this epoch, as they do today, whose frontiers and chronology are difficult to determine: one must establish the whole difference between Vercingetorix and
Cleopatra, between the Germans and the school of Alexandria, without considering the still more uncultivated civilizations of Africa or America, at least in less advanced regions. We lack especially synoptic tables listing precisely the material means which were available to man.

===== PAGE 165 =====

148
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
THE NEOLITHIC REVOLUTION
We will once again quote Leroi-Gourhan:
At the end of the Paleolithic epoch, in societies surrounding the Mediterranean a radical techno-economic conversion took place. Between 800 and 500 years before our era, the techno-economic mechanism based upon agriculture and animal husbandry appeared, and societies adopted a completely different form from their original one. On a geological scale, there is only an instant between the last hunter of aurochs and the
Mesopotamian scribe, and the accession to new economies is explosive.
This statement encompasses the whole problem.
Childe was the first to talk of a Neolithic "revolution." This archaeologist considers this to be one of the most significant and fundamental transformations experienced by humanity. Indeed, the first advanced technical system seems to have appeared at this epoch.
Population settlement, agriculture, animal domestication, and pot tery are the major acquisitions of the Neolithic period, and these were undoubtedly far more important developments than stone polishing.
And all this appears to have happened very quickly, in reality from the beginning of the eighth millennium BC to the fourth millennium
BC. Between 6500 and 5700 BC, <;atal Hiiyiik seems already to have possessed an advanced technical civilization: one need only admire the mirror of obsidian which was produced there to be convinced of this fact.
Did these important discoveries, the term "discovery" being more applicable in this instance than invention, arise spontaneously, or were they the result of progressive circulation among localized populations?
It will certainly be impossible to say. It is possible, however, in the light of present knowledge, which is rapidly increasing with the course of excavations, to define a region; a long band stretching from Thessaly and Greek Macedonia to Northern Iran, with continuations towards the South, in Syria and Palestine, beginning in the eighth and seventh millennia BC (fig. 1). In Iraq and Palestine, we may note in the ninth and eighth millennia BC the growing importance of harvesting grasses, executed with the aid of skillfully adapted equipment: sickles, grind stones, bowls and pestles. The domestication of sheep and goats is well on the way. Can this really be called true farming, even if silos appeared in Iraq during this period? In any case, specialists now favour

===== PAGE 166 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
149 Figure 1 . - The Eastern Mediterranean during the neolithic age.
this privileged zone. In this area, ceramics were developed after agriculture and animal domestication.
Reasons to explain this genuine technical revolution have naturally been sought, and many have been found, none of which are really convincing. Is this due to changes in the climate, or better natural conditions? The arguments for this do not seem to hold up. Indeed, the change of climate could equally have reached Italy or Spain, or any region situated on the same latitude. Climatic and natural differences in this zone, in particular in the soils, are so numerous that one searches in vain for any unity. As Leroi-Gourhan so correctly points out, tropical areas did not need to keep stocks and consequently had no need for agriculture: the nature of the products harvested, and the distribution of ripening times were sufficient to support a relatively large population. Why then the existence of Nordic regions and their

===== PAGE 167 =====

150
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
restrictions? The only valid explanation, again a theoretical one, is that it was man himself who reached technical maturity in this area. And we return to the problem presented above: it is not so much the cerebral capacity which is at stake, as the diverse proportions of the elements of the brain. While a discipline cannot provide an explanation, it naturally applies to other sectors of research, as if to exonerate its ignorance.
The map included here, conceived after the most recent and signifi cant discoveries, clearly shows the zone in which decisive technical advances were made. The same quantity of innovations cannot be found elsewhere for the same period.
Sedentarization From the very start we have assessed the importance of the notion of a system of techniques. Agriculture could not have developed without the settling of populations, and conversely, settlement could not have taken place without the existence of agriculture. Settlements were both a necessity and a consequence, as one might expect. Agriculture and animal domestication are equally products of cause and effect, although gathering, hunting and fishing, long remained comple mentary activities. If man settles, livestock will immediately appear.
Leroi-Gourhan attributes this situation to the restrictions of settling around food depots.
The following stages have been' cited for the first permanent popu lation settlements: around 7000 BC in Northern Iraq; 6000 BC in
Mesopotamia, Syria, Lebanon, Anatolia, Macedonia, and Thessaly;
5000 BC in Egypt; and 4000 BC from the Sudan to Baloutchistan.
Dwelling places, originally consisting of a single room, were built in groups. They were constructed of material intended to last for a relatively long period: generally mud-brick which was dried in the sun.
Buildings effectively became permanent in Jericho, Ras Shamra, in Kurdistan, and Ha<;ilar, during the eighth and sixth millennia BC.
Conglomerations exist where dwellings were made of clay walls, and supported on stone foundations. We have reached the upper stage of the proto-Neolithic period. During this last stage, dwellings also consisted of one room, either square or rectangular, possibly with a covered porch. But they were no more than simple huts, built of posts driven into the ground, supporting a wall made of grids of reeds and mud-plastered branches. The floor consisted of a layer of pebbles or a

===== PAGE 168 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
151 hard-packed surface. Dwellings were of this kind at Sesklo (Thessaly), and Nea Nikomedia (in Greek Macedonia). Despite the fragility and light weight of these constructions, they were, as other elements prove, stable buildings.
Some authorities have even suggested that towns existed. Today, we seem to have gone too far in interpreting archaeological data. Childe did not envisage an urban "revolution" until the third millennium BC.
The tenants of Neolithic towns cite two exceptional cases: Jericho A pre-pottery, at the beginning of the eighth millennium, and <;atal
Hiiyiik in the seventh and sixth millennia BC.
Obviously, everything depends on what one understands by the term "town." For some a town is a fortified conglomeration, such as the square tower of Jericho, a sort of rempart formed by the houses leaning against each other and without an opening towards the out side, which encircled the conglomeration of <;atal Hiiyiik. The facts themselves are very dubious. In Mesopotamia, the remparts were only generalized from the third millennium BC onwards. And the unquestionable towns of Egypt, or even those of the Late Roman
Empire, never had fortified walls. Others quote a few agricultural activities and a developed cottage industry. But archaeological digs do not provide very fruitful material. The obsidian mirror of <;atal Hiiyiik is feeble proof because it remains unique. No potters' quarters have been found in any of these conglomerations. We know that the numeric criteria of the population is not an obvious indication. While
Jericho probably had a population of 3000, we know from other sources that large villages had populations of 10,000 predominantly farming inhabitants. If one bases a theory on the criterion of urbanism, it is in the Mesopotamian and Syrian towns of the late fourth millen nium BC that one finds this concern in an evolution involving highways. We might even say, and with good reason, that these two
"towns" in fact represent a pre-urban stage.
On the other hand, there is evidence of early fortresses, a sign of an advanced society organized within a hierarchy. The remains of the sixth millennium BC towns of Sesklo and Dimini are striking. The acropolis of Dimini is surrounded by three concentric walls, foresha dowing Mycenaean palaces (fig. 2). It was to be the same at Troy, founded around 3500 or 3200 BC. But these cannot be called towns either. These fortress-palaces, however, witness the settling of peoples and the beginning of political power.

===== PAGE 169 =====

152
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
(
I!
20 metres Figure 2. - Plan of the site of Dhimini. (From Wace-Thompson, Prehistoric Thessaly,
Cambridge, 1972) Agriculture Agriculture appeared at the end of the Mesolithic epoch. Truth to tell, it is sometimes difficult to establish the facts concerning this, and our sources of documentation are often ambiguous. Seeds found in centres could equally have come from wild plants, in the beginning at least, as well as cultivated ones. This is similar for certain instruments which

===== PAGE 170 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
153 could be used either for gathering or cultivation, as is exemplified in the case of the sickle. The dawn of agriculture necessitated the recognition and selection of profitable varieties of plants consumed before, but as yet not cultivated, then improvements by hybridization.
It is a patent fact that in regions where farming appeared for the first time, agriculture was always linked with animal domestication, an obvious indication of the existence of a technical system. In preceding epochs, moreover, man had always practised a mixed food economy, finding a balance in the complementary exploitation of the animal and vegetable world. The change to a more settled way of life would therefore suggest two activities. Leroi-Gourhan estimates, however, that some farmers may have complemented their resources by hunting, and some shepherds complemented theirs by gathering wild plants.
Zoological and botanical conditions must have been equally favourable for the simultaneous appearance of animal rearing and agriculture in the same regions. Plants with consumable seeds were particularly developed throughout the temperate band, especially in the southern zone, Africa, to the north of the tropic zone, the Middle
East, and Central Asia. Again in the words of Leroi-Gourhan:
Before the present desication, during the epoch when man changed to proto-agriculture, the periodic exploitation of herbaceous plants and seeds certainly constituted an essential part in the search for food. Grasses themselves played an important part among these plants: despite the small size of their seeds, they represented a high quality nutritional food, and could be preserved for a long time. It has recently been suggested that the regions of the Near East, in particular the north of Iraq possessed grasses with large seeds, ancestors of present day cereals, at least from the middle of the last glacial period. The basic conditions for the progressively agricultural exploitation of wild wheat were consequently developed in the same regions where goat herding was first mani fested.
Agriculture was certainly not "invented," but was the result of a process of progressive change from one situation to another, sometimes without any links common to farming and animal rearing.
It is not clear which centuries witnessed the birth of agriculture, and scientists rarely agree with each other. According to some, cultivated cereals appeared with the Mesolithic Natufians of Palestine around
7800 BC, and in Jarmo and Jericho around 7000 BC. According to others, the growing importance of seeds in both Iraq and Palestine, in the ninth and eighth millennia BC, is attested by the abundance of

===== PAGE 171 =====

154
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
certain tools, such as sickles, grindstones, bowls and pestles made of stone. Decriptions of silos in Zawi-Chemi and Shanidar in Iraq have even been cited.
During the seventh millennium BC, the cultivation of wheat and barley seems to have been developed in a great number of regions in the Eastern basin of the Mediterranean. The proto-Neolithic period in
Sesklo, in Thessaly, has revealed the cultivation of hard wheat ( Triti cum dicoctum), einkorn (Triticum monococcum), barley (Hordeum vulgare), and millet (Panicum miliaceum). While millet is European, all the other cereals in Thessaly originated in Asia. The wild barley region stretches from Anatolia to Northern Iran. Hard wheat seems to have originated in the area of Palestine and Northern Iraq, and Asia
Minor seems to have known another primitive variety of wheat ( Triticum aegilopoides).
We are thus concerned with a system of agriculture which is already passably diversified, perhaps also already adapted to different soils.
This diversity is attested by the existence of other cultivated species:
the broad bean and the pea were known to ancient Neolithic man, and probably even to proto-Neolithic civilizations, and the lentil was also cultivated at this stage.
At Nea Nikomedia, in Greek Macedonia, virtually at the same period as at Sesklo, one finds wheat and barley, as well as peas and lentils. But nearby, the populations of Northern Greece lived on additional wild cereals and plants gathered in the area.
During the upper Neolithic culture, agriculture was definitively accepted in Dimini. New relationships between man and the natural environment were established. Ground was cleared to provide artifi cial environments which could be worked more easily. It is even possible that irrigation and manuring were introduced. Certainly, the same cereals were cultivated as those during the pre-pottery period. Of those leguminous plants which can be traced, the lentil, pea, and broad bean are extremely common. It is more difficult to say whether wild fruits were picked or whether fruit trees had already been cultivated.
In any case, the olive, pear, almond and fig have been pointed out.
It has been noted that towards the end of this epoch the vine may have been introduced, in evidence at the beginning of the ancient
Bronze age. It is well known that botanists are not in agreement about the origin of the vine. Nevertheless, it would seem that the greatest number of wild species were to be found in the vast zone stretching

===== PAGE 172 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
155 from the Black Sea to the Indus valley. The vine spread extensively during the period when temperatures rose, which took place between the last two glacial periods, as certain discoveries in the Neckar valley have shown.
Archaeological discoveries made in the last twenty years have thus clarified the transition from gathering to cultivation. The area of transition was previously thought to have been located between the
Mediterranean and the Caspian. Today, the north of Iraq, Syria, Libya, Palestine, Turkey and some parts of Greece, have been explored, revealing sites with traces of fairly diverse cultures, dating between 8000 and 6000 years BC: thus, sites have been found at J armo,
Shanidar, Zawi-Chemi and at <;atal Hiiyiik. "This transition took place almost imperceptibly; sickles, as we have said, were present before agriculture, and only statistics show that goats ceased to be hunted. The example of Iraq corresponds to an ideal demonstration, as within the space of a few centuries a transformation had taken place, without entailing any upheavals which could jeopardize cultural survival."
Animal Rearing The domestication of animals poses other problems which are just as complex and similar in nature. The material available is difficult to interprete. Moreover, animal rearing did not eliminate hunting. It is almost impossible to trace the genealogy of some animals, indeed even to distinguish between them at these remote epochs, and this is particularly so in the case of goats and sheep.
The development of animal domestication, which may have advanced with hunting, requires favourable environmental conditions, as it presupposes that the hunter and the hunted maintain some sort of personal relationship. Excluded from this fact are the great migrant herbivores, whose herds pass once or twice a year within weapon range; also excluded are the large and dangerous or fast herbivores, such as the ox, the bison, and the horse, animals covering a large area who are difficult to approach and impossible to contain. When one analyses the factors contributing to the possible transition towards animal domestication, one realizes that the conditions of the physical environment are even more imperative than bio-zoological conditions, and that the chances of animal husbandry being established in the Steppes of Africa or Central Asia were very slim.
We have seen that conditions were favourable in Northern Iraq for the domestication of the goat. From this one may conclude that proto-rearing most probably originated in the mountains.

===== PAGE 173 =====

156
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
To be honest, it is difficult to say whether the goat or the sheep was domesticated first. This probably varies (lCCording to the region. Dates suggested for this are as vague as those put forward for agriculture.
The sheep appears to have been domesticated around 9000 BC in Northern Iraq, according to the dates established for remains found on sites at Zawi-Chemi and Shanidar. It has been said that at Jarmo and
Jericho, an important interval existed between the domestication of the first animal and the second: in this area the goat appears to have been domesticated approximately 1500 years after the sheep.
In the seventh millennium BC, goats and sheep, the first domesti cated animals, were present throughout the zone which has been defined above for the beginning of farming. As has been noted, these two activities of man match almost exactly, both geographically and chronologically.
The transition from mountain goat or sheep herding to the rearing of larger herbivores and pigs has not yet been elucidated. It seems, however, to be linked to the first impetus given by proto-rearing of the bezoar, as it developed a little later, within a radius of the original centre. Between 6000 and 3000 years before our era, the sheep, ox, pig, donkey and horse were domesticated, then towards the Indus valley, the buffalo, zebu and elephant were reared and spread to the Near East, Asia, Europe and Africa. In this movement, the inital momentum is the only point to be called into question, as, apart from the American llama, all animal rearing constitutes a coherent historical block. The principle once accepted, its application to new species, offers fewer difficulties than the transition of ceramics to metallurgy. It is interesting to note that, apart from the reindeer whose food conditions are very particular, all the domesticated herbivores are actually grass eaters (cattle, sheep, horses and camels) , animals grouped in dense societies on a continuous vegetable carpet and whose flight behaviour is one of grouping. The beating action adopted by shepherds working with dogs applies equally to these animals. Leaf eaters ( cervidaes, living in small hordes under cover) whose flight behaviour is one of dispersion, remained undomesticated.
The same difficulties of dating still exist. Pigs and cattle existed in the proto-Neolithic period at Sesklo, in Thessaly. At Nea Nikomedia, around 6200 BC, goats and sheep lived together with cattle and pigs, and at Jarmo, pigs appeared towards 6500 BC, and oxen around
5000 BC.
By-products from animal rearing were very quickly used. Wool was produced at <;atal Hiiyiik in approximately 6000 BC, and cheese was made at Dimini during the recent Neolithic period.
Hunting, however, still remained a privileged method of providing supplies. At Dimini, in Thessaly, auroch, stag, deer, boar and hare were all hunted. Fishing also continued in this large sea-side village.

===== PAGE 174 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
157 But this type of food was very limited compared to what was provided by cultivation and animal rearing.
Let us note finally, and we will return to the subject of Egypt later, that there was an attempt, albeit much later, to rear certain species which were eventually abandoned, such as the hyena and the crane.
Broadly speaking, we do not know, during this perio'rl, of the rearing of poultry.
A specialist has tried to clarify these points:
When one sees the most perfect and universal of our domestic animals suddenly appear together, in what one could call the prehistoric history of civilisations, does one not wonder if this achievement was the venture of a special human race, endowed with faculties which his predecessors lacked, a race which was the result of sudden change rather than slow evolution, which suddenly created a new being.
We will now return to the ideas outlined above: the perfect concord ance and complementarity between agriculture and domestication.
"The processes started during the Mesolithic period in the Near East in around 8000, had already completely transformed the structure of societies in around 5000, from Mesopotamia to Turkey, Greece and
Egypt. A basic economy is formed even before the appearance of pottery, by the association of wheat or barley with sheep, goats and pigs, and the first permanent villages appeared."
We can easily imagine the influence of this fundamental technical change on forms of societies, even if, at the beginning, life styles were mixed before they became combined. Gatherers, hunters, farmers and shepherds probably lived side by side, definitively integrated in complex societies, which may have already recognized a certain division of labour.
Ceramics The reader will not be astonished to learn that the date or the area in which pottery was developed is not known. Specialists agree that it did not appear before 7000 BC. In fact, the material existed almost everywhere, and no concrete technical obstacle had blocked its devel opment since the Paleolithic period. To be honest, nomadism is hardly favourable for ceramic activity: drying is a slow process and frequent movements are not suitable for a material which, by definition, is relatively fragile. Sedentarization and agriculture were to favour its

===== PAGE 175 =====

158
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
development: harvests, the handling and stocking of products, and gradually refined methods of preparing food required numerous and varied receptacles, to complement the more ancient practice of basket work. We should also note that clay was employed in building as well.
Truth to tell, opinions differ. In this area, as in others recently discussed, every archaeologist claims to have discovered the oldest known document, usually accompanying it with astonishing expla nations, all of which must be verified.
Before the discoveries made at Sesklo and Nea Nikomedia, pottery from <;atal Hiiyiik dating from the seventh millennium BC or from the beginning of the sixth millennium, was thought to be an exception to the general rule. As far as carbon dating can be accurate, pottery existed at Sesklo, in Thessaly, in around 6500 BC, and at Nea
Nikomedia, in Greek Macedonia, in approximately 6200 BC. These dates would appear to be the oldest in comparison with others which have been suggested. According to some, this ceramic industry arose from the difficulty which these populations found in trying to make stone vases. Others estimate that pottery was also known in Iraq,
Syria, Libya, Palestine and Anatolia at this time. Different regional forms were quickly developed, a fact which eliminates the notion of a single centre of invention. In any case, in approximately 6000 BC, pottery appeared from Thrace to Iran, to <;atal Hiiyiik in Anatolia, and Ras Shamra in Northern Syria.
Between 6000 and 5000 BC, the development of ceramics reached neighbouring regions: Cyprus in approximately 5250 BC, Ha<;ilar around 5500 BC, and Jarmo towards 4600 BC. In most of these regions, pottery was developed after agriculture and animal hus bandry: in the northern regions of Europe it is the reverse, for pottery preceded farming and animal domestication. This is an important fact, as it completely destroys any idea of the logical development of a material culture.
The problem of fire similarly arises. Apart from its culinary use, in around 3500 BC fire made dyeing possible with ferruginous ochres:
rock paintings provide magnificent examples of this. The firing of clay, however, was accidentally produced in the fireplaces of cave dwellers.
One can only imagine the potter's kiln, used no doubt, for the creation of modelled figurines. Leroi-Gourhan suggests that plaster made its appearance at the same time as pottery, and that "from Mesopotamia to the Mediterranean, the reduction by fire of gypsum in plaster,

===== PAGE 176 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
159 provided a coating on the floor and walls of the kilns." Pottery and plaster indicate the existence of an already proved skill in obtaining temperatures between 500 and 700 degrees, and the possibility of exceeding 1000 degrees for very limited and adequately ventilated parts of the hearth. We will return later to the subject of the progres sive acquisition of higher temperatures which play a predominant role in the preparation of a large number of products (fig. 3).
Metallurgy Once again, we are dealing with new mysteries which are very obscure.
The notion that the use of native metals was relatively simple to conceive, already poses problems. It is not only the idea of metal which is involved, but also all the processes required to produce a usable material: it has to be heated and forged, thus entailing operations which bore no similarity to earlier techniques. Even the glitter of gold or silver is not sufficient to explain the appearance of metallurgy.
It is quite different for the reduction of minerals. Man must know how to extract the metal ore from the rock, how to choose the mineral and how to construct a furnace, which presupposes that heat is the essential vector of the operation; understanding that in some cases the mineral must first be roasted, and that melting agents must often be added, how to create charcoal and knowing that it is vital for reduc tion, presupposes workings of the mind which are quite astonishing and which would undoubtedly be impossible to reconstruct. According to some, one is left with the theory that all this resulted by chance, but this is difficult to accept: a potter's kiln built of stones containing a mineral, with lime or plaster as the reducing agent, and which, in certain places, was of a sufficient temperature could not have come about by mere accident. Let us simply admit our ignorance and agree with Leroi-Gourhan by saying that the production of pottery implies some knowledge of working with fire.
The region would also have had to have been rich in minerals. Here again, cultural, technical, geographical and geological interactions are necessary. We find the same notion of environment as was suggested for the birth of agriculture or animal husbandry. Access to exploitable deposits and the level of techno-economic development are indispen sable preconditions. It is unthinkable that metallurgy could have been developed during the Acheulean epoch. Social conditions must also be

===== PAGE 177 =====

160
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
 1 0 0 f.f tL-11 13 Figure 3. - Neolithic Greek sites and pottery. 1, outline of a dwelling of the Chalcolithic period; 2, statuette; 3 to 5, ceramic; 6 to 12, late Neolithic; 6, outline of dwelling; 7 and 8, arrowheads; 9, axe; 1 0, statuette; 1 1 and 1 2, ceramic; 1 3 to 20, Middle Neolithic (Sesklo); 1 3, outline of dwelling; 14 and 1 5, axe and adze; 16,statuette; 17, seal; 1 8 to 20,ceramic; 21 to 25, pre-ceramic Neolithic; 21 and 22, blades; 23, ear disc; 24, sling ball; 25, hook; 26 and 2 7, early
Neolithic: ceramic. (From A. Leroi-Gourhan, La Prehistoire, Paris, 1 968)

===== PAGE 178 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
161 reunited: ceramics may have been a family activity, but metallurgy could not have. Originally, the latter seems to have been the product of an industry of specialists, who worked for a market open to a fairly large number of people. It is a remarkable fact that the consumers, at least in early times, were often not the producers: either one bought outside the industry, or one seized the metal by means of war. These mineral riches are not all included in the zones which we have defined for agriculture and animal rearing, but are often close to their borders.
In the south-east of the Mediterranean, an experimental study has given some idea of the possible uses of fire as the transforming agent of matter. So that after pottery, metal, lime, and glass appeared at almost the same time. Notice also that the east Mediterranean had developed charcoal and a method of constructing a kiln which could reach very high temperatures. And as we have previously stated, this lime could already act as a flux.
In the last few years, the time at which metals might have been known, in one form or another, has been placed at perceptively earlier dates. c;atal Hiiyiik may have possessed lead and more abundantly copper in about 6600-6300 BC, that is to say at the same time as pottery was developed, and Hai;ilar towards 5400 BC. Around the beginning of the fifth millennium, the process of casting this metal was accomplished. It is very difficult to determine the content of this metal and its uses, but these examples seem to be exceptional isolated cases, due to the presence of sizeable deposits. It is certain that the Chalcoli thic civilization, taken as a whole, was much later.
The same may be said of bronze, which gave birth to certain empires. Bronze poses the same difficult problems. Was it a natural alloy, or an intentional one? We will probably never know the answer, or which one preceded the other.
Briefly, and we will return to this later, we should mention the presence of iron in the royal tombs of c;atal Hiiyiik, constructed in the third millennium. There is such a quantity of this material that we must suggest that it was undoubtedly in this area of Anatolia that iron metallurgy was invented.
Stone We should not forget, however, that stone was the basic tool material.
Even when metal was introduced, the metal tool was often made in

===== PAGE 179 =====

162
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
stone where metal deposits were not available.
Lithic industries are well differentiated throughout the zone we have defined, reaching a peak in the seventh millennium BC. All the sites already mentioned have provided an abundance of evidence testifying to this side of man's activity: Jarmo in Iraq, Ras Shamra in Syria,
Ha<;ilar in Anatolia, Nahal Oren on the Palestinian coast, Jericho in Palestine near the Jordan, and Seyl Aqlat in Southern Palestine. This industry is most often microlithic. On the other hand, there are few geometric microliths accompanying an industry of blades, bladelets and fine perforating implements in Iraq. In Northern Syria and
Anatolia, where the mesolithic substratum is poor, there was no microlith tool industry.
A tendancy for more diversified tools followed. The production of objects in bone, introduced in the recent Paleolithic period, was equally developed.
Neolithic tool material in the Near East seems in fact to have followed ancient tendancies, but in using this material, this culture resoundingly manifested imagination lacked by its precedessors.
THE TECHNIQUES OF THE
FIRST GREAT KINGDOMS
History really begins at the end of the fourth and the beginning of the third millennium BC. Thus the Near East already possessed agricul ture, metallurgy, and domesticated animals: it was very quickly to acquire the written word, towns, and political and military power.
The Egyptian civilization began around the years 3500 BC. Between 3500 and 3000 BC, the two kingdoms of North and South asserted themselves, and writing was introduced. Around 3000 BC, Narmer finally unified the country. In approximately 2780 BC, Djozer founded
Memphis and built the first pyramid at Saqqara, thus inaugurating the first stone architecture. From then on a new civilization was born.
It was around the year 2800 BC that the dynasties of Uruk and Ur were founded, and the royal tombs of Ur appeared. Here again, almost at the same time, civilization advanced rapidly and ceaselessly affirmed itself in the following centuries. This simultaneous emergence

===== PAGE 180 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
163 is extraordinary, even if no apparent influence was originally exerted by one region on the other.
Egypt Apart from some enigmas which have still to be solved and certain influences exerted by neighbouring civilizations, the technical system of the Egyptians poses some difficult problems.
When the first kingdoms emerged, men had been living for a long time on the lateral terraces of the Nile, hunters and fishermen who used stone tools. The advance of the desert had overtaken the swamps inhabited by other men. They then joined the first inhabitants of the valley and the oasis, whereupon they all began to gradually cultivate the land. Lithic industry was refined, and the first pottery was intro duced. So in around 3000 BC, Narmer unified the country, and established and wore the two crowns of Upper and Lower Egypt. He also introduced the first hieroglyphics, and it has been suggested that some of the drawings of this epoch recall the art of Mesopotamia. The pre-dynastic era saw the arrival of some metals: gold, silver, and lead were introduced at the beginning of the third millennium BC.
Buildings were constructed out of sun-dried mud-bricks. I and II Dynasty tombs have been found at Saqqara, Balla, Negada, and
Helowan. Furniture consisted mainly of pottery, alabaster and hard stone vases, and schist objects.
Suddenly - and herein lies the first problem - from the years 2778 to 2423 BC, the Old Kingdom became an advanced technical civilization.
From the III to the V Dynasty, we see the building of pyramids and this whole civilization which is illustrated in engraved and painted frescoes.
These frescoes, such as the Ti mastabas (dating around 2560) and the Mereruka mastabas (dating around 2420), reveal as much on the suject of agriculture as on the subject of the arts and crafts which possessed already definitively formed equipment and produced very elaborate products. It was at this epoch that stone architecture made its appea rance. Imhotep, architect, doctor, and chief minister to King Djoser, built the first pyramid at Saqqara, and a whole complex of palaces and shrines, where for the first time one saw fluted columns which were to make Karnak and Luxor famous. This dates back to 2780 BC. The earliest inscriptions, those from the palette of Narmer, present the image of a still primitive language and script.

===== PAGE 181 =====

164
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
It is difficult to account for the sudden appearance of an advanced technical system, and it is frequently awkward to determine the material of this equipment, almost certainly still Lithic in tradition, and possibly, in certain cases, made out of copper. Obviously our ignorance of the exact details of the Neolithic techniques which we have just mentioned, precludes the measurement of the differences between the two cultures. One cannot honestly say then if this was a sudden process or a slow change. May one talk of an "industrial revolution"? As G. Posener notes, "And then, the invention of a new tool suddenly opened up a whole crop of possibilities: the use of the hand hammer or the miner's pick which increased the power of the hand, probably inaugurated the era of using building stone, and of working quarries and mines. More inventions were to follow."
According to the same author, the gestation period seems to have begun in the pre-dynastic age and ceased with the Thinite kings, probably in the region of the years of the fifth millenium BC to about
3300 BC. "And from the first dynasties onwards, the Egyptians had developed all their building methods." Undoubtedly this view is rather too schematic and, as we have said above, the suddenness and the extent of this "revolution" must be qualified.
At this epoch, the evolution of the first dynasties conquered by Macedonia appears at first sight to have been limited, according to all the illustrations of the Egyptian civilization. New ideas or implements are rare, such as the bellows, or the Archimedes' screw. Similarly, some evolution in tools may be observed, due probably to the intro duction of metals; bronze in the Middle Kingdom (2160 to 1580 BC), and iron during the epoch of the XXV Dynasty (712 to 663 BC). But, on the whole, the technical system remained the same until the
Macedonian conquest in 332 BC. Only then were more advanced Greek techniques put into practice, particularly the use of machines.
Once again this statement needs qualifying. Our documentation is mostly pictorial in origin. In the main, these images date from most of the mastabas of Saqqara, that is towards the middle of the second millennium BC, then from the tombs of the Valley of the Kings, around the middle of the first millennium, and finally from the Scrolls of the Dead which are more recent. In fact, if exact comparisons were made - they have not been as yet -, one would realize that this stability is perhaps more apparent than real. We have already mentioned the bellows and the Archimedes' screw, but other examples may be cited;

===== PAGE 182 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
165 vertical weaving looms of the Middle Kingdom, the horse, the wheel and the wagon, and glass under the New Kingdom. And this is the same for tools where evolution and enrichment are manifest.
This would appear to be the classical evolution of a technical system, improvements of which are to some extent foreseeable from the outset. Also, is it not somewhat hazardous to suggest a blockage of
Egyptian techniques, as some have done? The Egyptian technical system seemed to reach its perfection at the same time as the Greek system. Let us say that evolution is slow and that the history of an independent Egypt largely unfolds under the same technical system.
Undoubtedly several reasons may account for this.
The civilization of Ancient Egypt was a purely rural one. The country was separated by the sea and by the marshes of the Nile delta which were difficult to cross. The Egyptians were never sailors although they were boatmen. One may refer to economical phe nomena, linked to the lie of the land and the nature of the soil and its hydrography. Others have considered that this relative stagnation was the result of a fixed society. For our part, we believe that all these factors could have been involved.
The beginnings Since around seventy years our knowledge of Neoli thic Egypt has increased considerably. So much so, that the notion of the sudden appearance of an advanced civilization must be rejected in favour of an evolution, perhaps a more rapid one than elsewhere, but nevertheless a continuous evolution which suddenly appeared revolu tionary when the technical system became coherent.
It is true that the region was favourable. Leaving aside for a moment the image of the Nile floods, one must remember that the valleys were lush with grasses, and that the fauna was particularly rich with wild asses, Barbary sheep, urns, antelopes, gazelles, and giraffes, lions and leopards. This fauna was still hunted in the Middle Kingdom. In the valley itself were elephants, kudus, two species of pig, hippopotami, crocodiles and wild boars.
The hunters of the plains had visited the valley during the Lower Paleolithic period: their tools have be found on the high terraces of the two banks, testifying to a Levallois tradition of microlithic forms. As the drought periods became more frequent, and the need for water more pressing, nomads took refuge in well-watered regions. They
"were to be found, therefore, in conditions which forced them to

===== PAGE 183 =====

166
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
abandon a parasitic life and lead a productive one." Some historians, in particular the American, Perry, have vehemently supported the theory of the birth of agriculture in Egypt: new life was produced without human labour, in the silt of the annual floods where plants, ancestors of wheat and barley, reproduced themselves.
The Badarian civilization, established on the barren spurs which swept down to the marshy valley below, had already cultivated emmer wheat and barley, as has been proved by grains found in their more or less permanent encampments. Hand mills, flag stones with concave surfaces, and grinders are manifest proof, and we have already men tioned the discovery of goat and sheep bones. But this is only the beginning of a new civilization: sedentarization does not appear to have taken place as yet, and hunting and fishing were still important activities. They worked with wood, pottery existed, and remains of cloth have been found. They probably also painted their faces and eyes.
Sites at the Fayum present an image of already very advanced techniques. The inhabitants of this area had managed to cultivate emmer and barley, but had also produced flax. Harvests were preser ved in silos sunk in the ground. Pigs, cattle, sheep and goats had been domesticated, and hunting and fishing with bows and arrows conti nued. The presence of scrapers suggests that animal skins were used for making clothes.
The site at Merimde gives a similar picture. There is evidence that the sling was used, and textile production is attested by spindle-whorls.
It is the same at Debono, near Helwan. From the Tasians to the Badarians, the same evolution towards new techniques can be found.
Further south, near Khartoum, one leaves these technically advanced tribes to rediscover a Neolithic culture, certainly one which is rela tively advanced, but which had not developed agriculture, even though an excellent well-fired ceramic industry existed, and goats were reared.
In Upper Egypt, the Badarian culture was followed by the Amra tian, and in all likelihood, proceeded from it. The inhabitants of this region embarked upon a systematic cultivation of the plains irrigated by the floods. Farming and animal rearing were henceforth balanced, and large cattle were reared for their milk, meat and labour, even though the complementary activities of hunting, fishing and gathering were still required. Rock paintings give us the impression of a new

===== PAGE 184 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
167 advance in the field of traditional techniques. Sedentarization gave birth to large and permanent villages. The flint industry was improved and it is posssible that the ass was already domesticated. The Amra tians navigated the Nile, but in crafts without sails. Pottery, on the other hand, made no notable progress and if anything ceramics were not as advanced as those of the Badarians.
It is not until the Gerzian epoch that a new stage begins. At this point agriculture became an essential resource and henceforth may have been founded, to a certain extent, on artificial irrigation. Pressure flaking reached a point of excellence in flint production. "The most significant development was the comprehension and application of the principle of a well-thought-out metallurgy. " Tools and weapons made of cast metal were used, although they . were relatively rare, and earthenware was developed although stone vessels were still numerous and were made of harder rock. All these products reveal a modified society, nourishing its specialists on a better developed agriculture.
Dwellings became more solid and more durable in construction. It is possible, if not highly probable, that the whole of this civilization was produced by outside influence.
Thus, we have defined a stage where new techniques were acquired and a new technical system was developed. There is no sudden transformation, but an evolution in which one may recognize peak periods and domains where old traditions continue or sometimes even decrease. But from now on everything had reached a stage where the first Pharaohs' dynasties could begin to initiate change: henceforth, agriculture is perfectly adapted, involving all the necessary secondary operations; metallurgy had already been tried and tested, writing was to be born, and the whole technical ensemble was to reach a level of equilibrium.
Exploitation techniques Agriculture had therefore existed in a certain number of regions in the Near East when the first dynasties began. We will not repeat here what has been widely discussed about the Nile floods and their relationship with farming operations. The many agricultural scenes in Egyptian tombs and imagery provide abundant documentation on this subject.
Cereal production was the basis of most cultivation. Barley, corn, emmer, and from the beginning of the New Kingdom, durra were the main crops. Leguminous plants were already numerous; lentils, beans,

===== PAGE 185 =====

168
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
onions, chick-peas, cucumbers and watermelon were richly repre sented. The Egyptians also grew a variety of fruits: pomegranates, figs, jujubes, olives, carobs and dates. It is more difficult to date the arrival of the vine. Sycamore provided both wood and fruit. This list demon strates that a wide variety of crops had already been developed, which were far from primitive species. To be honest, we have little infor mation about the species cultivated by the Ancient Egyptians. We must also add a new product to the above, flax which rapidly gained in importance as it was lifted and not cut for harvest.
Farming material was very limited. The hoe, used equally well for gardening as well as for making irrigation channels, was a special type as we have illustrated. It appears in this form on a Theban temple of the XVIII Dynasty ( 1580 to 1314 BC), now in the Louvre. On the other hand, more elaborate, modern-looking instruments with handles may also be found in the Ti mastaba (2500 BC) at Saqqara, and in the
Menna tomb (1412 BC) near Thebes (fig. 4) .
The instrument used for ploughing should not present any prob lems. This tool, the ard is virtually identical except for a few details to early representations of the V Dynasty (the Ti mastaba at Saqqara, dating around 2500 BC), that of the XVIII Dynasty ( 1580 to 1314 BC), and to the Deir el-Bahari papyrus of the XXI Dynasty (1060 to 950
BC), and we might safely say without too much risk of error that it resembles the plough used today (fig. 5). The absence of any modifi cation and the continued use of this tool is absolutely remarkable. This ard was quite different from the ard with a share-beam used on the northern border of the Mediterranean. This sort of plough is illustrated many times, both in tombs and on papyrus. It appears as an ard, (a primitive plough) with two stilts separated by one or more cross-bars, up to four in the Nakht tomb near Thebes (1415 BC). A link, usually made of rope, supported the coulter and shaft. The team always consisted of a pair of oxen. The shaft appears - drawings are sometimes very ambiguous in this respect - to have been linked to a light yoke placed in front of the animals' horns. But there are also illustrations of oxen yoked around the neck, like horses. Ploughing is generally performed by two men, one to hold the ard, and the other to lead the team. On a fresco in the tomb of Sennedjem, near Thebes, just one man is pictured performing both these operations. Lastly, an ard drawn by four men is represented on the same fresco in the Louvre which we have already mentioned. The Egyptian ard has a slim frame, and only scratches the surface of a loose, light soil.

===== PAGE 186 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
169 Harvesting was done using a sickle (fig. 6). This implement may have originally been equipped with small flint blades slotted into a wooden groove. Once it was made in metal, it became an instrument with a large curvature. This tool was also hardly modified between the
VI Dynasty, from which date the earliest representations, and the time of Alexander the Great. The method of harvesting allowed the worker to stand completely upright, as the ears of corn were held close to the grain and thus cut high on the stalk.
The ears of corn were then transported in large baskets, carried by two men by means of a bar placed on their shoulders. The grain was placed in a pile, then spread in a circle around the threshing floor. The only technique used at this epoch consisted of threshing under the hooves of either oxen or cattle. The grain then had to be cleaned, in order to separate it from the broken straw, which was done using spades, and finally it was winnowed. The last two operations were performed by women.
One realizes then that equipment was limited to only a few imple ments, even for one of the most essential cultures. Apart from a small number of modifications to tools arising from the introduction of metal, the equipment used in farming remained much the same during the many centuries of Ancient Egyptian history.
As a matter of fact, we know very little about types of cultivations, and we possess few illustrations of them. There is only a limited number of images illustrating the vine (from XVIII Dynasty Thebian tombs of Nakht and Sennedjem). They show high growing vines, not our modern low vines. Representations show the grape picking, pressing by foot, and then the way in which the liquid was stored in amphores. The method of grape pressing was certainly not adequate, for the grape really needs to be crushed. This was done by placing the grapes in sacks between two poles. In the Middle Kingdom, at
Beni-Hassan, this process was perfected. "The sack is placed in a stall and fixed to one side of a wall. On the other was a braid, through which three men passed a rigid shaft, enabling them to exert greater pressure on the sack. This system did not require either the compli cated gymnastics of the Ancient Kingdom, or very great effort." So there was some evolution from the Ti mastaba (approximately 2500
BC) to the tombs of the XVIII Dynasty (middle of the second millennium). Unfortunately we do not have any more images after this date.

===== PAGE 187 =====

170
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
F
E
Figure 4. - Sickle, adze, knife and hoe. (Old Kingdom) Figure 5. - Ard. (XVIII Dynasty) Figure 6. - Harvesting with a sickle.

===== PAGE 188 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
171 As G. Posener has pointed out, there was a certain degree of experimentation in animal rearing and in the species which man attempted to tame. The early domestication of some animals appears to have been abandoned later on, such as the hyena, the crane and a few other species, including the gazelle, oryx, bubale, addax and ibex.
Stabling was fairly standard, at least for some animals. Let us return to our Neolithic chronology.
Sheep appear to have been domesticated before goats, the latter being a mountain animal. Sheep rearing is only traced from the V
Dynasty onwards, but we are dependent on images for this infor mation. Cattle, on the other hand, were domesticated from the time of the early dynasties. According to a specialist, at this epoch, that is to say the middle of the third millennium BC, cattle breeding had not been a long precedence. Indeed, under the Ancient Kingdom one still sees cows tied by the legs while they were milked, a fact which suggests that they were still nearly wild (fig. 7). Two breeds of cattle can be distinguished for this period; one is large and strong with long horns, and the other is small without horns. Pigs had also been domesticated, but we do not know very much about this.
The Egyptian poultry was similarly well provided. The goose existed from the time of the early dynasties, as can be seen from the famous
Figure 7. - Milking a bound cow.

===== PAGE 189 =====

172
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
and very beautiful representation at Meidum, dating around 2720 BC.
Cranes were also well represented at Saqqara around 2500 BC. Geese and cranes were probably fattened for their meat rather than for the liver (fig. 8). Ducks were also a source of food, although they did not appear anywhere in the poultry: moreover, rearing ducks presented problems for the Romans who appear to have been the first to succeed in domesticating them. The pigeon, on the other hand, appeared as early as the very first dynasties: it was used for carrying news, but was probably also popular on the table.
There is some debate surrounding the subject of other animals.
Despite the opinions of other authors, the ass or donkey seems to have been around for a very long time: it threshed corn, worked or carried loads. "It was, until the XVIII Dynasty, the only animal which could transport people and lead caravans across the desert to the Red Sea."
Illustrations of this date from the XII Dynasty (fig. 9). The hen, most probably Persian in origin, only appears in sketchy representations during the Greek epoch. A mutilated passage from the epoch of
Tuthmosis III speaks of a bird which laid an egg per day. Could this be the hen? The horse only appeared in the New Kingdom, to be more exact after 1300 BC, and the camel was only domesticated in the last century before the beginning of our era.
From the IV Dynasty onwards, dogs were kept on leads, moreover with hyenas. Some of these dogs were genuine greyhounds, with svelte bodies, straight ears, thin flanks and fawn coats. One can also see a smaller dog, more solid in appearance, with a short muzzel and floppy ears. It is easy to recognize five very different breeds from the XII
Dynasty onwards, and soon seven types existed, including two sorts of greyhound, a hound with a medium muzzel, a sheepdog, a guard dog similar to a huge hound, and even a basset with bent legs. The cat appeared at the beginning of the Egyptian civilization. From the start, there are two types of feline distinguishable which bear similarities to the wild native species still in existence today.
The Egyptians may also have introduced beekeeping, that is to say the organized development of the world of bees: indeed, the rock painting of the honey gatherer, found at Bicorp in Spain, goes back to the Neolithic period - but which? - and proves that wild honey was collected. Under the Old Kingdom men knew how to make artificial hives, introduce the bees, and then collect the honey and wax. This is perfectly illustrated on a block originating from Abousir. All

===== PAGE 190 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
173 these operations are represented in the tomb of Rekhmire, near Thebes (XVIII Dynasty; fig. 10). The technique seems to have been perfected since the V dynasty. Beehives were smoked and honey poured into large jars, and it is possible that gingerbread was made.
Honey and wax were very important in the Egyptian way of life.
Hunting and fishing were abundant and ancient practices. Many scenes of these activities are illustrated, represented on funerary monuments and temples.
The bow, lasso and boomerang were used in hunting, traps were laid, and the falcon was employed, as can be seen on the mastaba at

===== PAGE 191 =====

174
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Mereruka at Saqqara (dating approximately 2400 BC). Nets for catching birds were also used (fig. 11). Hardly anything has changed since these distant epochs and it is probable that these hunting techniques were relatively old. The boomerang is not exactly the same as the one used by the Australians which returns to its starting point if it does not reach its target. It is really a throwing stick, curved at one end, designed to catch water birds as they fly over the reeds.
Hunting was certainly a means of obtaining additional food. But it was also a way of procuring animals for domestication. They hunted wild ox, ostrich, oryx, mouflon and gazelle. Hunting in thickets of reeds is often depicted, along with the hunting of hippopotami in small boats. First they would shoot it in the head with a harpoon, and then as
Figure 9. -One of the first representations of a donkey. (f omb ofBeni-Hassan, XI Dynasty)
Figure 10. - Removing honeycombs from Hives. (f omb of Rekhmire at Thebes, XVIII Dynasty)

===== PAGE 192 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
175 Figure 1 1. - Quails caught in nets in cornfields. (Old Kingdom) the beast turned round with its mouth open, they would kill it with a lance.
Fishing was done with nets, hoop nets, harpoons and fish spears, all of which have been in existence since the Old Kingdom and have hardly changed. Of course, this type of fishing was done only in rivers or lakes. Two boats were necessary when using hoop nets, as one can see in the Ti mastabah.
The production of metals in Ancient Egypt poses several problems, not all of which have been resolved. Or, to put it more accurately, all kinds of interpretations have been put forward, some of them somew hat thoughtlessly. Although there was an abundance of certain depos its, like the iron deposits in the Aswan region, for example, Egypt was not rich in any of the other metals, except for some copper mines in
Sinai. The Sinai mines were most probably developed around 2700 BC, but we know nothing about Egyptian mining techniques. We have found no trace of exploitation, nor any figurative representations.
Some think that they used pillars, while their solution to the problem of lighting remains a mystery.
We are uncertain about the chronology of metals. Gold, silver and lead were already in existence during the predynastic period, while

===== PAGE 193 =====

176
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
copper appeared during the Badarian period. The Old Kingdom has bequeathed us several tools made of copper (scissors, cold chisels and nails). Metal was hardened by hammering. Bronze was in common use by 1 160 BC, and probably came from abroad: it was known in Ur by
3500-3200 BC. There has been a great deal of discussion about iron.
Some people have spoken of ancient iron beads, but in fact this is probably meteoric iron. Others believe that iron first appeared during the VI Dynasty (2423-2263 BC) , or the XII Dynasty (2000-1785 BC) .
However, it is generally accepted today that iron was commonly used by the XXV Dynasty, despite the discovery of an iron dagger in
Tutankhamun's tomb, which was most probably imported.
The processes used to reduce minerals were probably the same for all of Europe and the Near East in the same period. It is impossible to say more, since no metallurgical furnace has been discovered in Egypt.
However, we possess some pictures depicting the working of metal.
The main difficulty appears to have been heating the metal to a sufficiently high temperature to be able to work it. This raises the problem of the bellows used, of which there were three types. There was probably no evolution involved here, since each one was adapted for a particular operation (fig. 12). The first of these was the blowpipe, which was most likely used for precision work (it is depicted on a XVIII
Dynasty tomb in relation to the making of enamel). Then there is the sarbacane, the first represent΍tion of which is found in the Mereuka mastabah (around 2400 BC): here it is being used to make jewelry.
Finally, there are the goatskin foot bellows (Rekhmara's tomb, XVIII Dynasty). The heel is used to keep the central opening of the diaph ragm closed during compression. The diaphragm is raised with a rope, with the foot at the point of the bellows, and the heel raised to keep the opening free.
Anyone who has seen the Egyptian monuments, at least those built after the III Dynasty, is quite justified in thinking that the Egyptians were skilled quarriers. Some of these quarries are still in existence, and furthermore, in quite good condition, which is hardly surprising sincesubsequent generations were content to dismember the ancient monuments to get the stone they needed for new buildings. There are quarries near Memphis and Aswan, the pink granite found in the latter region being used in abundance.
The techniques employed to cut stone are virtually unknown.
Limestone was not difficult to quarry, but it was completely otherwise

===== PAGE 194 =====

lHE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS 177
A
B
Fisure 1 2. - Blowpipe IAI, Sarbacane 181 and Goatskin bellows jq.

===== PAGE 195 =====

178
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
with granite and diorite. Large constructions were made with granite and limestone, while diorite was hardly used at all, except for sculpting.
The main difficulty was to detach the stone in regular blocks and the unfinished obelisk in the Aswan quarry reveals that the pieces were prepared as much as possible in order to avoid needless transportation.
Firstly, the layer of dust was cleared away, then a surface was prepared with diorite. The quarriers would then trace in the rock the desired shape of the blocks and mark the traces with a series of long, narrow holes. They then placed in these holes dry wooden wedges which were generally made of sycamore, after which they moistened the wood.
This would swell up in a uniform fashion along the same line, and thus split the stone according to the desired shape. There were some failures of which there is still some evidence.
The Artisan Class We are quite well informed about the artisan class, thanks to the existence of many paintings and engravings, and also the valuable miniature models depicting various scenes found in Meke tre's tomb in Deir-el-Bahari, and which date back to the XI Dynasty
(2160-2000 BC). Gold and silversmiths, sculptors, potters, carpenters, cobblers, bakers and brewers are among those most often represented.
Despite this, there is no general work about the various trades of Ancient Egypt.
It is curious to note that the objects dating from this period reveal remarkable technical skill, while the tools used seem to have been fairly basic, and developed only very slowly. This list of such tools is short, and particularly in the case of the woodworkers, whose products nonetheless show that the Egyptians were skillful cabinet makers.
Their tool kit (fig. 13) consisted mainly of the adze, used for trimming, and scissors and hammers, the bow drill, and the hand saw, which was invented during this period. The work bench, the vice and the nail were unknown. Despite this, however, Tutankhamun's thrones are finely sculpted, and are decorated with beautiful marquetry. They are assembled with mortice and tenon joints.
Leather was extremely important in Ancient Egypt. We have plenty of pictures showing the making of leather sandals and belts, but we know very little about how the skins were prepared. Tanin was produced from acacia pods, and chamois were made with oil which was pressed into the skins with a hammer, after which the leather was

===== PAGE 196 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
179 Figure 1 3. - Egyptian woodworker drilling with bow [abovej; hand saw, shears (Cutters) and hammer [belowj.
stretched to make it soft. Tawing was done with alum. The oldest depiction of this industry comes from the tomb of Ti (c. 2500 BC). The curriers are pictured stretching the skins. Leather belt makers can also be seen on Rekhmire's tomb (XVIII Dynasty). One of the workers is

===== PAGE 197 =====

180
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
cutting out round thongs from a large skin with a leather knife which closely resembles the same tool used today. Sandal makers also used the leather knife and an awl which made the holes for the straps. In this case, too, the number of tools is limited (fig. 14).
Weaving is a most ancient craft. Yet we possess only limited sources, and these date from the Middle Kingdom with the paintings by Beni
Hasan and the model of Meketre's tomb. The most frequently used fibre was linen, no doubt cotton was introduced only much later.
Spindles and distaffs were used for spinning, and the threads of the warp were prepared according to an ancient method using the surface of a wall, with dowels. In the model, the very elementary weaving
Figure 14. - Making sandals [above] and making leather straps in Ancient Egypt (below].

===== PAGE 198 =====

TIIE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
181 loom is placed flat. There was no comb, and the threads were tightened by use of a stick. From the time of the Middle Kingdom, large, upright weaving looms were used, and these were no doubt a fairly recent development. The remains of Djer were wrapped in material which had 60 threads on the warp, and 48 on the weft, which was already enough to produce a beautiful piece of fabric. Materials have been found dating back to the XVIII Dynasty which were 138 x 40 and 128 x
56. From this period onwards, the Egyptians became past masters in the art of cloth-making. The cloths were often dyed, using primary colours.
Pottery became important during the Old Kingdom, and was prac tised alongside the making of stone vases. Alabaster and schists were easily worked, but this was not so with diorite or granite. There are some pictures of workers making stone vases, but drills with weights on their upper end, chisels and hammers are not sufficient to enable us to understand exactly how large pieces were made. Terra cotta vases were made using a potter's wheel (fig. 15). Egyptian pottery were very ordinary, and there is no evidence that they used kilns. However, enamelling was very popular for making small figurines, and was done using silica and soda, with a copper-based colourant. These figurines were made using moulds, which explains their abundance. The oldest dated piece of glass is an imitation turquoise in the shape of a blue glass eye which was made during the reign of Amenophis I (1558-1530 BC).
The use of glass became popular after this, but glass-blowing was never practised by the Egyptians.
Figure 15. - Potters working with hand-driven wheels.

===== PAGE 199 =====

182
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The jewels in our museums, and Tutankhamun's treasure are more than sufficient evidence of the mastery of Egyptian jewellers when working with metals and stones. The paintings of jewellers at work in
Mereruka's mastabah in Sakkara ( c. 2400 BC) show that they too had a limited number of tools, without counting the blowpipes used for melting gold. The method used to make certain types of beads of stone is still a mystery for the historian of techniques.
The study of the ancient Egyptian artisans' techniques still remains to be done. It would be helpful, too, to have precise lists of tools, like the one compiled by Bliimner for classical Antiquity. And this should be accompanied by precise research into the objects which we do possess: the study of certain types of woodwork and certain cloths would be just as revealing as that of tomb paintings. But at this point a problem arises which is rarely discussed: the relationships between manual skills which can be passed on through apprenticeship, and the paucity of tools, and all this combined with an advanced knowledge of the materials being used. Certainly, there have been some important
Figure 16. - The appearance of the hammer with handle.

===== PAGE 200 =====

TIIE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
183 stages: the change from stone implements, which were used during the very first dynasties, to metal ones is a patent example (fig. 16). As for working with stone, it seems that they used strikers made of diorite, and abrasives - usually a quartz powder - were used for sawing and polishing. Finally, metal chisels were introduced. However, the drilling of carnelian pearls still remains a mystery.
Egyptian artisans can be credited with some spectacular achieve ments, but they also neglected certain areas: one of these was pottery, which was very badly fired. It is true that a civilization will achieve more in one field than it will in another. And one must not confuse the exceptional results achieved by a few, for a few, with everyday techniques, which in a sense are the yardstick of a technical system.
Construction The architectural achievements of the ancient Egyp tians still cause wonder and surprise, and one cannot deny that the pyramids at Sakkara and Gizeh, and the great temples of Karnak and
Luxor still arouse our admiration. But we should remember that we have seen only those buildings which were exceptional: temples and funeral monuments. Almost nothing remains of the palaces which must have existed, and absolutely nothing of ordinary architecture.
This is an important point, which tends to prove that as in Greece - but unlike Rome a,d the Roman Empire - monumental architecture was quite different to common architecture. This becomes more meaning ful when one thinks that we are familiar with the houses of <;atal
Hiiyiik and Jericho built during the neolithic period, but nothing is left of those in Thebes or Memphis, except a few famous remains. This is because these towns were built using clay bricks, an extremely perisha ble material. This is a whole side of construction techniques which eludes us. Ordinary construction, then, was done with clay bricks, and this was so for all villages and most towns. There are paintings which show these bricks being made, usually with a mixture of sand and straw to prevent shrinkage. They were sometimes moulded, but in any case, they were made according to standard sizes, and then dried in the sun.
This material was entirely suitable in a hot country such as this with very little rain. However, it could not escape the erosion of time, and the dwellings were constantly having to be rebuilt. This explains why nothing is left of these lightweight constructions.
The large stone constructions were an entirely different matter. The Egyptians built their heavy constructions in the same way as their

===== PAGE 201 =====

184
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
clay-brick houses. Just as the bricks had to be piled up, so did the heavy elements of the stone monuments. They had to take a great deal more care over the foundations, however.
The stone was assembled using dry joints; mortar was never used by the ancient Egyptians. The building of uneven foundations, which strengthened the solidity of the whole, was widely practiced from the
Old to the Middle Kingdom. It seems that dovetails with bronze reinforcements were used in Abydos during the XIX Dynasty (13141200 BC), and subsequently became very popular.
One problem which has aroused a great deal of curiosity and polemic concerns how they managed to lift giant slabs of stone, sometimes weighing several tons, to considerable heights. The dimen sions of the pyramids are well-known. The largest obelisk in Egypt is that of Queen Hatshepsut's in Karnak: it is 29.50 metres high and weighs 374 tons. The unfinished obelisk from the Aswan quarry would have measured 41. 75 metres high and weighed about 1200 tons. Before the Macedonian conquest, the Egyptians had no form of lifting apparatus so they used two types of simple machines which were the oldest known: the lever and the ramp (fig. 17). For transport, they used sledges and rollers (fig. 18). These great monuments, still standing today, were built with very primitive materials therefore, and using only the driving force of humans and animals.
In fact, the Egyptians must have embedded the constructjon into the sand as it got higher (the advancing desert embedding everything in its wake must have given them the idea for this), thus creating ramps on which they could mount the pieces into place. Obelisks were erected in the same fashion. Once the work was finished, the monument was levelled out. There does not seem to be any other satisfactory expla nation.
All this naturally implies long periods of time. The pyramid of Chephren was built in thitty years, and required a large work-force.
The temple at Edfu, built during the Ptolemaic period, took one hundred and eighty years to complete. Did time and the numbers of men compensate for the real lack of technological progress? It is almost certainly true that other factors were important as well. Fur thermore, we still have the same problems about the building of our great cathedrals.
The pyramids unleashed the imaginations of our contemporaries, who saw in them a reflection of all the esoteric sciences. Several

===== PAGE 202 =====

lHE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS 185 Air vents -.-.. - ',, General case; wall of clay bricks
Case of Queen Hatshepsut's obelisks; 4th pylon Figure 17. - Process of installing Queen Hatshepsut's obelisks, recommended by H.
Chevrier, architect of the Karnak monuments. (From "Batir': 84 April, 1959)

===== PAGE 203 =====
