TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 18. The sleigh.
corresponding stages have been identified within a fairly short, or at least, fairly limited period related to different construction techniques, even though our knowledge of them is still far from perfect. There are the step-pyramids, the first of which was built for King Zoser in
Saqqara (c. 2780 BC); the double-sided ("rhomboidal") pyramids of Medum and Dahchur (c. 2680 BC) ; the ordinary pyramids, like those of Gizeh and Abusir, and finally the smaller pyramids of Saqqara
(between 2650 BC and 2400 BC).
This evolution took place, therefore, over a relatively short period of three hundred years. There were no pyramids built before or after this time. It would be a delusion to think that there could have been a real technical revolution during this period, in such a short time. It is possible that geometry had become more precise, but any calculations must have been very elementary. A normal inclination was needed to avoid sliding, but this was hardly likely to happen with stone, and also to avoid too much cutting work, so that the stone could be easily hoisted. "These variations of the aspect," notes Serge Sauneron, "did they represent the evolution of ideas on pyramids, or were they simply the result of successive attempts by architects to find a more perfect structure?"
The temples were built according to very simple plans, which were repeated endlessly. Those of Karnak and Luxor are the most ancient, not including the restorations at Saqqara. But these two great monuments have been enlarged and transformed over several centu ries, beginning with the XVIII Dynasty. The somewhat unfortunate multiplication of columns can be explained by the weight of the slabs which formed the rooms' ceilings. Technically speaking, this archi tecture is simple, one could even say simplistic. The only real problem was putting the various elements in their place.

===== PAGE 204 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
187 The vault was known, but seldom used. The ribbed vault, made with mud bricks and without arches, on a mounting of oblique layers, dates back to the first Dynasty (3300-3000 BC). After the VI Dynasty, stone vaults with full arches demanded a more careful attention to form.
Corbelled vaults were also in use.
These were then, fairly crude architectural techniques which did not entail any great practical problems. This was grandeur without risks.
The temples of Deir el-Bahari, on the other hand, reveal a sense of balance and perspective verging on perfection.
Space . The organisation of space is dominated in Egypt by its geog raphy. Egypt is a long, narrow stretch of land with a river running through the centre. Major floods occur regularly, with various conse quences.
There was no need for any roads since the Nile was a natural route for transport. In any case, they would have been difficult to trace in the silt or the sand. Consequently, Egyptians did not use wagons, either.
The chariot was introduced much later on, but was used only for war.
The limited size of the oasis bordering the river meant that all large buildings were built near the water course. Sometimes, as in Aswan for example, canals were dug in order to reach the quarries.
Figure 19. - Construction of a boat using very small pieces of wood. (Middle Kingdom)

===== PAGE 205 =====

188
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Humans were usually used for transporting goods - pictures often show those great baskets suspended from a pole and carried on the shoulders of two men. This is how the harvests were usually brought in.
Animals, particularly donkeys, were also used for transport from the XII Dynasty. In other paintings we can see rollers and sledges being used to move large objects. The whole thing would be pulled either by men or by animals.
There were various types of small crafts used on the Nile. First of all, there was the small skiff used for hunting in marshy areas. There were also cargo boats, often depicted in later eras (fig. 19). They were of various sizes, but the prow and stern were always raised and the draught was slight. They carry a mast with a square sail, and are manoeuvred by two lateral rudders. They were powered either by sails or by oars. There is a painting from Deir el-Bahari depicting a large transport barge, of a more rounded form, carrying obelisks. It was most likely pulled by other ships (fig 20).
It is particularly difficult to reconstitute large pieces of work. There is a singular lack of paintings and written sources, and it is possible that some contemporary historians may have let their imaginations run away with them.
There were some towns in ancient Egypt, Memphis and Thebes being perhaps the most well-known. All of the buildings in these towns, except for the temples, were built using clay bricks with the result that they have almost completely disappeared. The only remaining dwellings today are from the temporary camps which housed those working on the temples or tombs, in Gizeh, Kahun or
Deir el-Bahari, for example. During the earliest times, the houses seem to have had elements which are still popular today: a reception room, a storage part and a private section. A chicane shaped entrance would open out on to the street. In Kahun, workers' dwellings had six to seven rooms (measuring about 100 square metres). Bigger houses with a large interior portico sometimes measured as much as 2400 square metres. The "workers town" of Tel el-Amarna was composed of four-room houses, all of them identical. There were some storeyed houses in the towns, probably because the land was almost certainly harder to come by and more expensive. We have pictures and models of these. The work was done on the ground floor, the reception rooms were on the first and the private section of the house was on the second floor with a terrace on the top. These working towns were built

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS

according to standard plans, but there were no plans for the large cities. Perimeter walls were very rare.
As both a water source and a natural route for transport, the Nile was a constant preoccupation of the political powers. They knew very little about it, where its source could be found, or why the floods took place. They were able to measure the floods, however. The first
"nilometers" were probably made of wood, like those depicted in the process of being made in a tomb (fig. 21). Later they used masonry structures, a type of stone shaft which reached up to the level of the water: they were able to determine the average level with markers or steps, and then the average or exceptional flood. Some of these shafts still remain, at Elephantine Island, at Aswan and near the temples of
Korn Ombo and Edfu, all buildings from the Ptolemaic period. These calculations made possible a certain amount of control work and also a basis for the assessment of taxes.
The Egyptians certainly hoped to regulate the floods, but the construction of dams similar to the modern dams at Aswan would have been unthinkable. However, it was possible to create diversions instead, at areas where the river was not bordered by cliffs. It is possible that Lake Karun (Moeres) was created in a depression with a similar aim in mind. It was supplied by a canal running first parallel with the Nile, then deviating towards the west. Two parallel works then linked it with the Nile downstream. At the same time, the oasis at
Figure 21. - Making a nilometer out of wood.

===== PAGE 208 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
191 Fayum was developed. It is difficult to say at which period these works were executed: the most ancient monuments of this region date back to the XII Dynasty (2000-1785 BC).
All the irrigation systems have completely disappeared. These were very lightweight works, and were probably remade after each flood. In the Nakht's tomb (around 1415 BC), workers can be seen in the process of digging a canal: one of them is cutting down a tree with an axe, while another is digging with a hoe. Like the Dutch, they had to raise the water from one level to another in order to use it. For this, they used two instruments, the shadouf, a very ancient implement, and the Archimedes' screw, which no doubt existed before the time of the famous scholar whose name it bears (fig. 22).
It was hardly likely that the Egyptians would not have thought of developing the Nile into a major channel of communication, but to actually do this was an entirely different matter. There has been a lot of discussion about the so-called canal of the two seas, the idea of which was taken up again in the 19th century, in competition with the Suez canal. A diversion was created downstream at Bubastis (Zagazig),
Figure 22. - A shaduf.

===== PAGE 209 =====

192
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
from the pelusic branch of the Nile, and finishing up at Ismailiya. From here, a canal was dug from the Bitter lakes to end up at what is now
Suez. It is claimed that its breadth was wide enough to take three triremes abreast. Some attribute this giant project to the VI Dynasty
(2423-2263 BC), but others, perhaps more accurately, date it at the end of the 6th century (around 520 BC).
There were no doubt many small utilitarian diversions, many of which were only used in case of flooding. We have already mentioned the Aswan quarries. The remains of a dam can still be seen at Helwan, dating back to the fourth millenium BC and which supplied water to the alabaster quarries. Some canals were built from the Nile to supply the towns and villages with water - as at Neuser-Re in Abussir, under the V Dynasty (2563-2423 BC) , for example. The piping was made of copper with plaster joints. At Tanis, pottery cones were used.
Research is still needed to complete the basic picture which we have just sketched: we can only hopes that someone will choose to do it soon.
It appears therefore, that the Egyptians did indeed possess a true coherent technical system, even if its means appear to have been limited. Some of their projects still arouse surprise, even if one considers their equivalents elsewhere (the pyramids in Mesopotamia and Mexico). The geographical conditions imposed limits which per haps blocked the system. The small part played by the wheel, which except for the potter's wheel was hardly used, certainly put the brake on some developments: mechanisation could only be found outside of
Egypt then, not only industrial mechanisation, but also the mechani sation of war, which was completely absent - even though it existed in the nearby Mesopotamian civilization, and was known to the Egyp tians.
Mesopotamia There are similarities and differences between Mesopotamia and Egypt, the former being no doubt apparent rather than real. Both regions are centred around large rivers. But bΎyond that, the climate, and the nature of the rivers and the soils are very different. There is less unity in the Tigris Basin and the Euphrates than there is in the Nile valley. Towards the south, Babylon is closely dependent upon the rivers whose flooding and irrigation are its life-source. Further north, a

===== PAGE 210 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
193 plain of low ground is crossed by several water-courses, each one of which is an economic entity in itself. In Assyria and Syria, rains from the west make agriculture possible without irrigation.
It would be impossible for two climates to be the same over such a long distance from north to south. Winter is mild in the southern regions in the Sumer countries, and this provides fertile ground for the date palm. In the Akkad countries in the north, however, winters are cold. Snow occasionaly falls in Assyria and Syria, and here the date palm naturally gives way to vines and fruit trees.
Natural resources are limited. Building stone is of a mediocre quality, and flints are rare. On the other hand, the abundance of minerals in Anatolia explains the early use of metal in this region.
It is only natural that these conditions should have influenced the evolution of techniques. It is also obvious that they imposed necessities of a different type than those which are found in Egypt. This is in spite of chronological coincidences which are largely the result of contact between the inhabitants of the Nile banks and those from the banks of the Tigris and the Euphrates.
A breaking off We have already spoken about certain sites which reveal an advanced Neolithic civilization, especially in the northern and western regions of this vast geographical entity. During the fourth millenium BC the entire country was able to profit from a fairly advanced technical civilization. The contribution of various different races - as in Egypt - must have enhanced the elaboration of a rapidly perfected technical system. The first stages are marked by a series of successive discoveries which appear to have displaced the centre of this civilization from the north towards the south. Jarmo has already been mentioned. The hamlet, or rather the superimposed hamlets, are situated on a spur above the valley of a tributary from the Tigris. It would seem that the beginnings of a farming industry led to the permanent settlement of part of the population. Grains of wheat and barley, millstones and sickle-teeth are in evidence, along with the bones of domestic animals (sheep or goats, cows, pigs and dogs). The houses are made of pressed clay, and pise, with reed floors. This site was no doubt formed by primitive farmers, and dates back to around
500 BC. The techniques used at this point were still simplistic.
The settlement at Hassuna, in Assyria on the western banks of the Tigris, marks a new stage of progress. A steady farming industry was

===== PAGE 211 =====

194
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
assured by two permanent water-courses and regular rain-fall. Some consider this to be the first farming industry in the world. Although there are almost no remains of any houses (fig. 23), large jars of coarse pottery have been found, mixed with chopped straw and imperfectly fired. Large hand-mills and tools which are generally agreed to be hoes, and the bones of large cattle, sheep and goats are evidence of farming and rearing. Slings were used for hunting, with bullets of baked clay. No arrowheads were used. Wood was almost certainly worked with axes and adzes. Subsequently, sedentary peasant villages were established near the first settlement, on six different levels. Only a few curvilinear walls remain of the oldest village. Level II already boasts rectangular houses. In villages III to V, the houses had three or four rooms, and were probably grouped around a court. The wooden doors were actually able to pivot on socketed stones. There were bread
Figure 23. - Isometric view of Hassuna 'standard' farm. (From Lloyd and Safar, Journal of near eastern Studies, 1 945)

===== PAGE 212 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
195 ovens and silos made of unbaked mud jars mixed with straw and externally coated with bitumen.
Farming tools were already growing more sophisticated. Their pottery consisted of a few different types of well-fired painted vases, decorated with motifs similar to those used in basketry. These small tribes imported obsidian and amethysts, which they knew how to perforate. They do not appear to have used any copper. Childe believes that the now well-established farming industry contributed to a population growth, which would explain the expansion of this civilization.
The pottery of level VI in Hassuna marks a break with the past. This new culture was christened "Halafian," after the Tel Halat site, and stretches from the Iranian hills to the east of the Tigris to the
Mediterranean. This stage was clearly more highly developed.
Emmer and barley were cultivated. They reared two types of cattle, sheep, goats and pigs, although hunting (still with slings) supplied much of their food. There is evidence of some form of a textile industry but we do not know if they used flax or wool. Copper had made its appearance in the form of small beads which probably came from local deposits. Obsidian and chert were still used to make knives and sickle-teeth, and adzes were still made using celts of polished stone mounted in knee-shafts.
An Halafian vase has led some to believe that the wheeled-vehicles existed at this time, but this interpretation is very unlikely to be correct. The Halafians had not heard of the saw, which is essential for wheel-making, and they had not even heard of the potter's wheel. In fact, we do not know of any spoked wheels dating back further than
2000 BC.
The paste used in pottery was very delicate and covered with a layer of slip, which was sometimes polished. It was already possible to heat the kilns to quite considerable temperatures, sometimes as high as
1200 degrees.
Stone-working was still excellent, even with the hardest of stones.
Vases, pearls and even amulets provide evidence of this.
If one accepts that a public highway is evidence of urbanisation, then it should be said that the roads in Arpachiya were paved with shingle.
Furthermore, there were constructions which have been called tholoi, from which primitive temples were made, or lofts, or shops, but which all signify the life of an organised collective. The Halafian civilization,

===== PAGE 213 =====

196
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
which was also fairly widespread in all this region, was supplanted by the sumerian civilization of al'Ubaid, on the delta site. The original
Sumerians, according to Childe, must have come from an older country, perhaps from the steppes in the North-West, or from the mountains in the East. Here, urials, mouftons and goats ran wild, and cereals grew naturally. In any case, this delta country was extraordina rily fertile, and was re-fertilised each year by alluvium deposits. The lagoons abounded in fish and prey of all kinds. This may have been a natural paradise, but it needed intensive work "and the organised cooperation of large human collectives." They had to create areas for cultivation, drain the marshes, and control the irrigation and flooding.
At the end of this chapter we shall consider the social and political conditions, also to be found in Egypt in this region, and which are perhaps at the basis of these technical innovations.
"It may be inferred that the Ubaid farmers of Sumer had devised a rural economy, based on irrigation, which was able to produce sufficient food to support a growing rural population, and also yielding a social surplus which could be used for non-productive works and trade."
There was no stone in the region, so it was necessary to maximise the resources they did have, which were essentially clay and reeds. Sickles were made with hard-baked clay but a new element was brought into construction, that of moulded clay, or the brick. The clay brick - destined for a long future - was first used in Eridu. Stone-tools were imported and were used in much the same way as during the Halafian period. Copper, which was cheaper, more durable, and could be sharpened, was hardly used. The imitations of metal tools made out of clay or stone are evidence that copper was known, however.
Pottery was hand-made, and the number of different types of pottery designs increased considerably. The surface was left unblazed but decorated with geometric designs using paint with a light sheen.
By now, the temple had become an extremely important factor in settlements. For these rural civilizations, which were based on a strict organisation of irrigation, the temple was not simply a religious sanctuary, but also a meeting-place. The insignificant remains and the lack of written sources prevents us from pursuing this point. "In any case, all known Ubaid settlements remain villages; their limited size and lack of functional differences tell us that these local groups of the population did not yet merit the name of cities. It was only during the
Uruk period that some of them were to achieve this distinction."

===== PAGE 214 =====

TIIE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
197 Archaeologists have defined the Uruk period by the appearance of unpainted red-slipped and grey pottery. The earlier stages of the U ruk civilization coincide with the final Ubaid period (c. 3900-3500 BC). It is thought that this new civilization was the result of people coming from the West or the North-West, who would have been Semites.
During this period, both technical and social structures were transfor med. Childe notes:
Even the earliest Uruk pottery, from Erech -IV and Eridu -V includes wheel-made vases. Admittedly, the potter's wheel was not only a significant discovery in itself, but it also implied the existence of a more articulated economy, the use of metal tools and therefore the existence of professional smiths, and is nearly always associated with wheeled vehicles.
In other words, a new technical system was evolving. And fur thermore:
The development of the Tigris-Euphrates delta would be calculated to produce just those social and economic conditions in which metallurgy and wheeled vehicles would be serviceable and under which the unit of settlement could and must be relatively large and the social surplus sufficient to the support of professional potters, smiths and other full-time specialists.
The growth in population was certainly a direct consequence of this.
The temple, a religious monument, a meeting place and a centre for stocking part of the farming produce, continued to evolve. At Eridu, the temples of ancient Uruk were made according to obeidian tradi tions, but on a larger scale. Temple -III was built on a base covered with limestone blocks. The first pillar temple with columns was built on level -IV B at Erech, the columns being made of brick. Some of the surface area was reinforced and decorated by a mosaic of baked clay cones hammered into the mud plaster when it was still soft.
The seals and tablets produced during the last part of the Uruk period are incised with conventional numerals and characters: these documents - the oldest written ones extant - represent the temple's bookkeeping. The numeration is sexagesimal, whilst the writing is pictographical and probably ideographical.
Civilization has taken there a great step forward. Writing was practised and conglomerations were being formed - Eridu, Erech, Ur,
Lagash, Ugair - which could almost be called towns. Temples were getting bigger. All this conveys an unprecendented degree of social

===== PAGE 215 =====

198
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
organisation and technical progress, two inextricably linked factors.
The plough - or more accurately speaking the swing-plough - appeared at this time, as did probably the metal scythe. Henceforth, farming was organised work. Cows provided milk, and goats were raised along with two types of sheep, one hairy, like the Egyptian sheep, and the other with curly fleece. Bows were used for hunting, and lances were tipped with metal points. Large and small rivers accomodated boats with high prow and stern, whilst sledges and wheeled carts were used on land. The traction animal most widely used was the ox, and it is possible that the Asiatic ass, the onager, had already been domesticated.
This group of advanced techniques supplied the necessary supplements to the ever-expanding group of craftsmen: there were already many workers of metal and leather, sculptors, carpenters and potters. They had learnt to alloy copper and lead, thus lowering the melting point of the former, and they could use wax casting. In contrast, tin bronze was unknown and most metals were imported from Anatolia. The art of pottery became more widespread and seems to have been industrialised during the later Uruk period.
We are now at the end of the fourht, and at the beginning of the third millenium BC. An entire technical system has thus evolved, a system which is coherent, with diverse and complementary activities. This technical system is very similar to that of Egypt during the thinite dynasties of the same epoque. The processes of development were identical, even if there were marked differences in detail. The clothes worn by lion-hunters were exactly the same, an Uruk ship was exactly the same as the "foreign" ships of the late Gerzean period. The jar with bent spout from the late Uruk period could almost be mistaken for the one depicted on the Narmer palette. Some characteristically Sumerian traits seem to indicate that in some respects, credit should go to that region.
Several hypotheses have been formed about this surprising synchro nism, even given the relative proximity of the two regions. The major factor seems to be the role played by Arab, Semitic peoples coming from the North of Arabia and spreading out to the west and east of their original territory, thereby transmitting to various areas discover ies made on the borders of the Nile, the Tigris and the Euphrates.
Some Mesopotamian techniques, like various forms of art for instance, would thus have been passed on to Egypt, while the bow and self coloured pottery would have come from the East to Sumer.

===== PAGE 216 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
199 We enter the historical period at around 300 BC. Unfortunately, as far as techniques are concerned, the historians of ancient Mesopota mia seem to be entirely unconcerned about establishing a strict chronology. As in the case of Egypt, not enough is made of the evolution of a technical system, which certainly did not remain fixed throughout the entire period and whose evolution was inevitable from the start. The technical system, in fact, had to be developed to its limits, and one can see this process beginning from the time of Jemdet
Nasr. Of course stone and baked clay were still largely used, but metal were becoming more and more popular, for making fish hooks, chisels, axes with handles, and copper, lead and silver vessels. They had also learnt to separate silver from lead. At the same time, Susa I A was still only a fairly densely populated village, but the materials in evidence
<;losely resemble those of Sumer. It was with Susa C during the late Uruk period, that a real civilization was born: writing and the estab lishment of proper towns bear witness to this fact. It is thought that the innovations of Susa B were due to the infiltration of elements which passed on identical techniques to Sumer during the Uruk period. Thus, it was between 300 and 1500 BC that the technical system was finally perfected, and once more this echoes the developments in Egypt.
Primary activities Agriculture held a place of prime importance in the economy at the beginning of the third millenium BC, which naturally made farming techniques very important. As with all types of agriculture, evolution was relatively slow, but it was progressive. Even the invasions and other upheavals suffered by these regions during this period do not seem to have made any great difference.
The plants grown were more or less the same as those cultivated during the advanced neolithic period, and those grown by the Egyp tians at the same time. Barley was the staple cereal, with emmer and millet. We shall not dwell on the point made by certain archaeologists, that Mesopotamia was the birthplace of cereals. It was only later that gardens were producing a complementary diet which was both more varied and superior to the leguminous plants produced in the neolithic period. We do have a list of these, a relatively late one dating only from the 8th century BC: garlic, onions, leeks, cabbages, lettuces, fennel, beet, rape and radishes. Several herbs were also grown : three types of mint, basil, saffron, coriander, rue, thyme, and pistachio. Persian ferrula was also popular and there were several varieties of poppy. The

===== PAGE 217 =====

20
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
present state of documentation on this period makes it very difficult to know from which date these various vegetables were first cultivated.
There is a similar lack of information about fruit trees. Vines were grown from the Sumerian period onwards. Fruit trees, the date palm first of all, became widespread rapidly in the more northern regions.
During the Babylonian period, virtually all fruits were grown: pome granates, medlars, apricots, peaches and figs.
A very few paintings depict farming implements. We saw that at the end of the archaic period, the swing plough seems to have comple mented the hoe which had long been in use. This presents a problem, which has not yet been resolved. In Egypt, the farmer used an ard;
here, on the other hand a characteristic tooth plough was used (fig. 24).
The difference is notable and deserves serious research. If the inter pretation is accurate, it was often surmounted by a seed coffer.
However, this addition seems to have been a later development (figs.
25 and 26). By the end of the Uruk period, the wooden sickle with small cutting stones had probably already given way to the sickle made of metal.
Figure 24. - An Ard, as depicted on an Uruk pictographical sign.
Figure 25. - A plough.
Figure 26. - A plough as depicted on a relief from Assarhadon.

===== PAGE 218 =====

TIIE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
201 Grain was usually kept in silos whose evolution was slow and limited. These techniques hardly differed from those of the archaic period.
Stock rearing rapidly developed: cattle, pigs, donkeys, goats and especially sheep were the first domestic animals. For a long time, since metal tools were lacking, the wool was ripped from the animal's back instead of being cut. From the Ubaid period, as illustrated in a famous bas-relief, milk, butter and cheese were being produced. Milking took place in cowsheds made of reeds. The milk was poured into a large jar, which was kept constantly moving, constituting the churn. The butter was filtered through a funnel which kept back all the lumps - these were then packed down into another jar.
Poultry quickly made their appearance: first geese and ducks, then much later the chicken.
It seems that hunting came to be practised largely by the ruling classes, for pleasure rather than for obtaining food. Hunting scenes are often represented, mainly that of lion hunting from the earlier periods.
Bows and lances were used. Boars and partridges were also hunted, and falcons were added to this list from the first millenium BC. It is also noteworthy that locusts were often eaten on brochettes.
Fishing was also widely practised, thanks to the many lagoons and canals. This was an important supplementary resource, and one tablet mentions eighteen different types of fish actually caught. Fishponds soon came into use. Rods and fish hooks (which were soon made of metal) were used for fishing, and also nets and harpoons for larger fish.
Mesopotamia had few resources of wood, so they were forced to import it. The same phenomenon was apparent in Egypt and we saw that it set limits to the development of certain techniques. The most important tree was the date palm, for its wood and fibres which were used for making rope, and for the fruit-stones which were used for fuel or crushed to make animal fodder.
There were no metalliferous deposits anywhere in this region.
Metals came either from the rich mines of Anatolia, or from the Persian Gulf. Copper appeared at the end of the Ubaid period, but was still rare. Mirrors were found in Susa made of plaques of polished copper, along with axes and other instruments made of the same metal. Analyses of the objects coming from the Susa necropolis reveal
92. 12% pure metal, with no iron, sulphur, lead, zinc or manganese, but with traces of nickel. So at the dawn of the third millenium BC,

===== PAGE 219 =====

202
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
copper was still used as a pure metal. It came from Oman, the Iranian plateau, and Anatolia.
Real bronze, which was not simply an alloy of lead and copper, appeared only during the third millenium. This was tin bronze or antimony bronze. A tablet from the III Ur Dynasty gives the compo sition of bronze as the following: 80:05% lead, and 5.84% antimony and the rest is unidentified.
The appearance of iron will no doubt remain a mystery for a long time. Here and there iron objects have been found which date back to declining periods, but these finds are isolated. In a predynastic tomb in
El Gerzeh in Egypt, some elements of iron oxide were found in a necklace, and some tools of soft iron in the Cheops pyramid. In
Mesopotamia, from around 2700 BC, which is about the same time, some fragments of iron were found at Tell-Asmar, and among some objects found from the 15th century BC was the handle of a bronze dagger which still contained some particles of the iron blade. Opinions are divided as to whether this was iron from deposits or meteoric iron, but the dagger's blade found at Khafaje was smelted from terrestrial minerals. Some, like Childe, believe it was imported, probably from
Armenia, where later events point to the fact that a primitive tribe had found an economic process for reducing the minerals which they managed to keep secret until the end of the second millenium BC.
It is almost certain that as in Egypt, iron was not an innovation of these two ancient civilisations, and furthermore, Egypt had some deposits which were totally lacking in Mesopotamia. It is,supposed that the Hittites supplied most of the iron. In the 18th century BC, iron was found in places, but still in relatively small quantities. It was only at the end of the 12th century BC that its use became more widespread.
We have seen that the real age of iron came even later in Egypt. It seems that iron was used originally for producing weapons, while most tools were still made using copper and bronze.
One material was adopted quite rapidly by the Mesopotamians, and that was glass. It was probably around the beginning of the second millenium BC that these peoples discovered not only ordinary glass, but also coloured glass.
In the field of primary activities, then, it seems that the Mesopota mians were somewhat in advance of the Egyptian civilization, espe cially where the production of materials was concerned. The inhabi tants of the Nile valley had the advantage of much larger, and more

===== PAGE 220 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
203 varied natural resources. The Mesopotamians depended largely on supplies from elsewhere, and there were periods when they lacked certain materials. At the very end of the Archaic Dynasty II, the
Sumerian copper workers were using bronze which contained 6 to 10% tin - we do not know where this tin came from. There were periods when tin was completely lacking.
Secondary activities In a general sense, we have very little infor mation about the techniques of Mesopotamia's secondary sector. We have hardly any written sources, and figurative representations, like those found in Egypt, are rare. And it is difficult to define the methods of fabrication simply using those objects which can still be found today.
This lack of information means that the following discussion will only skim the surface of the issue, and this all the more so because archaeologists have hardly dealt with these problems.
Textiles are usually dealt with first in these discussions, and this one is no exception. The main textile fibres were used successively, the first of which, wool, dominated the industry for a long time. For a long period, the wool was torn from the sheep and not sheared, which required large herds. The wool was first sheared at the beginning of the first millenium BC, when iron was finally introduced into the country.
Bleaching the wool - even though they knew how to marry white with black wools - was done with soap of various kinds (soda, potassium from ashes, alum and resin).
Linen was produced relatively early in Palestine. Once the wool had been torn off, it was dried in the sun on the roof of houses. After the retting, it was dried out again in kilns, after which it was hammered down and combed. In Mesopotamia, flax was unable to compete with wool. We should also mention hemp here, and cotton was also cultivated from time to time. Around 700 BC, Sennacherib attempted to increase the amounts grown in Assyria, with plants probably from the Indus valley. The fall of Nineveh in 612 BC halted this expansion.
The oldest textiles known to us are those discovered at Susa, which date back to the end of the Ubaid period. These materials range from untreated fabric, like swaddling cloth, to delicate cambrics. They were made of flax, with threads twisted at both ends for the weft. The instruments used produced poor results. There is one representation of a spinner from Susa, who as to be expected, is using a spindle and a distaff. One seal depicts a workshop for weaving (fig. 27) . The cloth,

===== PAGE 221 =====

204
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 27. - Scene depicting weaving. (From Contenau, la Civilisation d'Assur et de Baby/one, Paris, 1 937) half-completed, is stretched by two people probably on a horizontal loom. In Palestine, the vertical loom was adopted quite early. The shuttle appears only in a later document (Job, VII, 6).
Originally, clothes were almost certainly made using sheepskin. The designs used were then imitated using wool, then draping, which was finished with embroidery. During the first millenium BC, draping was abandoned in favour of the long tunic. Working men wore short tunics with a belt. Embroidery was very popular with the upper classes.
These cloths were probably dyed during the later periods. The primary colours were provided by indigo, saffron and cochineal, and other colours using mixtures of these. Carpets were probably used during the Assyrian period, evidenced by the step of a door from the palace of Khorsabad (8th century BC), which has a sculpture of an imitation carpet with fringes. It is not impossible that the Egyptian textile industry was, from a certain time onwards, technically superior to that of Mesopotamia.
Like the Egyptians, the Mesopotamians used leather a great deal, but its use was gradually limited to make shoes and military equip ment. The techniques used were probably identical to those used in
Egypt, but since there are no figurative representation of techniques using leather this cannot be proved. The skin, closed like a sack, was immersed in a tank filled with tanin. It was used to make shields,

===== PAGE 222 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
205 scabbards, quivers, baldrics and girths. But the Mesopotamians went further and used it to make waterbags, sacks and parasols. Even door-hinges and boats were made of sewn leather. The growth o(
Assyrian power prompted the substitution of leather protection for coats of mail and metal helmets.
The Mesopotamians were certainly not as skilled wood workers as the Egyptians. There is a major reason for this: the lack of raw material since the use of the date palm was restricted. This is evidenced by contracts for selling property, which reveal that vendors used to take all their precious wooden fencing with them. Probably the only construction to have frameworks made of wood were public buildings. Woodworkers and cabinet-makers - the number of whom was surpisingly high - made these frames, and handles for tools, weapons, and chests, which were perhaps the only type of furniture known.
We noted above that the Sumerians used fossilised sea-shells from the lagoons for glyptography and inlaying. These were of an ivory shade. Later, actual ivory was used: elephants could still be found in
Syria in the second half of the Second millenium. A few years ago, some interesting specimens of ivory-work were found in Assyria, Syria and Palestine.
Like everywhere else, fire was used a great deal in varied techniques which required different sorts of kilns. It seems that techniques using fire rapidly developed and kilns were built to reach temperatures of up to 1200°C. Fire was used primarily for preparing food and drink, and among the latter was beer, which was also popular in Egypt. Fermen ted barley, and very rarely, wheat were used, the grains of which were contained in large jars kept at a warm temperature. The grain was then dried in the sun and roasted in kilns to stop fermentation and conserve the malt sugar. After this, it was ground with a pestle and the husk was removed using riddles. Kept in jars, the malt was cooked in rounds with spices. From Ur III, it seems that pulp was preferred. The malt bread was soaked in water, the fermentation being carefully control led. Several types of yeast were used. The basic tool kit consisted of stone mortar boards, kilns and jars. Pottery, an ancient art, was still being perfected, and the appearance of the wheel from the fourth millenium BC which succeeded the lathe was a major step forward. We still have a painting of a potter's kiln on a Susa cylinder. It has an elongated form and has two low domes at both ends which maximise

===== PAGE 223 =====

206
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
the amount of heat retained. The humidity produced by firing escapes through two rows of ventilation holes. Other types of circular kiln were also used. Charcoal was obtained from the date palm, from undergrowth, dried scrub and date-stones.
Brick-making slowly developed. The rectangular bricks in the shape of tiles used in Uruk constructions were eventually replaced by small, piano-convex bricks of which one side was flat and the other "cushion shaped. " Originally, these bricks were mud bricks, dried in the sun in wooden moulds. Little by little they were relegated to the role of filling, and the facings were made of fired-brick and enamelled from the 15th century BC onwards. The total lack of stone forced Mesopo tamian civilizations to use brick alone while the Egyptians used an infinitely more solid material, at least for building their great monuments. Before the introduction of fired and enamelled brick, it is obvious that rebuilding must have been very frequent, even in a country where atmospheric precipitation was rare, which is the case in the southern regions.
Although the Mesopotamians had very few minerals, they nev ertheless worked much with metal. There were many blacksmiths, silver and goldsmiths, and the objects which remain for our scrutiny testify that they were extremely skilled craftsmen. Once again, we know little about the techniques employed: we know of no well preserved metallurgic furnace, or of the techniques used for moulding and casting, and little of the processes for forging the metal. The
Mesopotamians cast the metal in moulds, then using a file and chisel.
They also developed a method for stamping metal. For metal statuary, they used the methods employed by the Egyptians where thin sheets of metal were fixed with nails to a wooden model.
The tools used by the Mesopotamians were as limited as those used by the Egyptians during the same period, so the following list is relatively short:
Flat chisels with a pointed heel; saws with a tang projecting from the middle of the butt; small pliers formed of two metal bands soldered together; harpoons or hooks with two or three barbs; axes for hewing inclined towards the base relative to the socket; straight adzes or broad axes; oval daggers reinforced with a median rib; crescentic axes with scalloped backs; spear-heads with a projecting heel, the blade of which is leaf or poker shaped with an octagonal base from which a quadrangular tang tapers so that it can be inserted into a reed handle;

===== PAGE 224 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
207 double-barbed arrow-heads; single-barbed spear-heads; arrow and javelin heels, forked and fixed in a reed shaft.
These are quite limited tools which, like weapons, evolved very little despite the transition from one metal to another.
As was the case in Egypt, machines were virtually unknown in Mesopotamia. We should point out, however, that rams were used in military concerns, ancient representations of which are available. The lance and bow, together with the chariot, which will be discussed later, were the basic weapons.
Space Here again, we encounter the same problems, and apart from a few exceptions, the same solutions which were developed by the
Egyptians were practised by the Mesopotamians.
Transport was an absolute necessity, particularly for resupplying certain materials which were not available in the region. Land trans port followed fairly stable routes. Pack animals were undoubtedly widely used for carrying loads, as they were in the West up until the
Middle Ages.
Historians do not entirely agree on the date at which the first vehicle appeared, generally known as wagons or chariots. The existence of the wagon from the fourth millennium BC onwards is attested by archaic tablets from Uruq (level VI) , the semi-pictorial system of which illustrates a certain number of recognizable objects (fig. 29).
According to Paul Garelli, one of these might represent a four wheeled wagon, obviously derived from a sort of sledge. The military use of the chariot from the years 2800 BC is only attested by a vase painting from Hafagi (fig. 30) . Elena Cassin believes that the Vautours stele and the so-called Standard of Ur mosaic dating around the third millennium BC, show four-wheeled chariots with extensive bodywork, but the wheels are still solid. Moreover, in certain tombs at Ur and
Kisch, of the same epoch, illustrations of four-wheeled wagons pulled by oxen, donkeys and onagers can be found.
Thus an evolution has taken place, a branch of which leads to the development of the chariot of war. The first modification was made to the wheels. They were hollowed out to form four spokes. Acccording to Garelli, the Mesopotamians followed the Mitanian example by adopting the six or eight spoked-wheel, considerably reinforcing it from the 15th century BC. The chassis was also appreciably light ened.

===== PAGE 226 =====

209 Figure 29. - Chariot from a pictorial sign from tablets at Uruk.
Figure 30. - A military chariot. Funeral vase in scarlet pottery originating from Hafagi (British Museum)

===== PAGE 227 =====

210
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
But above all, and this fact is complementary to technical changes, the use of the horse most certainly revolutionized transport conditions.
The horse seems to have been used from early Antiquity in countries where it had been domesticated. Georges Contenau believes that it was gradually introduced into Mesopotamia, but that it was probably the Hyksos invasion, which had introduced the chariot of war led by teams of horses, that generalized the employment of the horse in countries which had only just begun to make use of it. The horse had occasionally been used around 2800 BC, is cited positively around 2000
BC, and was widely used from the 17th century BC onwards.
According to Amas Salonen, the quadriga first appeared between the end of the 9th century BC and the beginning of the 7th century BC.
The earliest representation of this is to be found on a fresco from Till Barsif, dating from the years 744/727 BC. The Sargonids had indeed created a chariot, which was heavier, larger and taller, with eight spoked wheels, designed to carry a mounted infantry.
There were many types of boats and we possess numerous pictorial representations of them. The most simple type was the raft, made of tree trunks, firmly secured together. Buoyancy may have been increased by additional supports. The "kelek" was also a raft fitted with goatskins. The most remarkable illustrations available of this vessel date from around 700 BC. The papyrus boat was similarly witnessed in Lower Mesopotamia. The "quffa" is a round vessel, constructed of a wooden frame over which sewn skins were stretched:
it has been compared to a large basket. It could only be used to travel downstream: the wood was sold on arrival and the skins were folded up and carried back. All these boats were powered by oarsmen, as they had no mast or sail.
Some images, however, portray warships with a mast and sail, but also using two rows of oarsmen. The prow was raised in the form of an animal's head or lowered like a spur. A bas-relief representing the
Sennacherib fleet in the Persian Gulf shows this sort of vessel. It may be that the Mesopotamians enlisted Phenician workers, whose boat building skills were famous, in order to create this advanced navy (fig.
31 to 35).
Of constructing, we only know that of palaces and temples. Houses were frequently built in mud-brick, but, as in Egypt, they have completely disappeared. The palace consisted of the cell type house, multiplied around a series of courtyards: Mari and Khorsabad are

===== PAGE 228 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
211 Figure 31. - Warship. (From Contenau) good examples of this. To avoid floods, these edifices were built on banked up and leveled terraces of land.
We have indicated the materials which were most frequently used, that is mud-brick. From the proto-historic period, bitumen mixed with clay was used as mortar, or as a putty and watertight covering. Treated in this way, bitumen was also widely employed for sea walls, quays, and in actual construction work, as well as for calking boats.
Roofs were terraced, but the short span of the palm trunks must be considered, as they dictated the construction of long, narrow rooms, and thick windowless walls to combat the materials' friability. The
Mesopotamians do not seem to have known about vaulting, but the absence of stone probably accounts for this. Contenau, however, cites some examples: a bas-relief portrays houses with mitre-shaped roofs
(fig. 36), and the rectangular King's chamber at Ur has an ellipsoidal vault. The sewer system of the palace at Khorsabad is composed of long, narrow corridors with corbelled vaulting, but this construction is much more recent, dating around the 8th century BC. The Sumerians used the column, which consisted of enormous square mud-brick pillars. The column, as we can figure it, is made of a stone socle and a wooden shaft. Here again, the absence of stone prevented the use of

===== PAGE 229 =====

212
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 3 2. - God of vegetation navigating a swamp where reeds are growing on board a boat known today as a belem. (From Contenau)
Figure 33. - Couffe type boat. (From Contenau)

===== PAGE 230 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
213 Figure 34. - Raft or kelek. (From Contenau) the column in Mesopotamian countries. In the Neo-Babylonian
Empire, fa<_;ades were covered in fired and enamelled bricks to pro duce reliefs of subjects.
The buildings which have attracted the most attention are the storied towers or "ziqqurat" which accompany temples, and are rather like the campaniles in Italy (fig. 37). Contenau has pointed out two types of tower. The Sumer type is composed of several superimposed terraces, quadrangular rather than square in shape, of unequal sizes.
On the last platform is a small temple. Steps at the side of the terraces allow climbing from one storey to the next. The ziqqurat at Ur is designed in this way, and dates back to the III Dynasty of Ur
(12th-1 1th centuries BC). The northern type of tower is examplified by the storied tower at Khorsabad. This has seven terraces, rather than five found in the Sumer ziqqurat, which are square in shape, and the top is reached by mounting a gentle ramp which runs around the edifice. The purpose of these edifices, prototypes of the Babel tower, and different both in conception and construction to the pyramids of
Ancient Egypt, has been extensively discussed. They represent an already advanced technology.

===== PAGE 231 =====

214
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Childe places the urban revolution of Mesopotamia in the third millennium. Standard plans were quickly used. In the square plan, perpendicular routes delimit the various quarters. But within these,

===== PAGE 232 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
215 Figure 36. - Roofs in Assyria.
free ranging imagination recovered its possession. A Babylonian tablet, dating from the Neo-Babylonian monarchy of the 6th-5th centuries BC, listed that the town had 103 temples, 900 chapels, and
180 altars dedicated to the goddess Hishtar.

===== PAGE 233 =====

216
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 3 7. - A Ziqqurat or step pyramid.
We know very little about the equipment of Mesopotamian towns.
Roads were only occasionally paved owing to the lack of stone. An aqueduct was found three hundred metres above a small valley, which supplied drinking water from the mountains to Nineveh, some fifty kilometres away. Stone slabs were cemented with bitumen to ensure they were watertight.
Towns were also fortified, which was a new idea in itself, for as we have seen fortification was rarely practised by the Egyptians. The surrounding walls were often doubled and they appears to have been flanked by many raised towers (fig. 38).
Agriculture naturally depended upon a water-supply. The Mesopo tamians used the same techniques as the Egyptians, with successive canals and "shadoofs," often built in batteries. All these constructions required close attention.
The technical systems of Mesopotamia and Egypt were thus close parallels, although there were most certainly reciprocal influences.
The differences between the two civilizations mainly stem from the natural conditions and probably from certain earlier traditions. Stone

===== PAGE 234 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
217 Figure 38. - Storming a town.
constructions are frequent in Egypt but rare in Mesopotamia. The use of metals, and the introduction of the wagon and the horse were certainly earlier on the banks of the Euphrates and the Tigris. Other techniques were first put into practice by the Mesopotamians. But the similarity of these two systems remains no less important. Until the
Macedonian conquest, Egypt and Mesopotamia existed on technical systems which were relatively advanced, rapidly established and slowly improved.
THE EXPANSION OF THE
NEW TECHNICAL SYSTEM
The technical system was definitively established in the region which stretches from the banks of the Nile to those of the Tigris and the
Euphrates, and after promising beginnings in Thessaly, Thrace, and Anatolia, it quite naturally spread to regions which had remained

===== PAGE 235 =====

218
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
technically backward. This technological transfer, as it is known today, obviously first affected closer regions, and then progressively reached more distant areas. Very little is known at present of the history of this diffusion, of its chronology or the nature of its form. Consequently, the table we have included is of necessity rather schematic and summary
(fig. 39).
Our ignorance of the expansion of a relatively advanced technical civilization is due to two main reasons. The first stems from the lack of documentation, particularly graphic documentation. In this respect, we are no longer in the realm of the riches of the Egyptian tombs. On the other hand, we are concerned with epochs and civilizations for whom art, in the general sense of the term, takes precedence over techniques, according to archaeologists and historians. Strictly speak ing we are entering the historical period where researchers, at least up until a few decades ago, were less concerned with purely material problems.
