Figure 39. - Diagram showing the diffusion of mesolithic then neolithic characteristics from the Near-East towards Europe. (From A. Leroi-Gourhan)

===== PAGE 236 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
219 The Near Regions These regions were obviously the first to be affected. Within these bordering areas were regions which were naturally destined to benefit rapidly from the technical progress of the Egyptians and the Mesopo tamians. To some extent these regions occupied an intermediary zone, which was true of both Syria and Palestine. In the Lower valleys of Ras
Shamra, Jericho and Elkhiam, agriculture developed very quickly, and towards the third millennium BC, groups of inhabitants gave birth to towns. Cereals were the basis of all food produce. Pottery appeared around 6000 BC in Northern Syria and at the same time in Thrace (Nea
Nikomedia), in Anatolia (<;atal Hiiyiil), and in Iran (Tepe Asiab).
Copper was produced in all these regions from approximately 5000 BC, and during the fourth millennium, the whole of the Near East had developed metal casting techniques.
When Mesopotamia swung towards a new technical civilization, at the end of the fourth millennium BC, all these regions, including
Egypt, benefitted almost immediately. The intermixing of peoples, moreover, facilitated this diffusion. Byblos and Ugarit had already reached a high level of technical culture.
Another problem confronts us, still more controversial and more difficult to explain. To the east of all these favoured regions, a long way to the east as the Lower valleys of the Indus and its tributaries are involved, a chronological synchronism exists with Egyptian and Meso potamian technical developments. It is true that these regions bear similar characteristics to Egypt and Babylonia, as they are based on alluvial plains upon which any sedentary agriculture relies on a natural or artificial irrigation system owing to the lack of sufficient rain. A coordinated, organized effort is required in some areas, which inevita bly leads to a remarkable cultural unity.
Urban civilization was already well under way during the third millennium BC, with developed walls and imposing citadels, protected by ramparts of mud-brick (Harappa and Mohenjo-daro). Capitals evolved, already housing both leaders and administrations, and serving as large grain depots. Towns were well built according to carefully studied plans and equipped with improved sewerage systems.
In the valleys, everything depended upon irrigated cultivation:
wheat was grown (Triticum compactum), barley, peas, sesame, and almost certainly rice, and dates were gathered. Among the animals

===== PAGE 237 =====

220
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
reared were Indinan hump-backed cattle, another breed of cattle without humps, buffalo, goats, and sheep, but poultry and elephants had also been domesticated. We are less certain whether pigs were domesticated at this stage.
Buildings were built in kiln-fired bricks. Often mud-bricks and fired-bricks were used alternately. Towers were frequently reinforced with beams. Stone was rare, but wood was relatively abundant.
The principal metals used by these civilizations were known to be gold and silver, lead and copper. Tin bronze was produced, as were alloys of copper and arsenic, with 3.4 to 4.4% for this last element. The peoples of the Indus knew how to cast iron using the wax casting method.
While the art of ceramics was more advanced in Sumer, weapons and tools were more primitive. On the one hand, there are slings and clubs, and on the other, there is an array of tools including flat chisels, enlarged and flattened towards the handle, flat axes with curved cutting edges, small saws, daggers, spear and arrow-heads, knives and razors, billhooks and sickles. The method of fitting handles onto all these tools differs from that used in Sumer. Carts and small boats are similar to those which were still in use in the country.
Childe's conclusions merit being reproduced in this discussion:
Enough has been said to show that India confronts Egypt and Babylonia by the third millennium BC with a thoroughly individual and independent civilization of her own, technically the peer of the rest. And plainly it is deeply rooted in Indian soil. The Indus civilization represents a very perfect adjustment of human life to a specific environment, that can only have resulted from years of patient effort. And it has endured; it is already specifically Indian and forms the basis of modern Indian culture. In architecture and industry, still more in dress and religion, Mohenjo-daro reveals features that have always been characteristic of historical India.
A technical civilization which thus remained independent. But how does one reconcile the developments of the technical civilizations of the East and West? Could it have been that for some the point of departure was situated on the borders of the Iranian plateau, for exchanges across this plateau would not have been difficult? This is where the process leading to material civilizations at a high level of development would have begun. The parallelism of these devel opments is undeniable, and similarities between certain products are striking. Cereals grew in the wild around the Iranian plateau, as did fruit trees, apricot, peach, and possibly the vine, and wild sheep

===== PAGE 238 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
221 wandered around there. The migrations from the valleys to the mountains turned this area into a region favourable for the "Neolithic revolution." While Persia remained without the written word until the first millennium BC, the civilizations of Western Iran had developed a mixed agricultural economy, which possessed sickles with flint teeth, had domesticated cattle and two breeds of sheep from which milk and wool could be used. They used adzes, hoes from shaped stone fragments, ceramics and metallurgy, and the potter's wheel, the same potter's wheel which was used in Mesopotamia x centuries before 3000 BC and in the Indus basin y centuries before
2500 BC.
Once again let us return to the words of Vere Gordon Childe:
The use of the potter's wheel or metal tools does not depend simply on technical knowledge. Mass production of fragile pots is only worthwhile when the numbers living together in the same village have reached a substantial, but as yet indeterminate, figure.
A community can only use metal tools when it is producing an effective social surplus. In each case the threshold is determined by economic and social factors - the efficiency of the rural economy and the concentration, or at least fluidity, of the social surplus.
Hand-made pots and stone tools may be indices of the smallness and poverty of a community rather than of its antiquity.
There we are dealing with the relations between a technical system and an economic, social and subsequently political system. But let us continue to study Childe:
In the second place, metallurgy and the manufacture of pots on the wheel and very likely wagon-building too, were crafts, exercised by full-time specialists. These were, at least economically, released from kinship bonds; their mysterious skills could earn them a livelihood wherever the requisite social surplus was available. After all, a normal farming village requires and can afford the services of only one or two smiths and potters. The younger children of such a village artisan had better seek employment and sustenance in some neighbouring settlement not yet provided. Accordingly at this stage the diffusion of ceramic techniques and even ceramic forms need not imply the migration of a whole people, but only the immigration of specialist potters.
Thus we may observe similarities in techniques and tools between the artisans of Mesopotamia and of the Indus basin.
Let us reconsider the dates above. Metallurgy and the use of the potter's wheel were both well-established in Mesopotamia during the
Uruk period, x centuries before 3000 BC. They reached the Indus basin during the Amri period y centuries before 2500 BC. In the case of pottery, "there is little evidence of divergent technical developments

===== PAGE 239 =====

222
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
in these two extreme regions. But the mounting of axes by a shaft hole through the head is, however, more than a local trick, and its supre macy has been demonstrated by its universal adoption." The fact that this process found its way to India only later in the third millenium BC even though metallurgy was already known, means that this process could not have been diffused along with the art of metallurgy itself.
Childe thus concludes that the technique of using the axe with handle "must be the result of a secondary diffusion eastwards. Sumer was not the birthplace of metallurgy, but simply a secondary centre where the shaft-hole axe was invented.
Between the "horns of the fertile crescent" were countries which were singularly well-placed for benefiting from the perfection of technical developments achieved as much by the Egyptians as the
Mesopotamians: this was especially so for Palestine and Syria. The civilizations of Ugarit and Byblos rapidly attained a higher level of techniques because of this. The potters wheel was being used in Byblos during the time of the first dynasty in Egypt and Byblos IV is surrounded by a thick wall, made of dressed sandstone blocks. The first temple was built during Byblos V, and a second during Byblos IV, around the time of the Egyptian II Dynasty. Metallurgy was estab lished after this, evidenced by silver ornaments and weapons of bronze.
In Palestine, neolithic Jericho was already a permanent settlement.
Stock rearing was practised.
The villagers built houses with walls of clay, plastered inside with lime and sometimes painted, with wooden posts helping to support the roofs. As wood-working tools polished celts of fine-grained rock were used besides flaked flint celts. Pottery was apparently unknown, but bowls were carved out of calcite and mortars from basalt and limestone blocks.
In the bronze age, fruit trees, vines and almond trees were cultivated along with wheat, barley millet and lentils. It was also at this time that the settlements grew into small walled towns: Jericho VII covered an area of 280 to 320 acres, and was encircled by a wall of mud bricks on a stone base. Ay and the Megiddo citadel had stone ramparts which were 6.5 and 4.5 metres thick.
Metal was known, but rare. Childe believes that metal work was done by itinerant smiths rather than resident professionals. Imple ments were usually made of stone, but there were copper adzes, and

===== PAGE 240 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
223 the latter was also used to make weapons, daggers, arrow-tips, and spears. Pottery was still largely hand-made during the old bronze age, but the wheel was used in some areas.
It is possible that some elements coming from Egypt during the early dynasties were transplanted in what was already an advanced autono mous civilization.
We must advance in time to examine the next stage. It was the first half of the second millenium BC that saw the flourishing of two civilizations already familiar with fairly advanced techniques. One of these, the Hittite civilization in Anatolia was slightly earlier than the second, the Cretan Minoans and the Mycenaean civilization from the
Greek world. These two civilizations were probably formed by the slow, progressive invasions of peoples who already had a technical history and who settled in regions which had already experienced the
"neolithic revolution." This represents an aggregate of civilizations therefore, who were also in contact with advanced techniques via
Egypt, Palestine and Syria.
The Hittites arrived in Anatolia at the beginning of the second millenium BC; they were the first historical Indo-Europeans. Their empire rapidly expanded with the seizure of Alep and Babylon at the end of the 19th century BC, and the Hyksos invasion of Egypt during the course of the 18th century BC. By the 9th century BC, the Hittite empire had disappeared. On arriving in Anatolia, the Hittites encoun tered the already advanced civilization of the Semite peoples. This is evidenced by the capadocian tablets, particularly those of Kultepe, which tell of advanced commercial exchanges. The Hittites adapted these practises to their own traditions, but they certainly borrowed a great deal from the peoples who preceded them.
The Hittites have been credited with bringing about two essential developments. It is well-known that metallurgy was one of the great riches of Asia Minor, but the innovation was the use of iron. It was used in the great Hittite empire during the 20th and 18th century BC.
Its use became widespread by 1 100 BC. Iron has been traced back to earlier civilizations, but these pieces were rare, often unique and almost certainly imported - we noticed the same for ancient Egypt. An iron amulet was discovered in Byblos, dating back to the 20th century
BC, and an iron ingot was found in a tomb in Knossos built at the end of the 19th century. It was the Hittites which transmitted this iron in appreciable quantities both to Egypt and Mesopotamia.

===== PAGE 241 =====

224
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The second major contribution was the horse. It is often difficult to date the arrival of the horse in ancient civilizations, but it is certain that the domestication of the donkey was centuries old. The four-wheeled chariot which figures on a Capadocian cylinder, seems to have been pulled by donkeys (fig. 40). The Sumerians designated the donkey with an ideogram, and when the horse was used eventually, it was desig nated by a periphrase, the mountain donkey , just as the camel was called the donkey from the South. The oldest royal tombs of Ur, which date back to 3000 BC, depict only donkeys. The earliest date at which there is unequivocal reference to the horse in Mesopotamia is only
2000 BC. Between this time, it seems that they used the hemione and no doubt also the onager, which came from Iran. At this time, the horse was becoming domesticated in Elam and Cappadocia. The
Hyksos invasion, the success of which was no doubt due to the fleets of chariots drawn by horses, was probably the first great expansion of the use of this animal. The Cappadocian horse will always be renowned in
Antiquity. A squire named Kikkuli, of the court of Boghaz-Keui in ancient Hattusa, wrote a treatise on horse training in the 14th century
BC: each day of this process, which lasted about six months is described in the smallest detail.
We have very little information about other techniques used by the Hittites. Judging by the amount of contact between them and Mesopo tamia and Egypt, the level of development was probably very similar.
The figurative representations which are most often of religious cere monies, do reveal some rare precise elements: bows and spears were used for hunting and in war, the two-horse chariot was the essential part of any invasion (the Egyptians were to profit from this.) Helmets with crests and greaves which are represented on the armour of the
Greek hop lite (armoured infantrymen) were found in Zendjirli, dating back to the New Empire.
We have virtually no information about the Hittite art of building or their organisation of space. The towns were planned in the same way as those in Mesopotamia and Syria. At Boghaz-Keui, the ramparts were made of earth with a stone facing, and in some places there was a double fac_;ade with an interior filling (fig. 41). The exterior towers no doubt served to consolidate the wall rather than to facilitate defence.
The gates are slightly different from those seen in areas near the banks of the Tigris and the Euphrates (fig. 42). The temples were probably decorated with wooden columns mounted on stone plinths.

===== PAGE 242 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
225 Figure 40. - Cappadocian cylinder from the de Clercq collection.
The facts are similar concerning the greek world, both on the continent and on the islands and in Crete, until the end of the
Mycenaean period. A more accurate contemporary knowledge of demographic movements indicates that the first people were Asiatic, followed by Inda-Europeans.
Farming was the main activity in this region, despite the marked development of trade, but farming techniques were among the most stable. The same plants cultivated were the same as those grown at the end of the Neolithic period : cereals and leguminous vegetables. Olive trees, vines, fig, pear and apple trees became widespread. The carob tree appears to have been imported from the Levant. Wine was probably introduced during the middle Minoan period in Crete, i.e . , at the beginning of the second millenium BC. They drank milk, beer, and infusions. The main fat used was oil. Stock rearing was also important, of long and short-horned beef cattle, sheep, goats and pigs. Dogs were added to this group later on.
The problem of the horse now seems to have been solved. The wagon with wheels finally appeared at the beginning of the second

===== PAGE 243 =====

226
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
HATT USA
Acropolis Figure 4 1 . - Plan of Boghaz-Keui. (From J. Garstang, The Hittite Empire, London, 1 929) millenium BC, and the horse was introduced with the chariot around
1400 to 1300 BC by the Acheans. All these innovations came from nearby Anatolia (fig. 43) .
Gold and copper were known from the Dimini period. Bronze first appeared at the end of the third and at the beginning of the second millenium BC. Greece possessed some of the rare stanniferous depos its which were found in the Near-East, near Kirrha, between Delphi and Corinth. Greece and its islands were also rich in mineral resources:

===== PAGE 244 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNIC.AL CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 42. - Zendjirli Gate. (From J. Garstang, The Hittite Empire, London, 1929) Figure 43. - Mycenian geometric chariots. (From Garlan, la Guerre dans l'Antiquite,
Paris, 1 972)

===== PAGE 245 =====

228
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
copper from Euboea, lead and silver from Laurion and gold, silver, copper and lead from the Cyclades. The populations of Asia minor came during the second half of the third millenium BC, bringing metallurgical techniques to the Cyclades and then towards continental
Greece. They appear to have established trading posts near the mining deposits, then a colonisation developed which reached as far as Crete by the early Minoan I period. Iron must have been known and worked from about 1200 BC. Metallurgy developed considerably after the middle of the second millenium BC, as much for everyday objects as for tools and weapons. The long bronze sword was perhaps a Myce naean innovation.
We know little about the techniques of the secondary sector, and the objects which we can examine reveal little about how they were made.
The development of pottery gradually phased out the stone vase. The potter's wheel was used from the first quarter of the second millenium
BC, coming either from Anatolia, or from Crete. The widespread use of the turning wheel followed closely.
We still have some elements of weaponry which reveal that materi als used for offensive and defensive purposes were getting progres sively lighter. In the 14th century BC, breastplates were made of linen and reinforced with metallic scales. The Dendra metallic breastplate was probably the first or one of the first of these types. At the end of the
13th century BC, linen and leather were used with metal scales to make coats of mail which were thus much lighter. Shields were also lighter, and were now circular. Bronze pointed spears, swords, daggers, slings and bows completed the military armoury.
Excavations have revealed the methods and systems of the construc tion and organisation of towns. The technique of building walls on a foundation of herring-bone masonry almost certainly came from
Anatolia. One can find buildings made in this way in Eutresis, and Beotia, as well as in Haghios Kosmas in Attica, or in Lerne in Pelopon nesia. This technique was used for a long time. Houses were smaller in the beginning of the Second millennium, and apsidal houses were first built at this time. No doubt these also came from Asia Minor- they can be seen in Troy - to the Cyclades first and then to continental Greece.
The most important development was the palatial revolution during the middle bronze period in Crete. It is certainly linked to a political revolution - the emergence of the first monarchies. We know little about those first palaces, those of Knossos, Mallia, and Phaistos,

===== PAGE 246 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
229 becauses they were constantly being rebuilt. They were erected on hillsides, the land being admirably suited to terracing. They were built around a vast central courtyard. The workshops and shops were situated on the ground floor, while the living rooms were on the first.
During this period, an aquaduct was built in Knossos to tap the water from fifteen kilometres away. The first half of the second millenium
BC was the great period for building palaces. The one at Knossos forms a rectangle of 150 by 100 metres, and as was customary, arranged around a courtyard with porticos (fig. 44).
We know very little about the towns, which were built using light materials so that they have almost completely disappeared. Exca vations in Gournia have revealed the absence of any actual plan for the town. The roads are winding, and the only element of any geometric symmetry is the palace around which they are centred (fig. 45).
Gournia was a town of craftsmen and potters. All Minoan cities in Crete were completely open, they therefore counted on their insularity and a powerful flotilla to protect them from invasion. Troy was fortified, but it appears to have suffered as much damage from earth tremors as from wars.
The only noticeable innovation was the tombs with cupola, which in Crete date back to 1600-1550 BC, and which rapidly became popular.
It seems that this was the only use made of the cupola (fig. 46).
The Mycenaeans did not make any significant changes to the techniques used in construction, but they did introduce a few inno vations, and they helped popularise the tombs with cupola. Greek territory was invaded by megaron style houses, enormous water tanks and agora porticos, but there were further changes. Palaces were being built very high up, and with the acropoles, they were the final part of an extremely well-developed system of defence. The outer walls were of a cyclopean type, with very few openings, and the town was dominated by the royal residential citadel. Houses had one or two floors, and were still built on stone bases. Wooden boards were inserted into the walls, which according to some made them more flexible - this would have been important in this area which was subject to earth tremors. The tiling was exceptional and the roofs were usually terraced. Water was supplied from wells or kept in water tanks. The walls were often decorated with paintings or frescoes.
We are prevented on several counts from giving an accurate assess ment of Greece in the bronze age. There is nothing like the wealth of

===== PAGE 247 =====

230
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
\ 40 I l l Figure 44. - Plan of the palace of Knossos. (From A. Evans, The Palace of Minos II,
London, 1 92 1 - 1 935) figurative representations from Egypt or the number of texts from Mesopotamia for the same period. To take but one example, we know nothing about ploughing implements used in Crete or during the
Mycenian empire. Our knowledge is very vague concerning metallur gical techniques. Only one thing is certain: in most cases, technical progress came from the outside, principaly from Asia Minor:
advanced metallurgical techniques and consequently the introduction

===== PAGE 248 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
231 f 40 Ill Figure 45. - Plan of Gournia. (From H. Boyd-Harves, Gournia, London, 1951) of iron, construction techniques, the horse and the chariot were actually all imported from Anatolia, and no doubt so were many more aspects of technology. This is a perfect example of the diffusion of a technically advanced civilization. And even when the Mycenaeans began to use the techniques of fortification, it is highly probably that they borrowed the ideas from Asia Minor. Hence, it is difficult to establish which innovations should be attributed to the people of

===== PAGE 249 =====

232
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 46. - Isometric view of "The Treasure of Atrea·: (From Hood, cited by Rachet, Archeologie de la Grece prehistorique, Verviers, 1969) ancient Greece: it seems that they are found more in art forms than in the techniques of production.
Distant Areas The problem of these distant areas is difficult to solve. There is often a break between prehistory and history which it is not easy to close.
There is often a lack of documentation and certain aspects, even in recent works, are rarely discussed: the development of agriculture, the emergence of the potter's wheel, and the domestication of animals are examples of this. It is also virtually impossible to make any compari-

===== PAGE 250 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
233 sons because of the lack of any chronological precision, even at the most basic level.
Certain reservations should be made clear from the start. The successive stages of progress made in the material world appear to have been a general rule: it was thus for the transition from the
Neolithic to the Chalcolithic period - which took place only in certain areas - and from thence to the bronze and iron ages. The speed of transition from one stage to another varied considerably, each region having its own evolutionary rhythm. The speed of progress seems to have depended largely on the state of natural resources. Where there were none of these, and before widespread trade enabled distant regions to benefit from the knowledge of advanced technical civili zations, the significant amount of borrowing seems to indicate that a knowledge of bronze could be acquired even where there was no local industry. Thus, in certain regions, stone implements were direct imitations of those made of bronze, even to the point of reproducing the flaws in the metal. In other sectors technical developments were reversed. Agriculture was known in the Near-East before pottery, but the whole of northern Eurasia was familiar with pottery some time before agriculture. These regions have a wealth of forests and water, where fishing and hunting were practised long before land cultivation.
It is difficult, therefore, to produce a synoptic picture of technical progress and the acquisition of new techniques. Population movements were often at the base of these technical transfers.
The Balkans, or rather the whole of south-east Europe no doubt soon benefited from the technical progress made in the eastern
Mediterranean. The development of south-east Europe was slightly behind that of south-west Asia but the difference is marginal. The lack of natural resources, and in particular the lack of animals which could be domesticated could have been one of the reasons for this. The sites in Nea Nikomedeia correspond to those in Bulgaria and Yugoslavia in the Starcevo civilization, which, allowing for regional variations, stretched from Yugoslavia to the Ukraine. The differences will become sharper during the fourth millenium BC. In the third mille nium BC, brorze appeared, and the copper age was born in Hungary.
An homogeneous civilization was in existence during the second half of the fifth millenium BC, which stretched from the Rhine to the western Ukraine. Only the most fertile land was developed, the famous loess. This farming was still nomadic, or at least semi-nomadic,

===== PAGE 251 =====

234
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
and the clearing was done by essarting. The cultivation of barley and wheat was of prime importance, more so than animal domestication, although all the species known during the Neolithic period were reared: cows, sheep, goats and pigs. Neither hunting or fishing were particularly important. As well as adzes made of hard rock, sickles were used, along with scrapers and arrows. The villages were sparsely populated. The houses were rectangular and had walls with wooden posts. They had double-sided roofs.
These differences point to the third millenium BC. Stock rearing was becoming more important, and the dwellings were being built higher up, or were surrounded by ditches. Wea pons were also increas ing in number.
A study of the central and western Mediterranean is not an easy undertaking. Although navigation had hardly developed by the third millenium BC, numerous changes must have taken place (fig. 47). The emergence of the Montserratian civilization along the Spanish, French and Ligurian coasts from the sixth millenium BC, is now attributed to acculturation, and not as was once thought, foreign imports. The rearing of small livestock was more important than farming, and hunting and fishing were widely practised. It was not until the third millenium BC that the growth of navigation brought about the devel opment of the chalcolithic civilizations of Sicily, Sardinia, Malta, and then Spain and France. There is no denying the influence of the
Figure 4 7. - Warship.

===== PAGE 252 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
235 Near-East. Relatively few metals were in use before the end of the third millenium BC.
We now know more about northern Africa, and it is certain that the coasts, as well as the Sudanian regions beginning with those nearest
Egypt, underwent technical development. Fishing and goat-rearing were the livelihood of the Sudan during the second half of the second millenium BC, and by the third millenium BC, it was the turn of the bovines. Farming developed relatively late in these regions, but it was known earlier on in northern Africa due to the influence of eastern populations. But here, as in Sicily, Spain, Sardinia and especially in
Tunisia, the real bonds were forged in Carthage by the Phoenician settlements, during the first half of the fourth millenium BC. It was this advanced civilization of Ugarit and Byblos which disembarked from the eastern ships.
Let us go back to the first half of the second millenium BC. All of Europe and the African shores of the Mediterranean were familiar with farming, rearing and no doubt the use of bronze in most areas.
The Phoenician region was clearly more advanced. As for continental Europe, we can glean some information from the striking rock engravings from the Val Camonica in northern Italy. We are able to reconstruct the village with the plan of Bedolina, the houses below, and higher up the mosaic of fields where the different types of cultivation are marked by a series of dots. A river winds its way through the countryside. Other lines mark with precision the separat ing walls, the furrows and the canals: all this reveals an already well-organised agriculture (figs 48 and 49) . It is difficult to discern exactly the type of swing plough used, but it seems most likely to have been the toothed-plough, which was used on all the northern banks of/ the Mediterannean, and one of the plougher's feet seems to be resting on the heel of the share (fig. 50). And what about the animals harnessed to this apparatus? At this date it is hardly likely to have been a horse. Equids? One of the animals seems to have a pair of horns, but this is only supposition. The only thing which is certain is that no horse has been depicted on any rock engraving, and the wagons which we shall see shortly were all pulled by oxen, and a donkey also seems to be distinguishable. Horses have been depicted on more recent engravings.
The picture of the smith is also unclear (fig. 51). Bronze, like copper was certainly forged, but iron smelting would have been very surpris-

===== PAGE 253 =====

236
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Й Й
L

===== PAGE 254 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
237 Figure 49. - A house at Camonica Valley.
ing at this date. Finally, we have included pictures of two vehicles, one with two wheels (fig. 52), and the other with four, the latter being pulled by oxen, as we indicated above (fig. 53) . Spears were frequently used as weapons and for hunting, and the animals most widely hunted were cervidae. Although the dating is sometimes a bit uncertain, and even if they are not always as perfect as the Egyptian paintings, the engravings of Val Camonica are exceptionally valuable to the historian concerned with material life.
We are about to see a fundamental difference between two regions very distant from the advanced civilizations which we have just discussed, and very distant from each other. China offered a field of possibilities similar to those of Mesopotamia and the Nile and Indus valleys. The neolithic period of northern China appears complete to some, in full possession of all its means, which makes it difficult to consider it as "invention" in seclusion. The dating is uncertain: was it the third millenium? It does seem to have had certain. characteristics peculiar to itself, however. The plants grown and the animals reared were indigeneous: these were millet, then later rice, and dogs, cows, pigs, sheep, poultry and silkworms. The pottery round first appeared during the second millenium BC. The shift towards southern China brought with it some changes, notably the predominance of rice over millet. Furthermore, bronze was first used at this time.

===== PAGE 255 =====

238
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Central America constitutes a second autonomous area of technical progress. At the end of the seventh and during the sixth millenium BC, the inhabitants of the Tehuacan valley used to collect wild plants, but

===== PAGE 256 =====

THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
239 Figure 51. - The smith (Camonica Valley).
Figure 52. - Barrow with two wheels (Camonica Valley).
they were also beginning to cultivate some of these, such as marrows and pimentoes. Implements were becoming more diverse and some remains of nets, cloths and baskets have been found. The number of plants grown for food also increased until the middle of the fourth millenium BC: maize, a type of haricot, and gourds, which were used

===== PAGE 257 =====

240
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 53. - Wagon with four wheels (Camonica Valley).
as recipients, were among these. It would have taken five milleniums to pass from an embryonic agriculture to a dominant one. Botanists have shown that wild rice was hardly profitable but the mutation of the pod-corn gene (around 2000 BC), the hybridization of maize with teosint and the effects of selection were to reverse this tendency. We have here, then, an extremely slow process of evolution, which, as we shall see, never completely developed in spite of a brilliant artistic civilization (fig. 54) .
There is no doubt that the bases for advanced techniques, the ancestors of our modern techniques, were established in the privileged areas of the Near-East. Some major innovations came from there:
agriculture, metallurgy, transport vehicles as well as writing all emerged during the first half of the first millenium BC with the great ancient civilizations. The change is still difficult to explain, as are the methods of transference towards less advanced civilizations, and advances and delays are still surrounded by mystery. These forms of progress should be linked with social transformations and economic development, which themselves depend in part on the development of contacts. The Hittites, Egyptians, Mesopotamians, Phoenicians and
Syrians all benefited from their constant relationships with other civilizations, and the contact with other techniques, processes and

products. One major point emerging from this is the fact that closed civilizations are less innovative than open ones.

===== PAGE 260 =====

THE GREEK
TECHNICAL SYSTEM
From the period of the Creto-Mycenaeans onwards, primitive Greek civilizations were already familiar with advanced techniques, some of which they had developed themselves, while others had been borrowed from their Egyptian or Mesopotamian neighbours. It seems that it was only from the 6th century BC onwards that a real technical system slowly began to develop, which was both different from and more sophisticated than earlier systems.
We should briefly emphasize some of the conditions under which this Greek technical system was formed, which may subsequently help to answer some of the many questfons asked, often inadequately, about this subject.
Firstly, the general poverty of the country of Greece must be indicated. Plains are relatively rare, often marshy, although having said this some very lush plains do exist. Mountains are dry, arid, and rocky, and there are only a few waterways which flow irregularly.
Forests are poor and regenerate with difficulty. There are almost no mining resources, apart from argentiferous lead. The wealth of the country therefore lies in dry farming lands on the one hand, and the sea on the other. The most important communications were enabled by the sea, which provided a large part of the food requirements, either in produce from the sea itself, or by facilitating the importation of various types of merchandize. These conditions explain why colonies were established in the richest areas in all respects.
Politically, this barren land was divided between a multitude of rival cities. Neither internal political quarrels nor civil wars were favourable for technical expansion. Small city-state economies, and perpetually unhealthy finances naturally limited any progress, if the latter was at all

===== PAGE 261 =====

244
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
possible. The arrival of the Macedonians from rich provinces was necessary to encourage the building of the first great empires. It was exactly at this moment that an interest in advanced techniques was felt, although this may have been a little late in the day.
Our knowledge of Greek economies is still rather fragmentary. In any case, the two points which we have just emphasized - economic division and the necessity for massive imports - were the only two factors which hindered technical development, and the importance of these cannot be stressed enough. Our knowledge of Greek demogra phy is no better, and we cannot say whether this also constituted an essential factor and what influence it may have exerted.
At the end of the chapter, we will return to the question of the contempt which the Greeks displayed with regard to the material world, although this characteristic of their nature may have been exaggerated.
TRADITIONAL SECTORS
AND PROGRESSIVE SECTORS
It is self evident that the technical development of the Greek civili zation was uneven, but these uneven advances were also typical of other civilizations. Some techniques obviously demand a slow process of evolution, while others are quickly introduced or improved. First and foremost, we should point out that from the beginning of the 6th century BC onwards, progress had been generalized in all sectors of material life to a greater or lesser degree, and this fact has undoubtedly not yet been fully developed. It would seem that "leading sectors" to some extent influenced more traditional ones. The formation of a coherent technical system required, moreover, a certain compatibility between the diverse techniques in use. We will return to this problem at the end of the chapter in order to make some conclusions about the blockage of technical thought.
Poor soil hardly encourages the development of agricultural tech niques, except perhaps in some of the more fertile plains. The
Mesogee plain in Attica with fertile red clays, the countryside of Thria and Eleusis, the Boeotian valley of Cephisus, the black lands with the

===== PAGE 262 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
245 chalky clays of Thessaly, Argolis and Mantinea, the valley of Pamisos, the rich alluvial deposits of Eurotas were as famous as the wealth of
Laconia. These areas did not produce enough, however, and the rest of Greece was occupied by mountains and rocky zones. Greece has consequently always depended upon large imports of foodstuffs from
Sicily, Egypt, and Thrace, or from the banks of the Black Sea. Soil improvement must have been very limited, as the lack of water did not enable a sizeable system of irrigation, and land was rarely drained. Soil enrichment was virtually impossible due to the absence of essential raw materials. Manure was rare as animal rearing was not yet widespread, and stalling was unecessary throughout most of the country because of the climatic conditions. Marl was also rarely available.
We have only vague ideas about the types of crops which were cultivated. In fact, Greek or Sicilian wheat were members of fairly diverse species. Paul Cloche notes that the most widespread crop was a sort of durum, an autumn species with a long bearded ear and reddish in colour, which was produced in the countryside of Metapontum,
Syracuse and Segesta. Poulard wheat or duck-bill, depicted on an obol from Orchomenus in Boeotia, had large ears of corn with bulging grain. During later periods, the Greeks cultivated clad corns which were grown less during the classical period, in favour of the cultivation of wheat. The most widespread type of wheat was einkorn, a long pointed grain which remained in the husk during shelling. Barley appeared to be the most popular cereal of the Greek world, as it was one of the first to be used for human consumption and formed an important crop, even after the introduction and large scale cultivation of wheat. Aristophanes notes that during the 5th century BC, milk gruel and barley flour were still eaten. Barley survived the dry summer periods of much better than other cereals. The most widespread type of barley cultivated was that with six rows. Greek botanists thought of oats as mere weeds, and rye was completely unknown.
Cultivation methods seem to have remained fairly primitive. There were three ploughing times, spring, summer, and autumn. Ploughing was very superficial, as the shallowness of the plant earth made deep ploughing impossible, except in the very fertile areas of the plains.
Although numerous pictorial representations illustrate that the swing plough was used in Egypt, the few representations which we have of
Greece demonstrate that the dental swing-plough was used (fig. 1).
The primitive swing-plough may have consisted of a single piece, as a

===== PAGE 263 =====

246
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Й Figure 1. - Greek ard.
text by Hesiod indicates. The image which Paul Cloche gives us is more fanciful and contrary to what he says, such an instrument would have been difficult to make out of one piece. A terracotta statuette from
Tanagra, dating from the 7th century BC, a cup of Nikosthenes from the 6th century BC (Berlin Museum), and an Attic cup from the 6th century BC (the Louvre), all depict identical instruments. The latter even illustrates the characteristic position of the foot on the plough tail. Hesiod, who also mentions this composite plough, writes that the beam was made of laurel or elm wood, the sole was made of oak, and the beam of ilex or holm oak. There was only one silt and for a long time the ploughshare lacked an iron support. At one time it was reinforced with frets or iron strips. During the 4th century BC, iron ploughshares were introduced. Other traditional instruments were used apart from the plough, including the pickaxe and the two pronged hoe. A hoe for weeding was also used, as well as the three-pronged fork for turning soil. The plough was pulled by a team of oxen who were yoked either around the horns or the withers.
Cereals were "sawn" using sickles. A certain time lapse was thought necessary between harvesting and threshing, as long as the ears of com were bound in sheaves. Threshing was done under animals' hooves.
Then the whole lot was pitched high with shovels so that the straw and chaff could be separated from the grain. The grain was then stored in earthenware jars for transportation, as can be seen on a vase in the
Louvre in Paris.
There were many flourishing olive plantations, as the olive tree was perfectly suited to the soil in a great many parts of Greece. This plant was well protected, moreover, even supported by the authorities, despite its delicacy and expense. The main fat element of Greek material life, olive oil was abundantly used in many ways. The olive

===== PAGE 264 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
247 could also be harvested straight from the tree, and at different times, for the fruits could be picked before they were ripe, half ripe or fully ripe depending upon the uses one wanted to make of it.
The grape vine could be successfully cultivated with the olive tree.
The Attic vines of Diacria and Corinthia created marvellous vine yards. The vineyards of the Greek islands and those on the shores of the Aegean Sea were famous, but vineyards could be found almost everywhere. The Greeks cultivated two types of grape vine, one being a trailing type, and the other a climbing vine which ran around a network of trees and was supported by a latticework of poles. Like olive picking, the grape harvest was one of the most popular occu pations in the Attic countryside.
Cereals, olives and grapes were not the only widespread crops. Fruit trees were also cultivated on a large scale, and figs were particularly popular.
The development of a certain mechanization, which we will study in more depth later, was to facilitate change in the whole process of agricultural production. A 6th century BC crater, now in Leningrad, shows grain being crushed in a cone-shaped mortar. As Hesiod indi cated, this mortar was made of wood, but those made of stone (basalt or granite) have also been found. The most widely used millstone up to the
Hellenistic period, was the so-called "coming and going" millstone, driven by hand on a stone platfrom around a pivot exterior to the centre stone. The grinder, similarly in stone, had a parallelepiped form: this was a sort of hollow funnel, with a long narrow opening at the base, and like the platform it was lined with herring bone ridges. A long wooden handle, linked to the pivot, was fixed in mortises on the upper side.
Two types of rotating treadmills were probably introduced at the end of the Hellenistic period. An example of the first, however, used for grinding minerals in the lead mines of the Laurion, may be found in the Classical period (fig. 2). The giant millstone is conical in shape at the top, and the running stone resembles an hour glass, the lower half of which covers the conical top of the fixed stone. The upper part of the hour glass serves as the funnel. A slight gap is kept between the two stones so that the moving stone can pivot around a wooden axle sunk into the fixed one; the latter's suspension on this axle enables the gap between the two stones to be maintained. This type of treadmill which could be driven by animals walking round the axle in an never-ending circle, was to enjoy a remarkable expansion in the Roman world.

===== PAGE 265 =====

248
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
2 0 Figure 2. - Grindstone for crushing.
The production of olive oil was more complicated than that of flour.
The first operation consisted of separating the stone from the pulp and extracting from the latter, firstly, the amorge - a bitter liquid which could be used as a fertilizer or a material for treating wood or leather - and secondly, the thick juice from the oil. Originally, the olives were undoubtedly crushed by treading, the feet being protected with clogs, and the amorge was collected by a pipe. It is even possible that pestles were used. At first, this pulp was crushed in a basket, using a heavy stone: such a scene is depicted on a Greek vase from Delos where the olives are shown piled into a large mortar. It is probably much later that the oil mill was introduced, which the Romans called a trapetum .
This consisted of two piano-convex millstones, placed vertically, supported by a horizontal axle turning on a vertical pivot. The recumbent millstone consisted of a stone vat, the sides of which fitted the external profile of the two running stones. Here again, a space had to be maintained between the two stones. Excavations at Olynthus have provided examples of this, dating from around the 5th century
BC. The pulp was then pressed, and a 6th century BC Greek vase depicts a still very primitive instrument, consisting of a sort of basket in

===== PAGE 266 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
249 which the pulp was pressed with a stone, as it was in the first stage of the operation. Presses similar to those used for making wine were then employed. The use of the lever balanced with enormous counter weights also attested by a vase from the 6th century BC, now preserved in Boston. The fruits to be pressed were placed in quadrangular envelopes mounted on a board, on which the lever was placed. The process of making wine was very similar to that of oil. The grapes were first trodden underfoot in transportable, wooden vats. The weighted lever was then used, and although it is difficult to specify when this was introduced, it must go back to the same period when the press for making oil was used. Finally, according to Heron of Alexandria, a system of numerous winches and pullies was employed instead of the feet.
Cloche states, but not precisely, that during the 4th century BC, methods of market gardening were greatly improved in some regions, such as those practised on the outskirts of Athens.
The nature of the soil necessarily dictated animal husbandry. There were a few rich areas in Greece where cattle and horses existed, such as in Thessaly, Epirus, Boeotia, Messene, and Euboea. Almost everyw here else donkeys and mules were used, or small livestock, such as sheep and goats were reared. The Athenians had to import most of the horses and cattle they needed. Pigs were very common, and two breeds of sheep were reared: the long-tailed sheep, like small Algerian sheep, and the broad-tailed sheep, endowed with a fatty growth which invaded the whole of the caudal appendix. Sheep were reared espe cially for their wool.
Hunting still continued and some animals existed in such a large quantity that the Greeks had to control their numbers by literally waging war on them. This was particularly true in the case of the hare which overran the islands. Fishing was obviously an important resource. At the time of Homer, the great have tended to scorn fish as a source of food. Fishing began in the Archaic period and fish gradually became plentiful on the markets. The Greeks knew and used every different fishing technique: fishing using a floating line, a sinking line, fly fishing, casting long nets, harpoon fishing and using a hoopnet, were probably methods which had been employed for a very long time.
Anchovies, sardines and tunas were the most common catches.
Thus, despite an environment which was not very favourable, at least in most of the Greek countryside, the Hellenistic civilization

===== PAGE 267 =====

250
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
managed to develop a more advanced system of agriculture than that of other, richer regions. Whatever types of plants were cultivated, a certain number of improvements were implemented in the methods used to grow them, and more particularly in preparing produce from the land. It is possible that agrarian problems, which existed within many Greek societies, may have contributed to the blockage of the development of farming techniques. It appears, however, that natural conditions and the technical environment played a more important role. Greek agriculture did not seem to have developed the means to progress beyond the stage at which it had arrived after considerable efforts.
Development of the sub-soil also raised problems which were difficult to overcome. We have said that Greece was not rich in mineral deposits. Copper was found in Euboea and Cyprus, iron in the
Cyclades, some gold in Boeotia and Euboea, and silver at Thasos and Siphnos. During the 7th and 6th centuries BC, and for this very reason these two towns were the major flourishing centres of silver produc tion. But all this mining activity was to be overshadowed by the
Laurion mines, where lead was concentrated in contact zones between schists and limestone.
It is likely that the Laurion was exploited very early on. There were various gallery types, square, trapezoidal, and irregular, but they were never more than a metre in height, often only as high as 60 centimetres, and 60 to 90 centimetres in width, thus forming veritable passages which did not demand excessive labour so that the veins could be reached quickly. If it was too costly to reach the veins by digging passages, either square or rectangular vertical wells were sunk, measuring a surface area of approximately 2 square metres. The facings were perfectly smooth, and mortises for the ladders were installed at various intervals giving access to the shafts. Thus they could dig to depths of about 120 metres. The tools used included the flat headed hammer and a spike. The former was used for striking the dresser, a rod measuring 2 to 3 centimetres in diameter and 25 to 30 centimetres in length, the end of which was bevelled with 2 or 4 edges.
The pick consisted of a flat, thick blade, pointed at one end, and folded at the other in a socket shape so that it could take a fairly strong handle.
The mattock was a sort of spade, the socket of which was curved like that of a modern spade. The first three tools were made of hammered and quenched iron. Lamps were usually made of terracotta, but

===== PAGE 268 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
251 occasionally of lead. Finally, the mineral was transported in bags or baskets (fig. 3).
The discovery of mineral deposits was relatively easy for those who had considered the location of metaliferous masses and realized that they usually lay in pockets. Depending upon where the gallery hit the pocket, the mineral could be extracted either by beginning at the top or at the bottom. Fire was only rarely used to break up the rock, if at all.
Ventilation was provided by waving ,eloths, but carefully caliphated vertical wood partitions were also used in the shafts, which produced a sort of siphon effect. As was the case in the Laurion mines, the passages through the schists and limestone did not need supporting works. A system of pillars was used in the deposit itself.
Considerable progress seems to have been made during the 5th and 4th centuries BC, and it is at this point that we arrive at the third contact. During this period, vertical shafts were improved and the knowledge of where the deposits lay and how to extract them increased. We know that production in the Laurion mines grew
Figure 3. - Mine gallery represented on a painted corinthian tablet from the 6th century BC.

===== PAGE 269 =====

252
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
considerably under the archonship of Nikomedes ( 484/483 BC) . Simi larly, we know of the remarkable work carried out at the hill of
Cephalari, near lake Copais, under the direction of the specialist engineer, Crates of Chalcis during the 4th century BC. All this would seem to lead to the conclusion, albeit not a concrete one, that noticeable progress was made in the art of mining at the end of the 6th and the beginning of the 5th century BC.
The mineral was first sorted and then crushed, originally in mortars, and later by the millstones with two conical parts which have already been described. The mineral was then washed in a simple tank, the floor of which was inclined. Water was probably collected in water tanks during propitious seasons, for there lay a major problem. The mineral was then roasted and reduced by methods of which we know very little. It seems that this operation was performed by artificially blowing air over the mineral. Finally, the lead and silver were sepa rated by the process of cupellation. Our knowledge of these operations is still too sketchy to say whether any real progress was made during this period. It seems likely that the growth in the production of minerals prompted not only an increase in processing plants, but also some degree of improvement in roasting and reducing methods.
Digging out clay did not present any great difficulties (an image on a Greek vase shows something which appears to be underground clay mining, which seems inconceivable), but quarry working was not so simple. Working in galleries appears to have been extremely rare:
some galleries existed in the marble quarries of Paros, but open air quarries were more common. The wall was worked in steps from above because it was easier to bring the blocks down. Sometimes, however, it was worked from below so that the overhangs could later be exploited.
The methods employed were exactly the same as those already used by the Egyptians for quarrying granite.
The study of Greek crafts has still to be completed. Such a study would probably reveal that in most cases the industry was fairly static, but this conclusion does not exclude the fact that there were some sectors in which significant progress was made.
Textile techniques were certainly the least advanced. Wool was the most common fibre used, flax was mainly imported from Egypt, and cotton had been introduced in Greece after the the campaigns of
Alexander in the Indus Valley. Weaving was still done using the spindle and distaff. The weaving loom was still a vertical one, the warp

===== PAGE 270 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
253 of which hung from a crosswise bar supported on two posts and stretched by weights attached at each end of the threads (fig. 4). A moving bar separated the even or odd threads or groups of threads, and the shuttle looked like a flat wooden needle. The cloth was woven from the top to the bottom and a card tightened the cross threads of the web. The length of the cloth was determined by the height of the loom.
Woodworking was important in a great many domains, such as construction, boat building (fig. 5), and furnishings, etc. In most cases the tools used were those which had been employed by preceding civilizations, except that the metal and the forms may have been improved. Originally the saw, axe and adze were used, followed by planes, augers, gimlets, bow-drills, bow-trepans, chisels, scissors, and polishers. The Greeks had invented two indispensable instruments, probably around the 6th century BC. The first was the curved saw which was easier to handle. The second and more important inno vation was the lathe, which was introduced by Theodore of Samos, bronze-smith, engineer, sculptor and architect, one of the men of the
Greek Belle Epoque similar to the "engineers" of the Renaissance.
Although the lathe did not operate on a connecting rod and crank system, the use of this instrument was to enable great improvement in woodworking and carpentry.
From the time of Homer, pot makers used the potter's wheel. A second person was needed to turn the wheel so that the potter could have both hands free to work the clay. Very little progress seems to have been made in this craft. After the vase had been dried, it was polished, but we will not dicuss at length techniques which have been studied in depth, such as the methods of firing and painting, and the colours used. Two points concerning the baked clay, however, must be clarified. The bricks, usually graded in three sizes, remained untreated for a long time, and were merely dried in the sun. We have examples of the moulds which were used in order to obtain an exact gauge. Some rare evidence of the processes employed before the appearance of
Roman techniques has been found from baked bricks. It is possible that this type of brick was used right up until the very moment when
Roman influence was exerted. On the other hand, terracotta tiles were extensively used as it was impossible to use mud tiles. There were two types of tiles, each of which had variations: the Laconian tile which was curved in shape with a curved lap-joint and the Corinthian tile which was straight with two edges and an angled lap-joint. According to

===== PAGE 272 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
255 Figure 5. - Greek boat. Painting on an Attic vase (circa 500/475 BC) Pliny, Corinthian tiles, which were also cast from moulds, date from the 6th century BC.
We know very little about the art of metalwork, but the Greeks appear to have been extremely skilled smiths. The images left to us, however, must be reinterpreted in a less fanciful way. The wine pitcher from the 6th century, now in the British Museum, depicts a forge and not a foundry workshop. The central image clearly demonstrates that iron rather than copper is being worked. The furnace is therefore merely a forge; indeed pig iron was not known at this epoch. Bellows were made out of two twin goat skins. Equipment was already very diversified, but there are no new tools at this stage.
Bronze was frequently used for making statues. Casting methods do not appear to have been very advanced as yet, as small and large statues were cast in several pieces and then assembled. Air bubbles and vitreous scoria appeared in the metal owing to the use of sand in moulds.
The early techniques which the Greeks visibly began to improve upon were those used in construction. Columns, pillars and architraves in early temples, as well as in domestic architecture, were all made of wood. This technique was continued until the middle of the 3rd

===== PAGE 273 =====

256
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
century, as the buildings at Samos or at the asclepieion on Kos illustrate. Virtually all the available types of wood were used, includ ing oak, fir, rotproof cypress and ceder, walnut, olive wood and even vine. Bricks were used for walls, but huge walls were also built out of clay. The temple of Patrai was built of brick. Similarly brick was used for the construction of surrounding walls: during the 5th century for the wall of Themistocles at Athens, the walls of Platea, Thebes, and
Tegea, in the 4th century for the walls of Apollonia and Mantinea, and even in the walls of Sparta. These walls were often mounted on stone socles and were reinforced with wooden beams, according to a tech nique widely used by the Creto-Mycenaeans. In the 3rd century BC,
Philo of Byzantium recommended that wooden chains be used for brick fortifications. They were also used for repairing parts of the brick wall of Athens in 307/306 BC.
Major revolution came about in the substitution of wood for other materials. This substitution began, tentatively as yet, around the middle of the 7th century BC, and the operation was undoubtedly progressive in the beginning: rotten wood columns were replaced in stone. This was the case at the Temple of Thermos at Heraion at
Olympia. Then, apart from reconstructions, this progressed to the replacement of the whole structure, as is demonstrated at Marmara,
Delphi, Corfu and Calydon. Nevertheless, entablatures were still made of wood and covered with terracotta. The use of stone was generalized during the 6th century and entablatures were "petrified," as can be seen on the Temple of Apollo at Corinth constructed in the second half of the 6th century BC. It is difficult to say whether these early columns were monolithic or not: this could have been possible with techniques which had been tried and tested in Egypt. In any case, the drum column rapidly appeared, and it was already in use in the
Temple of Hera at Olympia. One advantage certainly lay in the transportation of the materials required, as the column could only be mounted vertically, which presupposes that lifting equipment had already been developed. Although the Temple of Apollo at Corinth, erected around 540 BC, still possessed monolithic columns, it seems that this was probably a late and exceptional example.
Finally, the substitution of stone for other early materials resulted to a large extent from the development of transport and lifting instru ments. The Greeks employed Egyptian methods of transportation, as well as more improved equipment, such as rollers, carriages like the

===== PAGE 274 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
257 Eleusis' wheels for moving logs, and round packages of wood (fig. 6, 7 and 8). Roads were an obvious necessity, and examples of these have been found near quarries and temples. Lifting operations were more difficult: a drum column could weigh up to five tons, and a piece of entablature might be six to nine metres long and weigh as much as thirty tons. Temporary inclined levels may have been used for moving very large loads, as was so for the Parthenon entablatures. But these costly and clumsy procedures were replaced by lifting apparatus, based on a system of pullies, blocks and winches. Signs of such operations have been found at Delphi and at Olympia, dating from the second half of the 6th century BC. Chersiphron and Metagenes, who undertook the building of the temples of Ephesus, invented mechanisms for transporting and lifting the materials to the desired location. Some centuries later, Vitruvius wrote of the admiration which these con structions inspired in the building world, as they still do today.
Vitruvius also mentions the work which these two architects wrote on the new machines they had created, and which have been passed down through the centuries by generations of architects, without requiring any major improvements. Hoists and pullies were used in the construc tion of the Erechtheion, built at the end of the 5th century BC. At
Didyma, two machines were coupled together in order to hoist columns and pieces of entablature which measured 7 .5 metres long and
2.20 metres thick. Heron was to make a classification of these various machines, in particular, for hoists with 1 , 2, 3, or 4 beams forming a trestle.
The examination of archaeological remains has enabled a greater understanding of the construction methods used during this period.
Foundations were poor, and they were even content to level the rock when possible. A solid base was therefore essential. The walls of large temples were built with regularly drafted blocks, assembled with even courses without mortar. Finishings were done when the construction work had been completed, in other words, the columns were fluted after they had been erected. The outside surface of the wall was decorated after the whole wall had been built, although surfaces were marked for this operation before being erected. The most difficult problem was that of ensuring the solidity of this stack. The column drums were linked together with cedar pins, placed in a square cavity in the middle of the drum, and embedded with lead. The blocks of the walls were linked together, horizontally and vertically, with double

===== PAGE 275 =====

258
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
bracket dovetail-shaped metal clamps. Doubting, quite wrongly, the solidity of marble, Greek architects introduced veritable iron frameworks to reinforce their buildings. Thus, they not only strength ened the walls, but also lintels, corniches, and architraves, to prevent collapsing. The entablatures of the Propylaea at Delphi were reinfor ced in the 4th century BC, and we may observe the strength of the irons used.
A study of the marks on these stones illustrates the whole techno logy developed in the field of construction. Grooves, protuberances, and V- or U-shaped channels are manifest proof that apparatus, ropes, nippers and lewises were used from the 6th century BC onwards (fig.9 and 10). Marks made from assembling the buildings demonstrate that the work was logically organized. Some progress may even be detected, such as the use of the swallow-tail clamp for reinforcement at the end of the 6th to the beginning of the 5th century BC, and then the introduction of single or double brackets. Securing the drums was perfected around the middle of the 4th century BC by attaching central
