(From R. Martin) pins to tenons placed at the edges. It is likely that the architects worked from scale models made beforehand, and also used these to present their projects before the proper authorities. This procedure was still used by Renaissance architects at the beginning of the 16th century.
From the end of the 5th century BC, they benefited from all that the science of perspective and the study of shade could contribute. Presen tations of plan, section and elevation could thus clarify important points presented by the scale models. We cannot overemphasize the major improvements made to the building of large stone edifices during the height of the Classical period.
The art of roofing buildings, on the other hand, does not seem to have made very much progress. The framework merely consisted of a simple stack of wood, as is illustrated in the arsenal of Piraeus. This process was very expensive and meant that the timber frame was extremely heavy. Even if, according to some authorities, science could not contribute a better solution, the Greeks demonstrated great skill in other fields which required woodwork and which could have enabled them to modify roofing frameworks. This could also have resulted from the quality of timber which was used.

===== PAGE 279 =====

262
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
A
B
c Figure 10. - Different types of lewises. A, lewise and corresponding cavities; B, lewise with two oblique faces (Roman times); C, lewises with one oblique face (Greek times).
(From R. Martin) From the very beginning the art of corbelled vaults was practised.
According to Diodorus of Sicily and t Strabo, the modern vault had been invented by Democritus of Abdera, around 440 BC. The earliest examples we know of are thought to date from the 5th or 4th century
BC.
The delicate problem of transport now remains to be discussed. It is difficult to dissociate the different elements involved. We have already

===== PAGE 280 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
263 emphasized the extent to which the notions of structure and system are essential in order to arrive at worthwhile conclusions. If these elements were separated, we would end up with completely valueless judge ments. Transport and roadways, transport and harnessing, and trans port and cattle domestication are inseparable elements, as are ships and ports. Transport and economy, in other words, weight and bulk, must also be considered.
Land transport involves both roadways and all the different sizes of materials to be transported, whereas roadways are determined by the size of the country, its geographic layout, political organization, and financial status. Apart from these, the available materials and the appropriate techniques are also factors which determine the progress of transport. Crete had developed a road network from the second millennium BC, whereas there were very few Greek roads during the
Classical period, and those that did exist were badly maintained and were often no more than rough tracks. Bridges were virtually non existant, although the technology to build them existed, as we know of advanced paths and paved sacred ways which were not very long but which nevertheless went straight to their destination. The roadways at
Delphi, Eleusis, and Olympia were paved in this fashion and had raised pavements. In the towns, roads were more often than not reduced to mires. It was only from the 5th century BC onwards that urban routes were paved and maintained. Political division and the poor financial status of the towns were equally powerful blockages to any progress, as was the lack of technical initiative.
We should also stress the fact that goods were not widely circulated.
Trade was relatively limited and was only concerned with the quantity of goods imported, in particular with grain and certain materials. Up until very recently, at least until the end of the Middle Ages, beasts of burden could suffice if need be, particularly for transporting foodstuffs. The transportation of certain large loads could certainly be difficult, which may account for the limitation of some construction work. These loads were carried by donkeys and mules so that tracks were the only type of roadway required.
The Greek chariot was generally two-wheeled. An Attic crater dating from the 8th century BC, now in the National Museum of
Athens, may be interpreted as representing a four-wheeled vehicle.
But these are probably funeral chariots which only had to make short journeys. The four-wheeled vehicle, which did in fact exist, did not

===== PAGE 281 =====

264
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
possess a front axle assembly unit and was consequently almost impossible to manoeuvre (fig. 1 1). The system of wheels and axles was still very primitive. Antique harnessing is generally known to have been poorly adapted for haulage, as the harness strangled the animal around the throat and more or less prevented it from breathing. The horseshoe was also unknown. Loads transported in such a way could not exceed five hundred kilogrammes per vehicle. But the question of transport also raises another aspect; that of animal domestication.
Pastures were poor in most areas, and we have already seen that oats were virtually unknown. In such conditions, whatever improvement could be made to vehicles, no significant progress could be made in transport methods.
The horse probably first served as a mount from the 1 1th century BC. The texts of Xenophon seem to suggest that the saddle existed from the beginning of the 4th century BC, but spurs and stirrups were not known to the Greeks.
In a country bordered by coasts, maritime vessels were naturally the most developed form of transport. The most wealthy and powerful cities, at least those that remained so, were situated near the sea.
Three factors are involved in naval techniques: the ship, the port and navigation, although the latter was undoubtedly the most primitive, and we know very little about it. In fact, the Greeks had illustrious
Figure 1 1 . - Greek vehicle.

===== PAGE 282 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
265 Phoenician predecessors, from whom they had certainly borrowed the best part of their techniques. Coastal navigation was obviously practised, but ships could also sail straight across certain axes, such as those which lead to Egypt. Lack of observation means, and the absence of adequate maps undoubtedly restricted navigation to mere guesswork.
Many suggestions have been put forward on the subject of ships, few of which probably reflect the true nature of things. There is very little change, or at least hardly any significant transformation, from one image to another. Obviously merchant ships should be distinguished from war ships, the latter being subject to stricter strains, particularly where speed and manageability are concerned. War ships, which have been more often the subject of representations, were more stream lined than merchant ships and raised at the bow and the stern, as is exemplified on the Nikosthenes cup, now in the Louvre. Merchant ships were small, with a capacity of about forty to sixty tons, rounded and low in the water. The bridges in these ships were very reduced:
simple shelters, situated at the bow and the stern.
Propulsion was provided by both sails and oars. All the sails were quadrangular in shape and fixed to a single mast by means of one yard perpendicular to the top of the mast. The sails were made out of leather or canvas, but we have little information regarding their fabrication. The single sail could only be propelled by wind from behind, or at the very least allow supporting trim. It was therefore naturally impossible to tack or to hazard more exact manoeuvres. The oar was consequently indispensable, and the tiered rows of oarsmen have often been remarked upon; triremes and quadriremes would have been boats with three or four rows of oarsmen. This is a purely theoretical view, however, as ships would have had to have been very tall but these were unstable unless insufferable ballasting was used.
According to this view, the ship of Ptolemy Soter would therefore ha.ve carried twelve rows of oarsmen which is absolutely unthinkable. It is very likely that the terms used applied to the number of oarsmen per oar. Were there no ships which had several superimposed rows of oarsmen? It is difficult to answer this as iconographic interpretation is often difficult.
From the most remote antiquity the ship's rudder had always been a lateral one. It has been demonstrated that no great importance should be attached to this fact, especially for ships with oars which in fact did

===== PAGE 283 =====

266
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
not need a rudder, as the teams of oarsmen were well trained to row together.
The port was perhaps the most remarkable invention of the Greek Classical period. Formerly, as has been shown in the writings of
Homer, landing beaches were used, a fact which entails significant consequences. The Mediterranean has no tides, so it was therefore necessary to pull the boats ashore. This also meant that keels could not be very deep. The introduction of the port must have enabled some progress in the development of ships, as vessels could subsequently take a larger keel. The Greeks created every type of port, ranging from inlet ports which were easy to build due to the rocky coastline, to artificial ports constructed with dikes, quays, and moles.
Spectacular examples of these ports remain today. There was a double port at Cnidus, the building of which was enabled by the construction of jetties. We should also mention the port of Alexandria, where the island of Pharos was linked at the end of the 4th century BC to the mainland by a dike some one thousand three hundred metres long with two openings by which the two docks could communicate. In the port of Antioch, at Seleucia in Pietria, the dock was entirely dug in an unsheltered coast, and was linked to the sea by a canal. Finally, the port of Piraeus should also be mentioned as this was built in a remarkably propituous site.
It is curious that the Greeks attempted to cut through the Isthmus of Corinth. This work had been conceived around 600 BC by the tyrant
Periander, and was taken up by Demetrius Poliorcetes at the end of the 4th or the beginning of the 3rd century BC. This scheme was later abandoned because the engineers were convinced that there was a difference in the levels of the two coasts. This was the very problem which for some time prevented the building of the Suez Canal.
It was in 283 BC, under the reign of Ptolemy Soter, that the world's first lighthouse was built. The architect, Sostratus of Chidus, erected a tower nearly 85 metres in height, the levels of which were set back above each other. The light itself was created by a large furnace in front of a huge mirror which reflected light for more than 55 kilometres out to sea. Unfortunately it disappeared in the 14th century in an earthquake.
With construction and its lifting apparatus, with transport tech niques, and the whole geographical organization which they presup pose, we have reached a turning point. Diverse requirements exist for

===== PAGE 284 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
267 the creation of a technical system, taking into account relationships between the various technical, economic and social systems. It would be unfair to reproach the Greeks with the slowness of the evolution of their agricultural techniques, as climatic and pedagogical conditions, the absence of a comprehensive botanic science, and possibly the weakness of demographic movements are extremely restricting fac tors. And one can easily understand that despite the cost of transport, the importation of foodstuffs was preferable to expensive hydraulic installations, to the search for effective enrichment of the land, and to the development of new agricultural techniques. On the other hand, some techniques had to be renewed for various reasons, and we will now turn our attention to these.
The "Greek miracle" of the 5th and the beginning of the 4th century BC was to affect technical progress. As far as we can tell from rare existing texts, it was really during this epoch that the Greek, and for that matter the Antique technical system, was formed. Comparative studies with Mesopotamian or Egyptian technical civilizations are not yet detailed enough to enable us to establish very clear lines of demarcation and, in certain cases, doubts still remain as to the exact extent of the technical contribution made by the Greeks.
As most contemporary authors have liked to point out, technical progress of this period presents several aspects which should be emphasized. There is no global progress to speak of, but on the contrary the so-called "leading technology" may be observed to take place alongside traditional artisan techniques which evolve almost imperceptibly. We have only to look at the techniques of the textile industry which, as we have already seen, used distaff spinning and the vertical weaving loom for some time. The construction of standard buildings, rather than those which were to profit from the latest techniques, was not to change very radically. There is therefore a visible difference between stable technical sectors and progressive sectors.
As we will see, not only progressive techniques arise haphazardly, but rational techniques also occur, which require that science grad ually develop principles at the same time and which similarly provide the ktter with some of the elements which it previously lacked. Science may be an independant entity only when technology does not require collections of particular cases in order to resolve the problems which arise, but becomes the application of general principles. This link

===== PAGE 285 =====

268
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
between science and technology was to find fortune during the Renais sance. Although at the end of the 15th century, it was possible to bring this remarkable conjunction of two previously separate fields of research to light, this is much more difficult for the period which concerns us here. The history of science is known in its entirety, but that of techniques is less well known, and above all, there are few historians who have attempted to synthesize science and technology, which was one of the central preoccupations of the engineers of
Alexandria.
' And almost immediately an exact chronological concordance may be noted. This technological take-off, which began somewhat timidly during the 6th century BC, took shape in the following century and exploded in an extensive form at the end of the 4th and the beginning of the 3rd century BC. It was similarly from the 6th to the 4th century
BC that Hellenistic science, as Tannery terms it, developed from the emergence of the Ionian school, possessing similar techniques, to the sudden expansion of the Greek world after the conquests of Alexan der. Reciprocal interventions continued throughout the whole of this period. We find men who simultaneously lend their attention to both science and techniques. This tradition begins with Thales of Miletus, physician, mathematician and technician, to Archytas, one of the greatest scientists of his time and undoubtedly a very great technician, to whom one attributes perhaps too much, and continues with Archi medes. Thus the links between science and technology do not flag, on the contrary they grow closer.
It is true, however, that physics remained rather hesitant, concerned as this subject is with abstract notions and which future developments will sweep clean. Technicians could hardly use physics to aid them, and scientists could learn nothing from a technique whose problems only gradually arose within a system. We will judge later the extent of this separation between physics and technology, and the extent to which it was felt, with Strato of Lampsacus, who was not as celebrated as many of the others. "Physics for entertainment" with which Greek engineers have often been mocked, may only have been a reaction against a purely abstract science.
Progress made in mathematics during the same period was infinitely superior. Arithmetic, in which the Pythagoreans were passionately interested, continued to develop. This is also true of geometry which, indisputably born of practical problems, was raised to the rank of

===== PAGE 286 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
269 science, entailing all the latter's requirements. One has passed from intuition or guesswork and from technical demonstration, which was subsequently ceaselessly dismissed to the rank of impure unessentials, to discursive virtue. Geometry quickly took the lead over other scientific disciplines, as it lent itself better than any other science to purely intellectual thought, and because it also had a visual value of demonstration.
Geometry, however, remained closely linked to technology, and although it succeeded in becoming a very advanced body of doc trine, it provided the material arts with all the services which the latter had lent it for its own development. The most concrete link between science and technology is undoubtedly music. The range of notes had been perceived by the ear and later translated into a mathematical form. The translation from a range of notes to a range of numbers came about by constant confrontations between art and hypotheses. The study of technical vocabulary, which to our knowl edge has never been attempted, would contribute extremely important elements to this research. Architects, who were also technicians, and military "engineers" were to quickly make use of the science of numbers.
As scientists began to organize their knowledge by forming mathe matical formulae, technicians similarly started to compile the first technical treatises. Thus, a form of technology began to appear, precisely in the domains where changes were most pronounced.
Before going into detail on this issue, it would seem useful to clarify certain important changes which largely conditioned the evolution of a great number of techniques. The best example is provided by the definitive clarification of the classical kinematic chains. Their attribution and dating is somewhat hazardous. Archytas is credited with the screw. It is probably around the 5th and 4th centuries BC that the screw, pulley, cogwheel, and their meshing together contributed an appreciable number of fundamental new ideas to the technology of mechanics. This was also true of the complementary assembly of various kinematic chains; one was to progress from the weighted lever press to the press with winches and pullies.
From this moment on, all the problems of elevation, both in large constructions and in the mines, were part_ially resolved. And what about the use of the pulley in sailing ships? If energy sources remained the same, the means of transmitting forces and their reduction,

===== PAGE 287 =====

270
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
enabled by cogwheels and pullies combined to form block and tackle, led to the spread and diversification of the use of energy.
We are extremely badly informed on the subject of materials. Even at the height of their expansion, Greece was not rich in minerals, and we will return to this subject later. No noticeable progress seems to have been made in reducing furnaces, although work in the mines had been facilitated by elevation methods, indeed by the use of the
Archimedes' screw for raising or supporting mine levels. Furnaces discovered at Agros Sosti on the island of Sifnos, probably dating from the 6th century BC, offer the earliest examples of chimney furnaces, with a clay covering, an air vent, and a casting hole. While some progress was made in primitive furnaces, there does not seem to have been any later significant progress. It is highly likely that the Greeks used imported metals, the only exceptions being lead and silver which were produced by the famous Laurion mines.
Military techniques were probably no more spectacular, although more is known about these as Greek historiography has provided many facts on this subject, despite, on the whole, some late and often inaccurate compilations. Thucydides in particular, writes several pas sages on various machines, but apart from the case of battering rams, he also uses vague terms, the value of which the historian of techniques has many problt;:!ms in recognizing. It is therefore very difficult to draw an evolutive table of the military art of ancient Greece. It would also be indispensable to extend these studies to the whole of the Near East, from whom the Greeks often had to borrow techniques.
A few years ago, Andre Aymard referred to the long delay in the organization of military techniques, both for attacking and defending fortified towns. "It was only from the beginning of the 4th century BC that a profound evolution began to take place. This evolution was very slow until the stunning campaigns of Philip of Macedonia and his son
Alexander opened everybody's eyes." Formerly, the techniques of poliorcetics and war machines were totally rudimentary, even if the machines mentioned by our sources were employed. It is likely that only battering rams were used. Does not historians' uncertainty in this subject stem from the ambiguity of the language used? We have already seen this confusion in other areas.
Names are extremely rare before the 4th century BC. We may mention Mandrokles, who threw a floating bridge over the Bosporus
Straits for Darius' armies (522/485 BC) , "executing exactly what king

===== PAGE 288 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
271 Darius had envisaged." He was so delighted with the bridge that he commissioned a large picture of it. We should add moreover that
Mandrokles came from Samas.
Of course we would like to know more about Artemon of Cla zomenes (around 469/429 BC), whom some authorities have hailed as the "founder of the new art of siegecraft." He was an "engineer" and a builder of machines. He demonstrated his genius in both these fields at the siege of Samas ( 430/429 BC). As Diodorus of Sicily notes, "Per icles was the first to use machines known as battering rams and testudos, built to the designs of Artemon of Clazmenes." According to
Plutarch, the statesman admired these new machines, which may not have been in current use.
According to the writings of Ephorus it was the first time that battering engines had been used to knock down large walls, and Pericles thought this a wonderful new idea. The engineer who had invented them was carried about in a chair, in order to direct and hasten the work because he was crippled in one leg and for this reason he was called
Periphoretos.
Plutarch gives other accounts of this engineer which do not paint a very flattering picture of him.
He was so fearful of things that most of the time he didn't move from his house, where he sat attended by two servants at his side who held a copper shield over his head so that nothing would fall on him.
One can imagine the unfortunate inventor of these war machines absolutely terrified, although Plutarch may have exaggerated his story a little. Unless, of course, Artemon of Clazomenes may have seen himself as a moral victim of his instruments of death and felt the same feelings of regret which some of today's scientists experience.
Aymard notes that historians have long recognized the fact that a genuine military revolution took place in Greece from the 4th century
BC onwards, even before the intervention of Philip of Macedonia.
There was certainly a revolution in the infantry's armament, and in the use of mercenaries, but the role and the improvement of fortifications also grew during the course of the first half of the 4th century BC.
Cities built higher stone walls, dug ditches in order to ward off attacking machines, which would seem to prove that machines had become more powerful and effective. They also adapted plans to follow the relief of the land, and facilitated flanking by recessing walls, building jutting angles, and increasing the number of towers.

===== PAGE 289 =====

272
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The power of the new artillery - and we will encounter this phe nomenon on several occasions - thus compelled an evolution in fortification. It is possible that at this epoch, the mechanical genius of the Greeks was considerably advanced. It would not be surprising if military art, in particular the machines used, in fact profited from certain works which combined pure and applied research. Once again we come across Archytas of Tarentum, born around 430 BC, and who died in a shipwreck on the coast of Apulia in approximately 348 BC.
This great individual was both a statesman and a man of science, but was also interested in techniques. A disciple of Philolaus and a member of the school of Pythagoras, he wrote many works of which only minute fragments remain. He was interested in mathematics and physics, and proved himself to be a scientist equal if not superior to
Democritus or Pythagoras.
But on the other hand, Archytas was presented as a veritable inventor. The screw, pulley, rattle, and kite were all attributed to him.
He constructed a flying dove, which the whole of Antiquity related with admiration. Vitruvius quotes Archytas as having written tracts on applied mechanics. "Archytas, who excelled in the fabrication of machines, wished to incorporate geometry and speculation in everyday uses, having applied it in all sorts of ways." Again Plutarch writes of him:
The art of the inventor, of organizing instruments and machines which is termed mechanics or organics, greatly valued and admired by all sorts of people, was first put into practice by Archytas and Eudoxus of Cnidus, partly to embellish somewhat the science of geometry by this favour, and partly to support and strengthen some geometric propositions by visible and material examples of instruments, intellectual examples of which could only be found by concrete and necessary reasoning.
And Plutarch continues to cite the solution to the problem provided by this instrument which was called the mesolabe.
Although Plutarch may be an excellent and faithful portraitist, there is a more interesting element to be found in this short passage. All the evidence illustrates that Archytas was a prodigious inventor and mechanic, but he was never mentioned among the mechanics of the school of Alexander. But more especially, this passage enables us to grasp the link between science and technology to which we referred earlier. Like arithmetic, geometry was to henceforth serve the techni cian. Arithmetic was to aid the scientist in arriving at certain solutions which could not be achieved by applying the process of reason: thus

===== PAGE 290 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
273 special instruments were necessary, the most famous of which was the mesolabe. We will also return to this form of mechanics which was
"greatly valued and admired by all sorts of people," a statement which would appear to contradict other opinions.
As far as we may judge, Archytas appeared to play an essential role in introducing a more rational system of applied mechanics. He represents an irreplaceable stage, and it is most regrettable that his work is not better known. Let us say rather, in order not to favour an author to whom much has been attributed because he is not well known, that the end of the 5th century and the beginning of the 4th century BC certainly marked a turning point in the composition of infinitely more refined, advanced, researched, and not to say more scientific techniques. They did not have to wait long for the results of the evolution translated by this composition.
The considerable expansion of the art of poliorcetics may be obser ved in the first half of the 4th century BC. Henceforth, techniques were fairly well incorporated into a system for which complete expositions, if not a well established theory, could be made. These accounts are probably the first technical treatises which we possess. Unfortunately our sources are always extremely discreet and tenuous.
During this time, the Greeks certainly borrowed some of their war machines from other civilizations. The Orientals were past masters in this art of poliorcetics. Xenophon attributed his hero with a keen concern for the machines, which he did not use moreover, aimed at bringing down the walls of Babylon. Was this a personal experience taken from the first half of the 4th century? Or the result of a slow but sure evolution which took place in Greece? Whatever the case, extremely old Assyrian bas-reliefs depict battering rams mounted on a kind of tower on rollers.
It is possible that ideas were also borrowed through Sicily. The Carthaginians seem to have been the first to create a large collection of machines for use in sieges. To besiege Selinus, the Carthaginians took and erected a considerable number of such machines. Diodorus relates that the towers were taller than any others built so far, and that iron-tipped battering rams were also used. All these machines terrified the people of Selinus, and the walls of the city effectively gave way under their force.
Stirring accounts written some years later illustrate the preparations made by the tyrant Dionysius the Elder and his allies against the

===== PAGE 291 =====

274
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Carthaginians. Interest in machines had spread, and leaders undertook to develop and increase their number. In 399 BC, Diony sius called upon the workers of all the villages he had conquered to build his armament. But workers also came from Italy, Greece and even from the Carthaginian states, drawn by the promise of high wages and substantial rewards. The rationalization of this production should also be pointed out, as numerous types of machines had been adopted so that each troop on the pay role could find weapons used in his native country in this arsenal. Moreover, inventions were encouraged and were appropriately rewarded. According to Diodorus and Aelian but later contested by Pliny, the catapult was developed in this way.
Diodorus also states that quadrireme and pentaric ships were first constructed on this occasion. A considerable armament had therefore been formed bearing, one may suppose, valuable comparisons.
This whole armament was used against the town of Motya. It is interesting to note that when Dionysius arrived before the enemy city, he examined the defenses "with his architects." The siege did not only depend upon bravery, speed and attack, but also required technical understanding. The machines were positioned both against the fortress and an eventual rescue fleet. When the latter arrived, under the command of Himlico, catapults were launched against it, new missiles which had recently been invented "and which were used for the first time", striking terror in the heart of the enemy. The catapults pushed back the defenders of the walls which could then be battered down with the rams. Elsewhere mobile wooden towers, with six floors, moved on wheels and equipped with movable bridges which were proped against the top of the curtains, were also used.
It was therefore the first time that a modern siege had taken place using an important and extensive armament in a rational and calcu lated way. A new technique had truly been created, and it is not surprising that shortly after this the first treatise on siegecraft could be compiled.
Aeneas lived at the beginning of the reign of Philip of Macedonia.
According to Polybius, Suidas and even some texts by Aeneas which have come down to us, this author had compiled a number of military works: Signals by fire; Stratagems; Preparations for war; Finances; Of castrametation.
We have only the treatise on the defence of fortified towns, and that is merely an abridged version which, according to Aelian, was

===== PAGE 292 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
275 compiled several years later by Cyneas of Thessaly, general and confident to Pyrrhus, the king of Epirus. We are fairly certain of the period when Aeneas lived. According to the most recent episode mentioned in his work and in the absence of any mention of the third holy war or of the campaigns of Philip, we can date the work between
380 and 360 BC, in other words the first half of the 4th century BC.
There is no evidence to suppose that any author preceded him, and it is worth noting that his successors quote only him and appear to consider him the first and foremost military author whom they know.
Information on machines is very rare in this work on the defense of fortified towns. It is possible that Aeneas may have included them in his work on the preparations for war, or they were recorded in a specific treatise of which no trace remains. The remaining work does not deal either with fortifications, which formed the subject of a specific work by this author and has also disappeared. The subjects which do survive in the remaining work are notes on incendiary materials made from pitch, sulphur, oakum and grains of incense, and notes on methods of protecting oneself from them, particularly by using vinegar which the Ancients believed had great power. Aeneas also writes of countermines and the method of using bronze shields to detect the underground approach of the assailants.
The compilation of this technical work was in keeping with the taste for contemporary thought and didactic literature devoted to military subjects, as is attested by many of the works by Xenophon. Our knowledge of this early author remains very limited, for we know nothing of where he came from or of his life. We cannot say whether he merely compiled facts or whether he actually participated in these military campaigns.
It is highly likely that the need was felt for a sort of new codification of military rules and techniques following all the inventions which had revolutionized the art of war from Artemon of Clazomenes to the
Cathaginians and the engineers of Dionysius. In the first third of the 4th century BC, the Greek world thus possessed a new and relatively complete system of mechanized war machines, which needed only to be perfected. Against this, a new art of fortification was to arise, of which we are vaguely aware by observing the achievements of this epoch. Simultaneously, engineers, in the sense that they used engines, or architects existed - although it is difficult to name them - to whom powerful leaders, tyrants or monarchs, accorded favours in order to

===== PAGE 293 =====

276
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
procure their services. Artemon of Clazomenes was the first of these, and although we do not have the names of those who served Dionysius the Elder, it is already possible that these individuals had travelled throughout the Greek world and learnt their trade from their elders.
The campaigns of Philip and Alexander were to enable the whole of this partly new mechanization to be put into a concrete and more perfect form, as certain historians have already pointed out. Apart from this, schools also seem to have been formed during this period, and the most famous engineers were soon surrounded by pupils who disagreed with the teaching of their masters and dreamt of their own successes.
Polyidos of Thessaly was engineer to Philip of Macedonia. His followers depicted him as a talented man, a fact which does not deny the victories of his monarch. In particular he regularized the appli cation of the battering ram, used with great success at the siege of
Byzantium. Athenodorus and Vitruvius quote him for these same reasons and speak very highly of him.
We are better informed about his pupils, Diades and Charias, engineers to Alexander the Great, both of whom had learnt their trade from Polyidos. In the 1st century BC, Athenodorus still knew of
Diades' treatise on war engines, which we unfortunately no longer possess. In this treatise, Diades credited himself with the invention of transportable towers and engines known by the names of trepans,
"crows" and hanging bridges. He also employed the battering ram mounted on wheels, or at least he gives a description of it. It is only
Athenodorus who is content to summarize his predecessor, and he is the source of all our information on Diades' machines.
The height of the smallest movable tower was usually 60 cubits (27.72 m) and with a square base of 17 cubits (7.90 m) on the side. They had ten storeys, the highest of which was a smaller square, measuring a fifth of the base surface area. Other towers were 90 cubits high ( 41 .60 m) and had fifteen storeys, and lastly towers measuring 124 cubits
(about 57.30 m) were constructed, which had twenty storeys with a side base of23 l/2 cubits (10.86 m). Each tower storey was encircled by a covered way three cubits wide (0.39 m), to facilitate aid in case offire.
The towers were mounted on six or eight wheels, owing to their considerable mass. The height of the storeys decreased towards the top, as the size of the planes got smaller. Care was taken in the construction of these contraptions, to conserve the same proportions, dimensions, and materials in towers of different heights.

===== PAGE 294 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
277 This was also true of the testudo which carried the battering ram (fig.
12 and 13). The largest was 30 cubits in width (13.86 m) and 40 cubits in length (18.48 m), with a height of 13 cubits (6.00 m), without counting the attic or roof trussing which could be as much as 16 cubits (7. 39 m).
The attic had a covered way which encircled the apparatus. Diades constructed a three storeyed turret on the ground, which housed a catapult above and a water supply below in case of fire.
The trepan had a similar testudo. A channel was incorporated on the base, very like that in euthytonan catapults, and like these machines was equipped with a cross winch. Two pullies were fixed at the other end of the channel, used to fire the steel tipped beam which advanced on rollers. The beam was then pulled back by capstans. "With such a well constructed machine,'' notes Athenodorus, "an engineer could achieve glory."
Athenodorus did not describe the crow and Diades himself had not included a description of the hanging bridge. One notes, nevertheless, with Alexander's engineer, a singular growth in the types and numbers of Macedonian armed machines. Diades had also created war engines for naval combat, but we have no precise information on this subject.
Historians' accounts show us all this material at work, and we know the admiration excited by the skill with which Alexander's armies lay hold of towns. The famous sieges of Perinthus and Tyre were of special note. One must admit that this success was undoubtedly due as much to the extensive use of the machines as to the sustained violence of the attack.
Nam es of engineers proliferate at the end of the 4th century BC. The Athenian, Epimachos, was engineer to Alexander's nephew, Deme trius Poliorcetes. At the siege of Rhodes, he created the helepolis. This was a machine 83 cubits in height ( 41 .58 m) and 50 cubits wide (23 m), in the form of a tower which could withstand a stone of three talents
(78.53 kg), a feat which terrified the enemy and secured victory for the king of Macedonia. His distant followers continued to speak of it with great admiration and the machine was described in treatise after treatise.
Philo of Athens lived at the end of the 4th century BC. According to Vitruvius he worked on the temples of Ceres and Proserpine at
Eleusis, and he would have written a treatise on the proportions of the temples. Similarly, he should also be credited with the arsenal of
Piraeus, later destroyed by Sulla, and on which he wrote another tract

===== PAGE 295 =====

278
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 1 2. - Hegetor's testudo. (From Choisy, Vitruve, Paris, 1 905)

===== PAGE 296 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
Figure 1 3. - Plan and elevation of Hegetor's testudo. (From a I 0th century Greek manuscript)
279 which was still considered the definitive work at the time of Vitruvius.
Plutarch said that this arsenal was a unanimously admired edifice. An inscription engraved on a marble flag has since been preserved. The work was started in 346 BC and completed in 328 BC. The building incorporated both a public walk and an arsenal, situated between the agora and the ships' berths. The interior was long and narrow in the form of a gallery, divided into three naves by two rows of slender pillars, with a central nave reserved for people to walk down. Loop holes were built high in the walls and there were two large doors at either end. The walls were built without foundations, in layers of superimposed stones, without mortar but held together with iron fitments and sealed with lead. The framework consisted of supporting wooden structures, so the idea of roof timbers is absent. It consisted mainly of a pile of wood beams which rested against each other, the

===== PAGE 297 =====

280
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
weight of which acted vertically without causing tension. These prem ises were used to house naval material and war machines.
Philo of Athens also wrote a treatise on poliorcetics. Books IV and V by Philo of Byzantium were probably only a summary or an adaptation. Philo of Athens himself was inspired by Aeneas, and added some elements on military art provided by the conquests of
Philip and Alexander.
Athenodorus also quotes king Pyrrhus of Epirus, almost at the same date as Epimachos. The monarch would also have written works on the art of poliorcetics, in which specifications for mining techniques would have appeared.
Thus a tradition of authors was gradually established, whose discoveries and modifications, made for the most part during the 4th century BC, were handed down from generation to generation. A collection of works on military techniques was formed, before the school of Alexander had been established and the teaching of this subject had been systematized. Very little of this literature is available, but in the 1st century BC, Vitruvius still knew of these works and used them extensively. At the very most we may detect an evolution which progressed much more rapidly during the course of the 4th century.
Nevertheless, one must stress this attempt to synthesize, to put techni cal knowledge affecting many domains into a didactic form. The intro duction of a form of technical literature is an extremely important fact.
It is certainly true that our authors are far more meagre when it comes to other techniques. Indeed, Greece very rapidly undertook large scale works, whereby the skill and science of the engineer could be displayed. In many cases, this did not involve the simple work of the entrepreneur, but an obvious concern for the application of scientific theories was demonstrated, indeed even shown to be of paramount importance.
Information about Hippodamus, who lived in the second half of the 5th century BC, is rather more vague. According to some Antique authorities, he would have instigated a veritable revolution by con structing several large towns on a regular plan, with wide roads intersecting at right angles: Piraeus, Thurii in 443 BC, and Rhodes in
408/407 BC. This town planner would have had a profound influence.
Aristotle said that he was skilled in the science of nature.
In fact, the Ionians seem to have developed towns built to regular plans before Hippodamus, in particular colonies, or certain colonies of

===== PAGE 298 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
281 Miletus. Miletus was rebuilt on regular plan, moreover, in 479 BC, after the partial destruction of the town. When the Miton of
Aristophanes designed his town of Nephelococcygia, he did it according to a perfectly regular plan. In any case, most recent histori ans doubt that Hippodamus could have constructed both Piraeus, built in 479 BC, and Rhodes. Hippodamus would have been born in Miletus in around 500 BC, and he would have arrived in Athens under
Themistocles. His work is difficult to identify precisely. But Hippoda mus, or others, certainly developed towns on a regular plan, be they circular, square or rectangular, in the beginning of the 5th century BC.
Herodotes elaborated on the Samians because it was in their town he could see the three most famous works of Antiquity. The first was the temple, constructed by Rhoicos, son of Philo, of Samos. The second was the breakwater, actually built in the sea to protect the port. It was constructed at a depth of twenty orgyies and was the length of two stadiums. Lastly, there was the tunnel which enabled water con veyance to Samos. This had been built in the 6th century BC, under the direction of Eupalinus, son of Naustrophus, born at Megara. The underground conduit was 835 metres long and the tunnel which carried it across a hilly terrain was 1 100 metres long. Twenty vertical wells provided access holes so that the conduit could be cleaned and maintained. The height and length of the construction was eight feet.
The adduction canal, twenty cubits deep and three feet wide, ran alongside one of the walls, and the channel was made of terracotta. On the other side was a path where a man could walk round. The whole construction was faced and vaulted. The completion of this work presupposes the aquisition of major techniques: some defects show, however, that the technique or techniques employed were not abso lutely perfect. Building had been started simultaneously at both ends, so that a process of levelling had to be put into operation, possibly using methods which we will find once more with Hero of Alexandria.
Nevertheless, errors were made and at 425 metres from the south exit, the tunnel must have deverted, as it was designed to be straight. The slope was gentle, so gentle in fact that it proved to be inadequate. The end of the tunnel subsequently had to be dug deeper and deeper towards the north exit. After the tunnel, 400 metres of underground channel was laid to reach the town. Despite the faults which were later corrected, the Samos tunnel represents a significant achievement, requiring already advanced techniques.

===== PAGE 299 =====

282
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The techniques used in building aqueducts were both simpler and older. We should mention the aqueduct built by Pisistrates at Athens in the 6th century BC. This went from the Hymettus to the theatre of
Herodes Atticus, and had two branches. It incorporated the old distribution network by a system of water tanks. Water flowed regularly and freely, and manholes were built every thirty to forty metres along its length.
Plato rated Thales of Miletus among the men who "were learned in the mechanical arts." According to a tradition reported by Herodo tus, in 558 BC Thales had diverted the course of the river Halys, in order to enable Creosus' army to cross to the other side. At least he may have made it possible to ford by digging a diversion canal. There are still doubts about the origin of the siphon. The first example is that of the Lycia aqueduct, but unfortunately we do not know its date. In any case, it crossed a valley on a wall at the base of which there were two corbelled openings. Pergamum's aqueduct, built in the 2nd century BC, is the most interesting. The two end points were situated at 375 metres and 332 metres. But it had to cross two valleys whih were 172 metres and 195 metres deep. Pressure at the bottom of the siphons was 20 and 17 atmospheres respectively. The pipes were made of bronze or lead, as terracotta could not support such pres sures. There are not many examples of these but we can cite a few significant ones, which show that the Greeks had achieved a degree of skill in this field.
We cannot simply say that Greek techniques were blocked, to the point that some authorities have suggested, that the technical contri bution of the Greeks was relatively poor. It is therefore necessary to make an appraisal, and only after this, may we discover why Greek techniques did not undergo any other developments. Chronologies should not be forgotten either. We will see in a moment that the
Greeks themselves attempted this appraisal with the school of Alexan dria. Was it possible to continue the effort required in technical imagination? We have already reached the age of the explosion of
Alexander's empire, and the beginning of the first Roman enterprises.
The political situation was hardly favourable for this development, but contrary to the suggestions made by a number of historians, Greek science was not capable of providing the Hellenistic civilization with techniques, as these same historians have suggested. We will therefore very briefly consider these two problems.

===== PAGE 300 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
THE SCHOOL OF ALEXANDRIA
AND ITS POTENTIAL
283 At the end of the 4th century, Greek technology found its devel opment blocked, at the same time as institutions and societies of the
Classical period had just disappeared under the influence of the first great empires. But we could also suggest that the new masters of the
Antique world, smitten with power and concerned for efficiency, were to attach greater importance to the possession of a material world, or at the very least a material world which could be of service to them. As we have pointed out, Philip and Alexander had already manifested an interest with regard to this. Most astonishing is the work of the various
Ptolemies of Alexandria, who in many ways foreshadowed the efforts of the enlightened Renaissance princes. The extraordinary conver gence between science and techniques, both striving for fulfilment if not progress, should be emphasized.
Ptolemy Soter, who took the throne of Egypt in 305 BC, was certainly not very cultivated, but this is not so surprising considering the origins of the man. Nevertheless, he demonstrated great taste in patronizing and gathering together the greatest scientists and techni cians of his time. Yet again, the Renaissance provides similar examples. The Greek expansion had, to some extent, scattered these attempts, and the respective isolation of the various schools seemed to have slowed down any progress. It therefore seemed good politics, in every sense of the term, to reunite in one place the most distinguished men and books of the Greek world. On the succession of Ptolemy
Philadelphus, in 283 BC, for whom virtually everything had been completed, the school of Alexandria was already enjoying the height of its fame.
· Zenodotus, the chief head of the library, relentlessly collected the most famous works, bought them from many countries, and even requisitioned books found on ships. The expense involved and the fame of the library undoubtedly enabled a rapid increase in collections of works. Neleus, curator of the books of Aristotle and Theophrastus, sold those under his charge. During the epoch of Ptolemy Euergetes
(246/221 BC), Callimachus states that the library contained 400,000 unordered scrolls, containing several works, and 90,000 properly

===== PAGE 301 =====

284
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
arranged scrolls. A secondary library, containing 42,800 scrolls, was established at the Sarapeum. Even if some works were duplicated, the members of the school could certainly find there almost everything which Greece had provided.
Demetrius of Phaleron, who had established the peripatetic school at Athens, also founded the museum of Alexandria on the same model. Apart from a library, it had a walk, a large dinning room for communial meals, and sleeping quarters. But some completely new elements may be observed, such as the introduction of dissecting rooms, observatories, and a zoological garden.
Obviously we do not know exactly what Ptolemy Soter demanded of the people he sent for, or much about the people who often came to see the treasures collected at Alexandria. It was probably less to do with imagination than with acquiring knowledge, less concerned with pro gress than with an appraisal. The founders of the school of Alexandria wished above all to collect together all the information avaliable, and to make a sort of encyclopedia. We should certainly stress two points which may or may not have been known at the outset. Insofar as an assessment of the whole was undertaken, knowledge was accumulated and organized, that is to say the arrangement of scientific systems was to be initiated, which could in turn stimulate new discoveries. Apart from this - and we will become increasingly aware of this phenomenon
- they were to arrive at a confrontation of the various kinds of knowledge. Thus, they did not stop at the noble sciences, sciences which had become pure, but introduced into research not only medi cine, already elevated by Hippocrates, but also techniques; techniques which had fervent followers, and which, as we have seen, were the subjects of written works. Perhaps these pure sciences were found to be less pure than one had wished, and that the techniques had become more reasoned than had previously been thought. It would therefore seem interesting to make a very rapid survey of the whole range of works.
Euclid was one of these scientists who attempted to make such an appraisal: there were, moreover, earlier examples in certain subjects.
He must have arrived at Alexandria around 300 BC. He was both a mathematician and a physicist, but unfortunately we only possess a fragment of his work. Apart from the Elements which made him famous, he wrote three other books on Data (definitions), two books on Places on the surface, four books on Conics, which replaced the

===== PAGE 302 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
285 treatises by Apollonius of Perga, and one work on Paralogisms in geometry. On the subject of physics, he is credited with a work on optics, and astronomical phenomena. Finally, he probably compiled a work on mechanics, De levi et ponderoso. What is particularly inter esting in this work, is the attempt on the part of the author to generalize a geometric system. If Euclid followed the Pythagorean theory of rays emitted by the eye, he only made a geometric hypothe sis: his ray of light is in fact a straight line. By the way, he did reveal some facts which technicians may have known already, which they certainly used: shadows to calculate heights, laws of reflection on flat surfaces and the possibility of lighting a fire by using a mirror. He may have used identical methods for anything mechanical.
Strato of Lampsacus had followed the lessons of Theophrastus before coming to Alexandria where he was entrusted with the task of educating Ptolemy Soter's son, Philadelphus. He arrived in Alexan dria in about 299 BC, and left in 285 BC to succeed to Theophrastus. In the assessment he made of the physics of his age, and of which we only possess a few fragments, Strato gave experimentation a greater place, wishing to make physics a more positive activity, and thus more available to technicians. But in necessarily tackling specific questions, his form of physics could not be established in a system. His reflections on gravity and space are interesting but they could not be explanatory.
It was almost as a technician that he recognized the proportionality of gravity to mass, and the phenomenon of increasing acceleration in natural movements.
Herophilus was probably the founder of the medical school of Alexandria. He also appeared to be the true founder of the science of anatomy, on which he wrote a book which has since disappeared. With him began the reign of empiricism. He may have been the first to practise dissection, as he was interested in the vascular system and in certain organs. He studied the nervous system, the centre of which he located in the brain, and sketched an outline of the respiratory circulation. He did not seek to provide general explanations, or abstract relationships. His therapeutics were also based on concrete observation.
lȮristarchus of Samos had been Strato's pupil, and was interested in physics and the problem of light, but he was especially fascinated by astronomy, and made an observation of the summer solstice in either
281 BC or 280 BC. He wrote a treatise dealing with the dimensions and

===== PAGE 303 =====

286
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
distance of the sun and the moon, which has fortunately been preser ved. His method is purely geometric and founded on the measurement of angles and on a problem of trigonometry. The results are deceptive, mainly because he lacked suitable instruments. His compatriot,
Conon, was also interested in astronomy and geometry. He wrote seven books on astronomy, particularly on eclipses. Unfortunately he died too young to demonstrate all his ability.
Eratosthenes appeared in the second generation of the Alexandria school. Born at Cyrene, he went to Athens before arriving at Alexan dria, around 244 BC. There, he became head of the library. Two of his works are particularly important, the geography, and the treatise on measures, only parts of which are extant. He also attempted to construct a geometry of space: Aristarchus had tried to prove that the sun was the centre of the system, and Eratosthenes declared his belief that the earth was round. He managed to make a fairly accurate assessment of the distance between the earth and the sun, but his use of the famous method of the moon's dichotomy was not so successful, for his calculation of the distance beteen the moon and earth was wildly inaccurate.
Erasistratus, a doctor, was a contemporary of Eratosthenes, and it seems he was known chiefly for his works on physiology and pathologi cal anatomy. He also completed Herophilus' works on nerves and the brain. He studied the heart, but encountered several difficulties when it came to the circulation of the blood.
So within two generations, Alexandrine science had managed to make some important contributions. It is possible that the first of the
Ptolomies, whose education had been somewhat limited, had wanted to express his awareness of the culture in which he lived. This is difficult to prove of course, but if one considers this in the light of the
Renaissance, it is less easily dismissed. He therefore gave his son the best education possible, and this explains the almost gluttonous desire to bring together the greatest minds, works and scientific knowledge in this period of Greek history. It is also possible that he discouraged fruitless abstract discussion, and promoted the ideas of practical assessment, concrete results and clear explanation. All this was prac tised within the limited confines of their scientific knowledge consisting of geometry and arithmetics. Similar efforts were to be made by the
Sforza, Montefeltro and Malatesta families. The techniques of obser vation and experimentation were gradually acquired in this way: the

===== PAGE 304 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
287 improvement of techniques was the main object, and all the more so because this area of research above all others was closest to the
Sovereign's heart.
This preoccupation is evident in the problems of pseudo Aristotelian Mechanica, now considered to date from 280 BC, and which was written very much in the spirit of the Alexandrian school. It is a theoretical work, but nonetheless it still deals at length with practical aspects. All the problems of mechanics are explained with reference to the principle of the lever, which was itself derived from the circle. Distinctions are made between the natural aspects of this technique, and the human elements, which are often contrary to nature: by means of a lever, a light weight can lift a much heavier one.
This work shows a good understanding of statics, but the treatment of dynamics is not nearly so successful. Hero of Alexandria also dealt with a large number of these problems, and conducted many concrete experiments besides. He experimented with the transport of heavy loads by means of rollers and carts and with the chock wedge, capable of holding enormous loads in place, simply by opposing the latter's weight with the weight of the earth. He also experimented with pincers and finally, with groups of pulleys in the aim of reducing the amount of power necessary to lift a weight - this became known as the blade and tackle. One of the notable characteristics of Alexandrian scholars was their refusal to consider any technical problem that could not be explained in scientific terms. Thus, for example, they were able to explain bodies in equilibrium, but not in movement.
Archimedes is a good symbol of the technico-scientific ideal of the Alexandrian school. He lived in Alexandria when he was still fairly young, rubbing shoulders with the scientists we discussed above, and reaping all the benefits afforded by the exceptional library and museum. He strengthened the growing awareness of the close rela tionship between science and techniques as a result of his research into practical statics, and his declaration that every massive body has a centre of gravity. He then used a minimum of postulates - whose technical basis cannot be disputed - to establish a logical series of laws.
In fact, it was techniques which were the real basis of his theoretical work. The theory of floating bodies, which is the base of hydrostatics, is a similar case.
A scientist of genius, Archimedes also enjoyed the reputation of being a skilful technician, so much so that the Ancients, especially

===== PAGE 305 =====

288
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
during the later period, gave him the credit for a considerable number of inventions. Of course, geniuses are apt to receive the credit for everything, and it is noteworthy that the technical curiosities usually associated with him are also found related to all the other technicians from the School. He does not seem to have written anything technical, which according to Plutarch and those after him represented a disdain for material things but which in fact was not at all in the spirit of the
Alexandrian school. The catalogue of Archimedes' technical works, such as it has been passed down to us today, is hardly.comprehensive.
Archimedes' "inventions" can be divided into two groups, those concerning the siege of Syracuse and the others. Ballistas used for throwing stones, and arrows were already known at this time, and furthermore, Marcellus used a machine which was so enormous that it took eight galleys joined together to carry it. It is possible that the
Archimedes' machines were larger and better equipped, they were capable of throwing stones weighing ten talents - or about 260 kg - but the principles of these machine were no different to those already in use. We know that Euclid was familiar with the convergent mirror, which he had described in geometrical terms, but it is possible that he was unable to put theory into practice, because of the inadequacy of current polishing techniques. As far as fortification was concerned,
Archimedes confined himself to building some loopholes, and fortify ing the lower parts of the town. And after all, was not the town finally captured?
As for the rest, the so-called archimedean screw had been intro duced into the Nile valley long before the time of Archimedes, and it was mentioned in the pseudo-Aristotelian Mechanica. Hydraulic organs were known to Ctesibios, an older contemporary of Archi medes, whom we shall discuss later. The apparatuses for raising ships were perhaps more original - then again this was a problem that interested all technicians during this period.
There is a great deal of evidence to show that Archimedes was certainly interested in machines, and it is not impossible that with a more extensive knowledge of science, he could have improved the basic principles of some of these, and perhaps even have expounded the theories on which they were based. This brings to mind a term coined by Plutarch, "simple games of geometry." And once more we find the one great autonomous science (or which had become autonomous), being extended to science and techniques. The desire to

===== PAGE 306 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
289 reduce techniques to an exercise in geometry began at this point and continued right up until the Renaissance. But unfortunately, mechan ics does not consist merely of statics and geometry, and the failure by the scientists and technicians to grasp the theory of dynamics necessa rily limited their ambitions. With all due respect to Plutarch, it is very likely that Archimedes did not write anything on techniques, because since he did not invent anything of note, he had nothing particularly new or original to say. It is far easier to imagine Archimedes rationalis ing known techniques, pointing out mistakes of assembly or dimen sions, and in particular, attempting to induce general laws from the confused tangle of empirical results obtained from experiments.
The Alexandrian school was the centre then, of a remarkable convergence of all sorts of intellectual endeavours. The preference for concrete experiment and observation necessarily prompted an interest in techniques. Some works on techniques had already been written, but these were fragmented and incomplete, and an assessment was needed. The range of techniques considered in these assessments was relatively small. Military techniques, recent inventions, the techniques which could be "geometrised" and finally an element which has been rather scornfully called, "the physics for amusement," formed the main basis of such works. The works dating from this period reflect the limited scope of their concerns which did not begin to include archi tecture, artisan techniques, ships, and many other areas. But it is possible of course that some of the works may have disappeared.
We know very little about the apparent founder of the Alexandrian school, Ctesibios, and all that we do know has been gleaned from the rare allusions made to him in works written by his immediate and distant successors. He is mentioned along with Archimedes in Vitru vius' first book, as being one of main authorities for the training of engineers, and as being himself one of the most famous mechanicians in Antiquity. His written work was obviously still in existence at this time. He was a mysterious character, who lived during the first half of the 3rd century BC, which made him a contemporary of Archimedes.
He came from relatively humble origins, the son of a barber estab lished in a suburb of Alexandria. He appears to have manifested a remarkable gift for mechanics from a very early age, and his work on the subject was apparently a major one, which regrettably has entirely disappeared. This means that we are limited to discussing only those
"inventions" which are usually attributed to him.

===== PAGE 307 =====

290
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
It was Ctesibios who invented the water organ, which has been described by Philo of Byzantium, Hero, and Vitruvius (fig. 14). As the diagrams show, the organ was powered by hand-bellows. There was a tank of water whose pressure controlled and prolonged the air-flow which the bellows forced back into the apparatus. There were three sets of bellows arranged in a triangle. The air accumulated in goatskin bags, and was then passed into the apparatus, from whence it escaped at a regular pressure into the sound pipes. Philo and Vitruvius have given us some good descriptions of the force-pump, which is so well-known that its importance does not need to be emphasized here
(fig. 15). According to Vitruvius, the pistons were polished with a lathe, rubbed down and oiled. We know nothing about how the cylinders were made, but this was certainly a more complex operation.
The difficulties involved in constructing these machines meant that only relatively few were built.
Ctesibios is also supposed to have invented the water clock, whose basic principle is fairly simple, but Vitruvius did not conceal the fact that this clock was not entirely reliable. Philo also mentions a military apparatus which was apparently invented by Ctesibios, but he did not expand on this. Athenaios mentions a machine which was capable of penetrating enemy walls, consisting of a large pole which pivoted on spindles. There is mention of a machine called a "bronze-spring engine" (chalkeutone) which is essentially a ballista, but equipped with a metal spring instead of a rope. The spring could only have been made of steel, however, which was hardly known at this time. The final machine in this list was known as an air-spring engine, or aerotonon, which was in fact a machine using compressed air acting on a spring, so that it merely operated as a trigger. It was made of pounded bronze, shaped on a lathe with flat regular surfaces, and was equipped with pistons.
It seems that unlike his contemporaries, Ctesibios did not attempt to provide any assessments. As far as we can gather, he simply confined himself to expounding various ideas based on observation, such as the elasticity of air, for example, and the incompressibility of water. He also considered what could be taken from these ideas to create machines, even though the latter were in fact technically unrealisable.
At any event, he is certainly accepted as being the founder of this school of mechanicians, and his mechanical skills were clearly recog nised, as were his numerous inventions.

===== PAGE 308 =====

291 Figure 14. - Water organs. (From Choisy)

TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS

)\ t Figure 15. - Suction pump by Ctesibios. (From Choisy)

===== PAGE 310 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
293 Vitruvius, for his part, writes about him:
Furthermore, this was not the only inspirational idea of Ctesibios, for he also discovered another principle whose use was manifold: this was that of liquid forced under pressure to produce impulses found in nature. For example, blackbird song could be produced by the action of water and divers, and the same water could produce moving figures and many other things, whose effects charm the eye and delight the ear.
Ctesibios must also have attempted, therefore, the difficult art of constructing automatons, which was to give rise to many mechanisms so familiar to us today.
It is not inconceivable that a part of Ctesibios' work was included in that of his successor's, Philo of Byzantium, even though in certain passages the latter took great care to stress his originality with regard to the former. In any event, Philo's work is the first one by a mechanician which is still largely intact. This takes us to the pro gramme of mechanical "syntax," whose importance lasted until the
10th century. The titles themselves of the programme make interesting reading (the extant works are preceded by the "+" sign): Treatise on levers; (+) Pneumatics; Treatise on automatons; Treatise on wonderful instruments (organs and pipes) ; Treatise on the traction of heavy burdens (the Baroulkos) ; ( +) Treatise on clepsydras; Treatise on the building of sea ports; ( +) Treatise on the building of fortresses; ( +)
Treatise on war engines; ( +) Treatise on self-propelled wheels. It is possible that these various treatises were preceded by a general introduction on mathematics and general physics. This collection was completed by Hero. The progamme was concerned only with new techniques, not with traditional ones. Again, it was mainly an asses sment, so that not everything should be accredited to the authors.
The treatise on Pneumatics is known through an arabic translation:
it is well written, succinct, and comprehensive. It begins with a series of theories on the nature of air and its relationship and junction with water, and on vacuums. In other words, it deals with the same problems as those already considered by Ctesibios. The physics is fairly elementary, founded on a number of simple experiments and resulting in the idea that no vacuum can possibly exist. Philo also establishes the compressibility of air, the equilibrium of fluids in communicating vessels, and the principle of the siphon. The rest of the work is devoted to a presentation of the "marvellous containers", or
"magic jars" which use these various properties: like the intermittent

===== PAGE 311 =====

294
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
fountains, vessels with two liquids, and the wine-thieving cup. This section ends with a presentation of some automatons, such as the horse and trough, or the bird in its nest, flapping its wings and frightening away a snake which jumps up from the ground. A description of multi-channel taps follows this, along with whistling waterwheels and singing animals (compressed water, with air escaping at a controlled rate). The work ends with more descriptions of more utilitarian objects, like the noria with bucket line chains, Ctesibios' air pump, various elevating apparatuses with balance beams, winches, wheels with squirrel cages and camshafts. After one general essay, the
Pneumatics rapidly turns into what was later to be called a "theatre of machines. " The work on clepsydras was probably written by Philo, although there is no definite proof of this. We know of this work only through the arab version, which is somewhat truncated and poorly written. The dimensions of clepsydras are carefully noted in the account of the main mechanism. According to this particular description, the clock is regulated by an elbow-pipe, the perpendicular part of which turned within a semi-circle divided up into the signs of the zodiac. A pointer is connected to this pipe, which controls the flow. Time was calculated in terms of temporal hours; i.e. , hours which varied according to the seasons and climates. This was an ancient and widely used formula.
The apparatus could be equipped with various automatons, with bells and different characters, for example, such as the flute player which performed by means of a horn, a siphon and compressed air.
Philo's other treatises all dealt with the art of war. We still have a treatise on poliorcetics and a treatise on war engines. The treatise on fortification is the earliest complete work of this kind in our possession.
Philo was part of a long line of tradition, however, and his fourth and fifth books are merely taking up the ideas of a treatise by Philo of
Athens. The work lacks logic, although it does contain all the major principles of classical fortification in this period. There is less inno vation than codification, and all the references are there, in particular those refering to Rhodian engineers. It is in fact a scientific mixture of formulas and empiricism. "The location of all ramparts, as well as their slope, curvature and extensions, should always be determined by the nature of the area." This is the first rule: adapt according to the terrain.
It is only at the end of the work that Philo talks about the great systems of fortification built by his contempories, and the traces they adopted

===== PAGE 312 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
295 according to the type of environment: "maiander traces" or meander systems in fields, semi circular or saw-tooth traces for undulating areas, double systems for pronounced re-entrants, and traces with oblique curtains for triangular salients. Lateral flanking was evidently one of the main principles of these traces. Towers and posterns were essential elements, outflanking on the outside, and aligned on the inside. Notes about the principle of coveriQ_g the stonework with earth
"face-masking" the priority of thickness before height, the maximum tilt of flanking parts, and the need for an easy passage around the walls are accompanied by notations about the problems of providing supplies and the modernisation processes of ancient fortifications. The subject is dealt with thoroughly, therefore, and its treatment reveals a complete mastery of some techniques. The work ends with a discussion on attacks. Apart from a few psychological or tactical considerations, it represents in embryonic form a treatise on war machines: scaling instruments, testudos, and armoured moveable towers. It seems that the author has acknowledged the lessons learnt from the last great campaigns of Philip of Macedonia and Alexander the Great.
The treatise on missile-throwing machines is extremely important in that it enables us to take a much closer, more direct look at the way in which these mechanicians worked. According to Philo, previous authors - which proves that his was not the first treatise - differed on the essential principles, particularly about how to establish the propor tions of the smaller parts. Philo believed that a module should be used so that the machines could be constructed according to clearly defined proportions. This module, the Tovo®, represents the diameter of the rope of sinews which serves as the spring of the arm. Naturally, it is proportional to the weight of the projectile: by increasing the cubic root of the projectile's weight - calculated in dactyls - by a tenth, one can calculate the the diameter of the rope in inches. The extra tenth represents the approximation of the cubic root. The formula goes thus:
d = l,lVp They arrived at this formula by a process of trial and error, and by drawing up tables, some examples of the most common of which are provided by Philo. There was also the problem of measuring the projectiles, although Philo does not mention this. The dimensions of the various pieces of the machine were all drawn from this module: the

===== PAGE 313 =====

296
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
spring box, the thickness of the boss, the support, the arm and the length of the spring, which had to be twice that of the arm. And Philo provides us with all the various applicable proportions. In order to assess certain sizes, he employed the method of doubling the cube. The method he used for this was already known, but he took great pleasure in using it himself. Existing machines could be made smaller or larger using similar methods. This rationality in the construction of war engines was not a new phenomenon in Philo's time: "No-one dared depart from the formula,'' he notes. This was not technology, i.e. , a technique founded on a rational basis of scientific explanations, but there was something new in drawing up tables of repeated and organised observations - and the scientists of the Middle Ages and the
Renaissance were to do the same. Philo's reflections were particularly interesting with regard to a technique which would have welcomed a more rational basis, but which lacked the necessary scientific infor mation. Only the practitioner, armed with practical knowledge could obtain his objective, but this was invariably dependent upon the whims of chance.
Many practitioners have constructed machines of an equal size and identical compo sition to those built by the ancients, they have used similar pieces of wood, and equal quantities of iron. However, although they used different types of loading systems, the range of their machines was far longer, and the effects much greater. They were also a lot more powerful. If they were ever asked how they produced such results, however, they were unable to explain.
Philo considered this lack of rational justification as very serious.
However, there was also the negative side to the problem. "Some aspects cannot be understood by reason and mechancial processes alone. And many discoveries are the result of experiment." This was acknowledging the limits of contemporary science. The most important thing in Philo's opinion was that experiments should be organised, and not totally uncontrolled.
Philo's principle tenet was that technics could no longer be such an uncertain activity. A scientific technology was in sight, but still difficult to understand. The limits of science and practice ultimately blocked possible progress: but one wonders whether it was possible at all, or whether it was the techniques which had simply reached their limits.
