Philo was quite correct in noting that the art of machinery was difficult when so many necessary conditions came into play. "This is what

===== PAGE 314 =====

1HE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
297 happens to our art when so many calculations are required to obtain the final product, or to complete and perfect a work. The slightest deviation from the correct process can lead to faults in the final result."
Talking about one piece of a machine, he specifies: "It should not be considered haphazardly, but with a certain method. " Even though there should be no misunderstanding about the "many calculations",
Philo perceived - better than his teacher Ctesibios, that science was just one of the many tools indispensable to techniques. Moreover, he clearly stated that he would base the descriptions of his machines on
"explanations based as much on mechanical demonstration as on natural reasoning." He never neglected the opportunity to show off his scientific, geometrical or mechanical skills. This attitude of a techni cian goes beyond that of simply describing machines.
Philo also emphasised the "wedge machine," i.e. , the machine with penetrating arrows, stretched with wedges, of his own invention. The description of the mechanism is somewhat confusing, and the manus cripts have no illustrations for reference. The author remarked that the ancient machines were "tiresome and costly, and virtually worthless since they could not withstand prolonged tension." The new machine had a much longer range, it was easier to construct and dismantle, and it was cheaper. The aspects of dismantling and cost are mentioned here for the first time. Philo also insists on the quality of the materials. The iron used to make the springs had to be "extremely pure." They should not be subjected to brutal forging, but rather hammered when cold so that only the outside hardens while the inside remains supple. Metal, therefore, was coming into play; these machines were no longer simply neuro-ballistic.
After having described Ctesibios' machine, Philo goes on to talk about the machine called "polybole." This was probably invented by an Alexandrian "engineer" named Dionysus. The arrangement of this machine was quite unusual and very complicated, but often ingenious.
It required careful attention, however, being difficult to drive and to build. The final machine dealt with by Philo was Ctesibios' air-spring engine, or aerotonon.
This work by Philo of Byzantium, albeit narrowly conceived, is indicative of what we consider to be a state of technical and scientific progress in comparison with the past, even though we know so little about the latter. The most important point is that thanks to all the many scientists and important works under its roofs, the Alexandria

===== PAGE 315 =====

298
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
School enabled links to be established between science and techniques which hitherto had been somewhat ephemeral. Even if Philo was only a compiler, and even if there were other engineers whose names he mentions, nevertheless he conveys an attitude which appears to be highly evolved. Philo's "mechanical syntax" was to be widely studied, plagiarised and developed, and more publicised each time a new discovery was made.
No doubt parallel trends were developing elsewhere. Take the Rhodes School, for example, which we mentioned above. Apollonius of Perga, who was living in the first half of the 3rd century BC, spent a long time in Alexandria, where he studied geometry under the disci ples of Euclid. Apart from Conics, which quite rightly proved to be his greatest claim to fame, he also practised applied science. The Arabs have attributed him certain treatises on mechanics, which are now lost.
Vitruvius mentions him alongside Archytas and Archimedes and others too, who "with the help of calculations and the knowledge of
Nature's secrets, have made great discoveries in mechanics and gno mics, and have left expert treatises to their successors." We are still dealing with the same kind of channels here, then. According to
Proclus and Pappus, Apollonius also wrote about the screw. History still has a place for a man called Agesistratos, who, according to
Athenaeus, wrote a treatise on machines. This is confirmed by Vitru vius who also suggests that his reputation was a major one. Athenaeus' following statement is fairly representative:
It is obvious that if someone is placed in charge of a town's defence, they should be able to trace working drawings in order to provide defence against attacking machines. They should also be able to construct attacking engines capable of resisting defence. For a beginner, this would be extremely difficult, and only those who have made a careful study of this art, who have examined all the relevant research, and who are aware of the precise nature of all the experts' writings and the latest facts regarding this science, will find this task within their capabilities. We need to use worthwhile inventions, and not simply innovate for the sake of it, unless of course we wish to be like the people who seek to deceive those in ignorance, and who prefer the appearance of truth to truth itself.
The science of engines took on its definitive aspect; henceforth, in fact, it was blocked.
Hero of Alexandria is still the object of much discussion: as Dain quite rightly notes, "Hero was for applied sciences what Euclid was for geometry, and judging by the number of fragments which have been preserved under the name of this writer, he could be connected with

===== PAGE 316 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
299 every single treatise on applied science." Hero as a person is an elusive topic of study, but the halo surrounding his works makes them an equally awkward subject. He was probably a man of humble origins, like Ctesibios, and may have started off as a cobbler. The majority of those who have studied Hero's life believe that he featured during the end of the 2nd century BC, which is noticeably late.r than the other scientists we have already discussed. Athenaeus, Vitruvius and Pliny make no mention of him, but they do talk about Ctesibios and Philo.
On the other hand, Pappas, Eutocius and Hero of Byzantium, who were all writing later, mention Hero of Alexandria but not Ctesibios.
Apart from this chronological quarrel - which is not without its own interest - Hero quite clearly belongs to the lineage of the Alexandrian mechanicians.
He seems to have produced about the same amount of works as Philo, a large part of which, and perhaps the most important part, is still with us today. Thanks both to Hero himself and certain writers of a later period, we are able to list these works here:
Firstly, two works of a scientific nature: The Metrics; Definitions and Commentary on Euclid's Elements.
Four works mix scientific preoccupations with practical appli cations: ( +) General Mechanics and the Baroulkos; (+) Pneumatics;
Catoptrics; ( +) Dioptra.
Finally, four works of applied mechanics: Water clocks; ( +) War Engines; ( +) Cheiroballistra; (+) Automatons.
Those works preceded by a "+" are extant, and it is evident that most of these are particularly interesting. It is also worth noting that this list is slightly different to the one we drew up for Philo. Fortifi cation problems do not feature here, but is this not perhaps an indication that Hero was living at a time when the Roman conquest had made fortification largely futile? It is also possible that Hero chose not to deal with the techniques which had not made any marked progress, and which did not therefore require re-consideration.
We possess only fragments of the Metrics, with numerous interpo lations. This work is a kind of elementary manual of mathematics. The first part contains definitions of arithmetical terms with examples, like the approximate extraction of the square roots of numbers which are not perfect squares. The second part is devoted to geometrical terms.
Finally, there is a practical treatise on plane geometry. The Definitions and Commentary on Euclid's Elements was to be a succinct account of

===== PAGE 317 =====

300
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Euclid's geometry. It ended up in fact as the technicians' mathematical
ABC.
The same was to be true of Mechanics. This treatise owes a great deal to Aristotle and Archimedes - the latter is quoted nine times regarding the problems of equilibrium and the distribution of weights on supports. The stamp of peripatetic thought is evident in this work with the emphasis on causal explanations and the reduction of mechanical phenomena to basic principles. Hero reduces "powers" to a unique nature, the simplest concrete expression of which is to be found in the lever, and the abstract, and therefore symbolic expression of which is to be found in the circle. But the expression of the reduction of a wedges angle, and of the screw to the lever is less clear than in
Aristotle. The work is not without finesse, in its examination of the effect of percussion on wedges, and of the more or less favourable conditions in which screws could be applied. Nor does this work lack skill, which is attested by Hero's method of slowing down machines whose reduction ratio is greater. Hero devoted a great deal of time to studying circular movement, whether concentric or not. His concep tion of gravity was not totally Aristotelian: he clearly states that heavier bodies fall more quickly than lighter ones, and that the shape of these bodies is also significant.
This work is situated midway between science and techniques. Hero may have admitted that experimentation was the best teacher, which was thereby to admit that as a whole, science was unable to allow for technical reality, but he declared elsewhere: "It is quite clearly neces sary that those who are learning about mechanical arts should understand the nature of the centre of gravity and gravity itself."
Consequently, the treatise is laced with practical problems. The information on gearing gathers most of the propositions known at that time: two wheels on the same axle tum in the same direction; geared wheels tum in opposite directions; if two circles tum around the same axle, the larger ones will move more quickly than the smaller ones; if two circles tum on two different axles and are geared to one another, then the smaller ones will tum faster than the larger ones. All the problems concerning ratio and reduction are considered, reduced for the most part to the problem of levers. This book concludes with the problem of supporting heavy burdens, which was particularly relevant for architects (columns and lintels) and which itself was reduced to that of the equilibrium of a balance beam.

===== PAGE 318 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
301 The second part broaches more theoretical questions, relating to five simple machines, or the five kinematic chains: the windlass, the lever, the pulley, the wedge and the screw. The study of the windlass in summary: Hero establishes the law of equilibrium between power and weight, and provides elementary calculations of ratios. His treatment of levers is taken from Archimedes, and there is nothing original in the study of pulleys and mouftons. Hero emphasises the difficulties of constructing and using screws. His definition of the screw, which he likens to a curved wedge, is somewhat curious. The most obvious effect of all these machines is moving considerable weights with moderate forces.
Ultimately, this work may be merely one of compilation, but we should recognise that everything is clearly presented and logically ordered. As for the simple machines, the author notes: "for the most part we were content to agree with those who have dealt with the subject in the past." The practical demonstration of all these principles was provided by means of the baroulkos, a machine used for moving heavy weights, precisely with moderate forces. This machine was known before Hero's time, and was dealt with by Philo. The problem was to move a given burden with a given force, by means of a gearing system. In this case, a force of five talents was to be used to lift a burden of a thousand talents. With their knowledge of gearing, this was hardly a difficult task.
The third part is devoted to a number of composite machines and is the most practical part of the work. The first of these machines were the lifting engines: the mast crane and pulley, the engine with two posts and moufton, and the three or four-legged hoist. Hero suggested that it was better to avoid using nails which would weaken the resistance of the wood, and to use ropes instead, which are still used today. After this he deals with crabs, which pick up stones, and the work concludes with a discussion on various types of press: the screw press; the press with central screw; the press with screw and lever, the lever press with winch. According to Pliny, some of these apparatuses were new to
Hero's time. This part is fairly similar to the final book in Vitruvius' work.
There are some interesting comparisons to be made between Hero's Pneumatics and those of his predecessors. The basis of his work is the same as Philo's, but there are some notable differences, however. A.
G. Drachmann has drawn up some comparative tȭbles of the two

===== PAGE 319 =====

302
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
works. In Hero's work, forty-four of the apparatuses featured can be found in Philo's Pneumatics, twenty-three are completely new, and eight provide different solutions. On the other hand, entire groups of instruments dealt with by Philo are neglected by Hero. Hero's origina lity - and this has sometimes led to his fame being somewhat exagger ated - stems from the machines using the force of steam, like the famous aeolipile, for example.
The preambule of the treatise recalls certain basic principles, and these are accompanied by discussions on opinions which differ to
Hero's. Throughout the work, and this is the same with Philo's Pneumatics, there are the most ingenious applications of all the
Alexandrian scientists' knowledge concerning the elastic and motor force of steam and gases under the action of heat and pressure, and especially on the action of this compressed and dilated steam and gas on the equilibrium and movement of liquids. It would be tedious to relate all the experiments concerning suction pads, syringes, lamps, siphons, suction pumps and water organs (which are no longer the object of a single treatise). The fountain and aeolipile, the small whirling device driven by steam which escapes by means of an ingen ious mechanism composed of two bent pipes, are well known. In this work, Hero reveals a greater mastery of techniques than his predecessor.
Optical science was made up of three main parts: optics; catoptrics (mirrors, theory and application); and dioptrics (dioptra, theory and application). Science and techniques are closely linked here. Unfortu nately, as far as Hero is concerned, the extant texts are inaccurate, incomplete and interpolated. The fundamental elements of optics were to have been dealt with at the beginning of the section on catoptrics. As Ronchi has shown, Hero made the mistake of extending the law of the minimum way to mean refraction, which meant that he was led into making appalling generalisations about all phenomena of light. The first part of catoptrics contains two optical theorems and six theorems on flat and convex mirrors. In the second part, there are nine theories concerning the construction and use of various apparatuses composed of flat, convex and concave mirrors. As with the mechanics and pneumatics, Hero provides further evidence of his broad knowl edge and contributes some interesting complements.
The Treatise on dioptrics consists of a description of the dioptra, followed by several examples of how it could be applied. The dioptra is

===== PAGE 320 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
303 a surveying instrument. Hipparchos' dioptra had served to measure the visible diameter of the sun and the moon. Hero's dioptra consists principally of a mobile water-level on a tripod; but this level can be removed and replaced either by a simple mobile alidade placed horizontally or vertically, or by a circular plate divided up into degrees and which could be fixed according to any oblique plane (fig. 16). In some ways, this apparatus resembles the modern theodolite. The second part of this work deals with a number of measuring problems, several of which resemble the same ones experienced by the Roman agrimensors. Moreover, several solutions are obtained by a process of trial and error and trying different approaches: such as the tracing of a straight line between two points so that neither point can be seen from the other, or simply measuring a field, for example. Other problems concern civil or military operations. We should note in particular the aspects of underground works, such as tracing a straight line through a mountain to join together two points on the opposite ftancs, or digging shafts which drop perpendicularly onto an excavation or a gallery.
Hero was especially interested in the problem of measuring distances which were out of range, such as the length of a river, the distance between two far away points, or the height of an inaccessible point or the depth of a ditch, for example. He also considered the question of the best distance at which to place an artillery engine. Practical and agrarian geometry was also well represented, with measuring and parcelling up, etc. Finally, he also dealt with astronomical problems and the art of measuring angular distances in the heavens: he attempted to measure the angular distance between two stars, and to determine the distance between two places in different climates by observing an eclipse of the sun in two different areas - this was done in places whose latitude was apparently known. Hero also provides us with a description of the hodometer, a fairly ingenious counter adapted for wagons and consisting of an endless screw and cog-wheels.
The same apparatus, with the addition of a paddle wheel, makes it possible to measure the travel of a ship. Finally, he assesses the asterix, a very similar instrument to the Roman groma.
So in fact this is a treatise on practical geometry, and represents as such the precious remains of a long tradition. Hero may not have made a significantly original contribution to this tradition, but he at least proposed some new and sometimes elegant solutions here and there to old problems, such as calculating the area of a triangle according to its

===== PAGE 321 =====

304
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 16. - Hero's Dioptra.

===== PAGE 322 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
305 three sides. The most interesting aspect for us is the ensemble of technical means available to the Ancients for solving these problems.
Hero's treatise on throwing machines is less comprehensive than Philo's, however it is more detailed:
It is true that our predecessors wrote a great deal on ballistics, giving the dimensions and designs of machines. However, not one of them gives any proper indication as to how these should be built, or how they should be used, it being their habit to write only for those who were already in possession of the necessary details.
Hero effectively describes every piece of every machine, and gives their dimensions. But all these details do not make our understanding of these machines any more precise. It is noteworthy that Hero's text is very close to that of Vitruvius, who, in chapter 12 of Book X dealt with the same subjects.
Hero made a classification, and a somewhat artificial one, of throwing machines: (A) euthytone machines which cast only arrows.
These are sometimes called scorpions; (B) palintone machines which can throw not just arrows but stones as well - the latter machines are called "lithobolos."
The principles of all these machines are the same. A bow string is stretched between two flexible arms or held with a flexible material.
The string is stretched by means of a winch into a cross piece which is manoeuvred by a spring. This is the classic throwing machine of
Antiquity. "The aim of ballistics" Hero emphasises, "is to throw an arrow in such a way as to hit a long-distance object in a given direction. " He reminds us that ever since history began, people had sought to throw heavier and heavier projectiles for even greater distances. This had led to the time when the bow string could no longer be held by hand, giving rise to the introduction of winches.
The machine had to be completely mobile, and therefore dismanta ble and portable. The dimensions had to be completely normalised so that each used or broken piece could be easily replaced. Philo was already convinced that this was possible, and as far as the dimensions and proportions of the machines were concerned, Hero used the same method as that of his predecessor: determining a module and formulas from which all the dimensions could be drawn. All the formulas are identical. "The diameter of the stretcher's opening is the point of departure: it is therefore the stretcher which is the principle and foundation for all the rest." Hero adds to this the formula for the arrow

===== PAGE 323 =====

306
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
throwing machine, which did not feature in Philo. The module, which again was the diamater of the stretcher, had to be equal to a ninth of the arrow's length, or:
So for an arrow measuring 3 cubits, the module would be 8 inches.
Here again, Hero's attitude is very interesting. "It must be certain that the figures for the dimensions have been determined by experiment."
So this was not a matter of reasoned formulas, as certain contemporary historians have claimed. Hero emphasises that it took a long time to arrive at these results: "Little by little, they succeeded in constructing machines which were as logically ordered as they were powerful."
Nothing had changed since Philo's time, then. We said above that the science of war engines was blocked: there is no better proof than this.
It was only with Biton that the formulas were not uniquely the result of accumulated experiment. Vitruvius continued Hero's tradition.
There was already a long tradition of treatises on automatons by Hero's time. And no doubt emulation was probably at its height in this field. The imagination was of little use in the field of surveying instruments or war engines, but automatons were quite the opposite, giving full rein to inspiration. Everyone wanted to demonstrate their skill in this field. "We wanted to write something more original about fixed assembly automatons" noted Hero, "something better than our predecessors, and at the same time more instructive; and as far as this subject is concerned, we shall not include any of the writings of Philo of
Byzantium." We should note straight away that the field of automa tons has a particular significance for the history of techniques, with its combinations of the most diverse mechanisms, the invention of new types of transmission, and what is more important, of progamming.
These were to constitute an exceptional support for certain advanced techniques in the centuries to come.
The treatise is divided into two main parts: automatons on moving stages, and those on fixed stages. The former operated in effect on a kind of rolling chest which contained and therefore concealed the motor mechanism, which served not only to drive the theatrical automatons, but also to manoeuvre the stage around. As for the fixed automatons, the chest remained stationary, and was used as a veritable

===== PAGE 324 =====

TIE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
307 miniature theatre, where elegant puppets performed plays of several acts, with intervals and scene-changes. In both systems, the motor consisted of a descending counterweight. There were two types of theatrical representations. Hero devoted the first book of his treatise on mobile theatres to a description of Bacchus' apotheosis. There was only one scene, all the characters were arranged at the god's feet and they executed their roles in the open-air. There were no scene changes, and the whole thing took place in the middle of the audience which was seated in a circle around the stage. The second book, on the other hand, describes a performance of the myth of Nauplius, which was actually a tragedy in five acts, with intervals and scene-changes, and which took place on a fixed stage. Hero writes in remarkably precise detail about how the various processes should be executed. His obvious preference for fixed theatres is apparent in this system which is technically superior, not only by virtue of the fluent and dependable machanisms, but also because of the variety of theatrical combi nations.
The mechanism of the moving theatre is extremely simple. It consists of a three-wheeled carriage on rails. Movement is generated by a counterweight fixed to the end of a coiled string on the axle of the motor wheels. They were aware of the principle of accelerated movement in falling bodies, so the movement was controlled by lodging the counterweight in a container and resting it on a bed of grains of millet and mustard seeds. These were both light and slippery of course, and could escape through an appropriately-sized hole which opened and closed by means of a small gate. The weight was calculated according to the various resistances, one of which was the ascent of another counterweight, or in other words, the string winding around the other axle, which thereby produced movement in the opposite direction. The length of the strings was also determined. The same counterweight system applied to the fixed theatre, but a whole host of other mechanisms were added to this to bring the characters to life.
Since the stage no longer moved, the counterweight could be arranged more effectively. The seed was replaced with dry sand, which was rnQre economical and durable. There were several different types of m ·:hanism brought into play: levers, counterweights and ratchet wheels serving as cams. The main counterweight was linked all the various movements, and the mobile parts were supported by a dowel until this was released by the counterweight.

===== PAGE 325 =====

308
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Use was therefore made of mechanisms which could also be intro duced in other machines. All the kinematic chain systems are repre sented here, often adapted to produce a particular effect, like the cam, for example, which is effectively a lever defined in time. All the principles to be found in the Pneumatics are expressed here. Ulti mately, the automatons appear to represent the use of scientifically described phenomena which have been actually constructed to pro duce their successive effects in time and in an ordered fashion. But the very fact that these mechanisms were used for amusement inhibited some from transposing their assemblies for use in the more general material world. But this would be to forget that small operations cannot necessarily be effected on much larger scales. This was the main issue, transposing the model into reality, especially where war engines were concerned, and this was quite clearly perceived by the mechanics of this time. Vaucanson and Jacquet Droz's automatons were separ ated by a similar distance from certain modern automatic machines.
Apart from its popular and even literary interest, the automatic theatre is significant for its role in the research into mechanical combinations. It is difficult for us to measure Hero's progress in relation to Philo, because we do not have the latter's treatise. But nonetheless it would be unjust to ignore the incredible skill of this
Alexandrian mechanician in the very area where he wanted to contri bute something new. This tradition abated somewhat after Hero's time, only to be resumed with the Arab mechanics . The Renaissance
"engineers" occupied themselves with large automatons destined to colour princely fetes.
Hero clearly marks the end of the Alexandrian School. He may have refined some of his precursors' positions, but ultimately he worked with the same attitudes, the same methods, and not surprisingly, he achieved the same results. Of course it is difficult for us to appreciate the work done by the Alexandrians in terms of the general evolution of
Greek techniques because of the lack of earlier treatises, of which there was a considerable number. It is possible, however, that the confrontation of disciplines was greater in Alexandria than in any of the earlier schools, with the exception perhaps of Rhodes which we know very little about, except that some of its members were at one time scholars at the Alexandrian School.
One can understand why certain techniques, notably those concer ned with crafts, did not figure in Alexandrian research, but it is not so

===== PAGE 326 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
309 clear why certain others were omitted as well. This is particularly true of architecture, which could have stimulated some interesting devel opments, notably in terms of perspective and proportions. But per haps we are not aware of all the works produced by the School. And it is also possible that the existing treatises were felt to be adequate, and that no further revisions were needed. Hero did not actually write anything on this subject, but Aenaes' treatise was revised by Philo.
It hardly seems necessary to state that all this needs to be discussed in more precise terms. These texts are difficult to edit and translate and some of them have not been published since the 16th or 17th centuries.
This is one of the areas where collaboration between specialists of Greek language, technicians and historians would be particularly fruitful: the recent editions of Archimedes' works have shown us the way.
BLOCKAGE OR LIMITS
The historian of Greek techniques could not possibly avoid dealing with one particular problem which has and still is, causing a great deal of ink to flow. Apart from a few minor differences, all the solutions found thus far, except for a few variations, are largely in concordance.
Andre Aymard posed the question in clear terms:
At the heart of Antiquity, during the time of the Hellenic civilization, the stage was set for a steady yet radical transformation of the conditions of everyday life. But this transformation did not take place. The ingenuity brought to bear in using the properties of matter, and taming the great forces of nature, in order to fight off the enemy or entertain the innocent and the curious, gave way to an attitude verging on indifference when it came to using the same qualities to alleviate the misery and physical pain of man
( . . . ) so it was not through ignorance that the Ancients sinned then, but through their own refusal. A refusal which had its exceptions, but which is apparent in too many cases to bear witness to anything other than a principled attitude. A mental or social attitude, or both perhaps: the question cannot be ignored.
This means that there was probably a blockage in technical thought, even though all the elements of a major evolution were available to hand. This blockage may have been due either to a reaction against manual work and the components of material life, or it could have been because of the existence of a particular social system one of the

===== PAGE 327 =====

310
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
elements of which - and this is the essential point - was slavery.
Contemporary writers adhering to the first opinion have based their beliefs on texts, while those holding the second opinion base them selves merely on deduction. It is worth evaluating both sides of the argument.
Before anything else, we should say that these texts are relatively well known, and are as short as they are few in number. It is indisputable that slavery brought discredit to manual labour, even though the latter was practised largely by free men. In any case, both
Plato (Laws, VIII, 846) and Aristotle (Politics, III, 5) proposed that in their ideal cities no manual worker could be a citizen. Everything concerned with craft and handiwork brings shame and deforms the soul as well as the body. In the Gorgias, Plato lays into "engineers," the type of engineers known to us and who emerged during the very same period as this great philosopher. "You pour scorn on them and their art, you call them makers of (war) machines when you wish to insult them, and you would never give your daughter to their sons, or marry their daughters yourself. "
The other texts are later ones, being attributed to Plutarch. The first (Marcellus, XX.I) actually deals with some of Plato's attitudes. We shall now quote Amyot:
But since then, Plato became angry with them, maintaining that they were corrupting and spoiling the dignity and excellence of geometry by bringing it down from the realm of the intellectual and incorporeal to the lowly world of sensible and material things, and by using it with corporeal matter, which entails the vile and base practice of working with one's hands; since then, I say, mechanics, or the art of the engineer, has been separated from geometry, and having long been held in contempt by philosophers, became a military art.
In a way this is an exegesis of the texts cited above. The second quotation, taken from the life of Marcellus, deals with Archimedes' attitudes (Marcellus, XXVI):
Archimede's elevated soul, his profound and searching mind, and his wealth of geometrical theories were such that he wanted nothing left in writing about the construction of the machines which had brought him such glory ( .. . ) regarding mechan ics, and indeed any art which fulfils mankind's needs as obscure and vile.
And the other text, also well known, where Archimedes assesses the machines devised by Appius, Marcellus' lieutenant in the army which besieged Syracuse:

===== PAGE 328 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
311 Archimedes did not rate any of these machines very highly, which in fact were nothing in comparison to his own; not that he would have held his own inventions to be worth very much, he regarded them as mere geometrical games which had helped to fill some moments of leisure, and most of which were requested by King Hieron, who was constantly persuading him to deflect his skills from purely intellectual things towards sensible objects, and to make his reasonings accessible to the sense and palpable to the common man by applying them to everyday objects.
The conclusion is obvious:
And repudiating this entire science of inventing and building machines, and generally any art which serves some use, vile, base and mercenary, he turned his mind and studies instead to writing about things whose beauty and subtlety were in no way linked with necessity.
Pierre-Maxime Schulhl deduced quite rightly, with Aristotele, (Nicomachean Ethics X, 7) that for all philosophers the contemplative life was far superior to even the most higly rated of its practical elements. He estimated that "Even the greatest of the engineers from
Antiquity, Archimedes, did not succeed in convincing himself of the legitimacy of his works on mechanics." He notes that this school of thought began in Iona, particularly with Archytas and Eudoxos. It advocated the creation of a scientific technology but was eventually thwarted by Archimedes and by the platonist influence. It is thus possible to see the opposition of servility and freedom prolonged by that of technology and science. "Researching for practical application is to demean oneself, to lower oneself, and can only be admitted to as a form of diversion or amusement. " The Romans had much the same attitude:
For Seneca, contemporary inventions - the use of transparent glass, heating stoves ( . . . ) are the works of the vilest slaves, of experienced minds, penetrating perhaps, but not great, not elevated, just like everything else which can only be done with the body bent down, and the spirit turned towards earth. They are the products of reason, but not of the right reason: all these superflous inventions subject the soul to the body, as a master turned slave.
Aymard used a different approach, but arrived at the same conclu sions. His portrait of Archimedes conforms to the generally offered picture, but it is furnished with more detail which we shall consider later. "He steeped himself in aristocratic ideals, in the pride of the ancient, hereditary nobility, he heaped scorn on the acquisition of wealth which was disrupting the caste system, and on commerce which

===== PAGE 329 =====

312
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
was producing the nouveau-riche whose insolence he found appal ling." This ideal would have contributed to a decline in the status of techniques. It contained in effect a hierarchy of activities necessary to the life of a collective and the lowest of these were allocated to the most inferior ranks of society. This, then, represented both intellectual and social discredit: working with materials necessarily produces the soul of a slave.
We have already said that the texts were few in number and that they were inevitably ambiguous. We should first put aside an opposition to be found in the first of Plutarch's texts. There is no doubt that Plato disapproved of certain demonstration techniques: he felt that resolving the problem of doubling the cube using a mesolabe was not, and should not be seen by scientists as a rational process, as the process of a normal mind. Geometrical demonstration should be done in quite a different way. The engineers at the Alexandrian School obviously understood this: they struggled to use several exercises of pure science to show that they possessed a perfect understanding of the virtues of discursive demonstration. It was correct to have a clear separation between two disciplines which although sometimes related and which originally had been treated as one, did in fact derive from two different types of logic. No doubt this was no longer true of physics: Archimedes obviously clearly understood this, and the link between pure science and technical experimentation could still not be completly severed, even though it had been earlier with geometry. Plato firmly adhered to the tradition of ancient techniques which allowed only for the ruler and compasses: these were tools whose operations had logical results, results which could be seen and understood immediately by means of a rational intuition. The mesolabe was something quite different: it gave the solution without making clear the reasoning process. It was here that the fault lay. The formation of a system of geometry such as that devised by Euclid - a global and homogenous construction - required a logical development, not mechanical construction. Archytas was the last to pursue this line of thought. The necessary separation between science and technics and between mechanics and geometry was self evident. As soon as science was no longer an element of techniques, as soon as it had its own particular function, it could not be otherwise.
According to Eudemos, Pythagoras' greatest merit was to have made mathematics into a liberal discipline by studying it from an immaterial and rational point of view. Arithmetics and geometry were only able to

===== PAGE 330 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
313 assert their independence and become recognized as pure sciences by separating themselves from their founding father, technics.
The second problem is linked both to the preceding and the follow ing ones. It concerns the scorn that the Greeks may have had for manual labour. In fact, for the upper classes - and as Aymard has noted, scientists and philosophers belonged to these ranks - this may have been an intellectual attitude, instilled by the practise of a pure, logical and demonstrative science, as opposed to empirical activities which are the work of the body, not of the mind. It is also possible, as we noted earlier, that the existence of slavery brought material operations into disrepute, since these were performed only, - or nearly only - by slaves.
One point springs immediately to mind. The rejection by intellec tuals of a certain kind of matarial world is not a purely antique attitude.
It has existed in other periods and other places, without provoking a blockage in technical thought.
The second remark is perhaps more important. We are dealing with three testimonies, two of which come from philosophers, the other from a scientist who was also a great technician. These three testimo nies may be very prestigious, but do they reflect the general opinion?
Historians should have searched for evidence to the contrary, particu larly with regard to the facts. In opposition to Aristotle and Plato, one can also cite one of Solon's laws which required all Athenians to teach their sons a skill, and this included manual ones. There was another law in Athens which threatened to punish any citizen criticising another with regard to their profession. Glotz has noted that Athens and Corinth were the only towns where manual work was not frowned upon. This disdain - of a varying nature and origin which it would be worthwhile discussing in more precise terms - would have been prevalent in areas like Thebes, Thespies and Sparta, where technical progress was far less marked. Of course it could be objected that this leglislation was prior to Aristotle and Plato, and that it was promul gated precisely at a time when technical progress was still evident.
There is more to this, however. We emphasised earlier these words of Aymard's: "The acquisition of wealth which was disrupting and the role of trade which was creating the rise of the nouveaux riches whose insolence was found appalling." There was, perhaps, then, an attitude held by intellectuals in the face of a world which concerned itself precisely with material techniques. In a sense, it represented a reaction

===== PAGE 331 =====

314
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
against current trends. Similarly, one of Plutarch's phrases mentioned earlier is also noteworthy here: mechanics "so highly valued and loved by all sorts of people. " And if Plato refused to let his daughter marry the son of an engineer, was it not because such marriages actually took place? Our information on this subject is too scanty and imprecise to allow for anything other than hypotheses. It is undeniable that those people who had the difficult responsiblity of moulding the material world tended to concern themselves with the sort of problems which arose in their own lives every day, and that they favoured those who could bring them power and prosperity, that is, the technicians. On the other hand, it was no less natural that the creators of "a better world" should have sought refuge in immaterial principles: again, this was an attitude which existed in other periods and in other places.
And so finally to slavery. Two opposite strands of reasoning can be used here. The insufficient means available through techniques forced the perpetuation of slavery. Since there were neither sufficient sources of natural energy or mechanisation the ancient Greeks, like the ancient Romans after them, were forced to recruit the services of a servile work-force. This argument was also upheld by Lefebvre des
Noettes. But the reverse is also logical: the existence of slavery blocks technical progress because if a servile work-force is available, there is no need for improved techniques to reduce the amount of work.
Both these arguments have been presented by Aymard. He begins the discussion with Aristotle's well known reflection. At the beginning of his Politics, Aristotle indeed declares that slavery would cease to exist if shuttles and plectrums were able to start up by themselves. He did not say that slavery was actually preventing the shuttles and plectrums from starting up by themselves.
Aymard continues:
Sensing the links between the two facts, the Ancients saw the cause in one, the absence of machines, and the effect in the other, slavery. It is even possible, following Aristotle's example, that the most foresighted among them and also the most tormented, found in this cause-effect relationship something to appease their conciences. Because it enabled them to conceive of slavery, unjustifiable in principle, as an inescapable necessity in the life of a collective.
But the historian of Greek Antiquity believed this traditional expla nation to be opposed to "the very conduct of the Ancients" with regard to what we call technical progress.

===== PAGE 332 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
315 Far from being unaware of scientific knowledge or of practical developments which would have enabled them to initiate and develop [technical progress], it was actually by design that they refrained from using them to reduce the work load by a greater, faster rate of production. The absence of machines does not, therefore, constitute a first fact, from which one is entitled to explain the existence of slavery.
It was necessary then to find a cause, or causes for this deliberate refusal, and one of these was quite possibly the contempt held for manual work. After having acknowledged Aristotle's position, then,
Aymard arrives at the antithetical explanation: "slavery should not be considered as a consequence of an absence of machines: on the contrary, the latter presents itself to the historian as a consequence of slavery."
The problem is difficult to resolve. Was it really necessary to follow this line of reasoning? Aristotle quite clearly said that slavery was the result of technical insufficiencies. Why reverse his explanation under the pretext that it does not concord with the attitudes of the Ancients, and conclude that the technical blockage was a result of slavery? Our knowledge of slavery in Antiquity is still too fragmentary for us to draw any definite conclusions. Only reasoning a priori can set us on a more fruitful path which it would be worthwhile exploring in more detail.
Jacques Ellul noted quite rightly - and the comparisons are very enlightening - that technical progress was far greater in civilisations which practised slavery, like Egypt for example, than in those where it was virtually unknown, such as Israel. There was far more technical progress during the slavery period of Roman history than during the time of the great emancipations. And the freeing of slaves during the invasions did not lead to any marked technical progress.
The economic point of view is also fairly instructive. Even in difficult areas of work like mining, for example, the extensive use of slaves due to a lack of mechanisation made for a far less profitable concern than if techniques had been manpower-saving. This would appear self-evident.
And even in the craft industries, a great deal of capital which could have been used in other ways was needed to provide a professionally worthw hile slave. It is in their interests, continues Ellul, "not to waste [this capital], not to use it here, there and everywhere, and if it is possible to make the work easier and more efficient, then it is in the masters' interests to make sure that this is done, just as shown by Cato the Elder."
As for the idea that there was a technical blockage because of the existence of slavery, then, we gladly reiterate Ellul's judgement: "In

===== PAGE 333 =====

316
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
reality, we are faced here with one of those facile, sensationalist and completely non-historical explanations, habitually propounded by those whose sole purpose is to justify theories."
It was therefore necessary to put the problem another way, and specify some of its aspects. Contemporary historians are still divided about the actual development of techniques during certain periods, the boundaries of which are often unclear. Vernant believes that the
Greeks borrowed their technical knowledge very early on from the East, and that this knowledge was not profoundly changed by any new discoveries. "The innovations or improvements which they introduced in certain fields did not extend beyond the framework of the technolo gical system which appeared to be already fixed by the classical period." There is some chronological confusion on this matter. True enough, as we noted above, advanced techniques from the East and
Egypt were effectively available to the Greeks at the beginning of their era. After the 6th century BC, however, it is undeniable that some profound changes occured in clearly defined fields, which represented major developments of the techniques they had acquired from elsew here: invention - in the true sense of the term - is clearly what happened then. It is no less obvious that the classical technical system was structured at the beginning of the 3rd century BC. The Alexan drians did codify, indeed they eventually perfected several techniques, but there were no more major transformations. In our opinion, between the 6th and the 3rd century BC, the Greeks contributed a great deal to the field of techniques. The blockage probably occurred after the efforts to synthesise made by the Alexandrian School.
The second point is equally important. If there was actually a refusal of technical progress, it would be as well to define just what exactly was the object of this refusal. It is the nature of this blockage, then, which is in question here. There are two different standpoints with regard to this, different in spirit, certainly, but to a degree they are concordant, and one could almost say complementary. The idea of a technical blockage, according to many historians, emerged from the idea that the Greeks possessed all the necessary elements for major technical progress to take place. The following passage is again taken from
Aymard:
The Greeks were enlivened by a true scientific spirit, and all that was needed was for them to apply in practice the principles discovered through their reasoning ( . . . ). Even

===== PAGE 334 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
317 more important, they did actually began to apply them, either to satisfy royal fantasies or to defend Syracuse against the Romans ( . . . ) . It was not through ignorance that the people of Antiquity sinned, then, but through their own refusal.
Contemporary historians still believe that "the development of mechanisation would have been logically possible." One should ask, therefore, whether or not the Greeks had the necessary intellectual tools to promote technical progress. It is futile making the point - even though it is still done today - that Hero of Alexandria's aeolipile could not logically lead to the steam engine. The latter was born out of an understanding of vacuums, condensation and atmospheric pressure, notions which were unknown to the Greeks. And this could also reflect an inaccurate interpretation of texts. The same is true of the crank and connecting rod system, unknown in Antiquity, and which was the basis of all advanced mechanisation: there was no slavery in the Middle
Ages, but it was not discovered then, either. It is also somewhat futile to ask oneself why a mechanism so simple in principle was not invented earlier. The confusion between rotary and linear motion was first discussed only during the Renaissance. Moreover, the absence of a dynamic in the Greek scientific system considerably hindered the development of mechanisation. Levers, pullies, winches, cog wheels, reduction: all the potential of Greek physics had been realised. This had happened during the 6th and 3rd centuries BC and it represented a great deal of progress. Equally, the camshaft, used by Hero of
Alexandria, could not be put to practical use. The possible appli cations of Greek science should be specified. Apart from those which
'were effectively made, those aspects which could be applied were no doubt very few.
There were other difficulties of a purely material nature. Resources were limited in the Mediterranean countries, particularly Greece. The
Greeks were dependent for metal on foreign imports; these were sometimes from very distant regions al:n(yery often the routes were cut off. It was the same for wood; there was very little of it, it grew again only with difficulty, and it was often knotted and twisted. This shortage of wood caused a lot of problems, not only in terms of materials, but also of heat energy. Besides this, it is well known - as the history of techniques from the 15th to the 18th centuries clearly shows - that real mechanisation, at least advanced mechanisation, was conceivable only using metal. The lack of water precluded the widespread use of hydraulic power. As for harnessing, this was dependent on both the

===== PAGE 335 =====

318
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
development of animal domestication, which was more advanced in some areas than others, and on the existence of an expansive road network which the divisions of the States and the financial mediocrity of the cities made impossible. Furthermore, transport by animal porterage continued to be used until the Middle Ages: the absence of the front-axle unit, a much more recent invention, made heavy transport impossible, at least over long distances.
After the scientific impossibilities and material difficulties, J.-P.
Vernant adds a third aspect to his explanation. It is true that by the 6th century BC, the secularisation of techniques was an accepted fact. They were freed from magic and religion. According to
Espinas, the beginning of the 5th century BC was a watershed in technical history, when the unconsious practising of techniques gradually evolved into an actual technology. He also believed the sophist movement to have been the first to attempt to define and assert technical thought. Two movements grew from this: the theoretical elucidation of certain technical problems, found in the pseudo-Aristotelian work and a movement of codifying and teaching recipes which was expressed in "manuals" written by the mechanicians of the Alexandrian School. It was possible to develop the second movement, at least in those areas where techniques could be codified - this did not include the techniques of artisans, for example, where the work was done by hand - but it was not so easy for the first movement to base itself on a science still incomplete and difficult to use. We have already underlined the extent to which the absence of a dynamic, perhaps blocked by the arguments of Zeno of Elea, constituted a powerful break on the technical system. Philo of Byzantium insisted that technical research could not be conducted by pure reasoning and rigourous demon stration. Vernant has suggested that it was perhaps because of this and not through any aristocratic concerns, that Archimedes refrained from writing anything on techniques. The explanation of techniques would inevitably be a compromise between theory and experiment, each of them badly articulated. Vernant also believes that in the thought of Hero of Alexandria, the five kinematic chains formed a coherent system, closed in on itself, and excluding innovation and progress. The very rationality of the system implied its limitation and completion. J.-P. Vernant's conclusions are formal:

===== PAGE 336 =====

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
319 (Greek technics) did not yet have what we consider to be the characteristics of technical intelligence and which are the basis of its dynamism. It was rarely - or badly - linked to science. Experimental thought was not considered. And the failure to elaborate the notions of natural law, physical mechanisms and technical devices meant that there was no conceptual framework to assure its progress.
Denying the technical thought of the sophists, he concludes thus:
"The technical stagnation of the Greeks went hand in hand with the absence of any real technical thought."
Is it surprising, even without alluding to intellectual attitudes, or allowing for the existence of slave societies, that at a certain stage the
Greeks should find themselves in a technical impasse, and for reasons purely internal to techniques? It is also necessary to know if other systems and structures were able to bring the limits of this technical system to light, thereby generating essential transformations. We have seen that Alexander's creation of a great empire and the subsequent constitution of strong and extensive monarchies did facilitate the development, if not the transformation, of acquired techniques, in the nature of systemisation and improvements. A mediocre economy and a relatively stable population was hardly favourable to technical progress. The general conditions for a technical mutation were far from being united.

===== PAGE 337 =====

THE ROMANS
AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
It seems that generally speaking, the technical system perfected by the Greeks remained as it was for several centuries. There were a few changes, but these were only minor ones and they had little effect.
Even the relatively quick disappearance of slavery and the civili zation's shift to the North to areas which were far richer in resources did not profoundly modify the situation. The stability of tradition is testimony of this.
As far as techniques were concerned, the Romans were not innova tors. P.-M. Duval has made a list of Roman "inventions," which is relatively short and limited in its range. It is also debateable whether or not they did actually invent all the objects listed: the vaulted cupola or dome, the aqueduct, and the glass pane did definitely appear during the Roman period, however. And what is more important, perhaps, they also invented several tools, which will be considered later. It is less clear whether they did actually invent or not balances for weigh ing, direct action screw presses, and bows with catches. They also used water mills, chemical fertilisers, wax candles, metal imposts, codices and stenography. This list is certainly not insignificant, but it in no way represents a mutation of the system established by the Greeks and their eastern and Egyptian neighbours. Finally, it is important to remember that some of these inventions were not simply the the work of the Romans, but of their subject peoples as well.
The achievements of the Roman Empire are generally accepted.
Many historians, sometimes the same ones who emphasise the blockages inherent in the Greek technical system, find them a fascinat ing object of study. Much of their "success" lies with the visible remai,1s of their civilization: the carefully planned towns, the magni-

===== PAGE 338 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
321 ficent monuments ranging from the Pantheon in Rome to the Pont du Gard, the triomphal arches, still arouse admiration and rightly so, because they are evidence of their almost perfect mastery of the material world. Perhaps the Romans' greatest talent was their organi sation of space, which underpinned the Empire's greatness and still forms the basis of what has been called "the Roman genius."
CIRCUMSTANCES AND SURROUNDINGS
Since we intend to examine the principle elements of the Roman technical system and its differences with that of the Greeks, it would be useful first to outline briefly its circumstances and its context.
Good Pupils and Conscientious Observers Up until a fairly late period, the Romans, who were essentially a farming people, were familiar with all the agricultural techniques used in the Mediterranean world. It was their successive conquests which brought them into contact with civilizations technically more advanced than their own. From the first century BC, these conquests also brought them into contact with agricultural areas where conditions were quite different from those in the mediterranean basin, with heavier soils, larger forests, water courses, and more pronounced rainy climates. They became aware of the Hellenic world through their conquest of Sicily, and Carthage opened up similar but more varied paths. Later it was to be Spain, Gaul and then a part of Germany as well. Such that the Roman civilisation became a kind of melting pot which brought together very diverse civilizations, although their respective elements could not necessarily be applied in all areas throughout the Roman world.
The Romans were certainly good pupils and conscientious obser vers, but it was obviously in their interests to assimilate the tech niques unknown before their expansion, and which would permit them to exploit to the full their new-found immense empire. They probably not only assimilated these techniques, but improved them as well.

===== PAGE 339 =====

322
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
As good executors rather than innovators, the Romans were not prolific writers. There are only a few people who exercised their talent in this area, and none of them attempted to write one of the great encylopaedias so prized by the Greeks. Generally, the authors of latin technical works do acknowledge the debt owed to the writers of other civilizations of which theirs was essentially a tributary, and as mediocre technicians, they seem to have felt it just as important to rely on others as it was to show their erudition. This lends a particular characteristic to their works. They represent not so much actual technology as an insertion of technical norms into a general pattern.
The number of works on agronomy is hardly surprising and they have been well studied. These works seem to have several origins, the first of which seems to have been Carthaginian. We know from
Columella that Carthage counted among its people several agrono mists, of whom the most famous was Magon. His work was translated in Rome and was the most popular book of its kind. Magon dealt with agriculture on a very general level and was not confined to any one branch of interest, and this was probably the reason why his work became the farming bible for the Romans.
Columella cites about forty Greek agronomists, Varro about fifty, so that it is not surprising that Greek works on agriculture were more than abundant. However, apart from a few famous works like those by
Hesiod and Xenophon, very few of these are still in existence. Of course it is likely that they would have cited any author who had even the most tenuous links with agriculture just to show that they had read everything, such was the case with Aristotle, for example. It is probable that the Romans learned of Hellenic agronomy through the
Sicilian writers, of which there were about four.
The very first agronomic treatises date back to the 2nd century BC, and were written by Cato and the two Sarsenas, a father and son. The latter texts no longer extant, but no doubt they were very close to the former. Roger Martin has noted that these were not agronomic treatises as such, but more like these "maisons rustiques" which were so successful in the 16th century. These had no scientific pretensions, but were anthologies of all sorts of formulas for solving the hundred and one problems encountered every day by landowners. Cato's success was chiefly due to his personality, and Varro seems to have had little respect for his work. The latter is in fact an Economics, in the sense meant by Xenophon.

===== PAGE 340 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
323 We shall not deal much with Virgil, more a poet than an agronomist.
Yet his work is significant in that the descriptions of the Po valley reveal a richer, technically more advanced agriculture. After devoting a large part of his treatise to the rural economy, Varro deals with agriculture itself, in terms of its technical aspects. Book II is concerned with rearing, and Book III with specialised areas of breeding,
(beekeeping, raising fish and game). The part concerned with actual techniques is very short. One of the greatest Latin agronomists was probably Cneius Tremelius Scrofa, whose work unfortunately no longer exists, but which was highly praised by Varro. According to the latter, he had brought a new spirit to the subject "which one could go so far as to call rational and scientific," which brought him closer to
Hellenic techniques than Cato. Agriculture was for him an art i.e. , a technique and a science which implies both precise knowledge and specialised research. He anticipated Columella, and was responsible for the introduction of the actual science of agronomy into Rome.
Scrofa's conception of farming techniques made his work a major turning point.
Columella's work truly was a national manifesto, written in an attempt to save an ailing Latin agriculture. He believed that they should fight against the growing sterility of Italian soil, against the technical recession due to insufficient manuring, and finally against economic and social recession. Amateur dabbling in farming was not enough, landowners should be adequately trained even though there was no instruction in agriculture provided in Rome. Columella also advocated large scale exploitation as the basis of all good organisation and techniques.
It is worthwhile considering in a little more detail what can be learnt on a technical level from the Latin agronomists. We need to know more about their predecessors in order to assess their originality but this is easier said than done since we know very little about them. But it seems - and this is mere hypothesis - that most Latin agronomists were compilers, and contented themselves with developing and perfecting existing techniques. It is very unlikely that these changes altered the preceding technical system to any great extent.
Pliny the Eider's Natural History is a work in a category all of its own. He did not want it to be a technical treatise; it is rather a collection of observations, which is very rarely prescriptive. It is less valuable therefore, for the history of technical thought, than for the

===== PAGE 341 =====

324
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
history of techniques. Other than these differences, his method is like that of all the other Latin agronomists. "The perusal of about 2000 volumes very few of which, owing to the obscurity of their contents, are ever handled by scholars; 20,000 noteworthy facts obtained from one hundred authors that we have explored, and with a great number of other facts in addition that were either ignored by our predecessors or have been discovered by subsequent experience." This extract from the preface clearly reflects the characteristics of most Latin writers: an almost absurd erudition, and good observation which combine to result in what was essentially a collection rather than a work of systematic knowledge.
Vitruvius, the author of De Architectura, lived in Rome during the period of Augustus. Like Pliny, he wanted to compile a sort of compendium but in a much smaller area, that of architecture. and only one particular aspect of architecture at that. In one work he dealt with matters which had been treated separately by his forerunners, in a shortened, more accessible form, so that they would be useful not only to architects and professionals, but also to anyone who was interested in the subject. Like the writers above, Vitruvius drew from the best
Greek sources, and like them also, he was honest and generous in his acknowledgments so that thanks to him we know of works otherwise extant. His last books, particularly Book X which concerns civil and military machines, in fact consist solely of resumes of many books which we do still possess. This book is very similar to Athenaeus' treatise, who was probably slightly older than Vitruvius. Once more, we find this characteristic habit, shared by all these Latin writers, of collecting all that they could of their ancient - mainly Hellenic - heritage. The content of Vitruvius' work is surprising: the part on architecture is by no means complete, and it even leaves out some of the most notable aspects of Roman architecture, such as the theatres and the triomphal arches, etc. By contrast, most of the last book is concerned with military machines, which have very little to do with architecture, and the same goes for hydraulics, the subject of Book
VIII, in which aqueducts are barely mentioned.
Books I to VII deal with public and private buildings, considered separately or in groups, in terms of construction, decoration and fittings. There is a rather summary treatment of hydraulics in Book
VIII, and then the discussions on sun dials and clepsydras provide an excuse for him to talk about geometry and astronomy in Book IX.

===== PAGE 342 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
325 Book X of course is concerned with military and civil machinery.
Mortet has attempted to explain this surprising number of gaps - including as mentioned above the absence of certain monuments, and very cursory allusions to certain construction processes, arcades, vaults and arches for example. He believed that Vitruvius' treatise was meant to provide technical knowledge for the functionaries of the council of public works. But why all these gaps, and why a book on military machines? In our opinion, Vitruvius simply borrowed wholesale from his predecessors' works, in particular the Greeks, and for this reason wrote very little about Roman innovations, with the exception of the water mill which was also of Greek origin anyway, as its name in Vitruvius' work implies. Virtually everything in this work is
Greek, from the terminology to the architectural decoration.
Nothing is more representative of the methodical Latin approach to "technical treatises" than the works of Frontinus and Vegetius. The treatise on Roman aqueducts was written at the very end of the 1st century AD, and is essentially an administrative work, composed with information taken from the water service archives. Consequently, the technical problems it deals with are only those encountered by this small administration: problems with slopes, basins and especially with measuring the flow. One could say, and the same thing has been said in specialist critiques of this work, that this treatise is really only an examination of the administration of water in Rome, and of the solutions to the various related problems.
Vegetius De re militari, written at the end of the 4th century AD, can hardly b"e called a technical work, dealing as it does with the organi sation and administration of an army. One only has to set what he says about military machines, against the texts by Vitruvius or the Greek mechanicians of Alexandria: there is no comparison.
We shall end this rapid survey of Roman technical science with a brief look at the many agrimensors. The growth of the Empire and the subsequent growth of the administration considerably increased the task of those who were originally simply land surveyors. The wide spread use of cadastres had necessitated a review of the techniques used by the Alexandria School, which were already fairly advanced.
The replacement of the dioptra with the groma caused problems which are in evidence in all the technical treatises. Epaphroditus, Vitruvius
Rufus and Balbus, attempted to synthesise their knowledge on the subject and show that the change to the groma had provided a

===== PAGE 343 =====

326
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
satisfactory solution to the political and adminstrative problems. The problems of measuring land were thus overcome, as were the same sort of problems in construction: a new concept appeared for the first time in written form in these treatises, that of the "measurement. "
The Romans did not produce any outstanding scientists, and prob ably for the same reasons they had no outstanding technicians either.
There is striking confirmation of this in their written works. They knew what should be done, and they were sometimes able to add their own elements. But it was rare that they rationalised their actions. Their successes constituted a run of individual achievements, whose real causes were never examined. The mechanicians from Alexandria were effective in practice, but their mental attitude did not match this. Of course, there is always a certain discrepancy between technical litera ture - one of our major sources - and technical practice. Cicero pointed out quite rightly through one of his characters, that very few farmers, landowners and developers had ever read any agronomists.
And many agronomists display an empiricism which could only have been based on elementary practices. It is clear that they simply
"collected" information, which of course is the worst way to go about forming a rationalised set of techniques. Finally, it is worth noting that this system was very highly organised, so that any new technique inevitably caused problems. A well-ordered organisation necessarily needs fixed techniques.
Geography and Techniques It is undeniable that techniques in any epoch are closely linked to local natural resources. Large deposits of metal ores, an abundance of energy resources, natural motor forces, humid climates favouring agriculture and pasturage, rich soils, all these are factors which should be taken into account. Since the time of Bodin and Montchrestien, however, this has not been the case, except perhaps during the recent events concerning petrol.
The Mediterranean Basin and the outlying area, i.e. , the Near East, was not particularly rich in such resources. There were few water courses, and those that did exist were usually extremely difficult to manage, with the exception of the Po valley in northern Italy, and after the Roman conquest, certain areas of Spain and Gaul. There were few minerals, and the forests were meagre and slow-growing. On the

===== PAGE 344 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
327 whole, the situation was not a favourable one. It was exacerbated when supply routes were cut off and the effects were sometimes disastrous. Columella deplored the decline of food production in Italy, and the fact that the Romans had to use supplies from elsewhere, like
Egypt, for example.
Not enough research has gone into the dependence of ancient civilizations on others for their supplies, and the effect this had on their technical development, but there is at least an increased awareness of this subject's importance today. Thus, such reliance on external support meant that some technical developments were impossible, or at least very difficult.
The Romans' conquests brought them some important resources which did not exist in the Mediterranean, and this prompted some profound changes. The Romans found the iron they needed and which had been so lacking in Antiquity, in Spain, Gaul, Styria and Carinthia.
Spain also provided them with copper and lead. It was not only metallurgical techniques which changed as a result of this, but other techniques using these metals, such as iron working, for example.
Finally, it was the abundance of water courses that made possible the widespread use of the water mill.
On the other hand, as they moved further north, the Romans actually adapted some of their techniques, such as grape-cultivation, for example, which was changed to suit climates quite different from those in the Mediterranean.
There is another important point to be made here, namely that the so-called Barbarian peoples subjugated by the Romans all had their own techniques, which were the fruit of their natural resources and long-established traditions. It is very likely that their conquerors adopted many of these techniques where it was possible and in their interests to do so. "It is generally accepted," notes P.-M. Duval, "that from the 2nd and 3rd centuries, the Romans adopted the long, welded and damasked sword, which they had seen at the hands of their
Barbarian cavalry auxiliaries, and which had come from workshops in the Rhino-Danube frontiers." Pliny mentions hard iron from the Noric and Carinthia provinces. There has been a lot of discussion about the techniques borrowed from Gaul and no doubt these will continue: take the forecarriage ploughing instrument, for example, the famous gallic harvester, and the barrel.
It is possible that the demands made on the Empire by the Romans would have caused techniques to change even in those areas beyond

===== PAGE 345 =====

328
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
the famous limes. Excavations in Poland, in the Kieke region and therefore beyond the frontier, have provided evidence of the extensive exploitation of mineral resources between the 1st and 4th centuries.
Polish experts can think of only one good reason for such widespread exploitation: exporting to the Romans. Furthermore, it was at this time that the furnaces for reducing minerals were somewhat developed and improved.
Organisation The immensity of the Roman Empire demanded a highly structured organisation. The Romans were not only perfectly aware of this, but they also knew exactly how to put this into practise. Such an organi sation could only exist on a basis of advanced techniques from many diverse sectors.
The first of these was the institutional sector. The Roman preoccu pation with law, both public and private, is well-known and indeed, law was the fundamental base of this organisation. Some of these laws concerned major technical problems, of which two examples will suffice here. There was a limit on the weight of wagon-loads which could circulate on the roads, rather like the laws we have today. There was strict control of mine exploitation, - state authorisation was required and the inmost depths were state property, - and there were strict laws on extraction methods. There are many more examples of this.
The adminstration of such a large territory required a great deal of technical support. Again, two examples are sufficient to illustrate this.
I. - Water conveyance had been widely practised by the ancient Greeks, and as we mentioned above, this had required some large building projects. But Roman towns were even more extensive, so that water conveyance caused many more unprecedented problems. They had to go further afield to find water sources, and this itself entailed more complicated technques, such as the building of aqueducts - like the ones in Rome with which we are so familiar thanks to Frontinus - and also aqueduct bridges, like the famous ones in Gard in France, and
Segovia in Spain. Construction work on the first aqueduct (the Appian) began in 312 BC. After this the system gradually expanded as
Rome grew bigger, until it was finally reorganised and extended under Claudius and Nero (fig. 1).

===== PAGE 346 =====
