===== PAGE 347 =====

330
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
II. - The management of the Empire required a well-organised system of communications. This explains the immensity of the Roman road network which extended throughout the whole Empire. A large network was vital, but this was not enough without good roads, which were regularly maintained. And this helps explain the very solid structure of Roman roads. The Romans had a similar attitude when they built the ports for the mooring of their impressive fleets. All this entailed some interesting technical developments which we will con sider later.
Good organisation overcame some of these problems. They had to standardise certain products, if only military ones. It was the Romans who invented the large workshops which manufactured weapons and uniforms for their enormous armies, found all over the Empire, and which used to supply the armies within and on the frontiers. These workshops naturally worked using identical techniques, so that materials and tools were all interchangeable. We know very little about this subject, but we do at least know of these workshops, and that they were indispensable for the internal and external security of the Empire.
We can pursue this theme to say that techniques in a large, but centralised organisation tend to remain stable, which is the best guarantee of a good administration. Discoveries and innovations could only cause trouble. Uniformity, therefore, was the ransom of this centralised organisation.
THE EXPLOITATION OF
NATURAL RICHES
Natural resources were extremely important to those civilizations whose technical systems are not very advanced. For a long time, the
Romans were an agricultural people, and we have talked about the abundance of literature on agronomy. After their first period of expansion, they continued also to benefit from the wealth beneath the soil. As farmers and miners the Romans always remained primarily interested in these two areas of techniques.

===== PAGE 348 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
331 Agriculture Technically speaking, until the beginning of the modern period, Roman agriculture hardly differed from the other types of agriculture surrounding Mediterranean regions, from the Greek to the Carthagi nian. However, it did benefit from some of their developments, in the categories of cultivations, in the general organisation of labour, and in the types of tools used.
As far as we can tell from Pliny and other agronomists, the botanic and animal worlds underwent little change. The Romans even contri buted to the expansion of certain plant species which previously existed only in the mediterranean basin, such as the chestnut, peach and apricot trees. Perhaps their greatest contribution was to have cultivated vines which could stand the oceanic climate (Spain and
Bordelais in Aquitaine), the more northern climates stretching from the Rh6ne and Saone up to the Loire, the banks of the Moselle and finally up to Scandinavia.
The Latin writers do not fail to mention all the types of species grown, and how they differed according to climate and soil. There is evidence of more than six types of wheat, which Columella however reduced to three, soft wheat, durum wheat and "poulard" wheat, and that does not include spelt. Barley, millet and leguminous plants
(vetch, lupin, and chick-peas) constituted with wheat the staple pro ducts. Cato cites 7 varieties of vine, Columella 58, Pliny 91 (and he estimates that this figure was possibly as high as 400). The olive tree gradually found its way into Italy: 10 to 15 varieties have been cited.
The large number of varieties is testimony of a sound knowledge of arboriculture, but this is rarely alluded to in the treatises, however.
Progress was certainly made in the techniques of species breeding, in cutting, layering and especially grafting, but unfortunately, we know very little about this. This is more evidence of the Romans' concern to find plants and species suitable for all the regions of the Empire.
As for stock rearing, progress was slow, but constant. The Romans made very little contribution to the techniques of selection, which is hardly surprising since the main agronomists assumed the female had the dominant role in selection, which is completely wrong. Bovines were reared, but usually they lived on older types of cattle, such as the tall Epirus breed, selected during the Pyhrric period. It was the
Romans who began the practice of temporary stabling. All equine techniques had been developed exclusively by the Greeks.

===== PAGE 349 =====

332
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Four types of sheep were known during the Roman period: Etru scan, or Bergamask, those from Magna Graecia (southern Italy), the fat-tailed Steatopygous and Merinos. The Steatopygus was reared after the conquest of Carthage, and the Merinos came from Betica after Hannibal's defeat. The stabling of sheep was common.
Pigs and goats were reared in many areas, as they had been by the Greeks, but the donkey was a more recent acquisition. They reared the same poultry as the Greeks and only the rabbit had been recently imported. Aristotle did not seem to know of its existence, and the historians only allude to it from the beginning of this period, and even then it is considered a relatively exotic species. According fo Pliny and
Catullus it came from Spain; Strabo believed it came from the Baleares. It was probably still confined to certain areas of the Mediter ranean and some regions of North Africa. The Greeks knew of gallinaceans only towards the end of this period, and it was only with the dawn of the last century before the following period that the
Romans had several different races of this species, about half a dozen in fact. Their use became more widespread and the Romans also practised artificial incubation. The rearing of ducks was also a fairly recent practice when Columella was writing, and he suggests that precautions should be taken to prevent them from escaping, demon strating perhaps that these were still relatively wild species. Neither the guinea-fowl nor the turkey were reared by the Romans.
The history of cultivation methods and tools is a familiar subject, although the agronomists, including Pliny, were rather vague in their treatment of it. The first and most important of these instruments was the one used for ploughing. The type most often used was the swing plough, or ard, used for symetric ploughing. Of course, there were regional varieties of these. Pliny distinguishes between ploughs used for the denser soil around Rome, and those in Campania, where the soil was more porous. There has been a lot of discussion about the type of plough actually used. Some think that the Chambige plough was used in the Po valley and Tuscany, but it is generally accepted that the swing plough with share-beam was the most popular and widely used.
The ploughshare is widely depicted in ancient representations of ploughs: it is arranged vertically, but there is no space between the base of the beam and the share. This was so in both the Arezzo and
Talamone ploughs depicted on a Sicilian coin dating back, at the earliest, to about 258 BC.

===== PAGE 350 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
333 In more recent documents, the plough's beam and share are curved, each quite distinct, and fitted together within the share beam. All the colonial coins circulated during the two centuries before and after the birth of the Christian era depict the swing plough with share-beam, consequently, it is generally believed that this instrument was put into widespread use by the Romans, or even imposed by them.
It is not sufficient to simply know the type of instrument used, however. We should also understand how it worked. We know from
Columella that the Romans practised assymetric ploughing with a symetric instrument. All they needed to do was to tip the plough to one side. In the 1st century AD, Varro recommended attaching two planks to either side of the share during the third ploughing so that the seeds on the wings of the furrow could be covered. Virgil talked of "wings" as if it were a commonly used term. Palladius mentions not only the aratra simplicia, but also the aratra aurista which was designed specifically for flat land. It is possible that eventually one wing was used instead of two, and this became the mouldboard.
Pliny states: "The coulter is the part which cuts the hard earth before it is broken up, marking out the tracks for the future furrows with incisions which the share sloping backwards is to bite out in the process of ploughing". This statement has provoked a lot of argument. Some people believe that Pliny was referring to the plough with both mouldboard and coulter. According to more recent interpretations, however, Pliny was referring to the coutrier, or subsoil plough, which was quite independent of the plough. Nevertheless, this was a major step forward.
Pliny also distinguished several types of ploughshares, the most common of which was "a lever with a pointed beak" This sort of
"ard-point" was fairly common at this time. He mentions another type of plough which would correspond to the socketed ploughshare, placed on the extreme end of the share-beam. An arrow-shaped ploughshare cut the soil horizontally. Pliny also noted the innovatory use of the wheeled forecarriage in Rhaetia and Gaul. Far from being an ordinary plough as it has often mistakenly been called, this was a swing plough, a symetric implement, which was fixed to a small carriage.
The harrow also seems to have undergone some changes. Varro says that its use was limited, to pulling up weeds, and covering the seeds. It was a crude instrument, consisting of twisted riddles in a wooden box

===== PAGE 351 =====

334
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
with bundles of thorns, which by all accounts was virtually useless. The harrow described by Pliny had metal teeth and cut much deeper. This meant that some of the weeding was unnecessary.
Sickles were used for reaping and Pliny mentions a reaping machine with wheels. There were a number of hypotheses made about this before a picture on a somewhat dilapidated bas-relief was found in
Arion in Belgium. "An enormous box" writes Duval, "on two wheels, equipped with teeth, and pushed across the field by an ox, used for carrying the ears of corn pulled up by the teeth." R. Martin, after the descriptions of Pliny and Palladius, put this machine back in its proper place: it was used for pulling up corn ears, but the straw which the animals had trampled on obviously could not be used. This meant that part of the corn was inevitably lost because of the unequal length of the ears. Such an instrument would have been useless in areas where intensive cultivation was practised (fig. 2).
The Romans used to thresh the grain by using animals, sledges, rollers or tribula or corn drags, and of course, the flail.
The organisation of cultivation was another important question, and techniques for improving the land were widely practised. All the agronomists insist on the importance of fertilisers, and using the ash from stubble-burning was an age old process. Farm manure was more plentiful by now, as a result of the increase in stock rearing, especially that of bovines. In Gaul, marling was also used for this purpose.
Columella and Palladius estimated that between 16 and 21 tonnes of manure per acre was needed for growing wheat, providing that it was grown only for one year if crops were rotated.
Cato and Varro suggest that biennial rotation was the usual practice:
according to Virgil and Pliny, plants alternated with wheat, while Columella suggests that wheat alternated with beans. Columella advo cated leaving the land fallow for one year. Triennial rotation was much more rare, but it was practised nevertheless:
according to Columella: turnip - wheat - beans millet - spelt - barley according to Pliny:
millet - rape - wheat Long root crops were alternated with short rooted ones, and the Romans were obviously familiar with the beneficial effect of plants on the soil, particulary that of vetches and lupins. They also advocated that fallow land should be ploughed to prevent wild plants from

===== PAGE 352 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
335

===== PAGE 353 =====

336
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
weakening the earth. It seems that there was considerable progress made in all these areas from the time of the Republic to the Empire.
Western Europe will always be indebted to the Romans for extend ing the areas used for vine-growing. The vine was actually introduced into Gaul by the Greeks, but grapes were grown only in restricted areas near the Mediterranean coast. It was only much later that the large Narbonensis' vineyard was established in that province. It seems that the Romans originally wanted to protect the Italian vineyards:
only those with the rights of the Roman city were authorised to plant vines in Gaul. One of the first "Nordic" vineyards was set up in Gaillac,
Then Hermitage and the Coe Rotie were established, along the Rhone. New types of vine had been required for this, and the pinot grape became very popular as a result. The vines grown in Bordeaux had no doubt been imported from Spain. The conquest advanced rapidly: by the 3rd century AD, the Burgundy had become a major vine-growing region, and even Trier as well - Ausonius talks of
Moselle wines in the 4th century. Three and a half centuries after Strabo, vine-growing was practised extensively throughout France.
There were many different varieties according to the varying geogra phical conditions. In 71 BC, Pliny noted that a new type of grape was being grown in Vienne, in France. This was no doubt in the face of numerous obstacles, such as the edict passed by the Emperor Domi tian, which was passed in an attempt to stop expansion in order to protect the vineyards in Italy.
It is not surprising, therefore, that vines were carefully cultivated.
They were frequently earthed up, either with a hoe, or a light plough.
The agronomists have left us some clear descriptions of pruning techniques. Columella distinguishes several ways of leading the vines:
creeping vines; trellised low vines; trellised vines without yoke; trel lised vines with simple yoke; trellised vines with a yoke on four poles.
All the large Roman vineyards, of which the Falernian was perhaps the most prized, were arranged on trees.
The agronomists wrote in very precise terms about the work calen dar. This seems to be part of a tradition going back to Hesiod's Works and Days. As always, chronology is all important. Almost all the agronomists advocated multiple ploughing: Pliny for example, informs us that it was done five times in areas of hard earth, and up to nine times in Tuscany. Virgil believed four times was sufficient and most of the agronomists reduced this to three. Both Varro and Xenophon

===== PAGE 354 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
337 thought two ploughings were sufficient for vines (in early spring and mid-summer). Later, Columella and Palladius raised this to three ploughings. It is evident from all this that the number of ploughings depended very much on the nature of the soil and the type of cultivation.
The same was true of harvesting times, which were closely determined by weather conditions and the degree to which the grain was ripened. From the technical point of view, Columella distinguished between three different harvesting methods: the grain was cut either close to the ground, half-way up or at the very end of the stem.
Assessing the profitability of Roman farming is not easy, and the figures which we do have are certainly at the extreme ends of the spectrum. Furthermore, the units of measurement are often different.
Cicero talks of a yield of 140 to 180 gallons per acre, while Varro claims that in Etruria one acre produced 90 to 135 gallons, which hardly seems possible.
Our basic lack of knowledge about Greek agriculture, particularly that of the early period, prevents us from making any comparisons with the Roman period, or from accurately assessing the progress made during this time. We are probably fairly safe in saying that there was no revolution, however. There was a great deal of progress made in some areas however, and the success of the vine is a striking example of this, as is rearing and the introduction of some agricultural imple ments. It is not surprising that such a fundamentally rural people should seek to perfect the agricultural techniques of others rather than other types of techniques - this does not include their concern with urban projects, which we shall consider later. So perhaps the tech niques of Roman agriculture are best described as a synthesis of all the most refined techniques. Virgil, and Hesiod both wax lyrical on this subject, and the differences between the two are rather revealing.
However, it is no less true that the agrarian and argricultural technical system did not change, and that the Romans merely developed the potential of the preceding one. Unless of course, more specialised research eventually invalidates this interpretation.
The Mining Industry It is difficult to assess the progress made in this industry, because we know so little about the older Greek techniques, and because our

===== PAGE 355 =====

338
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
information on the Roman industry is so fragmentary and sometimes even suspect.
One aspect is of prime importance: there were relatively few areas mined in Ancient Greece, but these expanded enormously within the
Roman Empire in the wake of the conquests. The Spanish mines, in particular, provided an excellent test-bed for the conquerors to try out new improved techniques. In many other areas - and we are thinking particularly of iron ores here - the many banks of alluvial deposits needed only opencast mining. Furthermore, and this is important from the technical point of view, there were private mines alongside state owned ones, exploited either directly or in concession. In a system as diverse as the Roman mining industry, certain rules were needed, and record of these has been found on bronze tablets discovered in the mining region of Ajustrel, in Spain. These rules concerned both private and public rights, but they also dealt with exploitation and purely technical questions. The lex metallis dicta is present almost in its totality in the mining rule books of the Middle Ages.
Our sources on the details of exploitation are fairly thin on the ground, and apart from those we have just cited, they come mainly from the finds of archaeological excavations, particularly in Spain.
According to these, the Romans do not seem to have perfected Greek techniques in any significant way.
There is still one major problem, which does not appear to have been satisfactorily resolved. The Romans probably invented the wheel pump which raised water from the mines. We have two main pieces of evidence concerning this question: the Venafro wheel, which is now in the Museum of Naples, and the reconstructed assembly of machines from the Tartessus mines in southern Spain. (fig. 3) According to the available recreations, these wheels were driven by water, itself brought by successive flights of norias (fig. 4). Now it seems that friction and wastage - such as the loss of energy - made it impossible to pump all the water powering the wheels, and that consequently, the mines were easily flooded. They could not have used Ctesibios' force pump since they were unaware of the crankshaft system.
Some limited, but not unimportant progress was made in metal lurgy, which was largely due to the greater use of metals. In Noricum, for example, veins of "natural steel" - no doubt an alloy of iron and manganese - were exploited. The technique of steel-making by cementation was an age-old method, but it produced only superficial

===== PAGE 356 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
Figure 3. - The whole ensemble of wheels at Tharsis. (From J.-C. Baroja, Norias, Azudas, Acenas, Madrid)
Figure 4. - One of the Tharsis wheels. (From J.-C. Baroja) 339

===== PAGE 357 =====

340
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
plating. According to Duval, it was very likely the Romans who developed and perfected the type of bellows used, so that extra air could be supplied to the blast furnaces in use at this time. Bellows with an automatic valve appeared during the Gallo-roman period, and these replaced the old goatskin bellows used in ancient Egypt and
Greece. They consist of two wooden panels, wide at one end and narrow at the other. and joined together by folded leather. One of the two panels, the shutter, is articulated at the narrow end, or the headstall, which incorporates the nozzle. The automatic valve is fixed on one of the sides inside the bellow. These in fact are the type of bellows which still exist today, although they are rarely used except in some homes and forge workshops.
The Romans may have introduced some new developments into the use of chemical metallurgy, although this was not scientifically based.
According to Duval, they succeeded in separating gold from copper mineral, and silver from lead. They developed the techniques used for making gold leaf, and they succeded in obtaining mercury in the 1st century from the famous mines of Almaden in Spain. One of their more specialised techniques was the tinning of copper and silver, which according to Pliny was done with "white lead," i.e. tin. He believed this was a Gallic invention devised in order to make copper look like silver, and used for the ornamentation on horses' harnesses, and even on carts. Silvering first began in Alesia and among the Bituriges.
Recent research has shown that the famous orichalchum was in fact brass. In effect, there were a number of deposits of copper and zinc alloy, but the latter metal was never isolated during the Ancient period. Brass was still rarely used at the beginning of the Roman period. It was first made with a mixture of copper and calamine, and its use gradually became more widespread, especially after the conquest of the Republic which had such rich calamine-bearing deposits.
Orichalchum-brass was used in coinage from the reign of Augustus.
Energy The Ancients exploited only natural energy resources, and if one considers technical development in terms of the amount of energy being used, the Romans performed no better than the Greeks. Power was provided mostly by animals and humans. Wind power was used only by ships.

===== PAGE 358 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
341 The appearance of the water mill did not in fact constitute a revolution. The weak currents in some of the rivers of the Mediter ranean Basin, the irregular flow patterns, the strength of some moun tain currents and the sheer size of some of the larger rivers such as the
Po, were all major obstacles against its effective use. Besides which it seems that for some time its use was limited simply to milling grain.
"There should be no illusions about this," writes Marc Bloch, "the water mill is an ancient invention, which was age-old by the time it actually became widespread" (fig. 5).
Bloch has painstakingly sought out all the texts and evidence which mark the history of the water mill, a task which so far has almost not been followed up. There is evidence of a water mill in 18 BC, in
Figure 5. - Water-raising wheel (2nd century). From a mosaic found at Apemea. (From Singer, History of Technology)

===== PAGE 359 =====

342
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Cabeira in Pontus among the outbuildings of a palace erected by Mithridates between 120 and 63 BC. A Greek epigram, probably dating from the period of Augustus, suggests that the water mill was a recent development. Vitruvius describes a water mill in his De archi tectura, under its Greek name, hydraletes, and some time later, Pliny talks about water mills on the Italian rivers. It seems, then, that the water mill was first used to a significant extent during the last century
BC, in the mediterranean East. The mill was certainly not imported from the Chinese, although they were also using it at about this time.
There is very little evidence of these mills after this period. Ausonius mentions a water mill situated in the Moselle in the 3rd century, and in the 4th century, there is evidence of mills in the Janiculum supplied with water by an aqueduct. During the 6th century, there were no more than ten to be found in Gaul, despite the infinitely more favourable natural conditions (fig. 6).
We know very little about how these first mills were made. Most historians believe that the wheels were arranged horizontally, which
Figure 6. - A water mill. From a mosaic from the great palace of Byzantium (5th century). (From Singer)

===== PAGE 360 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
343 helped to avoid more complicated ways of transmitting the movement.
But the earliest, most precise description of a water mill provided by Vitruvius indicates the opposite, that the wheel was vertical. This is supported by further evidence of vertical wheel mills in Barbegal in
Provence, and a mosaic found at Apamea in the second century, together with the Byzantine mosaic from the first half of the fifth century consolidates this. Pliny states that the mills were established by the rivers in Italy, but we know that some were also powered by tributaries and, as in the case of the Janiculum mills, by aqueducts. The group of mills in Barbegal in southern France were built in the 3rd or
4th centuries. They were powered by an aqueduct with a distributing basin divided into two channels which supplied eight different cham bers and which had a fall in level of 18.60 metres. The Janiculum mills or the one at the agora of Athens, which all operated in the same way, date from the 5th century. There were also some boat mills, like those installed by Belisarius on the Tiber when supplies from the J aniculum mills were cut off by the siege of Rome.
Most of these mills, as we have said, were used for grinding corn, but the Mosellan mill described by Ausonius, may also have been used for sawing stone. The Apameus mosaic depicts a lifting machine, like the one described by Vitruvius (fig. 7). There may also have been some of these in the Tartessus mines in Portugal, but there is some doubt about this (fig 8).
IMPLEMENTS
The subject of implements has rarely attracted the attention of histori ans, even historians of techniques. The only exception to this is where prehistory is concerned, because apart from the remains of a few skeletons. tools are often the only form of evidence. For the sake of clarity, we shall divide this discussion into two categories: simple tools, and complex ones, or machines.
Simple Tools The historian of implements has only two main works at his disposal, by Bliimner and Fremond. The first has a much wider

===== PAGE 361 =====

344
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 7. - Reconstituted water wheels. (From Choisy, Vitruve)

TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
2 5 6 7 8 9 Figure 9. - Tools used in the Roman period. 1, plane (Pompei); 2, saw (Rome);
3, hammer-adze (Gaul); 4, scissors (Rondsen); 5, scissors (Priene); 6, Bit-brace (Naples);

===== PAGE 364 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
347 approach, but the second contains more refined analyses of certain tools.
We are more or less certain that implements underwent consider able development during the roman period (fig. 9). It is difficult to see why, because there were few changes in techniques as a whole. In part, this phenomenom can be explained by the availability of more metal, a greater diversity in the type of consumer products in demand, and the effect of new techniques introduced to the Romans by subject peoples.
This, again, has hardly been studied.
There are various different types of sources for this subject. There are the figurative representations, which were used especially by
Bliimner. Then there are archaeological objects, either the tools themselves which are the most valuable, or the objects made by these tools, which of course immediately precludes most of their wood work.
Our ignorance about Greek implements or those of the so-called Barbarian peoples also hinders a full appreciation of the Roman contribution in this field.
We are therefore limited to discussing only those areas which have been most fully researched. But we would hope that eventually, an overall assessment will be possible.
The Romans were quite innovative when it came to cutting imple ments. The first pivoted scissors, like the ones we use today, were first produced at this time, replacing the old spring scissors. We have them to thank for the frame saw, while older civilizations had known only the small hand saw. The blade was fixed firmly onto the frame. Stone saws have also been found, without teeth, which were probably used with sand. The Romans are also reputed to have invented the punch, the gimlet, the milling-cutter and the spiral gimlet with three points.
They are also thought to have invented the bit-brace, the auger probably coming from the Gallic civilization (the word taratrum seems to be of celtic origin).
The Romans were familiar with files: they used the scobina, or the wood rasp, and the lima, or metal file. The oldest ones seem to originate from the Gallo-roman period. They are of various shapes, flat, half-round and square. There are some with two crossed cutting edges, and others with just one, such as the adjusting file, for example.
There was one important development in wood working during the Roman period, which was the introduction of the plane, unknown in preceding civilizations. This made wooden structures far more solid. It

===== PAGE 365 =====

348
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
is thought that other tools related to the plane followed rapidly: the jack-plane, and the ordinary plane, longer than the one used today, the rabbet plane and planes used for preparing wood for use in assemblies. The working of metal and other substances with a vice probably dates from the Gallo-roman period.
If one compares the figurative representations of tools from the Roman period, and those from the Egyptian period, a quite remarkable path of development is evident. The type of tools used was to remain virtually unchanged from the later period until the
13th century. The changes wrought were mainly those of diversifi cation and increased specialisation of tools: the tool became more and more specifically designed for one clearly defined operation.
There were several types of hammer used during the Gallo-roman period, for example, each one designed for use in a different trade.
There were seperate hammers for the coppersmith, gold and silversmiths, coiners, cobblers, carpenters and woodworkers, stone cutters, and even hammers with nail claws, so that they could extract nails. There were many other types as well which we have not yet managed to identify. Scissors underwent the same type of evolution.
It is perhaps easiest to distinguish between the different types of operation in woodworking, and one very good example of this is the nature of the saw. The following group of saws used in the Roman period remained the same until the 14th century: (a) short saw (or semi-long) with a solid handle on one side, gripping the saw with a half-length of wood: this is a push saw which is the ancester of our small hand saw; ( b) the long stone saw, held in the middle of a large rectangular frame, like the saw used by pitsawyers; (c) the ordinary frame saw; (d) the stone saw for cutting crosswise (with two small handles): the equivalent of our backed saw; (e) "harnessed" saw, with one handle.
Bliimner attempted to establish an inventory of tools used by ancient peoples, particularly the Romans. This work, completed at the end of the last century is of immense value. However, it still needs to be reassessed and completed in the light of new archaeological discoveries. A good comprehensive inventory of these tools would provide directions for important fruitful research. This can only be achieved by searching all museums for their collections, many of which are probably lying forgotten in their storerooms.

===== PAGE 366 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
349 Machines The machine did not develop - except in certain specific sectors - as much as the basic implements. Vitruvius' work in this field demon strates this clearly, as it is based almost entirely on Greek sources.
Some machines evolved as a result of other innovations, such as the noria, derived from the water mill. There was progress made in agricultural machinery: of those used in cultivation (ploughs, harvesters), and in the preparation of agricultural products (especially presses).
The mechanisation of war, which had played such a large role in the initial growth of technology, remained largely dependent on the Greek system and heritage. Thus, artillery was still largely neuro-ballistic, the principle types of which were frequently used: the ballista; the catapult; the scorpion or the onager inspired by the principle of the sling.
The only new idea lay in the primitive crossbow, an image of which is provided by two bas-reliefs at the Musee du Puy, in France. It illustrates a hunting bow with catch. But could this not be a derivative of the chiroballista devised by Hero of Alexandria? The term arcubal lista can be found in the 4th century with Vegetius. So we agree with
P .-M. Duval when he suggests that the improvement of these machines had taken on the characteristics of an invention. We are certainly witnessing a process of development, and this process was relatively limited.
As for lifting appliances, Vitruvius' texts are virtually all Greek in origin. Lifting apparatus with squirrel cage wheels however, may be of
Roman origin (fig. 10). In any case these are the only pictorial representations we have for the whole of Antique civilization (fig. 11).
The use of the screw as an instrument of pressure in a machine must have been initiated much later. The only example we have of it, at least in its early form, is the screw press, used for pressing wine or cloth, the latter only attested by a wall painting at Pompeii. The cloth press was used to clean the material of all the liquid products which had been impregnated during the process of fabrication, and the instrument itself consisted of a frame with two wooden screws.
Similarly, the improvement of the bellows should also be men tioned. Up until the Roman age, a certain number of instruments had been used, the effects or results of which were identical but whose

===== PAGE 367 =====

350
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 10. - Lifting machine with squirrel cage (Bas-relief from Latran). (From Daremberg and Saglio)
"'

===== PAGE 368 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
Figure 1 1 . - Lifting machine with squirrel cage (Bas-relief from Capoua). (From Daremberg and Saglio)
Figure 1 2. - Winch and hoist (from Vitruvius of the Vatican). (From Daremberg and Saglio)
351

===== PAGE 369 =====

352
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
structures were completely different: blow pipes, goatskin bellows, diaphragm bellows, cylindrical and pump bellows. It seems that our modern fire bellows used in the home, which are angular deplacement bellows with a valve, a wooden framework and leather sides, may be of
Roman origin.
The same is true of Roman tools. Detailed and extensive inventories of all the machines used by Antiquity should be made according to different criterion: practical use, which implies the range of crafts, and the types of mechanisms employed, which implies the structural range in use, linked also to the type of energy harnessed (fig. 12). The long evolution of machines, in other words, of the technology which we consider today to be the most advanced, may be classed within strictly defined stages. This obviously first presupposes a classification of machines, which is still lacking despite some of the commendable efforts which have been made.
SOME TRADITIONAL TECHNIQUES
This is undoubtedly the most static sector in the domain of technology, and this was indeed the case for quite some time. We will quickly review the different aspects of this. Here we will observe a combi nation of traditional techniques taken from the Greeks and the
Barbarians, the origin of which is very difficult to discern.
There is nothing new, that is to say fundamentally new, in the textile industry, as spinning, weaving and fulling cloth remained completely unchanged. Only the weaver's card, used to separate threads, was introduced in the late Empire. As Duval has pointed out, changes were made in dress and costume. But here again, both the necessities of a more severe climate and the influence of the conquered peoples must have led to new ideas in clothing. The Gauls introduced clothes which were cut, close-fitted and sewn, as well as breeches (an early form of trousers) , socks, coats, the use of furs, and probably buckles. We have very little information on dyeing techniques which most probably evolved in the sense that a wider range of materials was used.
Ceramics must have been a highly saturated technique, as according to Duval, the only Roman contribution lay in the systematic use of the

===== PAGE 370 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
353 hollow or smooth decorated mould for the mass production of huge quantities of pottery pieces, which were often decorated in relief. But here again we find that the generalization of known methods, made possible by the new political organisation of geographical space, does not necessarily constitute change in the fullest sense of the term. It is perhaps useful to point out a new fact: the large scale organisation of production in regions which were highly concentrated, geographically speaking, such as Arezzo in Italy, Lezoux and La Gaufresenque in
France. We will consider this later. Much larger kilns were required to produce a greater amount of pottery. The kiln consisted of three parts on two levels: the hearth was below, usually under the ground, then an adjoining hearth, known as the hearth of the hovel, which was partitioned into two by a wall which helped to support the floor of the firing chamber on the third level. From an inscription at La Gaufresen que we know of kilns which could each hold 3000 vases.
The Romans were certainly more innovatory in glass-making tech niques. Cicero tells us that blown glass appeared in Rome in the year
20 AD, originating from Syria. Before the introduction of blown glass, the only other known forms of glass were drawn and pressed glass.
Henceforth the Romans were no longer limited by the imposition of one obligatory form, so that production could take on a greater freedom. In the 2nd century, translucent glass resembling crystal was already known, as were the window, the mirror on a metal backing, and the magnifying glass in the form of a water-filled ball. Once again, it is difficult to gauge what progress was made, as our ignorance of the beginnings of glass-making precludes any theories. Nevertheless, significant statistics exist to show the presence of glass in different archaeological levels.
We lack any detailed study on the subject of carpentry and furniture making. Roman furniture was undoubtedly produced in the Mediter ranean Basin but we do not know much more about this area. Here again, techniques used by conquered peoples, particularly those of the
Celts, must have been adopted and adapted. The Gallic wicker chair with a high, round back has already been mentioned. The dining table with four legs and covered with a fringed table-cloth, around which one sat to eat, was of the same origin. Duval notes that "the furniture industry advanced considerably during this period." But this is only a very general opinion, as details of the industry are unfortunately unavailable.

===== PAGE 371 =====

354
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
In the chemical industries, one should note the appearance of the wax candle, with a wick made of vegetable fibre, papyrus, oakum or off rush cores. It seems that soap (derived from the Gallic sapo) was first used to dye hair, and was only introduced in Rome as a hygiene product in the 4th century. It was originally made from a mixture of tallow, ashes, and probably red herb juice which had curative prop erties. It was later used to clean the skin and to wash clothes.
The codex succeeded the cumbersome scroll ( volumen). Pages were first made from papyrus, and then from parchment at the end of the
2nd century. Ink was based on a thinned down soot mixture: it was that which came from Pergamum (which gave its name to parchment).
BUILDING
The Romans have long been considered past masters in the art of construction. The abundance and size of some of their monuments, generally better preserved than those of earlier periods, provide a picture of their indisputable skill.
In fact, urban programmes and building styles seem to have been handed down by the Greeks. Two problems should be considered here: building types and the techniques used to construct them.
Types of Buildings In reality, research in this area falls within the subject of the history of architecture. But we should note here some of the forms for which
Roman architects and engineers became famous: the triumphal arch, the amphitheatre, the aqueduct, the bridge, the trophy, the pantheon, the votive column or decorated funerary pile, and the villa.
This list is not without ambiguity. As we have seen, the Greeks did actually construct aqueducts, and the lines and profiles of their water conduits were to be envied (fig. 13). It is more difficult to say whether or not non-Roman Antiquity had known bridge building. On the contrary, it seems that we must credit the Romans with this work, but only insofar as it fitted into a vast communications system which
Greece had not developed or had not needed to develop.

===== PAGE 372 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
10 m Figure 1 3. - Arch from the Pont-du-Gard [left] and arch from the Segovia aqueduct [right]. (From A. Choisy)
355 All this represents a phenomenon which depended upon the art of techniques and politics. Art in a technical form because certain edifices demanded new solutions, and in a political from insofar as these constructions were one of the ways in which the power and glory of
Rome could be manifested.
These buildings were the result of a certain organisation, either a political or social, rather than an economic one. A new concept of town planning also emerged, and political bodies existed which no longer enjoyed the same significance as they had previously had in
Greek civilizations, as from a particular period onwards the people were no longer masters of the city. From this ensued a profound

===== PAGE 373 =====

356
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
reorganisation of the structure of the town. The extension of the Roman civilization towards the north also necessarily prompted some changes: narrow streets, the covered passages, indispensable in the hot sun of the Mediterranean shores, were far less useful once a certain geographical limit was passed.
The Romans do not seem to have borrowed very much in this domain from the Barbarian peoples whom they subjugated. The latter, who had developed "towns" themselves, did not appear to possess a type of architecture which could be adopted by their con querors.
Most important in the Roman concept of various buildings was the unity of a technical programme, in which each edifice was linked to others, a concept which following civilizations were to adopt (such as the basilica which survived from the Roman into the Christian world) . In short, herein lies a whole technical system which is closely united.
Processes The Romans seem to have been the innovators of new methods in this field, and these processes may be divided into several categories.
Freestone was dry joined as had always been the case. Smaller stone, on the other hand, requires the use of mortar, especially if it is very small and irregular. Mortar was therefore very important in construction work of this type. Its composition had been determined for some time, as it was used in Hellenistic palaces, principally at
Pergamum, in a lime, sand and clay form. These mortars hardened over a period of time to form a conglomerate with the stones which they were to bind. It was easy to make and set fairly quickly. The quality of the mixture naturally depended upon that of its components, and the way in which it was used could prevent air bubbles from forming and remaining when it hardened. It was compressed by the use of a pestle working between vertical boards. Analysis of Roman mortar has shown that fine grains of sand, lime mixed with charcoal dust, clay, and if needed, gravel and crushed brick were used.
Throughout the Empire the proportions of the mixture were generally the same, from one century to another: one measure of lime to three of sand, or two to five , depending on what was required. Sand and water were mixed together with a binding agent of lime and clay. It was for

===== PAGE 374 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
357 spreading these mortars that the Romans invented the trowel, the shape of which was very similar to the trowel used today.
Care had to be taken to ensure that the wall did not warp during the drying process; also to ensure perfect cohesion of the facing itself and to prevent cracks. Pieces of scaffolding were left in the wall cavities when the supporting scaffolding was sawn off on either side. Brick level fittings were incorporated across the wall mass which re established a horizontal plane every time the work was restarted, and ensured the cohesion of the facing to the other surface for the total height of the structure. It is quite possible that these were similar in design to the latticework of wooden beams used by the Gauls. Courses were constructed diagonally for the wall facings, arranged in herring bone or ears of corn patterns. It was possible to build brick chains, increase the number of discharging arches, and apply coatings, owing to branches and geometric incisions made in the surface of the rubble stone wall facings.
Rome adopted the Greek tile, which we now call the Roman or round tile. Similarly, sheets of stone or marble were also used.
Window glass was incorporated in the walls from the 3rd century onwards, and coloured stained-glass windows were also introduced during this period.
Innovations, on the other hand, were implemented in the way in which doors were constructed and used. The Greeks had not developed the system of hanging doors on hinges as we do today. The
Romans appear to have used the split hinge, which consisted of alternate locks on either side of the hinge which then fitted together and were held by a peg. Valuable examples of these can be found in the museum of excavations in the villa of Catullus at Sirmio, on the shore of Lake Garda (fig. 14). The villa was built and extended between the 1st century BC and the 1st century AD. This was a visible improvement upon the old hinge system which consisted of manoeuvring pegs in the sockets at the top and the bottom of the door.
Similarly, the Romans must be credited with another important technique: the web key and the modern lock. Before this invention, the Romans probably only used the wooden latch, the reliability of which was questionable. It is difficult to give an exact date as to when this type of modern lock first appeared, but it may have been during the 3rd century; Fremont's research on this matter has largely been

===== PAGE 375 =====

358
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 14. - Split hinge (Sirmio).
outdated by archaeological finds made since the publication of his work on locksmithing.
The vault is a more controversial subject and there are many doubts surrounding this architectural feature. It is agreed, however, that it was of Greek origin, a fact which is forgotten in the face of the profusion of Roman vaults. The Romans, however, managed to build larger spans than those constructed in earlier periods. The increase in the number of storeys, stairs, bridges, tiers, and amphitheatres meant that the use of the vault became extremely generalized, and led to marked improvements in the way in which it was built (fig. 15). It provided the ideal cover for many public buildings. This architectural feature required, moreover, a particular size and cut of stone, and certain specific assembling techniques. It was certainly the acquisition of these techniques which enabled the remarkable spread of this method of construct-ion. Did stereotomy, the daughter of geometry, exist in the Greek civilization?
On the other hand, it is quite possible that the Romans invented the dome, a specialised form of vaulting (fig. 16). Whether they did or not,
Roman architects used two basic types of dome; the dome mounted on squinches, and the dome on pendentives. Once again, construction methods were the essential consideration, and the Romans quickly realized that the dome could be built without using scaffolding. The
Pantheon dome, in Rome, is one of the most magnificent examples of this.
The Romans also made great progress in the art of framework construction, whereas the Greeks had long remained at a semi primi tive stage. We might suggest that the building of immense scaffolding, necessitated by huge constructions, enabled Roman architects to resolve certain problems which had until then remained inadequately

===== PAGE 376 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
359 Figure 1 5. - The Basilica of Maxence. (From A Choisy) dealt with. The great glory of the age of Roman Antiquity subsequen tly lies in the invention of roof trussing and triangular structures which more than favourably replaced the timber piles built before. It was the beginning of modern framework construction as we know it today.
The arched bridge was undoubtedly a Roman invention. The gene ralization of the vault favoured this construction and ensured its sucess. As we have already said, it is unlikely that the Greeks knew the bridge, which may explain why they had virtually no road network.
The development of land communications and the necessity for effi cient administration, to ensure that couriers and troops could travel easily to their destinations, was to make the bridge an important architectural feature. Unfortunately we do not know how the piers of the bridges were based - and there are some which have a great many, often embedded in fast flowing rivers. The Alcantara bridge has arches

===== PAGE 377 =====

360
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
20 11 Figure 1 6. - The Pantheon. (From A. Choisy)

===== PAGE 378 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
361 with a 27 metre span, and the arches of the N arni bridge had a 32 metre span. The piers of the Saint Angelo bridge in Rome were sunk some five metres in the bed of the River Tiber. More detailed and systematic studies are required in order to understand the methods employed by
Roman engineers for the construction of such bridges. Even the list we have of the bridges that existed is hardly comprehensive.
Heating systems may also have been a Roman invention (fig. 17).
Heating was closely linked to the edifice itself, principally in the Thermae. The most popular system was the hypocaust, which consis ted of a chamber of warm air situated under the rooms to be heated.
Figure 17. - Cross-section of a hypocaust. (From M. Daumas)

===== PAGE 379 =====

362
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The process was certainly an old one, as this system had been in existence in the civilizations of the Indus from the time of the third millennium BC onwards. Systematization of this process necessarily entailed improvement, improvement which was aided by the use of brick consructions (fig. 18).
On the whole, the Romans adapted and developed the maximum from the techniques they had inherited. They had adopted the Greek techniques of working stone, and had extensively developed all the techniques of making and building with brick, which was to become the major material used in Roman Antiquity. They undoubtedly learnt from the Barbarians a certain number of techniques for working with wood, which was largely used in regions where resources of this
Figure 1 8. - Caracalla thermae. (From A. Choisy)

===== PAGE 380 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
363 material were infinitely superior to those known by the Mediterranean world. Their genius lay in their ability to combine all these techniques and to exploit them to their greatest potential, and it is above all this quality which enabled them to develop the expertise which we still admire today.
GEOGRAPHICAL SPACE:
A NEW SUBJECT OF
TECHNICAL ORGANISATION
Very large countries which could promote technical change did not really appear until the reign of Alexander. Ancient Egypt only consis ted of a narrow strip of land which stretched along the banks of the
Nile. The originality of the Roman Empire lay not only in its extraord inary geographical extension, but also in the fact that it spread to regions which were very diverse both in climate and natural resources.
We have already stressed the importance of this fact. Geographical space had to be organised, and as we have already seen, the techniques involved in this organisation were particularly pursued by the Romans.
Means of Transport Methods of transport concern both transport techniques themselves as well as all the resulting indispensable infrastructures. Our knowledge in this field is very unequal: the famous Roman roads have been the subject of thorough investigation. For several years, thanks to a fashion - to be sure, a very profitable fashion - naval archaeological research has increased with the exploration of wrecks. Apart from these favoured sectors, there are other fields which have hardly attracted historians' attention.
Need we remind the reader that the latin names of land vehicles were almost all borrowed from the Celtic language: carpentum, the chariot built on a framework, carrus, the cart, henna, the truck, the four-wheeled reda and petorritum. Most of the vehicles used by the
Romans seem to have been two-wheeled. Four-wheeled vehicles

===== PAGE 381 =====

364
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
might have had a mobile front-axle unit, for without this they would have been very difficult to manoeuvre. Iconographic sources depicting these do exist, but the use of such vehicles must have been extremely limited.
It is virtually unanimously agreed that the horseshoe was not yet used at this time. Similarly modern harness with a shoulder collar was not yet known either, and only the Antique form of harness, which went round the throat of the animal, was used, meaning that only light loads could be pulled. Land transport would thus have had to have been carried out by porterage rather than by vehicles, and this was to remain so until the late Middle Ages. Moreover, an edict by Diocletian limited the loads allowed for two-wheeled vehicles on important roads.
Navigable waterways were extensively used, and we possess numerous references to this. Once again, it would be useful to have a map showing the navigation routes followed in the Roman epoch, and this could certainly be done without too much difficulty. Some archaeological discoveries have brought to light the type of craft used.
The Utrecht Museum preserves a vessel which was probably used on the Rhine or the Moselle. The boat is relatively flat and raised at both ends which are absolutely parallel. The transport capacity of such a craft was fairly limited, if indeed it was used for transporting merchan dise.
Maritime navigation must have made considerable progress. Here again, the Romans had inherited traditions from the Greeks and almost certainly also profited from the techniques of other peoples, particularly the maritime tribes of Gaul (we know that Ceasar greatly admired the ships of the Veneti). Veneti ships had iron tackles, oak hulls fitted together with long nails, and semi-rigid sails of skins sewn together. According to Lucian, the boat which brought corn from
Egypt during the 2nd century, had sails made of skins. This system had its drawbacks, however, particularly when it came to manoeuvring the craft (fig. 19).
Underwater excavations have revealed a great deal of information on the Roman navy, whereas the historian formerly had to resort to studying pictorial representations which were often difficult to inter pret. Research has been carried out on more than twenty wrecks, ranging in date from the 2nd century BC to the 4th century AD. These are usually keeled vessels consisting of an internal structure formed of

===== PAGE 382 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
365 continuous or discontinuous frames, and planks which are joined longitudinally, (stringers and wales) and transversely (frames and beams). The planking, which may have been single or double, was always free board, held by mortice and tenon pin joints. It was carefully calked and sometimes covered with an exterior lead sheathing. The pieces were mainly joined to each other with pegs and nailed-down copper plates. The wood used for the construction of such vessels was fairly varied and was chosen according to the type and function of the ship to be built. Already curved wood was used, or it could be bent in the heat. Planking seemed to be joined on the keel, and the frames were inserted after the planking had been assembled. All these techniques were directly derived from Egyptian methods.
Figure 1 9. - Roman ship from Sausse. (From M. Daumas)

===== PAGE 383 =====

366
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Some evolution may be observed from the Greek type of wreck of the "Grand Congloue" of the 2nd century BC to those of the 4th century AD. After the middle of the 1st century BC, most ships were constructed in the single planking method, thus forming a lighter hull, which presupposes that the rib was reinforced. The practice of using lead sheathing was abandoned between the 1st and the end of the 2nd century AD, for reasons which are difficult to assertain. This evolution was to make the hull much lighter and to reinforce the internal framework (more nailing was used). This evolution was a continuation of the Mediterranean tradition initiated by the Egyptians. The last stage in this development of ship building is illustrated by the vessel found in 1975 at Marseille. Also noteworthy is the appearance of second mast which inclines towards the bow.
The standard capacity of the ship in the Hellenistic epoch was approximately 130 tons. During the Roman period, imperial transport reached a capacity of 340 tons, and 1300 tons could be carried by the ship built at Ostia, designed to transport corn from Egypt. We should lastly point out that there was a large number of different ships.
The Infrastructure of Transport Infrastructure techniques involved in transports were just as important as the means of transport themselves. In this area techniques were necessarily linked to each other, as well as being linked to other techniques of a different type.
It has. often been suggested that the Romans did not develop rivers and waterways, but this affirmation doe̚not seem to hold true. We have pointed out the existence of the canal built by Corbulo, between the Rhine and the Meuse, the Fossa Drusiana, crossing the dangerous delta, and the Marius canal, between Aries and Fos. This sector of
Roman engineering has been dreadfully neglected.
The Roman road, on the other hand, has been the object of high praise and has rightly remained famous (fig. 20). It represents an extremely important innovation which was probably developed at a relatively late date, as it was virtually non existant in Ancient Greece.
Its structure is well known: it mainly consists of a deep roadway set in a vast trench, flanked by drainage ditches and gentle embankments, so we might almost call it a sunken wall. The real innovation lay in the superposition of layers of large flat rubble stone, gravel, diagonally

===== PAGE 384 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
367 Figure 20. - Cross sections of a Roman road. (From M. Daumas) placed slabs, dry rubble, and sometimes layers of mortar. The road was steeply cambered and raised slightly above the surrounding terrain. It possessed a sort of elasticity which contributed to its solidity.
The only drawback was frost, particularly in the north of the Empire, as water could penetrate this veritable stonework and would, as it began to ice and expand, dislocate the stone construction.
Pliny recommended that the road be 18 feet (5.35 metres) wide, but main roads were often double this width. The problems of the course of the road have been the subject of many discussions but we should obviously qualify all this: the frequently used straight route was not as systematic in construction as was thought and could only be used in areas which were not hilly. The other idea which is generally accepted is the permanence of these roads. The Romans indisputably adapted their road network to traffic, and to the inevitable modifications in the latter. Whatever the case may be, the Romans established a vast road network: it has been estimated that 90,000 kilometres of major roads were built, and 200,000 kilometres of secondary roads existed.
The Western Mediterranean was appreciably poorer in natural ports than the rest of the area. This explains why the Romans had to create more artificial harbours than the Greeks. The Greeks, however, had shown themselves to be quite capable of building ports in the Hellen is-

===== PAGE 385 =====

368
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
tic epoch, as is demonstrated by the port of Alexandria. Remarkable examples of Roman work in this field have remained. The port of
Ostia, at the mouth of the River Tiber, consists of several successive basins, the largest of which was described by Pliny the Younger, and was built under Trajan. One could equally cite the ports of Civitavec chia, Terracina, Pozzuoli, Misenum in Italy, and Cherchell and Leptis
Magna in Africa. The port of Frejus in Gaul was dug out of the solid ground by hand; its basins were probably used right up into the 17th century. Although the Romans did not perfect any method of prevent ing silt from accumulating, they did, on the other hand, reach a high degree of improvement in quays constructing methods, as recent finds at Marseille have illustrated.
Roman Waterworks The extent of Roman waterworks and water systems is astonishing, but it was only partly innovatory. The Samos tunnel, the aqueduct of
Pisistratus, and the techniques for channelling water developed in Mesopotamia and Persia, are overwhelming proof that mankind had made great attempts to advance in this domain. Once again, the sheer scale of the work astonishes us today.
Extensive lead production, principally from Spanish mines, undoubtedly made possible the construction of the innovatory aque duct bridge for crossing small valleys, the development of the principle of the inverted siphon and enabled greater water distribution in towns and cities. The Romans, masters of an advanced technology, had solved the problems of levelling, and applied tried and tested systems of organisation, (a perfect example of which is provided by Fron tin us), and so did not hesitate to use water supplies which were situated 33, 50, or even 100 kilometres away. All the aqueducts were constructed in such a way as to ensure that the slope was both adequate and regular enough to transport the water. If the slope was too steep, then it was broken by a system of cascades. The twenty-four cascades between the
Morvan mountains and the town of Autun (France) thus made possi ble the transportation of a water supply to the town without any difficulty.
Tunnel passages demonstrate some of the mistakes which were made, as exemplified in Bougie around the middle of the 2nd century.
On the other hand, springs were harnessed very successfully, and

===== PAGE 386 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
369 reservoirs, manholes, and distribution towers were also successfully constructed. Slopes were usually slight, 0.2 in 1000. We need only look at the magnificent achievements of the Pont du Gard and Segovia aqueducts. Lyons' inverted siphon required some 26 kilometres oflead piping, and lead conduits also crossed from the Rhone to Aries.
The Arabs have often been credited for the extensive irrigation systems in both North Africa and Spain. In fact the Arabs often only reused and reactivated Roman installations. But again we must admit that we lack precise studies on these works, some of which were still in use at the end of the 16th century.
In all these fields, the extent of these achievements presented new problems. It was because of their geographical expansion that the
Romans could exploit to saturation point the techniques which they had inherited from the Greeks and the countries of the Near East. The
Greeks had been prevented from making further progress by the exiguity of their cities' territory. The Alexandrian Empire had lacked the time to develop techniques because it was involved in conquering other lands, but the Romans had both the space and the passage of many centuries in which to concentrate on technical development.
It is difficult to judge globaly accurately the technical level achieved by the Romans, and errors have undoubtedly been made on this account. We may summarize by suggesting that the part played by the
Romans was of stabilizing technical development which had almost been constant up until then.
We may detect only a very few new ideas. As we have emphasized several times, the Romans were the ideal inheritors of Greek tech niques in almost every sector and, via the latter, of all the techniques of the Eastern basin of the Mediterrranean.
Obviously their conquests and the geographical expansion of their civilization, meant that the Romans could profit from techniques which had been unknown in Mediterrranean regions, as has been exemplified by the use of Gallic or Germanic techniques as well as about Pergamum. It was in this way that the Mediterranean technical system could be completed, but without entailing any profound uphea vals. The so-called barbarian techniques were perfectly integrated into the pre-existing system.
Some sectors of technology remained in which improvements were possible and desirable, as we have quite clearly demonstrated for tools. But we should not forget that the Romans had at their disposal

===== PAGE 387 =====

370
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
an infinitely greater quantity of certain materials than had their precursors, their teachers: lead and copper from Spain, and iron provided by all the regions north of Italy. This supply was invaluable, as an abundance of materials necessarily leads not only to an abun dance of technical processes but also to their diversification. This we can easily notice nowadays.
Finally, let us review the conclusions which will terminate this discussion on the organisation of geographical space. The growth of the latter indubitably encourages unnecessary or needless improvements and adaptations in restricted areas. Even today, do we not talk of the technological power of great empires? Although the
Romans may not have been innovators as such, they were, on the other hand, obliged to be great realizers of technical systems, and the transition from small scale systems to large scale works could not have been achieved without initiating some changes.
Another problem has yet to be discussed which historians have virtually ignored, no doubt because it confuses their arguments. We have looked at the blockage which halted the development of Greek tech-niques: let us look again at the texts which have been frequently cited. They could equally apply to the Roman epoch which did not show any signs of either important innovation or of developing an advanced system of mechanization, even during the period when the reasons put forward for this, such as the institution of slavery or the contempt of manual labour, were less convincing or no longer existed.
And this was despite the improvement of certain material conditions (natural riches were far more abundant).
We must therefore find other reasons for this phenomenon, but they are more difficult to define. One explanation springs immediately to mind: the Romans made absolutely no progress in the field of science, or at least in sciences upon which technical progress could depend.
Neither their physics nor their knowledge of materials were superior to those which the Greeks could have known, notwithstanding the geographical growth of their civilization. Research in this domain is singularly limited, and we may be correct in suggesting that there was indeed a blockage, similar to the Chinese technological civilization which was also blocked from a certain point onwards. The perception of this halt, as this term is better than that of blockage, will always be as difficult to explain as the new advance which took place in the middle of the 12th century. We are asking a question which is impossible to

===== PAGE 388 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
371 answer, as it will almost always be impossible to account logically for any negative movements which occur within a civilization.
BYZANTIUM
The same characteristics and problems are to be found in this area, as Hellenistic Greece, Rome, and Byzantium shared the same technical system. Only narrow-minded specialists claim otherwise. The bril liance of the Byzantine civilization has often obscured its lack of technical imagination. Similarly, one may speak of the brilliance of the
Hellenistic and Roman civilizations.
Chronologically speaking, the history of Byzantium is very exten sive, as it develops over ten centuries, from around 500 AD to the middle of the 15th century. From the 11th century onwards we have rightly included Byzantium in the Western European world. The
"take-off" of Western Europe from the 12th century encompasses the whole of the Christian civilization, and so Byzantium too. From the 6th to the 1 1th century, Byzantium was a faithful reflection of earlier civilizations.
Technical Thought The blockage of technical thought is well illustrated by the appearance of technical literature which merely repeated old Hellenistic treatises.
Alphonse Dain has demonstrated how the relationship of ideas stemming from the Alexandrian school, or sometimes even earlier civilizations, were followed until the end of the 10th century. This is one of the most remarkable features of technical stabilization, of which there are few other examples. An attitude characteristic of a blockage of technical thought is exemplified by the fact that treatises dating back some twelve or thirteen centuries were thought to contain new ideas by mankind in the 10th century.
To a large extent works on military techniques were handed down from man to man, but unfortunately we know very little about these people, and are even uncertain of their names. Apollodorus of Dama scus was in fact very romanized and indeed worked for Trajan in

===== PAGE 389 =====

372
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Rome. He wrote a treatise on siegecraft, in which there are few innovations. On the whole, he tends to repeat Philo of Byzantium on the subject of fortification, and Athenodorus on the subject of war machines. The tactics of Onesandrus in the 1st century recalls those of
Ennius writing in the 4th century BC. Following in the same tradition were Aelian, Arrian and Polyaenus: only their historic examples change and demonstrate the stability of the techniques employed.
A certain awakening seems to have come about during the period of Justinian in the 6th century AD, but what had been written often several centuries earlier was still repeated. Possibly the most original works were those written by Urbicius, the strategies and a treatise on hunting, a tract on mechanical studies by Anthemius of Tralles who began the construction of Saint Sophia, and two anonymous works on strategies and siegecraft. But on the whole such treatises remained indisputably immersed in the tradition of the ancient texts, contribut ing only details. Thus the technical spirit of the age tended to degrade that of the Alexandrine epoch.
A similar revival took place in the 10th century, with Leo VI the Wise and Constantine VII Porphyrogenitus. Dain has listed seven compilations from this period, by which we can tell the subjects of the great texts of the Alexandrine school: compilations on machines, siegecraft, strategies and tactics, as well as compilations on naval war, which may be new as we do not have any evidence of earlier works on this subject. They broadly repeat all the known collections, and updating of older texts appear to be rare and fairly insignificant.
Nicephorus Ouranos, who was in a way the culmination of this movement, demonstrated little more originality. The Geoponica, the only collection of texts made in the same period on the subject of agriculture, really brought together everything contributed up until then by Antique agronomy.
One of the most celebrated authors of this epoch was unquestiona bly Hero of Byzantium. Both this author's name and work prove everything that has already been said on this subject. The name is significant as there is no evidence to show that such a person even existed. All the works on applied mechanics were probably attributed to one Hero. His Geodesy and his Treatise on machines seem to be a semi-complete reproduction of works from the Alexandrine period.
Of all these treatises only a scientific edition could obviously demon strate the stability of the Byzantine technical world.

===== PAGE 390 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
373 The Principal Techniques of Byzantium Although this area still requires more detailed study, major Byzantine techniques only contributed a few innovations. Like the Romans, the
Byzantines profited from some of the improvements made by neigh bouring peoples, and advances undoubtedly resulted from local condi tions rather than from inventive genius, which was extremely limited in any event.
No changes may be observed in exploitation techniques. On the subject of farming, to take just one example, the swing plough, an everyday tool according to pictorial representations, was an obvious legacy from Classical Antiquity. Cultivation techniques, other farming tools, and the crop types, remained the same. The Geoponica do not reveal any major transformations.
No new techniques were introduced either in mining or metal working. The only interesting fact was the exceptional skill displayed in smelting and casting bronze. The great bronze gates, which for many years opened out upon Western Europe, are proof of this expertise:
the gates of San Zeno in Verona, those of Saint Sabina in Rome, and possibly the gate-posts of Aix-la-Chapelle are the most favoured examples of this. Until the introduction of iron hinges, these bronze doors were certainly the best fences devised to enclose edifices which were high in quality and rich in materials. The Byzantine Empire was too poor in iron for any progress to be made in iron- and steel-making techniques.
Similarly, little progress was made in the domain of energy. Hydrau lic energy does not seem to have been particularly developed, even in areas of the Empire which were more favourable for its advance. But here again, we lack detailed studies to substantiate this claim. Nor may we base any opinions on iconographic sources, such as the Apamea mill, without being certain that this evidence is true to the overall situation.
Transport methods are identical to those used in Classical Anti quity. in fact, harnessing methods had changed significantly at the same time in Western Europe as in Byzantium, from the beginning of the 12th century, although the iron horseshoe and the shoulder collar had been introduced some decades earlier.
Recent studies have demonstrated that Byzantine ships were direc tly derived from Greek vessels, apart from the method of caulking

===== PAGE 391 =====

374
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
which had been borrowed from the Arabs. Despite this, some evolu tion may be observed in boat building.
Before the Arab invasions, ships were exactly the same as those used by the Greeks. We need only look at the dromon, which had a single row of oarsmen, and the liburnian, which was simply a light version of the dromon.
The period from the 7th to the 12th century marks the peak of the imperial Byzantine navy. A dromon, with two rows of oarsmen and which could carry up to a hundred men, was developed, but apart from this, the ships were identical to those used in preceding civilizations.
Although the Byzantines were undoubtedly skilled builders, they did not contribute any important innovations to the techniques handed down by either the Sassanids or more particularly by the Romans.
They mainly used brick according to the methods inspired by the former, but they used the Roman method of building vaults without curves or bends, known as groin vaulting. They also employed Roman mortar in brick constructions. Most of the achievements of the Byzan tine Empire, Saint Sophia (6th century, fig.21) and the church of Saint
Mark in Venice (11th century, fig.22), as well as aqueducts and water tanks, did not show any new technical innovations.
This is also true of town planning where again the Byzantines followed the tradition laid down by the Romans. At the end of the 5th century, edicts issued by Zeno specified road widths and projecting balconies according to the same norms which Ancient Rome had established some time before.
The grandeur of Byzantine techniques lay more in the perfection of the execution and decoration of their work than in the methods employed. We will illustrate this fact with just a few examples.
Weaving techniques had been borrowed from Egypt. A still very simple form of the simple loom seems to have been introduced during the 5th century. For some time, silk factories, which were controlled by the state, continued to use Sassanid techniques. The beauty and the variety of the colours and designs of Byzantine textiles, some of which were Persian in inspiration, were their most striking qualities, and they spread throughout the Western world. The same applies to their silver ware, and more especially to their cloisonne enamels which were particularly popular in Byzantium. Byzantine mosaics were also far more superior in quality to the work of the Romans, as the wealth of the materials, and the variety of colours, especially the gold

===== PAGE 392 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
375 Figure 21. - Saint Sophia. (From A. Choisy) backgrounds (small flakes of gold set between two glass sheets), are extremely impressive on first sight.
Byzantine techniques had therefore been borrowed from various conflicting civilizations which had gradually become integrated.
Byzantium was in fact the natural continuation of the Roman tradition of assimilation. The Mediterranean and southern position of the
Byzantine Empire explains certain choices. And we can attempt a list of the contributing countries, with the techniques they "gave" : (a) from Greece came the techniques of agriculture, hunting, fishing, and boat building; (b) from Rome, all the techniques of construction;

===== PAGE 393 =====

376
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 22. - Saint Mark. (From A. Choisy) (c) from the Orient stemmed Sassanid brick, Arabic damascening and calking, and Egyptian fabrics.
We should seek to find out why the Byzantine Empire preserved the technical system which it had inherited from its predecessors or its neighbours, as we have done for the Roman Empire.
