If historians appear not have asked this question, it is in fact because the technical reality was to some extent eclipsed by an incredibly brilliant civili zation. The artistic wealth of Byzantium actually hid a perfectly stable technology. Moreover, we should clarify the extent to which the
Byzantine Empire could or could not profit from new techniques introduced in Western Europe from the 12th century onwards. The ever limited geographical space which Arab and Turkish invasions left the Empire of the East, could no longer provide the raw materials indispensable for more advanced techniques. It is astonishing that the
Latin Empire of the Orient, arising moreover from the technical

===== PAGE 394 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
377 civilization of Western Europe, should have contributed so little in this area, other than better fortifications, and the windmill. Lack of water, and the absence of metalliferous deposits literally blocked the devel opment of techniques in this part of the Christian world. It could hardly do otherwise. The end of the Oriental Empire was not merely the result of a wave of invasions, but was also produced by the gradual disappearance of natural resources indispensable for the survival of a nation.
Greek Fire as an Example For a long time Byzantium has been credited with the use of what was known during the period as Greek fire, of which there may have been several types. Recent research has shown that this was not a Byzantine idea, and that naphtha, as well as a one particular powder, had been known for some time.
During the Persian siege of Athens in 480 BC, Herodotus men tioned the use of arrows tipped with oakum which was lit before the arrow was shot into the air. The oakum must therefore have been impregnated with an inflammable material. During the sieges of
Plataea in 479 BC and Delium in 424 BC, Thucydides cites the use of bellows for fanning the flames of a fire, the basic material of which was bitumen. Aeneas the Tactician, describes in some detail the use of incendary missiles in chapters 33 to 35 of his Tactical Theory. There are ma!ly such quotations up to the Byzantine period. The use of "Greek fire" was first mentioned in 678 whe,n it was employed against the Arab fleet. As we have just seen the name was new but the weapon itself was already quite old at this epoch.
The material used was most probably asphalt oil, either by itself or possibly mixed with sulphur and pitch. Athenodorus, and Julius the
African revealed the first mixtures of spontaneous ignition which were mainly composed of hydrocarbons, with quick lime to raise the temperature, and once again saltpetre, or "salt dust" as it was described in a Greek formula.
Although incendiary weapons were only sporadically used in Classical Antiquity, they became more standardized during the
Byzantine period, and it is very likely that their application was considerably improved. Parallel to the unlimited progress and great effectiveness of these incendiary weapons, was the introduction of a

===== PAGE 395 =====

378
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
new concept, which consisted of confining a fuse product in a relatively enclosed and sufficiently resistant space, like a tube for example.
These were steps towards the rocket, jet propelled by the escape of combustion gases. Reeds were often used for the tube, and although the mixture remained a state secret we know that it was formed of a hydocarbon, sulphur and an inflammable material which could only have been saltpetre.
Finally, tubes were required to launch the rockets. "It consisted of a reed pipe in which certain materials were packed; in order to use it, the reed pipe was placed in a bronze tube, lit at one end, and then after a great deal of thunder and smoke, it rose into the air like a burning meteor and reached its intended target." This suggests that fixed tubes must have been used, like those which can be seen on the bows of boats illustrated in some manuscript miniatures, as well as mobile tubes, of which we have no pictorial representations. Maurice Mercier, a
French historian who specializes in all these machines, suggests that the Byzantines were on the way to developing the canon and gun, where the rocket itself was to become the cartridge.
A miniature in a Vatican manuscript shows a "siphonal" boat, as they were termed by Leo VI the Wise. The tube was necessarily oblique in order to draw out the inflammable liquid. This liquid was drawn out by the aid of a suction and force pump, and launched ablaze towards the opponent. The tube was fixed rather like the mast of the bowsprit on the sterm of the ship, so it was useless in rough sea.
To conclude, Leo VI recommended that "the pots filled with fireworks would also set fire to their boats when they broke up."
Another text tells us that "these closed pots contained fire which suddenly burst in flashes and set fire to the objects which they hit." For a long time we have been unsure of the composition of the spontaneous ignition product. The chemist, Forbes, a good historian on the subject of oil, believes that if an exact combination of oil and quick lime was exposed to humidity, the heat developed by the contact of the lime with humidity might be sufficient to light the mixture spontaneously, particularly if it contained fine particles.
The Byzantines therefore appear to have contributed major improvements to extremely old techniques in this field, although some points have yet to be clarified.
The whole of this period, covering twelve centuries from the 2nd century BC to the 10th century AD, a history rich in events, reveals a

===== PAGE 396 =====

THE ROMANS AND THEIR SUCCESSORS
379 remarkable technical stability. In some instances, however, there was even some deterioration in the techniques which had already been acquired, as is demonstrated by the formulae applied to war machines by Philo of Byzantium and Hero of Alexandria. These disappear after
Vitruvius. We should certainly not ignore some of the interesting improvements made by both the Romans and the Byzantines, although these improvements do not constitute radical upheaval.

===== PAGE 397 =====

BLOCKED TECHNICAL SYSTEMS
To be honest, the title of this chapter is rather ambiguous, all the more so because it applies to three civilizations which are very removed from each other: pre-Colombian America, China and the Islamic world. We have already seen other blocked systems, such as that of the Egyptians, for example, and the same has been said of the Greek technical system.
Similarly, what of the so-called primitive peoples who remained on the technical level of stone tools?
From the 12th century onwards, only Western Europe saw succes sive changes in its technical system, in the industrial revolution of the
Middle Ages (to take the title of a recent work), in the technical revolution of the Renaissance, the technical revolution of the 18th century, that of the second half of the 19th century, and in yet another which is changing the world today. The real problem lies in the fact that other systems seem to have been blocked at the end of the Middle
Ages.
We hope, however, that a brief survey of the three civilizations mentioned above will help to reveal the crux of the problem.
CHINESE TECHNIQUES
Tlie study of Chinese techniques presents many, various difficulties.
Firstly, there is an internal problem, as the chronology of Chinese technical discoveries is uncertain. One particular authority, reputed moreover for his knowledge of material life in China, attributes one

===== PAGE 398 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
381 invention to epochs which are separated by several centuries and all in the space of a few pages. There is yet another problem. For some time
China has played an important part in the history of science and techniques. We may enviously recall the early beginnings of the
Chinese civilization, and its sudden halt in the 15th or 16th century.
Many tables have been drawn up to demonstrate the technical intel ligence of the Chinese during very early periods, and praises have been sung about inventions which mainly preceded discoveries made in the
West. And we should cite all the contributions made by the Chinese civilization during its technical expansion to European techniques in order to illustrate the extent of the progress made by the former.
But a redress was necessary: The relatively recent discovery of the fact that China remained fixed within an out-moded technical system has to some extent discredited the Far East. Suddenly we witness a complete reversal. This was also true of the Middle Ages in Western
Europe which had long remained in the dark and then was suddenly brought to light with some exaggerations. Should we need point out, moreover, that the division between these disciplines is such today that a sinologist is completely unaware of the civilization of Ancient Egypt and vice versa. There is a total lack of chronological tables relating to
Chinese technical progress, and it would be desirable if one could re-establish the correct balances and furthermore ask some essential questions. An exact chronology is therefore indispensable. The water mill is a very revealing example: Tsouei Leang (386/334 BC) has been cited for building water mills in the middle of the 4th century BC, but on the other hand, the construction of the first water mill has been dated in the year 30 BC. In this last case, it would have been exactly contemporary with the introduction of the Western mill. Without attempting to find forerunners, would it not be logical to suggest that technical acquisitions could have taken place at the same date in civilizations which were very removed from each other, but which were aware of similar developments?
The second question relates to the early beginnings of technical progress, at least in some areas of technology, and we should be cautious in our investigation here. During the Neolithic period, China was at exactly the same stage as the rest of Western Europe. Major developments were virtually contemporary with those made on the borders of the Oriental basin of the Mediterranean, but later than those of Mesopotamia and Egypt. Chinese techniques seem to have

===== PAGE 399 =====

382
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
began to advance over a technically fixed Classical civilization around the Christian era. What were the reasons for this, and what medium did technical progress in China take? We must admit that there is still a great deal to be learnt about the ancient economic and demographic evolution which took place in China. Our knowledge of the history of science is far more comprehensive.
Finally, there was a sudden halt in technical progress which should be taken into account and must also be explained. It is universally agreed that the Chinese technical system was blocked from a certain time onwards, but the causes and significance of this are still a subject for endless discussion.
The last problem concerns the transference of a number of Chinese techniques to Western Europe. Perhaps the contribution made by
China to a Western world which was trying to disengage itself from a Greco-Roman technical environment has been over-emphasized. The following elements are always cited: the voyage of Marco Polo, the compass, paper, gunpowder, and the modern harness. Paper undoub tedly originated in the Far East. Despite the linguistic efforts made by
Andre-Georges Haudricourt, harnessing still remains a rather vague subject. The idea that gunpowder was a Chinese contribution has recently been challenged, and it is very unlikely that the same pheno monen occurred for the techniques of smelting, unthinkable as it was for the water mill. We may suggest the parallel development of concomitant inventions, which were the result of identical technical reasoning: the water mill is one such example. All things considered, if we look at the mid 13th century, we would notice a brilliant technical civilization: should this exclude the remarkable cathedrals, irrigation systems of Spain, and the Scandinavian ships which crossed the
Atlantic?
Techniques and Technology One of the features of technically advanced civilizations is the devel opment of their own systems of technology. It is extremely instructive to define the domains covered by all these ancient treatises and, if the case arises, their chronological sequence. They exactly define the limits of techniques which had arrived at a certain maturity and which were consequently suitable material for dogmatic accounts. It is quite natural that China should have arrived at this stage. But here again, we

===== PAGE 400 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
383 should define chronological limits. Although a military treatise existed from the beginning of the 5th century BC, we may observe that most of these written works, in fact almost all, date from the 10th and 1 1th centuries, with only a few dating before this (one from the 6th century). This should be clarified in comparison to Western technical literature which was particularly prolific during the period from the 4th to the 1st centuries BC.
Some of these works testify that Chinese technology, at least certain Chinese techniques were not totally empirical, but that technical knowledge was organized in some areas. The great clock tower of
Kaifeng, built by Su Song in 1088, was preceeded by a specific theoretical treatise, developed by his pupil, Han Gong Lian, who studied gear systems and the basic principles of general mechanics.
This treatise should be compared to some of the very early works of the Alexandrian school. Such a comparison would enable us to ask a question which has rarely been considered: which techniques did
China borrow from the Western world?
Let us look at this type of literature in more detail, and attempt to classify such works according to their subject matter.
I - Agricultural literature was certainly very important. Six treatises have been attributed to the Han epoch (in other words approximately two centuries before and two centuries after our era). The first was written by Fang Cheng-tche in the 1st century AD but unfortunately these have all disappeared today. The first complete treatise available for our examination was written by Kia Sseu-Sie (533-546), and we have another by Tchou-Fou (1101-1103). Both works reflect a traditional farming system, the rules of which were no doubt estab lished and laid down many years before. Some more specialized treatises were also compiled, for example works written on horticul ture during the Song epoch (960-1279), eight treatises on veterinary art under the Souei dynasty (581-617), and the great tract on agricultural mechanization by Wang Chen, the preface of which dates back to
1313. The same dogmatic spirit can be found in all these works, together with the same ensemble of formulae naturally transposed into a slightly different environment, which may also be observed in the works of Latin agronomists. The spirit in which these works were written is identical.
II - Literature on the subject of building is much less prolific. There

===== PAGE 401 =====

384
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
is, however, the treatise on architecture by Li Jie (circa 1100), and a manual on carpentry by Yu Hao (10th century). The second work represents something new, as did the tract on farming machines mentioned above. It is significant that new areas were explored relatively late in comparison to Western technical literature.
III - The first large compendium of military techniques was compiled by Zeng Gong Liang aound 500 BC. Literature on the subject of military techniques does not appear to have undergone a very great development in China, although this was also true of
Europe, apart perhaps from works written on poliorcetics and treatises on war engines. In this field, China seems to have been behind the techniques developed by Western Europe.
IV - On the subject of mechanics, Chinese literature was on the whole well behind that of the West. We have already mentioned, however, an 11th century treatise on gear systems. Also, around 1030
Yan Su compiled a work on clock making which was far more advanced than the many treatises on water-clocks of Classical Anti quity. China does actually seem to have been ahead of Europe in this particular field, as it was only in the 14th century that an equivalent was produced in Europe with a tract by Dondi.
To the above list we may add a treatise on sugar by Weng Chao (960-1026), a work on wine by Sou Che (1036-1 101) , and a few descriptions on silk making. This virtually completes the survey of
Chinese technology.
Several remarks may be made ragarding this very brief survey of Chinese technical literature. The first point concerns the extent of this technology. It does not appear to differ radically from what we have seen in Greece and Rome, or elsewhere in the Middle Ages in
Europe. Many large collections dealt with agriculture and the military arts, and some important treatises concentrated on what is known today as the "latest technology." We have said before that we should think of this from a chronological viewpoint, but we unfortunately lack comprehensive editions in European languages, by which we could compare these two traditions and examine the Arab tradition which has long been suspected as having provided an intermediary between the Far East and Europe. The excellent drawings which are usually reproduced are extracts from the famous work on The
Exploitation of Works of Nature, written in 1637, at a time when

===== PAGE 402 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
385 Wes tern techniques were already more advanced than those of China.
Exploitation of Natural Resources Chinese agriculture as a whole seems to have known all the stages of other agricultural methods. The processes of technical development are virtually the same everywhere. Naturally variations of climatic and hydrologic regimes, and vegetable and biological differences play a part. It is upon these differences that we must concentrate, as the rest concerns a common technical system.
Like everywhere else, crop types tended to multiply, and this growth seems to have been very rapid. Under the Zhou dynasty, around the beginning of the first millennium BC, the main cereals of the country were already extensively cultivated: panic grass, different varieties of rice, and barley. Later, spikelet millet, hemp, sesame, and corn were grown. But rice very quickly became the most important crop. From the beginning of the Christian era to the first half of the first millennium, so from the Han to the Song dynasty, new plants appeared which came from either the steppes or tropical zones. Vine is thought to have been introduced at the end of the 6th or the beginning of the 7th century. At the end of the Tang dynasty, that is to say around the year 1000 AD, water rice was introduced, which was to spread from Southern to Northern China. Kapok was also extensively cultivated throughout the country between the 10th and
13th centuries.
If technical treatises are to be believed, then around the year 1000, Chinese agriculture was already based on a scientific method, as selection and hybridizations were common practices, and already seven types of rice were known.
Fruit trees were cultivated very early on; first of all the peach, apricot, guinee, and the jujube were introduced, and later the plum and pear tree. Grafting was often practised, and market gardening was equally widespread. At the beginning of the 12th century, there was a great variety of leguminous plants including the bean and the pea, and some specialized plants were to make their appearance. Egyptian experts may have arrived to improve sugar cane producing methods.
Here again, Chinese agronomic science had almost nothing to envy in the Arab gardens of Southern Spain. At the beginning of the 12th

===== PAGE 403 =====

386
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
century there were eleven varieties of apricot, eighteen types of bean, and eight species of pea.
The ancient practice of horticulture saw major development bet ween the 10th and 13th centuries. The peony, chrysanthemum, lotus, orchid, day lily, lily, magnolia, and begonia all featured in ornamental gardens. The peony and the narcissus were borrowed from the Arabs.
Farming tools and cultivation methods were certainly developed very quickly. The Chinese had a good knowledge of soils, and the standard practice of using manure is attested in the 13th century. But
China was not so fortunate as Western Europe which had an abun dance of manure. Chinese farmers consequently ploughed in nitrate fertilizers instead ofusing manure for crop cultivation. They also made a type of compost consisting of lime, cob from old walls, and mud from ditches. We will later examine how drainage and irrigation systems were extensively employed.
Farming tools undoubtedly profited from a highly developed metal lurgy. The swing plough evolved during the period between the Han and the Song dynasties, in other words in the first half of the first millennium AD. The sole-ard was used, different to the ard of
Classical Antiquity, but similar to the one which spread throughout the northern border of the Mediterranean during the same period. Other instruments differed little to those used by other civilizations.
Agricultural treatises, like governmental orders issued during epochs when agriculure was controlled by the state, established strict programmes for any farm work. A treatise dating from around the 10th century proves that crop rotation was practised, and that the alter nation of millet and beans was advocated.
Thus Chinese farming techniques showed not only some degree of evolution but were also at a fairly high level of development. But these techniques were barely more advanced than those of the Western world at the same period, except perhaps for those of horticulture.
This is also true of the principles involved in other techniques for the exploitation of nature. Neither animal domestication, hunting nor fishing demonstrates any fundamental differences.
Mining techniques appear to have been fairly well developed, but truth to tell we Jack precise data and subsequently any comprehensive chronology for these techniques. We will later examine the dates proposed for the appearance of some of the various metals. We do know that the Chinese quickly exploited salt deposits, as the industry

===== PAGE 404 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
387 goes back to the 1st century BC. A work dating from the year 1334 AD provides more precise information on this subject. The same tech niques were used by both China and the West: water was sent into the mine, and then the brine was brought up and reduced in boilers. In
1080, a well was sunk to a depth of 3000 feet. The Chinese were fortunate in having a choice material available for such a task, as bamboo was abundant and could be made into considerable quantities of very long pipes, by fitting one bamboo pole into another. We know very little, however, about other Chinese methods of mining coal and oil.
The vitality of a technical system and the power of an economy are often measured in relation to the quantity of energy available within a country. Energy problems are therefore primary considerations. On the one hand, there is natural energy, and China and Western Europe were well provided in this domain, far more so than Mediterranean countries, and on the other hand, there are energy converters.
Like everywhere else, the first type of energy to be exploited was animal energy. The wheel was certainly borrowed from Western civilizations. We know that harnessing methods were very important.
We can say very little on the subject of yokes, but the horse involves more innovations. The saddle, stirrup, and bit were probably invented in Central Asia, according to some authorities as early as the 3rd century BC. Haudricourt has shown that a very clear separation exists on either side of a line drawn from Central Vietnam to the Baltic.
Below this line was the chariot with a shaft attachment and pulled by two animals, and above is the chariot pulled by a single animal in
. shafts. Harnessing methods could not have been the same in both cases. Representations dating from the Han dynasty show an already advanced harnessing system. The two shafts were curved and joined together at one end, where a small yoke was attached which formed the main support. Traction was maintained by a breast strap, similar to our breast harness. Later, the yoke disappeared and the shafts were shortened, whereupon the support became a backing which was placed on the horse's back. The breast harness and the breech band remained fixed to the shafts, rather than to the chariot itself. This evolution more or less ended around the 8th and 9th centuries AD. The shoulder collar, fixed to the vehicle by traces, the swingle-tree, and the horse shoe were later imports of the 15th century, so China was well behind the West in this field.

===== PAGE 405 =====

388
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Dates quoted for the water mill do not match exactly. We have mentioned one mill builder who would have lived around the 4th century BC. But on the other hand, the first reliable evidence of a water mill dates in the year 30 BC which coincides exactly with its appearance in the Western basin of the Mediterranean (fig 1). A text by Du Shi, prefect of Nanyang, writes that in the year 31 AD water mills were used to power the bellows in metallurgic furnaces. We have a 14th century image of an iron tilt hammer (the oldest Western example of this instrument dates from the end of the 12th century).
Windmills were used for irrigation and drainage in Asia and China in around the 10th century (the 7th or 8th century in Iran).
Any developed system of mechanization required not only something to produce energy, but also basic mechanisms to transmit or reduce and transform movements. In this domain, China seems to have been greatly outdistanced by Western Europe and we will return to this fact later. Unfortunately documentation is very poor for this subject. The school of Alexandria with its already advanced theories of camshafts and cogwheels, most certainly outshone Chinese tech niques. It has been suggested that cogwheels were known during the
Han dynasty, in other words in the period of four centuries before the Christian era, but texts regarding this are not really very precise. China does not appear either to have developed the connecting rod and crank system.
Preparation of Raw Materials Once again we lack precise information regarding this subject. We should point out, however, the dates at which new techniques were introduced and what improvements were made to them. The Chinese appear to have been the initiators of several of these techniques in this field, notably in the domain of metallurgy.
Below are the most important dates in metallurgy:Copper was introduced in approximately 3500 BC (circa 5000 BC in Turkey, Syria,
Iraq and Iran).Bronze circa 1400 BC (the third millennium BC in Turkey, Greece and the Balkans).Iron in the 6th century BC (15th century BC in Anatolia).
It is quite obvious, as we have already indicated, that technical principles were developed on the continent in a region which lay approximately in the oriental basin of the Mediterranean, in the Near

===== PAGE 406 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
Figure 1 . - Waterwheel. (From J. Needham, Science and Civilisation in China, Cambridge University Press, 1 954- 1 967)
389

===== PAGE 407 =====

390
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
East, from which these techniques could spread both to the east and to the west. Due to the absence of Carbon 14 dating, the exact date of
Neolithic beginnings remains very inaccurate: perhaps the third mil lennium BC?
It was in Northern China that bronze first appeared, which was a higly developed metallurgy from the beginning. Chinese bronze casters were extremely skilled, as has been stated in many works which have been handed down to us. Over the years the proportion of copper used was replaced in favour of tin. In Southern China and in the bordering countries, bronze was only developed in the first millennium BC. The spread of metallurgic techniques was thus particularly slow.
The introduction of iron was also relatively late in comparison to Near Eastern civilizations. The imperial code of the T'sin state is reproduced on an iron cooking pot in 513 BC. The most remarkable phenomenon is the appearance of cast iron, which some authorities may have exaggerated by dating its appearance in the 4th century BC.
Cast iron could only have been made by using relatively powerful bellows, and was only produced in Western Europe with the aid of hydraulic bellows. In fact, the latter were only developed in China at the beginning of the Christian era. Contemporary historians only use vague and often absurd terms, and we only possess a few objects made of cast iron which are difficult to date. The oldest cast iron pagoda in the province of Hubei dates from 1061 AD. During the period when kingdoms were at war, in other words from the 5th to the 3rd centuries
BC, iron moulds were used to cast hoes and axes. It is difficult to believe that the metal cast could have been iron, as certain authors have maintained. Should one attribute the discovery of cast iron to the use of coal with a higher calorific value than that of charcoal? But coke would also have had to had been discovered for this process. And should the discovery of cylinders and pistons be attributed to the development of the bellows? The mystery remains virtually impene trable (fig. 2) .
The problem of the introduction of steel is just as difficult to resolve.
It is possible that steel was produced by "cofusion,'' according to a method which Reaumur clearly defined, despite the absence of precise scientific knowledge in the beginning of the 18th century. The process mainly involved the "cofusion" of iron and cast iron. The art of welding soft steels was probably known during the 3rd century AD, and would

===== PAGE 408 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
391 Figure 2. - Metallurgical blowing-engine worked by water-power. (From J. Needham) have lead to the development of damask steel used in Japanese sabres in the 11th century.
Other metals quickly followed suit; antimony at the end of the 3rd century AD, zinc at the beginning of the Christian era, and brass around the 5th century AD. White copper, which appears to have been nickel silver, was mentioned by a 4th century author. Lead by products were quickly used for make-up. Some authorities have accepted the hypothesis that this by-product was used in Rome at almost the same date, causing lead poisoning which might have contributed to the demographic decline of the Roman Empire.
Fire-based arts certainly made progress possible in metallurgic techniques. Glass has been found on prehistoric sites in China, Japan and Korea. Confusion and the absence of exact details often makes us doubt suggested theories and the reality of the facts cited. It may be that during much later periods, glass was imported from the West.
Glasses, the existence of which is attested by a 14th century text, had an Arabic name indicating their origin.

===== PAGE 409 =====

392
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
A strict chronology of Chinese pottery is required. There is no doubt that ceramics were developed during the Neolithic period, but at exactly which epoch did this happen? What form, moreover, did the evolution of this technique take? Slender glazed vases have been attributed to the 15th century BC. During the Zhou dynasty, of the first half of the first millennnium BC, the Chinese used powerful kilns by which they could obtain stoneware with an overall glazed paste.
Under the Han dynasty, potters could obtain a temperature of 1300reore C. Proto-porcelains were developed before 500 AD with the introduction of jade-green ceramics. This technique was very rapidly diversified, and real porcelain appeared in the form of almost tran slucent glazed stoneware, requiring temperatures of 1450°C. Ceramics reached their peak with the Ming vases during the period of the same name at the end of the 14th century.
The technique of lacquering, using the milky sap from Rhus vernici fera, would have come from Central and Southern Asia, during the
Chang dynasty. This quickly became a highly developed technique. In fact the scum or lather of the lacquer was used, often coloured with mineral dyes. This medium required a long preparation before it could be applied. For obvious reasons this technique was limited to the Far East.
Paper was undoubtedly one of the major discoveries of China. This fact is unquestionable despite all the obscurities which surround this invention. From the 3rd century BC, paper was produced on a small scale in Asia, using extremely diversified materials. Paper made from the sap of the mulbery tree dates from the 2nd century BC. Silk, bamboo, flax, straw or corn were employed with equal success. This method of paper making survived into the Middle Ages, owing to
Chinese workers who were taken prisonner by the Irano-Arabs during the battle of Talas (751).
We are less certain whether China should be credited with the invention of gunpowder. According to some authorities, China appears to have developed a mixture similar to gunpowder from the 1st century BC, but it was only much later that it was used in military arts.
Other sources are more precise and date the earliest use of gunpowder in the year AD 85. The Chinese were most probably the first to use sulphur, and colour the fire it produced with the aid of metal oxides, thus creating the first fireworks. We even know the formula they used:
75.7% of nitre, 14.4% of coal, and 9.9% of sulphur. It is unlikely that they used it for "fire-arms. "

===== PAGE 410 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
393 Other preparations were more specific. The sugar refining process most certainly came from the West, around the end of the 6th or the beginning of the 7th century. Every civilization, at some point in their history, developed a method of alcoholic fermentation. Cereal fer mentation was practised in China from the Zhou dynasty onwards.
Treatises written in the 11th and 12th centuries provide ample infor mation on these techniques which differ very little from those of
Western Europe. Many forms of alcohol were based on cereals (millet and rice). Wine made from grapes was only developed a short time before the beginning of the Christian era.
The Crafts China naturally practised all the crafts which may be observed in other civilizations, some with great perfection which still compel our admir ation today.
The textile industry took off in a big way. Of the textiles used, wool was never very important apart from in the north west of the country.
Felt was introduced in Central Asia and it would have arrived in China in the 4th century BC. Cotton only gradually spread towards the East from its region of origin, but the Chinese first bought it from India or
Java in the 4th century AD, and from Turkistan at the beginning of the 8th century AD. From the latter came hemp and flax. As we have seen the arrival of kapok was much later.
Silk, which established the fame of the Chinese textile industry, was obtained from the Eurasian region, and from either wild or domesti cated lepidopterans. The Chinese may have been the first to make silk material as they possessed the best silk producing insect, the Bombyx
Mori. We have examples of silk material dating from the Jin dynasty.
Hellenistic Greece particularly appreciated these Chinese products and tried to implant mulberry trees and silk worms in the Mediter ranean basin (most probably these imports came from the oases of
Central Asia).
We know very little about spinning techniques (fig. 3). The spinning wheel, which in all likelihood was powered by hand, was known to the
Chinese at the beginning of the Christian era. The first pictorial representation available dates from 1210. Similarly, a machine for unwinding silk existed in 1090. The cocoons were placed in a bath of warm water, the silk came out through small guiding rings and was

===== PAGE 411 =====

394
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
then wound smoothly onto a large bobin by a backwards and forwards motion (fig. 4).
We know more about the types of weaving loom used. The Book of agriculture and weaving, compiled in around 1210, provides images and descriptions of these, particularly for silk weaving. The superiority of Chinese weaving looms has already been mentioned. Two types of loom were used which left the weaver's arms free to work: the traction loom which may have originated in China and Europe, and the pedal loom which is attributed to the Chinese. Representations of these appear on 12th and 13th century manuscripts.
Upon close examination, these looms display many similarities to European looms of the same period. It has been said that damask silk cloth existed from the time of the Jin dynasty, and during the 2nd century BC, four heddle looms were built, which henceforth enabled the weaving of silk brocade. Egyptian cloth, which was also very high in quality, should be compared to Byzantine cloth which stemmed from Egyptian techniques, and strict chronologies could be proposed in order to determine the extent of some of the contributions made:
these contributions may have been reciprocal.
Printing was one of the major discoveries attributed to the Far East.
It seems very difficult to accept, as some have done, the theory that printing was derived from the seal, an object which was long known by other civilizations which did not develop printing. We should also try to establish a strict order of the stages contributing to the development of this process, which is illustrated by texts and objects.
X ylography was introduced from 770 AD onwards for the reproduc tion of Buddhist texts on paper rolls. In 1907, Sir Aurel Stein discovered the Diamond Stttra, printed in 868 on a paper scroll, in the caves of Tan-Huang. This must therefore be the first example of a printed "book." Some recent authors, however, deny that xylography could have been an ancestor of typography. Moreover, raised stamps, used for the capital letters of manuscripts, had long been known in the
West.
Whatever the cause, popular Buddhist works spread to the west and the east from the 9th century onwards. Was there an industrial stage for the production of relatively short texts rather than images? This question should be clarified. The translation in 982 of the Tripitaka, a
Buddhist canon, has been cited, which would have required 30,000 plates.

===== PAGE 412 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
395 Figure 3. - Silk spinning machine powered by a waterwheel (131 3). (From J. Needham)

===== PAGE 413 =====

396
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 4. - Machine for unwinding silk (I 090). (From J. Needham)

===== PAGE 414 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
397 The invention of typography has been attributed to Pi Cheng (1041-1048) who must have therefore imagined the first mobile cha racters, which were sculpted separately out of viscous clay, hardened in fire and then assembled with a medium composed of a mixture of resin, wax and paper ash. As a matter of fact, this material does not seem to have been very strong. Another question arises to which there seems to be no answer: was there a press? Pi Cheng's invention does not appear to have had any repercussions as it was so quickly for gotten.
Typography was therefore re-invented and improved by Wang Tchen, magistrate of Tsing-tu and author of a classical treatise on agriculture. In this work he relates the invention and perfection of typography. He found the handling of xylographes tedious, so he engraved the characters on mobile blocks, and facilitated their manipulation by classifying them in files, arranged on a surface which revolved on a vertical axis. The characters seem to have been made in wood, but we know nothing about the presses or the inks used. To make an actual impression, the characters were merely rubbed on the surface of the paper. As the wood would have split under the weight and action of the press, the main benefit of this form of printing lay in the fact that characters could be re-used for a greater length of time.
Metal characters (lead or copper) have been attributed to Korea, and their introduction is precisely dated for the year 1403. Korean characters would have arrived in China at the end of the 15th century, but they only enjoyed a modest success as Chinese paper was very fine and could not take metal characwrs, particularly if both sides of the paper were printed. The problem of ink was equally important: in
1398 Chen Ki-souen wrote a treatise on this subject. The inks which were employed were therefore unsuitable for use with metal cha racters.
In fact, true printing was really invented in Europe around the middle of the 15th century. It consisted of metal type characters made from a special alloy, a method for assembling these characters, a suitable ink, and a press. There seems to have been no influence whatsoever from the Far East on the inventions attributed to Guten berg. Only the printing techniques of China and Korea, which were only to be fully developed shortly before the middle of the 15th century, remain to be defined.

===== PAGE 415 =====

398
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Geography For much of its history, China was a unified country, and the Chinese Empire subsequently represented a geographical territory which only the Late Roman Empire rivalled in size. And that is naturally without taking into consideration outside exchanges. All large territories require many specialized and coherent techniques.
We have already discussed the problems of harnessing on the subject of land transports. The wheel was a Western invention.
Vehicles were two-wheeled, and China did not use, at least up until very recently, the mobile front axle unit. Porterage was certainly very widespread, as was also the case in Western Europe during the Middle
Ages. The wheel was as old an invention in China as in the West. But like the West, a logical road network could only result from a centra lized administration. Standardized, imperial roads date from the Han dynasty. Although the Chinese road may have been a little earlier than the Roman road, which was created during the expansion of the
Roman Empire, it was nevertheless very similar to its Latin count erpart, in that it was built as straight as possible, with organized stopping places and was a result of administrative and military con cerns. In fact, it is especially this sort of road which may be seen in
North China.
Certain aspects of the Chinese river network often led to highly original solutions. The flooding of large rivers and the abundance of swamps necessitated a range and scale of work which Mediterranean countries were not to develop. This was particularly so in the basin of the Yellow River where dykes and diversions were necessary in order to avoid disasters. From the end of the 4th century BC, Si Men-po ordered that twelve canals be dug the length of the Chang River, which flowed from the Yell ow river, in order to reduce the effects of low water. The same was executed at the end of the 3rd century AD, for the Min River, flowing from the Blue River. This work also provided irrigation for important zones. It was also during the 4th century BC that canalizations united the Blue River with the Hai River, the beginning of which was to become the Great Canal under the Souei dynasty. The Great Canal network, systematization of various works undertaken during most periods, was to be completed in the 13th and
14th centuries.
Bridge building in China appears to have been fairly successful at a relatively early age. Wooden bridges, and flexible rattan or bamboo

===== PAGE 416 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
399 suspension bridges were very old inventions. During the 7th century, bridges with segmented arches and bridges suspended by iron chains
Were built. Some authorities maintain that the latter type were devel oped in the 11th century. Stone bridges also existed. The bridge at Lou kou kiao (end of the 12th century) was 350 paces long, 18 wide and had
11 arches.
We know relatively little about boat building in Ancient China (fig.
5). In fact, techniques differed greatly from north to south, and from the rivers to the sea. There were various types of sampans and junks and these were continually modified (fig. 6). The large sea-going junk is thought to have existed in the 9th century; if not at this date, then it was certainly in use in the 11th century. The Chinese fleet continued to develop, but it is difficult to trace the progression of these changes. The few images which are available tend to prove that this evolution was virtually identical to that of Western ships. The stern rudder only seems to have been developed by the Chinese well after it had been
Figure 5. - Three mast junk (engraving from 1 757). (From J. Needham)

===== PAGE 417 =====

400
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS

===== PAGE 418 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
401 discovered in Western Europe, although an example of this rudder type has been cited for the 1st century BC.
Let us remind ourselves of the kite which was, however, produced by many civilizations at completely different epochs.
Very little has been written on Chinese towns and cities, or on fortifications which were probably built very quickly around the towns.
Only the story of the Great Wall is known, which cannot be compared to the Roman limes. In approximately 220 BC, old discontinued works were linked together to form a rampart of earth, about nine metres high, paved on either side with stones or bricks, and watchtowers were built at regular intervals along the length of the wall.
Blockage We have arrived at the main topic of this discussion, which may be summarized under two headings; the advance of Chinese techniques over Western methods, and the blockage of the Chinese technical system.
We indicated our position with regard to the first point at the beginning of this chapter. We still lack any exact chronological order of the appearance of Chinese techniques. We have been able to note concordances in many fields between West European and Chinese technical developments, a phenomenon which may be explained by their concomitant evolutions. Perhaps the practice of borrowing
Western techniques by China has not been emphasized enough.
Metallurgy, glass making and ceramics all originated from Western techniques, whereupon improvements were made within each techni cal zone. If we look at dates common to both China and Europe, it becomes evident that certain techniques were more advanced in one country, and that reciprocal influences within the other were defini tively less widespread than we might have thought. It is more difficult to accept certain opinions in other domains, a phenomenon which we will illustrate with just two examples.
Taking as first problem the measurement of time and distances, it would seem that the West was not behind the East (fig. 7). The water-clock was developed in both areas at almost the same time, although it may possibly have appeared slightly earlier in the West.
The Greeks of Alexandria had resolved the problem of the unequal length of days, whereas the Chinese had not come to any satisfactory

===== PAGE 419 =====

402
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 7. - Astronomical clock. (From J. Needham) Figure 8. - South-showing carriage. (From J. Needham)

===== PAGE 420 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
403 conclusion. It is highly unlikely that the Chinese developed the mechanical clock which the West had put into practice from the 14th century onwards.
As for the problem of measuring distances, in 1027, Lou Taolong presented the Emperor Jen Tsong with a chariot for measuring distances, while the Greeks of Alexandria had written of a similar method in the 3rd or 2nd century BC.
The second example which is often quoted is the compass. Let us ignore for the moment the "south-pointing carriage" which was a purely mechanical device (fig. 8) . The "south-pointing" needle is mentioned for the first time in the 11th century, by Chen Koua. There is certainly much evidence which testifies to a knowledge of the existence of the magnet from the 3rd century BC onwards. But the problem of the Earth's magnetism emerged much later. The use of the compass in navigation techniques dates from the 12th century BC. It remains necessary to find out the exact use of the compass for a type of navigation which was on the whole coastal. Pierre de Maricourt was one of the first in the West to study the effects of magnetism, at virtually the same time as the Chinese began to question its uses.
From this point of view, it is important that specialized scientists studying these different questions unite, in order to establish an assessement which the lone researcher has great difficulty in achieving.
In this case, the work to be undertaken notably exceeds the simple problem of priorities: in a word, it is a matter of establishing how certain techniques were spread abroad, and on the other hand, if virtually chronologically concomitant parallel developments were pos sible.
The second problem is that of a blockage of technical progress. We have seen that this was not peculiar to China alone at the end of the
Medieval period, but was also experienced by the Eygptians, the Greeks, and many other civilizations. There are several sides to this question. Blockages may have arisen from internal factors, in other words they were produced by purely technical problems. This is conceivable only insofar as global technical systems are envisaged, as traditional technical structures may block changes in the system even if advance is visible in other sectors. Equally, the coherence of a technical system with other economic, social, and scientific systems must be taken into account. The problem must therefore be con sidered on a large scale, as indeed it has been by many specialists.

===== PAGE 421 =====

404
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
In fact, the reasons for this blockage have been sought in the domains of intellectual pursuit and society, in the same way as the blockage of the Greek technical system. If we say that the great inventions, those which marked China's advance over the West, such as printing, gunpowder, and the compass, only had a slight repercus sion in the Chinese world, this manifests exactly the phenomenon we have just pointed out. Techniques which are today called the "leading" or the "latest technology" can only be incorporated into a global technical system if a certain coherence exists between those tech niques. The delay or slow progress of other technical structures will naturally block the introduction of more advanced inventions. In the
West, printing resulted from a prodigious intellectual movement, the compass only became an important instrument with the development of navigation techniques and the crossing of the Atlantic, and gun powder was introduced with the development of a sizeable artillery, which presupposes hundreds of other techniques (besides, the metal cannon was a virtually contemporary development in the Far East and the West, at the end of the 13th and the beginning of the 14th century; fig. 9).
More pertinent reasons are required to explain the blockage of Chinese techniques while the West, on the contrary, was initiating a new technical revolution at almost the same period. Apart from reasons specific to technology itself, other circumstances must be considered.
Techniques always apply, in one way or another, to science. Need ham has ably emphasized the stagnation of Chinese scientific thought during a time when the West was in the process of a scientific revolution which was to eventually culminate in the development of modern science. The product of both technicians and scientists, this modern science was to provide technology with new and irreplaceable elements. Or, to put it a better way, the union between technology and science was to provide the two domains, the scientific and the techno logical, with tremendous and enriching contributions. Nothing of this kind happened in China, where to some extent science gets bogged down in out-moded traditionalism. Here we must stress the blockage of thought, the significance of which is obvious.
The determining role, in every respect, of neo-Confucianism has been pointed out. The appearance of an ethic capable of resolving all nature of problems, scientific and technical, as well as economic,

===== PAGE 422 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
405 Figure 9. - Principle of Chinese crossbow, drawn to artillery's scale (1044). (The greek mechanics of Alexandria were more advanced.) (From J. Needham)

===== PAGE 423 =====

406
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
political and social questions, certainly contributed to the stagnation of the Chinese technical system. This agrees in part with what we have just stated.
The birth of a bureaucratic feudalism, which was rural in character, has been put forward as an explanation, and no doubt with good reason. Technical progress necessarily entails social upheavals. A powerful administration and a fairly rigid society are fundamentally hostile in such circumstances. These explanations may be found within societies which are basically different from each other, such as those of
South America and the Muslim world.
Finally, other reasons of a completely different nature may explain technical blockage. Chinese techniques indisputably profited from outside contributions. From the day when China was more or less cut off from the outside world, its development could only cease, as a technical spirit was no longer available either. The surge in Western technology, which began to gather momentum at the end of the second half of the 15th century, nourished itself, something China could no longer do. Lastly, "the absence of appropriate verbal equipment" has been indicated. This obstacle to the development of logical thought, which was undoubtedly also typical of Egypt, is certainly an important factor.
Needham is without doubt correct when he suggests that the West benefited from a capitalist, manufacturing, and mercantile economy, thus contradicting all the other previously accepted theories. It was really this type of economic structure which appeared, after many beginnings, during the 15th century, and enabled technical progress in the West which the Chinese civilization could never enjoy.
In fact, for the most part, these proposed explanations are not wholly supported. Authorities do not agree on the existence of some of the phenomena, and this naturally results in a sort of confused catalogue, some elements of which are often contradictory. Every author would like to find his reason or reasons to account for technical blockage. Needham, studying the absence of technological innovation in China from a certain period onwards, concludes in another text:
Tradesmen were always kept in their place and were not permitted to hold responsible positions within the State. Obviously there were guilds, but these were not as important as those in Europe. Perhaps here we can put our finger on the main reason to explain why the Chinese civilization could not create a modern technology, as it is universally accepted that the development of technology in Europe was closely linked to the growth in the power of the merchant class.

===== PAGE 424 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
407 Rostow criticizes this view, and would seem to be quite justified in doing so. He maintains that the merchant class in China was more powerful and less despised than has been previously thought. In the last analysis, guilds were hardly, if at all, inclined towards technical innovation.
Mark Elvin rejects other explanations, such as inadequate capital, limited markets, political obstacles, and the incapacity of the Chinese to create large scale and long term enterprise. He suggests that in fact the Chinese had arrived at a stage where sudden and significant progress had to be made. He also adds to the above the factors of the low income per head caused by demographic pressure, the increased cost of raw materials, and the lack of internal cotton resources. But here again, Rostow objects to this view on the grounds that this impasse was also true of the West, and stresses "the scientific, philo sophical, inventive and innovative ferment which was distinctive of
Europe during the same period". And he quotes Elvin:
Almost every element usually regarded by historians as a major contributory cause to the industrial revolution in north-western Europe was also present in China ( . . . ) Only the Galilean-Newtonian science was missing; but in the short run this was not important.
Had the Chinese possessed, or developed, the 17th European mania for tinkering and improving, they could easily have made an efficient spinning machine out of the primitive model described by Wang Chen. A steam engine would have been more difficult; but it should not have posed insuperable difficulties to a people who had been building double-acting piston flame-throwers in the Sung dynasty. The crucial point is that nobody tried. In most fields, agriculture being the chief exception, Chinese technology stopped progressing well before the point at which a lack of basic scientific knowledge had become a serious obstacle.
Rostow insists on the absence of a "scientific revolution" to explain this technical blockage.
The debate remains open to discussion.
PRE-COLUMBIAN
AMERICAN TECHNIQUES
The civilizations of pre-Columbian America present, more than any other, the image of a technical blockage at a fairly primitive level, which is astonishing to we Europeans. The existence today of an

===== PAGE 425 =====

408
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
overdeveloped America, the recollection of a frequently marvellous art, and an often incredible form of architecture, have all helped to conceal some aspects of the problem. We are less surprized that some
African, Melanesian, and Oceanian civilizations have remained at a more primitive stage. It would therefore seem illogical to conclude that more advanced pre-Columbian techniques were suddenly halted in their development. Many explanations for this have been given, none of which are really adequate, such as the isolation of one area in relation to other continents (Europe, Asia, and Africa which commu nicate easily between each other), the division of internal civilizations, and societies which are too rigidly structured. All, or virtually all, these explanations have been put forward. We are forced to fall back on rather vague formulae, which in truth only shift the emphasis of the problem: "the American man was not a technician by birth ( . . . )
Inventive stagnation, coupled with technical inertia which did not exclude manual skill." We are undoubtedly obsessed by the idea of natural, logical and inescapable technical progress. From this obsession stems our aston ishment at certain ignorances: the explanation, such at it is, therefore obliges us to look for exogeneous causes. We have already seen this in the techniques of Classical Greece. We must also add the many difficulties faced by the historian in the consideration of pre
Columbian techniques. Most peoples of pre-Columbian America did not have any system of writing, which is obviously an important technique for communication: we have no written evidence of the form it might have taken, and the only indications which we do possess date after the conquest, resulting either from natives but more often from conquerors who were careful to record all they had seen or noticed.
Technical material made, as we will see, almost totally from organic material has almost completely disappeared. And this lack of detailed and comprehensive documentation relates equally to the whole technical environment, in particular to the demography and the economy, as well as science, which are so closely linked to technology.
All this subsequently leaves the field open to often unwarranted and excessive suppositions.
The main point which should be remembered, and contemporary historians have all agreed on this, is constituted by a certain number of
"missing" techniques essential for the development of advanced technical systems. This is to some extent the key of the blockage.

===== PAGE 426 =====

BLOCKED SYS1EMS
409 Apart from writing, which we have just mentioned, there were three large technical sectors which did not evolve in any way for some time.
I - It is not strictly true to say that pre-Columbian America did not domesticate animals. Some domestication did take place in various regions and for different purposes. The Aztecs domesticated both the turkey and the dog for food, and the Incas domesticated several types of camelidae, such as the alpaca and the vicuna, which provided the textile industry with wool from its thick fleece. It is true that the llama and the guanaco were poor carriers (approximately 30 kilogrammes each), but over short distances they could serve as beasts of burden.
All these animals also provided meat, their skins could be used to make sandals, straps, and bags, and their bones were used to make needles and various other tools. Finally, their excrement was collected and used for fuel.
It is nevertheless essential to note that in many regions the absence of animal domestication led to important consequences. From the standpoint of providing food, there was no meat, milk or animal fats, and from the industrial standpoint, there was no leather, skins, or wool. It was probably in the domain of energy that these repercussions were the most serious, as there could be no porterage or traction, either for transport, agriculture, or power to drive grindstones or mill wheels, and of course no manure for cultivation.
Some questions must be asked which are rather awkward to answer.
The domestication of animals, and more widely animal rearing, required a certain number of necessary conditions. The first requirement was the existence of animals which could be domesti cated, and we know that America possessed neither the horse nor cattle, which were the most useful and valuable animals in the civili zations of the Ancient World. It is equally important to have the necessary food available to feed the animals reared, and again we know of the difficulty which Europe found with regard to this, even in regions which were relatively rich, whereas the great civilizations of
America only possessed extremely poor pastures. A balance was therefore necessary, to be achieved at a later date, for all animal rearing peoples, of human and animal food-producing cultures, as an agro-pastoral equilibrium was difficult to establish in areas where agriculture was not as yet very advanced. All this presupposes suitable civilizations and societies. A conjunction of a number of vital elements, one of which was insufficient alone, was therefore required.

===== PAGE 427 =====

410
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The available docu!lentation is undoubtedly too limited to provide the solutions to these questions.
II - The problem of metals is virtually the same. Broadly speaking, the Americans before Christopher Columbus hardly used all the metals available in their original form: gold, silver and copper. Lead had been used on a small scale, and bronze had probably been discovered by accident. The greatest handicap to these American civilizations was the absence of iron: meteoric iron was only used by the Eskimos. On the whole, historians are rather confused on this subject, as although pre-Columbian Americans understood the proce dures and tools need for the fusion of minerals, and we will return to this later, they were certainly far less skilled in the more complicated operation required for reducing them - complicated, that is, to such an extent that no one can clearly, or even vaguely, explain how reduction metallurgy was developed in the Old World. At the beginning they probably used native deposits as was the case in America, but there was no iron, apart from meteoric iron. It is absolutely impossible to determine the cause or causes of the following state of affairs: in some regions men discovered the method of reducing minerals, and in others they did not.
In certain areas of America, metallurgy had been particularly advanced for the metals we have just mentioned. Metallurgy was most probably developed somewhat later in the area between Panama and the Equator, present day Columbia, and we will return to this aspect of the question further on. In North America, there were large deposits of native copper. In Mexico, metallurgy was similarly fairly advanced.
The major deficiency, therefore, was that of iron. Neither copper nor bronze, and as one might expect gold or silver, were hard enough in quality for the fabrication of tools. This absence of a hard metal is well marked by the long persistence of stone tools, even though copper and bronze had been developed between the 8th and the 10th centuries
AD. The isolation, or relative isolation, of America during this period of the Western Middle Ages, may explain on the one hand, the lack of importations from far-off countries, which had existed in Europe and
Asia, and on the other, the transference of technology. It is obvious that in many domains, the ignorance of iron making was one of the main reasons for the blockage of a large number of techniques: there is little point in going into detail on this subject as the great drawback of this "lack" may easily be appreciated. It would therefore be useful to

===== PAGE 428 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
411 make a list of fire-based techniques, in order to understand better the blockage of American civilizations.
III - A third fact, still more curious and more difficult to explain, remains to be discussed, which has been attempted, so far unsucces sfully by many authors. Let us make it quite clear, however, that we do not intend to give either here, or further on, the definitive explanation, but to consider the elements of the problem. Before the 8th century, the children of Veracruz in Mexico played with toy dogs, made out of wood and mounted on four wheels which pivoted on two axles.
Despite that, the wheel was never used in the civilizations of pre Columbian America. Important consequences of this may be immediately observed: neither the pulley, trestle or lifting apparatus, nor the lathe or any type of land vehicle, or for that matter anything which could result from the wheel, could be developed, and subse quently the screw, flywheel, gears, and the reduction or exploitation of hydraulic or wind energy could not be initiated.
We should certainly not be mistaken on this account. The West developed the wheel much later in certain sectors, yet it is undeniably true that at the moment of conquest, Europe had developed virtually every possible use of wheel and consequently every form of continuous circular movement. We must emphasize that it is quite wrong to suppose the natural use of a given technique, and consequently to be surprised at its late introduction, or as the case is here, at its complete absence in some civilizations.
Let us summarize briefly what we have been able to observe in the development of techniques in the oldest civilizations of the Old World.
The most primitive use of the wheel was undoubtedly in land trans port. It was only after this development that the wheel could be employed for other jobs: the pulley and trestle, the potter's wheel, etc.
One might almost say that it was thanks to land transports, to the vehicle's wheels, that the East could become aware of the possibilities offered by the wheel, or more broadly, by circular movement. So what happens in America? Land transport naturally presupposes the devel opment of other techniques, particularly those of animal domesti cation (as vehicles pulled by humans could only have been of limited use), and the development of certain metallurgies used to reinforce the most exposed parts of the vehicle (axles, wheel hoops, etc.). America did not know of these associated techniques which gave birth to the development of the four-wheeled vehicle. The transition from the toy

===== PAGE 429 =====

412
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
to the land vehicle was not as simple as some authorities would have us think. Once again, the idea of a technical system increases in value. We will not cite, as some have done, the wheel barrow, which the West only developed much later, probably in the 8th century. Thus it seems we may better appreciate, if not fully comprehend, the astonishing absence on first sight of the wheel in the civilizations under discussion here.
The conjunction of these three important "lacks" must necessarily limit the technological development of the pre-Columbian civilizations of America, and moreover prevent some techniques, particularly those involving the arts, from reaching a very high level. The absence of certain techniques blocked to some extent those techniques estab lished at a relatively low level.
In the domain of agriculture, the distinction should be made bet ween soil development, cultural techniques and the species cultivated.
Soil development was certainly very limited. In some regions, in particular the Andes, there was systematic irrigation, with often highly developed canals - that of Chicoma was 120 kilometre long -, reser voirs, and certainly a whole organization which supposes the devel opment of complete networks. On the other hand, there appear to have been no drainage systems, but crops were frequently grown on terraces with dry stone walls. Clearings were made by burning and felling trees, and the terrain, exhausted after a period of a few years, returned to fallow land. But no progress seems to have been made in crop rotation. This derived partly from cultivation methods, from the plants they cultivated, and especially, the lack of fertilisers. Fish manure was used in Peru, and the Aztecs used human compost. But
American civilizations lacked natural forms of manure, because there was no large-scale animal rearing. Natural fertilisers were almost unknown.
The Americans also had no animal traction, which meant that the instruments used to work the land tended to be very primitive (fig. 10).
The shortage of iron exacerbated this problem, although of course Western civilizations also used very little iron in cultivation. The earth was usually worked with a hoe, made of wood and sometimes with a stone blade. Digging sticks were also used, they were curved, with a foot rest (fig. 11). In the central Andes, clods of earth were broken up using clubs with stone heads. These were also used as weapons.
Generally speaking, there was not a great deal of diversity in agricul-

===== PAGE 430 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
413 Figure 10. - Aztec digging stick. (From M. Dawnas, Histoire generate des techniques, P.U.F., Paris, 1962)
Figure 1 1. - Inca digging stick. (From M. Daumas) Figure 12. - Inca weeding. (From M. Daumas)

===== PAGE 431 =====

414
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
tural tools. Tubers and roots were gathered up with a spade or a hoe (fig. 12), and cereals were usually harvested with stone sickles. The produce was usually stored in jars, baskets and silos.
The pre-Colombian farmers had very limited means at their dispo sal, but nevertheless, they knew how to exploit to the full the plants and crops which they did grow. This was achieved principally through selection and specialisation. The Andean civilizations are supposed to have cultivated more than forty different types of crop, each one corresponding to a particular ecological stage. We cannot list all the plants grown, but the following are some of the most important:
potatoes, maize, haricots, peppers, sweet potatoes, gourds, cassava, peanuts, avocados, cotton, amaranth and sage. Another important crop was lakinoa, a kind of Andean rice which grows in coppices.
Many tubers were cultivated.
There were still many difficulties to overcome in this area, particu larly in the Andes, which has a mixed climate. The potato, for example, was grown on the level immediately below that of the steppe:
this gave a greater yield, but there were several problems because of the variations in climate. Hence, it was important to keep stocks. The tubers, dehydrated by the sun and the frost, remained edible for several years. Maize and rice were grown on lower land, in areas exposed to the sun but sheltered from winds.
Figure 1 3. - Aztec metal smelting. (From M. Daumas)

===== PAGE 432 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
415 Arboriculture was not widely practised: the only two fruit grown were pineapples and pawpaws. We know little about their use of forests, except that, like the West, wood was the material most widely used.
There was food in abundance, then, some of which was very rich.
However, overall it was not very well balanced. There were two major deficiences, in animal proteins and mineral salts.
Very few animals were reared, but dog meat was eaten, particularly in Mexico. Turkey was also popular, especially in America with the
Aztecs, who were to pass this on to the Europeans in the 16th century.
Alpaca, vicuna , llama and guanaco were all reared in the Andes, but this was for their fleece and for traction rather than their meat. They also hunted and fished for their food: game was regularly hunted, as were rabbit, hare, venison, peccaries, and all types of bird. This was despite the restrictions of a somewhat limited range of hunting instru ments. The equipment used for fishing by the Aztecs in Mexico was similarly restricted: they used nets and spears. Sage did contain some oil, but on the whole the food in pre-Columbian America had very little oil or fat. This meant that everything was either grilled, or as was more often the case, boiled. One of their main drinks was beer made from maize.
It seems that the American peoples knew exactly how to exploit the different types of tree growing in that region. But again, the tools they used for this were very crude. Apart from different types of finishing tools, such as knives, awls, scrapers, pincers and polishers, they also used adzes. The blade was made of polished stone, although the
Eskimos used meteoric iron, and in Mexico and Peru, they were made of copper. Sometimes, the same instrument was used for several different operations. It is difficult to see how they managed to make assemblies, because they had no nails, even copper ones. They did use fish glue, however, but this inevitably shortened the life of the con structions. Some assemblies were made by sewing the materials together with the roots from fir trees, in the same way as the Vikings made their ships.
It is difficult to establish the history of pre-Columbian metallurgy.
The metals used were the ones to be found in this region, mainly copper and gold, of which there were large deposits in North America.
This was the position for a very long time, no doubt because they had not conceived of reducing minerals. There were very few mines

===== PAGE 433 =====

416
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
originally, and even when they were forced to open up new ones because the surface deposits had been exhausted, they were fairly shallow: the greatest depth of a mine was seven metres. Real metal lurgy, i.e. , the reducing of minerals, began in the 8th and 10th century in Columbia and southern Peru. We are not sure of the processes of reduction: of the type of furnaces, how they achieved the air flow, how they melted the metals, or if the case arose, how they roasted sulpher ous minerals.
The only metals smelted were those with a low melting point, and this was usually gold. There are some pictures of cooking pots made of fired clay (fig. 13), and figure 14 shows a blowpipe being used to sustain the fire. The use of alloys was quickly established in South America, although this was probably originally by accident, either prompted by the existence of two metals in one deposit, or by mixtures created in founding furnaces. The copper-gold alloy is known to have been used in the Andes from the 6th century, but it was in Columbia from the
10th century that the alloys were systematised. One alloy used more often than most was tumbaga, made of 82% gold, and 18% copper. It was first heated then placed in an acid bath, giving it a surface of gold.
It was then retired and pounded, after which it had the same strength as bronze or soft steel. There were many binary alloys: gold-silver (with
35 to 50% silver); silver-copper (with 20% copper); and copper arsenic (with 5% arsenic). Most of these alloys came from the southern coast of Peru, where a tertiary alloy had been developed, that of gold, silver and copper. The copper-tin alloy, i.e. , bronze, appeared later, in
Tiahuanaco in the 11th and 14th centuries, then in the northern coast of Peru in the 14th and 15th centuries. A small proportion of tin, usually about 12% or less, produced a bronze which could be pounded, and with which tools were usually made. But, because of its low tin content, this same type of bronze was not usually very strong. Bronze with a higher proportion of tin could not be pounded, but it was useful in smelting and casting.
The south-American ethnic groups had certainly achieved a certain amount of expertise in the use of certain metals, although we should make clear that these alloys were usually for artistic purposes. This meant that except for bronze, whose applications were limited, there were not many metals which could be used for technical purposes. One can understand, therefore, why polished cut stone should have been used for so long.

===== PAGE 434 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
417 Figure 14. - Worker using a blowpipe. (From M. Daumas) Metal working remained fairly crude, mainly because of the lack of the necessary tools. The metals, used mainly for making ornaments or jewellery, were usually pounded and beaten into thin sheets or sometimes even leaf. These pieces were then carved, worked in repousse or embossed, and then engraved. Gilding and silvering were invented between the 5th and 11th centuries, by the Mochica civili zation from northern Peru. Heat working was usually done by anneal ing, lashing or welding with the hammer. This could be combined with quenching. But all these operations were unable to produce a metal as hard as iron or steel. Wax casting was the most widely used method.
Different pieces of metal were joined either by riveting or soldering.
The latter process was practiced in the central Andes between the 7th and the 11th centuries. These two different types of metal working, one with hammers, and the other with heat were used in conjunction.
Gilding could be done in three different ways. The colouring was achieved in the way described above for the copper-gold alloy. Alter natively, they covered the mould with a fine gold leaf before filling it with another metal. Plate-gilding was done by engraving the silver or copper object with acid, then coating the engraving with ground gold and mercury. The mercury disappeared in the heating process, while the gold was soldered to the other metal. Plants or siliceous sands were used for polishing.
The remaining momuments built by some of the pre-Columbian peoples are a testament of their expertise at working with stone.
Unfortunately, although the quarries and tools still exist, there are no written sources to tell us about the processes they used. Most of the tools were made of stone, but wood, reeds, bone, leather, and much

===== PAGE 435 =====

418
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
