later, copper, were also used. Miss Chita de la Calle has provided us with this excellent summary:
Stone tools, such as mallets, axes, wedges, scissors, scrapers, piercers and chisels enabled the craftsman to grind, break up, slit, crumble, sharpen, rasp, pierce, ream, engrave or polish. Those made of wood or bone were used to rasp, pierce, or alter by pressure. Reeds, bone and metal were used to perforate stone and split leather. Very hard abrasives were unknown, so the craftsman would use the same stone with which he was working, just as with diamonds. Sand and water were also used as abrasives.
The techniques of flint cutting were the same as those used in the rest of the world. Very hard stones which were difficult to work were used for ornamentation and jewellery, which were difficult to work: the
Aztecs used rock crystal in the 15th century, with emeries and a tool of quenched copper. Amethyst, chalcedony, agate, sard, carnelian, jasper, jade and turquoise were also worked. Jade was cut into plates by means of leather straps with sand and water. Tools made of more unlikely materials, such as bone, leather, reed and copper, were in fact only the supports, either of claystone and the same stone which was being worked, or of silaceous sands. The real tools are the abrasives.
They even went so far as to use ears of corn as gimlets, which in the 13th century were replaced by tubular piercers of copper which provided a regular supply of water and abrasives.
We know very little about how the large slabs of stone for construc tion - used mainly for great monuments - were quarried, worked, and dressed. The interior was made of piled up rubble-stones, or of flat volcanic stone. The coating was made of basalt, granite or sandstone, squared off or polished. No cement was used, but sometimes the rubble-stones were stuck together with mud. In Mexico, they used mortar made of sand and very hard lime.
Not everything was known about fire-using techniques during the pre-Columbian period, but nevertheless, they were widely practised throughout the continent. They were used in ceramics, in spite of the absence of the potter's wheel, which was so popular in other ancient civilizations. Of course this restricted the potters to sculpting.
According to Favre, the paste was usually rolled up then twisted into a coil to make the sides of the desired object. The techniques of moulding were adopted fairly quickly, thereby facilitating production on a larger scale. It was probably invented in the valley of Mexico during the 6th century, in the Maya region during the 8th century, from

===== PAGE 436 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
419 where it was probably passed to the northern coast of Peru (although some believe that there is evidence of moulding from the southern coast of Peru dating back to the beginning of the Christian age).
Moulding made possible the anthropromorphous and zoomorphic pottery whose realism is still so striking today. However, the use of moulding did not wipe out the technique of coiling used for large pieces, particularly jars for containing food. At any event, the abun dance of clay beds all over America greatly stimulated the devel opment of pottery industry.
Plastic clay was used for decoration purposes: cutting was done with a point or a stamp with die made of a hard material, usually fired clay or stone. Most pottery was painted, and archaelogical remains show the remarkable development of polychromy in certain regions, particu larly in the northern coast of Peru. The pottery was fired in a kiln, which was usually open. Wood was piled up around the pieces to be fired, and the fire was kept at a constant heat of about 400° to 600°C for several hours. In some areas, the firing was done in a well, with the hearth at the top. In both cases, however, they used the reduction process by means of the air current. Fired in this way, the crockery was extremely tough, and it assumed either a brown, red or cream shade characteristic of the pottery of certain civilizations. Precolumbian
America never used the horizontal corridor kiln so popular in the Old World. The kilns were not bricked up and tended to be vertical. They worked at a low temperature, and the air flow was fairly low, just enough to keep an oxidized atmosphere. The colouring was usually light as a result of this. It seems, therefore, that American pottery techniques were perfectly autonomous.
Bricks were also used in construction for building many great monuments. Usually, these bricks were simply dried in the sun. In the
Andes, however, all constructions were made of stone.
Assembly techniques tend to relate to those sectors where there is no great need for implements, but in wood assemblies for example, the lack of tools did prevent the creation of any complicated assemblies.
This meant that the elastic properties of wood were fully exploited:
frames were made of poles bent into an arc, and trunks were made of thin planks which were softened by steam so that they could be bent to a right angle. Neither nails or dowels were used, and the planks were sewn together. Basketry was popular, and there were many different types: spiraled basketwork among the Eskimos, spirals of woven

===== PAGE 437 =====

420
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
strands, especially in North America, but also in the Amazon valley, basketwork with twisted ropes, found along the West Coast, basket work with woven strands, which was popular almost everywhere, and diagonal basketwork for mats.
The Americans were very skilful in all textile techniques. It seems that they developed rapidly first in Peru, then spread throughout the continent. The most popular fibre was cotton, grown on the coasts of
South America, and again, especially in Peru. In a few regions, other vegetable fibres were used, notably agave and reed. They soon devel oped the use of animal fibres: rabbit hair, and even human hair, and also wool. The latter came from camelidae, llama and especially alpaca which were reared specifically for this, but also from wild animals, such as guanaco and vicuna. Wool from the latter was particularly fine and manageable, and was easily dyed. We do not know how these various fibres were prepared, but no doubt the techniques were very rudi mentary. Similarly, we know virtually nothing about dyeing tech niques, but the fibres were usually dyed before spinning, with vegeta ble and animal (shells) colourants; mineral dyes were also used: ochre, calcinated lime, chalk and manganese. Alum was used as the mordant.
The craftsmen from the southern coasts of Peru therefore had more than one hundred and ninety colours at their disposal.
The fibres were spun with a spindle, made of wood or thorn, with little ceramic spindle-whorls. The Peruvians used to let the spindle hang freely from the thread, but almost everywhere else they threw the spindle onto a plate or onto the ground. After a while, the spindle would slow down, and stop. It then needed to be wound on once more.
The type of distaff used is unclear, but the Incas of the 16th century used a forked stick to wind on the thread. As might be expected, the
Americans never used the spinning wheel.
The techniques of weaving were similarly crude. Looms with straps were widespread, and are very like the ones depicted on Greek vases
(fig. 15). The American loom was slightly more sophisticated: it had a beam, breastbeam, heddles and shafts, crossing rods, and loom slays with combs. But some elements were never used: the harness, treadles, or the shuttle with spool. The low warp loom was the most commonly used, the high warp loom was relatively rare. Brocade was a natural outcome of the invention of the warp. From the 7th century, tapestry, embroidery and gingham were all practised. Tapestry making was at its height from the 11th to the 12th centuries, with more

===== PAGE 438 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
421 Figure 1 5. - Inca weaving loom. (From M. Daumas) complicated fabrics, double cloths, braid, dyed cloth and velvet.
Knitting was also practised during this period; and it appears that this technique was unknown in Western medieval civilizations. After this, the most developed techniques tended to decline, in particular tapes try and embroidery, but also knitting and brocade. Gauze was used by the Mexicans. Textile production in Peru was a large industry, largely due to very elaborate techniques organised on a wide scale by the
"monasteries of the Sun." In the field of architecture, we know more about urban techniques than anything else. Of course there was a non-urban type of archi tecture, with several different styles, ranging from the tent to half buried houses with clay and wooden walls, and houses made of dried bricks and with thatched roofs. There were even some storeyed houses, with stairs of clay bricks. All these buildings had common characteristics: they had only a single room, no windows or floors, and they were ventilated through the roof or simply from above.
Architecture in the towns was certainly more sophisticated, but the lack of tools which were still at the neolithic stage meant that the development of techniques was particularly slow. Wood was used for the frame, lintel and roof, and stone for the fittings after which it was drafted. Pebbles and rubble-stones mixed with mud for the filling-in

===== PAGE 439 =====

422
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
were also used, however. We have already mentioned that they used clay bricks; real bricks were very rare but there is evidence of these in the ancient Maya empire between the 4th and 10th century, and in the
Mexico valley, from the 9th to the 12th century. Lime, mixed with sand and loose chippings, provided them with a particularly tough mortar.
Again, a distinction should be made between ordinary dwellings - less well conserved and therefore less well known - and the large public buildings, fortifications, palaces, temples and especially the pyramids, which were all built using more sophisticated techniques. It is invaria bly this type of construction which interests historians.
The foundations were rudimentary: terracing for buildings on mountains, and platforms for those on level land. The walls followed the natural angle of the mountain or hill slope and therefore retained the rubble stone on the inside. The drafting was still fairly crude: this was because the walls were rarely left plain, but were generally faced with stucco, usually polychrome or with mosaics. There were few openings onto the exterior, and ventilation was normally provided by small holes in the partitions and at the top of the walls. The doors usually consisted of a lintel and inclined posts open at the bottom.
They could not be closed, but there was usually a curtain or matting.
Roofs were mostly terraced on a frame which rested on the actual walls, and which was covered by shingle, lime and mortar. They were probably supported by columns or pillars. From the 4th century, the
Mayas were building false corbelled vaults, as well as arches. This technique also enabled them to build aquaducts, drains and triumphal arches. It is worth remembering here the slow development of the vault in Europe. The tools they possessed for this were much more sophisticated, and enabled them to cut with much more precision into harder stone which could withstand the pressures created by the vault.
The pilaster was used in the Mexico valley before the 10th century, at which time serpentine pillars could also be found. The stone column was a tolteque invention (10th-12th century): it was made of several assembled pieces, adjoined by mortice and tenon joints.
Most public buildings, palaces or temples had only one storey, but in some regions they had two, and sometimes even four, with recessed fronts.
It should be clear by now that the pyramids are not necessarily proof of an advanced technology. In Egypt the pyramids date from a time when techniques were still fairly crude and the stones were probably

===== PAGE 440 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
423 still being hoisted on inclined planes. The civilizations which built pyramids did not use the wheel, but whether of not they used rollers is uncertain.
The pre-Columbian Americans also built fortifications. The fortres ses of Sacsahuaman in Cuzco and of Ollantaytambo, in the valley of
Urumbamba, are both good examples of the type of military construc tion built by the Incas. The fortress of Ollantaytambo is composed of two high walls, which, like the triple remparts of the former, are made of cyclopean bonded megaliths. These buildings were fixed to the side of a natural hill dominated by an enormous tower. On the other hand, their armaments were relatively primitive. Their round shields made of wood or reeds were covered with feathers, and the double-edged swords had obsidian blades. Bows and other types of missile projectors were used, with pointed arrows of stone as well as javelins. They wore wooden helmets and their armour was a simple tunic padded with cotton.
Urbanism had developed under the pre-Columbian civilizations, and there were some large cities. The Incas and the Aztecs were both expert at such building: the towns were usually carefully planned, according to a quadrangular arrangement with a square in the centre, and roads coming off it at right angles. The lie of the land was particularly awkward in the city of Mexico, so embankments were used which enabled them to build a large town with wide straight roads and canals which carried most of the traffic. These canals had bridges made of large beams. The surface of the roads was made of beaten down earth. The only inconvenience of this arrrangement was the fluc tuations in the water level which either caused the canals to overflow onto the roads, or to run dry. They also had to make provisions for the supply of soft water to supplement other sources which proved to be insufficient. The aquaduct they built was five kilometres long and crossed the canals by means of hollow deep bridges. A second aqua duct eventually had to be built to supply the growing population. In
1449, dykes were built to protect certain areas of the town from flooding. The problem of household waste was solved by the availabi lity of a large workforce to dispose of it.
Despite the lack of certain means, these civilizations had obtained a quite high level of expertise in urban techniques. Unfortunately, it is difficult to study these pre-Columbian cities, because they have almost completely disappeared.

===== PAGE 441 =====

424
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
We shall close this section with a discussion of the problem of communications, which may provide the answer to where the blockage of the pre-Columbian technical system lies. A technique can only be fully exploited if it is applied within a large area both in geographical and human terms. As soon as there are difficulties in communication, and as soon as exchange becomes almost impossible, the entire system finds itself more or less blocked. So what was this region like before the
Europeans arrived?
We have already made the important point that at this time knowl edge could only be communicated orally. All messages were trans mitted in this way, and to help them, they used mnemonics such as designs or knotted cords. The absence of writing was already a severe handicap by this time.
The routes of communication were of course closely linked to the means of transport. Maritime transport was still very primitive, with canoes made of skin or bark mounted on frames being used in the
North. These were also used in tropical regions. There were also one-man boats and pirogues, made either using fire or with adzes. In the islands, the sails were made of matting. Further south, various types of rafts were used. At any event, the pre-Columbian peoples were not a maritime civilization. They did not possess the necessary equipment to build ships: they could have built the hull and the sails, but they had no winches, hoists, or pullies. Consequently no ports were built either.
There were two main techniques connected with land traffic, por terage and traction, which was either animal or human. Animal porterage was rarely used, because the animals were not big enough.
The peoples in .the North mainly used traction. Long-distance exchanges were practically impossible, and the consequent fragmen tation of economies was a prime cause of technical stagnation.
The roads were little more than paths, barely marked out, and these were used essentially to administer and control the country. Only the
Incas managed to establish a relatively extensive road network. Some of their roads were even paved in the areas surrounding the towns, no doubt to facilitate the supply of provisions coming in from neighbour ing areas. Some remains of these networks were found in Yucatan, dating back to the 7th century. The roads were 4.50 metres wide and were raised from about 0.60 to 2.50 metres above ground level. The base was made of heaped up stones, and the sides were protected by

===== PAGE 442 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
425 roughly shaped stones. The surface was made of metalling levelled out by a roller usually pushed by about fifteen men.
A civilization does not need very advanced techniques in order to stand out among the rest. Pre-Columbian civilizations were remarka ble for their arts and this often leads to erroneous conlusions about the level of techniques as a whole. It may be difficult to explain the technical deficiencies of this time, but it is through such deficiencies that one can understand the technical blocks which were to afflict this area until the European conquest.
THE MOSLEM WORLD
Even though there is an abundance of information and evidence about Arab and Moslem civilizations, any study of this subject seems to consist almost entirely of problems which are difficult to resolve.
In the words of one recent author writing about these civilizations:
"One has the impression, not of material progress, but rather of the stagnation of the Moslem world. It is difficult to see any consolidation of acquired knowledge, unless of course our perception of this period is distorted by discoveries made in more recent times, which has made us more severe in our judgement." This, then is the issue: the Moslem world is not seen to have made any significant contribution to the domain of techniques.
This is not then simply a case of a blocked technical system, but of the heritage of a blocked technical system, which proved impossible to change. In this sense, the Moslem world was almost regressing in comparison to the other civilizations which we have just discussed.
This ties in well with the more general historical context. The Arabs were nomads, and any nomadic people necessarily has a limited range of techniques - limited in the sense that they have no permanent settlements which are the condition of technical development. On the other hand, these people were conquerors, who sought to maximise the profit afforded by their conquests. They had no desire to change the techniques they acquired and practiced, because such change was bound to bring about social and economic disorder. It was in their interests therefore, to maintain the existing level of techniques.

===== PAGE 443 =====

426
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
However there is another side to this. At the time of their con quests, the Arabs were not a technically advanced people, which caused them several problems. Their approach to these problems is proof of their extremely flexible attitude.
They were able not only to use the techniques of the occupied countries, but also to gradually re-establish the techniques which had slowly disappeared in the past, particularly in the field of hydraulics.
The second point relates to all peoples who conquered large domains. The Arab empire covered diverse civilizations, all at dif ferent stages of technical evolution. It was one of the Arabs' strong points that they, like the Romans, ensured that all of the areas they occupied benefitted from the most advanced of these techniques.
The third point is no less important. The Arabs were able to assimilate all the most advanced techniques that they found in the countries they conquered. We noted above that many of the works of the Alexandrian School are known to us through their arabic versions, and the same was true of many agricultural treatises. This was almost certainly the result of preocupations in western caliphates, notably those of southern Spain.
Beyond these technological transfers, it is very likely that the Arabs were not so much an innovative people, but one which perfected the techniques they inherited. Al Jazari's treatise on automatons and certain agricultural treatises bear witness to this.
The Arabs seemed to have fulfilled the same role as the Romans, then, but perhaps they did so even with more dynamism. They also held a strategic position between the Western and Asiatic worlds, thus permitting vital technical exchanges to take place, such as the importation into the West of certain types of steel from India, along with paper and the compass, to take but a few examples. This meant that both Western and Eastern people were able to profit from the same developments. A fairly high level of technical education was essential for this. Very little research has been conducted into the technical mind of the Arabs, largely because precise facts are lacking, as is a precise chronology of the history of the Moslem technical world which might help us to appreciate its full value.
This is all the more curious because despite the Arabs reluctance to innovate, they possessed a relatively large number of written technical works. This feature is evident in Damas and Cordoba, the two great intellectual centres of the Moslem world at its height.

===== PAGE 444 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
427 Of course, not everything in these works was original. Far from having destroyed the Alexandria library, as some people have unjustly supposed, they in fact took great care to translate many of these Greek and Latin works. It would be helpful to have a proper inventory of these translations, since some of these were of Persian origin. Phil on of
Byzantium's work on pneumatic apparatuses is one of the works that we know of only through the Arab translation.
This Arab literature is just further proof that in the field of agricul ture, Islam drew from Greco-Roman experience. The numerous technical treatises are in fact mainly compilations, and it has been shown that the general course of development did not change. In fact, neither the land nor the climate necessitated any great alterations in the technical system. In the 10th century, when the centre of the arts and sciences shifted from Baghdad to Cordoba. the same traditions of the classical agronomists were perpetuated. However, the importance of the Persian- and Arab-inspired Agricultura nabatea should be noted. The treatise of Ibn-al-Awan, written in the 12th century, reveals the same influences of nabatean agriculture born in the
Chaldean region, possibly one of the cradles of agriculture itself. As well as the prescriptions which are to be found throughout the
Mediterranean basin, there is an element of magic which is very important. In fact, in the geographical zone occupied by the Arabs, the nature of the soil, the scarcity of water and the hot climates were all difficult constraints to overcome, and were necessarily imposed on all technicians of agriculture.
In other fields, Arabian literature was no doubt more limited. We shall quote once more the famous treatise on automatons by Al Jazari from the 13th century. This author was familiar with the Greek mechan icians from Alexandria, but he tried to go beyond them. Some of his writings on mechanics were certainly new, but the basic substance remained the same. A treatise on armoury, written for Saladin (in the second half of the 12th century) describes a type of machinery used in war - the counterweight artillery - which was borrowed almost completely from the West. We shall return to this point later.
The Arabs were good mathematicians and astronomers, not to mention doctors and alchemists, and their scientific literature is evi dence of this, with works by such famous scholars as A vicenne and
Averroes. However, it seems that they were less interested in more technical questions.

===== PAGE 445 =====

428
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Rather surprisingly, the Arabs do not seem to have transferred to certain parts of their empire those techniques which were improved in other areas. So while farming techniques in Egypt used the same processes, types of crop and instruments that had been in use for centuries, the techniques used in Southern Spain, on the other hand, underwent a marked development. Perhaps one of the reasons for this was the fragmentation of the Empire into independent caliphates.
Having invented nothing themselves, the Arabs had absolutely no interest in anything that might have been invented by the people they had conquered, thus sealing off one important channel of progress.
Any history of techniques in the Moslem world is eventually plagued by the lack of written sources. There are very few technical treatises and virtually no figurative representations. All that is available are a few Persian miniatures dating from the 12th century, which can give us only a vague idea of the techniques used in that area; and of course one should remember that what was valid in Persia was not necessarily so in
Egypt, North Africa or Spain.
The Arabs, and especially the Moslems, were not great innovators of cultivated species. At the very most, one can credit them with having extended the cultivation of certain crops, especially sugar cane, cotton and perhaps also rice. From the 13th century, these were grown in North Africa and throughout the southern regions of Europe:
Spain, Italy and Sicily. One distinct contribution of the Arabs was the group of exceptional gardens in the south of Spain, during the time of the Cordoba caliphate in the 10th century: these botanical gardens were used for the same purpose as experimental fields of seeds, to acclimatise and develop the various species coming from the East.
Most of these were medicinal plants, but experiments were also conducted on plants with a view to large scale cultivation.
There were no notable changes in agricultural implements, or in the techniques of tilling the land (fig. 16). Furthermore, it seems that the same regional varieties still persisted. The Persians used a swing plough with share-beam, for example, while the Egyptians were still using the heel-stilt plough (fig. 17).
The same phenomenon is apparent concerning the exploitation of the sub-soil. In Egypt, the Near-East, and Spain, the Arabs were content to simply work the old mines with old techniques (fig. 18). This is especially evident in the lead mines in southern Spain, and in the mercury mines at Almaden, further north. The Arabs were certainly

===== PAGE 446 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
429 Figure 16. - Spade with footrest. (From M. Daumas) Figure 1 7. - Persian ard. (From M. Daumas) short of iron, and the lack of fuels compounded the problems caused by the scarcity of deposits. Maurice Lombard has clearly shown how the Arab and Moslem worlds were dependent for wood and metals on the countries of Western Europe. In exchange, they supplied spices and gold.

===== PAGE 447 =====

430
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 1 8. - Miner with pick. (From M. Daumas) Animal domestication did undergo some changes, the most striking of which was the widespread practise of breeding horses. For a long time, Asia Minor was the equestrian reservoir of the Eastern basin of the Mediterranean. It was probably the Persian Sassanid dynasty (226 to 632) which perfected the art of horse breeding, and it was here that the Arabs found what later became known as the "Arab" horse, and which they were to use extensively. The Arabs were to practice horse-rearing on an enormous scale after this, and it was to reap dividends. The heavy cavalry of the Crusades found it extremely difficult to cope with the very mobile and fast Arabian cavalry. The
Arabs also found in conquered regions another very useful animal - the camel. The camels qualities of endurance and mobility were to make it an invaluable asset. In many of the conquered areas of Islam, wagons were totally impractical because of the muddy earth and desert sand. In some areas, the camel was just about the only means of transport. This also dispensed with the need to maintain what would have had to be an enormous road network. The Arabs developed the species of camel most suited to the kind of work required: the province of Oman in Southern Arabia was famous for its racing camels, for example. The Arabs may have found the horse which was to bear their name in Persia, but it seems they were actually responsible for developing the different camel species. Between 400 and 1300, the
East was closely dependent on this animal, causing the virtual disap pearance of the wagon.
The richness of the Iranian cuisine stands in great contrast to Arabian food, which had few variations, and was often badly pre-

===== PAGE 448 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
431 pared. Persian gastronomy was renowned even under the Sassanids. It was in the Moslem world that sugar cane first emerged, and its use spread eventually to the West and the Far East. We shall discuss a bit later the development of Morocco, which has been the subject of some recent studies. Moslem craftmanship is nearly always hailed as an exceptional achievement. However, it is noteworthy that the
Arabs found many of these developed techniques among the peoples they subjugated: Egyptian cloths are one example. Furthermore, a number of these highly praised craftsmen were not Arabs, but again, came from the subjugated peoples. And nomadic industries are invariably relatively primitive. Apart from a few improvements and additions, the tools used to not appear to have developed beyond the level of the antique period. (fig. 19). It has even been said that for certain trades, these tools were actually diminished in number. As nomads, they possessed hardly any tools, or furniture: this only changed when palaces were built. The textile industry, which was flourishing in Hellenic Egypt, was the glory of ancient Persia and byzantine Syria. Persian carpets were already being acclaimed by
Sidoine Apollinaire. Knotted carpets were already being made under the Sassanids. At the beginning of the 7th century, Huien-Tsiang from China expressed his surprise at the skills of Persian craftsmen and their expertise at weaving silk brocade, woollen cloths and carpets.
Figure 1 9. - Frame saw inherited from the Roman world. (From M. Daumas)

===== PAGE 449 =====

432
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
A Chinese, captured in the battle of Talas in 751 , is reputed to have taught the Arabs the art of paper-making. Papyrus was becoming scarce and expensive, so this technical transfer was all the more important. The first paper factory was esatablished in Baghdad in 795, and by the 10th century paper had taken over from papyrus. "Hun dreds" of paper mills are supposed to have existed in Fes from the 12th century. These were almost certainly hand mills.
Ceramics was an ancient occupation of the Arabs, and extremely fine pottery was made all over the Moslem world: it has been said that the pottery from Persia, Mesopotamia, Egypt and Syria easily rivalled the glazed earthenware of Tunis and Cordoba. The "tiles" from
Malaga became the Spanish azulejos, and also important was the pottery enamelled with reflecting mineral substances. The only treatise on ceramics which we still have today was written at the end of the 13th century by a Persian workshop manager from Kashan. There was diversity in all aspects of ceramic processes, from the manufactur ing techniques, the enamelling and the firing, to the forms and type of decoration.
Arab military techniques profited a great deal from western inven tions. All their equipment used in war was identical to that used in the
West, from the armour to the coats of mail, and from the swords to the lances. The manufacture of steel, described in the treatise on armoury written for Saladin, seems to be the product of a vivid imagination rather than sound technical knowledge. The crossbow was used by the
Arabs comparitively late, from the 9th century (figs. 20 and 21).
Most of the original elements of their equipment used in siege warfare seem to have come from Sassanid Persia. It appears that the
Arabs were not in fact the forerunners in the handling of count erweight artillery, which had been known in the West from the 9th century. It is possible however that some of the best machines were built by the Arabs, and better maintained, which would perhaps explain the surprise expressed by Joinville (fig. 22).
Both the Arabs and the Moslems were affected by the problems of space, which were very similar to those encountered by the Romans.
Consequently, some of their solutions were the same. The first major problem was posed by traffic. We have already mentioned that the intensive use of pack camels reduced the roads' network to a mini mum. It seems that there were very few civil engineering works created. In some countries, like North Africa and Spain, the Arabs

===== PAGE 450 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS

Figure 20. - Crossbow tower [above[; Triple crossbow [belowJ. (From M. Daumas) 433
