434
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 2 1 . - Crossbow used for launching naphta bottles. (From M. Daumas) Figure 22. - Trebuchet. (From M. Daumas)

===== PAGE 452 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
435 discovered Roman works. It was not before the 15th century that stone bridges were built in Turkish Europe.
River traffic on the Nile and the Euphrates remained much as it had been in Antiquity (fig. 23). Originally, the Arabs were not a great sea-going nation, and the only forms of naval transport they possessed were very crude small decked crafts. Around the coast of Oman in the
Red Sea, they used boats made of pieces of wood sewn together: in Syria, the planks were nailed. Wood from the coconut tree was used in the boats made of sewn wood, and the fibres were spun to make the masts. The leaves were used to make the sails and mooring ropes. (fig.
24). According to R. Lopez, it was the Arabs who made the first "lateen" sail. A Persian miniature painted in 1237 from the school of
Baghdad depicts a stem-post rudder, which it seems was already used on the seas of northern Europe.
It has been clearly shown that the importance of religion in Islamic life was the main reason why the towns were established. Indeed, at least twenty of these urban creations were established in the countries under Islamic rule. These were also based on the elements of preceding civilizations: the plans of Arabian and Moslem towns were very similar to those of Greece and Rome. As in Antiquity, these towns were organised around the area of worship which were usually beside a market place. Some were military towns, built around a military centre used for administering occupied territories.
These towns were often flanked by forteress-palaces, of which there are still some striking remains, from Grenada to Cairo. They were also often surrounded by high walls. There is no notable difference between this military architecture - unknown to the nomads but imposed on them by their conquests - and similar architecture in western Europe. The fortress of Cairo was built in the 11th century, and it was only after this that similar constructions were built in many other areas.
The building trade continued to use local techniques: they used bricks or stone depending on the area. The mosque of Ibo Tulun in
Cairo which dates from the 9th century, and which is traditionally believed to have been built by a Christian, is made entirely of bricks. In
Mesopotamia, Egypt and Persia, military constructions were often built using stones from antique monuments. The Arabs in Egypt were the first people to practice large scale demolition of the monuments built by the Pharaohs.

===== PAGE 453 =====

436
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 23. - Boat from the Euphrates. (From M. Daumas) Figure 24. - Small craft with sails. (From M. Daumas)

===== PAGE 454 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
437 The Moslems were very aware of the importance of hydraulics, and it was used to supply water to the many settlements that had been established before the conquest. Irrigation in these semi arid countries had been a vital necessity for centuries, so when the Arabs invaded, they were able to benefit from fairly advanced techniques, perfectly suited to the various needs of each region. They even had at their disposal the economic, social, judicial and political organisation which had evolved to make such works possible. In some cases, they were able to reap the benefits of these original efforts by systematizing and applying the same techniques throughout their Empire.
It was in Iran that they discovered the use of underground water levels built up by infiltrations - which tended to be large in areas where gravel and masses of fallen rocks accumulated at the bottom of mountain ranges - and by drainage tunnels called "quanats. " H.
Goblot, the main authority on this subject, has shown that this technique was relatively limited to Iran and the neighbouring regions.
Before the time of the Islamic civilization, it spread to the banks of the Mediterranean and the Arabian peninsular, after which it underwent a remarkable expansion in North Africa, Sicily and Spain. It is possible of course that after developing this technique, the Romans might already have introduced it into North Africa.
Building dams was a second method of irrigation. On the larger rivers, these were often simply water catchments. The Romans were no doubt already familiar with gravity dams. The arched dam was apparently first built in Glanum in Roman Gaul, in the 2nd or 3rd century. The Kebar dam in Iran, probably built in the 14th century, is twenty-six metres high. The Saveh dam dates from the 13th century.
At Kebar, the outlet at the bottom is situated about six metres below the first dam crest, and about ten metres.after the extra elevation. This ensured a very large cut off wall. The entire length of the crest was supposed to serve as a spillway in the event of flooding. It has been claimed that by this time, the Sassanids had already installed arched dams at Aden.
Roman antiquity had conceived of norias, which curiously enough is an arabic term denoting a specifically Roman instrument. These instruments were powered by horse-whims. Were the water-raising wheels an Arab invention? It is difficult to say. The oldest extant ones are to be found in Hama, on the Oronte, and they date back to the 8th and 9th centuries. The Toledo wheels, the oldest in Europe, are cited

===== PAGE 455 =====

438
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
by Al Ma'mun in 1043. Historians still disagree about the role of the Arabs in the transmission and development of these techniques.
The recent discoveries made by Bertier in Morocco have revealed the rather extraordinary installations built for irrigating sugar cane plantations and for powering hammer mills. It is difficult to date them, but they are most likely from the 13th century. Up until now, none like them had been found elsewhere, even in areas like Egypt for example, where sugar cane was extensively cultivated.
In general, the technical civilization of the Moslem world poses many problems. As in the case of China, we need an exact inventory and an exact chronology to study it properly, both of which are lacking.
Many myths still abound about the inventions or the transmission of inventions attributed to the Arabs. Some geographical sectors need to be better studied. Many comprehensive works have been written about Spain, so historians would do well to turn their efforts towards
Persia, particularly during the Sassanid period. Every step of the way, we find ancient examples of the most advanced techniques in these regions - in textiles, pottery, the building of dams, wind mills with horizontal sails, stirrups, etc. Furthermore, it is through the Persian miniatures that we know about the Moslem technical world. Was Iran an important centre of technical innovation? Was it a meeting point for the two technical civilizations of the East and the West? These questions still remain to be answered. One main point is certain: the
Arabs were not a particularly innovative people.
But let us leave the Arabs aside. They had no technical system as such, or more accurately perhaps, the techniques which they were using when still a nomadic people had hardly changed. They were able to profit from the advances made by the people they conquered, but they were content with simply this, and they did not exploit the situation nearly as much as they might have done. As a central focal point for several civilizations, their role was mainly one of transmis sion.
But the Chinese and the South-Americans did have technical systems: the former was advanced, the second was relatively limited.
The progress of both, however, ended abruptly. Isolation cannnot be blamed for this, especially since China was never totally isolated, and
Europe managed to advance without outside help. So the blockages must have been caused by something else. Was it because scientific knowledge ceased to progress? This is probably part of the reason, as,

===== PAGE 456 =====

BLOCKED SYSTEMS
439 if scientific knowledge is not directly useful in terms of techniques, yet it can act as a stimulant. This bears a certain resemblance to the case of
Greece during the hellenic period. In America, the absence of writing, and in China the complications of the writing system were no doubt also important factors. There was another point in common between these two civilizations: the rigidity of their social structures. This was probably one of the major causes of technical stagnation.

===== PAGE 457 =====

THE MEDIEVAL
TECHNICAL SYSTEM
Contrary to a once widely accepted theory, we now know that the Middle Ages was a period of intense technical activity, which we are beginning to understand better. Systematic research still remains to be completed in many domains, however, in order to achieve an exact view of the movement of medieval techniques. In some cases we are ignorant of the precise significance and the chronological progress of this movement. Some attempts in this field are interesting but still debatable.
As we have already seen on several occasions, our first step must be to establish some sort of chronological order: naturally techniques must be considered together with other human activities and recip rocal effects should not be isolated. And as we are partly concerned with these temporal divisions in conventions, we should try to put
"our" Middle Ages into perspective. Thus our period begins around the middle of the 12th century and ends in the early years of the 14th century. There was a marked rate of progress at the beginning and clear signs of crisis and depression at the end. At the outset, there were new elements and innovations and at the end, discord and war.
The 12th century has undoubtedly been less well studied in terms of its originality in comparison with preceding centuries. At least today, authorities stress a fairly rapid demographic growth, the increasing number of clearings which were made, and a form of feudalism which was henceforth organized. Cultural and technical exchanges increased and the Crusades opened wide the gates to the East and Asia. But already profound movements were taking shape, the importance of which was to grow: they were the natural consequence of demographic

===== PAGE 458 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
441 expansion, population intermixing and displacement and the movement of communal liberty.
Progress was constant in the second half of the 12th century and throughout the 13th century: the rapid boom of markets, urban development, the revival of striking gold coinage, large building works, and the strengthening of feudal monarchies, all seem to prove that growth was manifested in all walks of life. Obviously it is impos sible to provide figures, or to measure the extent of the expansion of the medieval West, but we believe that the collection of certain data would be sufficient to provide an extremely clear impression of this phenomenon.
Tensions began to arise at the very end of the 13th and the beginning of the 14th centuries, as monarchies, economies, and established positions became vulnerable, although the exact causes of the great crises of the 14th century have not yet been accurately detected. Philip the Fair of France was the first to implement monetary changes, as poor harvests and famines appeared in the first decades of the 14th century, and social problems began to manifest themselves in various regions. The West quickly began to know great financial crashes, devastating epidemics, and interminable wars.
This is the accepted outline of events today. For a century and a half, the European West had seen real expansion. This expansion was naturally to create tensions, and structures adapted much more slowly.
Is it not logical to suggest that in such a vast movement techniques might have played an important role, that they had a significant part in the processes of growth, as they had in those which produced these final tensions? Some authorities have suggested and maintained as much, but this may not yet be a general conviction. Inasmuch as a technical system and economic progress are closely linked, we should observe, if not a total technical revolution, then at least an ensemble of new technical ideas, or to be more precise significant technical inno vations, largely taking place around the middle of the 12th century. In fact, although some earlier discoveries had been made, a coherent body of advanced techniques was formed during this period. Similarly the pursuit for economic growth must have been maintained by a pronounced technical dynamism which we call development today.
Finally, we are able to observe a sort of decline in innovation at the end of the period thus defined, at the end of the 13th and the beginning of

===== PAGE 459 =====

442
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
the 14th century. This could provide a model for studying the creation and evolution of the medieval technical system.
THE FRAMEWORK OF
TECHNICAL PROGRESS
As we have already emphasized, techniques must be incorporated both within the history of mankind and an ensemble of material data.
In order to understand this better, it seems therefore necessary to precisely define the nature of its context, such as the particular intellectual environment and the physical conditions involved, in brief, everything that concerns technology "upstream," which is generally less well known than the economic evolution situated "downstream."
Medieval mysticism has often been discussed, a spirit of contemp lation and simultaneously a certain contempt for work, stemming from different motives, which would have prolonged an ancient attitưe.
We are dealing with the same difficulties here which arose in Anti quity, and it is probably for these same reasons that the problem has remained unsolved. Technology and the notion of work are closely linked. But unfortunately we lack any detailed and unbiased study on the second section. In a recent article, Maurice de Gandillac has demonstrated the value attached by some 13th century clerks to the
"mechanical arts." He refers to Lulle for both educational and econo mic reasons. The latter actually restores the notion of work to favour
"without reference to a theology (almost unknown during the Middle Ages) which put forward the example of Jesus the carpenter, and of
Paul making tents, in order to glorify this Formierung by which raw material was technically improved and converted into a specifically human form of work. " There is still cause to recall the mystics of the
Rhine, such as Eckart and Tauler who, half a century later, criticized the excess of monastic otium, but without praising the material fruits of labour. It is obvious that fidelity was still shown to Aristotle's tradi tional classification of various ways of life, where technical activity was placed well below others. But, as was the case with Antiquity, we still need to .neasure the exact significance of this notion and in any case not attribute to it a general character. The favour shown by princes

===== PAGE 460 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
443 towards "engineers" and technicians for many and various reasons, the attention displayed by some towns towards technical progress, the protection of manufacturing secrets and all the technical regulations, are once again proof of an interest which refutes the intellectual state of mind too complacently cited. We are here in the realm of the history of ideas, certainly, but provided that one distinguishes between the history of exceptional ideas and the history of standard ideas. The latter remains to be studied.
We will pass over the doubts of the moralist and the theologian.
Apart from these, there were scientists and practicians who, for whatever grounds, were committed to a material life. Even before the appearance of Vincent de Beauvais who is often cited, a genuine mystic, Hugo de Saint-Victor, demonstrated an intellectual spirit which is not commonly represented. The Didascalicon set out to instruct what one should read, the order in which one should read it, and to comment upon the text. Hugo de Saint-Victor saw four main sciences, which contained all the others: theory, which works towards the speculation of reality, practice, which is the discipline of morals, mechanics, which dispenses the necessary actions for life here below, and finally logic, which teaches one how to speak well and discuss with finesse. Theory was itself divided into theology, mathematics (or quadrivium) and an as yet imperfect system of physics. Mechanics comprised: (1) clothing (lanificium) ; (2) armament (haec duas habet species: architectonicum et fabrilem architectonica, dividitur in coementariam quae ad latomos et coementarios; et in carpentariam quae ad carpentarios et tignarios pertinet aliosque hujus modi artifices.
Fabrilis dividitur in mallestoriam (. . . ) et in exclusioram) ; (3) navi gation; ( 4) agriculture; (5) hunting; (6) medicine; (7) the organization of entertainments ( theatrica scientia).
Briefly then, these are the various aspects of material life. But the mechanical arts are only sciences to a certain degree: Poteste namque idem actus, et ad philosophiam pertinere secundum rationem suam, et ab ea excludi secundum administrationem. Verbigratia, agriculturae rationem philosophi est, administrationem rustici. Agronomy is a science, cultivation [is] the countryman's toil.
These texts are interesting for several reasons. Note briefly that this original classification of knowledge also incorporates techniques.
And, in this last case, a form of technology, albeit still partial, undeniably exists.

===== PAGE 461 =====

44
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
At almost the same epoch, Domingo Gundisalvo added to al Farabi's statistics of sciences new ideas which are in the same spirit as those of Hugo de Saint-Victor. The De scientiis was presented as a veritable treatise for careers' advising. What we today rather narrowly call science, formed in the classification of al-Farabi, scientia doctrina lis; arithmetic, geometry and optics (scientia de aspectibus), the science of weights (scientia de ponderibus, in other words statics and metro logy), and lastly, the science of machines (scientia de engeniis) or technology. One should also note that speculative mathematics, popu larized by Nicomachus and Boethius, which was paralleled with the arithmetic taught by treatises on abaci or algorithms ( arithmetica alia est pratica, alia est theoria), found numerous practical applications.
Similarly, geometry could be either active or speculative, the former being employed by surveyors, carpenters, smiths and builders.
The incorporation of the science of machines or engines in doctrinal knowledge was the major new idea. "The science of engines teaches us how to imagine and invent a way of adjusting natural bodies with ad hoc devices, conforming to a numerical calculation which may be applied to whatever purpose we wish." We may see the extent to which techniques, in the widest sense of the term, were integrated with didactic knowledge. The famous remark made by Leonardo da
Vinci, "Mechanics is the paradise of mathematics," is at the end of the road.
We find the same ideas with Vincent de Beauvais in the 13th century (Speculum doctrinale). He praised architecture to the skies and drew many forms of knowledge from it, particularly geometry, arithmetic for estimates, music for acoustics, and astronomy for sundials. We may therefore observe the same viewpoint and aims.
In 1296, Raymond Lulle reviewed these various sciences in his Arbor scientiae. Apart from trivium and quadrivium, he cited succes sively: de arte fabrili (metallurgy) ; de arte carpentaria seu aedificatoria
(building) ; de arte sartoriae (clothing); de arte agricolae (agriculture) ;
de arte mercimoniae (commerce) ; de arte nautarum (navigation) ; de arte militiae (military art). Like his predecessors, Lulle emphasized the need for theoretical knowledge required by technicians. A certain tendancy is therefore extremely clear. A didactic technique existed which closely affected speculative science, where in some instances the technique itself became a science and in every case, its usefulness was a guarantee of its respectability.

===== PAGE 462 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
445 Apart from this position of principle, other interesting elements may be observed. Scholars themselves maintained that science and techno logy were closely linked and that if technicians required theoretic knowledge, then science itself also required technology. Scholars' technical preoccupations were genuine, and from the 13th century they were to shatter the rigid barriers to which our manuals have made us accustomed. The Liber de ratione ponderis by Jordanus de Nemore, which was certainly written before 1200, deals with inclined planes and curved levers, and puts forwards a number of propositions, some of which were incorrect, which demonstrate, as Guy Beaujouan has shown, an undeniable interest for the work of engineers. This is also true of the 13th century work by Gerard of Brussels, Liber de motu, or of William of Moerbeke who had already translated the works of
Archimedes in around 1276.
Roger Bacon has drawn the portrait of Pierre de Maricourt who, according to him, incarnates the ideal scientist:
He knew by experiment the laws of nature, medicine and alchemy, as well as matters concerning the heavens and here below ( . . . ) He investigated the methods of metal casters, and what they do with gold, silver, other metals and all minerals; and he learned all about warfare, arms and hunting; he examined everything concerned with agricul ture, land surveying, and the work of peasants; he even studied the doings ofold witches, their divinations and incantations, and those of all the sorcerers, and likewise the illusions and tricks of all the jugglers ( . . . ) But this admirable man was not admired for his real worth, for had he wished to remain in the entourage of kings and princes, he would easily have found himself covered in honours and riches.
Although techniques and science had many and various links during the epoch which interests us here, this could not be otherwise in the field of combat. Moreover, the last phrase of Bacon's text which we have just cited proves this readily. The favours shown by the great clearly demonstrate the importance of material problems within a civilization which has been depicted rather too systematically as concerned with moral or religious problems. Certainly many princes honoured engineers and technicians, particularly military technicians who ensured their ruler's glory and power. Technicians were therefore received with honour at many courts. Roger II of Sicily called Arab mechanics to his court, and the Hohenstaufen, especially
Frederick II, followed this taste. In the 13th century, Alphonse the Wise, in Spain, had great respect for the engineers whose talents he employed.

===== PAGE 463 =====

446
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
As is so today, distinctions were certainly established between various techniques. There were respected techniques and poor rela tions. As always military techniques were frequently the first consider ation, and this was a substantial reason, albeit not always the major one, for progress. We should also mention the marvellous techniques made in automated toys and other things of amusement enjoyed by some princes. In both cases, such inventions were frequently beneficial to the whole technical system.
It is curious to note, moreover, the permanence of some sectors of technical research. Here we may bridge the gap between the work of those mechanics of Antiquity we have discussed, and the ideas of the
Renaissance, all too often illustrated solely by Leonardo da Vinci. In the Epistola de secretis geribus, Roger Bacon prefigured the great
Florentine and provides a detailed picture of the technical preoccu pations of his age:
It's possible to make Engines to sail withall, as that either fresh or salt water vessels may be guided by the help of one man, and made sail with a greater swiftness, than others will which are full of men to help them.
It's possible to make a Chariot move with an inestimable swiftness (such as the "currum falcati" were; wherein our forefathers of old fought,) and this motion to be without the help of any living creature.
It's possible to invent an Engine of a little bulk, yet of great efficacy, either to the depressing or elevation of the very greatest weight, which would be of much conse quence in several Accidents: For hereby a man may either ascend or descend any walls, delivering himself or comrads from prison; and this Engine is only three fingers high, and four broad.
A man may easily make an Instrument, whereby one man may in despight of all opposition, draw a thousand men to himself, or any other thing, which is tractable.
A man may make an Engine, whereby without any corporal danger, he may walk in the bottome of the Sea, or other water. These Alexander (as the Heathen Astronomer assures us) used to see the secrets of the deeps.
Such Engines as these were of old, and are made even in our dayes. These all of them (excepting only that instrument of flying, which I never saw or know any, who hath seen it, though I am exceedingly acquainted with a very prudent man, who hath invented the whole Artifice) with infinite such like inventions, Engines and devices are feasable, as making of Bridges over Rivers without pillars or supporters.
Does this not present the notion of a degree of technical progress?
Does not this demonstrate the belief that all material advances were possible? This was, in any case, to be the programme which all technical research was to take up until the Renaissance, illustrated in the famous letter by Leonardo da Vinci which is virtually an exact copy word for word of the above text.

===== PAGE 464 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
447 This whole attempt to achieve technical progress was to be trans lated by a remarkable and abundant technical literature. This litera ture presents two aims which aptly translate the different levels of technical research. On the one hand, were veritable treatises, still few in number, which were thus the object of a given knowledge. And on the other hand, were books of formulae, which only presented solutions to certain problems, a sort of range of successes in one or another field. Some may have been for a large public, while others were probably only personal collections of work.
The first group above all comprises of information which arrived in the Western Middle Ages from ancient literature. Greek traditions were virtually unknown and do not seem to have left the Byzantine
East where they may still be found in practice during the 10th century.
As we will see, they were only to reappear at the end of the 15th century. The most popular and widely read Latin author was indispu tably Vegetius. In 1151, the count of Anjou, Geoffrey the Fair, lay siege to Montreuil-Bellay, and the monks of Marmoutiers who went to see him, found him absorbed in reading Vegetius. We have a large number of manuscripts of his work, copied from the 11th to the 15th century. Despite all that has been said, Vitruvius was never forgotten and our libraries also preserve the manuscripts of De re architectura from the 11th century onwards. Latin agronomists were undoubtedly used less, mainly owing to the fact that medieval farming conditions were, at least in a large part of Western Europe, rather different from those of Antique Mediterranean agronomics.
Agriculture was possibly one of the most difficult techniques to translate into a didactic form. The diversity of soils and species, and the continuation of deeply rooted traditions naturally hindered the introduction of these great treatises which were nevertheless to increase from the time of the 16th century. In the 10th century,
Cordoba knew botanical gardens and literature on farming tech niques. The traditions of Antique agronomists were united with the arab or persian inspired Agricultura nabatea. Such works, resulting from two schools of thought, were numerous from the 11th century onwards: Latin or Castilian translations enabled these treatises to travel throughout the Christian world. Other, more suitable works, written as pratical manuals with the aim of providing the farmers of these great domains with indispensable ideas, appeared fairly rapidly in more northern areas. Anglo-Norman territories possessed a few of

===== PAGE 465 =====

448
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
these, such as the treatise by Walter of Henley On Husbandry, the Livre de Seneschausie, the Fleta, and various works by Grosseteste, which were all from the first half of the 13th century. The number of manuscripts is proof of their success. This is also true of a treatise found in France and published around the middle of the last century, which provides a good example of the problems presented by agricul ture.
The whole medieval agronomic science and all the recollections of the Latin authors are gathered together in the Ruralium commodorum opus, which the Italian, Pietro dei Crescenzi compiled between 1304 and 1306. This work provides a sort of mid point between compilations recalling Latin traditions and partly original essays, theoretical works, such as De vegetalibus by Albert the Great, or practical works illustrated by Anglo-Norman treatises. This work was translated, rather badly, into French at the request of Charles V, in 1373.
Parallel to agriculture, were numerous treatises on animal hus bandry. They mainly concerned the horse and are therefore of unquestionable military interest. A treatise by Giordano Ruffo, writ ten between 1250 and 1254, was translated into several languages. A
Dominican of Bologna and later bishop of Cervia, Teodorico Borgog noni composed a treatise in approximately 1266. We may also cite
Bartholomew of Messina and Moses of Palermo working in the second half of the 13th century, and the infante Don Fadrique, and Giraldo and Lorenzo Rusio at the beginning of the 14th century. These works may be compared to the many treatises on hunting and falconry which were superbly illustrated.
Apart from these coherent and systematic collections, there was also a whole litterature on formulae, compilations of processes and meth ods, technical tricks, and books on machines, covering a multitude of subjects. The first work of this nature was written by the monk
Theophilus: Schedula diversarum artium, in the 11th century, of which there are many manuscripts. Aside from artistic techniques which was his main subject of concern, there are also notations which are wholly technical in nature.
Books containing purely technical formulae were also numerous.
Some five hundred works have been counted in Paris for the entire Middle Ages, many of which undoubtedly repeat other works.
Subjects are more diverse, however, ranging from culinary art to medicine, from agriculture to colourings, and including metallurgy,

===== PAGE 466 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
449 jewellery, alchemy, automata etc. Some of these techniques were covered very thoroughly, such as colourings, and to a large extent, metallurgy.
Two other types of document, concerning more particularly archi tecture and mechanics, stem from these books of formulae. Archi tecture is more personal in nature, as this involves the notebooks of lists which architects and engineers kept for their own use, noting down anything about their craft or which attracted their curiosity. The only work of this type preserved from this epoch is the album of Villard de Honnecourt which probably dates from the first half of the 13th century. He cannot have been the only one in this genre, which enjoyed great popularity during the Renaissance.
The second type of document represents a more advanced type of notebook. These are compendiums of machines, drawings and expla nations, arranged around specific themes, many of which were military in nature. In 1268, the engineer Assaut wrote to ask for an audience with Alphonse de Poitiers, who was preparing for a crusade, in order to show him his collection of machines. In 1328, Guido da Vigevano made the same request to Philip VI, proposing to show him a treatise comprising entirely of a collection of machines designed for use in distant wars. We still have this manuscript. Here we have the first examples of what were to be termed, two centuries later, "theatres of machines" (fig. 1).
Techniques had therefore provided much food for thought during the Middle Ages, organized to a greater or lesser degree, and more or less systematic in nature depending upon the subjects considered.
Certainly, the technological aspect was still slight, but not completely absent: empiricism on the whole remained dominant. So too were technical instructions which encompassed the status of corporations:
this primarily concerned bans rather than actual instructions on the subject of the employment of a particular material or the use of a certain process. Yet there was nevertheless a desire to instruct the world in the best of the techncial knowledge of the age.
We should not forget either the intellectual entourage surrounding techniques. Very rapidly - for whatever reasons of wealth or power - authorities, scientists and philosophers leaned towards the problem of technology. While our lack of texts, particularly for more remote periods, should not be forgotten, we may nevertheless note that this preoccupation for techniques manifested itself around the middle of

===== PAGE 467 =====

450
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
B
c
D
Figure I . - Machines designed by Guido da Vigevano (circa 1 327). A, crank-propelled paddle-boat; B, portable suspension bridge; C, movable tower; D, wind-driven fighting car.

===== PAGE 468 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
451 the 12th century and continued to develop until the end of the 13th and the beginning of the 14th century. Here an additional concordance should be emphasized. Apart from a few rare examples, there is hardly any of the contempt shown for techniques which has been so fre quently suggested. In order to apply this thesis, might one not suggest that at the very beginning of our period, it was the Cistercian monks who initiated the rebirth of iron and steel making, developed the use of hydraulic energy, and invented new building methods? The introduc tion of a working monasticism and the vigour which it gave to some techniques would seem to constitute unquestionable proof. Was it not precisely as a result of this new spirit that techniques could once again make progress? In order to reply to a developing economy and an increasing population, they attempted to gather together both fairly ancient techniques and different traditional techniques from those of
Antiquity, and to create an ensemble of techniques at a level on which a new technical system could be formed. This necessitated the re reading of certain texts and the collection of particular facts with this aim in mind: in such a way, matters could be clarified.
The question of a natural context is equally important. The centre of gravity of the medieval West was no longer the Mediterranean region, but had visibly shifted further north. As all technology is necessarily dependent upon natural conditions, and even more so in early epochs than nowadays, new or adapted techniques naturally appeared. As we will see, although Antiquity did indeed invent certain methods and machines, the natural conditions of its geographical space prevented or prohibited any further development. Aside from these natural forces, economic factors also played a part, the effects of which are quite obvious: the climate is one of the most restricting forces.
The natural environment of the Middle Ages in the West was therefore not exactly the same as that of classical Antiquity. The
Mediterranean border of Europe had certainly not changed, and techniques which had barely evolved were still in use: biennial land rotation, the use of the swing-plough, the continuation of the kiln and many other elements are evidence of this. As one moves towards the
North, so conditions change totally. Lands are lush, dense and rich, and require modified tools. The Gallic plough, very different from the
Roman swing-plough, had already extended into the Po Valley from the time of Pliny the Elder. Different crops were also cultivated and there was a return to the widespread growth of certain Southern

===== PAGE 469 =====

452
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
plants, in particular the vine. On the other hand, plants which could not be grown in the dry climates of these regions could now be cultivated. Forests had a greater variety of species, thus enabling the medieval West to become a civilization of wood, far more so than had been the case in Antiquity. These Northern forests regenerated them selves more easily, providing a great quantity of wood which Antique civilizations had never known: the Parisian middle classes of the 13th century could use a fireplace for generous heating, consuming a lot of wood, while the Romans in the time of Caesar could only use a brazier.
This is also true of water systems, as rivers and waterways flowed more regularly and did not suffer the drought periods of Mediterranean rivers. The proliferation of the water mill, developed moreover in Late
Antiquity, was the most immediate result of this different state of affairs. Metals were more abundant, especially iron ore, deposits of which were scattered throughout almost the whole of Northern
Europe. Rome only enjoyed the exploitation of real mineral deposits after the conquest of Spain, and the Empire's iron came from Alpine or even more northern regions, and from still barbarian areas, even at the height of its expansion: recent discoveries in Poland seem to demonstrate that deposits between Warsaw and Cracow supported the
Roman Empire, endowing them with considerable importance.
Exchanges - and who will deny the pressure of exchanges on production - also required modification. The Antique Mediterranean survived on maritime commerce, but medieval Europe was to survive above all on land commerce, although with much more difficulty.
Antiquity could concentrate its food-producing crops, as the circu lation of food stuffs was easy, and could build marble temples, for marble columns were transported by ship (as underwater excavations have proved). Difficulty in transportation implies on the contrary that centres of production were widely dispersed in the Middle Ages. This explains why the vine was cultivated in higher regions, why smaller deposits of iron were exploited, why an undifferentiated and extensive cultivation was practiced, why the number of fish ponds increased, and the reason why local material was used. It is only at the end of our period that maritime trade was to be revitalized.
Aside from these physical conditions which were essential for material life, other economic factors played an important part. Here again, astonishing chronological coincidences may be observed. The history of the climate, which should one day be included in our general

===== PAGE 470 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
453 history, shows a certain unity, characterized by a glacial thrust. Thus temperatures generally cooled down around 1200, perhaps even as early as 1150, to 1300, maybe even to 1350, according to the outline suggested by Le Roy-Ladurie. The climate seems to have been fairly mild during the four centuries from the Carolingians to the great clearings. At this point in time the vine began to advance towards the north, although in the 11th century the hazel tree had not won back its optimum positions in the north of Scandinavia. From the end of the
12th century onwards, failing rainfall and rapid evaporation gave way to a far more humid and cooler climate. Even if such conclusions seem hasty, and without exaggerating the differences in temperature bet ween periods, this fact and its limits in time should be noted.
Demographic movement is the last of these great conjunctional movements which exerted a considerable influence on economic developments and subsequently on techniques. The rapid progress of demographic expansion is obvious, and virtually every contemporary author remarked upon it. It began around the middle of the 12th century and continued into the second half of the 13th century, perhaps even to the end of that century. Evidence available to prove this expansion emphasizes the significance of this phenomenon: exten sive clearings could only be undertaken by a larger population, and it is precisely for reasons of increased food requirements, very clear urbanization, (at least in some regions), together with all the impli cations involved in the development of towns, (particularly in the domains of industry and commerce) as well as social changes, which are the elements which naturally exert pressure on the technical systems in force. A more detailed study of the beginnings and the rapid take-off of this demographic growth would be most useful: some regions were more favoured than others in this respect.
Contrary to this, at the other end of our period, the importance of demographic experience may have placed a brake on technical pro gress, while the interval between the two movements had become too considerable. It is a generally accepted fact that an extremely dense population tends to preserve traditional techniques, as is exemplified by China at the same period, but undoubtedly there is more to this than meets the eye. Conversely, technical stability in the face of population growth prompts imbalances which present serious drawbacks to the economy. We will see that certain authorities have emphasized the inability of farming techniques to reach the level required by the

===== PAGE 471 =====

454
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
demographic density of the time, although clearing work had been undertaken. The transition from extensive to intensive agriculture could not be achieved, resulting in considerable food deficits. The great catastrophes of the 14th century, ranging from the first famines of
1316-1317 to the Great Plague of the middle of the 14th century, from the monetary changes of Philip IV the Fair to the great Italian crashes again during the middle of the 14th century, undoubtedly stem from these internal tensions in the medieval West in the centre of which were the problems posed by techniques.
In the light of the intellectual viewpoints discussed before, it would seem that the great technical movement of the Middle Ages should be situated within this whole natural complex, so that we might better comprehend this phenomenon. Let us remind ourselves of the two essential points in our demonstration. The first concerns chronological order and we have attempted to underline all the concordances involved. The second concerns the constitution of a new technical system which is largely different from Antique systems. We must therefore examine this new technical system.
THE INNOVATIVE FIELDS
We should certainly be cautious in our investigation here in order to avoid error. Any new technical system does not necessarily imply general progress and total upheaval of the old system. In some sectors the evolution need only be significant enough to initiate profound change in the conditions of economic life and to maintain some coherence between traditional sectors. If, at the end of the period in question, economic growth produces, in an unequal way, imbalances between the various advanced sectors, or between these and tradi tional sectors, a discrepancy will arise between technical structures and other structures, which will in turn lead to a state of crisis. It is with this example in mind that the progress of medieval techniques should be studied.
One cannot overemphasize the importance of distinguishing bet ween the birth of an invention and its generalized use, in other words

===== PAGE 472 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
455 its innovation. Social, economic and natural conditions thus come into play. The history of techniques is therefore situated on two levels, but it is easier to exemplify this by looking at concrete examples. The water mill was used in Antiquity, modern harnessing was known in the East from the 2nd century AD, and agriculture was far less developed than iron and steel making techniques: these are accepted facts. On a higher level, one might suggest that for various reasons the Western Middle
Ages had at their disposal the hydraulic energy necessary for the generalization of the use of the water mill, that from a particular time onwards a more progreS$ive economy based on products from the land could benefit from modern harnessing, that the richness of the land, the climate and the possibility of extensive farming could dispense with technical changes in cultivation methods, and that any other condi tions merely formed previous but uncirculated experience which could be coherently organised within a system from the middle of the 12th century. In this case, we are concerned not so much with listing inventions but eventually demonstrating that a new technical system emerged, necessarily heterogeneous in its origins, simultaneously composed of old inventions, some new techniques, and a degree of conservatism.
The importance of the quantity of available energy has always been an essential factor in the economy, even more so for economic growth.
On the other hand, forms of this available energy or energies (as there are usually several types at the same time), play a significant role in the evolution of techniques which are closely dependent upon this factor.
An increase in the availability of energy exerts a very clear pressure on all the techniques situated "downstream."
Antiquity already knew and used every form of energy employed by the Middle Ages in the West: animal energy had long been employed, wind power, particularly for ships, had been used for some time, and hydraulic energy was employed from the 1st century AD. The major achievement made in the Middle Ages lay in having considerably increased the quantity of available energy and using it more effec tively. The major assets of our period are therefore of two types. On one side, there is an infinitely greater quantity of natural energy worldwide: water, wood, and animal rearing progressed as a result of natural grassland for grazing. And on the other side, undoubtedly owing to this abundant energy source, a diversification of its uses was

===== PAGE 473 =====

456
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
possible with the employment of old or recent mechanisms in which there had been little previous interest, due to the lack of sufficient energy resources.
Truth to tell, very little may be said on the subject of animal energy.
Nature undoubtedly had a greater hand in things than the adoption of new energy converters, the exact limits of which we will examine later.
The abundance of the grasslands led to an increase in the number of animals and gave them greater strength and resistance. We will consider later the changes in harnessing and the introduction of the iron horseshoe which meant that the horse could be used better and more efficiently. The problem of breeds may possibly have entered into this, but our information on this subject is rather scant: new breeds were probably imported by the Barbarians, and breeds were improved by selection and cross-breeding. It was during this period that breeds from the Perche and the heavy draught-horse from the
Boulogne region were introduced. We know that veritable stud-farms existed, particularly in Normandy, where these slow changes may have originated. We have already considered above the progress of veter inary medicine. Although the history of the horse is better known, cattle, on the contrary, which were essential for farming work and for some types of transport over a short distance, have not yet been the subject of any serious study.
The large scale use of hydraulic energy undoubtedly represents a substantial medieval innovation. Hydraulic energy was used in Anti quity, but for natural reasons it could not enjoy the expansion it warranted. Between the 1st and 5th centuries the use of hydraulic systems became more widespread. We may observe here the prodi gious expansion of the use of this form of energy in more northern regions around the middle of the 12th century. Although statistics for this may be presented, we should point out, however, that we are necessarily dependent upon our documentation which is only really copious from the middle of the 12th century. Having said this, some interesting figures are available. At the end of the 11th century, the
Domesday Book mentioned in its census 5624 water mills in England, which may represent approximately 16,000 horsepowers. In the Aube department in France, where all the texts have been reviewed, they numbered 14 in the 11th century, 60 in the 12th and over 200 in the 13th century. In the Forez, where all such texts have been similarly published, there are none counted for the 12th century, but there are

===== PAGE 474 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
457 70 in the 13th century. According to all the studies undertaken in this field, it seems that the use of hydraulic energy must have already been manifest from the 9th century onwards, at least in some regions.
Hydraulic energy gradually increased in importance and appears to have grown rapidly from the middle of the 12th century.
A few points may be clarified with the aid of figurative represen tations, a recent census of which has been almost completed (fig. 2).
All the examples we have of these, from between the 9th and the 13th centuries, illustrate mills with vertical wheels, which was also the system used in Antiquity. The arrangement of the wheel in relation to the flow of water is equally important: the overshot wheel, powered by water falling on the wheel from paddles above, has the highest output, of approximately 75% against the 60% output of the breastshot wheel, where the wheel is placed at the side, and the 25% productivity of the undershot wheel, driven by a current moving against the paddles at the bottom of the wheel. The position of the wheel is closely linked to hydraulic conversion. The three wheel positions were all known in the Middle Ages as they had been in
Antiquity, but our texts do not permit any definitive conclusion to be made on this subject.
The water mill was infinitely more efficient than traditional tech niques. Most certainly, the continuation of hand-grinding, which was firmly defended in some regions, proves that the water mill was not economically vital. In order to maintain its use, it was necessary to implement a ban, but this was very rarely applied to other uses of the water mill. In other instances, on the contrary, there was considerable economic gain to be made and primitive techniques were no longer used, to such an extent that in some cases mills became collectively owned, such as fulling and tanning mills.
Let us look now at a last form of mill which used a particular type of energy, that is the tide mill, which operated with the ebb an<:f flow of the tides in areas where holding dikes could be readily built. It is easy to understand why Antiquity had not thought of developing these. The tide mills at the port of Dover apparently existed from the time of
William the Conqueror, and mills on the Adour River, in France, are mentioned around 1120-1125.
Apart from sailing, the use of wind energy was introduced in the West during the medieval age. Texts would seem to prove that windmills were used in the 7th century on the plateaux of Iran. Two

===== PAGE 475 =====

TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Persian geographers mention windmills in the province of Seistan around the middle of the 10th century.
Mechanical problems were more difficult to solve. It is probable that up until recently, windmills used in the Near East were on a vertical axis (fig. 3, A and B). Two Arab drawings from the 13th century still show them in this form. The first pictorial representations of windmills which we have for Western Europe, show the mills on a horizontal axis (fig. 3, C); but this led to the delicate problem of how to position the sails in the prevailing wind, the direction of which may change appreciably. Here again, all our documents tally: as a whole all these windmills pivoted on an enormous wooden tripod, or post-mill, which presupposes that it was a light construction always built in wood.
The difficult question of dating remains to be discussed. In Spain, windmills would have been used in Tarragona from the 10th century onwards, a fact which rules out the idea that the windmill was an importation from the Crusades as some authorities have suggested.
The oldest texts in our possession cite that there was a working windmill in Normandy around 1180, and a little later in England. From the 13th century onwards, the windmill was introduced throughout
Western Europe. All this would seem to prove, apart from a few exceptional cases, that the windmill appeared in our regions in the second half of the 12th century, and rapidly expanded to areas with regular wind forces from the beginning of the 13th century, finally reaching countries situated further to the east, such as Poland or
Sweden, in the first half of the 14th century. The oldest pictorial representations, those of the Audenarde rentier and the Saint Vaast d' Arras psalter all date from around 1270: there are no representations in the terrier of the bishop of Lille, which dates from the same period.
We are quite sure that at this date the windmill was only used for grinding grain.
We have already pointed out the exceptional abundance of wood in the more northern zones of Western and Central Europe. This therefore provided a considerable source of thermal energy which classical and Mediterranean Antiquity did not possess. The importance of this source of energy, for a great number of industries
(in lime, glass and metal production), for heating, and for the fabri cation of bricks and tiles, need hardly be emphasized. It stemmed from natural resources.

===== PAGE 478 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM

A
B
c Figure 3. - The windmill. A, Arab windmill with horizontal sails (from manuscript from Al
Dimashqi, circa 1 300); B, 1 3th century Arab windmill with horizontal sails (from Klemm); C, medieval windmill (from an English psalter, circa 1 270).

===== PAGE 479 =====

462
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
It therefore seems obvious that the availability of energy was far greater in the Middle Ages than it had been during Antiquity. Energy sources were more considerable and also more diversified, which was an additional advantage. This involves an important point which merits more detailed studies, especially in the field of statistics, in order to bring together the possible and approximative curves of economic growth and energy used. Let us hope that in the near future our knowledge in this domain will be more comprehensive and more detailed than it is now.
Energy and mechanization are closely linked, as it was thanks to advanced mechanization that energy could be developed to its full potential. It might seem surprising to include mechanization among the leading sectors of the Middle Ages: indeed, one may say that almost all the mechanisms used during this period were known in
Antiquity. It was precisely the extensive development of the use of energy that enabled these mechanisms to be thoroughly used and improved. As we have seen, the Ancients employed these mechanisms in many cases, but only in very marginal domains: perhaps the taste for automata was one of the most powerful elements of discovery, as will be seen many times in later centuries. Mechanisms used in the Middle
Ages progressed from a stage of potentiality to one of concrete technical reality.
There is little need to emphasize the use of reduction mechanisms which have already been discussed. The Middle Ages had in fact only developed the use of those lifting appliances handed down by Anti quity: pulleys, pulley blocks, and squirrel cage wheels (and one may think here of the famous cranes of Bruges and Lune burg). Similarly, the use of the worm or endless screw should also be pointed out, examples of which have already been provided by Hero and Pliny, particularly for screw presses. The Middle Ages extended its use to the jack, a perfect illustration of which is provided by Villard de Honne court in the first half of the 13th century.
We know little about the lathes used by the Ancients. If they possessed the brace, it is inconceivable that they did not use the the crank and wheel system, like those used in simple lathes, with a continuous drive, a good example of which is the hand-driven spinning wheel. By using a large wheel, a fly wheel could be formed, which if necessary left the driving hand free, at least periodically, for other uses.

===== PAGE 480 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
463 Most medieval lathes were drawback lathes wood, and were therefore reciprocating. A few rare pictorial representations of these remain, and two types of lathes may be distinguished among them:
A) The single treadle lathe was based on a system of belts wound around the lathe's axle and linked both to the treadle and a spring
(either in a wooden branch or pole). Operating alternately, the treadle and the spring turned the lathe in one direction and then the other (fig.
4, A and C).
B) The two treadle lathe was based on the same system, but the driving belt was activated alternately by one treadle and then the other. One of the rare illustrations of this can be seen in a stained-glass window in Chartres cathedral (13th century; fig. 4, B).
A typology of the uses of the water mill still remains to be done. We do possess, however, a few important elements. Direct transmissions with the possibility of reducing and changing planes were achieved by using cogwheels and lanterns with staver. Antiquity knew of these mechanisms and had even begun to construct the theory concerning them. On the other hand, the water mill could activate balanced apparatus and spring-operated systems, owing to the development of the cam. We may therefore classify the principal uses of the water mill into the following main categories:
A) Direct use of the circular movement with simple changes in planes, intensity, and speed: these are generally millstones for crushing or grinding. Our present knowledge enables us to draw the the following list:
1) the corn or wheat mill known from Antiquity;
2) the oil mill: quoted in the Forez in the 13th century;
3) the mustard mill: mentioned in the 13th century in the Forez;
4) the beer mill: vague texts in the 9th century in Picardy, and more certain texts from the middle of the 11th century;
5) the sugar mill: archeological examples from the 11th century in Morocco;
6) mills for grinding; the end of the 12th century in Normandy and in Beauvaisis;

===== PAGE 481 =====

464
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
B
A
c Figure 4. - Lathes. A, pole lathe (from "La Bible Moralisee, early 1 4th century); B, lathe with a pulley and two treadles (from a stained-glass window from Chartres, 1 3th century); C, pole lathe (from the Mendel Bruderbuch, circa 1 404 ).

===== PAGE 482 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
465 7) mills for turning: these are only cited in the 14th century in the Dauphine in France, but they could have been in existence before this date;
8) mills for raising water: these are attested to by Vitruvius, in the Near East in the 9th century, in Spain from the 10th century onwards
(fig. 5, top), and from the 11th century further north where they were used less;
9) some mills using hammers which could have originally operated with millstones, as has been substantiated by certain texts: dyestuff mills from the 13th century in Namur; examples of tanning mills from the 11th century.
B) The use of the cam for balanced or spring loaded apparatus:
1) iron mills: in Sweden from the end of the 12th century;
2) copper mills: operating on the same system as the preceding, but we do not possess any ancient example of them;
3) fulling mills: from the end of the 12th century in Normandy, Piedmont, and in the Milanese;
4) paper mills: in the middle of the 13th century in Spain;
5) hemp mills: in the 13th century;
6) saw mills: the only example of a spring mechanism is illustrated by Villard de Honnecourt, circa 1250 (fig. 5, bottom).
Obviously our knowledge needs to be more detailed and inter national research should be undertaken to this effect, but this simple list demonstrates the way in which the water mill constituted, in many areas, a real industrial revolution, to use a rather ambiguous term but one which has been confirmed through use. Although the water mill was known before the 1st century AD, and although various mechan isms were known to the Ancients - then again they had not been applied to hydraulic energy - one notes a totally new and remarkable situation from the 11th and 12th centuries. It was only from this time onwards that techniques for using hydraulic energy really came into their own.
There was, however, a new type of mechanism in existence in the Middle Ages: war engines. Some of these, such as rams or revolving towers, were very old. But it is true that the Middle Ages had

===== PAGE 483 =====

466
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 5. - Uses of hyudraulic energy. I above I Noria of Cordova (from a 1 4th century seal);
[below! Villard de Honnecourt's saw (circa 1 250).
abandoned the use of neuroballistic artillery used by the Ancients in favour of counterbalanced artillery which was probably more pow erful. Trebuchets used the ballistic properties of the sling, by employing a suitable mechanism, with greatly increased dimensions.
This in fact consisted of a very large pole which pivoted between two treadles and powered a huge sling and counterweight (fig. 6) The mangonel was a variant of the trebuchet. A straight long shot and a

===== PAGE 484 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
467 falling shot could therefore be obtained by using these two systems.
Without doubt some essential rules had been established for firing a shot, taking into account the weight of the ball, the distance to be covered, the length of the pole, and the angle at which the ball should be released, all of which were certainly calculated in an approximate and empirical way.
In such a way, an arsenal of "engines" was built up in the West during the Middle Ages. Some new machines were created which had not existed in Antiquity, but more particularly they continued to extensively use mechanisms or machines which had already been developed, the employment of which had remained very limited. The water mill and its various applications constituted the bulk of this mechanization.
It is often more difficult to comprehend the extent of the problems involved in transport. Before even broaching questions which have formed the subject of well-known books - the fame of which has undoubtedly distorted the perspective of this topic - it would be useful to clarify certain points concerning harnessing:
A) This new equipment was designed for the horse, the harnessing of cattle not having been modified. All the research undertaken shows that on equal terms the horse is tougher and faster than the ox. Yet one wishes to be able to break down the uses made of one or the other animal.
B) A second important point arises. Where transport is concerned, it would be indispensable to know - albeit in simple figures - on one hand, the volume transported, and on the other, the method of transport employed. The following essential facts have emerged from certain recent works: ( 1) in the first place, the volume of exchanges was relatively poor, as can be clearly seen by the number of tollgates; this is also demonstrated by the obvious diversity of a region's productions, even if they were limited, which was designed precisely to avoid the difficulties presented by transport; (2) in the second place, the modern horse harness could have found its perfect application insofar as large carts could have enabled the transportation of a particular volume or weight: we know that the absence of a pivoted front axle system made a four-wheeled cart virtually impracticable. Moreover, this would have required a developed and well-maintained road network; (3) this second observation means that animals had to be used for transporting

===== PAGE 485 =====

468
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
B
A
c Figure 6. - Assay balances. A and B, from a Parisian manuscript from the early 1 4th century;
C, from a 1 4th century manuscript from Cambridge; D, assay balance mounted on a boat, from the Roman de toute Chevalerie, Paris and Oxford, 14th century.

===== PAGE 486 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
469 most goods (here the iron horseshoe could intervene) or were carried by waterways.
Thus, although the iron horseshoe was a valuable technique, modern harnessing, on the other hand, was certainly of little interest, apart from transportation over short distances, and was restricted to agricultural uses which we will examine later.
We have a few pointers on the subject of the iron horseshoe which may give a fairly accurate idea of the invention and its significance.
From Antiquity onwards, it was well-known that the horse's hoof was relatively fragile. But apart from this, the initial idea of driving nails into a horse's hoof could not have been conceived without the knowl edge of the composition of the various tissues of the hoof and their function. The complete absence of any mention of this in ancient texts proves conclusively that classical Antiquity never knew of the horseshoe. Horseshoes may have been at the most just protective devices, designed to prevent lesions of the hoof, in particular laminitis which was undoubtedly fairly frequent at this period. Anything said on the subject of horseshoes regarding the Barbarians is purely hypo thetical.
Concrete facts to substantiate our claims are provided by reliable texts and objects. For the former, we actually have specific references:
the Byzantine Tactica of Leo VI (886-912), and the Miracula sancti Oudalrici by Gerhard (973). References become much more numerous from the end of the lltl\century. As for objects, the Crecy museum and the blacksmith museum at Saumur, in France, present examples which could date back to the 9th century: these are con sidered to be the oldest known horseshoes in existence.
Studies to date - and some worthwhile syntheses have appeared on this subject - enable us to suggest that the iron horseshoe could have been developed at the same time for the draught-horses in Byzantium and the West. Initially, small shoes seem to have been used: this is illustrated on a tapestry belonging to Queen Matilda. They are similar to Chinese horseshoes, although the latter were smaller, more delicate and fixed with violin peg nails. Shoes found in Norman tombs of the 9th and 10th centuries, measured three to six centimetres in length and were made of a metal plaque two centimetres thick. A point in the middle formed the calk, fitted with two awls which were driven into the surface of the hoof. Subsequently the horseshoe clearly evolved, as it

===== PAGE 487 =====

470
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
increased in size and thickness. There are still copies of shoes for sick horses which are preserved on the doorway of Chablis church: it may be that it was not the crusaders who nailed their horseshoes to the church doors, but more probably people who were grateful to some saint for having cured their animal.
When the book by Richard Lefebvre des Noettes first appeared, there was much discussion surrounding the origin and the date of the modern horse harness. We know that this technique was based mainly on the shoulder collar, the breast harness and the reins. Improvement had certainly been made on the Antique form of harnessing, which restricted the animal's breathing, although one geographer has sugges ted that in Antiquity the horse was trained accordingly.
Many quarrels surround the origin of these devices. Lefebvre des Noettes was content to point out their appearance in the 11th century, based solely on iconographic sources, and their rapid development in the 12th century. More recently, Andre-Georges Haudricourt has observed modern horse harnesses on Han bas-reliefs of the 2nd century AD. Using a linguistic approach, he demonstrates that Europe knew of the modern form of harnessing in the 6th century. Again according to this authority, the shoulder collar only arrived in the West in the 8th or 9th century. And still more recently, in the light of archaeological discoveries, Lynn White asked if modern harnessing had not slowly evolved from Antique harnessing - an idea which rules out any contribution from the East. The three ideas are interesting, and future research should be directed around these.
Here again, whatever the case might have been, we should empha size the late appearance of many precise texts and images. It is true, however, that the modern horse harness seems to have been exten sively used during the 12th century. Above all it was used for pulling farm tools, ploughs, harrows and hay carts, rather than for actual transport. In such a way, the horse, shod and suitably harnessed, was considered superior to the ox. English agronomists of the 13th century pointed out that a horse cost more to keep than an ox, but unfortu nately we lack any evidence to judge whether this was so or not. Even if it had only limited use, the modern harness constituted real technical progress, although less so than the iron horseshoe which saw more widespread use.
Although we do not yet possess a complete history of the medieval ship, our information is at least on the way to becoming more accurate

===== PAGE 488 =====

TifE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM 471 and more comprehensive. In many cases, material data is insufficient, apart from some spectacular finds, such as the Bremen ship. It is worth pointing out that the inventory of pictorial representations of the ship is today almost complete (fig. 7).
Firstly, Nordic and Mediterranean vessels should be distinguished, the techniques of which remained very different from each other before they became combined, both in hulls types and rigging systems.
We know something of the beginnings of Nordic ships, which have been better studied thanks to the abundance of images and astonishing excavations. A stone engraving, found on the island of Gotland and dating from the 5th century, provides an image which almost matches the two boats found at K valsund in Norway, which date back to the 7th century. The largest of these boats was 18 metres long and 3.5 metres wide. The planking of the keelson had already become a true keel. The hull was U-shaped in the centre and thus ensured some stability without the use of ballast. There seems to have been a mast, although no mast appears in the engraving or was found in the excavation.
Indeed, engravings show that Nordic boats were powered by sail from the 6th century onwards.
The engraved stones of Gotland from the 7th and the 8th centuries, appear to illustrate two types of ship. Some with sterns which formed a sharp angle with the keel, and others which were curved upwards. One might suggest that Viking merchant vessels required sail power, even more so than war ships, because equipment was limited, and were fitted with a bobstay piece. Sails were often diagonally rigged, so it was necessary to reinforce them. The hulls could not be decked, and the mechandise was stacked in the middle.
The Sutton Hoo warship (England) dates from the 7th centdry.
Different planking lengths were used, denoting a greater skill in boat building. The ship is 24 metres in length and 4.2 metres wide. The hold of 1.35 metres resembles that of a flat ship so it was easy to run aground. Thirty six frames reinforced the hull, and there were nineteen oars.
Two major discoveries were the Gokstad and Oseberg, found in Norway. The oldest ship, the Oseberg, is generally thought to date from the 9th century. It measures 21.4 metres in length and 5.1 metres in width. It was built in oak, fitted with twelve planks on either side, and has a deep keel which is slightly arched in the middle. The ends of the frames are pegged on the ninth and tenth planks, the tenth plank

===== PAGE 489 =====

472
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 7. - The evolution of the ship. A, viking ship, reef sails (La Rochelle seal, end of the
1 2th century); B, viking ship with fore and after castles (Dunwich seal, 1 199); C, viking ship
(Bayeux tapestry, 1 1 th century); D, viking ship with fore and after castles (H ythe seal, end of the 1 2th century or beginning of the 1 3th century); E, advanced viking ship with bow and straight stern (Stralsund seal, circa 1 250); F, viking ship with fore and after castles (Pevensey seal, 1 3th century); G, viking ship (Calais seal, 1 228); H, primitive viking ship (Gravelines seal, 1 328) butcertainly muchearlier than the dateofthe seal; I, vikingship (Calais seal, 1 270, from an older model); J, earliest representation of the Kog (Ipswich seal, circa 1 ,200) K, Kog
(Wismar seal, circa 1 250); L, Kog (Elbing seal, circa 1 242); M, Kog (Kiel seal, 1 3th century);
