Note that all the representations of the kog show a single stern tiller.

===== PAGE 490 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
473 being thicker and L-shaped. They are fixed to the other planks according to the traditional system and lie freely on the keel. This boat had fifteen pairs of oars and carried a mast and sail. The mast was placed in the front half of the ship, on a crone which rested on the crossbeam in two sockets. The deck was on intervals between the beams and raised at the stern and the bow to make a platform. The
Gokstad ship, which was built in the 10th century, was much larger and better suited to sailing: it was made of oak wood and was 23.33 metres long and 5.25 metres wide. The planking consisted of sixteen strakes fixed to seventeen ribs. The eight lower strakes were still fixed to the ribs with lashings which passed through lugs or cleats left in each plank,
(lashings were made of fine spruce roots). The other strakes were fixed together with iron nails. Round headed rivets took care of the holding.
There were nineteen pairs of oars.
All these ships were clinker-built: the planking was made of slim planks, each overlapping the one below, and caulked with thick cords and animal hair. It was this technique which meant the planks could be so thin. The ribs were made of curved wood, and these were also very light. There was only one mast, which could be detached. Sails seem to have been first used in the 6th or 7th century; they were definitely in use by the 8th century. The mast was taken down if the oars were being used, and the rudder was a lateral type. These ships did not have actual decks, but simply crossbeams resting on props which were fixed to the ribbing. Built at the end of the 10th century, Olaf Tryggvason's Long
Serpent was 42 metres long, and therefore of the same dimensions as the galleys of the 17th and 18th centuries.
Long ships were derived from the Gokstad type and they can be seen on the engraved stones from Gotland and the Bayeux tapestry. They are also depicted in almost exactly the same way on the seals of La
Rochelle and Lubeck. The ships depicted on the Bayeux tapestry are already slightly different: one has oars only in reserve, for example.
Their shape is more rounded, less refined, with more bearing, however. The flatter filling floors accentuated the freight character of these ships, they also made grounding easier, and provided stability when the ship was on the beach. These ships could therefore be decked. Although they were less easily manoeuvrable, they were more useful. They were probably the successors to the knorr, a merchant ship which gradually became more widespread in the 7th and 8th centuries.

===== PAGE 491 =====

474
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
By the end of the 1 1th century, the various types we have just described were rapidly evolving, and the differences between them were becoming more marked.
Both ends of the ship were raised to create the fore and aft castles, which eventu;lily went beyond the width of the hull. It is possible that at first these castles were simply added to the original structure - the seal of Dunwich (1199) indicates this, for example. On the seals of
Hythe (late 12th century), Pevensey (early 13th century) and Sandwich (1238), the castles appear to be light platforms supported by a structure of lancets and semi-circular arches. The seal of Dover
(1284) marks the progress ot the permanent castle, which was now incorporated as part of the hull itself.
Sails also changed, and this brought about changes in the types of mast as well. Sometimes the sail was furled on the upper yardarm, in the Roman fashion (as on the seals of Dunwich, Dover, Hythe and
Winchelsea): the topmen were therefore obliged to sit astride the yardarm, and this is evident in most pictorial representations. The reefing points which enabled just a part of the sail to be used, appears to have been a Scandinavian invention. These are first mentioned by
Wace, a Jerseyman brought up at Caen (1120-1183) They are either situated fairly low down, as on the seal of La Roch ells - and this would indicate that the yardarm could be lowered - or fairly high up, as in a
Parisian manuscript from the 13th century. It seems that they were usually placed lower down, however (as indicated by the seals of
Hastings, Bergen and Dublin). A spar is evident on the front of a ship depicted on the seal of Sandwich, and this may have been a forerunner of the bowsprit. The latter is featured on a miniature made for Eleanor of Castille in 1279. At first it was probably only used to provide a fixing, or more likely, a return point for a pulley, on the bowline of the single sail. The bowsprit rapidly came into general use, and was to have a significant effect on the development of sails: it can be seen on the seal of Elbing (1242), Wismar (1250), and Kiel (13th century).
There have been many attempts to solve the problem concerning the type of rudders used, but these do not appear to have been successful.
Technically speaking, this was a relatively simple matter. The two lateral rudders were replaced by the stern-post rudder, like the one still used today. Technicians are still not agreed about the actual efficiency of the new technique, especially since the developments made in the sails were the principal step forward to greater manoeuvrability, just as

===== PAGE 492 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
475 was navigation with oars. Then there is the question of dating and origins. The earliest document comes from the baptismal fonts of
Winchester Cathedral, carved in Belgium around 1180. Some histori ans believe that the rudder portrayed there is a lateral one, and that the efficient instrument in fact is situated on the stern. A 13th century engraving on the roughcast wall of a church in Fide, Gotland, from the
13th century, depicts what is indisputably a stern rudder. The seal of Elbing, the oldest issue of which dates back to 1242, and a Spanish miniature of 1350 are both further proof of the fact that the stern post rudder was probably developed in the northern Seas, between the 12th and the 13th century. It was not in widespread use however, and the lateral rudder figures in many more representations until well into the
13th century. On the other hand, a mid-13th century Persian miniature actually depicts a single stern rudder. It is difficult, therefore, to reach any certain conclusion.
It is reasonable to suppose that henceforth all these changes gave rise to a type of cargo ship in widespread use by the end of the 13th century, and known as the Hanse's cog. We know for certain that this ship was derived from Nordic merchant ships, although there is less certainty about the actual nature of the ship, or its origins. The seal of
Elbing (1242) depicts a ship with an upright stem of an indeterminate shape. The freeboard is raised, and the planking is clinker-built. Also noteworthy are the presence of both the bowsprit and the sternpost rudder. The seals of Wismar and Harderwijk and other towns control led by the Hanseatic League, also depict the same kind of ship. The seal of Elbing of 1350 provides us with a clearer picture of this vessel.
The prow and stern were rectilinear up to the keel, there was a long draught, and a long lateral plan. All these suppositions have recently been confirmed by the discovery of a ship of this type in the Weser sandbanks at Bremen. This ship incorporated a number of technical innovations: could it not have been this which made the Hanse's fortune?
It is much more difficult to study Mediterranean vessels, but two Greek manuscripts from the end of the 9th century provide us with a starting point. Small crafts resembling merchant ships were used, and these incorporated one indisputable innovation: the triangular lateen sail, of unknown origin. Mosaics in Saint Mark in Venice also provide evidence of another innovation: the splitting up of sails, and the use of several masts. Some of the ships have three masts and double steerage.

===== PAGE 493 =====

476
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
In 1268, Saint Louis ordered vessels from Venice and Genoa for his crusade. The Venetian ships were 25. 8 metres high in full sail, the keel was 17.4 metres in length, 6.45 metres wide and 6.60 metres deep.
They had two complete decks and two short rear decks, with provision for cabins. The Genoese ships were shorter, and this seems to have been more characteristic of the ships at the time. Figurative represen tations often depict ships with two masts, the foremast inclined towards the prow. There is a castle on the curved deck, as well as a forecastle on the stern. The rigging consisted of lateen sails.
We know very little about the medieval warships of this period.
There were galleys with two wings which were raised in a loop-like fashion at the bow. The ram at the stern was placed well above the level of the sea. The trireme had been replaced by the dromon and selandra.
The main significance of this period is the interchange between the two techniques. The Mediterranean merchant ship arrived in Bruges at the very end of the 13th century, and in 1304, the Bayonne pirates entered the Mediterranean in cogs. From the beginning of the 14th century, the square sail and the stern post rudder were used in the
Mediterranean.
The evolution of ships was slow, but there was more continuity with older techniques. These developments were quite significant, especially in the field of merchant shipping, which from the 13th century were to provide ships much more suited to the transport of goods.
Much progress has been made over the last thirty years in the history of iron and steel making, thanks to excavations undertaken in many countries. However, uncertainties still abound: the development of the Roman world is no doubt still better understood than that of the medieval age. The following interpretation is by no means generally accepted, since it is based on the still tentative conclusions drawn from archaelogical excavations and texts.
It is very difficult to situate the High Middle Ages (the period between the large-scale invasions and the year 1000) in the evolution of metallurgical techniques, but there are two essential observations to be made.
Some historians quite logically believe that the Barbarian invasions provoked a decline in techniques. It is certainly true that the Barba rians, accustomed to their own techniques, neglected those of the countries they invaded. According to Tylecote, Saxon techniques

===== PAGE 494 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
477 began at the point where the people of the first iron age left off.
Excavations in Ireland have revealed the remains of low hearths of the shaft type in nearly all of the workshops between the 6th and 13th century. It was the same in Scotland. Some of these techniques even survived until the end of the Middle Ages.
In excavations in Zelechovicia, in Czechoslovakia, the remains of an 8th and 9th century workshop were found with twenty four kilns, A collecting channel was used to collect the scoria. The kilns were bored with loess and the interior was coated with a triple layer of bottom stone. The temperature at the end of the tuyere was about 1400 degrees. Provision was thus made for an artificial air flow. The Zerotin furnaces were built in the 10th century: they look like a dish-shaped hollow, coated with red clay. In Radetice, a forge a 13th century forge had shaft furnaces with an interior diameter of 30 cm and an exterior diameter of 130 cm. It was about 1 to 1.3 metres high. The walls were made of rock stone and there was an opening for the outlet of scoria.
The air flow was artificial.
Excavations in England have uncovered several workshops. One in West Runton near Cromer dates back to the 13th or 14th century. The hearth was a tubular or tank type with a device for the outflow of the scoria. At High Bishopley in Durham, a 13th century workshop had two high furnaces, and a kidney-shaped crucible of fired clay: this most likely had an arched hearth with forced draught (fig. 8).
Beyond these traditional structures, there was a two-pronged evolu tion. The first led towards the Catalan furnace, one of which was found in Baysdale in North Yorkshire. Built in the 13th century, this forge has four kilns all of which are of the catalan type, made of clay and stone with hand bellows and a device for draining the scoria.
The second, more interesting course of evolution gave rise to the "sledge furnace." The forge at Radetice ·mentioned earlier, was no doubt a milestone in this evolution. The increased size of the furnace for greater productivity meant it had to be built above ground, with strong masonry and a hole near the bottom for extracting the lumps.
This kiln became a permanent fixture. The apparatus discovered at Lantherthal in Germany and described by J. W. Gilles is from the 11th century. It is made of siliceous alluvium covered with a kind of basket made of boards, then fired. It was a conical-shaped well, therefore.
The hearth was covered with sandstone taken from the ruins of Roman buildings. There were two blow holes (fig. 9).

===== PAGE 495 =====

478
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 8. - Early type of blast furnace. High Bishopley (Durham) 1 3th century.
The apparatus gradually became larger and larger, and was eventu ally built entirely of stone. Unfortunately, we have no sources, writ ten, pictorial or archaeological which would enable us to reconstruct the slow course of evolution that very probably ended in the 12th century. The development of the organisation of production, which went from the collective to the individual stage, and the many conces sions granted for the development of the iron and steel industry which began in all western countries around 1140, are evidence of this technical evolution.
The last element is the appearance of the water-powered hammer which we noted above. This apparatus' productivity rate was much higher than forging by hand, and it provides ample proof of the increase in production. This new instrument appears to have been first used during the second half of the 12th century. It seems, therefore, that there was a transformation during this period, from the primitive forms of production to the beginnings of an industrial type of produc tion. This was a major transformation of techniques and similarly, of production and the use of metal.

===== PAGE 496 =====

TIIE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
479 Figure 9. - Furnace at Lantherthal (Sarre) c. 1000-1 1 00.

===== PAGE 497 =====

480
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
So much research has been done on the textile industry that it has been assumed that everything is known about the medieval techniques employed. Some elements are still obscure and not properly understood, however, but the recent work by W. Endrei has gone some way to clearing up a few of these points. The techniques employed in the High Middle Ages were virtually the same as those in
Antiquity, the same materials were used, and the same tools and apparatuses. It seems that some tentative changes were made between the 10th and the 12th centuries, but those of the 13th were more far-reaching and significant.
New textiles emerged fairly early: cotton and hemp -which required particular preparation techniques - silk, which was imported, but it is not clear where from. Cotton was used in western civilisations after the decline of the Roman Empire: it was first grown in Spain in the 8th century, and its use as fustian began in the 12th century in France and northern Italy. Silk was known during the Empire, but it was expensive and rare. The Byzantine silkworm houses of Greece and Syria (6th-8th centuries), and those of Arabia, Sicily and Spain (8th-10th centuries) made an abundance of raw material available. The sudden flourishing of the silk industry at Lucca in the 11th and 12th centuries was caused by the influx of Greek and Sicilian weavers and dyers from Sicily and the neighbouring towns of southern Italy. Hemp was hardly used at all by the Ancients, except for making rope. It was not until the 8th century that it was used for making clothes. According to Albert the
Great, even stinging nettles were still used for making textiles. Alfalfa grass was found only in Spain, al}d even there its use was limited.
Since Aristophanes spoke through the lips of Lysistrata and Ca tall us described the spinning Fates, there was no mention of any change in the techniques of spinning wool until the 13th century. The wool was still washed in urine and rinsed in water. Hemp and linen were retted in stagnant water. The woollen strands were separated by beating the wool with sticks or a whip made of ropes. Sartching was used with flax:
excavations have revealed sartching troughs that probably come from the 10th century. We have no evidence of the brake being used at this time.
Carding and combing were already separate techniques by 1300, and from 1200 onwards the elements of the techniques used today were already evident. Carding and hackling were done with a fulling bur.
Combing made carding largely superfluous, and it even dispensed with

===== PAGE 498 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
481 the need to brake flax. It had long been used with flax, for we have examples of Neolithic handle cards. W. Endrei believes that it was first used for wool in the 9th century, and its use became more widespread in the 11th and 12th centuries. The flax card depicted in the Chartres cathedral is convincing proof that the foot card was in common use in the 12th century.
The classic spindle was the instrument most often used in spinning, but it was not the only one. But we do not know enough about the processes used in the 12th century to develop this point. The distaff certainly existed before this time: there are many traces of it in Gdansk which date back to the year 1000. The first representation of a distaff figure is to be found on a manuscript of Rabanus Maurus, which dates back to c. 1023. A hook bobbin with an upper ring was used for the winding on.
Research into the products of spinning during the Middle Ages has been fragmentary and partial: it needs to be systematic and methodi cal. The warp and weft of most of the woollen serges produced during the Middle Ages were of uneven twists, until the advantages of having equal torsions were discovered by the Flemish draperers. By 1000, 70
% of materials were made with equal twists (these were known as S twist yarns), although draperers in Gdansk did not change to this until the 11th and 12th century. In comparison to Antiquity, the materials produced in the Middle Ages appear to be much less delicate. Before
1200 silk of a much finer quality was being produced. At the same time, the linen and hemp industries were in decline.
As for weaving looms, the dating is very uncertain, but it does seem that three important developments took place during the Middle
Ages: the treadle loom was introduced, and after changes were made to cotton thread, the shuttle and the bobbin were also incorporated
(fig. 10). The horizontal cotton weaving loom with treadles seems to have come from the Balkans: in the 12th century it was commonly used in Slav territory and in Scandinavia. The fustian weaving loom appeared in christian Catalonia from the 12th century - fustian is a heavy, tight cloth, the three or four treadles required a heavy sley and a solid structure.
These few developments transformed the textile industry by 1300. It would not otherwise be possible to understand the extraordinary expansion of the Flemish industry, or that of northern Europe in general. The use of different tensions for the weft and the warp, the

482

1 3th century Cambridge manuscript).

===== PAGE 500 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
483 emergence of the treadle loom, and the adoption of new cloths constitued a group of quite significant innovations.
Major changes were also to take place in the 13th century, so much so that one is tempted to suggest that as in England in the 18th century, the textile industry played a vital dynamic role in technical progress.
It was the introduction of carding with metal points that completely transformed spinning operations. This operation was called carda, the weaver's card. This technique was discovered either in France or
Flanders. It was first depicted not in the Luttrell Psalter (1340) , but in the marvellous statue - still intact - of the royal porch at Chartres, which would date it from the beginning of the 13th century. The qualitative advantage of carding with metal points resides in the regular formation of the web. At this time, the warp and weft of quality cloth were made of combed wool. The making of the weft by carding brought about a qualitative change. In response to economic pressures, this process was eventually used for the warp as well (1377 in France). The card was fixed between both the operator's knees and was pulled with both hands. The use of the brake in the transfor mation of hemp and flax was also extremely important. Everything points to the fact that this too became widespread in the 13th century, even though it only features in written sources from the 14th century.
The widespread diffusion of the spinning wheel has quite rightly been hailed as a revolutionary innovation. Some historians believe that it evolved in Asian countries between 500 and 750 BC, and that
Moslem conquests brought it into Europe during the 8th and 9th centuries. It has also been said that the wheel was probably brought into France by Spanish fustian makers during the 12th century. The problems of using a spinning wheel, particularly knotting, for example, were not immediately apparent. But one factor was soon obvious: the spinning wheel was capable of a high productivity rate, so that spinners were tempted into going for quantity rather than quality, with a subsequent decline in the latter. Legislation became necessary, and several interdictions were passed: in 1224, restrictions were imposed in Venice and northern France, in 1268 in Paris (but only for cotton), in 1288 in Abbeville, 1292 in Sienna, 1298 in Speyer and
Valencia (only for the warp), in 1305 in Douai, and 1308 in Cham pagne. Those countries which had a cloth but no cotton industry ignored the interdictions. There is no doubt that the unwillingness of

===== PAGE 501 =====

484
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
A
B
c Figure 1 1. - Spinning wheels. A and B, spinning wheels with the spinner standing (A, from the Luttrell Psalter, circa 1 338 and B, from a 16th century Parisian manuscript); C, spinning wheel with the spinner sitting (Lyons' manuscript, 1 4th century).

===== PAGE 502 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
485 the drapers to use the spinning wheel stemmed from its original function and design as an instrument in the cotton industry.
The spinning wheel was mounted on a stool, like the treadle loom.
The size of the wheel was considerably increased as was the distance between the hub and the pin. It should be noted that the wheel was turned by hand, and at this time it had no pedals, which were to be introduced later. This meant that the spinner had to work standing up and not sitting, as they did in India, even though some pictures do show spinners sitting down (fig. 11). It is possible that these modifications were designed to make working with wool with long fibres easier, as it is less resistant, but more flexible. In fact, the greater the length of the thread to be the more a strong rotation could make it straight. The increasing size of the wheel meant that it did not have to be constantly powered: it could run of its own momentum.
At the beginning of the 13th century, a primitive type of throwing silk thread was already practised. In 1221, Jean de Garlande's dic tionary, and Etienne Boileau's Liv res des metiers both list several types of winding instruments and the history of the evolution of these machines in Boulogne (between 1270 and 1280) is probably fairly representative. From the beginning of the 14th century, several docu ments in Lucca allude to the complex apparatuses then in use.
Other instruments were perfected or appeared at this time, such as the reel, first used in the silk industry. The spooling spinning wheel became widespread, and was first depicted on a Chartres stained-glass window. The toothed warping frame replaced the wall warp frame, along with the bobbin reel spool (depicted on a stained-glass window in
Chartres and on a fresco in the Kunkelhaus of Cologne around 1300).
It is possible that the toothed warping frame also came from the silk industry: it made the warping more regular, and increased the length of the part which was warped.
We noted above that the use of the treadle weaving loom was adopted before the advent of the 13th century. It is likely that the need in Flanders for an improved apparatus, capable of producing cloth of a superior quality prompted its use from an early date. Flemish cloth produced between 1000 and 1200 was the most expensive, but by far the best. Spinning techniques hardly changed, but the spinning wheel did lower the quality of the thread. Finishing techniques were much less advanced than those used in Florence, which gave to that city its wealth; wool was imported from Spain or England. The only field in

===== PAGE 503 =====

486
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
which the Flemish could claim superiority therefore, was in weaving.
The two-man weaving loom was a characteristic result of the technical revolution of the 13th century. In comparison to the narrow treadle loom, it was capable of a much greater productivity. From the 13th century, the capacity of the latter was almost as great as that of the flying shuttle loom. Ypres' Keurebook (Book of Trades), written in
1320, contains a perfect example of this. Around 1 180, Alexander Neckam gave a dscribtion of the one weaver cloth treadle loom. The two-man loom was probably invented around the beginning of the 13th century in Flanders. It was the very first machine specifically designed for mass production.
There was a great deal of progress made in the textile industry, then, and there were several stages to this course of development. Some of these changes began to occur around the 8th century, which matured during the llOO's. In the first half of the 13th century, a second, perhaps more important technical revolution began to take place with the introduction of the spinning wheel, whose capacity was at least double that of the spindle. Other factors should be taken into account:
the raw materials, their preparation, the equipment of the spinning wheel, and greater spinning skills. Furthermore, the spinning wheel was put into widespread use, and this was also an important factor. The increased productivity of the two-man loom was probably not quite double that of the older looms, but nevertheless the increase must have been considerable. If one adds to this the new products and new raw materials, one can expect that there was a very significant rise in textile production.
These were the most important medieval innovations; the others which we have not discussed are relatively marginal changes. The latter were merely substitutions of older techniques in that there was a greater diversity of raw materials than in Antiquity, and these were either cheaper, or of a better quality. An example of this is the glass workers' replacement of potassium by soda, or the use of a transparent coating, mistakenly known as varnish or glaze, in pottery. The same is true of architectural techniques, with the use of wood and half timbering for houses, and the adoption of stone for use in more important buildings. The abundance of works on architecture enables us to paint a brief picture of the evolution of these techniques. At the very most it is worth noting - insofar as there was a revolution in technical thought - the solutions required by the great technical

===== PAGE 504 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
487 projects of this period. The wooden ceilings of the great Roman basilicas were replaced by the roman vault, then the gothic vault, for example. This transformation created many problems of balance, shoring and settling unknown during the Roman period, and it brought about major difficulties with stone-cutting. The history of these tech niques is well known, all failures having been carefully noted in the finest detail, providing us with a wealth of information which we would look for in vain in more contemporary work on techniques. There are several striking similarities between the two periods which present a fruitful topic of research. Of course techniques do not suddenly emerge, they evolve from existing ones, and several historians have been able to trace the birth of the rib feature and the first rib-vault, particularly in Morienval. Everyone insists quite rightly however, on the importance of the Saint Denis basilica, which was begun by Suger in 1137 and which launched for this new type of architecture. The latter underwent its greatest development during the 13th century.
The use of hydraulic energy on a large scale, the practice of iron shoeing as well as modern harnessing, textile developments, the transformations in iron and steel making, and finally the appearance of new types of ships: this entire ensemble of techniques gave the western
Medieval technical system a very different physionomy to the one inherited from the Ancients. An innovative attitude, a type of archi tecture and way of life which bore absolutely no relation to the past, and finally a more precise notion of technical progress seem to have been caused by the establishment of a new technical system. Bacon's letter, written in the beginning of the 15th century, and Guido da
Vigevano's extraordinary project of using prefabricated material for distant campaigns, are striking examples of this.
The upheaval did not occur in all areas. Some techniques evolved more slowly, but none remained unchanged.
THE FIELD OF TRADITION
Insofar as the techniques of Antiquity were able to satisfy the needs of a more developed economy, it was not necessary to change them. At the very most, one might hope that adaptations would be made to the

===== PAGE 505 =====

488
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
different natural environment - slight modifications which would then be integrated into a technology which had undergone a more profound transformation. Of all techniques, those of agriculture are the least susceptible to change. The reasons for this technical stability are evident: they are the techniques of scattered, less well-educated populations. It is true that an increasing population required more produce, but up to a certain point, more extensive cultivation - along with the great clearing projects which began around the mid-12th century - was able to solve this problem. The progress made in iron and steel making had increased the number of tools available to the peasant classes. This was perhaps the main secret of medieval agricul ture, more so than the triennial rotation of crops which left a good third of cultivated land unused. Such practises would have been impractica ble in an area with limited land and poorer soils. We have already discussed the other factors contributing to Medieval agriculture: the more abundant water sources, for example, and the thicker forests and richer grassland.
Nearly all the plants grown in the Middle Ages were already cultivated in Antiquity. Only a few new plants were introduced, and some slow improvements made in existing species. These were not enough to create any significant changes. The emergence of buckw heat is still a mystery: coming from Eastern Europe, this cereal was first cultivated in the West only at the end of the 13th century and by the middle of the 14th century it was still fairly rare. Hops are mentioned in a diploma of Pepin the Short in 768. Some historians believe that it was probably introduced into the West with the inva sions between the 3rd and 7th centuries. For a long time it remained a secondary cereal used mainly in beer. Sorghum is mentioned in sources from the 12th and 13th centuries in northern Italy and southern
France. Nearly all the other plants are first cited only at the very end of this period, such as rice in Italy and the south of the hungarian plain, and sugar cane on the shores of the Mediterranean. Edible plants were the same; there were about ten of these - starch was mainly provided from those gathered wild. At the very most can the Middle Ages be credited with having introduced chives, which feature in Charlemag ne's Capitulare de vii/is, chervil, watercress, and horse radish. As a whole therefore, these innovations were of marginal importance.
The efforts to improve existing species were also limited and slow.
The contribution of the Arab gardens in Cordoba was the object of a

===== PAGE 506 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
489 recent study: it seems the main area to benefit was that of medecinal plants. The authors of the treatises cited above, did insist on the importance of choosing the right seeds and on selecting them in order to obtain the best possible plants - but the Latin authors were already persuaded of this. Here again medieval agriculture was characterised by stability.
Triennial rotation was certainly an improvement on biennial rotation which had been practised in Antiquity, and which remained in practice in south-western Europe. The origins of triennial rotation, the time when it was first used, and the various stages of its expansion are still vague. Some believe this was a very old barbarian technique. We know that this process was based essentially on the alternation of a winter cereal with a spring one; during the third year the land was left fallow. There is evidence that this was practised in Germany from the
8th century. It was still rare in the Paris area at the time of the Saint-Germain-des-Pres' Polyptyque. England began using it only in the 12th century. Its progress was slow and was plagued by much resistance, especially at the time of the large clearing projects when the extension of cultivated land made it Jess necessary. Its adoption led to some fairly important changes. Leaving the land fallow provided conditions for animal husbandry, but no more so than forests where several types of animal pastured. Oats were an excellent spring cereal and were consequently extensively cultivated: the use of the grain - which had been limited in Antiquity - contributed to the upsurge in horse-rearing, which was much more practicable now that new harnes sing techniques had been introduced. The widespread practice of
1 triennial rotation which reached its height in the 14th century, also contributed to the expansion of wheat cultivation and the subsequent decline of millet.
All the techniques of drainage, and improving and enriching the soils were already practised in Antiquity: the Middle Ages brought with it no significant new elements.
It was the same with cultivation techniques, Most of the techniques and instruments used in medieval times had been used in Antiquity.
Much emphasis has been placed on the evolution of ploughing instru ments and especially the plough in the Middle Ages, i.e. the simulta neous use of the coulter, asymetric ploughshare and mouldboard (fig.
12). This problem was taken up again recently, even though in fact, it is a fairly straightforward line of research. We saw that the swing plough

===== PAGE 507 =====

490
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
E
G 'k Figure 1 2. - Ploughing instruments. A, handle-stilt plough (Greek manuscript, pre 830); B, tooth plough (Greek manuscript, 1 1 th century); C, tooth plough (Greek manuscript, 1 2th century); D, handle-stilt ? with coulter (German manuscript, 1 2th century); E, plough with wheeled forecarriage and coulter (Bayeux tapestry, 1 1th century); F, chambigeswing-plough
(terrier of the Bishop of Lille, circa 1 270); G, quadrangular swing-plough (clothpiece in the
Gerona cathedral, 1 2th century); H, plough with wheeled forecarriage, coulter and mouldboard (English manuscript, late 1 0th or early 1 1 th century); I, plough with wheeled forecarriage, forked handle, prop and coulter ( anglo-saxon calendar, 1 0th century);J, same as the preceding one (North of France manuscript, 1 2th century); K, plough with wheeled forecarriage and only one stilt - the coulter and the mouldboard can be discerned (English manuscript, 1 0th century); L, same plough as the preceding one (English manuscript,
I 0th century).

===== PAGE 508 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
491 - symetric ploughing - was most widely used in Antiquity, particularly the tooth plough. There is evidence of the wheeled forecarriage, coupled with the swing plough in Rhaetia in north Italy, dating back to the 1st century BC. The Barbarians had no other instruments: no doubt they used the chambige swing plough in the North, and the handle-stilt plough in central Europe. The transformation from one way ploughing to bed forming, which quite possibly began in the late
Roman period, meant that the plough had to be inclined, and necessa rily preceded by a coulter. The latter was a separate part at first, but it was eventually incorporated into the actual ploughing apparatus. The forecarriage helped this transformation.
One-way ploughing was practised in northern Europe from the late Middle Ages but the southern regions remained faithful to symetric ploughing, mainly because of the density of the soils. The one-way ploughing of heavy soils gradually led to changes in the type of instrument used. Some excavations in Czechoslovakia have brought to light asymetric ploughshares from the 8th and 9th centuries, even though symetric ploughshares were still being used. Graphical repre sentations after the 10th century depict quite different instruments: a wheeled forecarriage plough, equipped with one or two handles and often with a mouldboard and a coulter. It seems that until the 12th century, the instrument used in the extreme north-west of Europe, therefore, was quite unique, with several regional variations. From
1200 onwards, the history of the plough is much more complex, while that of the swing plough seems to be relatively stable. The main changes in the plough occured during the second half of the 13th century. The birth of the turnwest plough is evidenced in drawings by the presence of the osier tie, designed to wedge in the coulter.
Witnessed in Belgium at the end of the 13th century, its development was slow. The same was true of the control apparatus and the joints of the front and back wheels, which enabled the ploughshare's angle to be altered, and thereby the depth at which the land was ploughed. On the other hand, the instrument developed in England was the "crooked plough," with no forecarriage. Equally, Czechoslovakian excavations have revealed that the ploughshares used at the end of the 13th and beginning of the 14th century were getting increasingly bigger.
We have very little information about other techniques. Treading corn was abandoned, along with the roller - these were replaced by the flail. The reasons for this had more to do with climate than actual

===== PAGE 509 =====

492
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
techniques. The wheat had to be threshed in a barn because the weather often prevented them from using a threshing floor.
Triennial rotation and the emergence of the medieval plough consti tute, then, the two main innovations of the Middle Ages. But in fact these merely represent adaptations to different natural conditions. This is all the more obvious with regard to the cultivation of plants which hardly changed at all - viticulture is the most striking example of this.
Ultimately, we are obliged to measure the amount of progress made - if there was any actual progress at all. It seems at first sight that the increase in agricultural production was due to having richer soils, and by extending the land cultivated rather than by new techniques.
Unfortunately, we do not have precise figures for all the different sectors of this subject, so that we cannot consider it in terms of global production and yields and establish the actual changes compared to
Mediterranean agriculture after Antiquity. In a 13th century English manual, the yields are given as follows: rye, 7 for 1 ; barley, 8 for 1 , peas, 6 for 1 ; wheat, 5 for 1 ; oats, 4 for 1. Thierry d'Hin;on's estate in
Gosnay, Artois (France), produced a yield of wheat at 12. 9 for 1. The results were probably better for animal husbandry, which was greatly improved by a larger and more well-balanced diet. Certain practises may have begun in the Middle Ages, one of which was transhumance, but this may already have been practised during the early Roman
Empire. There is formal evidence from Bonnevaux Abbey of major migrations from the beginning of the 12th century (1122). There were still several difficulties with this field of agriculture, however: there was no permanent stabling available, and feeding was very difficult during winter.
Mining had been practised by the Ancients on a large scale, and their techniques remained virtually unchanged throughout the Middle
Ages, despite the innovatory methods of organising new mines adopted in this period. At any event, it was at the end of the 12th century and even more so at the beginning of the 13th that there was an even greater upsurge in mining activity: mining charters - all of which are fairly similar - and the evidence of some excavations have provided us with some interesting information on this subject.
The texts, and the technical language of mines seem to indicate a marked German influence of which there are several examples. Apart from this, the documents available show that there was no significant technical progress made during this period.

===== PAGE 510 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
493 The development of mines varied naturally according to the nature of the deposits and products mined. There were few gallery mines.
Iron was generally obtained with open cast mining, but there were gallery mines established in Champagne and the Dauphine during the
12th century, and later in the Pyrenees. The techniques used in areas with deposits of rock salt were quite unique.
It was invariably necessary to search underground for exploitable veins, and this meant building shafts. These shafts were usually vertical, but some were oblique. They had to provide easy passage for both the workers and the products (mineral and sterile). During this time, the shafts were of modest dimensions, measuring 1 to 1 .2 metres in diameter. They were built in large numbers, probably to avoid long galleries and because of the extreme fragmentation of these devel opments. The interior was usually rectangular, making the timbering easier, or trapezoidal in cases where there was strong exterior pres sure. There was usually a small hut at ground level which protected the entrance and the winch. The depth of these shafts varied considerably:
in the copper mine at Massa in Italy, the access shafts varied from fifty to a hundred metres, so boring these shafts was a long process. The workers descended by means of long ladders fixed to the walls, and there were joists placed at intervals to enable them to rest.
The shafts opened out onto the galleries and the latter sometimes opened out onto the hillsides (Massa in Italy, Vicdessos in the French
Pyrenees). There were several types of gallery, passages or rolling galleries, galleries for ventilation or the expulsion of water, and drift tunnels. The former were of various sizes, usually twice as high as they were wide. In Massa, they were 1 .6 to 1 .7 metres wide and 1.8 metres high. The walls were usually straight and well erected. At the Salins salt mines in the French Jura, the Amont shafts, built between the 10th and 13th centuries, had a straight barrel vault 10.30 metres high. The shafts at nearby Gres, were built with groin vaults, and were 11 metres high, 53 metres in breadth and 16 metres wide. The development galleries were less well-thought out, and the others were considerably smaller.
The layout of the galleries and their gradients admit to a fairly advanced knowledge of surveying, but we know nothing more than this. Timbering was certainly used as a support structure: traces of it have been found in the Massa mine. Mining regulations prove however that one gallery would often run into another, and they frequently

===== PAGE 511 =====

494
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
collapsed. This was the inevitable result of having several more or less individual developments in the same field of deposits. There were accidents and the lives of saints and tales of miracles provide many examples of these.
The development itself was very primitive. The intersecting galleries and cross headings in the Massa mines were often very long, indicating that the miners of this time (mining here was stopped in 1350) felt very sure about the extent of metal bearing deposits both horizontally and vertically. Rock picks, pickaxes and levers, sledgehammers and wedges constituted the main equipment used by the miners. Many of the picks which have been found in Massa have a well-steeled and carefully designed head. Fire was often used to break up the rocks. The veins were developed in straight, inverted terraces in larger devel opment chambers. Pillers were erected in these chambers, and the abandoned galleries were filled in with waste.
The lighting was very rudimentary. Iron and terra cotta lamps used with oil have been found in Massa, and in Vicdessos, the workers held a lamp in their mouths which was made of a small horn containing oil and a wick.
The ventilation galleries were used quite strictly for this purpose, and no-one was allowed to use them as a passage. Anything that needed transporting was usually carried on the mens' backs, either with baskets, as at Vicdessos, or with sacks made of buffalo skin, as in
Massa. Hand-operated winches and ropes were used to pull loads up the shafts. Very little is known about how they dealt with the problem of water, but it is likely that they got rid of it by taking it up in goatskins through the shafts. This problem was more easily solved of course when the galleries opened out onto the hillside.
At Salins in France, rock salt was not brought out of the mines untreated. In fact, the water was used to impregnate the salt, and it was this water which was then taken out in vessels supported by enormous balanced poles. The salt water was then placed in heaters.
In fact, there was even less progress made in mining techniques than there were in agricultural ones. Every single one of the techniques that we have just discussed was known in Antiquity. It is even possible that in certain sectors, the techniques used in the earlier period were more advanced: this was true regarding the problem of expelling the water, for example. Mine drainage and bringing the materials out of the mine were difficult problems, and it was probably these which blocked

===== PAGE 512 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
495 development and forced mining techniques to remain on a fairly crude level.
As for prime movers, the equipment used was largely of Roman type, with few changes. Miniatures of the Middle Ages depict hoisting apparatuses which are the same as those found on roman bas-reliefs:
simple winches, pulleys and squirrel cage cranes. In fact the cranes of Bruges or Lunebourg are exactly the same as those depicted on a roman bas-relief in the Vatican Museum.
The only innovation was the screw-jack, of which Villard de Honne court has given us a perfect representation: a wooden screw, ending in a capstan and fixed at the bottom, turns by means of pivots arranged in a stringer and capital. Two inclined uprights maintain the ensemble of horizontal pieces.
The same is true of presses: the machines used in Antiquity remained virtually unchanged. The winch or screw systems were used most often, and the frequent representations of wine presses in medieval iconography are evidence of this. Some of these presses are still in use today, the basic mechanism having undergone no change.
Some texts mention other machines, but the actual type of machine and its structure is not always clear: a machine for correcting deformed structures, a machine for launching ships . . .
Metallurgy apart, fire using techniques evolved only very slowly.
The furnaces and fuels - the basic elements - were still exactly the same. Only the choice of raw materials or the processes used could have provided possibilities for minor innovations. There is very little written informations on medieval pottery, but recent discoveries should mean that we shall soon be provided with some interesting studies on the subject. Matt pottery, made with a mixture of clay and blended sands was still used, while glazed pottery, which had been so popular in Antiquity had almost completely disappeared. The main problem was making the pottery waterproof. During the Carolingian period, a transparent lead coating was first used, commonly (and inaccurately) called varnish or glaze. It allowed the decoration of the pottery to remain visible. The varnished pottery was found in the
Middle Ages, and was used for everyday objects (tiling, vases, crockery).
Glass working was rare in the late Middle Ages, or at least there are very few mentions of it in written sources. For the 11th century the monk Theophilus bears witness that medieval glassworkers were

===== PAGE 513 =====

496
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
skilled in this field, however. The same raw materials were used:
vegetable ashes (particularly beech and fern) and sand. They were able to make only small pieces, which explains the importance of stained glass windows. Not much is known about how they obtained the colours, but it is possible that at the end of the 12th century, they stopped using potassium (obtained by burning vegetables) and used soda instead. They also used large amounts of alumina and iron oxide.
According to Theophilus, the crucibles contained about 65 kg. of glass and produced small rotating blowpipes. The inadequate refining gave rise to bubbles and streaks in the glass, whilst the rudimentary stretching left lumps. In fact, the more widespread use of glass in the
Middle Ages was not due so much to technical evolution as to a change in natural conditions. In the northern regions of Europe, blockages had to be more airtight - and glass made this possible.
Sea salt was very popular in Antiquity, produced by simply letting sea water evaporate in the sun. The same techniques were used during the Middle Ages, even in regions where there was less sun, like the
Baltic, for example. Rock salt was also used, and there were salt mines in eastern France, in Germany in the region of Liineburg, in Poland at
Wieliczka, and in Transylvania. These were probably developed in the 12th century, and it might have been earlier in the case of Salins. We have already stated that the salt was generally dissolved in water. Once it had been brought up from the mines, this salt water was led via wooden channels to vats where by this time it was already more concentrated. It was then heated. The heaters were made of iron sheeting assembled with rivets (the latter were used from around the
11th century). The furnaces were embedded in the ground, and the operation took between 12 and 18 hours. The amount of heat required depended of course on the weather. The walls of the heaters were whitewashed in order to avoid melting the salt. These techniques were actually unknown in Antiquity, and must have evolved during the 10th century in western Europe.
Distillation was an ancient technique, and we saw that it was often practised by the members of the Alexandria School. The Arabs perfected the art and used it to make perfume, and it is through them that the techniques became known to the medieval West. The heart shaped Alexandrian condenser was abandoned at this time - although in some areas it was still used right up until the Renaissance - and was replaced by the modern still, equipped with a spiraled, coiled or sprung

===== PAGE 514 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
497 tubular outlet, contained in a vessel in which cold water was circulated.
The distillers of Salerno were thus able to make alcohol, one of the main bases of chemical techniques. This technique was further improved with the use of dehydrants such as potassium carbonate.
The chemical techniques of the Middle Ages, which were the subject of a valuable study by Berthelot, were very rudimentary. No rational techniques were possible as long as they were unable to determine the exact properties of substances. This meant that there were very few base products. Around the middle of the 12th century, they succeeded in producing nitric acid by distilling saltpetre, alum and vitriol. Nitric acid was the only acid to be regularly used at this time.
The most frequently used chemical products were colourants.
These, along with drugs, constituted the only activities of something which could not yet be considered as a chemical industry. Mineral colourants were used to colour glass, such as cobalt oxide for blue, copper for red and green, and manganese for purple and violet. The textile industry of the Middle Ages used the same colourants as those used in Antiquity. Blue was obtained from the hard stone azur, but most of the other colours were obtained from plants. Woad produced indigo, madder supplied the red tones, and pink was obtained from braziletto. Yellow was obtained from weld and black from nut gall.
They were not particularly skilled at dyeing, and the colours tended to be rather uneven. Alum was used to fix the dye so that the cloth could be dressed.
The main contribution of the Middle Ages to the field of chemical products was the discovery of gunpowder. Unfortunately, written sources and pictures are too vague to enable us to establish exactly how they discovered explosives. It might also have been imported from the
Far-east rather than being of local origin. Albert the Great used saltpetre only for making nitric acid, and despite various peremptory claims, it seems that Bacon did not know about gunpowder. It is quite likely that gunpowder was discovered accidentally during the various processes used to purify saltpetre.
It is a great shame that we have no catalogue of the tools used in the Medieval period. As we said earlier, a systematic study of certain iconographical themes would make such catalogues possible. The value of such a catalogue is demonstrated by figure 13, which shows some of the tools used in building. If we had such a catalogue and were able to compare it with similar catalogues for Antiquity - of which

498
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS

( 1 250-127 5); N, Regensburg cathedral (1 3th and 1 4th centuries); 0, choir of Diest Saint

Van Tyghem) there are some excellent ones compiled by Bliimner - we would probably find that they were very similar. It is thought today that the plane was used in the earlier period, but some tools were most likely developed only later, such as the bit-brace, for example. In former times only the bow drill and rope drill were known. Ancient represen tations of forging tools (from Greek pottery and Roman or Gallo
Roman stelas) are further evidence of this continuity, and the same

===== PAGE 516 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
499 resemblances can be found even in the plates of the Encylopedie. It was said recently that "All identifiable Roman tools subsist in virtually unchanged forms." Unfortunately, the lack of more precise docu ments prevents us from examining this subject on a deeper level.
Despite this general view of continuity, some historians believe that tools did change from the 13th century onwards, and that these changes became more marked until the 16th century. Which means that the end of this period represents the beginning of a new period of evolution in this field of techniques. It is difficult to be more precise about this, however. The large felling axe which was often used as a military weapon, had a head symmetrical and axial to the eye, which made it a very solid instrument. Was this the tool used in the great clearing projects? It is impossible to say. On the other hand, the two-headed axe, which was widely used during the Gallo-Roman period, disappeared around the 12th century. Roman saws remained unchanged and were used until well into the Middle Ages. It was after the 13th century - and some of the products made after this period are evidence of this - that a large number of other types of planes were added to the traditional planes with a rectilinear cutting edge. These planes were thinner, more narrow, and had a profiled cutting edge;
they depended largely on the different types of moulding. Furnitures, doors and interior frameworks in dwellings were made in greater relief during this period, and this had required particular types of tools.
Equally, different types of anvils evolved: the classic two-horned anvil became most widespread in smelting and the blacksmith's trade. These changes are evidence not so much that the present group of tools in use was being modified, but that it was being added to and completed.
If one considers the primary importance of agricultural techniques and other related techniques using tools in the Middle Ages, it is easy to appreciate the powerful effect of a slowly evolving tradition.
A global assessment is necessary, however, to get the right balance and establish just what was new about this technical system. The greater use of energy resources meant that there was a greater supply of raw materials needed in certain industries - take for example the tanning mills or woad mills which quite literally prevented "bot tlenecks. " It also meant that production processes, which had been perfected "upstream," could be fulfilled. In the latter case, there is no better example than the water-powered tilt hammer or the grindstone used in iron and steel making, which increased the production of

===== PAGE 517 =====

500
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
metal, or the fulling mill which was able to meet the requirements of a new technical transformation in the making of textiles. The use of hydraulic energy in the form of corn mills, oil crushers, mustard mills and finally beer mills also helped the food industries to cope with the rise in population.
Elsewhere, innovation meant the more complete development of natural resources which had not figured in the technical system of
Antiquity. The saw mill was useful in dealing with the expansive forests, and the richer soil of these regions was more fully exploited by the adoption of triennial crop rotation, the heavy plough with mould board, and asymmetric ploughing. In a sense, the technical progress of the Middle Ages consisted precisely in the ability to exploit natural resources which were different in both quality and quantity. On a parallel with this, the changes in transport techniques meant that this natural wealth was more widely enjoyed. Again, it was natural condi tions which prompted the development of animal husbandry, in qualitative and quantitative terms.
Climatic conditions also had a role to play in some major transfor mations, and the rain and temperature were major factors in these changes. The construction and arrangement of houses, the shape of roofs, and the structure of frameworks were quite different to those used in Rome or Athens.
These were exterior factors which seem to suggest that the technical system of the Middle Ages was necessarily going to be different. But there were interior factors which lead to the same conclusion. An equilibrium is necessary within a technical system to maintain the interaction between techniques. Bottlenecks must be prevented, to avoid new techniques losing their viability at certain stages of produc tion. It was impossible to increase iron and steel production without balancing up the different stages of manufacture. Furthermore, this upsurge in iron and steel making would probably not have occured without the increased demand for these metals, thanks to the devel opment of iron implements, iron shoeing and weaponry.
It seems that there was a period of research which began - in certain fields- between the 6th and 8th centuries, while in other areas progress had been much slower, and continuous since the early Empire. But these elements of progress became effective only when an overall balance was obtained, and when the new technical system was almost completely formed. This technical progress finally "exploded" around

===== PAGE 518 =====

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
501 the middle of the 12th century. It did not matter that certain techniques retained their traditional characteristics as long as they did not halt the general progress of the system.
The "development" of the system meant that it could progress as a whole, or that individual imbalances could be rectified - the textile industry has numerous examples of the latter. The progress made in weaving and dressing cloths, for example, made certain innovations vital, such as the new finishing techniques, and the invention and the adoption of the spinning wheel.
Ultimately - but this remains to be verified - it is possible that the major crises at the end of the 14th century arose as a result of latent tensions in the technical system. This imbalance was either between advanced techniques, or between advanced techniques and traditional ones (historians have used agriculture as an example of this, but it is also evidenced in mining, when profitable mines had been exhausted, and deeper veins could not be exploited until the technical means were found, this giving rise to a sudden lapse in production). Of course one should also take into account the intervention of other structures, and the fact that technical and social structures are very closely linked.
When discord shows itself in one, it sends shockwaves throughout all the others, and there are several examples of such crises.

===== PAGE 519 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
About twenty years ago a colloquium took place in Paris which questioned the originality of the Renaissance. Amid all the problems raised concerning this subject, that of techniques was touched upon but hardly solved: allusions were made to printing, navigation and maritime construction, artillery, and fortification, but no conclusions were reached about techniques as a whole. It is inconceivable however, during a time when so many "inventions" in the wider sense occured, that there was no revival in techniques, when revivals were apparent in all other areas of interest, such as science and literature, the arts and politics. Research since the colloquium has not demon strated the birth of a new technical system, but it has enabled us to build a more extensive schema, applying the same methods used to examine other technical systems. It would be helpful first to establish the general historical context, not in order to show the causal links, but to emphasise the degree of interdependency between all aspects of history.
The end of the Middle Ages - or the period which we call the Middle Ages - is marked by the major crisis of the first half of the 14th century, and by the prolonged depression which followed it. The movement of recovery was not uniform in time or space, or with regard to the different areas of human activity. It would be futile to claim that the course of history in the 15th century was a rigorously logical one.
The lack of adequate research and documentation makes the history· of demography a difficult subject, and recent works are evidence of this. The losses suffered varied, and population growth and decline varied considerably from one area to another, but it is generally accepted that the period from 1380-1500 was one of a very slow,

===== PAGE 520 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
503 gradual recovery. This recovery began at different times in different regions: it is clearly marked in Spain and Italy from the beginning of the 15th century, but in Provence, Languedoc, Germany and the Low
Countries it did not emerge until the last quarter of the same century.
It does seem, however, that by 1500 conditions were on the whole more favourable.
After the loss suffered in some areas - England's population fell from 3. 7 to 2.2 million - it would seem that there was a new take-off - which came early or late, and developed at an irregular pace - so that by half way through or towards the end of the 15th century, the figures were the same as those from the late 1200's. In countries which had been less hard-hit by epidemics and war, the movement was one of stabilisation. Slightly "smothered" movements would go some way towards explaining the development of mechanisation in an economy whose recovery was more clearly marked. This mechanisation was meant to attenuate the effects of an insufficient workforce, and the growing needs of a population which in fact was increasing only gradually, but whose demands might well have been much greater.
There is still much to be discovered about this aspect which is a vital factor in explaining the technical progress which occured after the epidemics and troubles.
The economic renaissance which followed epidemics and distur bances is better understood, no doubt because it has been better studied. However, there are still some aspects which remain to be satisfactorily explained. Moreover, the phenomenon of the great discoveries was incorporated in a movement which was already well underway. The economy began to recover in Italy and Spain before the mid-14th century, and during the latter half of the century in the north.
It is possible that the major discoveries of this time were in fact a direct result of this recovery: might they not also be the result of the emergence of a new technical system?
It is worth noting two phenomena related to this problem. The first one is a need for money, which was to take Europeans to Africa and then on to America. Also relevant here is the opening or reopening of numerous mines of precious metals all over Europe, especially in the central regions. It was during the 15th century that mines in Hungary and Yugoslavia were redeveloped - and this was only possible because of technical advances. Similarly, the mining discoveries in southern
Saxony and northern Bohemia are generally supposed to have occured

===== PAGE 521 =====

504
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
around 1422, in other words towards the end of the 15th century's first quarter. The economic recovery brought with it a monetary restora tion and stabilisation. This movement began in Florence in 1464, in
England in 1470, Spain in 1471, Venice in 1472, and France in 1475.
These dates should be noted carefully; it will become apparent that they are symptomatic. The other major transformation was the birth of large scale capitalism, quite different to that of the large Italian banks of the former period, who became victims of the crisis in the mid-14th century. The Medici bank, whose role in the blossoming of the Renaissance was so important, was similar in type to the latter, however most of the newly-established concerns were run by mer chants or bankers, but also industrialists. The bank established by
Jacques Coeur in the latter half of the 15th century belonged to this category, as did that of the Fuggers in Germany, or the Hochstetter in
England. As farmers, manufacturers and mine owners with abundant funds at their disposal, they assumed this role quite naturally, and were able also to encourage technical progress. It would be inter esting to add to the studies on their contribution to the intellectual aspects of the Renaissance, a study of their contribution to the technical world.
These two economic phenomena must have had a direct influence on technical evolution in that they made investment possible and encouraged innovation. Capital was made available to the most highly skilled technicians, and money was invested in new processes and new, original machinery. It was these factors which have led econo mists to consider this period as the beginning of a time of economic growth.
A parallel development was the change and evolution of ideas and an attitude of mind which gave techniques a new dimension. The sheer extent of these changes illustrates that this was a veritable transfor mation, apparent in several different spheres.
It is widely accepted by historians that the economic and political upheavals of the 14th and 15th century greatly weakened the feudal regime. The first change was apparent in social structures. As feu dalism came to be no more than a system of land-ownership, the non-landed classes acquired more freedom and mobility. The growth of towns during this period is striking evidence of this, and the latter development was in turn encouraged by the policies of the sovereigns, whose power was gradually being restored. The second or third

===== PAGE 522 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
505 generation of fortified enclosures also provides evidence of increasing urbanisation. The commercial life of the towns was revived and began to develop, and it was these towns which provided the setting for technical innovation.
These changes were symptomatic of a larger movement - the gradual formation of modern "States." Centralisation and the desire for power - the main characteristics of these new states - led to economic intervention, and a greater attention to both economic and technical affairs, in terms of both commercial and military interests.
These new political frameworks began to take shape during the second half of the 15th century, and within these frameworks a new type of politics was born, that of mercantilism. Mercantile politics was to favour the introduction of new techniques, and that meant both inventions and new techniques acquired from neighbouring states.
The initiatives of individuals or groups, and encouragement from governments further enhanced the possibilities of technical progress.
The role of individuals was mainly one of actual innovation, while the function of groups and governments was mainly to encourage the more widespread use of new processes or techniques from abroad.
It would be useful to list the policies of states in the technical domain. Governments had always played an active role in the field of armaments, but their intervention greatly increased at this time and became more systematic. Louis XI of France hired Spanish crossbow men, and inventors of machines which could dig ditches and trenches.
All over Europe, princes were paying for the services of founders - the creators of modern artillery - and Italian architects who brought with them new means of fortification, as well as new ideas on urbanism and civil construction. One only has to consider the names of the great engineers employed by Francis I: Marini, Bellarmati,
Castriotto, for example. Gerolamo Marini worked on the frontiers of Champagne (La Fere, Laon, Soissons, Epernay, Vitry-le-Frarn;ois,
Chateau-Thierry, Troyes and Joinville); Bellarmati created Langes, Dijon, Besarn;on, Nuits, Vesoul and also Le Havre. Italian engineers could also be found in Spandau, Diisseldorf, and even in England.
Ivan III of Russia is a good example of this new generation of sovereigns whose aim was to "modernise" their country. He employed an Italian coiner, Gian Battista della Volpe, and the great architect and founder Fioravanti. In 1488, a mission was sent to Italy to recruit architects, silver and goldsmiths, founders and armourers. Three more

===== PAGE 523 =====

506
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
missions were sent in 1493, 1499, 1527. Miners were recruited from Hungary in 1484 and Saxony in 1488, and printers were brought in from Denmark in 1524.
In 1450, Charles VII engaged a German, Claus Smerment to run the newly-acquired mines established by Jacques Coeur. In 1452, Henry
VI of England recruited miners from Bohemia and Hungary. Louis XI recruited German printers and Italian silk manufacturers. The list of such examples is endless; again, it would be very useful to make a catalogue of such exchanges, to note the specialised fields of each country, and to examine the role of political motive or royal alliances in the making of such choices.
Beyond this, individual initiative and unofficial movements had a role to play in the expansion of new techniques. We still need to examine the movements of technicians, official or unofficial, their successes and failures, and the assimilation of these men, their tech niques and their language. We have only very few examples of this type of study. It is true that erudite scholars have always been interested in this field of interest, and there has been some research into the displacements of printers and their setting up of new workshops. But there were many displacements like these, more modest perhaps, but which still warrant closer examination. Take for example the master salt-merchant from the Franche-Comte who travelled across Europe learning about foreign techniques of salt-mining, or the smiths from
Germany and Lorraine who travelled to Berry in 1440. Language is extremely useful as an indication of such movements: a striking example of this is the number of French words in the English vocabu lary of iron and steel making.
It would be unjust not to mention the Italian princes of the 15th century in this discussion. As sovereigns of small states, they had no real power, but they were the most remarkable craftsmen of a new spirit - the spirit of the Renaissance. These dynasties, which were founded on military strength or commercial power, (and it is possible that this might be a significant factor) tried to systematise and coordi nate different areas of knowledge, just as the Alexandrians had done.
These princes actually realised the importance of technical problems, and it is thanks to them that the material world was finally acknow ledged as an integral part of global knowledge. They gathered around them not only artists and scientists, but also expert technicians. This was to become the hallmark of this period.

===== PAGE 524 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
507 Francisco Sforza, the duke of Milan (1450-1466) called for the development of the water systems of the Po valley, and the digging of canals and diversions. He employed Fioravanti and Bartolomeo della
Valle to complete the cathedral, and build the castle which still exists today. His successor was to enlist the help of the Filarete brothers,
Bramante, and Leonardo da Vinci. He protected the Cardans and consulted Francesco di Giorgio Martini. Their library, whose catalo gues still exist, provides evidence of their endless curiosity and concern to promote humanism, science and technical efficiency. The Malatesta from Rimini were not quite as cultivated: Valturio put their military knowledge into concrete form, (fig. 1) and Alberti built their mauso leum. It does seem however, that the Malatesta considered many military problems and their contribution to this field, especially con cerning the techniques of war, was quite considerable. Urbino prob ably made the limelight with the Montefeltro family, who gathered a large collection of specialists in their court, just as they collected all the most interesting works for their library: this is strongly reminiscent of
Alexandria in the 3rd century BC. Francesco di Giorgio was among the engineers, one of the artists was Piero della Francesca, and Luca
Pacioli was among the scientists, to name the most important. Their library was perhaps the setting where the new civilization first emerged in a concrete form. Should the Medicis have a place in this discussion?
As traders rather than industrialists or military men, their interests were not naturally directed towards techniques, in the strict sense of the term - their energy was invested primarily in the arts and sciences.
Their entourage has also been studied less than those of other princes, and further study of this subject would no doubt correct this first hasty impression. These courts shared one single characteristic: they all manifested the same new spirit of the Renaissance. They may not have created this spirit, but it was they who were the first to perceive and benefit from it.
All this helps to explain the increasing importance of techniques in the early Renaissance, but here it would be helpful if we made some more precise points. Technicians on building sites or in workshops were confronted daily with substances, tools, apparatuses, and with social and economic problems, so that for them any new invention or innovation was never considered as an isolated factor. Other techni cians had a more scientific attitude towards such matters. They reflec ted on technical problems, tried to solve them rationally, and wherever

===== PAGE 525 =====

508
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure I . - Fantastic war machine (Valturio).

===== PAGE 526 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
509 possible, they attempted to link technical knowledge with other disciplines. As we mentioned above, the technical mind underwent quite a significant transformation during this period.
The rise of the bourgeoisie from the rubble of feudalism, and the princes' desire for power encouraged a change from merely specula tive research, to one which could be applied wherever possible. "The realist and bourgeois order" noted a German historian, "replaced the universal hierarchy, the abstract belief in a superior unity was aban doned and the understanding of existence became more direct and realist. The mind became less involved with the spirit, and more involved with the exterior world. " The Belgian historian Fierens made the same points with regard to the history of art: "they broke loose from medieval spirituality, and became impassioned by the real prop erties of objects, the nature of things and the spectacle of the uni verse." This interpretation made in the context of art history could apply equally to that of the history of science, and with perhaps even more relevance. Realism, utilitarianism, empiricism, and eventually the growing appreciation of experimentation and mathematics were the moods of the age.
The teaching of mathematics gained ground gradually, first of all in Oxford, then Paris, then in a number of other universities. Here again, speculative mathematics was less favoured than applied mathematics.
It was from these first elements that a pure, systematic mathematical science was born, and it is hardly surprising that some of the first true mathematicians were also technicians: the number of accountants and abacists are proof of this. Luca Pacioli lived in the remarkable centre of
Urbino, and it was here that he conducted his research into the problems of accounting. Then there was the great Dutch engineer,
Stevin. Mathematics and techniques were now inextricably linked; this was already apparent to Leonardo da Vinci: "Mechanics is the para dise of mathematics, because it is through the former that the latter is fully realised. " And he added, "nothing is certain which cannot be explained by the mathematical sciences, or by those based on mathe matics. " Descartes used the same formulas, recognising that the principle function of mathematics concerned its application to the mechanical arts. Since Antiquity, geometry had been the science of land surveyors, architects and carpenters; arithmetics was the indispensable base of commerce. There is no need to give further examples of this.

===== PAGE 527 =====

510
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
