The same was true of physics and techniques. The partial, isolated problems of physics posed by physicists at this time were a direct parallel of those encountered by technicians. They would avidly read all the ancient treatises: readers will no doubt recall the anguished search for works by Archimedes, for example. This is not the place to examine the emergence of modern physics, but it is worth noting the emergence of ballistics from the work of artillery men, and the contribution of hydraulic and general engineers to a science which had still yet to be systematised. From Francesco di Giorgio to Galileo, it was the technicians who were the most reflective. The creation of this new science meant inevitably that the technical mind and the principles involved were modified. We shall come back to this later.
The meeting (this term is a neutral one and avoids the fruitless disputes about which discipline is more worthy than the other) of a fragmented science and a group of disparate technical elements is again similar to the characteristics of the Alexandria School. Art, for example, is associated with perspective, with anatomy, the landscape, and with casting. According to Francastel:
( .. . ) the replacement of a universe of essences by an experimental universe was fundamental. The idea that the world was simply an attribute of God was completely abandoned. This conception of nature, of man as an actor on the theatre of the world, was accompanied by this extraordinary exploration of the universe which constituted the great contribution of those of the Renaissance. Consequently, they had a particular conception of invention. Invention implied the idea of a universal logic, and this explains the fact that one of the major objectives of inventors was not simply to enhance their technical knowledge of the disparate elements, but also to establish organised schemas and models of the universe. Their main effort thus consisted of selecting and segregating the possibilities of action and the theoretical possibilities of intellection offered by techniques.
This gave rise to perpetual confrontations between the various disciplines and activities, and furthermore, it encouraged, alongside the traditional channels of knowledge of the craftsmen and other practicioners, the emergence of what could now be called technology.
Progressive stages were now evident: from the single operation, the process, the tool and henceforth the machine, - all of which were disparate, perfected elements - to the group, and the organised ensemble, to the tables formulated in answer to those drawn up by scientists, and finally to the explanations and the "reasons" which supplied the general framework within which all the various particu-

===== PAGE 528 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
511 lars were incorporated. This progression of stages is again reminiscent of the Alexandria School.
We know very little about those labour workers who probably contributed, in small but significant ways to technical progress, from partial, isolated improvements, to definitive transformations and from the correction of imbalances to the creation of an entirely new system.
There is more information about those who collected particular cases and images and who from this "theatre of machines" managed to construct a more elaborate body of technical knowledge. The follow ing discussion represents an attempt to trace the evolution of their thought.
Around the turn of the 14th century, the first anthologies were produced in Germany, devoted entirely to machines used in war. They are probably based on Konrad Kyeser's work, who was almost certainly a soldier. His Bel/if ortis is dedicated to the emperor Ruprecht of Palatinate (1400-1410). It was composed of ten books each one devoted to a separate subject: chariots; siege engines; hydraulic machines; elevating machines; firearms; defensive arms; wonderful secrets; ammunition; fireworks for war and for festivities; tools and work instruments. This may bring to mind previous works already mentioned, and indeed these were the very same themes dealt with in
Byzantine encylopaedias, and even Hellenic works; Bacon also reflec ted upon these subjects, and they form the content of Leonardo da
Vinci's letter to Prince Sforza. So this work was one of a long tradition.
The same subjects are dealt with again and again: cannons, the first portable arms, assault chariots armed with artillery, mobile bridges, paddle boats, the archimedean screw, mills, drilling machines, and underwater divers. In fact, Keyser's work was similar in type to that of
Guido da Vegevano, and it was to be followed by many others even into the 17th century, all coming under the category of "theatres of machines" (fig. 2). The so-called manuscript of the Hussite War, written around 1430, revealed the same preoccupations, but was slightly different. It was not an organised work, but consisted of a jumbled collection of information written down by a technician, or a civil or military engineer, who seemed to be trying to collect as many original ideas - old or new - as he could to enrich his technical knowledge. Most of the information concerned military machinery, but this modest work did deal with some civil machinery as well, such as mills, and machines for boring wooden pipes (fig. 3), which may well

===== PAGE 529 =====

512
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
c Figure 2. - Wind traction. A, Guido da Vigevano; B, Taccola; C, Valturio.

===== PAGE 530 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
513 have been used in Nuremburg, and finally a machine for polishing gems, which was used in Venice (figs. 4 and 5).
This movement was more profound and widespread in Italy.
Brunelleschi was a contemporary of Kyeser and he was the very epitome of the technical artist of the Renaissance. He began as a goldsmith and sculptor, then he became an architect. He also invented optical apparatuses, and built machines. Unfortunately, he has not left us any written works. He was the first of this generation of Italian engineers, and his training, his preferences and interests were mark edly different to those of his German contempories.
Fontana was a doctor of the Most Serene Republic (Venice). His main interests were the natural sciences and physics, and he wrote some interesting works on these subjects. He was the author of the
Bellicorum instrumentorum fiber. The texts are punctuated with some brief remarks in cryptographical writing, and in fact this work is also a
"theatre of machines." His interests were far from being exclusively military, and he dealt with hydraulics, fountains, water conveyance and particularly automatons (fig. 6).
Marian us Jacobus Taccola represents the height of this first generation. He was a great authority in his time and became known as the "sienese Archimedes. " Like Kyeser, he was admired and imitated by many, and like his German predecessor he wrote a well-composed treatise De machinis libri X - a true "theatre of machines." He is perhaps the last representative of the Middle Ages. His preoccu pations were the same: machines of war, siege apparatuses, and the use of hydraulic and wind energy. He was not an innovator, but he knew all there was to know about the techniques of his time. He was what we could now define as "technically erudite" (fig. 7).
Roberto Valturio merely compiled the military technical ideas of Sigismund Malatesta. But of course, the originality of a work is only relative. The main interest of this work De re militari resides in the fact that it is the first of such treatises to deal with the ensemble of a technique. It is not simply a collection of different elements; an effort has been made at systematisation. The large work by Leo Battista
Alberti - philosopher, scientist, architect, and engineer - was written in the same spirit but concerned quite a different field. His De re aedificatoria, the first major treatise on architecture written during this period, was completed in 1450. Like his predecessor Vitruvius, he went beyond the narrow confines of architecture and dealt in precise

===== PAGE 531 =====

514
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 3. - Machine for boring wooden pipes (faccola).
Figure 4. - Machine for polishing gems (c. 1 475).

===== PAGE 532 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
515 Figure 5. - Lathe for polishing stones (c. 1 430).
terms with the problems of urbanism and military concerns. He was to be followed by Antonio Averlino, known as Filarete, an architect working for the Sforza family. Filarete was a skilled mechanician who had the same breadth of mind as Alberti, linking architecture with several other fields of activity. The two Fioravanti brothers did not write about their work, but they were successful architects and skilled mechanicians. Ridolfo Fioravanti, the younger, helped transport the
Italian Renaissance to Moscow. Lorenzo Ghiberti has left us a valu able notebook full of precious indications about architecture, metal lurgy, artillery and casting.
The age of the great technical treatises began with Valturio, Alberti and Filarete, but there are many others dealing with different topics.
Some of these were written before this period, and they have already

===== PAGE 533 =====

516
1ECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 6. - Machine for removing rocks from the sea-bed (Fontana).
been mentioned, such as the large treatise on agriculture written towards the end of the Middle Ages and attributed to Pietro de
Crescenzi. In the 15th century, there were treatises on astrolabes, and "books on canonry ,'' which are still lying untouched on our library shelves, and which have yet to be satisfactorily researched. All these works represent an attempt to deal with the ensemble of a given technique.

===== PAGE 534 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
517 Figure 7. - Piston pump (faccola).
One can now outline the profile of these engineers of the Renais sance: generally beginning as an artist, but an artist preoccupied with reality and the solving of practical problems. This concerned all aspects of art-painting was based on perspective, for example. Gold and silver working entailed the use of metals, and sculpting the use of various materials. Usually he was well practised in all the arts, or at least the most important. He was also an architect, and this of course made him a true technician, daily encountering such problems as stone-cutting, making buildings stable, and the problem of lifting apparatuses. As a sculptor, he also became skilled in casting when sculpture began to be cast in moulds. This inevitably led to learning about other types of casting, especially the casting of cannons. The combination of military architecture and casting cannons of course made him a soldier, and some added to this vast wealth of knowledge a preoccupation with hydraulics. The Renaissance engineer was curious about everything in a world whose evolutionary pace was accelerating so rapidly: he prac tised philosophy, he handled the first notions of a new science which he had helped construct with his own material knowledge. Between 1450 and 1475, it was his knowledge and outlook on the world which was to turn the face of the world towards modernity.
Francesco di Giorgio Martini is a perfect example of such a man; he was one of the great men of the following generation. He was born in
Siena, where, if he did not know Taccola, he certainly read his works.
He began as a skilled painter and sculptor, and some of his great skills,

===== PAGE 535 =====

518
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
if not his genius, are apparent in his work, a part of which still exists today. From 1469, he was in charge of water conveyance to Siena, then in 1477 he was engaged by the Montefeltre in a military capacity, for architectural work and fortress construction. By 1486, he was renow ned throughout Italy, and his services were called for from all over the country. It was in this way that he became involved with the completion of the cathedral of Milan, and through this he met Leo nardo da Vinci. Martini was both awed and inspired by Leonardo, who became his mentor for some time. He worked in the Papal states and as far as Naples and between 1470 and 1480 he wrote a large treatise in three parts. The first part was a treatise on architecture, very similar in spirit to those of his predecessors. However, the part dealing with fortifications was much more advanced and in fact is the first real treatise on fortification. The third part deals with mechanics, and this, again is similar to those written previously, to the extent that it is another "theatre of machines" (fig. 8). This work was translated, copied and was very widely read. Other contemporary engineers had very similar careers." Take for example the Sangallo brothers, who are perhaps the most well-known.
Technical curiosity was a widespread movement, then, and it stimulated the research of both ancient texts, such as those by
Archimedes and Vitruvius, as well as contemporary works. There were "editions" of Kyeser and the writers of the following generations to be found in all european libraries. Matthias Corvinus, the King of
Hungary, the Montefeltre, and even the popes collected all the technical works available to line the bookshelves of their enormous libraries. Once more, this is reminiscent of the Alexandria School and its Library. And indeed, now that it had become possible, many of the treatises were then actually printed: this is indicative not only of the great importance attached to these works but also of the numbers of people interested in reading them. Some of the treatises we have talked about were the first to be printed: Valturio's work was published in 1472, and Alberti's in 1485. And to complete this vast store of knowledge, older texts dealing with traditional techniques and still felt to be useful were also printed: Pietro de Crescenzi's work was published in 1471, Pliny in 1469, Vegetius was published in 1471, and this work went through eight editions before the end of the century.
The Latin agronomist works were published in 1472, Frontinus in · 1480, and Vitruvius in 1487.

===== PAGE 536 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
519 Figure 8. - Francesco di Giorgio's automobile vehicle.
These men were part of a new spirit, they had at their disposal a wealth of literature, their knowledge was more advanced. Their hopes were high, and the lure of success, glory and power all contributed to give techniques a place of the highest importance.
Leonardo da Vinci can only be properly understood if he is placed in this framework. He had the same qualities and faults as his predeces sors and contemporaries. He had the same burning desire for knowl edge, and perhaps because he was less erudite than the others, he felt the same boundless need to study every single work available, and to acquire its wisdom. He also had a passionate longing to work as a technician, perhaps, again, because he was less in demand than someone like Francesco di Giorgio, for example. Gradually, he became more withdrawn but his inspiration never ceased and if anything his ideas became more audacious as he became aware that they would probably never be realised. He became more reflective, and thus helped pave the way for the new explanatory type of technology. He sought the "reasons" behind things, and attempted to

===== PAGE 537 =====

520
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
draw general principles, the base and formulation of which he understood to be mathematical, or broadly speaking scientific. This is far more indicative of his genius than the innumerable inventions which have been attributed to him. But because he did not have the necessary elements, and because there were so many gaps in knowl edge - despite the books he read and his conversations with his contemporaries - he was unable to put into concrete form the systema tic treatises which he formulated on various subjects, and which many others had attempted before him: treatises on architecture, on fortifi cations, on mechanics, and hydraulics. His research was more pro found, he was not simply content to dole out recipes like most of his predecessors, but he tried to rationalise. With Leonardo, one can really sense this transition from "recipe" to "reason." There are some striking examples of this at the end of the 16th century.
Henceforth, a change of direction is quite evident, and Leonardo da Vinci was very present at its turning point. This is partly because he led technical research down new paths: his research into hydraulics used models very similar to the ones we use today for example. His research into gearing, and his appraisal of the workers' postures represented something quite new, and almost put technology in advance of science during this period. On the other hand, Leonardo met with several obstacles, and the sheer extent of his vision was one of them. It seems technology had become so wide that specialisation had become neces sary once more. This was all the more important now that new methods had been developed, and these were able to bridge the gaps between the various techniques.
Consequently, it would be useful to continue the line of research which we have just outlined. A study of the technical mind of the 16th century would be invaluable. Some interesting work has been done on this subject, but it is not sufficient. Further study would explore the falling into place of the new technical system, the correction of some errors and the necessary adaptations made. A brief framework of perspectives for this is provided below.
The rather hazy pattern of trends at the beginning of this period was finally brought into sharp relief in the 16th century. The population increase, for example, became more marked and more general, and economic recovery was more apparent. It is worth noting, however, that when the large influx of precious metals and the first signs of inflation began in the mid-16th century, most technical transfor-

===== PAGE 538 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
521 mations were already taking place. It is thus vital that we have precise chronologies for this period. One would have thought that inflation would have provoked people into finding ways of producing goods at a lower cost, but this does not appear to have been the case. There were many inventions in the former period, indeed, the latter is renowned for these inventions, but there were no technical mutations. Inflation caused an increase in production, but no significant changes in the methods used. A new technical system was expanding which had not yet been fully integrated in the western world.
The most difficult task was that undertaken by Leonardo da Vinci.
The rationalisation of techniques without the necessary scientific tools was impossible. Every so often there is evidence of techniques being dealt with in terms of numbers, rather than empirically, but the transition was obviously difficult, and the method was still not scienti fic. This would explain the proliferation of tables. A theory of ships, for example would have been impossible at this time; it only became possible in the 18th century, when first developed by Euler. However, carpenters did use tables for the layout and the shape of ships, with varying degrees of success. This is clearly illustrated by the papers of
Matthew Baker, a famous English shipbuilder working in the mid-16th century.
This represents a change in technical thought. In some rare cases, there is evidence of controlled experiments being conducted, rather than experiments controlled and initiated by the investigator - scienti fic experiments to replace the experience which the technician undergoes at work. At the very end of the 15th century, for example, on a beach near Naples, the artillery men of Charles VIII fired cannonballs (over a course which was marked out) through pieces of canvas placed at intervals; this was in order to establish the nature of the trajectories. There were several other experiments conducted to try and tabulate the ranges of fire. The work of Kricka, a Czechoslova kian caster and well-borer reveals similar attempts to avoid failures which were difficult to explain.
The "theatre of machines," so popular in the 15th century, emerged once more in the 16th. Much of the information was borrowed from the earlier works, and at the end of the 16th century, some designs were still being borrowed directly from the work of Francesco di
Giorgio. Two famous writers produced works of this genre. Jacques Besson, who called himself an engineeer and mathematician, was

===== PAGE 539 =====

522
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
professor of mathematics before he sought exile in Geneva. In 1569, he published his Theatre des instruments et machines, written in latin, but translated into French, German and Spanish. The Italian, Ramelli, was a military engineer in Charles V's armies before becoming advisor to Henri d' Anjou, king of Poland, the future Henri III. His Livre des diverses et artificieuses machines appeared in Italian and French in
1588. These two works are an anthology of various fields of mechanics, and often many solutions are provided for the same problem. As
Fran15ois Russo has noted, some aspects of these works were serious, and were designed to help experts, but for the most part they were an odd mixture of erudition and mechanical imagination which was applied largely for amusement and diversion.
Perhaps more interesting is the proliferation of long treatises dealing with a much broader range of techniques. It is through these treatises that one can perceive the emergence of a new technical system and its developments. They are also indicative of those sectors which have remained traditional and therefore fairly stable. Agriculture is fore most among the latter, indicated by the number of ancient agrono mists' works published - thirty-one editions were printed between
1470 and 1550. Pietro dei Crecenzi was widely published and trans lated. But these treatises were dealing with ancient techniques which were largely invalid and they were also mainly limited to Mediter ranean agriculture. It is not surprising, therefore, that the new treatises published in the 16th century reveal that techniques had made little progress since the Middle Ages: take the treatise of Fitzherbert, from England, for example, which was published in 1523 and went through eight editions before 1550, and the Libra de agricultura by
Alonso Herrera from Spain (1539), and la Coltivatione by Luigi Alamanni from Italy (1546). The first editions of La Maison rustique which had a long-lasting success, were published just after 1530. The
Theatre d' agriculture by Olivier de Serres does not seem to have progressed at all from the works of his predecessors. The most original work remains that of Bernard Palissy.
By contrast, the treatises on mining and metallurgy reflect consider able progress. They range from the short Bergbuchlein (published in
1505, with five more editions by 1540) to the long, "modern" treatise by Georg Agricola, De re metallica (repr. in Basel, 1969), which deals with both mining and metallurgy, and finally Pyrotechnics by the
Italian Biringuccio who dealt exclusively with metallurgy. The

===== PAGE 540 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
523 treatises on chemistry were similarly successful. The Liber de arte distillandi by Brunschwygk, was published in Strasbourg in 1500, and this was widely emulated. A treatise by an anonymous author on dyeing, Mariegola dell'arte di tintori appeared in 1510. The first great treatise on dyeing was written by Rosetti and published in 1548 in
Venice. There were many treatises on architecture, all of which provide evidence of the new style. The same is true of fortifications:
the first main work was written by Albrecht Diirer in 1527, and this inspired some worthy imitations, by Battisto della Valle, for example, whose work went through ten editions from 1524 to 1558, and the work by Erard of Bar-le-Due published at the end of the century. We mentioned above just how useful a full and detailed inventory of such publications would be to historians of this period and subject.
These works are interesting in that they caused some changes in the processes used at that time, and they are a testament to the technical transformation which was taking place. The publication of new editions and their continuing importance until well into the 17th century reveals not only that contemporaries were conscious of this mutation but that they believed no other transformations would take place. They sought less to innovate than to perfect the current system and extend it to its limits. Their only apparent objective was to expand the use of mechanisation, and to apply it to all fields of activity.
THE CLASSICAL SYSTEM
It is probably the concept of mechanisation which best characterises the technical system of the Renaissance. We may smile when we con sider the mechanisation of this period in comparison to our own, but considerable progress had been made since medieval times. The only obstacles to mechanisation during this period were the materials available: gearings and transmissions were difficult because all machines were made of wood. The impossibility of constructing correct assemblies meant that after a while the machines became very rickety this limited their power and reduced the speed at which they worked.
The discovery of the crank and connecting rod system, which converted rotary into reciprocal motion was at the basis of modern

===== PAGE 541 =====

524
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
mechanisation. This transformation of movement was unknown in Antiquity and the Middle Ages, at least there is no mention of it in any of our written or pictorial sources. And if they had known of this concept, the mechanisation achieved during these two periods would have been considerably more advanced. This mechanism had several disadvantages. Mobile assemblies were difficult to construct from a strictly material point of view, and friction absorbed much of the machines energy. There was another difficulty which technicians and scientists did not fail to notice: the passage between the two dead points at each end of the diameter of the connecting rod's extension.
The first relatively crude example of this mechanism is found in Kyeser's manuscript: it is used on a grindstone, powered by hand; the crank being an extension of the arm. The development of this mechan ism can be traced by examining the engineers' notes which we cited above. As they developed this system, they also tried to solve its drawbacks. The flywheel was incorporated to sustain movement and nullify the effect of the dead centres. Francesco di Giorgio came very close to conceiving of the ball and chain governor; the control of the apparatus was still being done by hand.
This apparatus was not actually used for quite some time. In all the earlier representations, the crank and connecting rod system was used only in hand-mills. It was eventually used in some pedal machines such as the spinning wheel, which according to all figurative representations was powered by hand, and from thence to all machines powered by pedals, like the grinding mill, for example. Later, the system was adapted for larger machines - particularly those powered by mill wheels whose motion was circular and which therefore required the conversion of motion - such as hydraulic saws and suction pumps.
Sixteenth century works reveal that the expansion of this new mechan ism was progressive but slow, on account of the difficulties in assembly.
We believe that the crank and connecting rod is one of the most important inventions of this period because all of modern mechani sation was to emerge from this system. The first pictorially represented pedal spinning wheel, which was in fact the first pedal lathe, is featured on a manuscript from 1470. Some drawings of hydraulic saws by
Francesco di Giorgio appeared at about the same time. But it was not until the mid-16th century that Agricola depicted the first suction pumps powered by a water wheel with the crank-connecting rod system. But difficulties with the construction and running of these

===== PAGE 542 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
525 machines reduced the machine's capacity. The expansion of this mechanism was slow, but it gave the machine pride of place in technical progress.
The development of mechanisation prompted the need for more energy. The main resources of energy were the simple, relatively feeble water wheel, and wind energy which was hardly used. No other mechanical form of energy was conceivable at this time, and the claims about attempts to create a steam engine should not be taken too seriously. Leonardo's research and Branca's attempts to construct a steam turbine in the 17th century were merely expressing what had been known about the power of steam since Hero's aeolipile. Branca's machine did actually work on a small scale. It worked in the same way as roasting jacks, which were powered by hot air. The apparatus devised by Leonardo da Vinci which incorporated a cylinder and piston, could not work as long as the effects of condensation were still not properly understood. The concept of the void was denied at the time, and that of atmospheric pressure was unknown. No new "con verters" were created, therefore.
The only way of increasing the amount of energy available, therefore, was by perfecting existing machines. It is in this light that
Leonardo's much more interesting research into mill wheels should be appreciated, together with his considerations on the most efficient shape for the vanes and the angle at which they hit the water. This type of research reflects the beginning of a technology of the mill wheel - the first of its kind. It seems however, that the course set by the great
Florentine was not pursued by any of his contemporaries or immediate sucessors.
There has been a lot of discussion about horizontal mill wheels, so much so that there is really no need to go into it any further at this point. It would simply be worth noting here that the passage from the wheel with vertical axles to the water turbine was a long one: these two machines were very different. There were some similarities - in both machines the wheel is placed under the water in order to prevent rotting and the effects of frost. The difference lies in the fact that the water acts on both sides of the wheel, but since the hydraulic turbine is enclosed and isolated from the water, and if the flow is correctly channelled, the water acts on only one side of the turbine. Moreover, it was necessary to include a suitable device which would put the vanes under maximum pressure from the water.

===== PAGE 543 =====

526
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The first figurative representations of mills with horizontal wheels are to be found in the so-called manuscript of the Hussite War (c.
1430). This was simply a horizontal wheel, it was not a true hydraulic turbine. It was later declared that this type of mill was introduced during the 16th century just about everywhere, from Bavaria to the
Pyrenees. Francesco di Giorgio went slightly further than this: his drawings provide a link with the origins of the hydraulic turbine.
Almost all the elements were there: the horizontal wheel, the inclined blades, and the tube with appropriate fitting through which the water was conveyed to the wheel (fig. 9) . It seems as if the wheel is isolated from the current, but we will never be entirely sure. This technique was not widely used: it seems that its first application was the installation of the Bazacle mills near Toulouse (which are beautifully illustrated in the Encylopeedie) in the 18th century.
It seems that the creative imagination was more effective in dealing with the windmill. The main problem was to cater for the changes in wind direction so that the blades would move continuously. The only solution for this in the Middle Ages had been to build wooden mills on enormous tripods. The stone mill first appeared in the 15th century, it was much more solid, and the roof turned on rollers. Of course, this had meant that the interior mechanisms had to change. The new type of windmill appeared around the beginning of the 15th century, although we cannot be sure about this. The tripod mill alone features in manuscripts from the end of the 14th century to the mid 15th century. Now that the main body of the mill was actually fixed and stationary, it could be used for purposes other than simply grinding wheat. And in the early to mid-15th century, windmills were used in
Holland to power archimedean screws which were used to drain low-level land. Towards the end of this century they were used for sawing; and this again was only made possible with the crank and connnecting rod system.
The great expansion of the windmill came in Holland during the second half of the 16th century. It was only in the 17th century that it became more widely used in other countries. But in Spain in the mid-16th century, Ortiz was already advocating the extensive use of windmills in regions with few or unsuitable rivers. Readers are no doubt familiar with Don Quixote's struggle against windmills, which were seen as innovations running against the traditional order of things.

===== PAGE 544 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
Figure 9. - Using hydraulic power. [above[ The earliest example of a turbine by Francesco di Giorgio Martini; [below[ Archimedean screw and noria by Leonardo da Vinci.
527

===== PAGE 545 =====

528
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
A parallel development was the attempt to reduce the absorption of energy by the different gears which naturally were made entirely of wood (figs. 10 to 12). Once more it was Leonardo who was the first to begin this line of research. He sought to reduce the excessive amount of friction, and the consequent wearing of parts and erratic functioning of the machine. He may not have solved the problem satisfactorily, but he did also consider detailed problems, like the design of the teeth, for example. He even arrived at the idea of trapezoidal gears. Besson, whom we mentioned earlier, provided us with the earliest pictures of the irregular gearing used in lathes. Again, these were made of wood, the use of which made the development and perfection of movement transferring parts very difficult.
There is no doubt that the new technical system showed itself most clearly in agricultural techniques. Agriculture remained one of the essential activities of western Europe, and it was a very traditional one.
Indeed, it was precisely when dealing with the problem of this sector that the medieval technical system had been at its most ineffective. We
Figure 10. - Types of gearing (Leonardo da Vinci).

===== PAGE 546 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
529 Figure 11. - Using the endless screw (Francesco di Giorgio).
Figure 12. - First attempt at regulation (Francesco di Giorgio).
also noted the very slow evolution of agronomy, illustrated by the continued reliance upon medieval or even ancient works. We are still dealing with recipes rather than reasons.

===== PAGE 547 =====

530
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The techniques of actual cultivation and crop rotation systems hardly changed at all. At the very most, the heavy plough was more widely used, at least in areas of dense soils. The expansion of metal lurgy caused an overall improvement of tools, more of which were being made of metal. It is possible also that they were more efficient in looking after the areas of grassland: irrigation and dressing of land were the methods generally used. This also contributed to the devel opment of animal husbandry.
In fact the greatest change came about with the introduction of new plants, but even so some of these were first used only towards the end of this period. These new plants came not only from the newly discovered world of the Americas, but as well, if not more so, from the creation of edible, cultivable varieties from those which hitherto had only grown wild, or were hardly edible. Historians have always been seduced by the contribution of the New World to such developments, and conse quently not enough emphasis has been placed on the botanic revolu tions taking place in Italian nurseries, for example, which in some cases acted as transit centres. The growth of botanic science was certainly important, but it occured after these discoveries, in the mid-16th century. In some cases, the American contribution has long been the centre of heated discussions, and the issues are far from being closed
Й today. Some plants were definitely of American origin, while others seem to be a hybrid of American plants and varieties already known in pre-Columbian Europe. Maize is an example of this, and is known to have existed in Andalusia from 1500, and Portugal from 1515 onwards.
Some historians believe that maize had been cultivated in Europe since before 1492, but it was hardly profitable at this time, and probably also hardly edible. This means it would have been an American variety which was first cultivated on a wide scale. The same doubts persist concerning other plants. The humanist scholar, Valeriano planted har icots in Belluno, Italy, but it is not clear whether or not these came from
America. In fact, the only product that we know for sure came from the New World was the potato plant which according to many sources was introduced towards the end of this period. As a whole, then, it seems that the American contribution was fairly limited. It did not match the number of important plants sent by western Europe to the New World, which were to become a future source of great wealth to this area- such as coffee, sugar cane and certain cereals which had been unknown there before the arrival of Christopher Columbus.

===== PAGE 548 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
531 Italy's achievements in developing and expanding the numbers of edible plants was probably a more important factor. Very little is known about this subject, which warrants extensive research. As far as we can gather from the information that we do have, it seems that in
Italy two main channels converged. The first of these was the contribution from arab countries and from the East in general. Plants which had been grown or developed in these areas were imported into
Italy either from the south, or from Venice, the two regions in contact with the eastern basin of the Mediterranean. A few examples of what would be a very interesting inventory will suffice here to demonstrate the importance of these plants. The artichoke - the thistle head - was gradually developed by the Arabs before it was introduced to the table. It was first introduced into Naples in 1466 from whence it passed into Florence in 1466, and Venice in 1480. It crossed the French border during the beginning of the following century. These dates are taken from well-known documents, but they are not necessarily completely accurate. And the same goes for the melon, which is supposed to have been known in Avignon by the end of the 14th century. Then there is the cauliflower, which came from the East in the 16th century.
Other plants came from different channels, such as Portugal, for example, and this was no doubt due to their importance as a maritime nation and especially their activities off the African coast: they prob ably introduced cloves and definitely cinnamon, which Vasco de Gama brought back from the Moluccas in 1498.
The other main channel was actually Italian. Many plants were improved or domesticated in the Italian botanic and kitchen gardens, and this aspect requires much more rigourous research. Our infor mation on this subject is particularly scanty, so we can only talk in general terms. It seems that the cos lettuce and other lettuces already extensively cultivated at the beginning of the 15th century, the marrow, pumpkins and aubergines were first grown in these gardens.
Other plants were developed - the carrot was one of these, which previously had been so woody as to be practically inedible, and the beetroot was developed from changes and improvements made to beet. The same progress is evident with regards to fruit. During the latter half of the 14th century, strawberries which had previously been gathered from woods, were grown in gardens for the first time.
Raspberries and currants were also now grown in gardens.

===== PAGE 549 =====

532
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Other plants were also far more widely cultivated in the course of the following centuries, but we know very little about them. At the end of the 15th century, some species of plants were cultivated which were to assure the later development of artificial grassland. Alfalfa had been known in Ancient Greece, and in the Middle Ages it returned to
Venice then up to Lombardy in the 15th century. It finally made its way into France during the early 1500's. In a sense, this was a rediscovery.
The same was true of sainfoin, and red clover was introduced slightly later.
Other developments reached their limits at this time: buckwheat was now known in all of western Europe, and hops continued to advance towards the West, gradually replacing barley. Older plants made way for these new ones: sorghum, panic grass, and what was left of millet disappeared in the wake of maize. Our task would be much easier if we had tables and maps to hand but these have yet to be compiled. They would enable us to measure precisely the extent of these botanical changes, which appear on the surface to have been very important indeed.
Furthermore, the significance of these changes is probably greater than they appear superficially. Food now consisted of a much greater variety of vegetables, salad vegetables and fruits, which enhanced the dietetic balance of the peoples of these regions. Unfortunately, there are some aspects about which we know very little, such as animal rearing.
The Renaissance does not seem to have brought with it many changes in the forests, and at the very most they may have introduced a hardier poplar. The main concern of the centralised states was to organise a more efficient use of forests - insofar as wood was still the primary resource. The first major reforms were passed in France at the end of the 14th century. In this same country, the organisation and administration of forests (i.e. , their surveillance) were dealt with in the great royal ordinances of 1520 and 1544.
Nor were there many changes in fishing. Large shoals of cod had been discovered during the voyages made to America, which were apparently fished by the Basques - among others - from the middle of the 15th century. Guillaume Beukels is reputed to have invented the process of curing and barrelling of herring around 1447. The fish were prepared, salted and put in barrels on board the ships before they reached the ports. This greatly increased the ultimate yield. But this

===== PAGE 550 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
533 was also to introduce important changes in the activites of coastal peoples, for this work had been their livelihood.
Animal husbandry did not develop a great deal, although some botanical inventions probably improved it slightly. Jean de Brie's
Treatise on shepherding was written in 1379 and printed before 1500.
Nothing is known about the evolution of domestic breeds, but two new animals were introduced into the farm yard, the guinea fowl, intro duced in the 16th century from Guinea, and the turkey appeared in the
15th century. Large amounts of game were served on the tables of the well-to-do, brought in by hunting. Other sectors of the population ate little meat, and usually this was pork. Again, our sources for this subject are very thin on the ground.
One cannot say that just because the Renaissance brought with it significant changes in certain areas of human activity, the same was necessarily true of agriculture. Even though current agricultural techniques had been in use for centuries, they were still capable of being developed. And according to Oliver de Serres, changes did take place, especially in the types of tools used. More progress was made in botany with the introduction of stocktaking, and more important perhaps, with the establishment of more botanical gardens: the one in
Padua was created in 1533, that of Sologne in 1567, and Leyden in 1577. These botanic gardens evolved considerably; this is illustrated by the change in character of French botanical gardens, for example.
In 1540, In Touvoie, near Le Mans, the explorer Belon built a garden for the bishop Rene du Bellay. Guillaume Rondelet established a garden near Montpellier, and the apothecaries of Paris established the jardin des simples in the rue de l'Arbalete. These gardens were designed primarily for curiosity's sake, or for the growing of medicinal plants. The first large botanic garden meant for wider purposes was built in Montpellier in 1593, in response to an edict of Henri IV. In
Paris, the Jardin des Plantes was founded in 1626 and completed in 1635. Its original function was to provide medicinal plants, but it eventually became a garden for experimentation and demonstration.
In fact, the main laboratory proved to be that of the common vegetable garden, which first emerged in the 15th century; previously vegetables had been grown in fields. Vegetables were cultivated in kitchen gardens with more attention and care, the work was more intensive, and effectively these almost became laboratories of plant research.

===== PAGE 551 =====

534
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
One curious fact is that the contemporaries of this period were actually conscious of the stabilisation of farming techniques in all its aspects. The sacrosanct virtues of experience still reigned, and inno vations were incorporated only with difficulty. It would have been possible to give up digging the vineyards by hand and use light ploughs instead, but this technique was first used in the Bordelais only in the
15th century. It is also possible, therefore, that even the diversification of instruments and tools took place only very slowly. A systematic list of the figurative representations of tools would be very helpful on this subject. At any rate the general impression at the moment is that although instruments were developed, particularly by a more wide spread use of metal, there was no actual increase in the number of different types of tool used.
Written sources bear this interpretation out quite clearly. At the end of the 16th century, Olivier de Serres - who has been mistakenly called an innovator - recommended the continued use of existing tools: "it is best that the ploughshare remain unchanged, so as not to incur the losses which always result from a transformation. " This phrase is typically representative of the prevailing attitudes. On the other hand,
Bernard Palissy noted that while the military engineers might have succeeded in improving all techniques in this field, from fortifications to armaments, it was apparent that farming techniques and imple ments held no potential for man's ingenuity, and so were looked on with disdain. The sometimes arbitrary transposition and simplification of traditional techniques meant that the practitioners of such tech niques and those who advocated the use of them sometimes lapsed into error. Olivier de Serres advocated, as did Charles Estienne in La
Maison rustique, the general use of the plough, and rejected the idea of the swing plough, thus failing to take into account the different nature of the terrain, the density of plant layers and topography. This attitude was simplistic, and was not without some major problems.
Here again, progress was made without the help of the theoreti cians. The only notable development in ploughing implements was the introduction of the stock which was probably first used by artillery men. The first, remarkable example of this appears in a manuscript of the Duke of Berry (France) in 1404. The two-wheeled forecarriage has a support made of a forked stick, which can be adjusted by means of a dowel (peg), and used in five different positions, and on which is placed the leg of the plough. The closer the dowel to the coulter, the

===== PAGE 552 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
535 less deep the ploughing. A manuscript by Gilles de Rome dating back to the beginning of the 15th century, depicts another type of adjustment device.
As far as the other operations involved in cultivation are concerned, such as the use of the roller, or of the harrow with wooden or iron teeth
Olivier de Serres and Estienne simply reiterated what had been known for centuries.
Insofar as some historians believe that the medieval farming system had arrived at its limits and that the recovery only began with the onset of a new technical system, it seems that farming techniques during the early 15th century hardly changed. Some variety had been introduced into agricultural production, but did this mean that there had been no quantitative increase? This, was still not really necessary at this point because the rise in population in fact had served only to compensate for the severe demographic losses of the 1300's, and this was so until the end of the 15th century. On the other hand, it would be worthwhile studying 16th century agriculture in that there was a significant rise in the number of people during that period, and this coupled with the increase in individual consumption meant that farming techniques were forced to change. Historians have shown little interest in this aspect of history, probably because of the lack of sources, but a study of this question would lead us to a whole new avenue of discovery, from which the history of techniques has everything to gain.
There was a profound transformation in the use made of the underground. There has always been a tendency to represent Agrico la's famous treatise as a work dealing with medieval techniques, but on the contrary, his work epitomises the characteristics of the Renais sance. We mentioned above that many mines had gradually fallen into disuse by the end of the Middle Ages, but work was resumed in many of these towards the mid-15th century. Crisis and recovery were generated by many factors, one of which was the influence of tech niques. Once the superficial veins had been fully exploited, for example, the development of deeper veins posed many difficult techni cal problems such as the extracting of waste and minerals and water drainage, to name the most important. Because of the lack of instru ments or of the necessary machines, this work was either impossible, very slow, or very expensive.
Since this mutation also depended on economic factors, its evolution occured in stages rather than all at once. The retable in Annaberg, a

===== PAGE 553 =====

536
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
town in Saxony which was situated right in the middle of the mining district discovered around 1425, and which was painted by Hans Hesse a century afterwards in 1520-21 , reveals that the techniques used were still relatively crude. Galleries were built on a wooden base, winches were powered by hand, and ordinary pick-axes were used for extracting the minerals. The same is true of the manuscript of Sainte
Marie-aux-Mines, painted by Heinrich Gross around 1530: man powered winches were used to raise the minerals, but the mining wagon, which rolled on wooden rails was also used. Agricola's work represents a very sudden transformation: the apparatuses are much more advanced.
Just as botany served agriculture, so geology, or at least the first elements of geology, were to prove indispensable for the research and development of mineral veins. Chance was certainly a factor in this as well: one only has to consider the number of discoveries attributed to miraculous intervention. The divining stick was henceforth no longer used - if it ever had been in the first place. Both the Bergbuchlein, the first editions of which date back to the beginning of the 16th century, and Agricola's work reveal the slow gradual evolution of geology. It was still incomplete of course, but they were already able to use it for prospecting.
All the manuscripts mentioned above, from those of Annaberg and Sainte-Marie-aux-Mines to the Kuttenberg manuscript (or Kutna
Hora, in Bohemia - the manuscript is now in Vienna), that is, all those prior to Agricola reveal evidence of very traditional techniques being used, to bore wells, for timbering, for the layout of the galleries, and for the ventilation wells. It is traditionally thought that powder was used for the first time in 1527 in Chemnitz. It is also possible that they used compasses.
The technical revolution which is apparent in Agricola's work rests essentially on a more advanced mechanisation which began around th'e turn of the 15th century. This is also revealed in the evidence found in the Polish salt mines of Wielicka, which underwent a marked devel opment in the first half of the 16th century. Mechanisation was developed at certain key stages of the mining process.
The extraction of water, which may have been the root cause for the eventual disuse of many mines, was achieved using machines which may have been devised before this time, such as the chain and bucket systems: there are examples of this in Agricola and in the Salins

===== PAGE 554 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
537 tapestry which appeared slightly earlier. The mechanism was powered by a horsewhim or other types of treadmills. But Agricola provides evidence of another machine - the suction pump powered by mill wheels and incorporating the crank and connecting rod system. At deeper levels, they had to use several pumps one above the other, linked by return systems to a single energy source. As long as the piping was correct - and Agricola was very interested in this aspect - the means of water drainage were much more effective than the ones used in the past, although since this machinery was made of wood, it was still relatively inefficient.
There were developments in the type of lifting machines used as well. The Kuttenberg manuscript, for example, shows a treadmill being used instead of hand winches. Agricola provides evidence of a somewhat extraordinary water machine (fig. 13). It is made of two wheels fixed together, whose blades are inclined at opposite angles.
The water used to power it is supplied from a reservoir with sluice gates. It was designed so that the enormous winch could be turned in one direction or the other, simply by opening one or other of the gates.
The three-wheeled wagons running on wooden tracks and known as "dogs" were now used for all transport within mines. Agricola has provided us with some very precise drawings of these machines. It is also possible that the wheelbarrow was more widely used.
It was this more advanced mechanisation and the effective use of hydraulic energy which created the upsurge in the mining industry. As we said above, the picture painted by Agricola should be qualified: the developments were only relatively advanced, and even these devel opments were not immediately used everywhere. It was only in 1531, for example, that the suction pump was used in Liege. Apart from the problem of water, other machines were only necessary if the tech niques developed as well. There was no point in having more powerful lifting machines if the techniques used to extract the products were still those of the Middle Ages. Again, this needs closer study - if powder was used during the 16th century, then there was some justification in using lifting machines. In other words, the idea and application of such new techniques presupposes analogous changes in parallel techniques.
It seems that most of these new techniques were first introduced by the miners of Bohemia, Saxony and even Liege. It is for this reason that they were so much in demand in western Europe, from France or
England, and even from Russia.

===== PAGE 555 =====

538
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 13. - Elevatory machine with reversible motion (Agricola, 1556).

===== PAGE 556 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
539 There is another element of prime importance. We are now dealing with large scale exploitations like that of Jacques Coeur, and no longer with disparate individual enterprises. This meant that the organisation was more rationalised, and that much more powerful means could be employed. The organisation of mining production was an important factor in the development of techniques, and this was evidenced by the manuscript of Sainte-Marie-aux-Mines, and later by Agricola. We noted earlier the importance of a particular type of capitalism regarding the passage to a new technical system, and the mining industry provides possibly the most striking example of this.
The developments in metallurgy are perhaps most representative of the new technical system. Here again, research so far has been inadequate. The dating is uncertain, and there are still many problems to be solved. We have nonetheless attempted to establish a schema for the emergence and development of new techniques, even if some of its elements are mere hypothesis which still need to be verified.
By the end of the preceding period, iron was probably being produced in blast furnaces which already constituted an advance on the old open hearths. Nevertheless, research and excavations in
England, Bohemia and Hungary have shown that the old style furnaces were still used for some time in certain areas. The blast furnace was a large permanent apparatus. It could only be made larger if the air-supply was increased, and there were two major problems in this respect: the problem of the air-flow, and of transforming the operation so as to produce pig iron as opposed to ordinary iron. We shall not dwell too long on these unverifiable points.
There are hardly any written sources before the 16th century and the figurative representations are virtually non-existent or ambiguous.
The dating of machines which we do actually still possess is also very arbitrary. There was no way of obtaining more air other than by using hydraulic energy. A text dating from 1323, from the Brie region provides us with the first evidence of this technique being used, and there are several more examples of this from engineers' notes dating from the mid-15th century (fig. 14). But the introduction of hydraulic air-pumping does not necessarily entail the production of pig-iron at the end of the operation.
It is very likely that the combination of new elements, the hydraulic air-pumping and the larger dimensions of the furnaces led to the accidental production of pig-iron, which was then probably put on the

===== PAGE 557 =====

540
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 14. - Water-driven bellows (faccola).
scrap heap. If the production of pig-iron was deliberate, that meant that they knew how to refine the latter in order to produce pure iron.
The first method was relatively easy, but the second was not without its difficulties. But in each case, some of the inspiration may have come from related techniques: we have already examined the importance of these technological transfers. Both bronze-smelting which was widely practised at this time, and copper-refining may have been very reveal ing in this respect.
It seems that the diffusion of the blast furnace, the new, essential element of the so-called "indirect" process can be situated in the last quarter of the 15th century without too many problems, and in fact, this does actually concord with other technical transformations. Smel ting iron was probably first produced deliberately in Liege. The blast furnace was probably introduced to the rest of France from here, along

===== PAGE 558 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
541 with the "wallonne" refining method, and by the end of the 15th century it was in widespread use in Lorraine, Normandy, Champagne, and Nivernais. There is evidence of its being used in Sussex, from 1496
(fig. 15). During the 16th century, it continued to spread throughout Europe to the rest of France and Western Germany (Agricola had not heard of it), then into southern and eastern Europe during the second half of the century. In some areas, such as southern France and Spain, for example, it was not adopted until the modern era. It is difficult to understand why, but they continued to use the direct process known as the Catalan method until this time.
The adoption of the blast furnace completely changed iron and steel production. There was now a new product involved, cast iron, which was to force other changes. The production of cast iron balls was to
Figure 1 5. - A blast furnace (16th century English drawing).

===== PAGE 559 =====

542
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
cause a revolution in artillery, and similarly, cast iron was soon being used to make the cannons. Many other objects were made of this metal, from firebacks to cooking pots. We need precise inventories listing cast-iron objects to appreciate the full value of this innovation.
With larger apparatuses, production could be significantly increased.
And finally, the iron produced by refining was much softer than that made by the older method, and therefore easier to work. Here again, a list of the objects made of this new type of iron in the 16th century would be very useful in measuring the increased use of this metal.
Research has hardly begun on this topic.
The increase in production and the use of materials which were easier to work risked causing a bottleneck during the stage at which the metal was transformed. The main instrument used in forging was the hydraulic hammer which had been incorporated during the preceding period. But both its structure and its function were now ineffective.
Therefore is it not astonishing that we witness, in this domain and during the epoch which interests us here, considerable development in the mechanisation of iron and steel making methods. In fact we know very little about the evolution of the hydraulic hammer. It would appear that the Middle Ages only knew the so-called terminal hammer, the smallest hammer with the least output, at least for certain types of work. It is possible that during the Renaissance a heavier tool was developed, the so-called lateral hammer where the attack of the cam was operated between the pivotal axle and the head of the hammer, producing less stress on the shaft and subsequently enabling the weight of the hammer to be increased. It was henceforth possible to forge much larger pieces (fig. 16).
On the other hand, a series of instruments was to come into play, the development, dates, and place of origin of which we know very little.
They were powered by hydraulic energy, which here again seemed to be the mainstay of advanced mechanisation. Let us try to review these tools, indicating all that we do know about them.
The rolling mill, composed of two cylinders turning in opposite directions to each other, is one of the instruments fundamental to modern iron production. The hot bar of iron is flattened and tightened by being continually passed between the two cylinders which moved progressively closer together. The operation could be continued for some time in order to produce sheet metal, which was also a large consumer product. This instrument raises two important difficulties:

===== PAGE 560 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
543 Figure 16. - Hammer for forging large objects (Leonardo da Vinci).
firstly, the way in which the cylinders were made, and secondly, the method of tightening. The existence of cast iron which could be moulded, facilitated the fabrication of the rollers, even though the cast iron may have been particularly brittle at this stage. Similarly, the use of cast iron made possible the creation of gears which transmitted the movement of the hydraulic wheel to the apparatus: it would have been very difficult to use wood in this process. Tightening could be done with the aid of nuts. It is difficult to know where and when the rolling mill was first developed. Many authorities believe that the first example of a rolling mill was produced in 1615 by the great engineer,
Salomon de Caus. But it seems that this invention is probably much older than this, dating back to the last quarter of the 16th century, as
Liege texts would seem to prove that the rolling mill was already in use in this region and at this date. The history of the rolling mill still

===== PAGE 561 =====

544
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
remains to be completed, as we should trace the development of this mill from its origin to present day.
Slitting was merely a different form of rolling, using cutting edges which overlapped from one cylinder to the next, to cut the iron in bars.
Here again, the fabrication of the cylinders must have been problema tic, all the more so since greater stress was involved. It seems very likely that the Liege texts, those we have cited above, suggest that industry in this region had developed slitting processes from the end of the 16th century. According to Cyril Stanley Smith this development was much later and was only introduced in the 17th century, perhaps even at the end of the century.
Hydraulic wire-drawing mills really only consisted of applying hydraulic energy to a technique which had been known for an extremely long time. The operation of passing wire through gradually smaller and smaller holes was one which had in fact existed in
Antiquity. This process was relatively easy for malleable metals, such as gold or silver, but was far more difficult for iron. A far greater amount of energy was therefore necessary. One solution was to drive the spool using power from a mill wheel. An image by Biringuccio, from the middle of the 16th century, illustrates that the instrument was already in use at this time.
This whole evolution in the techniques of ironsmelting thus contri buted a more abundant output for a much greater range of products.
The use of metal - and the new quality of irons should also be taken into consideration - could thus be increased in considerable propor tions. We could even say that the metal age really began during this epoch.
Although our perception of the problems presented by the iron and steel industry is fairly keen, our knowledge of the development of non-ferrous metals is rather more vague, despite long passages which appear on this subject in treatises by Agricola and Biringuccio. Of course, techniques involved in non-ferrous metalworking obviously profited to a certain extent from the new techniques and machines used in ironsmelting. The discovery of America prompted some attempts to find precious metals. It was in 1451, however, according to a tradition which remains difficult to verify, that a certain Johansen Funcken developed a method of separating silver from lead and copper. The amalgam for the production of gold dates from the middle of the 16th century. Far more local metals were used, as the process of separating

===== PAGE 562 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
545 metals remained problematic, and a lot of silver must have been left in the argentiferous lead extracted from some mines.
Techniques employed in the production of copper appear to have been modified between 1450 and 1550, as copper - up until then used particularly for making bells and copperware - was suddenly in great demand for casting bronze cannons. The Germans called the factories where these new methods were in practice Saigerhiitte, although we do not know exactly how the new techniques differed from the old ones.
This explains the extent of cupriferous exploitations undertaken by Jacques Coeur and why many new mines were set up in Bohemia and
Saxony. The use of hydraulic energy was generalized to power bellows and hammers. Shaft furnaces and open-hearth furnaces were employed for both lead and copper production.
The bronze used in cannons was different from that used in bells.
The latter contained 23 to 26% of tin, while bronze used in artillery contained only 8 to 12% . The important factor in all these bronze using techniques, from the traditional bell to the production of cannons and statues, was the introduction of the mould and the material from which this was made. Many works illustrate the way in which this was done. It is quite possible that the great demand for cast bronze pieces enabled these methods to be perfected.
A last development in the art of metallurgy may be attributed to our period. This does not concern an alloy but a combination of two metals, which was in turn to give rise to the new product of tin- plate - the use of which was limited in the 15th and 16th centuries but which was to enjoy a considerable application in the future. Mechanical problems were not involved here, although the fabrication of large sizes of sheet metal which were relatively regular in thickness could still present difficulties. What was most important was how a layer of tin could be stably fixed to cover this sheet metal. Products used to this effect remained secret for a long time. It is likely that empiricism was king at the beginning, and that all sorts of materials were used to fix the tin layer.
Our documentation contains virtually nothing on the origins of this process. It seems from later documents, in particular those which reflect all the efforts made by some nations to import this method of fabrication, that tin-plate was first developed in Germany. Tradition again says that the birth of tin-plate took place in Nuremberg in the second half of the 15th century.

===== PAGE 563 =====

546
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Other fire-based techniques were also noticeably modified, but we must ascertain whether these were favoured by a change in the type of fuel used. Indeed, increased production could have resulted in the destruction of the forest, especially as wood was needed in greater quantities to provide more heating for a larger population, and also to reply to urban expansion and to the development of the navy which imposed major discoveries. The only other fuel was coal, the use of which had been known for a very long time, but which one was reluctant to employ because of the fear that it would introduce impurities in the materials with which it came in contact. Indeed, unrefined coal was often excluded from some operations because of its impurities, particularly in the production of sulphur: such was the case in the blast furnace although many attempts had been made to resolve this from the second half of the 16th century. Gradually, however, coal was introduced into a number of processes. Some authorities have suggested that coal was used in the forge in the Belgian village of
Marchienne at the beginning of the 16th century. This date is undoub tedly suspect, but nevertheless it seems that fears about using coal slowly began to disappear: mining in the Liege region was already underway in the 16th century, and at, the end of this century and the beginning of the 17th century we witness considerable expansion in the coal industry in England. Such movements would not have taken place if the demand for coal had not risen.
The glass-making industry also saw some changes, a few of which we will examine, but without an overall and exact view. The increase in the number of stained-glass windows is surely an indication of this.
More archaeological evidence enables us to put forward a few hypotheses on this subject. Modification was not so much in the construction of the furnaces, and a London manuscript from the end of the 15th century provides a perfect example of this, but the greatest change lay in the composition of the material which was modified approximately between 1450 and 1550. Soda gradually replaced
P<?tash, and we know that this gave a more even, white and fusible glass, which was both easier to work and to make smoother and purer.
We should also note one factor here which has already been pointed out on the subject of metallurgy: obtaining a product which was easier to work and superior in quality, which in turn constitutes real technical progress - outside the question of the volume of the production and the cost of fabrication of which we know very little. Henceforth, glass

===== PAGE 564 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
547 could resolve better problems previously presented, seal larger surfaces and preserve increased transparency, although it was by no means perfect as yet.
A new product appeared in the second half of the 15th century, whose reputation was to grow rapidly throughout the whole of
Western Europe and which still retains its lustre today - that is Venetian or crystalline glass. Only an as yet still unverifiable tradition, provides us with our first landmark: its invention or its introduction might be attributed to Beroverio, a reputable glassworker from
Murano, in 1463. Other glassworkers, particularly Norman artists from Languedoc, claimed authorship for this. Venetain glass is a silico-alcaline glass (silicate of potassium hydroxide and lime) , which was later to be replaced by a lead based form of glass (silicate of potassium hydroxide and lead) which resulted in the creation of a true crystal. Whatever the case, high quality products, especially sodas obtained by the combustion of certain plants, arrived in Venice.
There were also differences in the fabrication of coloured glass. If we compare the stained-glass windows of the Middle Ages with those of the 15th and 16th centuries, we may appreciate these differences easily, not only in style but also in the techniques applied. The fabrication of glass by doubling meant that a greater range of colours could be obtained. Some colours could be employed by mechanical means, and similarly different layers of colours could be added.
The widespread production of smooth or flat glass clearly demon strates that aside from an increase in the number of glass-making concerns, some technical progress must have been made. Once again, we should note that research must have been undertaken with this aim in mind. The development of the mirror, particularly the Venetian mirror, illustrates that techniques in making smooth glass must have been improved and perfected. Air was blown in a cylinder to make crystalline glass. Here again, this invention has been traditionally attributed to the Del Gallo brothers, who mentioned this technique in a document of 1503.
Of all the fire-using techniques, pottery, we have noticed, remained the most traditional, to such an extent that it is frequently impossible to distinguish chronologically between some potteries made from the
Gallo-Roman period to those of the dawn of the 19th century. Only the preparation of pastes and the techniques of varnishing might have evolved. These transformations may again be observed in Italy, for

===== PAGE 565 =====

548
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
potters had progressed there through the stages of ceramics which were standard, unfinished, shiny or glazed, to earthenware which was to be developed to the product we know today. In fact, the main change lay in the coating which covered the clay. Although the paste used remained the same as before, i.e. clay, sand and chalky marl, the coating henceforth consisted of stanniferous enamel, the decoration having been placed on crude enamel, whereupon the colouring oxides mixed with the enamel during the firing process. This technique of producing earthenware may have been known to the Arabs, but we do know that this process took off in our regions from Faenza. Bernard
Palissy introduced these techniques in France, using a lead-based enamel.
We again come up against the same problems of material life, of which we know so little. It is true that earthenware, owing to its solidity, impermeability and durability, was clearly superior to the pottery used up until then which was brittle, porous and difficult to clean.
Techniques involved in mechanical operations had undoubtedly progressed considerably more, thanks to the improvement of tools and to the development of the different types of mechanisms which we have discussed, the two factors remaining fundamentally linked together.
We have already stated several times that the history of the tool remains to be completed, and we are convinced of this yet again. By accumulating images we may establish some idea of the tools which existed at different epochs. Our methodogical introduction has demonstrated both the riches and the limits of such studies. The history of the tool contains several essential aspects: the material or materials, and the form and types. What we have already said of iron is still valid on the subject of materials. The blast furnace and refining process produced a metal which was both easier to work and to make into steel, insofar as or perhaps despite the use of this method of refining, the obtained iron was less pure than that produced by direct methods. We should also note that steelmaking seems to have been improved during the 16th century, since we have the tools from this period.
We have already observed that forms were considerably modified as of the 13th century. This evolution had in fact been extremely gradual in the beginning and probably accelerated from the 15th century

===== PAGE 566 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
549 onwards. During this century and the two which followed it, the forms and proportions of the tools were definitively established. The tool not only took its definitive form at this stage, although many had remained the same since Roman times, but also became more diversified as demands increased, for the number of operations increased with development in techniques, simultaneously requiring this differenti ation in tools. The introduction of new or modified tools may also be noticed, although unfortunately the absence of precise inventories limits us to citing only a few examples. During the 15th century fretsaws, jigsaws, and hacksaws were introduced. Planes and rabbet planes, as well as concave and raised moulders, established their definitive qualities during the 15th and 16th centuries. Elsewhere cutting edges were improved between the 14th and the 16th centuries.
At the end of the 14th century, the bit became detachable in boring or drilling tools, the breast drill appeared, and pliers were perfected during the 15th century. Note also the creation of better precision tools, such as the calliper rule which was developed in the 16th century.
The whole method of working wood had been widely transformed by the modifications made in tools.
Apart from this, and perhaps because tools were improved, insofar as new mechanisms gave machines more flexibility, the union between these two elements became greater and more profound. To illustrate this statement one need only think of the movement contributed by the crank and connnecting rod to hydraulic saws: the machine therefore increased in power and speed (fig. 17). From the 15th century onwards the use of hand, horse-driven or hydraulically-powered tools increased. An example of this is provided by machines for reaming cannons, some extremely interesting drawings of which date from the
15th century. Reaming was done vertically, as is illustrated in Breugh el's painting The Fire. The work of Biringuccio, on the other hand, shows that reaming was horizontal, the tool being operated by a squirrel cage. The tool itself had also evolved: here steel blades were used, mounted on wood and supported by a bronze ring, then the whole head of the tool was made of steel. In the manuscript on the crafts of Nuremberg, dating from the end of the 14th century, files were still cut by hand, using a criss-cross pattern of grooves. Leonardo da Vinci had already envisaged the use of machines for sharpening files and we know of all the improvements made during the 15th century to the form of this tool and to rasps. The manuscript from the Hussite

===== PAGE 567 =====

550
