TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
war, around 1430, contains a drawing of a machine which appeared to be driven by a mill-wheel, used for the horizontal drilling of wood pipes. A vertical machine of this type also appears in Leonardo da
Vinci's work. In the same manuscript of 1430, the first machine for polishing gems may already be noted. This machine must have been quickly developed, for a manuscript from the end of the 15th century provides an almost definitive image of it, showing the disk and stones fitted into small adjustable vices. A still unverifiable legend attributed to the German, Louis Berken, in 1476, the idea of polishing diamonds with their own dust. And finally, there were glass or mirror polishers, which may be seen in Leonardo da Vinci's sketches.
Figure 17. - Hydraulic saw (Francesco di Giorgio).

===== PAGE 568 =====

CLASSICAL SYSlEMS 551 The system of the crank and connecting rod similarly made possible the multiplication and diversification of lathes, which were certainly already in use, but for which only reciprocating movements were employed. In 1470, treadle spinning wheels, which left the worker's hand free, were introduced. All the treadle grindstones also required a reduced work force. In the second half of the 16th century, Besson introduced the first irregular lathes, after Leonardo da Vinci's drawings which the artist had probably abandoned. Another problem arose regarding this which was to receive different solutions: the object either had to be introduced to the mobile tool, or turned on an immobile tool. The problem therefore lay in transporting the tool or the object. In the case of almost all the machines for working metal, it was the tool which was transported. In the case of wood, however, the object was mobile (fig. 18 and 19). Leonardo da Vinci supplied the famous drawing of a machine for cutting screws, probably made out of wood. Here, the chuck was the mobile piece, sliding on two endless screws. The object to be fashioned was placed in the middle and turned through the medium of a crank which also engaged the screw of the chuck. Accordingly, the tool itself was fixed on its chuck; thus two parts were mobile: the chuck and the part to be worked.
Although these machines were well designed and thought out, they did not yet function perfectly. Made of wood with rough joints, movements could only be jerky and they were probably very difficult to manoeuvre. Indeed, they must have been a far cry from the precision of the drawings. The brewers' museum of Antwerp and the
Munich museum have preserved lathes from the 16th century and we may measure the gap which exists between the actual machines and the engravings in our treatises. Only machines made of metal, or at least partially of metal, were wholly suitable. Such examples may be found in the balance coining-press, invented by Cellini around 1530, and the printing press.
Here again, we require catalogues of these machines, images, and descriptions (notarized acts in particular) in order to ascertain the importance which they commanded from the second half of the 15th century. Such catalogues would enable us to fully comprehend the extraordinary development which these machines underwent during the 15th and 16th centuries (fig. 20). This was one of the domains where technical change implemented by the Renaissance was un doubtedly the most remarkable in extent and depth.

===== PAGE 569 =====

552
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure l8. - J. Besson's lathe (1 578).

===== PAGE 570 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
553 Figure 1 9. - J. Besson's lathe (1578).
Progress in the field of chemical treatments could only result from experimentation: only the creation of modern chemistry, in the second half of the 18th century, was capable of radically transforming the techniques involved. The introduction of the lathe in dyeing kettles, at the end of the 16th century, was a mechanical rather than a chemical contribution. In fact, the only domain in which some notable change took place was in that of explosives. The developments of the artillery were due as much to the improvement of explosives as to the weapons themselves. Gunpowder was made up of saltpetre, sulphur, and charcoal dust. A long series of manuscripts, mainly German in origin, provide us with the various compositions of the mixture from the 15th century onwards:

===== PAGE 571 =====

554
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 20. - Variable speed lathe (Cherubin, 1671).

1 2 3 555 In France, this primitive gunpowder consisted of 75% of saltpetre and the rest was half sulphur and half charcoal dust: this mixture burnt but did not explode. From the 15th century onwards hydraulic mallets were used for grinding the mixture, which were similar in principle to the mallets used in paper mills. Virtually no noticeable progress was made in distilling.
The improvement of equipment enabled assembly techniques to increase in scope and scale. From the end of the 14th century, undoubtedly owing to the great crisis of the middle of the same century, less costly techniques, using products and machines generally prohibited by earlier regulations, were now looked to: such as the use of lower quality wools - particularly lamb's wool or slipe - the oiling of wools, then carding, the use of the wheel for spinning it, and multi heddle looms. In the domain which interests us here, the most notable progress was made in the expansion of carding and the introduction of the treadle wheel and flyer-wheels. On the subject of silk making, the expansion of hydraulic spinning mills (fig. 21) should be cited as well as the use of the loom, apparently designed by Jean le
Calabrais which was particularly widespread in the 15th century.
Leonardo da Vinci was extremely interested in the complicated projects of designing thread- and rope-making machines, shearing machines, and in the curious idea of an automatic weaving loom (fig.
22 to 24).
It seems, however, that the weaving loom was only slowly improved, perhaps insofar as some of its parts were henceforth made in metal (fig.
25). The most spectacular invention was without doubt the machine for knitting stockings, invented by the English engineer, Lee, at the end of the 16th century. The actual method of knitting presented many questions which probably remained unanswered for some time. Weav ing thread appears to have been developed in the 15th century, and one quickly changed from knitting by hand to using a machine of already advanced automatism, which Diderot was to praise even in the

===== PAGE 573 =====

556
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 2 1 . - Machine for unwinding cocoons. (Florence, 1 48 7).
Figure 22. - Cloth shearing machine. (Leonardo da Vinci)

===== PAGE 574 =====

CLASSICAL SYS1EMS
557 Figure 23. - Cloth carding machine. (Leonardo da Vinci) middle of the 18th century. We know of the misfortunes suffered by the inventor who was rejected, indeed even threatened, because his invention overturned production methods and terrified the working population. As we have already noted, the printers' strikes in the middle of the 16th century were the first reactions demonstrated by workers faced with the domination of the machine, and this was to be one more proof of the importance of mechanisation during this period.
Similar stability may be noted in wooden constructions. From the 15th century, grooves and tongues were used to join boards and planks, made possible by the improvement of the plane. Although carpentry techniques had remained virtually unchanged, furniture, on the other hand, could evolve considerably, due to the greater ease in woodworking. Yet again, we require detailed research on the typology of furniture, the growth of which corresponds to the change in interiors

===== PAGE 575 =====

558
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 24. - Automatic weaving loom (Leonardo da Vinci).
and appreciably different life styles: furniture indisputably changed in structure, which naturally implies that there was parallel change in the structures of life styles and behaviour. The chest, or coffer, tended to be replaced by the cupboard or wardrobe in two parts, which we begin to see around the middle of the 16th century. The table had fixed legs and was more monumental in appearance, and the shape of the armchair was simplified to become a carver. The four-poster bed virtually became the symbol of the Renaissance.
In the domain of carpentry, there was little radical change in construction methods but greater adaption to new conditions. This was a result of the increase in the demand for wood - and we will return to this later - albeit for ship building requirements. One was therefore required to limit oneself to smaller sizes of wood for construction. The new method relied upon splitting posts which henceforth dictated the height of floors or levels: horizontal pieces were superimposed on vertical ones. Springers were assembled on posts and a string-piece was placed on the whole construction separating the levels. The impost

===== PAGE 576 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
Figure 25. - Weaving loom (Nuremberg, end of the 1 5th century).
thus covered the post, instead of penetrating it as before, with a half-mounted joint which ensured the stability of the edifice - the impost and string-piece being held immobile to each other by a dovetail joint. The upper post was mounted on the string-piece, whereupon the angle was stiffened by a triangularly placed element
(pigeatre). These techniques were introduced at the end of the 15th century and at the beginning of the 16th century, as has been excellen tly demonstrated in Rouen. With these techniques, corbelled con structions could more easily be built and their use in urban buildings increased, many examples of which remain today.

===== PAGE 577 =====

560
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
As the last major assembly technique is so well known, we will only add a few words. This concerns printing works. The importance of the printing press for the circulation of knowledge may be fully appreci ated and at the start of the chapter, we stressed the significance of printed technical literature from the very beginning of this invention.
This statement certainly demands qualification. The work of copyists had been facilitated by dividing manuscripts into quires, and these same workshops had considerably increased in number. The advan tage of the printing press had been to significantly lower the price of works and similarly to develop better education, particularly through reading. Leonardo da Vinci points out to us the difficulty he had in finding some works. It is also true, however, that books remained a luxury product for a long time, almost reserved for the elite: it was mainly the social stratum below this elite class, although still the upper classes, who were to benefit from this technique, especially a few of the more superior technicians, of whom Leonardo da Vinci is a good representative.
We should note also that the development of printing was closely linked to that of the paper-making industry. This explains the parallel growth of the paper mill. Moreover, this expansion had begun slightly before the definitive introduction of the printing press. Problems posed by energy demands had yet to be resolved, as well as the more difficult question of supplying raw materials.
Even before the invention by Gutenberg, stamps cut out in relief were used to reproduce the decorated capital letters in manuscripts.
Similarly, wood cuts were used for printing on fabric, a technique which probably originated in the East, and the first xylographic impressions may have come from these wood cuts, following the process of technological transference which has already been discus sed. From the first half of the 15th century, xylograph workshops existed in the Rhine region and in the Burgundian states. These images, in the main pious, enjoyed great success, and rapidly became associated with short legends. But the birth of the printing press and typography required separate letters, and the assembly and fabrication of these letters was in metal. Printing even brought to xylography thick ink and the press.
Printing, then, required movable metal type or characters of a cer tain life span, thick ink, and the press. The technique of engraving stamps had already been used by goldsmiths. The stamp was engraved

===== PAGE 578 =====

CLASSICAL SYS1EMS
561 in relief, whereupon it was punched in a less resistant metal mould (tin or lead) which provided the mould for the final character. Sand moulds may possibly have been used in the early days, made possible by the techniques of casting and medal making, these sand moulds being made with wooden matrices.
It is possible that research in this direction was undertaken in Avignon on the one hand, by a goldsmith from Prague, and in Mainz on the other, by the Gutenbergs, who were both goldsmiths and money makers. Other transferences of technology may be found at this point. In 1457, the Mainz Psalter was published, the first printed work of its kind and which was very high in quality. The take-off of new techniques was extremely rapid. Henri-Jean Martin has estimated that at the end of the 15th century, thirty five thousand editions had been published, representing, at the lowest estimate, fifteen to twenty million copies. 77% of these were in Latin, 45% were religious works, and two hundred and thirty six towns participated in this production.
The fabrication of characters was quickly perfected, giving birth to specialist workshops. The first moulds to be used were made of brass or bronze, and matrices were made of lead. Schoff er would have used the first steel moulds and copper patterns. Type characters probably took longer to develop a definitive form. In 1570, Aldo Maunzio changed the character in each volume. A tin based alloy was certainly used, avoiding a high proportion of lead which damaged the pattern, and adding a small quantity of antimony. But these early characters did not have any grooves to hold them in their correct position.
We know very little about the first printing presses. They may have consisted of a simple wooden press which came from the wine producing industry. The German engineer, Husz, still used a huge screw press in Lyons, in around 1500. More elaborated forms were arrived at by a process of experimentation.
Major progress seems to have already been made around the middle of the 16th century. A sort of standardization of character types was introduced after the general adoption of Roman. Standardization of the composition, page and size was established when large workshops replaced the hundreds of small cottage printing works. The arrangement of the letters in the type-case, and the position of the latter in relation to the compositor was accompanied by modification in the presses. While the platen, placed at the bottom of the screw, was still limited in size, the table had become movable and slid on two rails,

===== PAGE 579 =====

562
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
and could carry a much larger forme. This facilitated inking, and it was also easier to introduce the second part of the forme between the two movements of the bar.
Military techniques involve a much larger technical complex, invol ving a great many tributary techniques, a few of which have already been discussed. The development of artillery is a fact which dominates the whole history of military techniques. Although progress in this domain had been relatively slow between the middle of the 14th and the middle of the 15th century, development was to be considerable and very rapid after 1450.
Artillery was the first domain in which fire-arms were used. Early cannon were formed of iron bars shaped into cylinders and hooped.
The powder chamber was screwed into the actual cannon. The whole was then transported on carriages or carts. This necessitated supplying ammunition, which consisted of stone cannonballs, peculiar to each type of cannon. In the second half of the 15th century, the introduction of cannon made out of one piece, due to the improvement of casting techniques and to the development of cast iron, which were more regular and used cast iron cannonballs, enabled artillery to fulfil its potential. Cast iron cannons were introduced in the first half of the
16th century and were henceforth to be the traditional armament of warships. Here again, the standardization of products was a great aid.
The perfection in boring or drilling could reduce calibres and thus ensure a regular supply of ammunition. The invention, between 1550 and 1561 , of iron or steel sights meant that these objects could last longer.
Henceforth artillery was permanent, while wooden machines of the past had usually been built in situ. Gun carriages without wheels were replaced by those with wheels, soon followed by independent front axle units, which permitted easier manoeuvring. Once a few more elementary laws of ballistics were understood - the study of which was begun at the beginning of the 16th century by the Italian, Tartaglia - the cannon was adjusted: the carriage was thus divided into two superimposed parts, the upper part could describe the arc of a circle and set a determined position due to the addition of a rack: thus the back sight adjuster was born (fig. 26). From this point on, artillery was far more widely used. The cannon of Charles the Bold, taken without great harm by the Swiss at Marat, in 1476, may be said to represent the first real modern weapons. Military historians believe that one may

===== PAGE 580 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
563 Figure 26. - The first types of advanced cannons.

===== PAGE 581 =====

564
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
speak of real artillery manoeuvres taking place at Ravenna, in 1512. At Melegnano, in 1515, the role of the artillery was already very important. The French royal decree of 1544 introduced six definitive calibres, ensuring that artillery retained military and logistic efficacy.
The history of portable fire-arms is not as well known. Firstly, existing arms were improved, in particular the formidable cross-bow, by reinforcing the spring and consequently the power and impact of the weapon. The 15th century was to witness the birth of the steel cross-bow which was still more powerful and easier to handle than the cross-bow.
The earliest representations of portable fire- arms are illustrated by Kyeser at the beginning of the 15th century. These consisted of small iron tubes, descended from a modest size cannon (veuglaire). From this, portable cannon mounted on wood were developed at the end of the 15th and the beginning of the 16th century, of which there were two types, the arquebus and the harquebut. One was manoeuvred by hand, and the other mounted on a trestle which was replaced, in 1520, by a fork. These were the portable fire-arms which secured victory for
Charles V of Spain at Pavia in 1523. Already, towards the end of the 16th century, portable fire-arms had advanced considerably in light ness and precision.
It was this slow progress in artillery which was to revolutionize the principles of fortification, and undoubtedly the use of powder in mining (fig. 27). In 1515, the French captured Milan Castle, thanks both to the artillery developed by Galiot de Genouillac and to the mines of Pedro Navarro. The high medieval wall was no longer of interest from then on.
The first idea was to adapt old techniques or technology. Langeais castle, a traditional design, was begun in around 1465, but it was never to be completed. The first architects, aware of military changes, looked to earlier plans, but lowered the height of the walls, made them thicker and built platforms for artillery at the top of the towers.
Examples are provided by the castle of Nantes, and Saint Malo, both begun around 1466-1470, and the castle of Ham, in Picardy (14701475). Undoubtedly the best example is Salses castle, not far from
Perpignan, built by the Spanish in 1497, under the direction of the engineer, Ramirez. As Raymond Ritter notes, this was the first large sunken fortification to be mainly concealed, while its layout remained very traditional. From this, they progressed to circular bastions,

===== PAGE 582 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
565 Figure 27. - Leonardo da Vinci's fortification designs.

===== PAGE 583 =====

566
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
descendants of the sunken medieval tower, with paved platforms for the artillery, but also with two vaulted block-houses with embrasures for law-built batteries: the bastion of Saint Nicolas de Langres, built at the end of the 15th century is such an example, as is the huge tower of
Toulon, begun in 1514, under the direction of the Italian, Jean Antoine Della Porta, similar to the great tower of Le Havre, which unfortunately no longer exists today.
The Italians were certainly the first to begin work on these new forms. Tentative changes were made at the start. Lorenzo di Pietro, known as 11 Vecchietta, directed his pupil, Francesco di Giorgio
Martini, whose designs were still very traditional in appearance. The tenaille system therefore appeared in some parts of the fortification, particularly in weak points. From this probably stemmed the idea of reinforcing old types of fortifications by building constructions in front of them to protect the old walls. Jean Richard has pointed out the work achieved by Frarn5ois de Surienne at Dijon, from 1461, as well as the castle of Fougeres built by the same man. This was also the case at
Rhodes in 1496. Besieging artillery was therefore forced to retreat a long way back. In 1521, the Italian, Basilio Della Scuola of Vicenza, still at Rhodes, linked the bulwarks with the escarpment wall, the bulwarks henceforth forming a continuous construction with the cur tain.
The ace of spades shaped bastion was adopted by the Sangallo at Civita Castellana (1494-1497), and at Nettuno (1501-1502) where ace of spades shaped bastions flanked a traditional rectangular plan.
Antonio da Sangallo finally envisaged, in 1515, polygonal plans at Civitavecchia, then Ancona in 1527. Lastly, orillion bastions appeared. The della Maddelena bastion in Verona was built according to this plan, in 1527 by Michele Leoni. Sanmichele made it his pride.
These designs were adopted in France, at Troyes in 1524-1529, at Saint-Paul-de-Vence, and at Navarrenx (1543-1569). One might say that modern fortification was finally born. Albert Diirer was oile of the first theoreticians in 1527, followed by Battista Della Valle. The whole technique was codified by Erard of Bar-le-Due in la Fortification reduite en art et demontree (1594). In this he defined the methods that provide shelter using features of the terrain, instituted inclined planes designed to avoid the surprise of downward views and succeeded in concealing the flanks of the bastion from the enemy by building carefully placed curtains. He also invented the cavalier and stand-

===== PAGE 584 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
567 ardized the thickness of the ramparts. We are approaching the era of Vauban.
The greatest work of the Renaissance was certainly in the conquest of geographical boundaries. The considerable growth of trade and traffic and simultaneously the organisation of space, were mainly the preoccupations of technicians and after them various authorities.
The first problem concerns transport. The centralization of king doms and the extension of the known world required modified means of transport in order to reply to the growth of trade and the length of the journeys which had to be undertaken. Although harnessing had been modified - at least only a few details remained to be solved - the vehicle on the other hand, which was in fact hardly used and only then over short distances, was badly adapted to fulfill increased demand.
Several innovations were to enable an evolution in the vehicle. The first, as yet not fully studied, involves the pivoted front axle unit, which considerably facilitated manoeuvring the four-wheeled vehicle. The first example of this is illustrated on Francesco da Carrara's seal, dating from the very end of the 14th century, although the image is somewhat clumsy and difficult to interpret. A more detailed image may be found, however, on a manuscript dating from around 1470. This shows two carts, both with shafts. The cart at the back is fixed by a shaft to a pivot placed on the cart in front. But this vehicle could easily topple over due to the lack of balance, and could only take very wide turns, not sharp corners. It was to be some years before the new system was estab lished. We know that Ravaillac was able to commit his infamous crime because Henry IV's coach did not have a pivoted front axle unit and had difficulty in turning the corner of the rue de la Ferronnerie. At the end of the 16th century, however, the pivoted front axle unit was already in everyday use.
The other problem was that of suspension. This not only concerned transporting people, but also all the jolts and bumps suffered by the vehicle as it went along the road. Firstly, the vehicle's chassis was suspended by chains or straps, as can be seen on a drawing of 1405.
Some authorities have been able to identify "swaying carriages" in this drawing which were attested from 1398 onwards. It seems that more recently, even older representations of suspension have been found: in a South German manuscript, dating from 1330-1350 (a miniature representing the flight from Egypt), and in an English manuscript from the second quarter of the 16th century. The most significant

===== PAGE 585 =====

568
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
improvement made in the middle of the 16th century, would therefore have been to attach chains or straps, not to the framework, but to the large springs resting on it. A German manuscript of 1568 shows a very clear representation of this.
Other inventions or discoveries remain to be discussed. Attempts were made to improve wheels, and during the 16th century two particular techniques came into being. The wooden wheel was protec ted by nailing plates onto it: examples of these date from the Middle
Ages, and the Ancients already used bronze plates. Emergency hoops which were placed on vehicle wheels were a later development made in
16th century. The second improvement was what has been termed dishing wheels, in other words inclining the spokes of the wheel on the hub at the angle. Use of this system meant that the great stress exerted on the wheels could be avoided. This technique was undoubtedly quickly employed for carriages used for carrying artillery.
Land transport and roads are closely linked. Truth to tell, the techniques used in road networks could hardly be modified except on the level of organisation: infrastructure in this domain is absolutely essential, as is so today. In France, to take but one example which has been the object of extensive research, the general road network began to become a governmental consideration from the time of Louis XI, with paving, the supervision of particular encroachment, and even the creation of new routes. It was at the express command of the king that the Mount Viso tunnel was built, designed to provide a communication route between the Dauphine and the marquisate of Saluces. The tunnel cut through the mountain side at an altitude of more than two thousand metres, was two metres high and two and a half metres wide.
An error in layout resulted in a slight reorientation. This work, executed from 1478 to 1480, was the major event of the century: it was the first great tunnel of modern time. Bridge building techniques which increased at the end of the 14th century should also be studied, but unfortunately no work of any scale has as yet been devoted to this subject.
River navigation was still a convenient and extensive means of transport during the period in question. Here, technical transfor mations in navigation played a smaller part in realizing significant progress than the 0rganisation of river and waterways. Renaissance engineers quickly perceived that river trade, which was extremely important fropi the beginning, could be improved by undertaking

===== PAGE 586 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
569 large scale work. Hazards of the terrain and the vastness of the work to be done often halted even the most daring projects. Levellings, embankments and supplying water, all presented difficulties which were not easy to overcome. In cases of different levels, the problem of closing locks remained without any practical solution. It seemed that the most noticeable progress involved, on the one hand, the digging of canals (and we know that Leonardo da Vinci was particularly inter ested in this), and on the other, the question of locks. Leonardo da
Vinci worked on the Milan canals under the direction of his elders, who must have used techniques about which we have little infor mation. We know almost nothing about the techniques involved in banks and making them watertight. And as for locks, historians have tried to shed some light on this problem. Locks had already been used in building the canal linking Bruges to the sea, constructed in the 13th century. Drawings by Renaissance engineers show that there were in fact two solutions: the inclined plane upon which small craft landed, and actual lock gates. There would also have been mobile gates on the canal created by Jean, Duke of Berry in 1394, which went from Niort to the Atlantic. At the same date, similar locks may have been used on the Juine, in the earldom of Etampes (France). Improvements would have taken place during the 15th century, such as flaps on the lock gates, and henceforth, the sluice gate and the lock were to establish their definitive appearance. Filippo Visconti had these built in 1440, and the Venetians installed them on the Piovego in 1481. Alberti wrote a perfect description of this construction. The opening and closing systems, which had formerly been the principal difficulty, now enabled this expansion.
Henceforth, and because of the existence of centralized and rela tively wealthy states, it was possible to create a canal network where natural river ways were unusable or inadequate. The flow and course of natural waterways could even be regularized. From 1468, the regularization of the Loire, begun some time earlier, was taken up again, and the work continued down a whole series of tributaries which were to make the Loire a main communication route in an extensive territory: the Auron river for Bourges, the Brenne river to carry wood to Tours, the Clain river to link Poitiers to this network, then the
Loire, Maine and Sarthe rivers. The same was also achieved in Lom bardy. During the 14th century, the diversion of the Ticino was con tinued right up to Milan, and the Po was partially regularized. Marble

===== PAGE 587 =====

570
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
for the cathedral could be transported, owing to the use of locks built in 1395. The diversion of the Martesana was begun in 1457, initially to supply water to Milan, before being used for navigation: Leonardo da
Vinci worked on this under the direction of Bertola da Novate. At the beginning of the 14th century the Stecknitz river was regularized up to
Lubeck, where a canal linked it to the Elbe, for the first time crossing, in 1391-1398, the dividing line between the two basins.
Major finds have supplied evidence on the progress made in mari time navigation. This progress extended in several directions: firstly, of course, the ship, then navigation techniques and finally the building of ports. Although many works exist on this subject, the history of the ship has yet to . be completed. Evolution was certainly slow and progressive, without any sudden changes. The Nordic vessel was fairly widespread, but it in turn was to benefit from certain developments in
Mediterranean sailing ships. During the 15th century, cargo ships differed little from the vessels of the 13th century. The fore and aft castles were definitively incorporated into the hull which remained very broad and flat to facilitate running aground. In order to maintain the solidity of these hulls, exterior reinforcements were used which reduced sailing. At the beginning of the 16th century, the hulls tended to become lighter by dispensing with the carrack bow and lowering the prow to the level of the bridge, while the aft castles increased in height with progressive displacements. The caravel was introduced after a long evolution, finally taking its definitive form at the end of the 15th century; while a more refined hull was developed which facilitated sailing and was more resistant to drift. The essential factor seems to lie in the mixture of Nordic and Latin sails. The ship was henceforth to possess three masts. The foremast carried a square sail, and the mainmast formerly the old large mast, also carried a square sail. The mizzen-mast, placed on the aft castle, had a lateen sail. Only the main mast and its sail had any importance, as the others were to act as a counterbalance, to maintain the ship's course, and were to evolve separately themselves. Between 1450 and 1550, this rigging was to be divided and the mizzen-mast was coupled with a counter-mizzen-mast, also carrying a lateen sail. The bowsprit was to be fixed in front, underwhich the spritsail was hung; this was to facilitate both the manoeuvre of tacking and to maintain holding a steady course.
Throughout the whole of the 16th century these ships were to be continually improved upon and were to increase in a variety of types;

===== PAGE 588 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
571 the galleon, which established routes to America, appeared in the second half of the 16th century.
The art of sailing was to be fairly radically tranformed even more than the ship itself. Discoveries made at the end of the 15th century and the beginning of the 16th obviously modified cartography, but it was not until Gerhardus Mercator that flat maps were transformed into the proper maps we know today, the first of which dates from around
1569. Henceforth, it was possible to trace a route on a map.
Throughout the 15th century trigonometry tables were perfected. As Guy Beaujouan notes, they enabled calculations of distances in terms of a boat being taken off course by the wind, but which could keep to its route by following a chosen rhomb. The differences of latitudes between ports could also be calculated. Astronomical observations on the land were made for some time, while navigation was essentially coastal. Before 1480, there was probably no astronomical navigation.
The nautical astrolabe appeared for the first time on a map dating from 1529, now preserved in the Vatican. The impossibility of establishing a point of latitude on a map did not require extreme precision, moreover. The first declination tables of the sun were printed in
Venice in 1483. Until the middle of the 16th century, navigation remained a matter of guess-work. It was still difficult to ascertain an exact route and speed. The use of the compass, known in the preceding epoch, the hour-glass, and then the clock - albeit difficult to use at sea - and the invention of the log, mentioned for the first time in 1577, was gradually to solve the problem of loxodromics and to ensure more precise navigation.
The multitude of ships, and the increase in their tonnages presented the problem of harbours. Obviously this problem was even greater in tidal seas. Consequently, the method of landing by running aground continued to be employed. The only real large port of northern Europe was Bruges, but the canal which linked it to the sea was straight, fairly shallow and silted up slowly but surely. Dredging, the creation of flush ponds, and even of a new canal did not help, and from the end of the
16th century, Antwerp had finally taken its place. In Antwerp most of the Channel ports, Harfleur, Granville, and Port-en-Bessin, it was necessary to harness rivers and create these tidal basins which were to increase in number throughout the 15th century. For a long time, ports were cleared with spades and barrows, before the improvement of dredges facilitated this work. These flush reservoirs enabled the

===== PAGE 589 =====

572
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
creation of the port of Le Havre at the beginning of the 16th century.
Similarly, it seems that the construction of dikes and jetties was mainly perfected in the north, and likewise in the south where a more rocky coast without tides made the creation and development of ports difficult. Large scale work was undertaken in this direction in Naples in
1447, in Palermo in 1541 , and in Livorno towards the end of the century. This was also the same at Genoa where the jetty was extended and given a lighthouse. All the techniques involved in harbours and ports need to be studied in greater depth as they were an essential element in the development of maritime navigation: research does not really appear to have been undertaken in this domain, the importance of which is obvious for the period between the end of the 15th century and throughout the 16th century.
The organisation of space was extended to other techniques, which were also made easier by some evolution in techniques themselves, as well as by political centralization. Draining had been put into practice for some time, as had been the case in the Poitou marshes and on the shores of the North Sea, to take but two examples. In the 15th century, these works were systematized and large overall programmes were established. Work was restarted in the Poitou marshes between 1438 and 1443, and the general plan was launched in 1526. Between 1440 and 1460, large scale work was undertaken on the Venetian coast, doubling the outlets of the great rivers which flowed to the coast.
Between 1530 and 1540, a network perpendicular to the lines of the slope were established. After the formation of the Zuyderzee, around
1300, and the great floods of 1421 which broke the dike built in the 14th century - perhaps due to the sluice gates of the river Maas - a systematic attempt was yet again undertaken in this direction, facili tated by the use of windmills. From 1435 onwards, polders were created with a control and a series of superimposed dikes. The great dikes of the Island of Walcheren, dating from the end of the 15th century, were nearly four kilometres long, one hundred metres thick, and towered four metres and eighty centimetres above the high seas;
they were nevertheless broken through in 1530, but rebuilt in later years. The Friesland huge dikes in Frise were constructed around 1570 by the Spaniard, Caspare de Robles. Protected from the sea and the great rivers, the lower terrains could thus be drained. The Dutch created a vast administration which was directed by a team of remarka ble men, such as Andre Verligh, from 1552, and then Simon Stevin.

===== PAGE 590 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
573 Past masters in this art, the Dutch were summoned almost everyw here: in France for the Vernier marshes on the lower Seine, and in the mouth of the Vistula, between Elblag and Danzig, between 1528 and
1562.
At the same time, extensive irrigation work was undertaken in dry countries. An example, in a field which has been little studied, is that of the great dam of Almansa in Southern Spain, which undoubtedly dates from the middle of the 16th century: it is 20.69 metres in height and 89 metres in length (fig. 28). The Tibi dam, also in the south of Spain, would have been built in the third quarter of the 16th century by the architect of Escorial, Herrera.
All these great works - ports, drainage systems, irrigation networks - certainly presuppose that the methods used were perfected (fig. 29 to
31). Unfortunately we know very little about these. Although Leonardo da Vinci left us many drawings of equipment, particularly a large canal excavator, several lifting machines, and machines for driving in posts, and although other drawings illustrate dredgers, we are still not sure how embankments were made. It seems certain that a developed form of mechanization was introduced, especially in the first half of the 16th century. Engineers who thought of these machines or executed them may be cited almost everywhere. One looked to inventors. From 1413, the people of Marseilles engaged the services of a Genoese engineer who invented a new machine for cleaning out the ports.
Lastly, urban techniques, or town planning was one of the major concerns of Renaissance men. Although some of them have been studied in detail, there were others of which we know nothing.
Certainly the birth of theoretic urban planning is proved conclusively to have existed: like Leonardo da Vinci, Alberti, Francesco di Gior gio, and Filarete prolifically wrote and drew plans of regular towns, orthagonal towns, with new elements such as the public square, the first examples of which are provided by the cities of Corsignano and
Vigevano around 1460, including regulations on the length and width of roads, the height of houses, and the cleaning of public edifices.
Some new towns made their appearance where town planners adapted regular plans to terrains which were not. We know of the development of Rome between 1464 and 1540, the creation of Corte Maggiore between 1470 and 1481, of Le Havre by Bellarmati in 1541 , and of the new town of Nancy, established by Citoni in 1596.

===== PAGE 591 =====

574
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 28. - Cross-section and elevation of the Almansa dam (circa 1 550-1 560).

===== PAGE 592 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
575 Figure 29. - Crane designed by Leonardo da Vinci.

===== PAGE 593 =====

576
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 30. - Lifting apparatus designed by Francesco di Giorgio.
Municipal administration was improved according to earlier tech niques used: roads were paved, regularly cleaned, and drains ·were serviced. One of the most important problems was that of water. In
Rome, old aqueducts were repaired and two new ones built at the end of the 16th century. The Roman aqueduct in Segovia was repaired in
1481 . Note that in 1499 Paris had twelve fountains. At the end of the 15th century, the Liege water supply came from the drainage channels in the mines. Huge suction and force pumps were used in Toledo in
1526, in Gloucester in 1542, in Ausburg in 1548, in London in 1582, and in Paris in 1608. Machines for drilling wood pipes facilitated the distribution of water.
We will now return to the original subject of discussion. The Renaissance was a time of profound technical change: major discover ies and economic progress were positive proof of this. If we look at the

===== PAGE 594 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
577 situation in the second half of the 16th century, we will see that there was considerable change in a great number of more diverse tech niques. In many such instances, this involved not only the continuation and development of earlier techniques, but also the introduction of completely new features. Highly developed mechanisation introduced with the discovery of the crank and connecting rod system similarly invaded large scale works, such as mining and public works, as well as industrial productions. Many procedures were transformed in metal lurgy in general, with the development of casting iron using the indirect method, and with the amalgam for gold. The "industrial revolution" was also very profound in the domain of transport. And a new technical system was born, insofar as all the new inventions were complementary to each other. The appearance of the world had really changed.
Figure 31. - Pestles for driving in posts designed by Francesco di Giorgio.

===== PAGE 595 =====

578
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
DEVELOPMENT AND LIMITATIONS
It was upon this classical system that the whole of the 17th century was to be founded, and a good half of the 18th century. Terms, such as the halt of technical progress, and the blockage of technical thought, have been used in order to grasp a situation which is difficult to comprehend, and the causes of which are even more difficult to distinguish.
Some important facts may certainly be highlighted, and have been so, which partly explain the depression which followed the extensive growth in the 16th century. Among other problems were political difficulties which weighed very heavily. There were various internal wars, especially in the second half of the 16th century, and then long wars which were to devastate much of Europe in the first half of the
17th century. Again one would have thought that military techniques would benefit from this state of affairs. Obviously, improvements were made, particularly in the domain of portable firearms. It was in fact around 1630 that flint guns and pistols were ultimately developed and these forms were to remain virtually the same right up until the beginning of the 19th century. Very little development was to be made in artillery, apart from the addition of a few minor details. As for systems of fortification, these had been codified, as we have just seen, by Erard de Bar-le-Due, at the very end of the 16th century. Les
Fortifications by the Chevalier de Ville (1628), established a more elaborate system, but it differed little from the former. The Traite des fortifications, by Blaise Frarn;ois de Pagan, earl of Merveilles (1645), introduced a few modifications. Although Pagan had never directed any work, and although no fortification was built according to the
"Pagan system," his pupil, Vauban nevertheless based his science upon his master's theories, with the gradual introduction of greater defence and exterior covered ways. Vauban was only to contribute limited improvements to, and to adapt to various circumstances a science which was already highly evolved. War did not contribute any profound changes to the techniques established during the 15th and
16th centuries.
Political and internal difficulties were never without demographic consequences. Poverty, reduction in the level of production, and epidemics which generally resulted from this, led to sudden demo-

===== PAGE 596 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
579 graphic losses. To such an extent that one recent author has termed this period, as have many others, the "terrible 17th century." The plague again became endemic in Western Europe between 1620 and
1640. And once again, the problem of the relationship between technical progress and the demographic level presents itself. It would certainly seem, however, that in the circumstances decrease in demand and long stagnation of production enabled some sort of classical technical system to persist, even without significant developments, and to survive without major drawbacks, without resulting in economic and social imbalances and tensions.
We lack a comprehensive history of technical ideas, and this is all the more regrettable for the 17th century as this period could certainly offer fundamental elements to such a study. We have considered the privileged position enjoyed by technology in the second half of the
15th century. But this technology had remained largely empirical in nature, rewarding those who did successfully practice techniques with a considerable audience. The men working at the beginning of the
Renaissance do not appear to have had any great successors in the 16th century - apart from exceptions, of course. Mankind was preoccupied with religious matters, or in spirituality in the widest sense of the term, and were somewhat detached from material concerns which seemed to have reached a peak. Men were now more used to the introduction and circulation of new techniques, which no longer excited the same astonishment or admiration. Rabelais' pupil, interested in material problems, visited workshops and craftsmen. Montaigne's pupil was completely different. On the whole, the 16th century represents a void of interest in techniques and in the goverment of a material world.
But from the end of the 16th century and until the end of the 17th century, technology began to take off in another direction, despite all the troubles and poverty of the period. It did not, however, inspire the sort of enthusiasm which was prevalent during the time of Leonardo da
Vinci: proof of this fact is exemplified in the drop in the number of technical treatises printed in the 17th century. Henceforth, the techni cal spirit was to change completely. We do not intend to write a history of the development of scientific thought as that may easily be found elsewhere. We should nevertheless clarify that it was precisely at the end of the 16th century and throughout the 17th century that the modern scientific spirit was to be born. Although some sciences, such as chemistry, were to remain the same - and yet still to make wonderful

===== PAGE 597 =====

580
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
discoveries in their fields - physics, mathematics, and astronomy underwent profound change.
It seems therefore that the relationship between science and techno logy was turned about to some degree. At the end of the 16th century, some scientists - we should not generalize here - still based their theories on techniques. The names of Galileo, Stevin - and we could cite other men who were no less effective - remain to prove this. But their science very rapidly rose to a superior level. Unlike their 15th century predecessors, these men knew how to draw scientific facts from their observations on techniques, and to integrate these elements into a more organised, logical and more universal science. Was it not
Galileo, fountain-maker to the princes of Florence, who reasoned out and proved the theory of atmospheric pressure and the existence of a vacuum? And did not Stevin make conclusive progress in mathematics while pertinently dealing with problems of draining land in Holland?
This reversal was to quickly come about at the beginning of the 17th century. Henceforth, science was to be more autonomous in nature, initiating its own developments which were to lead, only in a limited measure it is true, to technical action. The explanation for this technical activity must lie either in the method or the machine.
Combinations still existed, however: Roberval was certainly an engi neer, but Desargues literally created the science of cutting stone.
Huygens invented the regulating balance or pendulum in clocks and then the spiral hair spring. Formerly the technican had instructed the scientist, be it the same person or not, of the nature of the problems to be solved. During the 17th century, the scientist taught the technician, meeting less and less in the same person, of the theory behind his technique.
This change is important, although it entails drawbacks which should be stressed. It is certainly interesting to study these reasons and they may serve to lead either to the implementation of improvements, or to the choice between the various possible courses of action. On the other hand, and we have noted this at the beginning of this work, in most cases, science was still at a level where it could not initiate real technical progress. Science attempted to explain but could not support innovation. Furthermore, science sterilized technical progress, insofar as technology seems to become dependent in some way upon science, and where science could not cross certain technical barriers. Naturally one meets the as yet insurmountable problem of making the transition

===== PAGE 598 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
581 from scientific imagination to technical imagination. We will try to illustrate this with some examples.
Firstly, an important fact must be emphasized. We have already stated that technical progress was naturaly dependent upon the notion of the nature of techniques on the one hand, and on certain economic conceptions on the other. Merchants based their politics on a static or unchanging state of affairs. To put it another way, throughout the world, all quantities (population, production, and commerce) were fixed. A nation could only become richer if it took from others. But as everything is stationary, techniques must also remain the same. From this results the notion that no technical progress is possible, but only the simple improvement of the existing techniques may be effected. So the effort did not consist of initiating other techniques, but of imposing those which did exist on other countries and not at home. The policy taken by Colbert, and by a great many princes, consisted of importing manufacturing processes of products which formerly had obliged non-producing states to export large quantities of gold.
We have tangible evidence of this desire for perfection. For reasons of military power, Colbert wished France to possess the best ships.
Carpenters from various arsenals were therefore required to make written records of their science, and to do a sort of project on ship building. Some of these compendiums have been preserved and merit detailed studied. A "commission" was then charged to choose all the best elements from these projects so as to construct the definitive ship, the ideal ship. The latter was actually built and, not surprisingly, was not very successful.
Knowing the "reasons" behind a technique was certainly one way of perfecting it. Similarly, it is not astonishing that considerable attempts were made, not so much to rationalize techniques, but to establish their scientific foundations. At the end of the 16th century and the beginning of the 17th, Galileo and Stevin were already on the way to doing this. To give another example, did not Wren, Huygens and
Wallis study the hammer in the middle of the 18th century? Similarly, the effects of projectiles and pile drivers were studied. It would be interesting to know more about the beginnings of a technology which
Renaissance men had only begun to glimpse, and which could only take shape once science had already been formed. "Science in the middle of the 17th century was sure of itself," writes Claire Salomon
Bayet, "believing that the domains of the life sciences and the science

===== PAGE 599 =====

582
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
of machines could be opened to geometric jurisdiction ( . . . ) Mathema tical "science" is mature whereas experimental "technology" is occa sionally practical, always curious, and almost never illustrative in nature."
Inasmuch as technology "aims, according to Guillaume and Sebes tik, for the constitution of a discourse on technical operations like a scientific type of discourse," Salomon-Bayet has demonstrated that it has progressed through three stages. In 1675, Buot envisaged this discourse as a catalogue; from 1693 to 1695, Filleau des Billettes considered the conditions of the possibility of such a discourse which he wished to be both logical and methodical; in 1699, the reform of the
Academie des sciences marked the failure of this attempt. Indeed, in 1675, the king of France charged the Academie des sciences with the task of "examining the methods of writing a treatise on mechanics, with an exact description of all the machines which were currently used in industry in France and throughout Europe." The work of devel oping this was given to Buot who envisaged a two-part treatise on mechanics: one part was to be theoretical, setting out principles, and the second part was to explain everything which concerned the practice and application of the theory of machines. This idea does not appear to have progressed much further, and in any case Buot died in 1677. In
1692, the Abbe Bignon took up the project. He established a group comprising three specialists named by the king: Father Sebastien,
Jaugenon and des Billettes, as well as an engraver, Simonneau, a "consultant," Anisson, director of the royal printing works, and a technical adviser, Granjon, engraver of moulds and patterns. For the first time on the 16th of January, 1693, Bignon gathered together what might have become an Academy of arts. "A very precise treatise on the arts which was almost to be a type of Encyclopedia." It was these men who began a description of the arts, designing patterns for the plates.
They began by describing the printing press while numerous plates were engraved. The reform of 1699 brought into the Academie des sciences some of the members of this abortive Academie des arts. The rules prescribed by the Academie revised the examination of machines, which was to become the initial section of a school, and the continuation of the work directed by Bignon. We know that it was only in 1762 that the first volume was published, after the scandal concer ning the plates of Diderot's Encyclopedie.

===== PAGE 600 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
583 Description was certainly a beginning but demonstration could also have been employed, in the same way that a physicist or a doctor operates by demonstration in order for his science to be understood.
From the first half of the 17th century, the idea of an exhibition of machines was implemented. Arthur Birembault has soundly assessed an exhibition of machines which was held at Paris, in the rue de la
Harpe, in 1683. It was an exhibition of twenty-one scale models which had been executed from some of the drawings from the "theatre of machines," by Besson, Bockler, Ramelli, Salomon de Caus, and
Strada. The other models represented the inventions of two inventors.
The explanation of Machines and moving Forces must be considered to be a serious, important and very useful subject to the Public, for by theory and practice everyone may quickly acquire a level of perfection in his craft or profession. It is an illustrative means which instructs by a single glance , and which is determined by concrete and effective experiment.
Demonstration on the one hand, and experimentation on models on the other, were therefore the means by which one hoped to achieve perfection. This whole programme was to be continued up until the
Conservatoire des arts et metiers was established, born from Vaucan son's model collection, and to the research undertaken by Watt on the scale model.
Let us recapitulate all that: scientific description, demonstration and experimentation were the real means by which technology aspired.
And like the Encyclopedists of the 18th century, the only difference being that they merely justified their ultimate purpose, we wonder what the value of technical literature was towards the end of the 17th century, as the latter did not have the same lustre which it had known from the end of the 15th to the end of the 16th century.
Technical literature of the 17th century often resumed works which were more than a century old, such as the various editions of La
Maison rustique or out-dated formulae such as the "theatres of machines." Among the latter, we may cite those of Della Porta (1601), and of Branca (1629) in Italy, the works of the Germans, Zeisingk
(1612), Schott (1657), Strada (1629) and Bockler (1601), and the Frenchman, Salomon de Caus (1615). Moreover, other works may be found up to the first half of the 18th century: Leupold (1724), Weid lerus (1728), Konig (1752) or Polhem (1729). In more specialized

===== PAGE 601 =====

584
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
sectors, aside from works on architecture, there are no more of these treatises which had been so numerous and diverse in the 16th century.
One may cite a Spanish tract on metallurgy by Alvarez Alonso Barba (1640). And even if valuable works had been produced at the end of the century, they virtually all disappeared in the wave of mediocre works which were easy to read and often well illustrated. Such were the works of Barnaby Googe (Four Books of Husbandry, 1577) who had already realized the revolutionary role of weeding arable land and artificial meadows. The 17th century thus appeared to lack a certain technical vigour. This may explain why evolutions were so slow, or at least the lack of of any significant change.
A historian of agriculture recently characterized the 17th century as having "a deficiency of innovatory spirit. " Certain trends were carried on: buckwheat gradually spread throughout the areas where millet had been grown, hops spread from the continent to Britain where it enjoyed a remarkable circulation. It would seem that it was Flanders and Holland who had, at this epoch, begun the evolution which was to create the wealth of the 18th century, by advocating the practice of weeding crops in the fields and introducing fodder plants, particularly clover and lucerne. Ship building presents an almost identical picture.
The shape and conception of the ship had slowly evolved until the most perfect results, such as the "Sovereign of Sea" by Pett (1637) and the
French "Couronne" (1638) were established examples. But at the same time, research was continued in order to build crafts on a more scientific basis. Thus we witness the Hydrographie by Father Fournier
(1643), although a point was reached where the titles of such works were more ambitious than the content, but this nevertheless expresses the hope for a rational technology. In Britain, Anthony Deane pro duced a Doctrine of Naval Architecture (1670), in France, Father Paul
Hoste wrote a Theorie de la construction des vaisseaux (1697), where, for the first time, the centre of gravity is located above the centre of hydrostatic pressure. The dry dock was this age's only novelty, which facilitated repairs considerably.
This is also true of the techniques used in the exploitation of resources. Mining techniques had been hardly modified at all. By comparing Agricola writing in the middle of the 16th century and le
Morand writing in the middle of the 18th, one is aware that methods and means had remained stationary. At the very most we should point out the mechanical devices used to bring up products of either open

===== PAGE 602 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
585 cast or deep mines developed by the Swede, Polhem. Barely any modifications were made in the iron and steel industry apart from improvements made to some machines, such as splitting machines. As for the rest, the technology described by Diderot in the Encyclopedie remained exactly the same as when it had been established at the end of the 15th century.
Technical initiative was not completely lacking, however. In many domains there were partial inventions which made possible the devel opment of old techniques which had not progressed at all. A few examples of these will suffice here. It seems that chemical techniques were noticeably perfected from the middle of the 17th century, although no radical change took place. A historian of chemistry has recently demonstrated that there was no actual modification in the methods used, but arrangements were better and conceived in a more rational way: as was the case for alums and aquafortis, and mineral salts. A number of mineral compounds used for dyeing were intro duced: borax, for example, or tin chloride designed to make a brilliant scarlet mordant from cochineal. The perfecting of candle making led to better lighting and made possible public lighting at the end of the
17th century: we know that in France, in 1697, public lighting was recommended for every town of a certain size.
Some techniques even demonstrate remarkable vitality. The start of mechanisation was a result of many small, often repeated operations.
Such was the case for ribbon weaving and stocking knitting: one stocking required 150,000 to 157,000 stitches. The bar loom, invented by Willem Diericksoon Van Sonnevelt, from Hondschoote, then a great textile centre, was apparently established in 1604, enabling several ribbons to be woven simultaneously on only one loom and operated by one worker. Up to twenty-four ribbons could be produced with these synchronized movements. We have already discussed
William Lee's loom for knitting stockings (1589), which was improved, by Hindret's addition of fairly complicated transmissions, in France in the middle of the 17th century. Cloth-crimping machines, rollers for glossing sayettes, and gig mills henceforth produced continuous oper ations, although movements in wool weaving machines were still not synchronized. In cloth shearing, stopping and starting procedures were made easier: this machine was to lead to some problems in
Leyden, in 1680. The best idea was the crimping machine, set up in Troyes in 1678.

===== PAGE 603 =====

586
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
It was also at this date that the automatic weaving loom was introduced, designed by M. de Gennes, a French naval officer, an account of which appeared in the Journal des Savants. It was undoub tedly inspired by the ribbon loom, except that there was no shuttle.
Whatever, the progress made is measurable: indeed we begin with a series of old projects, many of which are illustrated in Leonardo da
Vinci's notebooks, continue with the first sketches of the beginning of the 17th century, as is demonstrated by Zonca, and then finally to the achievements of the middle of the century. Thus, technological lines are perfectedly continued.
What had formerly been potentiality in techniques in the domain of turning at the end of the 16th century, was to become reality during the
17th century. A clear evolution may be noted starting with Besson, in 1578 when the first. mandrels and the first back rests were introduced on a lathe, to L'Art de tourner by Father Plumier (1693). The spherical glass making machine, where the glass was stuck on the nose of the mandrel was produced by Father Cherubin in 1671 , as was the first sketch of a face lathe. Besson and Ramelli had developed drawings for conical gears by the end of the 16th century. In 1650-1660, Gerard
Desargues envisaged the epicycloid line of the gear cogs. Shortly afterwards, Philip de la Hire was to write a Traite des epicycloides et leur usage en mecanique (1694). In the following year the same author was to publish a Traite de mecanique which to some degree reunited the preoccupations of the powers that be and the desires of the French
Academic des sciences.
The strength of centralization, and better organisation of public services enabled "national and regional development" perhaps more than technical inventions, to an extent which had been formerly unknown. We have cited the introduction of dry docks and the massive work on ports which the increase of ship building required. We know that it was during the 17th century that the idea of linking these great river basins was put into practice: from the beginning of the 17th century, the famous Briare canal linked the Loire and the Seine basins.
In 1626, a plan was initiated to link the Rhine and the Meuse. Need one emphasize the canal in Southern France, which links two seas with twenty-six locks from the Atlantic to Toulouse and with seventy-four locks and a tunnel from the Mediterranean coast?
The process was similar for water conveyances. Although large suction and force pumps were still in use, albeit only usable for a

===== PAGE 604 =====

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
587 limited flow, improvement was made in the method of canalization.
The first cast iron pipes seem to have been made for the waters at Versailles, and in the elevator machine at Marly, so cast iron pipes favourably replaced wood or pottery pipes. The difficult problem of relaying pipes over a great distance, that is to say transporting energy over some distance, still had to be practically solved. At the end of the
16th century, the Spaniard, Turriano, had designed mechanisms based on articulated parallelograms, a system which the Swede, Polhem, was to use at the beginning of the 18th century. Salomon de Caus partially saw the solution to this but could not develop any practical application. It should be noted that the surveyor's level with telescopic sights by Picard (1675) considerably facilitated canal construction. But we know how they wavered between the various solutions for the waters at Versailles, at the expense of quite useless work.
It was possibly in the domain of clock-making that absolutely fundamental inventions were made. Huygens is credited with the development of the balance, using the principle of small isochronous oscillations (1657), then the regulating spiral spring (1675) which increased precision fivefold.
In fact, the 17th century lived well on the new techniques and ideas launched during the Renaissance. They sought "perfections," devel opments, installations, and concrete achievement of all that had been previously envisaged, rather than new ideas. Let us go even further:
the Encyclopedie by Diderot and d' Alembert more or less represents the techniques of the classical epoch (in particular the steam machine which is almost hidden in the article on "Fire") . Upon reading it, one may gauge the limits reached by the technical system of this epoch. It also demonstrates how little the crank and connecting rod system was used in various machines, the difficulties of energy production, and the extent to which the use of the small tool was henceforth fixed. "The artist's hand is essential," declares d'Alembert's preamble. Because the research carried out by the Encyclopedists was done in
"workshops," we have a reflection of a system which was traditional rather than the current system of the time, and the Encyclopedie therefore represents the preceding system. The notion of technical progress, and the idea of a new technical system were perfect strangers to this publication which -was so rich in information in other respects.
Classical technology is the technology of a static population. It is also the technology of a strict world, half closed in on itself. Demo-

===== PAGE 605 =====

588
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
graphic pressure after the first third of the 18th century, and outside expansion - no longer in the form of exploitation but in the direction of the conquest of new markets - were to produce tensions which generated progress.

===== PAGE 606 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
The term "industrial revolution" is now an accepted part of historical vocabulary. However this does not mean that it is no longer the subject of disputes or that it no longer needs justification. But if we examine this factor in the same perspective as the rest of the book, the justification is self-evident. A new technical system was created, which was one of the fundamental elements of a new economic recovery.
This is evident in Great Britain from the end of the 18th century, and in other European countries from 1825-30, to 1850-60. It seems, therefore, that this framework does actually coincide with reality.
Some historians tend to confuse industrial with technical revolution, and since they consider that technical progress was a gradual conti nuous process, they deny that there was any industrial revolution. This is probably just a matter of semantics. If the history of techniques is merely a list of inventions, then of course it will appear continuous. If however, - and this is what we have attempted to do - one considers the progress made in the 18th century it is evident that all the main manufacturing processes, and not such-and-such process or machine, represent the end result of new techniques applied during the
1780's. Furthermore, it was around this time that a balance was established between the interrelated techniques. So it was only after this period, after 1780, that is, that the new techniques reached their full potential: and the same phenomenon is evident in other periods.
An industrial revolution must be dealt with in global terms, not in terms of isolated disparate elements.
This discussion will not be concerned with the details of new techniques and their development, even though such research is fascinating and extremely informative. We shall be concentrating

===== PAGE 607 =====

590
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
more on the conditions surrounding the changes, and on their global aspects and consequences. And since this is in a historical context, it would be useful first to establish a chronological framework.
The long economic recession which historians generally believe to have begun about half way through the 17th century, continued for almost a century, and it was only between 1730 and 1750 that condi tions began to look more favourable. There is little need to dwell here on two other important phenomena: the steady decrease in the influx of precious metals, and the gradual lowering of prices.
It is difficult to be precise about the period at which this recovery began, but this is hardly surprising, since it relates to different sectors, different regions and different countries. At any rate, the upturn only became really evident after 1780, and in some places not until after
1800.
Similarly, it is possible to identify several different attitudes which appear to be quite important on a technical level, in that they form the very basis of a politics of technology. One of these attitudes is best described as "stationary," and Colbert's policies are a perfect example of this. A stationary state necessarily implies a stable level of quanti ties, in terms of both population and production and this is on a global scale. Realistic policies can succeed, therefore, only in modifying the apportioning of these quantities, and that implies that no further technical progress is possible. Efficient policies consist in perfecting what already exists, developing techniques to their full potential, and importing advanced techniques from abroad. We saw that with a few rare exceptions, i.e. , those who were preparing for the ensuing transformation, this was the politics of monopoly.
The lowering of prices created difficulties for the entrepreneur of various kinds, but alongside this, the cost of research was also decreasing.
The latter attitude was reinforced by two important factors which seemed to have played a major role, and which therefore deserve more precise investigation.
The first of these is the exhaustion of some raw materials which were at the basis of preceding technical systems. One example of this is the shortage of wood which affected many countries, in particular Great
Britain.
The second factor is the change in colonial policies. It was no longer a matter of the economically most advanced countries using the raw

===== PAGE 608 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
591 materials of other parts of the world, but of their attempting to create markets in these areas for their products. This expansion was double edged: not only did it concern the discoveries made in preceding periods, but also the creation of new colonial empires. The conse quences of the treaty of Utrecht in 1713 was of primary importance in this respect.
All this led to adaptations in techniques, which in turn led to the formation of a new technical system. The interlocking of techniques and the establishment of a satisfactory equilibrium required the per fection of certain techniques, inventions and innovations, and the integration of older inventions which had been unable to find a place in preceding systems. We made this point earlier: a technical system is not simply the result of inventions made during the particualar period when it is developing, but also of anterior elements.
CONCORDANCES
We need to consider the circumstances surrounding these devel opments, just as we did for other epochs. This seems more effective and significant than trying to establish hypothetical lines of causality, especially since these causal relationships are usually reciprocal. We are not claiming circumstances are all-important by giving them priority; they are simply providing a framework for a clearer discus sion.
Several different conclusions have been drawn from research into demographic movements. The population in England rose consider ably during the second half of the 18th century to reach 5.5 million inhabitants by 1700. This figure was six and a half million by 1750, and nine million by 1800.
As for France, opinion is more divided, but it is generally thought that there was a sharp increase in population during the second half of the 18th century. Some historians believe that this rise simply served to fill the gaps created by the severe losses sustained in the 17th century, and that during the last decades of the Ancien Regime, the population figures stagnated or actually declined in some areas. It is generally thought that the populations of Spain and Germany rose during this

===== PAGE 609 =====

592
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
period, but more precise studies are needed to confirm or deny this very general view.
The causes of these demographic movements still need satisfactory assessment. One important reason however, seems to be a marked decrease in mortality. This is an important point, particularly with regard to the evolution of techniques.
But any study of a population cannot confine itself to quantitative research. And from our point of view particularly, the distribution of population is very important. The proportion of able-bodied men, for example, is very important - and the decrease in mortality is particu larly interesting in this sense. The size of the professional class is also significant. There is a view prevalent in history today that rural workers in England were forced by enclosures to leave the countryside and enter the towns. The large available work-force provided the conditions for a growth in industry. It seems, however, that the reality was not quite so simple. However, it is no less true that if techniques are not sufficiently developed, a large workforce is needed to compen sate for the lack of technology at certain stages of production. Even if the depopulation of the countryside was not as systematic as it has been made out to be, nonetheless, the influx into the towns was sufficient to meet the needs of industry. Aside from this, technical progress is certainly sustained by demographic rises, but too sharp an increase can have adverse effects.
As for economic developments, there is little need to discuss them here: there are already plenty of excellent studies on the subject.
Recent studies have revealed that the main thrust of economic recovery took place between 1730 and 1760; this was the very period when there was a renewed impetus to technical progress, indeed, the latter began just after this period. One point is clear, therefore. The economic recovery preceded the establishment of the new technical system. The research of Dean and Cole has shown that there was a marked change in economic development from about 1730, at a time when none of the great inventions had been put into productive use.
This leads us to believe that before the main movement of technical progress began, there was an intermediate stage of imperceptible progress coinciding with the increase in population and other phe nomena. It was only after this stage that both the technical changes and the main demographic increase actually took place. And was this not so of the 15th century before the enormous expansion of the 16th

===== PAGE 610 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
593 century? Research in this field would be absolutely invaluable to the history of techniques.
As always, this general view needs to be qualified. Growth needs to be measured in quantitative terms, but the qualitative changes also need to be examined. Production during the first decades of economic growth was very different to what it had been at the beginning of this period, and this was partly due to technical progress.
Technical progress is always conditional upon investment, which means that there had necessarily been an accumulation of capital.
Some countries had the benefit of a stable currency which had been established at the beginning of the century: this was particuarly true of
