Great Britain and to a lesser degree, France. The extent of this increased wealth is not clear. Global enrichment was necessary, but so too was the accumulation of capital in the hands of potential investors.
Here again, the mechanisms are difficult to define: some believe that the capital accumulated in commercial sectors, and this was probably true of Great Britain, while others believe that the landed sectors were more significant - and this in turn was probably true of France.
However, this problem is a marginal one, but it is worth noting because it may help explain the differences in technical activity between the two countries.
But even this is not sufficient to explain such a complex movement.
Investment is partly controlled by mechanisms of a legal nature. The new technical system necessarily changed the dimensions of enterprises, and this in turn generated changes in the latters' legal organisation, which after all represents a field of techniques. Less bound by rigid structures, Great Britian was able to adapt fairly rapidly. But France was able to change only by bending the law (this was the illegal beginning of the public or limited liability company, in the modern sense of the term, which was originally called the "false" partnership) or by modifying it (the Miromesnil project at the end of the 18th century)
We shall not dwell any more on this exterior aspect of technical progress. In order to make clear certain aspects, however, some related problems should be considered.
It is difficult to appreciate the man of eighteenth century Europe in his relation to techniques. It is no less difficult to do this in a few words.
It is perhaps easiest to establish a brief schema which contrasts two opposing attitudes. On the one hand there is Diderot's "Encylopedie"

===== PAGE 611 =====

594
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
which holds an important place in technical preoccupations. On the other hand there is the English classical school, of which Adam Smith was the prime exponent. He had only a mild interest in techniques and their effect on the economy: at the very most he was responsible - apart from the famous concept of the division of labour - for the idea that the "secrets of production" could affect prices, independently of supply and demand. On the one hand there were the good mercanti lists, the governments whose policies were actively in favour of technical progress, while on the other, there was the general public, who were more or less indifferent to the transformations taking place.
In other words, it seems that technical progress was largely the concern of small groups of people who, according to the different countries, came from different environments. In Great Britain, despite all that was written on this subject, technical progress was mainly the work of men who were already engaged in production, or at any rate working fairly closely with it. By far the majority of inventions were due to entrepreneurs or foremen, or to machine builders or carpenters. There is no better example of this than the Darby family in the field of iron and steel production, or the association between
Boulton, a famous entrepreneur, and an instrument maker employed by the University of Glasgow called Watt. Another example is the
Wilkinson family, this time in the field of machine tools. Exactly the same kind of examples can be found in the textile industry. True, there is a priest among the list of these inventors, but he came from an industrial region where he could not possibly have been unaware of certain technical difficulties. The main characteristic of this phase of
British technical history was the individual nature of these efforts.
Boulton and Wilkinson represent perhaps the only examples of techni cal research being undertaken by large enterprises. The coherence of advanced techniques depends largely on a country's economy.
In France, on the other hand, the work was done on a collective level, and was much more systematic. No doubt this was partly due to the lack of dynamism on the part of entrepreneurs, who had to be spurred on by government policy. The best example of this is a text by
Turgot written in 1773, which passed almost unnoticed:
After removing all taxes on the production, transport, selling and consumption of foodstuffs, there can only be one thing left to do to help trade. That is by training, and by the encouragement of scientific and artistic research which may help further devel opment, and especially by helping to extend the knowledge of processes which have been kept secret through plain avarice.

===== PAGE 612 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
595 Turgot's method was to use colbertist policies: this consisted essentially of sending missions abroad to learn about advanced tech niques. Various missions were organised to Britain, and also to
Germany and the northern countries.
This collective role of the government was expressed as Turgot hoped it would be, in the provisions made for technical training. Their primary function was to establish a state body of technical personnel, but this was in fact largely superseded by the state itself, which played a large part in the promotion of technical progress. A centralised state needed a trained technical personnel, and this gave rise to the famous higher training schools of today, such as the Ecole des Ponts et chaussees, which emerged from a small office of designers, and all the military schools, (engineering, artillery, and shipbuilding). At the same time, a body of manufacturing inspectors was established to enforce very strict industrial controls. In fact, all these institutions had been planned during the time of Colbert. These executives were soon closely involved in industry, through their attention to products supplied to the state, and they paid increasing attention to techniques in particular. There is no better example than the creation of the Ecole des mines at the end of the 18th century, which was designed not only to establish a body of inspectors, but also to improve the technical knowledge of those developing the mines.
Technically trained personnel and state inspectors naturally enhanced technical progress. And considering the vaguely defined position of the civil servants at this time, it is easy to understand the close interaction between representatives of the state and those of the private sector which is especially apparent in the latter half of the 18th century. A considerable number of the inspectors came from this private sector. After they had been employed on a mission abroad they joined the public sector on a permanent basis. In an industrial country which was hardly inclined towards technical development, state interference was a vital factor of progress. Other industrialised countries were in a similar position, such as Germany, for example, where a mining school was set up in Freiberg at the beginning of the
18th century. Similar schools were also set up in Sweden and Austria.
These differences were also apparent in other areas, one of which was technical literature. French industrial controls were just as much technical as they were economic or administrative. It was only natural, therefore, that they should seek to codify the use of techniques. This

===== PAGE 613 =====

596
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
gave rise to the publication of treatises which promoted the correct rules; we shall see later how this idea was systematised. Britain was more pragmatic- the government simply trusted in the technical minds and imagination of British practitioners. Only one technical treatise was produced in the 18th century, an important one nevertheless, written by Jethro Tull and entitled Horse-hoeing Husbandry, the first edition of which was published in 1731. Even though the author was interested in certain new crops, such as beetroot, sainfoin and alfalfa,
"he does not" according to A.-J. Bourde, "consider any way in which they could be included in a type of agriculture with tranformed structures of cultivation." He considers them only as tributary crops, outside of the traditional patterns of farming, but protected against common grazing land. It is true that Britain had a long tradition of such treatises. But is it not surprising that she failed to produce during this time a single treatise on naval construction?
France, on the other hand, was steeped in technical literature which, furthermore, was of a somewhat erratic quality. In effect, it represents the course begun by Colbert and culminating in the industrial and technical controls to which they were so partial. It was not a matter of progressing, but simply of perfecting that which already existed, and establishing the rules. Such was the nature of the technical part of
Diderot's Encylopedie. The prospectus itself actually recognised the impossiblity of producing a systematic technical literature, or of a technology which did not simply consist of descriptions:
Furthermore, the workers are the artists, and it is not by consulting books that people learn their trade. In this work, craftsmen will encounter perspectives which may be new to them, and observations which may otherwise only have become apparent after many years of work. The studious reader is offered that which they would have learned from observing a craftsman at work in order to satisfy their curiosity; we offer craftsmen, on the other hand, the chance to learn from philosophers, and thereby hopefully to advance further towards perfection.
Which brings us back to descriptions once more. It would be interesting actually to establish just how much of the Encyclopedie was original, and how much was borrowed from the technical literature which it so scornfully frowned upon, and that is not to mention the pillaging of the plates from the Academie des Sciences. Diderot's greatest merit is to have established a table of different types of knowledge which includes techniques. This was also attempted by the
Academie des sciences with the Description des arts et metiers, begun at

===== PAGE 614 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
597 the end of the 17th century but never completed. The publication of the Encylopedie provided the necessary impetus. The first volume,
L'Art du charbonnier (the charcoals burner's art) appeared in 1761.
But these works had been in preparation for a long time, and some of them were quite obviously out of date in technical terms - the same was true of the Encylopedie, because much of that was copied from older works. Between 1761 and 1788, seventy-eight volumes were published, dealing with techniques of all kinds. Only a part of them dealt with industrial techniques, or with the ensemble of the tech niques of an industry. Many of them examined artisan techniques, and finally a smaller portion dealt with single techniques on their own. The first volumes were quite traditional, while the latter reveal a greater sensitivity on the part of the authors to technical progress. The last major collection was begun at the very end of the 18th century, and was finally completed in the early 1800's. This was the Encylopedie meth odique of which two parts are of most interest to us: the Arts et metiers mecaniques (1783-1791) and Manufactures, arts et me tiers (1784-1790).
There is also some valuable information to be found in the scientific volumes, however. Although this new publication was meant to pay hommage to Diderot, it was also a critique of the Encylopedie. The new Encylopedie was a compilation and revised collection of previous works - furthermore they were also able to include new elements which had emerged as a result of the technical progress made in
Britain.
If the historian of techniques studied nothing else but these antholo gies, they would be justified in thinking that there was a fixed level of techniques, set, precisely, at what it was before the great technical transformation of the 18th century. However, there were many publi cations besides these. One of the most prolific and competent writers with a truly encyclopedic mind was Duhamel du Monceau, who was appointed inspecteur general de la marine (general inspector of the navy). Apart from the seventeen volumes that he wrote for the
Academie des Sciences' collection, he published some of the greatest works on French agronomy. His Traite de la culture des terres (1756) was adapted from Jethro Tull, and the Elements d'agriculture (1762) was the result of his own experience. He converted his land in
Denainvilliers in the Gf atinais, into a veritable laboratory for farming experiments. His work with shipping had made him an expert on wood, and this is reflected in his Traite des arbres written in 1755. He

===== PAGE 615 =====

598
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
also wrote Elements de /'architecture navale (1752), which was a basic text used in the Ecole des constructeurs de navires (school of naval construction), founded in 1765. These few indications are quite suffi cient to convey the prime importance of these works in French techno logy.
The abundance of technical literature is equally apparent if one reverses the approach and deals only with one particular technique.
There were twelve treatises on naval construction written in the 17th century, and sixteen in the following one, many of which were important works. The first main work on this subject was written by the
Dutch Witsen, in 1671. The first theory of ships was expounded by Bernoulli in 1714, and this was followed in 1749 by Euler's work. Many of these works represent a two-fold consideration, the technicians' practical experience, and their knowledge of theory. Such is the nature of works by Bouguer (1727 and 1746), Duhamel (1752), Chapman from Sweden (1768), and Vial de Clerbois from France (1776).
We would need an entire chapter to discuss adequately the technical literature of the 18th century. There are famous works dealing with all types of industry, both traditional and modern: those of Swedenborg,
Bouchu and Courtivon for metallurgy, Morand for mining, Berthoud for clock-making, Monge for cannon-construction, and Belidor for
"engineering science" and hydraulic architecture. It should be recog nised that the contribution of France to technical literature was an important one, no doubt for the reasons we discussed above. The
Swedish and the Germans were also important in this respect, expe cially for their work on mining and metallurgy. The Dutch wrote some important works on naval construction. Again, we can only repeat that analytical inventories would be of great use to historians of techniques:
some of them are being compiled at the moment.
We have already emphasised the close links between science and techniques. A science which is in the process of being created, or that has just undergone some transformation needs technical support, either to provide problems to be solved, or to permit experimentation.
It is no less evident that at a certain stage of development, a technique needs the support of scientific notions which can solve problems and allow techniques to be considered on a general level and not just in terms of particular cases. Studies by Maurice Daumas have shown how much science has benefited from techniques which have provided the former with every type of equipment. On the other hand, it would have

===== PAGE 616 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
599 been impossible to conceive of the steam engine without some scienti fic knowledge of atmospheric pressure. Again, it is not a matter of deciding which of the disciplines is more important than the other, but simply of establishing the reciprocal relationships between them, and showing how each can favour or hinder the development of the other.
Of course as far as our particular point of view is concerned, it is true that in many cases, scientific knowledge is not necessary. During this period, for example, it was not required in the textile industry. In other cases, science could have helped techniques but the latter was able to advance anyway without the support of the former. Metallurgy was also able to develop before there was proper scientific understanding about the different Etats de fer (states of iron), to use the title of the definitive pamphlet published by Berthollet, Monge and Vander monde in 1788. However, the phlogiston theory had considerably delayed this moment and had sent Reaumur, for example, completely up the wrong paths. The steam engine was developed at least half a century before the theory was expounded.
Most links between science and techniques are situated somewhere in the middle of these two extremes. It is obvious, for example, that the chemical industry could not develop until after the new discover ies and techniques were made available, until people were familiar with the work of Lavoisier, Priestley and Cavendish. This was an indispensable condition. Watt reaped great benefit from the scientific milieu at Glasgow University and from the works of his professors, especially Black, and it was this that enabled him to complete the development of his steam engine. In the first case, science was an indispensable base, while in the second, it was simply a support.
Another example of the latter case, are Bernoulli and Euler's works on ships which may have helped develop ship construction in a certain way, but certainly did not completely transform it. Euler's important work on reaction wheels did not bear fruit until the early
19th century.
There are various different points of contact between technique and science, then. There is often chronological concordance between the two courses of development, and also concordance in terms of activity and current areas of interest. Once more, we are bound to note both the serious lack of research on this subject, and a lack of serious research: studies of this period have always separated the two disci plines, to the extent that they are now extremely difficult to link.

===== PAGE 617 =====

600
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
We have therefore been forced to build a new model, which will certainly not be the last. The best approach would be to define the common thresholds of science and techniques, and also the break points where science and techniques become autonomous, and where there is no possibility of reciprocity.
The creation of a chemical industry during the last part of the 18th century was a direct result of the emergence of modern chemistry: the production of artificial soda and the use of chlorine to bleach materials would have been inconceivable before this time. But apart from this, modern chemistry has had little effect on other techniques. The role of carbon was clearly established in iron chemistry a year before the
French Revolution, but this discovey had no effect whatsoever on either traditional or modern techniques: all descriptions of iron pro duction in technical literature make it quite apparent that those actually involved in iron production went entirely by how things looked, and nothing else. Reaumur's research was different in this respect. however. Reaumur was actually able to control some aspects of the treating of iron and steel, (the running of blast furnaces and quenching, for example) by examining and analysing these "appea rances," such as the breaking of a test-tube, for example. But it was years - not until the beginning of the end of the 19th century - before this research was to yield effective results for iron and steel production.
This industry was finally delivered of one stranglehold when the phlogistic theory was finally abandoned.
The developments made in physics were useful only insofar as they enabled phenomena to be quantified, which in turn helped provide solutions to certain problems. A few works written in the first half of the 18th century, like those of Bernoulli and Euler, for example, made some important developments possible. Black's discoveries about latent heat put Watt on a fertile path. But all these relationships were limited. In most cases, numeric controls established empirically were sufficient for technics, and the building of boat hulls is a good example of this. Duhamel did not trust the work of theorists and had little confidence in the application of mathematics. Black, who was a member of the Royal Society, attempted to apply certain calculations to the dimensions of steam engine cylinders and their effect: these attempts did not provide any new effective methods, and Clapeyron's charts were not assembled until 1834. Agriculture reaped few benefits from the progress of botany and plant physiology.

===== PAGE 618 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
601 So in the vast majority of cases, techniques remained an empirical activity, with no permananent or significant links with science. Tech niques certainly helped the progress of science by extending its experi mental field, but it did not actually dictate what course these experi ments should take. There were still two types of logic: scientific logic, and the logic of an accumulation of results of experience, rather than experiment. There is no better example of this than ship building.
Colbert, for example, attempted to take the best elements of all ships in order to build the ideal vessel. Some of these notes still exist today, and they deserve careful research. In the 18th century, both the British and the French had different conceptions of the design of the hull, for example, but both studied the other's ideas carefully, and took the best elements from each. Technical progress was the fruit of observation, experiment and imagination - not the result of constructive reasoning, except in rare cases. It was for this reason that the emergence of the principal inventions was a slow process. The technique which emerged in Britain at the end of the 18th century, had been in embryo stage for over a century. It finally evolved as the result of productive failures and sucessful imitations. A study of technical failures would be extremely instructive.
There was a vague sense towards the end of the 18th century, especially in France, that science and techniques were very closely linked, and that only their reconciliation could bring about the estab lishment of a true form of technology, which was not mere discourse or description, or based on exterior appearance or empirical tables. The creation of the Ecole Polytechnique, which was to provide scientific training for civil servants whose occupations would be extremely diverse, is a striking concrete result of this awareness.
What we need most of all is an inventory of all the points of contact between science and techniques, but no-one has yet even begun such a project. There were some technicians during this period which could almost be called scientists, like Duhamel du Monceau, Belidor or
Grignon. And there were also scientists who were close to being technicians - the most famous of these was Bernoulli and Euler. A study of the different works of these men would provide valuable indications about the links between science and technique. It is possible that from the middle of the 18th century, scientists were able to provide technicians with refined models which potential users only managed to avoid.

===== PAGE 619 =====

602
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
GLOBAL PROGRESS
The ever-increasing interdependence of techniques, coupled with technical progress and the impetus created by economic growth has meant that the effects of successive technical revolutions have become more and more profound and far-reaching. There have been partial changes, affecting exclusively a number of important sectors whilst other sectors have developed and still maintained crude levels of development, without creating too many awkward distortions. The more technical advance that is made, the greater the hold of each individual technical development. Henceforth, technical changes were to become more and more global.
It is true that mechanisation began before the 18th century: we have already seen its remarkable development during the Renaissance period and the strong desire to develop mechanisation as much as possible. However, mechanisation assumes another dimension during the period with which we are presently concerned. This is no doubt due to several factors, which should be outlined.
Firstly, there was the question of materials. However well they were made, machines made out of wood could never be effective. The play of the assembly's fixings, the friction and the subsequent wear of the moving parts were major problems which were difficult to overcome.
These ramshackle machines were erratic and did not work well. They deteriorated rapidly and since they were made of wood, they were incapable of performing heavy duty work. Furthermore, the machine parts frequently broke and there were continual stoppages.
These problems are sufficient to explain the small amount of pro gress made in the field of mechanisation, considering its great potential resulting from the refinement of new mechanisms discovered during the Renaissance period. A brief survey of the illustrations in Diderot's
Encylopedia will confirm this point.
The idea of constructing machines using metal could have come quite naturally and without a great deal of effort to the engineers of the classical period. However, there were several factors making this impossible. The first one is concerned with the nature of the metal itself. For a long time, the iron produced was of a poor, and sometimes extremely bad quality. It was irregular, brittle, difficult to weld and difficult to work. The progress made in ferrous metallurgy during the

===== PAGE 620 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
603 18th century brought about the production of a metal which was both more resistant and easier to work, as well as being available in greater supply. Secondly, the implements used to work metal were incapable of creating even those assemblies which could be made using wood.
The working of metal, using manual tools, was, moreover, a longer and more costly process than that of working wood. The production of cast steel, which began in the middle of the 18th century- which we will discuss later - and subsequently, the production of machine-tools for the working of metal, in particular Wilkinson's reaming machine
(1772), threading machines (for screws and nuts), and planing machines, finally paved the way for machines made of metal. (figs. 1 and 2).
The development of mechanisation was limited, in the second place, by the fact that although there was an abundance of energy available overall - we are thinking here particularly of hydraulic energy - it was broken up into small units of production with only a minor capacity; it was dependent upon natural conditions, and was necessarily localised.
Apart from a few exceptions, the only example of which is the Bazacle mills near Toulouse which were run by a series of very primitive turbines, the units of production rarely surpassed ten horsepower.
Furthermore, the irregular flow and freezing of rivers provoked prolonged interruptions. Factories had to be installed beside the water sources, and this entailed either high transport costs or the creation of large hydraulic plants.
The steam-powered machine, which has become the symbol of the new technical system of the 18th century, freed the production of energy from many of its restrictions. There was now a possibility, albeit a progressive one, of obtaining larger quantities of energy, which could be produced anywhere. Similarly, however, this led to the choice of other sites or to the development of new means of transport in order to diminish the cost of the fuel, and at the same time it created the conditions for the concentration of manufacturing.
Finally, it is possible to point out at this stage a change in fuel types, and this concerned a whole series of operations (the production of mechanical, thermic and chemical energy). In certain regions, this was an urgent requirement, due to the notable disappearance of plant fuels. Thus, the basic trilogy of the new technical system, within which there were many interactions, finally came into being: metal, coal, machines powered by steam. The history of the steam engine is now

===== PAGE 621 =====

604
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS

Figure 1. - Cutting lathe (1 785).

===== PAGE 622 =====

TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
well-known. There were several stages to this process, due to a sequence of developments which were of both a scientific and technical nature.
I - Stage of the atmospheric machine: there are two main successive elements: the force exerted by steam and that of atmospheric pressure.
This in itself meant that the machine was severly limited, and this limitation was all the greater because the power capacity of atmo spheric pressure was weak.
Several attempts made before the end of the 17th century to overcome this have already been cited, most of which were hardly convincing. The machine designed by the marquess of Worcester is merely hypothetical. Papin's "Digester" was not a steam-driven machine, but was on the way to being so, and it did incorporate some useful elements, in particular the safety valve. Following this, Papin resumed his experiments with Huygen's gunpowder-machine and in
1707, devised the idea of an atmospheric machine with a floating piston, which was not without its merits. However, it was too compli cated and its productive capacity was less than satisfactory (fig. 3).
In 1698, Savery perfected the first, workable, steam-driven machine. The principle is extremely simple (fig. 4). It is based on a container with two valves. The pressure of steam forces the water in this container upwards, and the vacuum created by condensation draws up the water from below. This machine has no piston, therefore, and it is meant to perform only a single function, that of drawing up cold water - counter weight pump
Figure 3. - Principle of Papin's machine.

===== PAGE 624 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
607 water from the mines (fig. 5). No doubt, it was with this machine in mind that Papin, in 1707, reviewed his ideas and re-introduced the piston. This machine was capable of up to two-thirds horse-power, and apart from the idea of placing hot irons in the cylinder to heat up the water, Papin had some interesting, but as yet inapplicable notions, such as the superheating of steam, and the bell-jar, the idea of which he borrowed from Mautsch of Nuremberg. In fact, the latter served only to draw up the water and to let it flow down again onto a hydraulic wheel. The first, practical, steam-driven engine was designed by
Newcomen in 1712. Similarly, the basic principle was very simple (fig.
6). The pressure of steam forces a piston upwards, then the vacuum created by condensation enables atmospheric pressure to drive it back down again. The cylinder is, therefore. open at the top. Newcomen still had the same idea in mind: the pumping of water forced out again by steam. This was logical, since it was necessary to maximise the use of the more powerful form of energy. A whole balancing system served to counteract the superfluous weight, notably the rods, the piston and the entire apparatus of the pump.
II - Following stages considered by Watt: when Watt took up this problem, the steam-driven machine had already undergone considerwater to be drained
Figure 4. - Principle of Savery's machine.

===== PAGE 625 =====

608
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 5. - Savery's machine perfected by Kier and Wrigley.
able development: there was automatic control of the circuit's valves by using the balance beam; there was a regular input of steam provided by the use of multiple boilers in a single cylinder, and Cameau's boilers were incorporated into the system. All things considered, New comen's machine worked reasonably well. It was widely used, particu larly in mines in Cornwall. After 1765, Sm ea ton attempted to dispense with the cumbersome balancing crossbeam. His success was such that his design remained in use for a long time, even after the discoveries of
Watt.
Apart from the limitations resulting from the use of atmospheric pressure, Watt perceived the existence of another problem, which was that of the considerable heat loss, without any corresponding mechani cal effect. Heat was lost during the forming of condensation; and there

===== PAGE 626 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
609 piston cylinder =* mobile hearth Figure 6. - Principle of Newcomen's machine.
was a loss of steam when it was introduced into a cooled cylinder. The solutions to these problems were the following: the process should take place in a closed, not an open, cylinder, and condensation should take place outside the cylinder, not within. Between 1765 and 1769,
Watt perfected several vital elements: (a) a separate condenser was incorporated, which meant that the cylinder would be kept at a constant heat; (b) the heat within the condenser could be maintained by using an interior lining and steam circulated between this lining and the cylinder; (c) water was ejected by means of a condenser pump;
(d) atmospheric pressure was no longer used, it was replaced by the action of steam on both sides of the piston (fig. 7).
The capacity of the new machine was infinitely higher, which considerably lowered fuel consumption and therefore energy costs.
Furthermore, the construction of the machine had vastly improved after 1775 with Wilkinson's invention of the reaming machine for the production of cylinders. This apparatus was costly however, and was only profitable for use with larger machines.
The steam engine acquired its definitive aspects between 1780 and 1787: (a) the beam was made redundant by the use of two cylinders and the double effect of steam and its release on two sides of the piston (fig.
7) ; ( b) the invention of the articulated parallelogram assumed the beam's function of assuring the smooth play between the valves (fig.
8); (c) the ball governor regulated the running of the machine (fig. 9).

TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Watt's discoveries were used and adopted by other scientists and technicians. In 1781 , for example, Johnathon Hornblower used the double release of steam. The gaskets which were responsible for the considerable loss of steam were also improved. The fundamental principle of the steam engine remained the same; efforts were now concentrated on economising on steam, so that maximum use could be made of the heat-generated mechanical power.
As far as the mechanisms were concerned, there were no marked changes, and the crank and connecting rod system remained the essential element in the transmission and transformation of primary movement. It became singularly important only because the steam engine was a reciprocating engine. Watt's invention in 1781 of epicy coidal gearing, otherwise known as the planetary system, was in fact an aberration made necessary only by the excessive patenting in 1779 of the crank and connecting rod system.
The greatest acquisition of this period was as we have already suggested, the substitution of metal for wooden gearing. Machines were able to withstand much greater pressure, there was less using of parts, and they worked at more regular speeds. The use of cast iron meant that wheels and cogwheels could be made more economically.
In 1787, John Imison wrote a work entitled The School of Arts, in which he expounded the theory of encyclical gearing.
The most seminal idea was no doubt that of automatism. This was an ancient concept, as we saw during the discussion on Antiquity. There are several aspects regarding this.
The automatic control of movement was a relatively recent idea, and we saw this in the case of the grain hopper. The flywheel, which controls movement by means of inertia had been invented during the
Renaissance, and there was also a type of ball governor which in fact was little more than a modified flywheel. Watt made it into a perfect governor by designing it so that it acted directly on the intake of steam.
But there was more to this. They were able to conceive of a machine performing several different successive functions according to a given programme. The Greeks have left us some descriptions of automa tons: movement was created by a system of weights and ropes, and the various different effects by using cams. Such mechanisms were also possible using toothed wheels which were geared together: this was the principle of the first calculating machines. The work of the German

===== PAGE 630 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
613 Schickhardt (1592-1635), Pascal's machine (1642-1645), and findings of Leibniz (1673-1710) were milestones in this course of history.
The mechanical clock was also based on analogous principles. The only difficulty came with controlling the movement, but the adoption of the spiral spring in the second half of the 17th century almost solved this completely. The main issue was deciding which metal to use for these springs: it was with this object in mind that Huntsman created the method of forging steel in a crucible.
Mechanisms used in musical instruments and automatons both brought into play diverse types of other mechanisms, within which the use of cams was paricularly important (fig. 10) - Vaucanson from
France and the Droz brothers from Switzerland were particularly important in this respect.
The use of cams meant that programming was possible, and this development was further advanced when the punch card was invented.
making programming possible - the latter was indispensable if automatism was to develop. This was devised between 1728 and 1734 when Falcon, after Bouchon, used perforated cards to replace the draw-boys in weaving looms for tailored cloths. imitating perhaps the stitched cards used in the cylinders of hydraulic organs.
The oldest known automatic machine for industrial use was Jesse Ramsden's circular dividing engine devised in England in 1773. "The circle is turned through a determined arc by depressing the treadle, then the scriber traces the division on the plate."
All these factors - more powerful energy, more control, better methods of construction, and more diverse mechanisms - indicated the emergence of machine-tools. This had taken some time to get started, but the development was well under way by the end of the 18th century.
In 1751, Vaucanson built the first metal slide-lathe; in 1775, Wilkin son produced the first reaming wheel - and the importance of the latter has already been indicated with regard to the use of cylinders in steam engines. In 1795, Senot from France produced the first threading lathe, which similarly was made entirely of metal. This was before the lathes devised by Maudslay (1797) and Wilkinson (1798). In 1761 , the planing machine was devised by Focq.
Henceforth, industrial machinery was to come into widespread use in all industries. On the 17 March 1799, Japy, a watch maker applied for a patent for ten machines, in a quite remarkable effort to mechan-

===== PAGE 631 =====

614
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
i u '5 00 ise his industry. Also relevant here are the Hollanders used in making paper which in 1798 led to the invention of the continuous paper making machine by Robert, from France.
Also noteworthy in terms of this rather summary chronology is the mechanisation of all types of transport. After attempts had been made by many others to achieve the same object, Hulls, an American, succeeded in powering a ship with steam for the first time. This was in
1736. After Auxiron, Jouffroy d'Abbans from France also conducted

===== PAGE 632 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
615 some successful experiments on the Saone in 1782; he was emulated by several others experimenters, such as Fitch, Rumsey and Fulton. The first steam-powered land vehicle was devised by Cugnot (1770), and it incorporated an atmospheric machine. This was followed by a similar machine invented by Trevithivk ( 1796-1797). The first locomotives were built shortly after this, as we shall see below.
The trilogy of iron, coal and steam was by now clearly the basis of the new technical system which lasted throughout almost the entire 19th century. Two basic elements of the preceding system were thereby abandoned - these were wood and water. This transformation was reinforced by a whole series of radical changes in many techniques: the dates concerned are again in perfect concordance with the general view that the basis of this transformation was completed during the decade of 1780 to 1790.
The structures of development techniques were changed quite considerably during the 18th century, but to varying degrees. The transformations in agriculture and the iron and steel industry were marked, but that of the mining industry was apparent only indirectly, outside of the mining industry.
"British" agriculture was not a sudden development, it evolved gradually and was only fully incorporated in its entirety in the mid-18th century. Its emergence was linked to general changes, the most important of which was no doubt the considerable expansion of animal husbandry. Another important change in agrarian structures was the use of enclosures, which allowed for the birth of new techniques of cultivation. As a contemporary historian put it: "land had become the property of a few, and it was this small sector which developed the land, thereby creating veritable farming monopolies. The association between the lord and the large farmer in the 'landlord tenant system' became a major characteristic of the British countryside, and one of the elements of technical progress. " Three new developments allowed the incorporation of this new type of agiculture to take place:
A) The first of these was the emergence and expansion of root crops which cleaned and enriched the earth. Maize was unable to grow in the
North for climatic reasons, but the potato was a hardy crop and was suited to colder regions. The main expansion of potato-growing took place during the 18th century, when the plant was improved and finally became an acceptable form of alimentation.

===== PAGE 633 =====

616
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
B) The turnip represented a veritable revolution. It probably came from Belgium, but neither Googe (1577) nor Weston (1645) who both advocated growing it in fields and using it in artificial fodder had any success. Turnips were not used for ordinary food, but only for animals who used to pasture on fallow land at this time. Turnips were widely grown in Norfolk and Sussex to feed sheep. J. Tull advocated that turnip seeds should be sown in rows and only half-ploughed.
C) The idea of artificial grassland had been known for a long time (clover, sainfoin, and lucerne), but it had hardly been used. In fact, the obsession with wheat meant that fields were very rarely given over to plants considered as more or less useless. The combination of these various factors clashed with the earlier system of cultivation and with its surrounding social and agrarian elements (particularly the practices of fallowing land and using common grazing land). The first step was to grow root crops instead of fallowing land, which was now done on only half the scale in the some regions. The great agronomist Tull, passed by this essential transformation, and it was only in the the mid-18th century that the new type of triennial rotation between artificial grassland root crops and cereals was first practised. The main idea of this was to provide richer food for cattle, especially in winter.
This fundamental revolution was accompanied by more detailed changes: land which had not been cultivated because it was unsuitable for the earlier types of crops grown was now cleared and the soils were improved by drainage, marling, and liming. On the other hand, there was little change in agricultural implements. At the very end of this period, the first threshing machines appeared which were used for shelling, winnowing and sifting the grain. Their use was still limited, however.
Animal breeding, or at least one form of breeding underwent profound change within the agricultural system, but here again, the evolution was slow and gradual. It was only gradually that they began to import the best breeds, and practise cross-breeding by selection - in a far more rigourous way than in botanic science. The creation of new ovine and bovine breeds, not to mention the birth of a new thor oughbred English horse born from an Arab one and brought into
England at the end of the 17th century, was simply a matter of time.
These technical innovations in agriculture, however vague, were extremely important. They led not only to a more effective use ofland,

===== PAGE 634 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
617 the development of animal rearing and breeding, and consequently to the possibility of improving their diet, but also to the discarding of social structures and habits which had played such a major role in the civilizations of the Ancien Regime.
In contrast, the development of mines seemed to have conserved the technical systems which emerged during the Renaissance: the basis of these techniques was the same as it had been in Agricola's day, i.e. in the mid-16th century. There was some progress, but this affected only two areas.
The first was water drainage, and it was this which prompted the emergence of the steam-powered machine. The latter was first devel oped on a wide scale in this industry, and this in itself vastly improved mining development. The mining industry was particularly important in the expansion of the steam engine, because the latter was less profitable than other forms of energy, but it was the only one that was technically feasible for use in mines.
The second aspect of progress was also a marginal one, and concer ned the extraction of deposits. Wagons running on rails were used to transport coal either within the galleries or to the larger transport network beyond the mines - such as the canals and ports - and this practice represented a considerable economy. The first actual rails were made of wood, so that originally they were used only inside the mines. The drawings of the mine at Sainte-Marie-aux-Mines depict wooden planks which were used to prevent ruts. The first true stream lined rails with an adaptable wheel were first used in 1630. When metallurgical techniques were finally capable of producing a more durable metal, cast iron rails were adopted. Jars indicates that they were being used in Britain by 1765. They were also being used in Le
Creusot by 1783. There is a map of Newcastle in 1788 which clearly demonstrates the advantages of using such a technique for trans porting the goods to nearby rivers. This technique was also less costly.
The impact of this new method is a well-known phenomenon and does not need discussion here.
On the other hand, the techniques and tools of the actual mining processes underwent no significant change. The main difference was the gradual adoption of powder, which was being used in Chemnitz by
1625.
Of all those industries concerned with transforming matter, the iron and steel industry probably underwent the most complete and total

===== PAGE 635 =====

618
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
evolution. There were three basic elements to the equilibrium of classical iron and steel production: minerals and wood were used for fuel, and the motor force was water. The new form of this industry was based on minerals and coal. A detailed discussion of this is unneces sary; there has already been a lot of serious research into this subject.
We shall simply include a short summary of the evolution of this important aspect.
It was the shortage of wood which precipitated the need for a new fuel, and this could only be coal. The latter cannot be used directly because it contains sulphur which makes iron brittle, however if it is burned in isolation it produces coke which is perfectly suitable (there are still some types of coal from which coke cannot be obtained, or only with difficulty). The solution to the refinement of coal - or
"desulphering" as it was known in the 17th century - was first sought in the 16th century. It is possible that coke was already being used by the end of the 18th century to dry malt and in the copper industry, however we do not know enough about this to be sure. One interesting point however, is that Abraham Darby had worked successfully in the malt and copper industries before he became interested in iron and steel production. And it was he who in 1709 was the first to use coke in a blast furnace to produce cast iron. This new process was put to industrial use only later, around 1735-40. Most other areas were slow to adopt it after this time. Coke was more resistant to compression than charcoal, and furthermore, it could be used in much larger furnaces, thereby increasing production. Finally, the progress of the mining industry and transport reduced costs. Cast iron now became a cheaper and more useful product than many other materials (figs. 12 and 13).
Figure 1 2. - A large factory of the 1 8th century: Le Creusot (1785). The blower.

===== PAGE 636 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
619 Figure 1 3. - The installation of blast furnaces in Le Creusot (1 785). (From M. Daumas)
There was, because of the abundance of cast iron available, the risk of a bottleneck happening. Traditional methods were still used to refine cast iron into pure iron; these were slow, costly, and required a large workforce. It was Cort who in 1783-1784, after having tried in vain, was lucky enough to discover the process of puddling. This consisted of mixing the melted cast iron on a hearth in a refractory furnace with the decarbonising oxygen of the furnace's atmosphere. In this way the metal had no contact with the solid part of the fuel without having to use air-pumping apparatuses. Henceforth it was possible to process with coke all the cast iron produced.
Now that iron could be puddled after coke smelting, it was also necessary to find another instrument to forge it. The hydraulic hammer currently in use was unable to produce a sufficiently high yield. Once more it was Cort who solved this problem by putting the puddled iron balls through the cylinders of a fluted rolling mill. He patented this idea in 1783 (figs. 14 to 16).

===== PAGE 637 =====

620
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 14. - Breakdown and cogging tolls of a rolling mil (1 9th century).
Figure 1 5. - Breakdown rolls [left] and cogging rolls [right]; early 1 9th century.
We should also note that the adoption of the steam-powered machine for use in furnaces and rolling mills, finally dispensed with the need for hydraulic energy.
The last invention in this group was destined for a less general use. It was in order to produce steel springs for watches that Huntsman, from
England, developed the production of crucible steel, an old technique

===== PAGE 638 =====

Figure 16. - Finishing equipment at the Rybrick forges (c. 1 830). (From Daumas) Sl m z ti c:
1 ;i;.
r &;
< 0 r ç 0 z §

===== PAGE 639 =====

622
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
which had been known for centuries in some civilisations, but was hardly practised at all in western Europe. In fact it was simply the process of fusing cast iron and iron to produce steel.
Both iron and cast iron were produced in abundance by the end of the 18th century. They could be put to wider use, and they were cheaper than wood, being more resistant. These two metals had not yet fully replaced the use of wood by the end of the century, but the transformation was well under way.
We have already mentioned that iron (cast iron most probably) rapidly became indispensable in constructing machines, but in the building trade its progress was slower. By 1780, after the mechani sation of the textile industry had begun, spinning mills were equipped with iron columns rather than wooden beams. And in the same year, a
French architect, Louis, used an iron frame for the first time in the construction of Bordeaux theatre.
In 1787, iron barges were built for river and canal traffic. This period also saw the use of iron in naval construction for the first time. It was still sometime before this technique became general, however.
Then of course there was the replacement of wooden for iron rails for the transport of coal in English mines from about 1765.
The changes in iron production at the Darby factory in Coalbrook dale provide evidence of the increasing use of cast iron. Darby conceived of smelting iron for making cast iron pottery, and by 1758, the factory had already cast more than a hundred cylinders for steam powered machines. In 1767 the first iron rails were made. In 1779, the first metal bridge was built across the Severn: it was 90 metres long,
7.30 metres wide and the arch was 30 metres high. The entire bridge was constructed with pieces of moulded cast iron. This increasingly diverse use of cast iron is ample evidence of its advantages over other metals.
As we mentioned earlier, the chemical industry was only just beginning to evolve at this time. and by the end of the 18th century the brilliant future that lay ahead was barely discernible.
The base product was soda, obtained from plants. Sulphuric acid (then called vitriolic acid) and hydrochloride were hardly used. The chemical industry at this time mainly consisted of making soap and vitriol.
A whole series of discoveries was to bring about major changes in this industry. The first of these was the discovery of chlorine by Scheele

===== PAGE 640 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
623 in 1774. Eleven years later in 1785, Berthollet established all the different properties of chlorine, and found that it was extremely useful for dyeing cloth - a process which, up until then had been extremely slow and laborious. From 1777, the Javel factory near Paris began the large scale production of chlorine, which itself required large quanti ties of hydrochloric acid.
The second problem was how to produce soda in greater quantities.
For some time, attempts were made to produce artificial soda, but then in 1776, the Academie des Sciences in Paris offered a prize to the person who could find a process for extracting soda from calcium chloride.
This process was finally discovered by Leblanc in 1790, but there were still some difficulties. Sulphuric acid was costly, and there was also a problem with by-products, especially hydrochloric acid. This was to be solved with the discoveries of chlorine and lime sulphide.
Sulphuric acid was produced from iron sulphate or by burning sulphur in a bell jar. In 1736, Ward attempted the same process using carboys, but the yield was no greater. In 1746, Roebuck developed the concept of lead chambers while trying to find a way of using a metal on which acid was ineffective. This process was introduced into France in
1774, by Holker the younger.
These completely new techniques had been practically applied then, by the eve of the French Revolution, but they were not so advanced that they could support a very extensive chemical industry.
The techniques used in the textile industry are a familiar subject to historians, largely because of the great deal of progress made in this field of industry. We shall therefore limit our discussion once more to simply providing a schematic framework.
The reasons for the renewed impetus of the iron and steel industry (the lack of fuel) are easy enough to understand, as is the development of the steam powered machine and the chemical industry. It is not so easy to explain the reasons behind the technical transformations of the textile industry. There had been no significant increase in the popu lation figures, nor had there been any marked change in social or industrial sectors in the first half of the 18th century which would help explain the radical changes taking place. The problem remains to be answered: in this case technology does not appear to have been an induced phenomenon. Once the first discoveries had been made, the later changes occured quite naturally. The imbalances between the various stages of the manufacturing process provided the impetus for

===== PAGE 641 =====

624
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
complementary inventions; from this point onwards, there was a kind of linking process which restored the broken equilibrium. A more or less definitive balance was finally achieved towards the end of the century.
It is therefore quite valid to present the main inventions in the following fashion:
I - John Kay's flying shuttle (1735) made it possible to weave larger fabrics. It meant that only one worker as opposed to two were required to work the loom, and this represented a considerable economy. The weaving process was made much faster by this invention, and the widespread application of this process caused an increase in the demand for thread.
II - The developments in spinning processes were made in succes sive stages, at relatively short intervals of a few years.
A) The date of the first invention, by Wyatt and Lewis Paul (around 1733), indicates that it was separate from John Kay's. Besides, this machine was not put to widespread use (fig. 17).
B) The next two great inventions occurred in quick succession: the spinning jenny was invented in 1765 by Hargreaves, and Arkwright invented the water frame in 1767 (fig. 18). Mantoux noted that the first represented a transition from manual labour to mechanisation. It was in effect a multiple and partially mechanised spinning wheel - a simple and fairly uncostly machine. The water frame, which was conceived at around the same time, produced a much stronger and more resistant cotton, solving the problem of mixed fabrics.
C) Crompton's mule jenny (1777) was a hybrid machine, developed on the principles of the two preceding inventions. It produced a strong, fine thread, and furthermore, it was completely mechanised. Around
1783, more sophisticated machines were being built with cog wheels and metal cylinders: by 1790, they were able to support up to four hundred spindles.
III - Once more, the process had become imbalanced - spinning had developed more rapidly than weaving. Mechanical weaving had long been a major preoccupation of inventors. The first machine of this kind, devised by Cartwright in 1785 was difficult to work but successive improvements solved most of the problems. And once this was

===== PAGE 642 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
625 Figure 17. - Threading machine by Paul and Wyatt.
achieved, an equilibrium was attained once more (fig. 19). From 1789, the steam-powered machine was used during all stages of textile manufacture.
Such was the technical revolution of the textile industry. These machines were first conceived for use with cotton, but they could easily be adapted for use with wool. Other inventions completed the arsenal of textile machines: the carding machine was devised by Bourn in 1748, and fitted with two spools by Arkwright in 1775. Despite the fact that a machine was devised for combing in 1792, this process was much more difficult to develop. We have already mentioned the machines using punch cards invented by Bouchon and Falcon for silk working, and these were developed by Vaucanson in 1775 (fig. 20). Vaucanson also devised a machine for making taffetas by 1744. It was only in the following century that all these machines were incorporated into a single one (fig. 21). It was Bell, from Scotland, who invented the first process of mechanical textile-printing (1783).

===== PAGE 643 =====

626
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS

Figure 21. - PrincipleoftheJacquard loom.A, theneedledoes not reach the hole, the hook is not above the metal blade: when the treadle is depressed, the blade does not raise the thread;
B, the needle goes through the hole, and the hook caps the metal blade: when the treadle is depressed, the blade rises and leads the warp threads upwards. (From Furia and Serre,
Techniques et Societes, Paris, 1 970) It appears clearly that all these dates fall into place with each other.
It was in the decade preceding the Revolution that most of the developments took place. And as we saw, there was considerable interaction of diverse techniques which made this possible: bigger looms could be built using metal, and these could be driven by machines powered with steam. At this point we find ourselves in a new technical system.
We should be able to compare the progress of one technical system with the preceding one, but unfortunately this is made difficult by the lack of precise figures. Furthermore, there were usually benefits to be found further afield, beyond the simple increase in production. There is no better example of this than the steam powered machine. It was more powerful, certainly, but there were other advantages besides.

===== PAGE 646 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
629 The energy supplied by such machines was constant, it was not subject to natural conditions, nor was it confined to certain geographical areas.
It is not easy to integrate these notions into a purely mathematical model.
Of course, we do have some numerical data, but are these valid in terms of production as a whole? And insofar as progress was slow and gradual, there must have been occasions when changes were simply matters of substitution. If, for example, a small number of modern factories replace a large number of traditional ones, it is quite likely that there will be no marked change in the figures of global production.
The new technical system modified structures - it did not change production as a whole. Now that we have stated our reservations, we can provide a few significant examples. After it had been in use for some time, the coke-burning blast furnace was capable of up to three times the yield of the wood-burning blast furnace. The capacity of the puddling furnace was fifteen times that of the old refining processes. In
1787, the Crawshay, from Cyforthfa, had difficulty in producing 500 tons of iron bars - by 1812, this output had increased to 10,000 tons.
The figures are even more striking in the textile industry, where progress had been even more marked. One small spinning jenny did the work of six to eight workers using traditional instruments. The growth in productivity was even more significant once steam-powered machines were used, which meant that machines could be built to unlimited sizes.
The period from 1785 to 1790 was quite clearly one of industrial revolution (fig. 22). From the technical point of view, the innovations had been developed to their fullest potential, and each technique had acquired an internal equilibrium. It was at this time that the first links between the various techniques became apparent. The steam-powered machine was in widespread use in the mining industry, and in the iron and steel and textile industries. Iron was put to widespread use, for making rails and various types of machines, such as those used in the textile and construction industries. These radical changes only became really apparent some time after they had actually begun. As is to be eYpected, this transformation was initially more indicative than real. It would be useful to study the transformations of British industry and particularly its structures, in order to appreciate the rapidity with which the new technical system imposed itself. This transformation was particularly marked in certain areas, such as the iron and steel
