industry for example. It is true that between 1790 and 1815, other factors, of a political and economical order, were equally important.
Most of the major imbalances had been corrected. However there were still several problems to be resolved.
DEVELOPMENT
The new technical system was in operation by the end of the 18th century, but had not reached its definitive state. It was still capable of development and further progress in some areas. The progress made was gradual and steady - there were no sudden transformations. This mutation continued well into the mid-19th century, albeit at a slower pace, now that most of the major elements were already present. It was

===== PAGE 648 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
631 not until after the Napoleonic Wars that the movement was well under way, however. Besides which it was necessary to complete the assimi lation of the first set of innovations.
One of the main consequences of the industrial revolution of the 18th century was the recognition of technical and economic problems.
This was a new phenomenon, and was clearly evident in certain areas.
M. Courtheoux noted that the notion of real prices and its use in measuring technical progress was a familiar one to the first exponents of the classical school. Adam Smith opened The Wealth of Nations with a "book" relating to the "causes which perfected the productive faculties of labour." In his work Principles of Population, Malthus wrote at length on the "inventions which reduced the work load of the work force, and which were considered as a stimulant to the constant increase in wealth". But as we have already stated, these statements represent only a passing interest in technical problems: the latter would never be at the centre of a branch of thought whose central preoccupation is the mechanisms of market forces.
From the beginning of the 19th century, there was an ever-growing interest in machines. Jean-Baptiste Say, in his Traite and Ler;on went from Essais dans !es Arts (Essays on the arts) to Service des machines
(Servicing machines), Emploi des moteurs aveugles, (The use of blind engines) and the Etalonnage dans !es manufactures, (Standardisation of Manufactured Products). Smith was concerned with the conditions of an increasing productivity (accumulation of capital, acquisition of skills, use of machines, improvement of the land, division of labour), while Ricardo insisted on improvement of the land, as did Stuart Mill.
But it was Say who attributed the notion of productivity with its technical characteristics. Curiously enough, remarks Courtheoux, it was his works, rather than those of the classical school, which were imbued with the spirit of the industrial revolution. "To obtain a greater productivity with the same amount of labour - that is the main objective of industry." From this stemmed his idea of technical progress contributing to the lowering of production costs.
The question of unemployment as a consequence of technology was considered at a very early date. One of the first people to deal with this idea, Sismondi, did so with vehemence, writing that the invention of any machine which assumed the task of a single worker amounted to a catastrophe. Bastiat opposed this, maintaining that the rather less obvious advantages - the saving of money, for example - outweighed

===== PAGE 649 =====

632
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
the importance of the more obvious ones, such as removed or displaced labour. Say's position on this was somewhere between these two interpretations. He claimed that the appearance of a new machine was favourable to the working class, but it sometimes had "the rather serious consequence of changing the nature of working class occu pations." Ultimately, however, although the introduction of expedi tious machines did sometimes make some jobs redundant, "they do not reduce the chances of the working class to earn a living. " Ricardo noted the changes in employment patterns during times of technical progress, and notably the movements from the primary sector to the secondary or tertiary sectors. He also tried to relate innovations vis-a-vis "net profit" and "gross profit": "Some innovations increase the net profit (i.e., the real profit), as perceived by the capitalists, even though they reduce the gross profit (i.e. , the sum total of the products)
" At any rate, all the theoreticians were surprised by the fall in prices resulting from technical progress. This fall in prices was an indispensa ble element of the development of the consumer market, and the expansion of new machines and processes. General welfare was improved as a result, and the increased demand created a price war which inevitably brought about the diffusion of new techniques. Say was very interested in this particular aspect of technical progress, but the same was not true of Ricardo and the English classical economists who estimated that the developments were unable to check the price rise which followed the decrease in yields. The consumer may possibly be able to benefit from technical progress, but Ricardo and his compatriots believed that technical progress also caused a decrease in revenue and the erosion of capital. The latter observation clearly highlights the problems concerned with the limits of technical pro gress.
So it was the British industrial revolution which led economists to attempt to integrate technical issues into a global theory of economic movements and balances. There were theorists of other disciplines who noted the "immense progress" made in Britain, and who also began to tackle the problem from their own particular point of view.
The development of technology, which was still in embryonic form during the 18th century, and the relationship between science and techniques changed a great deal in the first part of the 19th century.
The birth of a scientific technique necessitated the forging of links with

===== PAGE 650 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
633 other purely scientific disciplines - but the connections were still tenuous. The progress of the chemical industry, for example, is closely linked to that of science, but in the field of mechanics, technical progress often precedes scientific explanation. As we stated earlier,
Carnot developed the theory of the heat engine only in 1824. Also noteworthy, however, are the efforts made at systematisation which inevitably brought about the use of scientific data. At the end of the
18th century, Beckmann, in spite of his promise to treat the subject scientifically, contented himself with simple empirical descriptions of several different trades which were linked neither to each other, or to scientific principles. In 1806, with his Entwurf der allgemeinen Techno logie, he abandoned the descriptive divisions so characteristic of all the treatises and encylopedias of this period, and actually pursued a single principle theme, which enabled him to regroup all the operations used in different trades, and put them into separate classes. As stated by another author:
It is the general view of technology which is changing: the use of technology is no longer confined to informing administrators and functionaries - non-specialists - about the operations of a trade by using simplified schemas. Technology is now capable of contributing to the development of the actual trades themselves: the listing and comparison of different methods used to achieve the same object made possible the transference of operations from one trade to another. The importance of this concept of technical transference cannot be over-emphasised: it makes it possible to go beyond the limits of a given trade, - in which classical technology was confined - and to establish links with other, very different occupations.
Hachette's Traite elementaire des machines (1811 ), led directly to the Cours de mecanique appliquee aux machines (1836) by Poncelet.
Mathematical calculation and notions of physics found practical appli cations and all the more so because the works of Monge had provided representations which made a certain amount of rationalisation possi ble. Thus the perfect image and global thinking substituted themselves to the plates featured in the Encyclopedie, and in the Descriptions of the Academie des Sciences.
But contrary to the introduction of the Encyclopedie, the artist's hand no longer does everything, and so it was possible to develop education at every level. One was more preoccupied with attaining the highest levels possible, the lower level naturally being linked to general primary education which was difficult to establish. The French example, which was more systematic, was copied widely.

===== PAGE 651 =====

634
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The Ecole Polytechnique was the result of an idea of providing precisely scientific information fundamental to crafts and professions which were later taught in schools of instruction. The Ecole Polytech nique had been created by the Convention, at the same time moreover as the Conservatoire des arts et metiers which itself remained based upon the old principles of description and demonstration. Did not
Vaucanson's collection of models, begun at the end of the 18th century, follow on from the plates in the Encyclopedie? Did not
Diderot and d' Alembert prove that they too could make small scale models? And did not Watt himself work on and from models? This encompasses the whole difference: the model or the drawing, and the
Ecole Poly technique or the Conservatoire. The former was finding its boundaries with the major state corps, but its pupils very rapidly began to slip away towards the private sector. The need was felt, however, for the creation of an industrial school and it was in 1829 that the Ecole centrale des arts et manufactures appeared.
Such examples involve a number of other countries. Great Britain retained its empiricism, and Germany set up more polytechniques: at
Karlsruhe (1825), Munich (1829), Dresden (1829), Stuttgart (1839) and in Hanova (1831). Austria created polytechniques in Prague
(1806), and Vienna (1811). Almost everywhere, the image became reality.
At the end of the 18th century, La Rochefoucauld-Liancourt had created the first school for arts and crafts. This idea was taken up under the Empire at Compiegne, later at Chalons, to form what Napoleon had called non-commissioned officers of industry, in other words foremen. The Restoration was to increase the number of schools for arts and crafts which were in fact to become engineering schools, owing to the dearth of management for an industry which was defini tively "taking-off." Louis-Philippe was to create one last school in
Aix-en-Provence. Lastly, there were schools, originally on a lower level, which were restricted to the mining industry: the schools of
Douai, Ales, or Saint Etienne really trained foremen. In this domain, other countries did not seem to adopt the French system, the higher levels of which they were content to copy.
Finally, education was introduced for the working masses. As technology was growing ever more complex, so the requirement for minimum training became essential. Almost all over the world the old practice of apprenticeship undoubtedly continued. But this did not in

===== PAGE 652 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
635 fact provide the "worker's hand," to use d' Alembert's expression.
Henceforth, notions of calculation, the ability to know how to "read" a drawing, and certain mental processes were required. The problem was situated on two levels. It was necessary to found what today we call primary education. It is significant that we may observe from a survey done in 1834, that French textile manufacturers pointed out that one of the superior qualities of British industry was precisely the existence of a working class which possessed the foundations of elementary educa tion. In France, the Guizot law of 1833 endeavoured - as yet imperfec tly but it was to become more effective - to establish the foundations for primary education. Elsewhere this was not attempted until the second half of the century. Apart from this, it was necessary to replace the practice of apprenticeship which had never or no longer existed in more industrialized sectors. Typical in this respect were private associ ations in France and Germany designed to provide the working classes with the basic elements of calculation and drawing which would be useful to them. These associations enjoyed a marked success, indeed so much so in France that on the eve of the 1848 Revolution these
"evening classes" had enrolled over 100,000 students. Like the famous school of La Martiniere, in Lyons, these were to become one of the first professional establishments.
Training and information are closely linked. Therefore, it is not astonishing that technical literature saw considerable development at the beginning of the 19th century, and especially in countries which had yet to learn their apprenticeship from the English industrial revolution. Need we emphasize that we still lack the bare minimum of knowledge indispensable to assess this phenomenon? Hence, we may only make a few general statements on this subject.
Obviously, the use of earlier knowledge or experience was conti nued or resumed. Such was the case for the Encyclopedic methodique, begun at the very end of the 18th century, the last volumes of which were published in 1834. Thus, because of the absence of more recent works, 18th century treatises in particular were frequently republished. The treatise on ship building by the Swede, Chapman, translated by Vial de Clerbois, was still being republished in Paris in
1839. Moreover, we should compare these old and more modern editions in order to observe the differences between them.
Many industries, however, provided material for more recent works, especially industries which had been more or less revolu-

===== PAGE 653 =====

636
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
tionized by British inventions. We need only look at the techniques of metallurgy for proof of this: old works were discontinued and replaced by the works of the Frenchman, Hassenfratz (la Siderotechnie . . . ,
1812), and of Karsten (System der Metallurgie . . . , Berlin, 1831), before the appearance of the major work written by the Englishman, Percy, which dates after 1850. It would be useful to trace this production, industry by industry, in order to analyse both the exact technical content and the general spirit of the age.
The taste for encyclopedias, even on the subject of techniques, had not died. In the first half of the 19th century, we still see a large number of dictionaries, some of which are very general, and others which are given over to technical data, such as the Dictionnaire technologique, ou nouveau dictionnaire universe[ des arts et metiers, published by "a gathering of scientists and artists" in Paris in 1835. Bessemer's only education was gained through reading one of these dictionaries, published in London in 1831. Again these require detailed research so that we may establish the exact nature of their content.
Lastly, one more factor remains to be considered, the importance of which has continued to grow- technical journals which appeared at the same period and which were a major contribution to the diffusion of established progress and to the preparation of future progress. The
French Revolution gave birth to a number of these, some of which were even official publications: Journal des Sciences, Arts et Metiers
(1792), the officiallournal des Arts et Manufactures (1795-1797), or the remarkable journal published by a state organization, the Journal des
Mines, the publication of which began in 1794. Some publications produced by certain associations mindful of technical progress should also be cited, such as the Bulletin de la Societe d' Encouragement pour l'lndustrie nationale (from 1801). To a certain extent, this movement, which was unquestionably born in France although its origins may be traced back to the 18th century, was imitated abroad. Among these important and regular publications was the Polytechnisches Journal, which first appeared in Berlin in 1820, and the Mechanics Magazine published in London in 1823. It would be too tedious to make a list here of all the titles of these publications. They served many roles, the most important of which was to provide information on techniques and processes which had been developed. A recent study on French ironmasters has revealed the enthusiasm with which the Journal des
Mines was, received which enabled unfruitful attempts and many

===== PAGE 654 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
637 unnecessary expenses to be avoided. Aside from this, the comparison of industrial experiments, frequent in these periodicals and easier than in well written treatises, led to both indispensable adaptations and progressive developments, indeed even to the revival of practised techniques. This circulation of technical knowledge was to become the source of future progress.
The two actions, the formation of frameworks and the diffusion of technical knowledge, remain closely linked in this evolution, giving rise to a new type of man which the 18th century could only imagine.
Whatever systems were adopted in different countries, the "engineer" was born out of this progress, not only from the progress completed in the last years of the Ancien Regime, but from all the developments following it. He is no longer tied to the state, as had been the case in the
18th century, but he knows all that is necessary for the direction of this new institution, be it a factory or a large mining business. He must also understand equally well the fabrication procedures of his specialist product and the workings of machines or the construction of the necessary buildings. Although he is not polyvalent, as some authorities would have us believe, he is at least multivalent. It would be extremely interesting to know exactly how these men and their careers were moulded. French schools have been thoroughly studied. In Britain, some businesses were breeding grounds for engineers, and herein lies the strength of the country: the firm which produced the gifted engineer, Maudslay, the "Maudslay Nursery,'' as one contemporary author aptly calls it, represents this type of British factory where technical progress was achieved. Invention and innovation were at the heart of business in Britain, while this was not always the case on the
Continent where attempts and achievements took longer to establish.
Technological developments required other considerations which only saw the light of day in the first half of the 19th century. Let us look again at our simple outline. The new technical system had virtually been formed at the end of the 18th century. It was only applicable, however, in modified economic structures. Requirements for this are complementary to each other: mass production was henceforth possi ble in a great many industries, but mass production must take place at the heart of more and more powerful, but less and less numerous centres of production. We will return to this factor later. This concen tration of production centres must also be matched by a parallel concentration of capital. Thus, on the one hand, the small family

===== PAGE 655 =====

638
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
business disappears, while on the other hand, more and more abun dant capital is assembled. Business techniques as such, and financial techniques must consequently be adapted to these new circumstances.
The engineer, businessman, and entrepreneur similarly change, as does the legal framework within which his action develops. Changes also affect the structures of the circulation of money. The promul gation, particularly on the Continent, of the 1807 French code of commerce, should be pointed out, as this created the sort of industrial or commercial societies whose capacity was to advise - and here we should also stress the rapid and enormous expansion of transferable assets - and which stabilized social capital without hindering the mobility of fortunes. We should, however, clarify that the late devel opment of some structures, such as banking structures for example, did influence the problems of adopting a new technical system, for the state was no longer an effective support for new businesses which it had been during the 18th century. The state could therefore only deal with infrastructures indispensable to economic development.
Concordances of a demographic nature are less clear cut. Although the populations of more advanced countries continued to grow, birth rates were to reach their peak around the years 1820-1830, whereupon they began to decrease. If these elements are carefully examined, we realize, particularly in Britain, that industries compensated for this less dynamic demography by considerably increasing exports. This con sumption certainly continued to grow in industrialized countries, all the more so as popultitions aged, but the development of exterior outlets is another dimension altogether.
Economic growth has now been the subject of so many works that we need hardly labour the point. The intervals which may be observed, however, are most significant. Let us take the example of the produc tion of cast iron (fig. 23). The following figures will illustrate the considerable differences that exist: between 1800 and 1850, the pro duction of cast iron in millions of tonnes gives the following results:
Britain, from 600 to 2249; France, from 200 to 406; Germany, from 70 to 144; Belgium, from 50 to 144. To put it another way, Britain almost qLadrupled its production, Belgium tripled it, and the other two countries merely doubled theirs. But are gross figures indicative of the situation? Do they have any significance whatsoever? Was this cast iron produced from coke or wood? And above all, for our purposes, what is the significance of the new techniques? It is evident that if

===== PAGE 656 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
639 Britain enjoyed this considerable increase in production, it must have been due to the application of new techniques. The situation is completely different in other countries and old traditional industry had to make an additional effort. As far as we can judge for the latter and for this industry, the initial increase was not necessarily the result of adopting the new technical system. The natural conditions and the natural adaptation of these new techniques was to take some time. In other words, growth, at least at the beginning, was the product of enormous tension within the old technical system. The few studies we have on the subject of cost prices prove this conclusively. The Con tinent had adopted puddling more easily than the production of cast iron using coke (fig.24).
We should therefore study, in what we call development, two series of distinct actions. Some were adaptation to production conditions, other than those which existed in countries which initiated new techniques. Within this arises an important problem to which we will return later. Secondly, we must also consider the repercussions of partial discoveries and the necessity of re-establishing wholesale bal ance. This will be our first consideration.
It was in the first half of the 19th century that the techniques of the industrial revolution of the 18th century were improved and took their potential form. Inevitably, improvements were most often imple mented after the Napoleonic wars. Economically, this fact is easy to understand, as the return of peace, the end of the Continental system as well as the absorption of earlier innovations were necessary. This therefore explains, to a large extent, at least why the completion of technical innovation only came about between 1815 and the revolution of 1848, whereas technical research per se remained a continuing process.
We should note that the development of new techniques was henceforth not reserved to Britain alone. Now aware of technical problems and of industrial power and its strength, other countries acquired new techniques and more willingly participated in progress.
As we have already pointed out, a certain enthusiasm was born and its inevitable counterparts were the movements of romanticism and love of nature. With the return of peace, every enlightened man became aware of the importance of Britain and the lead which this country had over the others. Here too, the reaction was very sudden and was manifested by the introduction of all sorts of customs' barriers to

===== PAGE 657 =====

640
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 23. - Blast furnace at Gluwitz (Silesia), circa 1830.

===== PAGE 658 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
641

===== PAGE 659 =====

642
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
protect oneself from foreign competition, from a country which had already realized industrial change. In an economy based on profit, any technical transformation could only succeed with massive investments, the profitability of which had to be ensured at any price.
Be that as it may, we are still dealing with the same technical system.
Essentially, detail improvements are required to reduce production costs by using raw materials better, thus increasing productivity.
It is interesting to note that in the domain of energy technical progress continued simultaneously in old techniques, those of hydrau lic energy, and in more recent techniques, those of the steam machine.
The techniques of the former were even modified in order to incorpo rate them into the future technical system. Several attempts were made in primitive turbines: for example, the Bazacle wheels, which only produced 15 to 25% of the theoretical power of the waterfall, the projects of Branca (1629) and Barker (1741) for the use of reaction wheels, the theory of which was provided by Euler (1750-1754), and several attempts which were made at the end of the 18th century and in the early years of the 19th century. It was claimed that standard wheels flooded if the water level rose so they could not turn, and that they used only small waterfalls, a factor which meant that large waterfalls had to be divided. The wheels were therefore necessarily bulky and heavy, the output for the wheels above was no greater, and the wheels at the side could only turn very slowly, and subsequently the gearing absor bed a large part of the power. All in all the whole system was not efficient. The Societe d'encouragement considered this question in its programme and offered prizes for solutions. In 1822, the engineer,
Burdin, came up with the solution, but his was only a theoretical concept which lacked clarity, and actually consisted of Euler's 1754 machine. A trial attempt was carried out at Ardes-sur-Couzes in 1828, where they hoped to get an output of 65 to 70% .
During this epoch, Fourneyron, a pupil of Burdin, had managed to make his first turbine turn (April 1827). Two others, one with 50 horsepower, had been used in industry. At the same time, Fourneyron had written a paper, a genuine treatise on the construction of turbines, containing the whole theory, based on the principle of active forces, of the "universal and continuous pressure wheel or the hydraulic tur bine." Fourneyron had managed to distribute water without any pressure drop, thus retaining the potential energy to within a few centimetres of the tail race; the water was directed at a suitable angle

===== PAGE 660 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
643 into the receptor where it was stripped of all its energy in a very short time and the length of the distance was reduced to a minimum, reducing the speed absolutely necessary for its evacuation at the exit.
The new turbine could thus turn in a flooded environment, unaffected by the rise in tail water and frost. This turbine, set up at Pont-sur l'Ognon (Doubs), powered a rolling mill. It provided 6 horsepower under a waterfall of 1.40 metres. The relationship of theoretical effect to produced effect reached 80% in the first experiments, and 87% during later experiments. The two other turbines were used in the bellows in the blast furnaces at Dampierre and with 50 horsepower in the ironworks at Fraisans. Over 80 horsepower was the power achieved in a textile factory at Inval, and this rose to 220 horsepower in a spinning mill in Augsburg. In 1835, the facilities of two waterfalls measuring 108 metres and 114 metres in height were studied for the spinning mills situated at Saint Blaise, in the Black Forest: turbines were installed in 1837 and 1838.
This invention was significant insofar as it could compete with steam engines in regions which were situated too far from coalfields. In any case, it prolonged the use of water power, and in some countries, such as the United States of America, it enabled industrialization without having to recourse to coal.
The steam engine also saw some important developments which improved its working or output. Boilers were operated at high pres sure in order to use the whole reduction in pressure, and the system of steam distribution or piston-lagging was improved. High pressure was adopted by Trevithick (1797), and then by Evans (1804). Arthur
Woolf finally completed the high pressure double expansion engine, or the "compound" engine (1811) which enabled a fuel saving of around
50 % . It was not until 1830 that Seguin envisaged the tubular boiler with a large heating surface. In 1797, Edmund Cartwright first used a non frictious metal for the pistons. In 1816, Barton designed segments pressed against the surface by small springs. Distribution by four-way taps, and then by sidevalves (Murdock, 1799) simplified the distribution instruments. In 1836, Farcot was to patent the first distri bution system which practically realized variable reduction in pressure by the regulator. It was the British engineer, Maudslay, who in 1807 managed to abolish the bulky balance wheel, the rod of the piston being guided vertically on two sliding channels, thanks to a wheel which was placed on the end. In 1815, Manby obtained the first patent

===== PAGE 661 =====

644
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
for a machine with an oscillating cylinder. There are many such examples of patents which tried to find an improvement for the steam engine. Neither the principle, however, nor the basic details were changed, but it remained fundamentally the same as Watt's original engine: attempts had only succeeded in removing imperfections which its first realizers had already noted. Better adapted materials and some ingenious ideas had done the rest.
Progress was slow and partial in the domain of exploitation tech niques: they remained on the same lines as those realized in the 18th century, without revolutionizing whatever improvements may have been made.
As for agriculture, it was mainly the tools themselves which bene fited from the progress realized in neighbouring techniques. The use of iron rather than wood instruments, and the mecanistion of certain operations were the major new ideas. Mathieu de Dombasle at his
Roville farm, and Bella at the school of Grignon, were concerned with perfecting ploughs and diversifying their types according to the tasks to be undertaken and the nature of the land to be worked. These tools were already made entirely of iron, In 1825, however, Fondeur finalized the swivel plough, which had a wooden beam and triangular ploughshares with wooden helicoidal mould boards, an ancestor of the machines used all over the world today. In 1837, the American, John
Deere, introduced the first steel ploughshare.
Mechanisation appeared in the second half of the 18th century. This merely entailed the improvement of machines and making worthwhile practical tools. Around 1800, James Smith constructed an articulated ploughshare seeder which was called the "king of seeders."
Throughout the first half of the 19th century, there were many types of seeders. Robert Meares and Taylor had inaugurated reseach into the modern harvester. The first major harvesters which worked suitably were those introduced by Mac Cormick (1831-1833) and Hussey
(1833). In 1834, Harris Morre introduced the first combined harvester, the threshing-machine itself dating from the attempts made by Meikle in 1775. Similarly, the first plough powered by a steam engine and a cable was put to the test in 1834.
Progress was even more gradual and imperceptible in crop growing and soil improvement. One may note, however, considerable efforts made to protect arable lands but no worthwile results had been obtained as yet. Despite this, we should point out the success of the

===== PAGE 662 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
645 attempts made by Raclet, in 1828, in his battle against pyralis of the vine, by scalding the wood before the vegetation started to grow.
The success of some breeds ensured the development of animal domestication and breeding. These breeds were mostly created by the
British. The Dishley breed, imported into France in 1833, was crossed with the merinos in 1837. From the famous Hubback bull, the Colling brothers had launched the Durham bovine breed in 1785, also imported throughout Europe (e.g. in 1825 in the French province of
Nivernais). Pig breeding was similar: the Yorkshire breed arrived in France from 1819. Between 1815 and 1830, the French introduced two major breeds of draught horse, one from the Boulogne region for heavy draughts, and the Perch for lighter draughts.
All these efforts served to stabilize and perfect the agronomic system developed in the 18th century. Linked to agrarian and social changes, this agronomic system proved to be perfectly suitable for rich regions in temperate zones.
Progress was extremely restricted in mining techniques, and to some extent this progress was a lateral one. Humphrey Davy's invention, in
1816, of the metal cloth lamp - the safety lamp for firedamp - visible improvement of ventilation techniques, and the appearance in 1833 of the first hoisting cage, developed by T.Y. Hall are virtually the only developments which may be cited. In the middle of the century, metal linings were also introduced. The mining industry had to be content with profiting as best it could from neighbouring techniques.
More significant technological modifications were made in the iron and steel industry. They all had the same aims: to reduce the consump tion of raw materials, particularly in the blast furnace, to increase productivity in other operations such as puddling or lamination, and to create new tools for use in ironworks.
The blast furnace undoubtedly retained the same structure which it had originally been given in the 15th century. It was, however, to receive two lateral developments which resulted in a considerable reduction of production costs. The first invention was that of introduc ing hot air into the furnace, developed by the Englishman, Nelson, in
1828. From 1830, air was blown into the furnace, raising the tem perature to 315reore C. Naturally this involved taking several pre cautions, particularly in cooling the twyers. This procedure could perceptibly reduce fuel consumption. In the ironworks on the Clyde, coal consumption was reduced from eleven tonnes in 1811 to eight

===== PAGE 663 =====

646
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
tonnes in 1828, and from four in 1831 to two and a half in 1832. The apparatus used was quickly developed for general employment. In
1833, the Frenchman, Cabral, also succeeded in saving the same quantity of fuel with slightly different apparatuses. The second inven tion, apparently French in origin, was no less important. The recovery of throat gases provided a source of thermic energy which had pre viously been overlooked. This was used both to reheat the air blown into the furnaces and for steam engines: here again was another important fuel saving innovation.
At the same time, the apparatus itself, the blast furnace, attracted the attention of technicians. The form of the blast furnace had long been a matter of discussion from the 18th century, but no worthwhile conclusions had been arrived at yet. In any case everybody agreed that the shape of the furnace was important for the regular supply of loads and, subsequently, for the smooth running of the furnace. In a pamphlet, published in 1839, a Staffordshire ironmaster, John Gib bons, recognized the importance of the modern profile of the blast furnace: he believed that the gradient ofthe bosh could be reduced and that the bosh should be longer with a steeper incline.
The puddling furnace envisaged by Henry Cort was not yet a perfect instrument. In 1816-1818, the English engineer, Samuel Rogers, suggested that the hearth of the furnace should be lined, and a few years later the inner walls also received the same treatment. The drawbacks of the primitive furnace, which had necessitated pre refining, were therefore abolished. Weekly production in the furnaces increased from eight to twenty tonnes. In 1825-1832, Joseph Hall devised hot puddling, once again in order to reduce fuel consumption.
The rolling mill was gradually improved, but unfortunately its history remains vague. On the other hand, the power hammer was introduced in the first half of the 19th century in 1839-1841, the paternity of which is disputed between the Frenchman, Eugene
Bourdon, and the Englishman, John Nasmyth (fig. 25). Other engi neers had already considered this invention: among others, Watt and the French builder, Cave. The essential purpose of the new tool was to enable large objects in the ironworks to be forged, particularly the steam-ship propeller shafts.
If we add to this list the progress of some complementary tech niques, such as the fabrication of coke, and ore roasting, we notice that the iron and steel industry had too encouraged the almost complete

===== PAGE 664 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
647 Figure 25. -The first power hamer by Bourdon.
and total development of its own techniques, following the lead exemplified by the British in the 18th century. From this resulted a very clear reduction in production costs and a growth in productivity. Only then iron- and steel-using techniques could in turn expand and enjoy every possible development.
Consequences of that situtation were undoubtedly most remarkable in the transport industry. Mere indications of this at the end of the 18th century were to become profound change in the first half of the 19th century.
The first steam-powered ships were river vessels. Indeed, Jouffroy d' Abbans was the first to launch the steam-driven boat in 1783, an idea

===== PAGE 665 =====

648
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
which others had only envisaged before him. This was also true of Foulton in 1803 and 1807. From 1815, men began to dream of building steam-driven ships which could cross the high seas. The first Channel crossing was made in 1816 by the Elise, and the first Atlantic crossing was completed by the Savannah in 1819. All these early ships were still very traditional in concept: hulls were made of wood, and they still depended on sail, using the wind for a large part of the journey. In
1837, the Sirius was the first ship to cross the Atlantic using nothing but steam power.
Originally, all these vessels were paddle boats. We know that enormous wheels were necessary, which made the navigability of these constructions extremely difficult owing to their bulk and weight. The use of the propeller had been discussed from the start of the 18th century, but the first to be used effectively was put into practice by
John Fitch in 1796: this consisted of a single thread screw which described three successive turns, with only part of its diameter submer ged. Attempts had not been very successful, and Ressel's spiral (1812) did not have any better luck. It was finally the Frenchman, Frederic
Sauvage, who in 1836 and 1837 introduced the propeller with blades as we know it today. It had actually been designed by Meusnier in 1784 for aerial propulsion and was described as a marine propeller by
Bushnell in 1776, by Foulton in 1800 and by Steven in 1804. The propeller certainly abolished the huge paddle wheels, but also presen ted other difficulties, especially in making the propeller shaft for which the power hammer was invented.
The last development, linked in part to the preceding one, was the iron hull. Here again, the first iron ships were river craft: the first appears to have been launched in 1777, on the river Foss, in Yorkshire.
Barges were constructed later, part of the frame of which was still made of wood. The first steam-driven boat was the Aaron Manby, which took the name of its creator (1822). It was sent to France in kit form, where it was constructed for use on the Paris to Le Havre route.
The second vessel, the Commerce de Paris (1823), built by the same man and for the same journey, was also supplied in kit form. In 1818, five iron steamers were in use on the Seine river. As Charles Dollfus notes, wooden boats could only carry out twenty journeys per year between Rouen and Le Havre, transporting a total of 3300 tonnes, whereas iron boats could make thirty-six to forty such trips, carrying
5000 tonnes. Britain and the United States were to quickly follow suit.

===== PAGE 666 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
649 Maritime navigation was also to use iron. The first ship made completely of iron to sail the open seas was probably the Iron Sides, launched in 1838, and she successfully crossed the Atlantic. In 1839, the Queen of the East, a vessel weighing 2618 tonnes with an engine of
600 horsepower, established a service between London and Calcutta.
The results of all these combined efforts was the "Great Britain,'' a propeller and steam-powered vessel, made wholly of iron, built by
Brunnel in 1844. It still had sails, however. The sheet metal plating was twelve to fifteen millimetres thick, and the engine had a capacity of
1500 horsepower. It could travel at a speed of eleven knotts, and cross the Atlantic in fourteen days instead of the twenty-eight days taken by the Savannah. The hull of the Great Britain still survives today, evidence of the quality of the metal. The majority of these early propelled boats were constructed of iron, which is indicative itself of the twofold progress made.
The appearance of the first railways, and the perfect application of steam traction to land vehicles is so well known that we need hardly dwell on this for long. After the early attempts made by Trevithick in
1801 , Stephenson built the first practical steam-powered locomotive in 1814, the final model of which was the Rocket of 1829. Shortly afterwards, the tubular boiler by Seguin contributed an important development. The earliest bogie locomotive was constructed in 1833.
At the end of the period, the North Star haulled trains at a speed of 64 kilometres per hour.
The development of the railway industry presented many and varied problems: financial (the organisation of a large amount of capital), legal (the compulsary puchase of land), institutional (the establishing of monopolies) and more directly technical difficulties. Other than complementary techniques (and we are thinking here of the tech niques involved in public works which had to be visibly improved for the ex nihilo creation of new tracks), the creation of railways necessi tated extensive modifications in the iron and steel industry. The iron rail was infinitely superior in quality to the cast iron rail which was too brittle. It was therefore necessary to finalize lamination methods, with ftutec cylinders, directly providing the large quantity of rails required with better adapted forms. Improvements were made both in the loads which had to be supported and in the speed of the trains. The track shape corresponded to that of the wheels of the wagons and the engines. From his first locomotive of 1814, Stephenson had devised

===== PAGE 667 =====

650
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
flanged wheels, while some tracks, on the other hand, were built with flanged rails. Stephenson's idea prevailed. The problem of wheel resistance rapidly arose and it was only at the end of the period that the wheels were shrouded in order to avoid huge expenses, and a little later shrouding without welding was developed. The wagon brakes presented yet another problem.
Perhaps the importance of the notion of a technical system may be better judged at this point. More diverse techniques using extremely varied material or materials came into play in the railway. It was therefore necessary on the one hand, that all the different techniques were at the same level of development, and on the other, that continued improvements should visibly reduce costs. Similarly, one may easily imagine the pressure which the railway could exert on techniques "upstream," in particular on iron and steel making and mechanical techniques.
The widespread use of metal, and the considerable increase in demand for this material was to compel parallel changes in the domain of machine tools. Certainly, the main machine tools had been in use before 1800 (lathe tools or planing machines, drills, reamers, taps and milling cutters). Even so, certain important achievements had been introduced, although this was just a modest beginning. Machine builders endeavoured,more than ever, on the one hand, to ensure the solidity of their equipment by generally using metal, cast iron or iron, and on the other, to transfer the skill from the worker to the machine.
The British were real pioneers in this domain: Henry Maudslay, Roberts and Sharpe, Nasmyth, Whitworth and Fox were to give the machine tool its real modern concept. We should also mention Calla,
Decoster and Cave, the Frenchmen who also worked towards this aim.
Carriage lathes were, moreover, well known. These were to be replaced by parallel lathes (Roberts in 1817 for the lathe used for planing), the feed-rod (Fox), and the separation of the chuck and the sliding poppet. When progress in automatic functioning was achieved and adopted in the screw-cutting slide lathe by Whitworth (1835), the worker became a mere supervisor (fig. 26). In 1839, Bodmer patented the vertical turning lathe (the circular planing machine). Whitworth's lathe for wooden screws (1835) exploited the mechanisms which resulted in greater automation: the feed-rod, the clamping by pliers controlled by a conical socket, and the camshaft. Vertical boring machines were also designed (1840). Planing machines by Fox (1814),

===== PAGE 668 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
651 Figure 26. - Whitworth's lathe (1835). (From RS. Woodbury, Studies in the History of Machine tools, Cambridge, M.l.T., 1972)
Roberts (1817), Clement (1820) and Whitworth (1835) mark the stages in this rapid progress.
We should also make a systematic study of the evolution of the machine tool. Some research has been done or is in progress, but no well-expounded work has yet been attempted with regard to this. Two points, however, must be emphasized. The first concerns the actual structure of the machine: improvements in gears, perfection in the method of producing the gears (machines for making gears were developed by Pihet in 1827, Whitworth in 1835, and Decoster in 1843) , and the improvement of kinetic transmissions (here we should recall
Gaile's patent in 1829 for his articulated chain) . The second point, which has already been stressed, is the pressure exerted by other techniques. The development of the railways and ship building should also be cited: wheel-making lathes, riveting machines and machines for curving metal were developed to fulfil the requirements of these industries.
Progress in the textile industry had been extensive in the 18th century but much remained to be done. The 18th century had devel oped not perfect but at least highly advanced machines for spinning.
The use of metal, the perfection of mechanisms and locking systems,

===== PAGE 669 =====

652
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
the increase in the number of bobbins, and the greater perfection of products resulted in "self acting" machines. The number of spindles per frame increased from 80 in around 1800, to over 200 in around
1825, almost 400 in 1835, and 600 in 1845. At the end of our period it was possible to achieve 6000 revolutions per minute. Visible progress was also made in the preparation of materials: the cotton gin was invented by the American, Elias Whitney in 1793; mechanical gearing for long fibres by Mac Carthy, dating from 1845. It was from 1810 that the Frenchman, Philippe de Girard, designed the machine for spinning flax.
Mechanical weaving could only come into effect when essential operations which did not require human intervention had been real ized: opening of the shed, passage of the weft thread, packing down the threads using a card and the progressive advance of the warp.
Cartwright's machine, developed in 1785, was still made of wood and as yet far from perfect. The use of metal and the perfection of mechanisms was the work of the first half of the 19th century:
Horrocks, Roberts and Hattersley in Britain, Heilmann, Koechlin and Magnan in France, and Schonherr in Germany were the principle authors of this work. Control mechanisms were gradually introduced and established. In 1796, Miller had invented the catch which blocked the shutter if the shuttle broke down in the middle of a row. Heavy, broad looms appeared between 1823 and 1845. But evolution was still slow due to all sorts of resistances to progress, which we will discuss later. In the silk making industry, Jacquard synthesized the looms of
Falcon and Vaucanson: he introduced the mechanical operation of a series of cardboard rectangles which had been devised by Falcon, almost a century before. Dressings also received important improvements. Mechanical fulling dates from 1838. Shearing machines were first built by Samuel G. Dorr, of Albany, in 1792. R.
Dorr used helicoidal blades in 1807, and in 1819, Ellis developed the mechanical shears.
This constant progress virtually led textile techniques to their temporary completion. The great boost which the British had given this industry in the 18th century therefore continued over almost a century.
The chemical industry could only guess at what its future might hold at the end of the 18th century. But during the first half of the 19th century this industry was to be really established. The industrial

===== PAGE 670 =====

lHE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION 653 development of the manufacture of artifical soda took place between
1810 and 1820. At this point sulphuric acid was to find a veritable opening: lead chambers considerably increased in size. The combus tion of sulphur needed to be improved, however, by using a carriage which could easily be reloaded. Sulphuric anhydride was absorbed by water placed in layers on the ground. This water was then trickled down the walls of the chamber, and lastly, between 1820 and 1825, the water was pulverized by a slope situated above. Successive chambers were developed, and in order to reduce cost prices, attempts were made to improve the copper pyrites: the inventions of the French chemist, Perret, rapidly spread throughout the chemical industry.
Following this, Leblanc's process was also perfected by using a dual reverberatory furnace: in one, the chloride became sulphate and in the other, it became sulphate carbonate. The arrangement of the kilns and methods of soda leaching at the exit were gradually modified. Simi larly, when the production of soda visibly increased, it was necessary to collect the hydrauchloric gases. In 1830, the Englishman, William
Gossage, tried to pass this gas through a coke-filled tower in which water streamed. The solution provided thus enabled the preparation of chlorine which the textile industry required in ever greater quanti ties.
At the very end of the 18th century, the process of distilling coal in order to obtain an inflammable gas was developed. Between 1790 and
1800, William Murdock in England and Philippe Lebon in France succeeded in collecting and burning the distillation gases, giving a flame for lighting. From 1802, this gas provided the necessary light for the Soho workshops of Boulton and Watt, and from 1805, lit a spinning mill in Manchester. The first development company was created in
1812. Gradual improvements were necessary in order to arrive, around the years 1820-1830, at the more or less definitive apparatus required for distillation and storage. They quickly learnt to use the by-products of the distillation process, in particular the various forms of tar.
These are the specific circumstances which led to the appearance of new techniques in the domain of foodstuffs. Both war and the Con tinental system had dried up the sources of colonial sugar. At the end of the 18th century, the German, Achard, had described the process of extracting sugar from beet. After unsuccessful attempts to manufac ture grape sugar, the Frenchman, Derosne studied the method of

===== PAGE 671 =====

654
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
extracting sugar from beet, from 1806 onwards and, in 1812, the first sugar refinery at Passy was built. From this was born the crushing machine and the hydraulic press which Perier introduced in France.
Purification was first done using sulphuric acid, and later lime, until 1811 when Pierre Figuier discovered the absorbing and decolorizing properties of bone black. Evaporation rapidly required the mechanical processes of stiring and pumping. Throughout the first half of the 19th century, modifications were to constantly improve the production of sugar beet.
Long voyages as well as the resupplying of great armies in the field presented the problem of resupplying provisions. This resulted in the formation of the canning industry which presented a dual problem: the preparation of the contents on the one hand, and the discovery of a container which was less fragile than glass on the other. We know that it was the jam maker, Nicolas Appert, who, under the Empire, used pressure-sealing to destroy fermentation germs. Around 1812, the
English employed soldered tin cans which resolved the second prob lem. Tinned food was born, but only for a limited number of foodstuffs.
Total coherence of the new technical system was therefore estab lished, which, around 1850, seemed to have developed as far as it was able. The analysis of the evolution of many other techniques, which would require many more pages, confirms this conclusively. This new system of techniques was based on three essential elements:
A) The generalized use of metal enabled evolution in a great number of techniques: a real era of mechanisation could be developed, and transport techniques were completely revolutionized. Iron began to invade construction work: after the structure of the Bordeaux theatre, the Pont des Arts, and the dome of the corn exchange in Paris, a new era was inaugurated, further testified by the Polonceau farm.
Henceforth, metal was the universal material, dethroning wood once and for all.
B) The steam engine liberated energy production from the con straints of nature and also became a universal energy itself due to the advance of mechanisation. We have already discussed the benefits which were now available with the mobile engine. Despite some drawbacks, those of weight and bulk, it was successfully applied to both industry and transports.

===== PAGE 672 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
655 C) Lastly, coal provided the link between the two preceding elements. It was adapted for use in the iron and steel industry and in all the operations involved in thermic energy, as well as being the ideal fuel for the steam engine.
If we add to this the benefits contributed by the chemical industry which offered a multitude of new products, we may estimate the extent to which the balance of the world had changed. Even within more traditional sectors, such as agriculture, techniques underwent trans formations which raised them to the level of all other techniques.
CONSEQUENCES OF THE
TECHNICAL REVOLUTION
Apart from a few limited sectors, the technical history of this technical revolution is today complete, but perhaps less profoundly in its developments in the first half of the 19th century where there are still many obscure areas. But other aspects appear to have been neglected to a greater or lesser degree, the emphasis having been placed mainly on the links between science and techniques, and on the obvious relationship between technical change and the beginnings of pro longed economic growth. In many domains, however, research remains very vague.
The reader must forgive a little disorder in this account: studies on this subject are on the whole still too inadequate to permit any strict synthesis. In many cases there are only impressions which are more or less justified, but there are too many gaps to enable a perfect account.
The consequences of technical progress are of two orders: quantita tive and qualitative. The latter has undoubtedly been emphasized, although the former is equally important. Difference of quality presents itself under two points of view. Firstly, the product is not the same in any of its properties as the product produced using old techniques. The respective value of iron smelting with wood and iron smelting with coal has been the subject of long tracts. At the time of the
1829 survey on iron types in France, the Englishman, Wilson, an associate of Manby, both of whom had become directors of Le

===== PAGE 673 =====

656
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Creusot, held the common opinion that iron produced using wood was better. And at the time of the 1860 survey on the commercial treaty with England, the great industrialist, Talabot, still claimed this to be so. This was one of the "brakes" to the expansion of certain new techniques.
Aside from this, new techniques could still provide products which the old methods of fabrication could not. The idea of making iron rails using classical iron and steel making techniques was unthinkable. New techniques therefore offered to the public a wide range of products which had until then remained unknown, be they the process of adding to old techniques, such as finer numbers in spinning, or enabling small or great substitutions. Unfortunately, we are in an area where research is very poor.
The increase in quantities, on the other hand, is better felt if not better known. Among the most important consequences of technical progress was the growth of productivity and the consequences arising from this, the most significant of which is the reduction in production costs, and therefore prices in certain conditions. Calculations, moreover incomplete ones, have been made for textile techniques.
While the "water frame" and the "jenny" may have been inferior in output at the beginning compared to the spinning wheel, the possibility of operating a machine, on the other hand, with twenty-five spindles and only one spinner, increased productivity eight or tenfold (figs. 27 and 28). The improvement of machines and their perfection was to increase these figures one hundredfold. According to the figures we have for other industries, while progress was not as spectacular, it was at least considerable. Mechanical carding represented five times the productivity of carding by hand. Cartwright's carding machine increased the productivity of one worker from 5.33 to 133.33 pounds per day.
According to the report by Heron de Villefosse on the French iron and steel industry, a blast furnace using wood produced on average 416 tonnes of cast iron per year, and a blast furnace using coke produced
1325 tonnes, with a far lower fuel consumption which was to be further reduced still the adoption of hot air and the recovery of throat gases.
We have observed that output with puddling was fifteen times higher than that produced by old refining processes.
Increase in productivity, and the frequent use of raw materials obtained at a better price or consumed in smaller quantities, was to

===== PAGE 674 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
657 10000 +-+-+--+--+--+--l--+-+1-+ IJt'nny]-- 2000 Spinning wheel 500 300 200 1000 1100 1200
1300 1'400 1soo 1eoo noo 1aoo Figure 27. - Change in spinning yields. Production expressed in hour-metres per person.
(From Endrei, L'Evolution des techniques du filage et du tissage, Paris, 1968) contribute to the reduction of production costs. The price of thread number 100 was still 36 shillings in 1786, falling to 10 shillings in 1795, and to 6.9 in 1807. In France, the thread number 3 which was worth
12.60 francs per kilo in 1816 fell to 6.40 in 1824, 5.60 in 1834, and 3.60 in 1844. A survey made in 1834 gives the following figures for France:

===== PAGE 676 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
659 Confronted to underconsumption, which was widespread at the beginning, these new prices ensured extensive outlets, more important perhaps than those resulting from a population growth or the expan sion of outside trade. Whatever the cause, all the sales added up.
Particular circumstances may arise which may ensure the manufac turer and the producer very low cost prices compared to those where old techniques were used, but may also limit the fall in market prices.
This might have been the situation described by Turgot, likewise by Ricardo, of an entrepreneur finding himself with the monopoly on a new technique. This could also be true of a country sheltered from foreign competition, in fact British competition, and aided by protec tive, indeed prohibitionist rates. These rates could only be calculated by comparative study of the cost price of old techniques: that is to say those who initiated innovations therefore had the opportunity - by aligning their selling prices with those of traditional entrepreneurs - of receiving considerable profit margins, and sources of self-financing which often necessitated a very strict capital market. In 1828, iron produced in the Champagne region was worth 280 francs per tonne, puddled and laminated iron was 200 francs, and British iron from
Cardiff was worth 175 francs. Note further more that for limited markets such as that of rails (in France in 1842 there were only fourteen possible suppliers), customs' protection enabled the formation of policies specifically intended to maintain high prices. It is also true that throughout the first part of the 19th century, there was a constant and notable fall in prices in the French iron and steel industry. The price of a quintal, in francs was as follows: 1820: 46.0; 1830: 42.5; 1840: 34.7;
1850: 24.0.
In some industries, the growth of production and demand was to prompt consequences on the technical level itself. At this time when a much larger quantity of products was put on the market, it was necessary to standardize and normalize them. Here, technology itself required this process, and fabrication methods were only conceived with this aim in mind. It would have been unthinakble that different types of rails be used on the same line. The links between networks necessarily deman ded a uniform type and a uniform gauge of track, which Britain had been quick to realize. The production of the rolling mill ensured the imple mentation of this uniformization at the very beginning.
Here, this uniformization was a necessity in itself, but it was a major contribution in facilitating the use of products and subsequently the

===== PAGE 677 =====

660
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
sale of products. As Andre Garanger has noted, the consumption of nuts and bolts in the early days of mechanical construction was so low that each mechanic had to make whatever he needed. Maudslay was the first to research and establish standardization of threads between
1800 and 1810. In 1841, the "Withvoth thread" was adopted, in other words true standardization was imposed. Meanwhile in the United
States, Elias Whitney studied the problem of mass production, having received an order for several tens of thousands of guns, and to solve the problem he constructed the first milling machine in 1818. At the same time, Blanchard invented a copying lathe to produce a large quantity of rifle butts. Normalization was thus imposed upon certain mass produced goods: but what was quite natural for nuts and bolts or the various parts of a gun was still not so for large machines. Furthermore, the comparatively frequent change in types, as was the case in the construction of locomotives, did not permit standardization.
What was born out of necessity in some cases, could become a new method of production in others, where standardization and normali zation were not imposed. Glove and shoe making could only be individual work, as each product was destined for one particular person. Around 1835, the Grenoble glovemaker, Jouvin, and the
Chfiteaurenaud shoemaker, Pinet, had the idea of reducing produc tion to a particular number of patterns and sizes established after a survey: hence the birth of sizing. From this point on it was possible to devise mass fabrication and to realize the machines necessary for this
(the first were machines for cutting several layers of material for gloves). From individual production, it was henceforth possible to develop mass production. At the very end of our period, the founder of the Belle Jardiniere, a department store in Paris, tried the experiment on clothes and subsequently introduced what is now called the clothing industry. This was aided by the invention of the sewing machine in
1845 by the Frenchman, Thimonnier.
The increase in tool productivity and procedures, and the cost of necessary new plants inevitably led to important modifications in production structures: the large scale business was to replace small individual producers and family businesses. Although the notion of industrial concentration was not new in itself - and we have already seen that Colbert had established this with his privileged manufac turers who brought into play techniques which had formerly not been applied within the country - it had not yet been put into practice.

===== PAGE 678 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
661 Henceforth, the manufacture of rails or the use of large textile machines could no more be the work of small enterprises.
This concentration had begun on a modest scale at the end of the 18th century, even in France where the technical revolution only infiltrated very gradually. In an account of 1785, out of 87 factories belonging to the cotton industry, 69 manufactured less than ten thousand products per year, but 11 produced ten to twenty thousand, 6 produced twenty to thirty thousand and only one produced over thirty thousand objects. At Abbeville during the same period, Van Robais employed more than one thousand, two hundred factory workers and ten thousand people working at home. The huge silk making oper ations in the provinces of the South-East, the paper mills in Essonnes made possible by the use of Dutch cylinders, and the Le Creusot furnaces, signal the transition from one type of business to another, conceived on a completely different scale. Saint Gobain had already announced semi-monopolies of businesses in the mirror making industry.
This movement was far more rapid in England, and from the first decades of the 19th century concentration was already considerable in the iron and steel, coal or textile industries, although in the latter this was less so in weaving than in spinning. There are many such individual examples, but no study of the whole has as yet been undertaken. The large enterprises of Boulton and Wilkinson may be cited, however, dating from before the French revolution, which were both concen trated and integrated, even becoming, as has already been pointed out, initiators of technical progress.
Some examples of concentration in France, where the process has been more thoroughly studied, prove the impact of technical progress on the evolution of structures. Although coal production was less technically advanced than other industries, it was, however, very rapidly concentrated. Around 1840, the ten leading businesses pro duced over half the total value of French coal production, exactly
55.5% . With fifteen businesses in operation, this percentage increased to 62.86% . The leading company, Anzin, alone produced
23 .24 % . In 1851, the two leading companies were responsible for almost half the production (48.8%), and the ten larger companies accumulated about 82% of the total production. In most instances the technical pumping problems were the cause of these successive concentrations.

===== PAGE 679 =====

662
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figures are less clear for the iron and steel industry, doubtless owing to the lack of sufficient information. In 1810, the ten major companies would have represented 43.6% of the turnover of the French iron industry, but this result should be considered with caution. According to more reliable data, in 1828 the ten leading companies represented
22.2% of the cast iron production, and 24.35% of the iron production.
In approximately 1840, this percentage was to fall to 13.44% for production value. This phenonemon is more easy to explain than one would think. Customs' protection, poor geographical distribution of raw materials, and the lack of developed transports to some extent constituted a setback to technical innovation. The overall statistics for production demonstrates that faced with almost complete stagnation of iron produced using wood and the noticeable growth of iron produced using coal, cast iron continued to be produced for the most part using wood. The growth of certain large modern or semi-modern firms clearly illustrates the direction in which the balance swung
(growth rate during the period 1830 and 1848):
