350 Le Creusot . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
200 Fourchambault. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
178.3 We do not possess similar tables for other industries, especially for the textile industry. We may suggest, and we will return to the particular case of France later, that although large scale enterprise had not yet succeeded with new techniques, it was nevertheless on the way:
it was only when all the techniques were at the same stage of devel opment, particularly for those of transports, that the triumph of industrial concentration was to become evident.
It is remarkable that the appearance of these large production centres aroused some interest in the organisation of labour, which was to be termed industrial economy for a long time. Certainly, Adam
Smith had praised the division of labour, but this was merely a purple passage and the manufacture of pins was probably more symbolic than real. It was in London in 1832 that Charles Babbage published the first work on the economics of machines and factories (On the Economy of
Machinery and Manufactures). It was undoubtedly still ahead of its

===== PAGE 680 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
663 time, as despite some success this work still did not have a long lasting effect.
There is another significant aspect of the technical revolution, that of the transfers which are implemented in different sectors of econo mic life. Although the transfers implemented in the world of labour astounded both contemporaries and historians, it hardly referred to transfers put into effect in capital and incomes. Obviously, industries existed which previously operated if not without any capital at all, then at least with a limited amount of capital. Such was the case in the textile industry: virtually no machines were used in the spinning mills, and they were still of little value in the weaving industry. The introduction of better looms, and the necessity for large buildings to house a more concentrated industry, naturally presented the problem of capital.
Again the amount of capital required was not enormous and could be put together by an individual, indeed by a family. Throughout the first part of the 19th century, the textile business was still largely a family business; companies with capital were very rare. Families of industria lists existed both in France and England: originally either craftsmen or even farmers, they gradually became distributors of raw materials and collectors of finished products, even renting the equipment required for production. These men were also to become the textile industria lists in the early years of the 19th century. By accumulating capital they could participate in the innovation of techniques. In France and many other areas, the secularization of the property of the clergy enabled industrialists to acquire large buildings (particularly ab bi es) very cheaply (which may be recalled in the case of Ourscamp, Royaumont and Senones: Cistercian abbies which had advanced hydraulic systems were perfectly suited for these uses).
The problem was rather different in the iron and steel industry. This transformation was progressive in England, so that old ironmasters could adapt their trade to technical change. On the Continent, however, this transformation was more sudden and more global because it took place much later. It was therefore necessary to rally together considerable amounts of capital. A large number of enterprises were subsequently formed in France (Societe de Saint
Etienne, Alais, Decazeville); but the disposition of capital and the organisation of financial markets hardly facilitated these operations.
We will see later that the distribution of raw materials, which had largely favoured technical change in England, were on the contrary in

===== PAGE 681 =====

664
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
France a considerable restraint on progress. Guy Thuillier has empha sized another difficulty in the province of Nivernais (but this may also be encountered elsewhere) : most ironmasters were tenants and the relatively short length of leases was an obstacle to technological investments. The rigour of the capital structure naturally curbed any transfer.
We require more detailed studies in order to measure the strength of the links which existed between technical transformation and the development of capitalism, of the techniques of capitalism: the chemical industry, now in the process of being studied, provides a good example of an entirely new industry. Several years ago, a symposium organized on the beginnings of steam sailing demonstrated the exist ence of identical facts. The development of various techniques was to aggravate the problem to some extent: no single investment but repeated investments were made in order to adapt quickly to technical change. Here again, we suffer from the absence of precise studies. In
1834, a Parisian millowner, Griolet, observed this phenomenon: "In industry, when there is no progress there is regression." In the same year, Vayson, a carpet maker in Abbeville, had already changed his equipment twice since the beginning of the Restoration. Others, however, testified of the limits with which they collided. This was so in the case of the great industrialist from Lille, Mimerel: "We hesitate to replace the old machines with new ones, we repair our old equip ment. " Only customs' protection, enabling prices to be aligned on the highest rates imposed in a country, allowed repeated investments by self-financing. It is easy to comprehend the motives behind these protectionist politics, declared by some authorities to be provisionary, which from 1821 spread throughout the whole of Western Europe:
they precisely ensured technological change by directing capital for investment towards new techniques at a time when Britain, which had mainly taken the lead, had almost exhausted her first industrial transformation."
Propensity to innovate and invest are naturally bound together.
Even during this period, men were perfectly aware of this. One of the mdst clear-sighted men in this respect was the Parisian banker, Laf fitte. From 1824-1825, he firmly upheld the two fundamental mechan isms by which he intended to achieve capital transfer. The first was reduction in the rate of public loans: by acting thus, loans were directed towards a more private rather than industrial province and

===== PAGE 682 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
665 more mobile capital was released for investments in new economic sectors. The second was the creation of the Societe commanditaire de l'industrie (Limited company for industry), destined to favour the indispensable accumulation of capital. According to Laffitte this rate conversion was "to reduce interest in our provinces, contribute capital within them and with capital aid the provinces could then develop means of communication, enable the formation of industrial estab lishments and revive work." And he added significantly: "This pen chant for work is naturally accompanied by another constant factor, that of innovating and improving, a no less favourable tendancy than the former towards industrial progress." One could hardly make it clearer. The object of the Societe commanditaire was to "contribute and participate in the success of any enterprise, any invention and any improvement concerning agriculture, industry and trade." it was later specified that enterprises which solicited its support were to exploit either a new branch of industry or an "industry improved by new methods." In the first half of the 19th century, no other position as logical or any other programme as coherent as that of Laffitte's existed.
The problem of transfering revenue has not been the subject of any research, either as a whole or in part. Nevertheless, this question would seem to be significant. We will return later to the subject of moving manpower, which really is the transfer of salaries. Owing to the absence of any work on this subject, we will look at just two examples.
The textile industry was mainly a cottage industry. We have just said that at Abbeville, Van Robais employed 1200 workers in his factories and 10,000 employees working at home: most textile industries were based on the same system; at the end of the 18th century or at the beginning of the 19th, Ternaux or Richard-Lenoir found themselves in exactly the same situation. The mechanisation of textile operations and the concentration of labour removed a source of revenue from the country which, although was only a supplement to other resources, was nevertheless a means of income. This was also true of the concentration of silk making. While the 18th century had searched every possible avenue to provide rural communities with subsidiary income (as was the case in France for the expansion of the spinning wheel), technical progress tended to upset a balance which had been established with some difficulty. According to the proportion which

===== PAGE 683 =====

66
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
this supplementary revenue represented in global resources, men could either follow or not follow machines.
The second example is provided by the 1829 survey on the iron and steel industry in France. A simple problem was presented and a more wide reaching and more complex question was evoked. The simple problem was quite clearly the loss which proprietors of woods and forests experienced or were to experience through the expansion of coal production, the iron and steel industry being their only outlet.
And already the total loss was calculated at around thirty million francs. The second argument was more specious. By maintaining high prices, customs' protection imposed considerable charges upon con sumers. Freedom of trade would have given a country much cheaper iron supplies. Moreover, the capital invested in all these modern enterprises, estimated to be between 70 and 94 million francs during this period, would have been better used in improving agriculture. It was even claimed that the French iron industry led to the increase of woods and forests to the detriment of arable lands.
The acquisition of new techniques by non innovatory countries was the subject of a very recent synposium. Presented in this way, the question may reveal two principal aspects. Firstly, one general fact may be observed: the introduction of new techniques, whether they imported ideas or not. We have already recognized the changes in structure which could encourage the adoption of a new technique. In the period which concerns us here, we have seen that the most important phenomenon was the concentration of industry which was henceforth to become the necessary corollary. We have already examined the financial consequences of this, and there are others which more particularly involve man.
The first and the best known factor is what is termed technological unemployment. From the beginning of the 19th century, the question arose as to whether technical progress actually suppressed labour or only induced transfers. Although today we are generally persuaded that the second proposition is the most likely, the first has often violently manifested itself from the 16th century onwards. Stories of inventors being threatened and machines being smashed have punc tuated the history of technical progress and this behaviour may be found throughout every industrial sector: these facts have been highlighted often enough, so we need not debate this point any longer.

===== PAGE 684 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
667 The transfer of the working class and the formation of these masses have not been studied in as much depth. Some research has demon strated the difficulties which the French, Belgium or Austrian iron and steel industry experienced in assembling the manpower needed in large modern factories, and the fears generated by this concentration of workers, who were part of the "dangerous classes." Although it is wirth being underlined here, this problem is not part of our investi gation.
The transmission of a technical system from a pioneering country to other countries is somewhat different, and we need only look at Britain in the 18th century for proof of this. The first objective was to acquire an understanding or knowledge of the new techniques. Certainly, many techniques were circulated throughout Europe which illustrates the technological advance enjoyed by England after 1815. In order to preserve this lead, the British tried to check the export of machines:
they were quickly convinced of the fact that it was difficult, if not impossible, to block the spread of technical progress. A famous
Parliamentary Report of 1825 provides plenty of information on this topic. Equally well known are the beginnings of what could be called industrial espionage, about which there are almost legendary accounts: the Frenchman, de Wendel, became a worker in a metallur gic factory in Wales, and Scrive, of Lille, smuggled out the plans of
English textile machines by sewing them into his jacket; there are many other such examples. The distribution of technical journals quickly solved this problem.
Difficulties could vary in nature. While some industries merely depended upon knowledge of new procedures or equipment, there were others in which natural manufaturing conditions played an important role. A good example of this is provided by an account written by the French ironmaster, Rambourg, in 1815:
Those who so easily propose the substitution of mineral coal with vegetable coal do not seem to comprehend that this entails changing virtually everything in furnaces, refiner ies, mechanics and workshops, and that one must be near the coalmines supplying the suitable type of coal, have the mineral within range of the fuel, and able to employ workers capable of carrying out this work. We would only be deluding ourselves by claiming that most of our ironworks rival the price of English iron. Favoured by nature for fuel and minerals which may be found in the same pit, they convert that into coke and, by using steam and rotary engines, they give their cylinders enormous power with the capacity to draw this former product into bars. Numerous channels are then used to receive these irons and to facilitate their transport to the sea. All these advantages, which we are far from possessing, are huge sources of economy for the English.

===== PAGE 685 =====

668
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
This explains why innovation was often partial in this sector of French industry. Although large factories, Saint Etienne, Alais or
Decazeville, adopted global innovation, coke furnaces, puddling workshops and rolling mills, within which there were still serious problems to be solved, the French were content to build ironworks to
British design, supplied with iron produced by smelting with wood:
such were de Wendel's factories at Chatillon and Fourchambault. Of the thirty-one "English" ironworks which existed in France in 1828, only two belonged to factories which had adopted all the new technical ideas. In many cases the new transport techniques were necessary in order to reunite the two essential elements used in making iron with coke - coal and minerals -, at a suitable price (that is to say prices which were lower than those produced in the old metallurgy process which used wood). In the Austrian-Hungarian empire, the first blast furnace was only built at Vitkovice in 1836, when the railway supplied Styrian minerals to the coal basin at Ostrava.
Originally, it was also indispensable that the purchasers of new techniques could employ machines at the very moment when a national industry of mechanical construction took over. National production of textile machines seems to have been rapidly established:
plans and designs rather than machines themselves were sought in Britain. In other industries, on the other hand, only the importation of
British machines enabled the adoption of new processes, the construc tion of heavy mechanical equipment being far more difficult to put into practice due to the limited market. England therefore supplied France with equipment for ironworks, railway material and sea going machines up until 1835 and even later in other countries.
The last point involves men. From the 18th century onwards, entrepreneurs and workers, many of whom were British, contributed recently effected techniques to the Continent. After John Kay, the inventor of the flying shuttle summoned by the French government,
Holk er who became inspecteur des manufactures, and Wilkinson who participated in the founding of Le Creusot, other Britons went to
France after 1815: Jackson contributed the techniques of cast steel to Saint Etienne, and Manby and Wislon created an "English" ironworks ard a workshop for mechanical constructions at Charenton and also took over the Le Creusot factory for several years. In Belgium, another Briton, Cockerill, founded the first modern iron factories and equipped the textile industry of Belgium and the Rhineland, indeed

===== PAGE 686 =====

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
669 even Poland and Russia. British builders constructed railway lines almost everywhere. All this has been amply demonstrated.
Apart from entrepreneurs, workers also travelled abroad to set up machines and operate them until the local workforce could take over.
Many British workers were transplanted abroad, particularly in heavy industries, metallurgy and mechanical construction. In 1828, in the factory ofFourchambault in the province ofNivernais, the substitution of French personel for British was virtually completed: only ten puddlers and ten lamina tors remained of the latter. "The French employees having less skill and less dexterity have cost us greater fuel consumption and an even greater waste of cast iron beyond that which we allowed them." At Charenton, where a factory had been founded by the British, all the workers were originally this nationality. At the time of the survey, half the puddlers were French, but almost all the laminators were still British "because the work required a dexterity which the French had great difficulty in achieving. " The major industrialist, Boigues, summed up the opinion of the French ironmas ters thus: "Workers from England brought up from childhood in the ironworks had acquired, without trying, a skill which was of use to them. When the children of French labourers had the strength to work they proved themselves to be as good as the English." The Lillonais mill owner, Mimerel, also considered that English workers had more experience and were industrially better educated. "They produced more and better quality goods for the same price. "
This situation was typical of most European countries who wished to introduce British techniques into their industry. When these advanced techniques were introduced into the forges of Vitkovice, in Moravia, we see that foreign workers represented around 20 % of the workforce. Shift workers already appear to have been used, and there was a large proportion of highly qualified Prussians and Poles among these foreigners. As for the British, there were extremely highly qualified specialists, particularly the puddlers and laminators, who were instructed to train local workers in the new techniques.
No one isolated problem of techniques exists, indeed it cannot, owing to its very nature. The adoption of a new technical system, in whichever country, requires an overall framework of adapted struc tures in every domain.
It is therefore certain that an industrial revolution took place in the true sense of the term, insofar as every structure became coherently

===== PAGE 687 =====

670
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
integrated. It is difficult, however, to specifiy which sectors played motivating roles as events were so interwoven.
Historians will probably continue to dispute and interminably debate this subject. Similarly, some industries only enjoyed partial progress, at least in the first phase of development, and countries also existed where production conditions, especially material conditions, did not produce an industrial revolution similar to the one in Britain which became the example for other countries. The Czech, Purs, has demonstrated the expansion of new techniques in Bohemia, favoured by the Continental System which provided this region with all the
Central European markets. In 1799 there were 40,283 spinners, 12,000 in 1815, and 5391 in 1825 when production was considerably increased:
mechanisation of spinning was finalized from the 1820's. After 1830, mechanisation was established throughout the textile industry.
Railway building after 1835 involved the iron industry and mechanical construction (the Englishmen, Evans and Lee undertook this oper ation in Prague). The slow development and gradual use of the steam engine has been cited against this thesis: in fact some countries employed large hydraulic resources. In 1848, in the district of Liberec, energy supplies represented 1895 horsepowers in terms of hydraulic energy, and 91 horsepowers in terms of steam engines. But if Britain had enjoyed the availability of these hydraulic resources would she have developed the steam engine to such an extent? In North America, the same processes were met in the first stage of industrialization. As one authority has recently suggested, "Waterfalls were the primary source of industrial energy in the United States up until the third quarter of the 19th century." Major businesses, Lowell and Manches ter, were founded upon hydraulic energy. We have said that the turbine had raised hydraulic power to the same level as steam energy.
In order for an industrial revolution to come about, it was indispensa ble that all the elements which had contributed to the wealth of
England be reunited.
The adoption of techniques involved in modern production, industrial concentration, and the appearance of a certain form of capitalism are conclusive proof by which the adoption of a new technical system may be defined.
Bertrand Gille (Translated by P. Southgate and T. Williamson)

===== PAGE 688 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
The history of how the modern technical system came into being - that same system which is in the process of disappearing today - has not yet been adequately studied, apart from some instances, at least not in its entirety, and still less in its interpretation. And even in detail, there are a great many points which have yet to be properly clarified.
Historians of economics seem to have been blinded by the industrial revolution in 18th century England, and have neglected to evaluate the remarkable technical change which took place in the second half of the 19th century. It may be that the relatively recent notion of economic growth has played an important role in this respect. This long and continuous period, which has been considered as one of the motivating factors of the English industrial revolution, has been highlighted. But the very continuity of this movement defined in this way conceals the possibility of another transformation as marked as the first. Most works, even the most recent, have consequently insisted on a parallel continuation of technical progress without distinguishing any precise phases.
We will try to break at this point with this oversimplified pattern and replace it with another, based precisely on the existence of another industrial revolution which could have created, in the second half of the 19th century, a technical system which was very different from that of the end of the 18th century or of the first half of the 19th. Although the techniques in use on the eve of the First World War have not been the subject of serious study, one may observe that they were completely different from those which were generalized around 1850;

===== PAGE 689 =====

672
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
even though certain old sectors, so to speak, were carried over into the new and more advanced technical system. The aeroplane, oil, thermal turbines, steel, organic chemicals, and electric power, to cite but a few examples, were manifestations of a system of techniques which was completely new by comparison with the system of 1850 with its reciprocating steam engine, iron and coal, etc. This view of the relative decline of certain economic powers, such as England, may be explained in terms of the country's devotion to techniques of the old system, while the rapid expansion of other economies, such as those of the United States of America and Japan, provide a striking image, the development of which coincides exactly with the appearance and the immediate adoption of a new technical system.
Let us now list these developments, using approximate dates. The new "industrial revolution" took place in two distinct phases. The first phase may be placed roughly between 1855 and 1870. In order for a technical system to be definitively established, a period of adaptation is necessary which is both of a technical and economic nature, as indeed we have already noted for the 18th century. It is therefore logical to assume that a second stage was necessary, and this can be situated approximately between 1880 and 1900. This was finally followed by all the developments and improvements of which the modern system was capable, possibly stimulated by the demands of the First World War.
It is agreed that economic growth or development is necessarily aided by technical progress. In other words, we should seek to find out if this continuity of growth, unanimously and statistically observed and evident everywhere, was compatible with the permanence of a technical system, even when it had reached the final stage of its development. Would it not be better to say that at a particular stage, accounting for a certain number of circumstances which have not yet been adequately emphasized, economic growth would not have been possible without some change in the technical system? The very notion of a technical system implies, to a certain extent, that a global transformation would have had to be general and systematic, rather than piecemeal progress in one or more series of independent inventions. This explanation, arbitary though it might appear, nev ertheless merits greater consideration, and it is in this way that we intend to approach the problem.

===== PAGE 690 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
ENVIRONMENTS AND CONDITIONS
OF AN INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
673 As we have seen repeatedly, any technical mutation depends upon both internal and external causes. And it is from these causes that stem the necessity and the possibility of mutation. Apart from these causes, technical change takes place within a framework and a context, as indeed we have already observed, where cause and effect are far less evident than convergence and concomitance. It is these circumstances that we would first like to try to apprehend.
We have already stressed for the 18th century, the possible importance of demographic expansion in the introduction of a new technical system. But the second half of the 18th century undoubtedly saw no such expansion in industrialized countries, at least in those where the new technical system was likely to arise. The worldwide population growth of these privileged countries was infinitely less than it had been between 1750 and 1850. But we will not dwell on facts which are relatively well known. The birth rate dropped constantly, only compensated by the decline in the mortality rate, which resulted in the aging of the population. A superficial analysis of this would hazard a complete separation of the population movement and techni cal mutations.
We have already corrected this first assessment of the effects of demographic expansion for the 18th century. Obviously the increase in the number of people affects the volume of demand. In societies which were less advanced in the domain of consumption, as was so in the case of Western Europe at the end of the 18th century, the population growth was effectively something important, indeed of paramount importance. But in more advanced countries, such as those of Western
Europe in the second half of the 19th century and all the more so in our day and age, the number of consumers certainly counted less than the volume of consumption. The constantly growing wealth of these countries and the aging of their populations contributed considerably to the increase in the level of consumption. This may be seen in the greatly increased consumption of industrial products by comparison with food. In France, the annual consumption of wheat flour per head was 1 .76 hundredweight in 1841-1850, increasing to 2.45 bun-

===== PAGE 691 =====

674
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
dredweight in 1891-1900. The following figures are for cotton: 1 .7 kilogrammes in 1850, against 4.1 in 1900. Although these figures apply to a slightly larger population, they are nevertheless still highly significant. Thus despite rapidly reduced demographic expansion, the pressure of demand remained very high. One need hardly multiply these examples as the figures for other countries in the same stage of economic expansion are analagous.
There is another phenomenon which is equally important. Increase of demand was not only felt on the local level, inside industrialized countries, but there was considerable expansion of foreign markets.
The decision to open the Suez canal dates from 1854, and it was completed in 1869. The penetration of Eastern countries, such as
China (1860) or Japan (1858-1868), and the beginnings of great colonial expansion in Africa, both by France and England, also provided new outlets which required equipment and consummer goods. And here, on the contrary, it was the amount which compen sated for the poor level of individual consumption. We encounter yet again influences which may contribute to demographic evolution taken in a different sense from the one usually considered.
Economic conditions are no less important factors. It is mundane to state that technical progress, or a new technical system must be compatible with particular economic institutions. There are two essential elements for this economic organisation: the accumulation of capital and the growth of major enterprises. The former is indispensa ble, and the second may be a consequence rather than a pre-existing necessity.
Although self-financing had mainly been and was to remain an effective way of accumulating capital, it soon became apparent that sources of capital external to the enterprise were indispensable, particularly as technical change stimulated new industries and therefore new businesses, such as the electrical and automobile industry. We should immediately note that this accumulation of capital required not only movements of capital within countries, but also the international transfer of capital that is so important today.
Several phenomena may be observed, the chronological concord ance of which was almost perfect. Between 1854 and 1860, restrictions on interest rates had been abolished throughout most of the Western world, although England had attempted this from 1834. This liberali zation was to greatly facilitate the movement of capital. And between

===== PAGE 692 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
675 1856 and 1867, governments generally lifted restrictions on limited liability corporations, giving a freer hand to the directors of enterprise, while at the same time the public grew accustomed to the new forms of investment. The major international expositions, the first of which was in 1851, definitively launched the great industrial and technical era, even fostering a taste for technology. At the same period we may notice this change of attitude in the face of action or obligation, and also in face of state loans. The major projects of the railway companies between the years 1845 and 1847 had been an indication of this, but due to the interest shown, the action of the railway could almost be compared to the loans invested in it. Henceforth, began the great adventure in finance and industry. In 1850, the Paris stock exchange listed 42 stocks; by 1869, the number had already grown to 182 by 1869.
Equally significant is the appearance of well developed banking systems. Here again the dates of this development match those of the new technical system. On the Continent, the founding of the French
Credit Mobilier in 1852, marked the birth of the commercial banking network: especially devoted to financing industrial innovation, it was infinitely superior to similar establishments of the preceding period.
The deposit bank was first created in France in 1859, with the Credit Industriel et Commercial, quickly followed by the Credit Lyonnais
(1863) and by the Societe Generale (1864). This example was quickly followed by the whole of Europe, and there was a veritable banking explosion as Germany, Austria, Scandinavia and other countries followed suit. In the second phase after 1870, the proliferation of counters, made possible by branch banks, contributed to the systema tic draining of small deposits to create capital for investment. Once again we will not dwell on a phenomenon which has already been emphasized several times.
The concentration of enterprises is another aspect of this structural revolution in the economy. Truth to tell, it appeared both as a condition and as a consequence of technical progress. It was a precon dition in that only large concentrated enterprises could initiate major technical innovations; it was a consequence in that the new techniques were inconceivable without the concentration of production. Today we are fairly accustomed to this evolution. The availability of more abundant capital naturally facilitated this increase in the size of business, remembering that in France this concentration of business was considered by the Pereire brothers to be one of the essential

===== PAGE 693 =====

676
TECHNIQUES AND CMLIZATIONS
factors in the policies of Credit Mobilier. In the same country, the first great wave of concentration was set in motion in 1854-55, by the formation of the first great railway networks from 1852.
Changes were only possible if intellectual and cultural values and attitudes encouraged them. After 1850, romanticism was stifled, the love of nature exhausted itself and was replaced by other values of the age. It was undoubtedly from this time on that Europe felt the industrial spirit that had permeated England in the first half of the 19th century. Refusal to accept industrialism was manifested almost until the end of the reign of Louis-Philippe in both France and Belgium, and fears of industrialism disappeared in their turn. Industrial power was no longer only power but it was now the source of happiness as well as profit. After 1850, a new generation came to economic power, which had first demonstrated its power in the economy during the reign of
Louis-Philippe, and even practised a literary, political and economic sort of romanticism. This generation took over many industrial enterprises from individualistic founders, supporters of these small isolated and scattered units, who had only been belatedly aware of the first industrial revolution and had even feared it. The family firm gave way to a specialized body of management. Henceforth, they were to leave the ways of their ancestors, concentrate their resources, break out of the old confines, transform manufacturing processes as well as the method of labour itself, open enterprise to the outside, and, in short, revolutionize "industrial organization. " It would be interesting to study the employers class from this angle but this research remains to be done. Once again this is a matter of cause and effect, and one may observe that social and mental attitudes and values persisted far longer in sectors where technical transformation was more gradual and less radical. The textile industry is probably the best example of this.
The new men had also been trained in a different way. Apart from the Ecole polytechnique in Paris and its administrating schools, new engineering schools were founded throughout Europe between 1830 and 1848. It was therefore after this date that the first graduates arrived at commanding positions in industry. Both the subject matter and the spirit of instruction had changed.
Around 1834, a French industrialist attributed the economic success of England partly to the general standard of education of the working classes. The development of primary education (remembering that the
Guizot law was created in 1833), and the creation and development of

===== PAGE 694 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
677 adult education enabled the introduction of technical knowledge, which was not yet a closed world, to more and more people on all levels of society. This was one of the motivating factors of technical progress.
It became relatively easy, even for a self-taught person to improve his education to the standard of a useful and working knowledge.
Yet another fact merits more detailed research. This concerns the extraordinary diffusion of scientific knowledge and its technical appli cation. Reviews and encyclopedias proliferated, and scientific and technical journals became abundant, but a critical list and an analysis of their content is required. In other words, it was possible or had become possible to acquire the knowledge necessary for certain inventions even without a university education. Bessemer learned much from these encyclopedias. James B. Francis, who improved
Fourneyron's water turbine, was an educated and learned man. He gave an account of his experiments published in Lowell Hydraulic
Experiments in 1855. And Pelton, who took the turbine beyond the achievements of Francis, was only a village carpenter, working on water mills, but he had bought and studied a copy of Francis' book which gave him an elementary training (figs. 1 and 2). Gramme is yet another example; he had learnt his skills from Ruhmkorff, since the oral transmission of knowledge was still practised; he may have attended Becquerel's lectures at the Conservatoire des arts et metiers, and he had read Ganot's manual with the aid of a dictionary. So although Gramme was a mechanic, he also possessed more than a little useful scientific knowledge. The diffusion of a certain level of scientific and technical knowledge to a broader public already provided with primary education was of fundamental importance to technical inven tion.
A particular place should be given to the domain which is situated half way between science and techniques: that of measurements.
Measuring instruments, created partly with a scientific aim in mind, were quickly found to be indispensable for techniques. Most of them were created before our period, but in refinements and new methods obtained by experimentation, measurement was all important. It has been suggested that American hydraulics could not have developed without Prony's brake, and in the the mechanical industries, there was the invention in 1849 (at least this is the date of the patent), of the gauge equipped with "screw and circular vernier" by Palmer.
Although some detailed studies have been done, research still remains

===== PAGE 695 =====

678
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Level above Level below Figure 1. - The Francis turbine. (From Lehr, Les Moteurs, Paris, 1970)

===== PAGE 696 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
679 Figure 2. - Pelton's wheel. (From Lehr) to be completed on the subject of the introduction of measuring apparatuses for manufacturers and the way in which progress in this domain was achieved. Measuring instruments were above all required for mass production. From the brake created by Prony to the man ometer or gauge by Bourdon, and from the inventions of Palmer to the shims by Johansson and Ellstrom, there was a continued effort to make improvements.
The relations between science and techniques varied greatly depending upon the field. We have already referred to this with regard to the education of technicians. It is, however, difficult to locate points of convergence, as their level varies according to both techniques and sciences. In a very general fashion, we may distinguish three levels of interaction: an elementary level where the links between science and techniques were very uncertain, then a level where techniques sought to acquire a scientific logic, and finally, a level where the interchanges between science and techniques were numerous and constant.

===== PAGE 697 =====

680
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
In the second half of the 19th century, the scientific level of inventions remained quite low in some technical sectors. The way for progress was open wide. Louis C. Hunter has provided a few inter esting facts with regard to the successive improvements of the hydrau lic turbine:
The advance in the design, building, and running of hydraulic turbines was achieved in a typically American fashion: not by scientific analysis where mathematical calculations were tested by experiments, but by purely empirical methods applied by mechanics and craftsmen.
Just after the First World War a famous engineer wrote the following:
All the scientific teachings of Boyden and Francis were thrown to the wind, and the slogan "cut and try" prevailed throughout. It a turbine did not perform well, its buckets were pulled back, raised, or lowered, and its blades shaped until it worked better (fig.3).
But constructors did not claim to understand the theory behind their art, and there were only two who agreed that theory was at the root of what they did: everything was based on trial and error. Of one of the most successful turbine builders it was said that "his theories were false, but his ignorance did not prevent him from succeeding." A manual written around 1850 on the construction of mills also gave similar conclusions: "research for scientific theories have not contri buted much to the improvement of the mechanical arts of this country
( . . . ) No progress was due to scientists in the domain of practical mechanics." We have tried to show the limits of this interpretation of technical progress. It was often the product of a bias, and we should not suppose that these inventors were geniuses who arrived at the desired result by pure intuition, unaided by any scientific knowledge.
The birth of real "technology," in other words the rational expla nation of how technical operations could be used systematically, appeared in the middle of the 19th century, even though, as we have seen in the preceding chapter, attempts had been made in certains domains. The accumulation of technical knowledge, diffused by spe cialized periodicals, led quite naturally to a more logical ordering and more general explanations. "Descriptions" were abandoned in favour of "treatises." On the subject of metallurgy, we should assess the difference between the treatise by Percy and the works of his predeces-

sors. We should also analyse the methods used in expositions, the respective sides of scientific reasoning, and the experiments already made, where there are discrepancies between various works. Unfortu nately any detailed work is completely lacking in this area.
Naturally, technical education was also radically transformed. The Paris Conservatoire des arts et metiers had originally been linked to a gallery of machines. Instruction was by demonstration on the machines, in the same way that the subject of physics had been taught before being created into a logical and universal system. A spinning course was begun in the same establishment in 1804. But after 1819, this kind of instruction disappeared and was replaced by courses intended to provide technicians with a better scientific understanding:
general mechanics, chemistry, drafting, and even political economy.
It was only in November, 1852, and once again the chronologic concordance may be noted, that a spinning and weaving course was established. Also significant to the development of technology as formal knowledge was the creation in 1869 of a commercial testing centre for turbines at Lowell in the United States, selling its services to industry. It was later moved to the Connecticut valley under the control of the Holyoke Water Power Company, and was developed into a national testing centre for the productivity and conditionning of turbines.
In such a way, science and techniques were beginning to interact. It would be fascinating to trace the history of the development of scientific technology as it began to be formed at the boundary between science and techniques, borrowing elements from both worlds. From the stage of a series of logically organized empirical results, it passed during this period to the stage of specific scientific knowledge resulting from experiments designed and carried out to produce such knowl edge. Such were Mushet's 1845 discoveries of tungsten and manganese and their action as alloys, which were to have significant repercussions later by preparing the way for steel alloys. Visible progress was to be made during this period in the domain of scientific metallurgy. Henry
Le Chatelier, devoted himself to the study of three major problems:
the measurement of high temperatures in metallurgical furnaces, solved in 1886 by means of using the thermo-electric method (and the use of electricity in this demonstrates the reality of the technical system), with the platinum-platinum thermocouple, which was useful in thermic processing furnaces, and chemical mechanics (the laws of

===== PAGE 700 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
683 equilibrium being applied to the reduction of ores in the blast furnace and to the phenomena of deoxygenation of steel: the law of the displacement of equilibirum in 1884), which permitted slag masses and their composition to be taken into account in the problems of devel opment in the blast furnaces and steelmaking; and finally, the prepara tion of alumina for polishing metallographical samples which was followed in 1900 by the construction of a microscope with inverted lens.
Meanwhile, in 1885-1886, Floris Osmond created two methods of investigation. The first was microscopic metallography, concluding the research undertaken by Reaumur. The images he obtained made it possible to determine the correct treatment of steels, notably spe cialized steels. He used the second method to determine the critical points of steels as well as to characterize them. The theory of tem pering had been provided by Gore in 1869, and were refined by Barrett in 1873. Osmond, together with Werth, completed the theory in 1885.
In 1894, he defined the peculiarities of ferro-nickels. In 1895, he determined the qualities of martensite, used for quenching and auste nite, an element of super-quenching. So gradually a scientific techno logy of ferrous metallurgy and its alloys was formed.
Let us examine this history of scientific metallurgy to its conclusion as it is a valuable example and underwent a fundamental evolution. As the technology of iron and steelmaking became increasingly scientific, so invention could no longer be accidental, but on the contrary, invention was now sought in order to bring about a specific result. The entrepreneur thus anticipated future development and now had only one way in which to implement his plans: to set up a laboratory and direct technical research according to a scientific rationale. When in
1867, Boussingault, a chemist known for his work on metals, arrived at the Holtzer metallurgical plant near Saint-Etienne, he completely changed the previous research methods. Helped by the engineer,
Brustlein, Boussingault had by 1877 developed ferro-chromes. By mixing them with oxygen-free charges of the crucible, chromed steel was obtained. Other firms followed suit. By 1888, similar research was being carried out at Imphy, south of Nevers, especially on nickel steel.
On the request of the International bureau of weights and measures, the correct proportions were calculated which were to lead in 1897 to the production of new steels, invar and elinvar, whose coefficients of elasticity did not vary with temperature. Once again we require the

===== PAGE 701 =====

684
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
exact and detailed history of these factory laboratories, their results, their methods and their policies of research, both from a scientific and technical point of view, in order to appreciate precisely the way in which science and techniques are related to each other. And this applies to every kind of industry involved. These laboratories designed for technological research were no longer to be the fruit of individual initiative, but were soon to be organised on a collective level, as in 1911 a research laboratory was linked to the chair of the textile industry of the Conservatoire des arts et metiers.
As one advances, so technology becomes an extension of science.
The development of the steam turbine and its successive improvements were the result of many applications of the theory of thermodynamics and fluid mechanics. It would certainly be quite simple to determine precisely those zones of scientific restriction that exist within different techniques, but as yet no one has attempted to trace these limitations.
It is therefore in this important context, where every chronological division may be found, that technical change could be brought about.
At this point of our anaylsis we should emphasize the exact conver gence between techniques and economy.
We have already begun to see such a convergence: at this level, continual growth naturally requires a new technical system. For technical systems have their limits, and if the pressure of economic requirements continues to grow, technical mutation becomes imperative. Inside the technical system in use in the middle of the 19th century, we may observe a certain number of tensions resulting in several ways.
An increase in demand must result in an increase of production. The first problem is therefore a problem of quantities. Greater quantities may be obtained without a change of techniques simply by increasing the size of individ-ual enterprises or by increasing their number. But this is not always possible, if one considers production factors, owing to difficulties of securing raw materials, assembling labour, and a greater amount of capital investment. And any given technique imposes limits of marginal utility on these various factors of produc tion. A few figures will prove this statement. For example, the production of pig iron in the most advanced countries of Europe increased considerably between 1850-1854 and 1910-1913, as is shown in table I, in figures of thousands of metric tonnes.

===== PAGE 702 =====

1850-1854 . . . .
1910-1913 . . . .
THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
TABLE I
FRANCE
561 4664
GERMANY
245 14,836
ENGLAND
2716 9727 685 Without a general technical mutation in the whole complex repre sented by pig iron production, in other words in the machinery itself, in the mining of ore and coal and coking, etc. , no such increase in production would have been possible. It would have been necessary to increase the number of blast furnaces in France ninefold, and sixty times as many would have been required in Germany. These figures also reveal the difference in demand between the three countries.
The problem of cost demands no less important research. To produce a constant quantity at a lower cost was a temporary solution, as sooner or later demand would have increased to absorb the full capacity of the existing plant. As everybody knows, the ideal is to produce more at lower costs. Here again the technical system must eventually reach its limits and these same limits come into play when more is produced at constant costs. It would therefore seem logical to assume that two fundamental principles are valid. Logically, then, economic growth could not have continued without fundamental technical mutation or progress, otherwise the limits of the existing technical system would have brought it to a halt. On the other hand, the very notion of a technical system implies an overall mutation to assure the necessary equilibria at each level of the system.
The historian should therefore look first to the tensions which were manifested towards the middle of the 19th century, or which would have been manifested had there been no technical progress. He should then explain how other factors responded to this, either by improvement of old techniques where possible, or by the adoption of new methods. Finally, the existence of the new technical system should be demonstrated. Here again this is merely wishful thinking as no research to date is adequate enough for us to make definite conclu sions. We are therefore compelled to limit the discussion to a few examples.
Consider the production of energy in around 1850. Hydraulic energy had made considerable progress since Fourneyron's development of

===== PAGE 703 =====

686
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
the water turbine and since the improvements made to it by the Americans, Boyden and Francis, on the eve of the period in question.
Efficiency had been maximized, and the power could be increased only within certain limits. Moreover, hydraulic energy required its users to be located close to an adequate water supply, and without heavy investments and planning, they were at the mercy of natural hazards.
The first of Boyden's turbines (1844) produced 75 horsepower with a trial efficiency of 78% ; the second (1846) developed 190 horsepower with a maxiumum efficiency of 88% . More progress was possible, particularly in horsepower. Even before 1850, 500 and even 700 horsepower had been reached. Better results were obtained between
1850 and 1890, but only using low falls and slow wheels owing to the fact that the materials could not bear any greater stress. Manufacturing techniques were improved, enabling the cost of turbines to be cut considerably. From more than 200 dollars per installed horsepower in
1850, the Boyden turbines were available at 70 dollars per installed horsepower in 1861, and then 5-8 dollars twenty-five or thirty years later. About 1890, a power of 1000 hp was attained, presenting the final limit, but this had enabled both the power to be increased and the cost to be reduced.
Consider the steam engine, the old alternating engine which was the glory of the preceding industrial revolution. Obviously, it could never escape the limits it was born with, from both a purely technical and an economic viewpoint. Either considerable time was lost starting it up after each shutdown, or else it had to be kept running continually. Its register, or range of power, was severely limited for various reasons:
much like all other alternating machines, it could only be used for large power demands that did not exceed 5000 horsepower. Maintenance of this engine, especially lubrication, was costly and continuous. Stoking the boiler and removing cinders required a large workforce. To these drawbacks should be added the costs of construction, installation, and housing, as well as the difficulty of adjusting its running to variations in demand for power, and in some cases excessive congestion
(transports). Lastly, there was the problem of poor efficiency of 6 to 10% . These limitations meant that the alternating steam engine could only be used in certain well-determined sectors: industries with heavy concentrations of mechanised equipment, and heavy land transport for which securing fuel and water presented few problems. The steam engine was difficult to use in land transport where slight power was

===== PAGE 704 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
687 needed. In maritime transport, its space and weight requirements imposed strict limits on the speed and size of ships. Lastly, the alternating steam engine proved itself to be poorly suited to driving electric generators.
Nevertheless the classic steam engine did undergo some important improvements, but these simply pushed back the limits rather than removing them completely. The change to high pressure and the use of expansion in the compound engine had been the first such devel opments in 1830-1834. At the beginning of our period, after improvements in accessories (the boiler to take but one example), engineers concentrated on regenerated steam first considered by
Seguin at the Academie des sciences on the 3rd January, 1855, and in the attempts made by Siemens. This brought about a fuel saving of two thirds. Then came superheating, a process in which a portion of the steam produced was taken out and carried to a very high temperature and pressure. When this steam was returned to the boiler it turned the drops of water that had escaped the first heating into new steam. The
1855 exposition demonstrated its effects. But note here again the necessity of parallel developments in complementary techniques, particularly in the metals necessary for very high pressure, which did not increase the weight, bulk or cost of the machine. Efficiencies and, simultaneously, horsepower produced, were therefore able to increase considerably, (in terms of kilogrammes of coal burnt per horsepower per hour):
The Newcomen Engine . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
The Watt Engine . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
The Trevithick (high pressure) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
The compound engine . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
With superheating . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
8.40 2.90 l.50 0.90 0.325 But we should repeat that such developments made in a certain technique could not have taken place without the appropriate changes in neighbouring techniques and the aquisition of the scientific knowl edge necessary. Induced progress is more important than autonomous progress. Indeed, it could be argued that the progress of the steam engine was induced by advances in complementary techniques.
Neglect, as we have already noticed, of such interdependencies among technical sectors has long served to distort the history of techniques.
But in any case the limits of the steam engine could not be overcome:

===== PAGE 705 =====

688
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
ever greater horsepower, even lower costs, and more diversity and flexibility of operation were indispensable for continued economic growth (fig. 4 to 6).
Let us take another, equally striking example of the limits met by techniques. In 1850, the railway was already in operation. Investment in the new means of transport had been very large, and so long as costs remained fairly low, it was to contribute a major factor in future economic growth. Frarn;ois Caron has aptly defined the situation:
For the engineer charged with managing the great transport service of the railway, technical progress provides the only form of escape from the contradictions in the centre of which he finds himself. Administrators and supervision demand that he ensures an increasing traffic and better services which cost less while maintaining profits. The only way of achieving this is to reduce cost prices. There were three problems: firstly, the problem of wages, particularly after 1860; secondly, the unlimited expansion of the railway network necessitated employing methods of exploitation for reduced traffic which were in proportion to the length of the network rather to the traffic itself. And thirdly, there was the risk of a glut or obstruction on the main lines, on which rising traffic could only be handled by the immediate accumulation of expensive operating methods.
These were the three reasons which compelled the smallholder to determine a reduction in the workforce, fuel, and installations. But, in order for innovation to be adopted, annual expenses brought about by loans could no longer be higher than the economy of the business which it made possible.
In the early years of the railroad operation, the replacement of worn out iron rails was a financial abyss. Nor was the cost of broken axles and wheels negligible. Failing some sort of technical solution, the growth of transport would have been abruptly halted. The advent of cheap steel in large quantities was to solve this problem. Meanwhile, considerable progress was made in tractive power which resulted in the perfection of the machines. A Crampton engine of 1850 weighed 125 kilos per horsepower; a 1894 locomotive weighed 72, and in 1913 only
50 kilos per horsepower.
Maritime transport presented a similar case except that the limit of its development would be more quickly reached. Any increase in speed required a greater increase in power, to take but one criterion, and the power required had to increase by approximately the cube of the speed. Thus for faster and bigger ships, engines needed to be vastly more powerful without becoming too heavy and bulky. Around 1900, the engine of a big liner comprised of alternating steam engines and cylindrical boilers capable of holding a large volume of water, which were consequently extremely heavy, not to mention the coal and water
