691 supply the ship required. The total weight per installed horsepower was approximately 150 kilos, and the machinery of the engine took up between a third and a half of the ship's usable cubage. It is not surprising in these conditions that between 1907 and 1914 the French naval engineer, Bertin, following actually the principles set forth by
Jacques-Auguste Normand in 1897, demonstrated mathematically the limitations of these displacements. According to Bertin, the stress bearing upon the hull of a large ship would require it to be made of increasing thick metal as displacement was increased. The fraction of the weight of the metal hull of a liner in relation to the total weight was expressed by the simple formula: C = 0.2166 + 0.01176 P 11. He calculated that the optimum displacement, giving the best proportion of the available fraction of displacement (engine and load), that is where the engine occupied the smallest fraction of the hull, was in a

===== PAGE 709 =====

692
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
ship of 29,588 tonnes, and that the maximum load would be reached with a displacement of 50,876 tonnes, which was about the size of the big liners at the time. At a displacement of95,324 tonnes, the hull itself would take up all the displacement, leaving a float with no room for engine or cargo. Here, assuming the continued use of existing tech niques, is a theoretical example of the limiting case. Fortunately, the new techniques that would permit these barriers to be broken were just being developed.
Certainly, many more examples of this kind are required to illustrate the limits of the division between the existing technical system and the beginning of a new one. It should therefore be possible to determine the sectors where technical change was necessary in order for econo mic growth to continue at whatever rate it did. Unfortunately research is not detailed enough to provide but a provisional outline. The analysis of the demand for energy, the need for diversified chemical products, and the necessity for suitable materials have not been the subject of any study. So one needs to study the fact that substitutes had to be found for certain products, and the need which there was to develop new products to fulfil modifications in consumption. In such a way, we might be able to value old inventions which had not yet been put to any use, and explain the attempt made to find new discoveries.
A series of imbalances, a series of tensions caused by economic growth itself, whatever rate it took, was thus leading towards new techniques. But due to the necessary links between diverse techniques, mutation was general. The automobile is not just a horseless carriage with a motor, with all its diverse components made of appropriate materials, such as the rubber tyre. It is also the network of fuel stations, and the road system designed for the automobile. Electrifi cation was not merely electricity, but it was also used for hydraulic turbines operated by high falls and great speed, or Diesel engines.
Economic progress takes place through technical progress, and techni cal progress could take place only through the supersession of one technical system by another.
This kind of analysis should also show all the pressures that econo mic and technical progress exercised upon the social system. The call for ever increasing numbers of workers led to the decline of the rural population, and the exodus from the countryside was on a larger scale after 1850. Compensating institutions were sought in the face of economic expansion and technical change which modified the rela-

===== PAGE 710 =====

THE MODERN 1ECHNICAL SYS1EM
693 tionship of strength. Here again the dates match almost exactly; In France the law against unions was repealed in 1864 and full labour legislation was passed in 1884. We find the same stages once again.
Coherence between systems cannot fail to exist.
THE GREAT MUTATIONS
A description of all the elements which the new technical system enforced between 1850 and 1900 in two major stages as well as the continuation of its development, perhaps owing to the First World
War as well as to the Great Depression beginning in 1929, would fill volumes. We must therefore limit ourselves once more to indicating the principal lines of development and to the main outlines of events in the most important sectors. Among these sectors we should emphasize the problems of energy, as well as those of natural resources, the modifications made in materials, and lastly, the main instruments of industrial production.
We have already pointed out the constraints of the preceding system on the subject of energy production, and we have also discussed how the evolution of other techniques, particularly those of materials, had enabled energy convertors handed down from the old technical system to be best used.
The classic alternating steam engine was therefore able to continue its career. After some early attempts in superheating shortly after
1850, it was mainly in its fabrication, in the use of specialized steels that the steam engine could provide greater sources of power and higher efficiency. Everybody knows, however, that few further developments could be made in this direction. By contrast, improvement of the hydraulic turbine was to be continuous and significant. Progress was at first slow after the modifications made by Boyden and Francis shortly before 1850. As Louis Hunter observes: "These turbines were dif ferent in the originality of their shape, the simplicity and solidity of their construction, their large scale manufacture at reduced costs and to standard sizes, and their output which was soon to exceed that of the wheels carefully designed and constructed by Boyden and Francis."
Engineers concentrated their efforts on turbines using falls of 15 to 20

===== PAGE 711 =====

694
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
feet, developing moderate horsepower of 35 to 75 hp. They had to be cheap, reliable, and efficient. In the wheel elements, the precision of the sizes, manufacturing and hand fitting was reduced to a minimum.
As Thurston has observed, American turbines were usually cast with their buckets in place and could be put into operation as soon as they left the factory.
But these turbines still suffered certain limitations when in use: low speed and efficiency when the gates were partly opened, and a tendency to become clogged by debris because of the small size and opening of the buckets. Low speed goes hand in hand with large volumes and weights compared with the energy produced and with a proportionally high cost of installation. These limitations were finally eliminated by the successful development of the inward flow turbine:
speed was increased by deepening and then enlarging the buckets and extending them towards the centre of the wheel, but this left little room in the centre to evacuate water. So the buckets had to be orientated towards the bottom to guide the water: the blades were curved towards the bottom and the outside in such a way that the water passing through the wheel ran out in a gentle and continuous spiral. Moreover, reducing the number of buckets increased power and avoided obstruction by debris. Thus in half a century, from 1840 to 1890 the turbine designers obtained the same power in half the space and for one fifth of the cost.
Final improvements sought to save water by increasing efficiency at half gate. A Swain turbine with an efficiency of 75% at full gate would drop to 66% at three quarters and to 50% at half gate. By 1879-1880, engineers had virtually eliminated efficiency loss at half gate. In the mines of California, the most improved tangential wheel was Pelton's creation; it was developed so much in size and power, attaining efficiencies of 80% and 1000 horsepower, that it could replace steam engines, saving at least two thirds of the energy cost. But here again, it was not possible to go any further than this.
As materials with better physical properties, steel and then spe cialized steel, became available, it was possible to use the hydraulic turbine in different conditions. As we have already seen, the use of high falls with great power and volume had been envisaged from 1848, but cast iron wheels limited both the pressure and speed of rotation.
The development of wheels and conduits made of steel meant that high falls could be used. The first attempts seem to have been made in

===== PAGE 712 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
695 France to drive heavy papermaking machinery which required a considerable amount of power. Matussiere in Domene (1870), Fredet in Brignoud (1871), and Horteur in Saint-Remy-de-Maurienne (1878) were forerunners in this field. In 1882, Berges succeeded in harnessing a fall of 500 metres. The Pelton turbine appeared just in time to be used as an energy convertor, and was to be used to great advantage in the production of electricity, as we shall see later.
The new energy convertors were in fact directly descended from two main earlier machines: the old alternating piston and cylinder steam engine and the turbine. This is a perfect example of technological continuity. Lenoir used the basic mechanisms of the steam engine in his internal combustion engine, and Diesel's great idea was to invent a high efficiency steam engine. Parsons and Laval substituted steam for water to turn their turbines. The reason that these ideas took so long to implement and were often spread over more than one century was due to the fact that essential elements were not discovered until the period in question now, that is to say after 1850.
The Abbe de Hautefeuille and then Huygens outlined the first ideas of an engine powered by internal combustion before the actual devel opment of the steam engine. But until the end of the 18th century, with the work of the Englishman, Barber, inventors hesitated between explosion and combustion, between direct use of this combustion or indirect use through the intermediary of hot air. Moreover, they worked towards the use of the mechanical power of hot air: the work of the Englishman, Stirling, in 1826, was taken up and completed by the
Swede, Ericsson. But this only involved a single acting machine driven by hot air and therefore entailed serious drawbacks. The firebox was dangerous in a small machine intended for use in the home, the coal it used as fuel was cumbersome, and the cylinder buckled under the heat of internal combustion. In 1824, the Englishman, Brown, used a mixture of air and gas after the many suggestions put forward at the beginning of the 19th century, by Lebon among others.
The real engine was the result of a convergence of several partial inventions shaped by clearly defined design criteria. In fact the main problem which had to be solved was that of a small engine to drive machinery in the home, and for particular mechanisms which had recently been developed, such as the sewing machine. It needed to operate at low power, take up little space as well as use less fuel and avoid fire hazards. The most practical source of energy was illuminat-

===== PAGE 713 =====

696
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
ing gas, which had come into extensive use since 1815. Here was a widely distributed fuel, one which required no storage. The machine itself was quite simply put together from the components of the classic alternating steam engine. Air and gas had to be mixed: Barber had sketched the idea of the carburettor at the end of the 18th century. In
1843, Father Eugenio Barsanti thought of using the Voltaic pile to ignite the combustible mixture with a spark. In 1852, this same
Barsanti together with the engineer, Felix Mateucci, studied the problem of the proper proportions of the combustible and also discovered the ground-return. Henceforth anything was possible. The
Belgian, Lenoir, developed the motor in 1859, and patented it in 1860.
The patent was for a "motor worked by hot, expanding air created by the combustion of gas ignited by electricity and capable of replacing steam as a driving force. " The combination was extremely simple. "I use a part of the main pieces of a horizontal steam engine, that is, a fly wheel, a crank-shaft, a connecting rod, a slide bar, a cylinder and two cams to activate the slide valves." The spark plug was made of two platinum wires insulated with porcelain and connected to a Ruhmkorff coil used to change the tension of the current supplied by the batteries.
One slide valve was used to let the mixture of air and gas into the cylinder, and the other to exhaust fumes after combustion. The mixture was 95% air and 5% gas. The cylinder was cooled by water circulating between it and a cast iron jacket.
It is curious to note the remarks made by Lenoir's contemporaries.
Lenoir had at first contemplated inventing an electric motor. "But," wrote Figuier in 1870, "it did not take long to recognize that this was hardly possible because of the insignificant mechanical power pro duced by electromagnatism." Lenoir's gas engine was by no means perfect. The slide valves, particularly when they overheated, were frequently problematic. Lastly, as Figuier remarks, "this engine was not economic." Lenoir had originally estimated that one cubic metre of gas was needed to produce one horsepower. At a cost of thirty centimes, this meant a cost of three francs per ten hour day, while even a mediocre steam engine required five or six kilogrammes of coal per hour and per horsepower. Lenoir's design certainly saved in bulk, avoiding the cumbersome boiler, fuel and smoke. In fact it consumed eight times more gas than expected. The estimated heat power of that gas being put at 5000 calories, the mechanical equivalent of heat being put to 635 calories per horsepower per hour, its efficiency was barely

===== PAGE 714 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
697 3.52% to 4.65% , this for the two types which were tried. This engine could only be used for very small powers, of one-half to two horsepower.
But Lenoir's internal combustion engine was open to improvement.
Firstly, it required a good theory to substantiate it, and this was provided by the Frenchman, Beau de Rochas, who in his patent of
January 16, 1862, defined the theory of the four-stroke engine, made possible by the work on thermodynamics developed since Carnot
(1842), by Joule, the principle of equivalence formulated by Mayer (1842), and the studies of William Thomson, and Clausius. The notion of preliminary compression of the combustible mixture existed in embryo in the patent of another Frenchman, Degrand, (June, 1858), and secondly in the Italian, De Cristoforis' suggestion of compressing the air before it was mixed with the gas (1859). But it was Beau de
Rochas who proved, shortly after Lenoir had built his engine, how the use of a four-stoke piston could achieve better efficiency: suction, compression, explosion and expansion, with the evacuation of the burnt gases.
Research was also being carried out in other areas. In 1862, the German, Nicolas-August Otto, built a four-cylinder motor with floating pistons behind the driving pistons, hoping that the floating pistons would complete the evacuation of the burnt gases. He had noticed that if the cylinder was poorly filled, the ensuing explosion was not sufficient enough to drive the piston through a full stroke. Indeed, it was pulled back in the opposite direction by the depression left when the burnt gas was exhausted. In other words, the mechanical effect was not completely used, and the atmospheric pressure was coming into play even before the piston reached the end of its stroke. So Otto modified the gas-air mixture, filled the cylinder completely with the mixture during almost all of the stroke, and let the crank-shaft turn in the opposite direction in order to compress the mixture, setting the ignition to take place at the point of maximum compression. To his great surprise, the flywheel completed several rapid revolutions at the same time as developing much more power. On his own he was discovering the virtues of prior compression. This is why he conceived a four-cylinder motor with cylinders opposite to each other, two by two, and then decided upon four cylinders, in order to have a uniform torque. The motor operated in January, 1862, but sudden shocks caused during ignition led to its failure. Nevertheless, Otto's intuition

===== PAGE 715 =====

698
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
was correct, but his theoretical knowledge was poor. The French engineer, Pierre Hugon, whose research began in 1853 and was completed in 1858, used the two-stroke cylinder, without compres sion, and developed motors using direct and indirect action. His patent of 1860 demonstrates in fact a motor driven by atmospheric pressure, the piston operating in a water flooded area which enabled regular strokes. In March, 1865, Hugon adopted the two-stroke motor, with a vertical cylinder for water injection and supply bellows.
Between 1864 and 1867, Otto secured the help of the engineer, Eugen Langen, and developed a motor with a vertical cylinder where the expansion could continue until the pressure it was in was less than the atmospheric pressure. This long expansion improved the efficiency of the the two-stroke engine - a remarkable achievement at this epoch.
Its fuel consumption was reduced to two thirds of that of Lenoir's engine.
Thus by 1870, three internal combustion motors were in existence:
the Lenoir, the Hugon and the Otto-Langen, as well as Beau de Rochas' clear definition of the four-stroke cycle. Fuel efficiency had been improved but was still poor. These small engines were used for low power requirements despite the fact that they were extremely noisy when in operation. Otto's last engine had an efficiency of 13. 7% at 106 revolutions per minute, and 8.25% for 75 revolutions. Fuel consumption was still large so this form of energy was not cheap but these motors were nevertheless the most practical in certain condi tions. Virtually no progress was made between 1867 and 1876. But old ideas were to be resumed, for in 1859 De Cristoforis suggested the use of petroleum instead of illuminating gas. In 1872-1873, a Munich watchmaker, Christian Reithmann, built the first real four-stroke motor, and in America between 1872-1876, Brayton devised a carbu rator using petroleum: a sponge soaked with petroleum through which air passed driving fine droplets onto a metal screen behind which combustion was continuous without explosion. This was therefore the first internal combustion engine using petroleum.
In 1876, Otto, aided by Daimler and Maybach, adopted the four stroke engine, and the definitive engine, at least in concept and main design, appeared in 1877. It was still not perfect and minor improvements followed. Daimler perfected the carburettor, Forest devised magneto ignition in 1885, and in 1890, water cooling was adopted. Around 1892, the four-stroke motor was complete, having

===== PAGE 716 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
699 also benefited from progress made in metallurgy and the change in fuel which gave it greater mobility. Henceforth, its purpose was to change completely. Originally conceived as the engine to power small industry in the home, it was now to become the essential instrument in a new means of transport which was to enjoy great success.
Rudolph Diesel's main idea was to build a motor that would come as close as possible to the Carnot cycle. In 1883, he began working on steam engines using ammonia in place of steam. The theory was dreadful, but he gained technical experience in attempting to put this idea into practice. In 1890, he departed in another much more fruitful direction:
How the fundamental idea came to me of replacing ammonia with a true gas, i.e. highly compressed hot air, of slowly injecting fine particles of fuel into it and letting it expand during the combustion of the particles, so that the greatest possible amount of the heat produced was turned into mechanical work - that I do not know.
In any case he arrived at several conclusions: (A) superheated steam or gases are required to reduce heat losses through cylinder walls;
(B) high pressure is necessary to achieve the great temperature drop indispensable to the high efficiency envisioned in Carnot's theory;
( C) combustion must take place in the cylinder itself in order to avoid heat loss in transmission; (D) the temperature of combustion and ignition must be reached by compression alone.
From this followed a perfectly logical cycle, comprising: (A) a first phase of compression kept isothermic (20°C.) by injecting water; (B) a second phase of adiabatic compression raising the temperature above the flammable point: 250 atmospheres and 800°C. Then came the ignition of the gradually introduced fuel; ( C) a first phase of expansion with fuel being injected and isothermic combusion: to 90 atmospheres with the temperature at 800°C; (D) a second phase of adiabatic expansion. Then the return to the original 20° in less than one atmosphere of pressure.
Such a machine was extremely difficult to realize, as it entailed very high maximal pressures against a very low average pressure. Diesel therefore changed the cycle by eliminating the first phase of compres sion, which made it possible for ignition to take place at an appreciably lower pressure. He also had to retreat from the extremes of theoretical compression and expansion and settle for nonisothermal initial combustion and incomplete expansion. With the support of a large

===== PAGE 717 =====

700
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
mechanical firm and of Krupp, he was able to build his first motor where no cooling of the cylinder was provided with a view to using mechanic fuel injection. Attempts to do so took place in August 1893.
While the principles were sound, the engine worked poorly. Water cooling and pneumatic injection were therefore adopted, and the engine built in 1895 worked much better; its 20 horsepower successor of 1897 was fully developed, achieving an efficiency of about 26% But he had to abandon all hopes of achieving greater efficiency by imple menting the theoretical Carnot cycle.
The steam turbine was based on an idea which had already been tried, the hydraulic turbine, and combined it with another old idea, that of energy produced by steam in certain conditions. The devel opment of this new technique took place in several stages, as the theory was perfected (fig. 7). In 1884, Charles Parsons was the first to build a steam turbine which was multicellular and developed 10 horsepower at 18,000 revolutions per minute. Here again it had been necessary to wait for the development of adequate materials. In 1890, the Swede, Laval, independently of Parsons, built a steam turbine consisting of a single disk turning at 30,000 revolutions per minute. In order to develop the idea, he had to overcome many difficulties, such as the design and machining of precision gearing, and a novel rotor shaft which was thin enough to be flexible (fig. 8). All that, together with considerable stress and high speeds, required machine tools and materials that had recently appeared in other sectors of the modern technical system. The superiority of the turbine over other machines was its rotary movement which enabled the construction of machines with almost an unlimited range of horsepower. Power for power, it compared with the classic alternating steam engine, and moreover, at any horsepower, it was lighter and required much less space. And this made it particularly advantageous as a marine engine. Maintenance costs were low; its lubrication required only a third the grease con sumed by the steam engine. The only disadvantage at the beginning was a considerably higher consumption of steam.
Further development was therefore aimed at affirming the advan tages of the steam turbine and eliminating any drawbacks. In 1896,
Curtis increased efficiency by building a vertical axis turbine that used not only the pressure of steam, but also its expansion. Rateau had begun to work on the problem of ventilation in the mines, and therefore turned his attention to rotative mechanics. In 1892, he

===== PAGE 718 =====

TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
