published the first work on turbo-machines, particularly in connection with mine ventilators. Then in 1900, his treatise on turbo-machines proper was published. In the same year he invented his first multicellu lar turbine and during the course of the First World War he built his remarkable turbo-supercharger driven by exhaust gases, thus liberat ing the turbine from steam.
The use of superheating and the change of fuel types, from coal to oil, was to lead to greater development of the turbine. Improvements were rapidly made and fuel consumption was reduced as far as possible.
The electric motor constituted a secondary energy converter. It is dependent upon a primary converter to furnish its energy in the form of mechanical energy. But on the other hand, we will see that this form of electricity may be transformed back into mechanical energy without an intermediary. The creation of the electric motor presupposes the existence of a number of preliminary discoveries, of which the elec tromagnetism of Galvani and the electrodynamics of Oersted,
Ampere and Faraday were the most important. Faraday was the first, in 1828, to explain the principle of electromagnetic induction. In 1832,
Pixii created the first working model of the magneto-electric genera-

===== PAGE 720 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
703 tor, inducing the current in the two coils of a stationary electromagnet by rotating the field magnet beneath it and then rectifying the current with a simple commutator. In 1834, Moritz Hermann von Jacobi, inventor of galvanoplating, published a paper on the application of electromagnetism for powering machines. Then in 1843, Stohrer, and
Woolrich in 1844, designed a large steam-driven electromagnetic machine, finally built in 1867. In 1849, Clark set up one of the first electromagnetic machines, quickly followed by Little, in 1852, and the
Dane, Soren Hjorth, in 1854. Meanwhile, in 1851, Wilhelm Josef Sinsteden built the first monophase alternator with an exciter mag neto. We may therefore observe that progress was slow in domains where both scientific discovery and technical knowledge played their parts.
Antoine Pacinotti was looking for a practical way of measuring a current. He finally had the idea of creating an electrodynamic device connecting a stationary circuit and a mobile circuit, both traversed by the current to be measured. The device was practically impossible to build, but the inventor understood that some slight changes would turn it into a direct current electromagnetic machine. He wound wire in a spiral on a revolving ring, and on each side of this ring armature he placed two sliding contacts; then he placed two magnetized steel bars with their poles near the ring. On January 10, 1859, he attached a galvanometer to the sliding contacts and turned the ring between the opposing poles of the magnets. The needle of the galvanometer jumped to the end of the scale. This was the first time that a continuous current had been induced by a non-unipolar machine. An improved model was built in 1860: the permanent magnets were replaced by a single electromagnet provided with two arched polar pieces, each one of which curved around, on opposite sides, a little more than one third of the circumference of the ring. The continuous spiral had been replaced by sixteen coils mounted in series and equidistant from each other, with projecting pieces of iron between them to reduce the distance between the ring and the polar pieces. Electrical contact with the coils was effected by a cylindrical commutator, centred upon the axle of the ring and carrying sixteen metal segments, each communi cating with the sixteen pieces of copper wire which linked up the sixteen coils in series. Lastly, the sliding contacts were replaced by metal rollers. But Pacinotti never succeeded in building the full size machine.

===== PAGE 721 =====

704
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Work by Werner Siemens contributed to the decisive stage.
Working at the same time as Pacinotti, Siemens had been trying to finalize the development of the electromagnetic machine since 1857. In
1865, he devised the dynamo using the principle of the electromagnet with an auxilary generator to excite the magnet. Together with Charles
Wheatston, Siemens succeeded in producing some currents of strong intensity.
But it was the Belgian, Gramme, who finally succeeded in building the first practical dynamo by putting together elements that had already been used several times by his predecessors. By doing so, he ousted the battery, the storage battery developed by Plante in 1859, and the alternator (fig. 9). After his first patent of February, 1867, for several improvements to alternating current machines, he took out the definitive patent in November, 1869, "for different improvements to magneto-electric machines,'' and this was completed by two certi ficates, one of which, the certificate of April, 1870, contained the theory of the machine. The first major generator was built in 1875, with a small axial dynamo exciting the magnets. At the International
Exposition at Vienna in 1873, the Frenchman, Hippolyte Fontaine, Figure 9. - Principle of the Dynamo. (From Lehr)

===== PAGE 722 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
705 inadvertently connected up a dynamo with another machine running 250 metres away. If this was indeed the way in which the reversibility of
Gramme's machine was discovered, he would also have found that electricity could be transmitted over some distance. Marcel Deprez's successful demonstrations of transmitting electricity between Creil and Paris in 1883, and Gaulard's invention of the transformer in 1884 gave electricity its real dimension.
But the electrification of the modern system - and we will see this in a number of domains - remained dependent upon energy converters.
The hydraulic turbines powered by high waterfalls, the steam turbine - and Parsons' first turbine was precisely intended to turn an electric engine - and Diesel's engine were all developed just in time to be used to produce electricity. In fact, Parsons presented his first steam engine in 1884, the same year that Gaulard built the first hydroelectric generating plant driven by the waterfall at Bellegarde in the French
Alps.
The problem of energy was therefore completely transformed. The old prime energy converters which were limited, inflexible, and sta tionary, were replaced by superior converters with almost limitless ranges of power, some of them mobile as well, enabling the transport of energy over long distances, thus fulfilling one of the most fervent dreams of mankind.
The second great advance of this industrial revolution seems to have been made in the field of materials, for it was materials that largely conditioned many other technical innovations of the period, and conversely new materials naturally depended upon advances made in the energy and chemical sectors. This involves a whole history of events, and the reader will not be surprised to read that once again, despite the existence of a particularly rich area of documentation, much remains to be done. There are, however, some exemplary cases, such as steel and man-made textiles, but there are many others which are equally not well documented and we are therefore obliged to make rather vague statements.
We have already said that iron imposed strict limits in certain technical fields. Although in other cases, such as the framing of bridges and buildings, which were relatively few in number, iron would increase in use for some time to come. Large scale production of rolled iron, particularly of "T" and "U" beams, supplied directly from the rolling mills and no longer laboriously forged, had begun about

===== PAGE 723 =====

706
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
1845-1848. Thus, the iron frame, which had until then been almost an object of curiosity, became a standard building technique. The first train sheds of the preceding period, the development of which goes hand in hand with the railway, were henceforth used in more diverse buildings: from the roof of the Bibliotheque nationale constructed by
Labrouste to Baltard's famous umbrellas of iron and glass over Les Halles, to name but two in Paris. And if one considers the late second half of the century, one rediscovers the dominance of iron, at the Eiffel
Tower, completed in 1889.
The pressure of certain requirements was, however, to direct the iron industry along other roads and towards a new material, even in circumstances where iron could have sufficed. One should point out from the first the fact that the appearance of a metal which was already known, but was produced in cheap large quantities, naturally entailed a number of modifications in the equipment used in this industry, both
"upstream" and "downstream" of the actual production of steel itself.
Steel was indeed the solution to many essential requirements and was therefore to be produced in ever greater quantities. "Upstream" lay the problem of pig iron production, which as we shall see presented special difficulties. The blast furnace was the same as before, but output had to be increased: its mechanisation, especially of the charging operation, was to reach its peak by using electrification. The operation was improved, with the aid of technological and scientific research, so as to obtain the products necessary for the fabrication of steel. Around 1875, at the Terrenoire works, Pourcel adapted the blast furnace to produce the ferromanganese indispensable for this process:
he eliminated the corrosive action of the manganese on the crucible's . siliceous linings, by lining it with carbon bricks, and also found that the furnace had to be heated to a very high temperature and with a slag which showed a high index of basicity.
The most spectacular innovations led to the mass production of steel. During the time of the Crimean War, Henry Bessemer, a self-taught and prolific inventor, became interested in the problem of artillery. At first he intended to create a aerodynamically shaped shell, which he patented in 1854. Rebuffed by the British War Office,
Bessemer persuaded French authorities to carry out trials at Vincen nes where it was quickly apparent that only steel cannons were strong enough to fire this new shell. The problem was now how to produce this steel in large quantities and at a cheap rate. Bessemer quickly set

===== PAGE 724 =====

TIIE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
707 to work and began by trying to produce more steel with the processes already in use. His first patent of January 1, 1855, dealt with nothing more than the very old process of cofusing pig iron and wrought iron, to produce a median, steel. The second patent of June, 1855, also kept to well beaten paths. A third series of experiments took him closer to his goal. Bessemer injected air and water into molten raw iron. This was in fact the old process of refining pig iron already resumed by
James Nasmyth in a patent of May, 1854. The effect of injecting air or steam was to agitate the molten raw iron and cause chemical reactions with the carbon and sulphur in the iron owing to the introduction of oxygen and hydrogen. But the injections also caused cooling and excessive oxydation, thus involving a fire risk. The water steam was first used to remove the sulphur, following Nasmyth's quite erroneous notion of the role of hydrogen. The converter first appeared in the patent of December 5, 1855 (fig. 10). Although the problem of chemical reactions was important and could only be resolved by successive empirical attempts, another essential problem existed, that of the molten iron's container, which affected everything else. Follow ing the 18th century example of Huntsman's crucible, in which mallea ble iron was obtained by the injection of air, Bessemer devised a crucible of refractory material housed in a wrought iron casing. On the advice of his friends, Bessemer demonstrated his converter before the
British Association for the Advancement of Science at Cheltenham on August 11, 1856. The response was enthusiastic and the London Times covered the whole exhibition on August 14. A picture of the converter was reproduced in the Illustrated London News three days later.
Immediately, the large ironmaking firm of Dowlais, envisaged an annual production of 70,000 tonnes of steel. Bessemer quickly sold licences, taking care to deal only with large firms which were capable of perfecting an invention which was still far from its definitive devel opment. Then a series of tremendous setbacks ensued.
The reasons for these setbacks should be sought. Indeed they reflect the limitations of the inventor. Bessemer's knowledge only came from reading popular technical encyclopedias. Every time he had a problem he immediately consulted all the patents which had anything to do with his invention. Although he managed to build his converter, he could not do much beyond this. The converter presented many problems, the most fundamental of which was the nature of the lining used in the retort. Bessemer therefore sought the advice of two prominent met-

===== PAGE 725 =====

708
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
JT
a = Figure 10. - The Bessemer converter from the 1855 patent.
allurgists, Riley and Percy who advised that the raw iron should be completely free of phosphorous. But neither the use of pure hydrogen or boric acid, nor the use of metallic oxides and many other remedies were successful. The Swedish metallurgist, JOran Frederik Goransson, who had obtained a licence as early as 1857, increased the volume of air and injected it at the bottom of the converter rather than from the side.
A successful trial took place in July, 1858. It was Robert Forester Mushet, a professional metallurgist of renown, who perceived that an excess of oxygen was causing the molten iron to be too completely decarburized. He therefore added a mixture of wrought iron, manag nese, and carbon, and obtained a good quality steel in Septem ber ,1856, and then again in 1857, by stopping the decarburation earlier. The addition of manganese also facilitated the operation.
Muchet took out patents in 1856 and 1857, but neglected to renew them in 1859, and Bessemer took out similar patents in 1861. But by that time no backer was willing to bear the expense for the devel opment of the definitive version, so in 1859 Bessemer was forced to set up his own pilot steel plant at Sheffield. At the same time, Jackson,

===== PAGE 726 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
709 whose father had introduced crucible steel in France, bought a licence and set up another pilot plant at Saint-Seurin, near Bordeaux. There, the converter was given its definitive form and mode of operation, for which the additional patents were taken out. Note that Bessemer meanwhile had an American emulator, William Kelly, who may have discovered the same process between 1847 and 1851 but failed to bring it to the industrial stage.
The Bessemer process suffered from two major disadvantages. With a siliceous, that is, acid lining, neither phosphorous nor sulphur could be eliminated, which considerably limited the range of useable ores. A large part of iron ore deposits contained various quantities of phosphd rus. Moreover, as it received no thermal heating whatsoever from the outside, the converter could neither remelt or reuse the scrap iron.
May we digress for a moment to discuss another subject. Fuel, firebox, and heat were the essential elements in metallurgical tech niques. All the techniques used could only reach a certain tem perature, which presented serious limitations upon the methods employed so that steel could not be made out of scrap iron or steel as they both required a high temperature for fusion. Wilhelm Siemens took up an idea which had already been patented in 1816 by the Scott,
Robert Stirling. Friederich Siemens, brother of Wilhelm, took out a patent on December 2, 1856 on the regeneration of heat. They suggested capturing the hot gases as they travelled up the furnace chimney and using them to preheat the air going into the combustion chamber. Thus part of the lost heat would be used to raise the combustion temperature sufficiently without much increase in oper ating costs. The "regeneration" chambers were lined with bricks and the captured hot gases were directed through chambers, and directed back and forth among them by a system of vanes. The inversion of the vanes enabled fresh air to be drawn into this exchanger and heated before being blown into the combustion chamber. Wilhelm Siemens saw the possibility of using these heat "regenerators" to attain tem peratures high enough to melt metals which were difficult to fuse, and took out a patent for this in May, 1857. But trials conducted at
Sheffield with coke gases failed owing to the bad quality of the refractory material. So the apparatus was only used in glass and porcelain industries. In January, 1861 , the Siemens brothers patented a gas-producer with inclined walls which could produce gas from bituminous coal. An incomplete combustion with little air yielded an

===== PAGE 727 =====

710
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
oxide of carbon which was combustible at 500°C and was a very versatile fuel; it could even be preheated to 800° or 1000°C. Siemens' regenerator apparatus was quickly put to use to reheat the air blown into the furnaces by using the throat gases. Cowper built the first apparatus at the factories at Clarence in 1860. But due to the inability to decoke the stacks, the process was abandoned. It was only in 1869 that Whitwell and Cowper succeeded in developing better purification and designed ways of keeping the apparatus from clogging so that the regenerators could be used again.
In 1861, two French metallurgists, the Martins father·and son, gave up their own research on gas-generator and the regeneration of heat.
They then took out a licence from the Siemens and the Siemens company sent them one of their own engineers, Nehse, to convert the form and material of their reverberatory furnaces, using siliceous material. On April 23, 1863, the younger Martin succeeded in remel ting a piece of crucible steel on the hearth, without using a crucible.
Meanwhile, in February, 1863, following the work of Louis Le Chate lier, the Fourchambault Company took out an analogous patent on the methods of making steel from mixtures that later became today's two major processes, called the scrap iron and ore processes. Nehse once again travelled from Germany to develop the processes, but he failed because of the bad quality of the refractory material. It was Martin who persevered and succeeded on April 8, 1864, in melting a mixture of pig iron and wrought iron, conforming entirely to the method
Reaumur had advocated a century before. His experiment resulted in several patents, from August, 1864, to March, 1865. Bessemer had never experienced any temperature problems as the combustion of carbon in contact with air was sufficient to raise the temperature of the smelting bath. In contrast to this, Martin required a high temperature to use the principle of cofusion. Thus Reaumur's idea and the Siemens' apparatus formed the basis for producing Martin's steel.
Bessemer steel and Martin steel both had their advantages. The first method was quick and relatively cheap. The second was slower but sure, allowing time to make adjustments and control the quality of the steel during the operation. Both processes were limited, however. The linings used in both were siliceous and hence acidic, which meant that only very pure ores could be used, with no trace of phosphorus, the elimination of which presupposes the use of a basic slag rich in lime.
This had been perfectly demonstrated by Gruner in 1869. Research in

===== PAGE 728 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
711 this area was undertaken immediately. From this date, Muller advo cated the use of magnesian refractory material. In 1872, Snelus came to the same conclusion. But trials carried out at the Krupp works in
1877 were a failure. Two cousins, Sidney Gilchrist Thomas and Percy Gilchrist, presented their discovery on May 28, 1878, at the London
Iron and Steel Institute amid shouts of laughter. They used dolomite, a mixture of lime and magnesium. The first trial took place in the
Bolckow and Vaughan factories and the first basic casting took place on April 4, 1879, in a Bessemer converter with the new lining. In
France, Pourcel at Terrenoire and Walrand at Le Creusot did the same with the Martin process. The Thomas process was first used in the De
Wendel plants at Hayange, a region rich in phosphorous ores, and his method was definitively perfected in 1880-1881. From 1874, the steel rail took the lead from the iron rail which had virtually disappeared by
1885. Sheet steel supplanted sheet iron from 1891 . But only in 1900 did commercial steels surpass the tonnage of commercial irons which in
1913 still represented a third of the total production.
But the process went further. Once steel had been developed, it had to be endowed with various properties for particular functions. These properties were sought for in one well determined area, that of alloys, which had been made possible by the discovery of numerous metals and the development of the industrial processes to produce them. The fabrication of armour-plating may have been the basis for these efforts. Boussingault, working for Holtzer near Saint-Etienne, began chemical research and gave an engineer, Aime Brustlein, the task of working out the industrial applications using the crucible furnaces in the plant. Brustlein began by making a ferro-chrome by using a charge of chromite and charcoal with tar as an agglomerant. The steel obtained titrated at 10 to 84% chrome and 2.5 to 11 % carbon. By incorporating them into the charges of the crucibles, the first chrome steel was produced in 1878. These steels proved to be self-hardening.
The age of special steels had thus begun.
From 1883, Marbeau began to develop nickel steels, and research continued in this direction. Le Creusot took out a patent on the production of ferro-chromes in October, 1888. At the same time, the
Englishman, Harvey, was developing nickel steel with only 2 to 3% nickel content, destined for armour-plate production. By 1888, Robert
Hadfield of Sheffield was working on the systematization of special steels. He finalized the process of making maganese steel, and intro-

===== PAGE 729 =====

712
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
duced silicon steel. Thus science could resume the rather disorganised relay in industrial research. The laboratory at Imphy, in the Nievre region, made indilatable steels in 1897, then alloys with particular physical properties (elinvar), a steel with 34 to 36% nickel and 12% chrome. From 1891, alloys of steel-nickel-chrome were available.
Towards 1900, it was the turn of rapid cutting steels, developed by White and Frederick Winslow Taylor, with 18 to 20% tungsten and 5 to
7% chrome. Henceforth, steelmaking was no longer subject to trial and error; the industry now possessed the scientific knowledge neces sary to carry out systematic research.
The introduction of steel as the basic material of the iron and steel industry, and alloy steels with special properties were henceforth materials vital to the modern technical system. Machines conceived of but previously impossible to build could now be produced: electrifi cation and the diverse new energy converters, the new means of transportation, and machine tools immediately took advantage of the new discoveries which also revolutionized other techniques, particu larly military techniques.
The domain of chemistry and chemicals, which also played an important part in the materials necessary to other industries, was to be transformed no less radically. Until 1850, the processes discovered shortly at the end of the 18th century and in the early years of the 19th century to make hydrochloric acid and sulphuric acid, the two essential products of the industry, remained almost the same. Soda, chlorine, and sulphuric acid were the fundamental trilogy. Despite some improvements in the equipment and some variations in chemical processes, the chemical industry had not changed much. Solvay's discovery in 1866 of the fabrication of ammonia-soda was of more economic than technical interest because it produced only soda and not hydrochloric acid, which was an important product. As for sulphu ric acid, the combining of the Guy-Lassac tower (used industrially at
Chauny in 1842) and Glover's tower of 1859 allowed certain by products to be reused, especially nitrous products.
The chemical "revolution" in fact resulted from several important inventions which we will briefly list:
I - The chemistry of dyes was completely transformed by William Henry Perkin's 1856 discovery of aniline which was used to produce mauve dye. Gradually all the other dye colours were to be produced in the same way: red in 1859 and black in 1863.

===== PAGE 730 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
713 II - This was to lead to a chemistry of synthetics that would first replace vegetable dyes and other natural products and then very quickly produce a range of products unavailable in nature except in limited amounts. Synthetic quinine was followed in 1879 by Baeyer's synthetic indigo, and in 1875, vanilline opened the way to synthetic perfumes.
III - It was only a step away from the development of the first artificial materials, that is to say, materials unknown in nature before, which could become the basis, and the fundamental basis at that, for other techniques, or better substitutes for natural products owing to economic rather than purely technical advantages (greater physical or chemical qualities and greater diversity). In 1868, Hyatt developed the process of producing celluloid, the first artificial material. In 1897, galalithe replaced animal horn, and bakelite was developed in 1909 to replace natural resins. We must also cite the discovery of artificial silk in 1884, by the Count of Chardonnet, and dynamite in 1867, by the
Swede, Nobel. These were only a few of the new products.
We should point out that there is no good history of the chemical industry. Such a history would have to begin by listing the products obtained and the processes by which they were produced. This would reveal their technological lines of descent and clarify the inevitable interdependencies which were intensified by the reversibility of most of these production methods. Such a history would analyse the recip rocal effects between these new chemical and other technical sectors, and the industries that employed them. This would establish the necessary links between the technical and economic needs of the user industries and the chemical industry's efforts to improve its old processes, create substitutes for existing materials, and invent new ones. We should emphasize that we are a long way off from such a study. And what of the race for greater effectiveness, such as the advance from dynamite (1867) to melinite (1884) and then to tolite
(T.N.T. , 1910), or, in a broader context, the race between comple mentary techniques, such as the endless battle between armour plating and firepower; similarly, the birth of cement, then reinforced concrete used in contruction work, superphosphates for agriculture, or all the successes of photography after Eastman (1886).
In the domain of the production of materials, we will witness another "marriage,'' that of electricity with a certain number of fabrications, owing to the former's thermal and chemical properties.

===== PAGE 731 =====

714
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The first electric furnace was patented by William Siemens in 1879, and it was quickly discovered that electricity had certain properties which could decompose particular bodies and deposit the different elements on one terminal or the other. In 1800, Carlisle and Nicholson had succeeded in decomposing water by electrolysis, but only as a labora tory experiment. Industrial application naturally had to await the advent of cheap electricity in large quantities and the availability of the materials necessary for such fabrications. Meanwhile, research conti nued throughout the first two thirds of the 19th century, especially on anodes and cathodes.
To return to a history, the beginnings of which we have outlined, it was between 1880 and 1885 that electrolysis was applied industrially to produce a soda derivative. The first work to manufacture aluminum by electrolysis, patented by both Hall and Heroult, dates from 1886. A few years later in 1892, Moissan used a variant of Heroult's apparatus to reduce metal oxides with carbon, and to produce carbides or ferro-alloys. But Moissan only used electricity as a source of heat. At the same time, Chaplet, in conjunction with the company La Neo
Metallurgie, endeavoured to industrialize Moissan's furnace and his scientific work. A patent resulted from this work in 1895, concerning a process to alloy refractory metals with more easily melted metals. One of the latter was melted first, whereupon the refractory metal was introduced in the oxide state, mixed with the amount of coal necessary to reduce it. Paul Heroult resumed his research and adapted his furnace for making iron alloys and then steel. In 1898, he patented the process for making chrome irons with a high chrome content, by treating any chromite in the electric furnace, keeping the current on
"after the reduction and melting are complete, until the total or partial volatilization of the iron. As the boiling point of chrome is higher, the product gains in chrome while losing in iron;" in 1900, the patent for the production of steel was issued.
All electric arc furnaces comprise of a tank with a refractory lining;
the electric current is brought in by a carbon electrode through the top.
In the systems designed by Hero ult and Keller, the current is provided by other vertical carbon electrodes, suspended in such a way that the electrode tips nearly touched the bath of molten metal. Flashing between one electrode to the other, the electric arc passed through the bath of molten metal. In Chaplet's design the current returned through the bath, then through an "ad hoc" canal to a steel electrode on the

===== PAGE 732 =====

THE MODERN 1ECHNICAL SYS1EM
715 outside of the tank. In this case the arc flashed between the positive electrode and the bath. Paul Girod's mechanism, published in 1898, was similar to the preceding one, in that the floor of the tank was a conductor in the circuit of the returning current. But it was Heroult's design that stood the test of time.
Ernesto Stassano, an artillery engineer in charge of the steel foun deries at the arms factory at Terni, north of Rome, tackled a different problem. He tried to use electricity to make steel directly from the ore.
In his patent of March, 1898, he adopted Moissan's arc-furnace, using the thermal energy of the arc to effect the physico-chemical transfor mations involved in ore-based production. The patent specified the preparation of the charge and the form and details of building the furnace. It was to some extent, the combination of an electric furnace and a blast-furnace, controlled to obtain a quality product. He succeeded in obtaining a product with only a few traces of sulphur. But for economic and practical reasons, like those designed by the Swede,
Kjellin, in 1900, these furnaces were quickly forgotten.
Electricity was therefore an important contribution in the devel opment of certain materials, apart from its role in the transmission of mechanical energy. Until the beginning of the 20th century, it was above all thanks to electrolysis, that electricity could contribute to this major work, the creation of new and economically viable materials.
In any case, these new techniques depended upon improved exploi tation of natural resources, that is, precisely the field where technical progress had always been more or less slow. Although progress may have been gradual and hardly noticeable, technical changes were nevertheless extremely striking. And if our manuals, or "histories" barely mention them, it is probably due to the fact that this progress affected the technical surroundings of these sectors more than the central techniques of natural resource exploitation. These surrounding techniques were in turn heavily dependent upon other tributary techniques.
Agriculture is certainly one of the best examples of this phenone mon, and it is particularly regrettable that we have no good history of agriculture in the 19th and early 20th centuries, as it would surely demonstrate progress taking place during this period, and the predo minant influence of other techniques in almost every field. This would show, if necessary, how the notion of a technical revolution is closely linked to the research undertaken: everybody is aware of the "agrarian

===== PAGE 733 =====

716
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
revolution" of the 18th century, as recent works have defined it, but there is a complete lack of information for later periods. In agriculture itself, there are many long lasting problems, with slow and gradual solutions: such is the case of the species cultivated, linked now to botany and to agricultural experiment stations, be they official or not.
Today, the increasing number of hybrids allows much more land to be cultivated, with much higher yields. But during the period of the modem technical system, the real advances were undoubtedly of another sort, three of which should be emphasized.
The first was soil regeneration owing to the use of fertilizer. This made it possible not only to maintain yields but also to cultivate newly developed high yield plants. Of course the use of fertilizers had been known for a long time, as we have already noted, but their effects were still limited and only natural fertilizers had been used. After 1850, guano from South America was used on a growing scale. Phosphates were also used on an increasing scale, coming from Wissant after 1857 and Tunisia after 1873. But chemistry soon came to the aid of agriculture by furnishing it with man-made fertilizers: hence the development of superphosphates after 1870.
The second advance was also linked to scientific progress. It invol ved the protection of plants. Traditional methods were gradually superseded by scientifically developed techniques. Small wonder then that Pasteur was interested in this. First, it was necessary to diagnose the ills to be treated and then seek the appropriate treatment. After
1850, oidium was treated with sulphur, and after 1869, milldew was combatted with sulphate. The battle against parasites was far more difficult. We know that when phylloxera made its appearance, threat ening to destroy the vineyards of Europe, all sorts of extreme cures were tried, even submersion. But in the end the vineyards were to be saved only by grafting on American vine stocks.
One of the most resounding manifestations of agricultural progress was the development of machinery. Some farm machinery had certainly appeared before 1850, but its real development, dependent upon mechanical improvements and special energy converters, actually came about in the last half of the 19th century. Considerable progress was made in the machines themselves, developed for more and more comprehensive functions. The development was as follows:
the reaper of 1835 became the reaper-baler of 1852, which became the harvester-thresher of 1855. In 1858, 73,000 reapers did 70% of the

===== PAGE 734 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
717 harvesting west of the Alleghanies in the United States. Seeders became effective, and fertilizer spreaders were introduced. Even before 1870, the main agricultural machinery was in use, and it was to be developed and improved as it came to be powered not by animals but by engines. Steam engines, first used in 1849-1850, were wide spread by 1870; petrol-driven tractors first appeared in the United
States in the 1890's. It was this group of innovations that overcame the scarcity and high cost of labour which was to enable such vast expanses of land to be cultivated in America, where production could still be increased by bringing new land under cultivation. Although the fundamental structures of agriculture could hardly be changed, at least its complementary structures benefited in this period from numerous useful inventions.
Similar changes may be observed in the mining industry. Here again neighbouring techniques contributed essential elements in technical mutation. Firstly, we should point out the use of new materials. Thus wooden structures were replaced with cast iron, wrought iron, and then steel structures. This evolution began shortly before 1850. In
1838, Triger suggested lining shafts with tubulated cast iron joined with lead instead of the old caulked wood lining. The development of iron framing allowed wood to be replaced in all its traditional mining uses. In 1899-1900, at the end of our period, Portier made the shaft lining water-tight with injections of cement. Progress was also made in the sinking of shafts in soft or watery earth. In 1840, the same Triger perfected a method of digging shafts with the aid of compressed air. In order to sink a vertical mineshaft so that the lining could be inserted at the same time, Triger instructed the workers to dig in a compartment at the bottom of the pit, where compressed air, maintained at a bearable pressure of three kilogrammes per square centimetre, over came the pressure of the water, forcing it out through a pipe. This compartment was separated from the open air by a double closing which acted as a lock-chamber.
Much more important were the advances made in the actual mine working itself and in the mechanisation of a whole series of accessory operations. And these advances were quite naturally the result of the progress of techniques in neighbouring techniques. The compressed air percussion drill was invented by Sommeiller in December 1858, for drilling the Mont-Cenis tunnel. It was quickly adopted by the mining industry, in replacement for the old pick and crow bar. The subsequent

===== PAGE 735 =====

718
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
increase in productivity was enormous, but at the same time it became necessary to mechanise the operations following this process. Electric motors were used for transport within the mines, and for manoeuvring the cages. The same was true of the operations of washing, grading and crushing. Furthermore, the problems of water drainage and venti lation also benefited from the progress made in motors. It was at the end of the 19th century and at the beginning of the 20th that the first
Rateau turbines were used for ventilation purposes.
We need only mention briefly the new source of energy, oil. Oil "sources" had existed for a long time, but the real beginning of this industry came with the drilling of the first oil wells by "Colonel"
Drake, in Titusville, U.S.A. , in 1859. There were two main branches of techniques in this industry. The first of these was concerned with extracting the oil, and progress in this field was extremely rapid between 1860 and 1900. Suitable materials were soon found for the drills and pipes. We shall consider the problem of transport further on.
The second branch of techniques was concerned with refining the petrol. Originally, the petrol was used for lighting, and the manufac turing of paraffin lamps meant that little or no use was made of the by-products. The development of the internal combustion engine brought about the use of petrol to its fullest potential. At this point the oil refineries had to perfect the processes used, and vastly increase their output. Furthermore, this had to be done fairly rapidly.
There were profound changes in transport techniques, and this was in fact vital because as production was developing, there was more and more traffic to be catered for. Happily, this is one area where there has actually been a lot of research, even if there are still some sectors to be investigated.
The railway was one of the areas of techniques which maintained from the preceding period a slow but steady progress. We have already examined some aspects of this progress, and established that this particular form of transport might have reached its limits if a new technical revolution had not introduced some critical elements in its development. The use of new materials, particularly steel for the rails, and for the wheel bands which were made without welding, and also for the boilers used in locomotives, was a great economy for the industry, and this also encouraged the development of the actual trains and the speed at which they ran. Other forms of progress, such as technical transfers, supplemented this first group of innovations.

===== PAGE 736 =====

TIE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
719 Most of the research was concerned with traction. Giffard's injector, which provided an automatic supply of steam to boilers (1858), the locomotive with coupled axles (1864) and Anatole Mallet's compound machine (1878) all paved the way for the large modern machines whcih were approaching their limits of development. The "Pacific" locomo tive built in 1907, used superheated steam, weighed 100 tons, and was capable of producing 3500 horsepower. But the speed and weight of trains meant that further safety measures were required. In 1869, an
American, George Westinghouse, developed the compressed air brake. The automatic engaging of shuntings and signals was devised by a french engineer, Viguier and industrialised in 1880 by Saxby and
Farmer from England. After 1885, electric signalling was introduced, and this meant that much higher speeds were now possible. Thus, despite appearances, the railways at the end of the 19th century were in fact considerably different to those of the mid-century. But as far as steam traction is concerned, there was no possible further devel opment.
The modern ship began to evolve during the mid-19th century. At this time, ships were made of iron and powered by steam. The large paddle wheels had been abandoned in favour of screw propellors. Not all ships were the same, however; there were three main types. There was the warship, the transatlantic liner, which was first built around this time, and finally, freight ships. Speed was the essence of the first two, and maximum carriage was the priority of the third. Of course, the speed and power of a machine are interlinked, and it is well known that at this time ships were usually powered by a steam-powered reciprocating engine, heavy and cumbersome, especially if one includes the freight. Since most maritime transport did not require great speed initially, it was natural that the older types of sailing ships continued to be used with fairly weak auxiliary engines. In contrast, part of the available carriage space had been sacrificed in liners and warships, to obtain higher speeds. At any rate, there were still several problems to be overcome. Unfortunately, the history of shipping has usually been concentrated on the more unusual vessels, so that the overall problem is difficult to assess.
Progress was naturally concentrated on the more exceptional types of ship. After a certain time, the differences between liners and immigrant ships became more marked. There were problems in two main areas, on the one hand with speed and tonnage, and on the other

===== PAGE 737 =====

720
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
with the power and bulk of the engine apparatus. Inevitably, efforts were concentrated on solving the latter, whilst at the same time trying to reduce as much as possible the weight of the hull. Only the improvements made to the classic steam engine and the emergence of new materials were capable of solving the problems created by the development of maritime trade - the ship's power could now be increased without any corresponding increase in the size or the weight of the machine. It was also possible to increase the ship's tonnage, with lighter, but more resistant hulls. The progress from 2500 ton ships in the mid-19th century to 20,000 ton vessels at the beginning of the 20th century, was slow but steady. The same was true of warships, and all the more so after the technique of armour-plating was developed after
1854. Sometimes, engineers were too quick to introduce new elements, and this usually resulted in failure. Take the case of Brunel's
"Great Eastern" for example, which was launched in 1857. It had a capacity of 19,000 tons and was 211 metres long. It could carry up to
4000 passengers, and 6000 tons of freight. One cannot say that he was ahead of his time; it is more likely that there was not a large enough market as yet for this type of ship. We should also point out that it was still powered by enormous paddle wheels measuring 17 metres in diameter, and each one weighing 185 tons. They were unable to provide it with a powerful enough motor,· and they had to compensate for this by using large sails. The limits of existing techniques had been reached.
The adoption of the steam turbine constituted a major step forward.
The "Lusitania" and the "Mauritania," built in 1907, were the first large liners to be equipped with these. This meant that suddenly these vessels were capable of 68,000 horsepower, and a displacement of almost 40,000 tons. Sails continued to be used for a long time on slow freight ships, however. In 1850, forty sails were used for every three steam engines, in 1870, sixteen sails were used for every three engines, and by 1885, this ratio was one to one. It is possible to consider the revolution in maritime transport as complete by this date.
The combustion engine gave rise to other means of transport.
People had dreamed of creating means of road traction, and despite all the difficulties involved, Cugnot had devised the first steam-powered motor vehicle (1769-1771). There were many attempts throughout the
19th century, which culminated in the various vehicles devised by the Bollees, built in Le Mans, and which were derived precisely from the

===== PAGE 738 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
721 research on the steam engine. The first of these was the Obeissante, built in 1872, which was a twelve-seater weighing 5 tons and capable of reaching 40 km/h. Then there was the Mance/le, (1878) designed to carry 16 passengers and capable of going at 42 kmh. This vehicle was already much more "modern" in appearance. The Rapide, built in
1881 , reached speeds of up to 60 kmh. Obviously, other avenues had to be explored.
Lenoir had already considered using his engine in road vehicles. But it was not until engines were improved, or petrol was first used, along with lighter materials, or until certain other tributary elements devel oped, that more practical, manageable vehicles could be produced.
The first three-wheeled vehicle was created by Benz in 1886, then Daimler attempted to devise a workable model with Maybach in 1889 and Panhard from France in 1891 (fig. 11). Peugeot also had a role to play in these developments (1887-1890) which ultimately succeeded.
Motor races, the first of which was from Paris to Rouen in 1894 and the motor shows which began in 1898, both contributed to the expansion of these techniques. As the reader is no doubt aware, it would be very useful to write a comprehensive history of the automobile, with a thorough technical analysis of all the constitutive elements of this new technical ensemble, from the motor and transmission, to the bodywork, and from the materials used to more detailed aspects like the discovery of direct drive by Renault (9th February 1899). The
Mercedes of 1901 had the framework of the chassis stamped out, 4 cylinders developing 35 hp, gear change and a cellular radiator. The automobile industry has a large archive collection, which makes it almost easier to study in fact than more recent industries. The abun dance of written sources helps to compensate for the lack of large motor museums, and we are thus able to obtain information about virtually all the models which have ever been made. There are other factors worthy of study, such as the economic and social context, the imperatives and needs, and all the by-products of the motor car, from the motorcycle to the lorry. Also significant are the technical requirements which have conditioned to a considerable degree the nature of certain projects.
The history of aviation is more well-known, probably because it is more spectacular. Ever since Montgolfier devised the first aerostat, people had been trying to conquer the skies. The balloon and airship were interesting side developments, but they remained outside of the

===== PAGE 739 =====

722
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ .__ _ _ _ _ _ _
I
Figure 11. - Global mechanical organisation of a Panhard and Levassor dog-cart (1891-1894). The engine is a V-type two-cylinder 4 h.p. engine (2 cylinders 80/120).
mainstream aviation industry. The first airships were launched in 1851 and 1852 by Arnauld and Giffard, then by Le Bris in 1857, and Penaud and Gauchot in 1876. It was Clement Ader who devised the first motor-powered aircraft, in 1890. The Eole as it was known, was a 20 hp machine, weighing 15 kg in horseweight, and it actually succeeded in taking off. In 1897, the A vion beat this by travelling over 300 metres.

===== PAGE 740 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
723 This apparatus was shaped like a bat, it was 16 metres wide, and had two propellers each driven by a small steam engine. Exactly the same processes were used in the automobile - the only difference was in the problems they had to solve. In fact, Ader's experiments made some elements much clearer: firstly, the engine should be as light as possible
(even though it was a steam engine, it weighed only 3 kg per hp.) Then there was the frame and the stability to consider, and finally, the science of piloting. It was also necessary to abandon the idea sustained by the myth of Icarus, and perpetuated by Leonardo Da Vinci, that anything heavier than air should be built like a bird. Mouillard in 1881 and Marey in 1889 were still convinced of the truth of this theory. The experiments conducted by Lilienthal which ended so tragically in 1896, led to its refutation and this greatly benefited the work of the American Chanute.
The two brothers Wilfred and Orville Wright adopted a scientific approach to all aspects of the problem, and this was to prove succes sful. They began their research in 1889, resuming the studies conduc ted by Chanute. They adopted the biplane and modified the rudder apparatus. Finally, they incorporated a key element, the internal combustion engine. In 1903, they succeeded in taking off, but they only publicised this fact in 1908, by which time, several others had succeeded in doing the same thing, people such as Ferber, Esnault
Pelterie, Voisin, and Breguet. Levavasseur devised the V-type engine, and the Gnome motor was also developed. This was a rotary motor which proved to be very successful.
The historians of aviation have contented themselves with simply naming names, dates and performances. This is insufficient - what we need is the same catalogue of successive, global developments which finally led to the first successful flying machine. We need to examine the kind of motor used, the quality of the materials used, the frame and the rudders, the control systems and the methods of flying. The characteristics of the early flying industry cannot be over-emphasised.
It was a sport, planes were made in a makeshift fashion and there was a sense that anyone could have a go. It was this, not economic consider ations, which was the principal dynamic of this industry.
The methods of transport and communication had thus been completely transformed, and they were to benefit from new resources.
Some were exclusively technical, while others were effective in more indirect, complementary ways. These should be considered more carefully.

===== PAGE 741 =====

724
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The new complementary techniques available stemmed largely from the new methods of traction, i.e. the engine. There are two main principles to this, and in chronological order these are electricity and the Diesel motor. Since the discovery of the reversibility of the
Gramme machine, it was possible to conceive of means of transport powered by electric motors. Deprez' work on the transmission of electric energy showed that it would be possible to convey electricity by aeriel lines. Siemens conceived of the first electric locomotive in
1879, but originally it was used only for urban transport, largely because they were unable to consider electricity being conveyed over long distances. Tramways, above ground, were used for the first time in Berlin in 1879, and the underground trains ran for the first time in
London in 1887. It was only some years later that the process was applied to railways, that is, over larger distances. In 1907, the Austrian line of St. Polten to Mariazell was one of the first to be electrified.
The second means of traction was the Diesel motor, whose range of power was such that it was suitable for a number of uses. In 1903, the
French constructor Sautter-Harle used diesel motors in barges, thus radically tranforming the conditions of navigation on rivers and canals.
The logical progression of this was the use of Diesel motors in maritime navigation. It was also the first step in another major branch of navigation, that of submarines. At the end of the 19th century, the
Gymnote and the Gustave Zede worked with the aid of electric motors linked to accumulator batteries. These were extremely heavy, and relatively weak for their size. The transformation to autonomous submarines, with the Laubeuf's Narval and the American Holland, was effected by using a Diesel motor, the function of which was essentially to recharge the accumulators between two dives.
The changes in methods of transport did not simply change the conditions of transport, but they also extended the network of trade.
This gave rise to new problems, concerning with transport itself, with stocking the merchandise and with safety measures. One of the first problems solved was the storing of grain, which of course was very important for reducing the problems caused by bad harvests. Origi nally, the grain was simply piled up which resulted in fermentation, making it unfit for consumption. Between 1850 and 1860, the first modern silos were devised, which kept the grain constantly moving so that it was unable to ferment. The use of electricity greatly helped this new process. The second main problem to be solved was the transport

===== PAGE 742 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
725 and conservation of perishable foodstuffs. The creation of large types of refrigerators using ammoniac or compression, was to provide an easy solution to this. In 1876, Charles Tellier devised the first ship with refrigeration. the Frigorifique. He worked not only on refrigerating transport, but also on all types of cold systems. Again, electricity was to play a major role in this field.
The second problem was transporting the new type of fuel, petrol.
Petrol was first transported in crates, or "barrels," but for land transport at least, these were soon replaced by tankers. The main concern regarding maritime transport was fire, and in particular, the obstruction of the Suez Canal if this problem should arise. The first crossing of the canal in 1892 by the Murex, which transported the petrol in bulk, inaugurated the era of oil tankers. On land, pipelines soon replaced other forms of transport.
Transport is necessarily linked to space, such that the use of the latter needs to be planned and developed if transport is to be possible. As soon as the railways were built, supply routes had to be established.
The supply of coal was not too much of a problem, but water supply was more difficult. They went so far as to consider digging channels bet ween the rails, and having a shovel which could be lowered to force water up to the tenders. Even more difficult was the problem of the ports of call for steam ships. The invention of tinned food had given sailing ships a considerable degree of independence, but steam ships needed to be supplied regularly with more fuel and fresh water. The political geography of the world is a strong reflection of this. The Diesel motor did away with the problem of water, but the problem of fuel remained. This was even more acute given the distribution of depots, which posed many political problems some of which are still important factors today. Cars, of course, also needed to be refuelled with petrol, and furthermore, suitable routes needed to be built to accomodate them. This is a prime example of the interaction of techniques, and is worth noting here - a technical problem can never be an isolated one.
The notion of a technical system does actually reflect a fundamental reality. Any line of research which is too isolated does not allow for the technical requirements of inventions, and technical changes. The history of technical progress will be fully developed as a discipline only when historians are made properly aware of this interaction. The reader should realise that we have schematised this discussion deliber ately: we shall return to this point later on.

===== PAGE 743 =====

726
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Labour processes were to benefit from the progress made in the manufacture of certain materials - particularly the production of special types of steel - and of new energy convertors. Again we are restricted to illustrating this with only a few succinct examples. The history of implements used in forging still remains to be written.
Around 1880, the large steam power-hammers had reached their technical limit. These enormous hammers, the first of which had been installed in Le Creusot in France and in Terni in Italy, weighed one hundred tons. It was around this time that the first large presses were used, which were to reach up to 20,000 tons - this would have been unthinkable with the old machines. Once more, then, it had proved possible to go beyond certain limits. At the same time, electricity was to lead to the creation of reversible rolling mills. This dispensed with the need for delicate manoeuvres, which had limited the size of the pieces it was possible to work. It would be helpful to have an analysis of the effect of these changes on output, something which would not be so difficult, given the wealth of statistics to hand. Between 1850 and 1900, blast furnaces increased in capacity from 150 to 750 cubic metres. The increased speed and power of all equipment used in mechanical processes, caused output to be raised tenfold. In other words, some techniques may appear to have remained stable, in that the principles on which they were based did not change, but changes in neighbouring techniques considerably modified the conditions of production. The refining of waste gases using the Cowper apparatus, the production of coke, which developed between 1850 and 1860, and the emergence of reversible rolling mills are very important factors, which usually go unnoticed.
This is not such a problem with the study of machine-tools, where the relationships of reaction and interaction are multiple and much more obvious. A machine has the external supports of materials or energy, but as they become more sophisticated machines are actually capable of providing themselves with the required pieces. We have already noted several times that this phenomenon occurs fairly fre quently in the history of techniques. In 1873, Spencer invented the revolving lathe, a semi-automatic machine, introducing several econo mies in the assembly and dismantling of tools, to work the pieces.
More sophisticated forms of milling machines and grinding machines were created and put into widespread use. Because of its large output, the milling machine was more widely used in mass production than any

===== PAGE 744 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
727 other machine tool. And at the Philapdelphis Exhibition of 1876, it was demonstrated that the grinding machine had obtained more precision than ever before. With greater power and an increased productivity available, tolerances became more narrowly defined. Rathenau used to claim that up until about 1880, work done with hand tools could still compete with that dorie using machine tools, but after this, his claim was no longer true (fig. 12). Semi-automatic machines were replaced by machines which were fully automatic. The high speed steel used to replace carbon steel invented by Brustlein, Taylor and White, increased efficiency by one hundred per cent: carbon steel permitted a cutting speed (the relative speed of the tool on the surface being worked) of ten metres per minute. By 1912, this figure had risen to between thirty and fifty metres a minute. The history of machine-tools is marked by more and more radical advances: Browne and Sharpe's universal milling machine (1862); Hartness' semi-automatic lathe with revolving turret (1862); Spencer's automatic lathe; Gleason's machine for cutting bevel gears (1874); Brown and Sharpe's grinding machines
(1876); Fellow's machine for cutting spur gears (1890); Kreutzberger's machine for sharpening milling cutters (1874) ; and finally Reinecker's machine for backing off tools (1882).
We need an inventory of all these tools, the context in which they were used, and the effects they had on other techniques. From this point, the machine tool was an indispensable part of production.
A global inventory would require not just a single chapter, but several volumes. We had only one choice, and that was to use examples whic;h perform two functions. They not only demonstrate the existence of a new technical system, they also help explain the meaning and the significance of technical changes. We would like to emphasise here the importance of certain phenomena.
The first of these is the incredible diversity of the new materials available. We have already cited some of the latter, but added to these were alloys, which were a fundamental part of the new means of transport. These alloys were usually metals which combined lightness with other properties. One of these was iron brass, which was created by Dick in 1883 and used to make ship propellers. Aluminium bronzes were perfected by Sainte-Claire Deville and Debray in 1855: this metal was an alloy of 90% bronze and 10% aluminium, and had a high chemical resistance. In 1908, in a factory at Duren, Willm discovered duralumin, which was finally perfected by 1910. This metal was a

===== PAGE 745 =====

728
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS

===== PAGE 746 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
729 mixture of 93% aluminium, 5% copper, 1% manganese, and 0.5% magnesium. If nickel was added, this metal could be drawn. It was eventually used in the transport of electricity. Perhaps it would also be relevant to mention here the addition of new properties in quite a different area, that of food. Take for example the creation of an
"artificial butter" or margerine, by Mege-Mouries in 1869. Calcium carbide, which was discovered by Moissan was immediately used for several purposes. The discovery of acetylene in 1892, was to revolu tionise the use of blowtorches.
A second aspect of this revolution was the increased output and therefore speed of production. Transport had to adapt to these new speeds, and so did the various means of communication. Morse conceived of the electric telegraph in 1843, but it was in 1851 that Brett succeeded in sending the first telegram across the Channel. Telegrams were first sent across the Atlantic in 1858. Branly and Marconi succeeded in making the first wireless transmissions in 1897. In 1866,
Marinoni assembled the first rotary presses for continuous printing, just at the very time when paper pulp was being made from wood, and no longer from rags. The next advance after this was the creation of linotype in 1886, and then monotype in 1900. The first attempts to construct writing machines go right back to 1714, but it was only after
Wheatstone and Remington's work, from 1851 to 1860 and from 1876 to 1878 respectively, that these machines were actually created.
Ideally, any work on this subject would be divided into three different stages. The first would consist of a monograph - and some excellent ones have already been written, particularly in the last few years on machine-tools. Secondly, all the applications of any given invention should be demonstrated. Take the widespread application of electric ity for example, or the incandescent lamp (1879) or Alexander Gra ham Bell's telephone (1876). The final part would be the other end of the spectrum, determining all the necessary conditions for the emergence of a new technique. Divergent and convergent studies could provide an effective framework for studying the problem of the technical system.
Complementary research would be concerned with certain aspects of the installation of the new technical system. We still do not properly understand the dynamics or the resistance of these structures, and this problem has rarely been dealt with. The discrepancy begins when an invention becomes more and more a scientific construction, such that

===== PAGE 747 =====

730
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
the entrepreneur is no longer able to function alone - at this point, any transformations are made by teams of managers, rather than by the heads of firms. But the latter tend to think in terms of investment, of materials, and of the problems of obsolescence, for example.
Bessemer's discovery aroused even less enthusiasm because the research and development had been a long, drawn-out process. There were also problems with materials - very pure iron was needed. It is not surprising, therefore, that there was a great deal of reluctance about the whole project. It is true that the Bessemer convertor was quickly adopted in certain cases, in the Marine steelworks at Assailly in 1862, and in the ironworks at Terrenoire. In 1863, the directors of the Firminy company acknowledged the usefulness of the new process, but judged it to be unsuitable for use in their factories. The directors of the Alais factory even went to England, where they noted the limitations of the process regarding the different types of cast iron. "It seemed to us that this process only worked with certain types of cast iron. There is currently a major experiment being conducted into this process, and it includes all the elements of a normal large scale production of steel." It seems that most people preferred to wait for the results of the experiments conducted by others, and fur thermore, to wait for the price of the licence to go down. In Allevard, they spoke of "a new, rather strange process, six or seven years old, with apparently no future, and which has been rejected both in theory and in practice." So everyone waited to see what everyone else would do. "Manufacturers seem reluctant to adopt it, and those who have given it a try are not fully satisfied. It seems that this type of steel is only suitable for limited purposes, - for rails and axles. The Terre noire, La Marine, and Fourchambault companies have all bought licences, and they seem to have faith in this new method of production. We shall certainly examine this method, and we will also visit England in order to get a clearer perspective, but so far there is little evidence to persuade us that we too should adopt this new process." Schneider, in Le Creusot, waited for the results before he too embarked upon this new adventure in 1864. The steel works at
Saint Etienne admitted that they did not have the necessary capital to make the investment.
In fact, it seems that the adoption of the new process was the work of a monopoly. One of the only ores providing suitable cast iron was found in the Algerian mines at Mokta. Now the latter were organised

===== PAGE 748 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
731 by a group which were part of the Societe generale de France, and furthermore, this group was also comprised of the Marine, Fourcham bault and Le Creusot ironworks. In 1869, Firminy, who was not yet linked to this group, and Alais (1867) adopted the open hearth steel plant. The directors of Mokta had wanted to profit from this situation and create concentrations and regroupments. It was thus an entire complex of particular situations which demanded the adoption of the new process.
The same was true of the Diesel engine. Several industrialists were approached from 1894, before the engine was finally developed in
1897. While Krupp and the Machinen Fabrik in Augsburg in Germany, and Sulzer, a construction engineer from Winterthur in Switzerland quickly applied for licences, thus financing the last stages of research, the main French building companies, in particular Cail and the Societe alsacienne de construction franaise still had their reservations. "We have adopted the policy" wrote the managing director of the Say refinery, "of concerning ourselves only with those aspects which concern our particular industry." Schneider's response was similar.
"We have examined your report with interest, but we believe that the complexity of our current business will not enable us to satisfactorily follow up your communication."
A category of authors have found their pleasure in highlighting a certain type of intellectual myopia - yet, wasn't it Pechiney himself who was uncertain about the future of aluminium? And what about the banks which were often called in to put up the capital needed for investment? Some banks established services for the funding of industrial and technical research: the Societe generale established such a service in 1867, and the Rothschilds in 1870. There is another interesting turning point apparent here. It was not only techniques at stake in these situations, but mental attitudes and prejudices, which were far more difficult to change. There were concerns of all kinds, financial, industrial, and sometimes even technical, and of course that good old trust in tried and tested methods. It was audacity, the possibility of making a profit from a monopoly, even if it was only temporary, and a fondness for risk which proved to be the impetus for technical progress. The evidence seems to point to the fact that it was around the period between 1870 and 1880 that a completely new conception of the material world evolved. This deserves far more attention from historians than they have given it so far.

===== PAGE 749 =====

732
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Amongst all the other problems caused by technical progress and the necessary adaptations to be made within technical systems and other, non-technical systems, one problem in particular is paramount.
The passage from manual labour with tools to machine labour brought with it changes in labour capacity, and consequently, changes in the organisation of labour in the workshops and factories. After 1865, there was a massive wave of immigration into the United States. These immigrants were mainly unskilled (they were often unable to read or write in their native language) and as such they were unable to supply the needs of American industry. "The lack of skilled workers," noted
Charlotte Erickson, "the difficulties of recruiting workers from abroad and training them and the ill will of the unions, means that American management has been forced into a policy of mechanisation, so that they do not have to meet the demands of skilled workers from
Europe." The machine tool would no doubt have been introduced into America eventually, but progress would certainly have been slower if it were not for these problems. This explains, then, the intensity of the
Americans' efforts to create and perfect machine-tools.
All that remains to be discussed now is organisation. Most people are familiar with Frederick Winslow Taylor, worker, foreman, then director of a turning shop in an iron and steel factory. Taylor's main preoccupation was with machine output. First of all he explored the technical possibilities: the composition of steel used for tools, the cutting speed, the tools' angles, the depth of the cut, the length, etc, and he was to solve many of these important problems. He realised that besides the use of the machine, there were other variables which were just as important. No matter how perfect the machine, it was only was good as the worker that was running it. This research, begun in the
1880's, - and Taylor was not the only one to be concentrating on these elements - did not become a cohesive body of doctrine until the early
20th century. The starting point was the timing of all the various operations, and Frank Gilbreth added to this the study of movements.
It was necessary to understand that the machine was not simply an isolated unit: it soon proved necessary to extend this research to an examination of the workshop as a whole, and then to the entire business. They also established subsidiary bodies to study general methods and planning, so that the global output could be maximised.
C. Barth lent his mathematical knowledge, and H. Gantt, his methods of graphical representation. S. Thompson contributed to the

===== PAGE 750 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
733 improvements made in the timing of operations. "As well as these pioneers" writes H. Pastermadjian, "other names deserve a mention, such as Person, who elucidated the actual principles of organisation, and C.B. Thompson and the great industrialist H. Dennison who both advocated their application.
We have been forced to schematise, but it is no less true that a new technical system was set up during the second half of the 19th century.
Table II illustrates the rise in productivity per worker (base 100 in 1900).
TABLE II
Cast iron Oil Mines and Periods (Germany) (Germany) factories (U.S.A.) 1833-43 9 46 22 1844-52
