86 1895-02 97 These figures illustrate the technical progress made during this period.
They also explain to a large extent the reasons why growth was maintained.
DEVELOPMENTS
On the eve of the First World War, the new technical system had become reality. Two observations can be made from this fact. The first is related to its worldwide expansion. Not surprisingly, in many cases, even the most useful inventions had not replaced old techniques or methods. The statistics to substantiate this would certainly be easy to find, and would prove that in some sectors the "industrial revolution" was already well under way. By 1914, Ford had already manufactured

===== PAGE 751 =====

734
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
240,000 vehicles, a considerable number which cannot be ignored. By 1913, 1388 millions tons of coal had been mined, and approximately 45 million tons of oil had been extracted. The production of electricity was still relatively small. Even though the large liners had begun to use steam turbines between 1907 and 1914, almost all merchant vessels were still driven by reciprocating systems. One must also consider the number of telephones, houses with electric lifts, and petrol tractors in use. It would be most welcome to have an assessment of the wide spread application of the modern technical system for the year 1914.
The second point is equally important. The way in which the new technical system was formed in 1914 had the potential for often considerable development in almost every sphere. There is little need to emphasise this fact as concrete examples immediately come to mind: the automobile, the aircraft, wireless telegraphy which was still at this point only telegraphy, and electric and hydraulic installations, were all open to considerable improvement and advance before fulfilling their potential.
Many of the advances made in technology were unquestionably the result of the war effort between 1914 and 1918. Te pressure exerted by the requirements of the military, a systematic expansion of technologi cal research which was encouraged and supported by governments, visibly accelerated the improvement of the latest modifications and the earliest refinements of the system. Mass production, the assembly line, the search for superior materials in both armaments and transport, presented problems many of which were not resolved; in most cases these problems were to pave the way for fecund developments from which the post-war period was to profit.
Although statistics for the end of the 19th century are available, the period between August 1914 to the beginning of the Second World
War is far less clear. Both historians and econominsts gave up in the face of increasingly more complicated problems. Technicians appear to be more interested in remote periods of history than in recently outdated technical systems. Consequenly the nature of this work must remain more and more allusive, and the account must be restricted to a few particularly significant examples, althought these may in some respects distort the argument.
On the subject of energy, the discussion still concerns the same type of energy converter: steam-driven turbines (progressively replacing the old reciprocating engines which were consequently disappearing

===== PAGE 752 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
735 almost completely) (fig. 13); hydraulic turbines, and petrol and diesel internal combustion engines. Each of these became more powerful and increased in output capacity by almost imperceptible refinements which, principally relied upon support systems, by the use of ever superior materials and by a more efficient manufacturing of the different components. The change occurs in the elements around these engine systems, resulting from the use of new combinations; as for example, the coupling of the diesel and electric engine, which meant that an inflexible energy could be converted into a more adaptable form and thus respond better to the variations of demand. This also increasingly involved the substitution of hydrocarbons for solid fuels.
Henceforth, however, such developments were more laborious, hal ted by necessary adaptations and by all the difficulties created in the process. Perhaps the most considerable efforst were made in the development of the aircraft engine, from which stemmed the most important results. The paths for the development of both the power and the weight of the engine were very limited. In 1920, the power to weight ratio was already less than one kilo per horse power. The problem of pressure took longer to resolve for when over an altitude of
5000 metres, the power would fall by half. The discovery of the altimetric corrector, superfueling and supercharging enabled a breakthrough in previously very restricted boundaries. If the questions
Figure 13. - Multicellular turbine (Rateau). Eight pressure levels, the first level containing two levels of speed.

===== PAGE 753 =====

736
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
of efficiency in fixed installations was the only one to arise, mobile cnverters on the other hand, essentially those used in transport systems, could only advance either by resorting to very different techniques, as regards the developments in materials and comple mentary systems, or by the reorganisation of installation, which included the instruction of system operators, a repair network, a fuel distribution network, etc.

===== PAGE 754 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
737 Without doubt, the best illustration of the method we have sugges ted is provided by the techniques of transport. In this area in particular transport technology brought into play an extensive environment. It appears that from the end of the First World War, transport was in a sense conditioned by two important factors; on the one hand, cost, and on the other, speed. Cost is certainly an extremely complex notion, both technically and economically; technically insofar as the producti vity of materials aims for maximum output (it would be fascinating to study the strength of the materials used, the efficiency of the machines, and many other such problems); economically, insofar as everything must come within a cost system which is not entirely dependent on the technical one. It is necessary to cite only one example to illustrate this last point. The production of coal in France after 1918, shows the distortions which are contingent upon a large number of factors. An evenning out of the price of the various mineral fields was then established in order to make life more convenient for certain of them.
It is the classic example of economic imbalance created by technical development, and by particular conditions. In fact the main reason for this policy results from the lack of workforce mobility. Similarly, to use a present day example, oil entails many political problems now that it has become a major source of energy. Once again, consistencies must not only exist within the technical system itself but also within the whole system network.
We have already discussed the extent to which the railway was able to expand rapidly due to secondary systems. Apart from these first innovations, which have already been mentioned, one must also add the track circuit, developed in 1879, and the block system in 1892.
Henceforth the organisation of traffic was to reach its perfection: the arrival of centralised controls, and "dispatching" in 1930-1933 completed the evolution, so to speak. The second problem, other than the actual technology of transport itself, is the light weight of the material: the development of specialised steel, and the advance in metallurgy led to appreciable weight saving advantages. This conse quently enabled the development of the first metallic cars. At the same time the growth of the power of the locomotive influences load and speed. In 1900, on the Paris-Bordeaux line, and engine of 1000 horsepower pulled a load of 255 tons at a speed of 90 km/h. In 1925, an engine of 2000 horsepower pulled a load of 450 tons at 95 km/h, and in
1935, an engine of 4000 horsepower pulled 760 tons at 130 km/h. There

===== PAGE 755 =====

738
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
was definite progress made in the engine itself, but also in secondary systems, for instance that of automatic lubricating, to . take but one example.
The most important development was that made in the transfor mation of the locomotive. Electric traction was generalised particu larly after the First World War. But the first electric diesel engines also appeared at this point. They certainly did not show an immediate technical improvement, but they did bring about savings on invest ment, which must be taken into consideration.
The most important development was that made in the transfor mation of the locomotive. Electric traction was generalised particulary after the First World War. But the first electric diesel engines also appeared at this point. They certainly did not show an immediate technical improvement, but they did bring about a saving of invest ment which must be taken into consideration.
Although the diesel engine represents a weight of 4 kg horseweight, and although the electric diesel engine was able to exceed this by 20 to
30 kg, as well as this, the starting and stopping modes were very much facilitated by the flexibility of electric energy. We also remember the first "michelines" [named after the Michelin tyres they used] or
"railcars" produced in 1931, although this is still a lateral innovation.
The grip of the rubber tyre is almost three times that of the adhesions of steel to steel. In order to finish what is essentially an inventory, we should examine the development of aerodynamics, which is relevant to a good many other forms of transport: it was possible to achieve speeds of up to 100 km/h, and to reduce air resistance to 60% . This brings us back to the essential problem: the limits of the railways are the result of factors which are not necessarily of a purely technical nature.
The development of maritime transport was slightly different, even though the nature of the results was the same - both speed and tonnage were increased. However, like the railways, the performance of ships meant that the volume of traffic was fairly low, and of a particular nature. We have tried to schematise these developments and their particular aspects in the schematic table III. Insofar as we are dealing with performance - which we shall consider later - obviously tech niques are of primary importance. According to this recent text:
The development of engines from 1920 to 1930 was considerable. Oil-firing was adopted and the use of geared turbines meant that engines turned faster and were thus more productive, while the propellor turned more slowly. Water heaters were made lighter,

===== PAGE 756 =====

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
739 and this meant a higher pressure could be obtained (materials): the original heaters produced steam at 15 kg in saturated vapour, and they had a coal consumption of 640 to
700 grammes hp per hour. The gross weight of the engine was thus reduced from 125 to 75 kg horseweight. The fuel consumption fell from 700 g of coal to 465 g of oil in direct turbines, then to 450 g in the first geared turbines, with saturated steam. The pressure of steam went from 15 to 30 kg, and when superheating of 350 to 400°C was introduced, fuel consumption fell to 325 g hp per hour. In brief, the weight of the propulsive apparatus had been reduced by half since 1900. Added to this was the reduction of the amount of fuel required, which represented a drop of over 50% for an Atlantic crossing.
Special types of steel were used to make hulls lighter, but they were still as solid. And even if Bertin's theory about the maximum size of ships was still true, nonetheless, these boundaries had been widened.
TABLE III
Speed Machine Power Year Ship Tonnage in knots (type) (hp) 1858 Great 28,800 13.4 2 reciprocating
Eastern machines and wheels 1863 Scotia 3,871 15.3 ditto 1893 Campania 19,000 22 reciprocating, propellor
1914 Aquitania 51 ,700 24 turbines 60,000 1935 Normandie 68,960 28.64 turbines, 160,000 reciprocators
1935 Queen 4,000 29.68 ditto 200,00 Mary The development of warships was very similar. The problem of materials was more important, perhaps, because of the need for armour-plating. Steel had already reduced the weight of ships by 50 to
55% in comparison to wooden ones, and by 30 to 45% in comparison to ships made of iron. Specially made steel and welding reduced this weight even further. By contrast, merchant ships hardly changed.
Their tonnage was modest, and the old alternating machines were still used with coal. Speed was not so necessary for these ships, and Diesel proved to be perfectly suitable for this kind of transport. The Palmyre, which is representative of this type of ship had a tonnage of 22,000 tons dead weight, and 30,000 tons load displacements.
The car had begun to conquer the world, now that it was no longer the work of an artisan but the product of an assembly line. The war had

===== PAGE 757 =====

740
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
been a major factor in this. The French army, for example, had 6000 vehicles in 1914, and by 1918, it had 92,000. Ford had an annual production of 240,000 cars in 1914, and this increased to 950,000 in
1919. Once again, materials were an important factor, as much for the engines as for the bodywork and the chassis. The French Omega six was the first car to be made almost completely with special steel.
Sheet metal was first used for the bodywork in America in 1920.
However, the "lateral" progress was probably more important. The electric starter was invented by Bossu in France in 1905, and developed by the American, Bendix in 1912. After 1923, four-wheel braking was finally adopted, and the addition of a servo-assisted brake increased braking power without having to increase the pressure on the pedal. Low pressure tyres, larger than the older type, were now used in all cars from this period. The first "balloon" tyres caused severe vibration on the front axles (which came to be known as the "shimmy" after a fashionable dance of the time). As a historian of cars wrote recently, "they had to completely revise the types of suspension, shock absorbers and brake systems; they soon discovered that a car's road handling depended on all these factors, which all interlinked." Tyres could cover distances of 1500 km in 1906, 4000 km in 1914, and over 8000 km in 1925. Several changes completed the definitive look of the car by 1934: independant suspension on the four wheels, along with suspended engines and gearboxes which had been highly improved from 1925, pressure lubrication throughout the chassis, were widely used, and finally in 1924 cellulose varnish replaced oil paint. The greatest revolution was in 1934 when the postioning of the major organs of the car was called into question and completely rearranged, so that cars now could have front wheel drive.
Another important fact is noteworthy, - despite the big increases in prices at this time, the price of cars in 1925 rose by only 1 .5% to 2% at the most.
There is no doubt that the war had made aeroplanes a familiar sight in the world. Between 1914 and 1918, work in this field had been characterised by haste and improvisation. But the requirements of war meant that only planes of a particular type had been built. It was only when the war was over and so many crafts were available, that they were first used for civil purposes. The first air services were operated between Paris and London, for eleven passengers, and Paris and
Brussels, for five passengers. These journeys were made with the

===== PAGE 758 =====

1HE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
741 Goliath F 60, but this was simply a military plane which had been adapted, whereas quite different planes were required. As in the case of cars, competition encouraged increases in both speed and distance.
In 1920, the Spad 20 A attained a speed of 265 kmh. At the end of the same year, the Nieuport-Delage 309 kmh, which was thought then to be the fastest speed possible. In 1924, a speed of 448 kmh had been reached, and this remained unbeaten for ten years. In 1927, the first crossings were made over the north and south Atlantic.
We saw that the power and weight of engines were developed, but the very conception of these apparatuses also changed. The mono plane with internal armature was widely used and the retractible was adopted. The airframes became more streamlined and comfortable.
After 1930 light alloys and special steels were used, dispensing with need for shrouding. The modern plane had arrived.
The plane was an ideal way of transporting mail, and it was soon used for carrying passengers on a wide scale. A German company was formed to link Berlin, Hamburg and Leipzig in 1919. The first British company was founded in August of the same year. A few statistics are sufficient to show the development of this new form of transport - the size of the air network in 1919 was 5150 km, in 1929 it was 202,455 km, and by 1937, this had more than doubled to 536,717 km.
There had been a veritable revolution in transport from the end of the 19th century, then, but the development and consolidation of these changes was a long and difficult process.
Other techniques had developed more slowly. We are familiar with the general outlines of the evolution of these techniques, but the various stages still require more precise investigation, especially in terms of chronology. The lack of precise data in this sense means that we are unable to isolate the break points as we did for the development of transport. We can only repeat here that what is needed above all is a distinction between technical research, which is becoming more and more continuous, and industrial techniques. If such a history was written, this would no doubt substantiate our view that technical systems are part of industrial reality, that these systems have limits, and that the passage from one system to another can only take place insofar as there has been prior research in one system which has - still establishing balances between all the different areas of progress - led into the following one. Research was continuous between 1920 and
1939, of that there is no doubt: penicillin and nylon date from this

===== PAGE 759 =====

742
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
period, but they could not be incorporated into the current technical system. There was even organised research on a large scale: take for example the Office central d'etude de materiel de chemin de fer,
(Central Office for the research on materials used in railways), the research department of the main French railway network.
The iron and steel industry was able to make considerable progress in this way, without modifying the structures of its apparatuses and processes. Between 1900 and 1950, the diameter of crucibles in blast furnaces more than doubled in size, raising the daily production rate to
1000 tons - the equivalent to the annual production of a blast furnace in the 18th century. The output of Thomas convertors was raised from 30 to 60 tons. The first open hearth convertors had an output of 15 to 20 tons, by 1950, this figure had gone up to 550 tons. By 1930, coals which were unsuitable for use in the iron and steel industry could be coked.
Rounds bars could be laminated by 1900, at first only with up to diametres of 150, and at an hourly rate of 8 to 10 tons. By 1950, diametres of 550 were possible, at an hourly rate of 80 to 100 tons.
Continuous laminating of sheet metal was introduced in Teplitz, in 1892. In 1916, in the United States, an iron strip 600 mm wide could be laminated in a continuous rolling mill, whose shaping groove consisted of a universal mill. From the 100 tons of the power hammer, they had progressed to the 20,000 tons of forging presses.
Many of the processes in mining and agriculture were mechanised.
The transport of energy was generalised by the use of electricity, and the latter was used more and more extensively for its heat as well as for its chemical properties. We could only make an accurate assessment of the development of certain techniques if we had maps of the various transport networks of different periods (electricity, telephone, pipe lines etc). The first projects to build tidal power stations date from
1918: research at the Arcachon basin (1921), at La Rochelle (1919), and on the Severn (1918). Once again and as we shall see in the following chapter, productivity is perhaps the best measure of techni cal progress in this field.
In the 18th century, the labour of one rural worker provided for 2.5 persons. This figure had gone up to 3.7 persons by 1925, to 4.3 in 1929, and to 5 . 1 in 1939. Progress was moderate until the eve of the Second
World War, therefore. Table IV illustrates the rise in productivity in the United States, taking a base of 100 in 1929:

(million of tonnes with iron included until 1990) 7 19 41 70 110 160 These figures pose one final, important problem, that of the discrep ancies between advanced and undeveloped countries. It is a common place to say that the economic development of various countries is inextricably linked to technical transfers: "international technological transfers are an essential factor of industrial development." We prefer the term "acquisition" of techniques by non-pioneering countries,

===== PAGE 761 =====

744
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
reserving the term "transfer" for the passage of a technical process from one industry to another.
In fact, the problem of the acquisition of techniques by non pioneering countries is one of the very cases where the notion of compatibility between systems is vital. In other words, a technique, no matter what type, presupposes the existence of a social and economic system which can accomodate it. The existence of a working class - one which has had some education, that is - the training of management, possibilities for investment, the need to supply certain consumer demands, all these are significant factors in the assimilation of a new technique. The period we are currently discussing is extremely informative on this subject, but there is still a great need for more research. The technological development of the United States needs further study as do those countries where technology has suddenly become an important factor ,such as Russia, Italy, and particularly in this respect, Japan. The importance of capital from abroad - and the end of the 19th century was the most important period for foreign investment before the finanacial crises caused by the First World War the "export" of factories, of technicians, the "imitation" of foreign practices, the missions abroad, and the system of licencing are all areas of research which have been left virtually untouched. And these must all be considered in relation to particularly delicate monetary prob lems: the industrialisation of Italy was certainly facilitated by its forced currency.
It is difficult to schematise the nature of the technical world between 1929 and the beginning of the Second World War. But we can be sure in saying that by 1939, only a few vestiges of the British technical revolution of the 18th century remained. Only the steam locomotive, which was giving way to electric or diesel traction, and the steam boat remained as a testament to the old technical system. The plane, the car, electricity and petrol, the telephone and wireless telegraphy,
Linotype, steel, margerine, and artificial silk are typical representa tives of the new technical system.

===== PAGE 762 =====

TOW ARDS A CONTEMPORARY
TECHNICAL SYSTEM
The historian is always slightly disarmed when required to assess his own age, for he is not distanced from the subject and often fears misapprehending the events, incorrectly analyzing the phenonema observed, and misjudging the dimensions of the world which surrounds him. So it is with some hesitation that this chapter has been written.
We are confronted by yet another difficulty, for documentation on the subject is now overabundant, so much so that technical problems play an important part in our civilization, our intellectual and social culture, and even in our daily lives. Documentation varies widely and is often factually conflicting, as is conclusively proved by all the literature which concerns nuclear centres. These contradictions also become all the more apparent in reality. Perhaps man has progressed too quickly in some sectors of the major movement which is currently taking place within the technical system, and may also have taken paths which appear to be closed to him today. We know how much debate surrounds the subjects of "Concorde," supertankers, and the nuclear engines used by the merchant navy.
We should first classify these questions, and more importantly present them in clear and precise terms, which is not as easy as it sounds. This will also explain why this final chapter is so long. It is a that chapter will soon become outdated, moreover so we hope that the reader will be indulgent.
We will continue to apply the outlines established at the beginning of this work, even though certain problems remain unsolved. In other words, we must first seek the reasons to explain why the preceding technical system was no longer viable, or at least why it appeared to be

===== PAGE 763 =====

746
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
no longer effective in some conditions. Herein lies the most difficult aspect of research, one which has therefore been rarely attempted. We are subsequently forced to fall back upon suggestions and suppo sitions, but a considerable effort should be made in this direction in order to reach valid explanations.
We should then examine some of the major technical mutations existing within the technical ensemble, particularly those mutations which had brought about the evolution of other techniques. Attention should be drawn to some particular aspects of driving zones, as the innovations which are manifested represent these "centres of devel opment" noted by economists. Obviously these must be listed and examined.
We will now turn our attention from major innovations to look at their industrial application, indicating all the transformations which resulted from this. Not only were products changed and new ones launched, but subsequently an evolution in the structures of produc tion took place, which was no less important. Here again, a choice must be made, as volumes could be written on this, only some of which have already been published on subjects where the infomation has rapidly become outmoded.
The transition from invention to innovation is far more rapid today than it was during the last century and during the first half of the 20th century. The problems do not only involve technical development, but also extend to the financial and social levels. The same compatibi lity between different systems is at stake here. Although much research has been completed in this domain, a lot more still remains to be done.
The political class was not the only one aware of the problems presented by technology, as had long been the case. Entire popu lations were now variously concerned with technical change, as techni cal progress had numerous repercussions on daily life, the envir onment, and on economic or political evolution. Indeed these repercussions were so numerous that they are difficult to distinguish.
This vast and important question inevitably leads to reactions being centred on two contradictory opinions, with fears on the one hand and hopes on the other. Analyses are once again numerous and diverse in their conclusions, and it is increasingly evident that progress does not only manifest itself as it advances.

===== PAGE 764 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
747
THE "REASONS"
As we have just said, the reasons for a technical revolution are the most difficult to explain, particularly as technical systems are improved. An analysis must therefore be focused on several points:
I - Firstly, the extent of requirements and the possibility of supply must be studied, from the point of view of quantity, quality, and price, or at least short-term prices.
II - Secondly, saturated techniques should be determined in the same way, and the so-called bottlenecks which arose in this domain should be distinguished.
III - Supply and demand curves should be deduced from technical innovations which are naturally linked to technical progress and fundamental research.
In the words of Rostow:
Viewed in this way, the volume of resources, including human talent, devoted to fundamental science and to invention in a given society at a particular period of time can be symbolized by quite conventional supply and demand curves. A demand curve would exhibit the expected yield to be derived from the application of additional resources to the pursuit of fundamental science, with the existing stock given. Since the results of pure science do not enter directly into the private economy (except in certain contempo rary sophisticated industries with their own huge laboratories), achievement may reflect the premium in prestige and academic status a given society attaches to such achieve ment, or public subsidy. A supply curve would exhibit the volume of resources actually offered by a given society to the pursuit of fundamental science at a particular period of time in response to such incentives. A particular individual may or may not be responsive to the expected yield from the pursuit of fundamental science. Some men are driven to search for new knowledge by inner compulsions not closely related to external reward, but for substantial numbers of human beings one can expect that talent would be responsive in degree to the rewards, financial and otherwise, a society offers for scientific achievement. A similar pair of curves would exhibit the demand for inventions and the supply of talent and resources offered in response to the expected yields. Here we are closer to the marketplace. Therefore, for invention, one can probably presume some what greater elasticity of supply in relation to expected yields, and this is borne out for our period by the fluctuation of inventions with peace and war, prosperity and depression. But we are also dealing with men with an instinct to express a creative talent, and the shape pf the supply curve for inventors may also reflect non-material rewards.
This is a rather interesting model insofar as it incites reflexion, and insofar as we must go beyond it. Firstly, we should emphasize that the

===== PAGE 765 =====

748
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
period to which Rostow refers is not the same which interests us here and that there are considerable differences between the two epochs. It is not easy to trace the curves of supply and demand as they are not concerned with simple quantities or prices, but with manufacturing methods or quality. So outside this useful if rather too sophisticatedly established pattern, we deem it necessary to give prominence to a certain number of major phenomena which have encouraged fundamental research, improvement, and innovation. In other words, the same reasons continue from one technical system to another, and the same coherences are established as quickly as possible within the new technical system.
Need we even mention the fact that authors do not agree on the way in which the new technical system was enforced. We must therefore be content with some vague suggestions, accompanied by many question marks.
There are really three major phenomena, the first of which is undoubtedly the great crisis in the Western economy during the period
1929-1932. Numerous diverse and conflicting reasons have been pro vided to explain this, and we do not intend to present yet another. Let us firstly look at the idea put forward by the Czech writer, Richta:
"Several Marxist economists have suggested that the extent and depth of the crisis of the 1930's was linked to the prodromes to the scientific and technical revolution which caught the capital system of the epoch unprepared." Such a view obviously admits the concept of a "scientific and technical revolution," which some authorities may challenge in the same way that the notion of a late 18th century "industrial revolution" has also been challenged. It is vital if we are to accept this argument, not to deny that we are faced with a new technical system in the process of development today. One might then recognize as a correct inter pretation that the great depression of the 1930's may have been due partly to the saturation of the preceding technical system. Certainly
Galbraith is not far from this idea in his analysis of The New Industrial State and the crisis of 1929.
One may suggest that in the face of fierce demand dominating the world's economy from the end of the First World War to the Wall
Street Crash in November, 1929, technology could not fulfil this very keen growth either quantity or price wise. The production of steel in
America almost doubled, soaring from 31 million to over 57 million tonnes in 1929, and without any basic change being made in the

===== PAGE 766 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
749 techniques of the industry. Such a leap could not have been produced without some effort. And this fact is not only reserved to the iron and steel industry, as similar proportions may be observed within a great many industries. Some technical progress had been made between the period of 1914 to 1939, such as continuous hot laminating (1924), then cold laminating, and the Ugine-Perrin method of refining (1933), all of which were rapid and complete developments. Similarly, the capacity of the apparatus continued to grow, and around 1940 large blast furnaces could produce 1000 to 1200 tonnes of iron or steel per day, while the Martin furnaces reached a capacity of 30 tonnes and rose to
100 tonnes in the United States, the Thomas retorts produced 20 to 40 tonnes, and the electric furnaces produced 15 to 25 tonnes per day.
No one would deny that some technical progress was in preparation during the period between the two world wars. Although nylon, the jet engine, and penicillin were introduced between 1930 and 1940, their real entry into the economy dates from the war or the post-war period.
It is not surprising, moreover, that the great depression led to research in some sectors before 1940. On the very eve of the war, a few of these inventions had already been industrialized.
The second phenomenon, the war itself, again lacks the benefit of study. We have an overall impression, indeed some facts, but no details or proof on this score. We require information on the state of technical research during the decade before the conflict, as well as a history of technical research during the war, in order to assess the effect which the war had upon particular areas of research. Obviously everyone knows of the development of rifles and jet engines. Wiener was directed to expound his research and succeeded in developing cybernetics, but much remains to be uncovered. We have already noticed in a preceding chapter that the First World War did not lead to great technical change, but this is not true of the Second World War.
There is a notable difference but it is difficult to explain.
A explanatory summary may be useful though not absolutely valid.
In 1914, the existing technical system was still capable of further development and it was probably in this direction that progress was made, but progress was not to be the same in 1940. As we have just seen, the great crisis of 1929 had already started a process of change within the technical system. The enormity of the conflict, which took place throughout most of the Northern hemisphere, required infinitely superior methods to those which were mobilized during the First

===== PAGE 767 =====

750
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
World War. Man had passed from an essentially European war to one which was truly worldwide, involving enormous distances and dif ferent climates. It is certainly useless to quote at length everything concerning the techniques of armament.
The third phenomenon is undoubtedly the enormous increase in demand. In the United States, the annual increase in production per head was as follows: 1 .3% from 1913 to 1929; 1 .6% from 1929 to 1950;
2% from 1950 to 1965; and 3.2% from 1965 to 1970.
The beginning and end figures of the whole period are 0.8 to 2.4% for Britain, 0.9 to 3.8% for France, and 0.5 to 5.9% for Germany.
Steel production in the United States of America remained at 57 million tonnes in 1929, but in 1974, it was over 93 million tonnes.
Figures are similar for other countries and other sectors, and one need only peruse all the works of retrospective statistics to see this. We have already observed several times, that the only possible reply to a massive increase in demand was to transfer from one technical system to another, and here the demand for invention comes into full play. As for supply, this first brought into play the whole stock of existing inventions, but unused for various reasons, and then required con siderable investment both in basic and applied research.
Apart from this explosion in demand, competition between the two worldwide political systems was also taken into account. These systems demonstrated that they were both equally capable of resolving technical problems with which they were presented. Technical compe tition, as much on the industrial level itself as well as on the level of prestige, certainly contributed to the development of certain tech niques, other than military techniques of course.
Thus a "causal network" was established, by which we may try to explain the technical mutation which we have witnessed: a crisis which emphasized the inadequacies of the preceding technical system, a worldwide conflict which required radical transformations, and lastly, an explosive demand which necessitated new production methods. But other phenomena intervened in the logical formation of a new technical system. Naturally, there were inevitable time-lags in the rate of these mutations' progress, some taking place more quickly than others, and to an even greater extent, certain mutations producing the disappearance of other techniques by a sort of ricochet effect. Let us look at an example of this which has recently been stressed.

===== PAGE 768 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
751 Techniques used in chemistry were essentially based on coal. In effect, the chemical process of extraction, which today is not so important, was replaced by the chemical process of transformation, which introduced artifical silks and celluloid, then progressed to organic chemistry, linked to the process of synthesis, the devel opment of which dates from the middle of the last century. This form of chemistry employed three main resources: (a) the carbonization of wood which gave acetic acid, methanol, acetone, and formal. . . ;
(b) the fermentation of alcohol which produced ethyl alcohol; and (c) the carbonization of coal which was the source of benzene, naphtalene, anthracene and more generally a large proportion of aromatics.
After 1918, the carbonization of coal opened a way to the devel opment of aliphatic products using the process of obtaining acetylene from calcium carbide. "Coal is therefore at the root of the devel opment of industrial chemistry which may be schematically character ized as that of acetylene and aromatics."
This technical ensemble was linked to other techniques, such as the fabrication of town gas from coke, and the production of metallurgic coke. Chemical resources no longer existed on the day when town gas had largely been replaced by natural gas, when metallurgic coke had been mainly replaced by heating oil and oxygen, when electricity was oil-based, and when the steam engine was virtually no longer in use.
When this happened, the balance upon which the economy of the chemical industry was based was broken. And as we have already pointed out, at the same time the increase in the demand for basic products and major intermediaries was a completely new phenome non. "The inadequacy between requirements and availability offered by coal gave rise to extensive mutation in the field of petrochemistry and the chemistry of natural gas." And in addition to this, both these fields - petrochemistry and the chemistry of natural gas - in turn opened up the way for the development of new products.
These re-establishments of previously lost balances and the search for a new coherence with other techniques are relatively numerous and it was through them that technical progress spread throughout the whole system. There were, on the other hand, sectors where progress was slow and where the number of obstacles increased, as for example in construction and building where dispite some progress there were still many delays.

===== PAGE 769 =====

752
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
These analyses are inadequate, but by presenting these problems in a very broad way we hope to open up the field for more research which will help us to comprehend better a recent past and perhaps foresee an immediate future.
GREAT TECHNICAL MUTATIONS
Everybody, or almost everybody, agrees that we have entered a new technical era, and some authorities talk of a second or third technical revolution. This revolution or new era may be broadly characterized by the mutations implemented in a certain number of technological sectors, that is nuclear energy, the electronic revolution, and new materials.
A detailed examination of the implications of these new techniques reveals that they did indeed revolutionize the material world, differing completely from the its earlier form before the great crisis of 1929.
A new technical system was indeed created where the most signifi cant factors were already established, forming the coherence which is indispensable within any system. We will see that the coherence between various techniques in relation to each other was imposed in a very restricting way. This fact is all the more remarkable when the latest technology or advanced techniques are involved.
It is also true, and we have noted this fact in the case of each of the preceding technical systems, that delays and discrepancies persist, leading to inevitable imbalances and distortions which are translated equally well on the purely technical level in relation to other systems.
What should be revealed here, is not the fact that leading sectors continued to progress, leaving other sectors way behind them, as has been done all too often in the subject of technological expectation, but more particularly whether the imbalances produced were finally adjusted and if these distortions were eliminated, as this is the most serious point of the evolution.
We will observe that technological research could henceforth only be undertaken by very rich countries, and therefore of a certain size. A
"technological gulf" was consequently created between these and other countries which was fairly profound depending upon the degree

===== PAGE 770 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
753 of development in the latter, surmountable for industrialized countries, but often catastrophic for Third World countries.
Disequilibria have been known to be produced within the technical system of a developed country. Agriculture had followed technical progress, but was considerably slower in momentum. This is also true of building techniques, where building sites seemed to be far less advanced and consequently relied upon a greater workforce. Contrary to this, the rapid advance of technical progress in some sectors stimulated mutation in the structure of the profession of employees, indeed even in global social systems: such was the case, for example, in the development of automation, although its advance was far more gradual and less widespread than most have thought. And we need only consider the town congestion caused by cars, roads saturated as soon as they have been built, and traffic jams. There are certainly hundreds of examples.
Now that research has been undertaken in different countries, we are in a position to understand better the influence of technical progress, or in this case the transition from on̚ technical system to another, on economic structures and systems. But here again, mutations are long and frequently incomplete, and any adaptation is subsequently difficult to put into effect.
Much more still remains to be discovered. Essaies by Lewis Mum ford and the Belgian, Henri Janne, still only offer us descriptions, which despite their interest are not capable of providing us with elements for profound and systematic analysis. This analysis should obviously begin by studying the technical mutations in existence and then try to bring out in each case the points of interaction with other techniques. Only after this technical analysis may one consider the consequences of change within the global system.
Energy The events concerning petroleum at the end of 1973 have made the public aware of the importance of energy problems and the difficulty experienced in trying to resolve them. As the reader may have noticed, we have used the plural for "problems" as these are numerous and very diverse in nature. The first and foremost is undoubtedly the problem of quantities which has captured the attention of the general public, and has presented itself in several forms. In October, 1973, several factors

===== PAGE 771 =====

754
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
became apparent, such as the importance of oil costs, the selling price and all the repercussions entailed by these costs in every economy generally based on oil producing techniques. But at the same time, and in accordance with well known economic laws, the increase in the price of petroleum created competition between energy sources which had not been competitive before, such as coal and underwater deposits, insofar as new techniques added to a purely economic phenomenon.
The second point is of a political nature: oil subjected a whole group of developed countries to the mercy of foreign powers and decisions: the way to national independence seemed to pass through energy autonomy. There were warning signs of this last aspect of the problem before the crisis came about. Some authorities have indeed shown that classic energy resources were likely to be exhausted, and that as a result of this the world was rapidly heading towards a more or less pronounced energy shortage.
The qualitative aspects of these energy problems have undoubtedly already been emphasized. There is not just one worldwide energy problem but several. Firstly, and this is true of all forms of energy, the availability of energy, and subsequently its transportation and storing are essential considerations. This is partly why oil became such a successful form of energy, as it was easy to store and to transport in pipe-lines. This is also why electricity, which might be called an intermediary energy, was successful, except of course that it is impos sible to store. The ways in which energy is employed is also an important consideration. Energy may be used in a directly employed caloric form: such is the case in the fuel necessary in a blast furnace, and for every industry which consumes heat (tile-kilns and bakeries), and it is equally necessary for individual heating which represents a very large percentage of the overall energy consumption. Note that electricity was indirectly employed for the same purposes, apart from mechanical uses upon which we need hardly dwell, at least regarding their quantitative significance.
An energy source may be converted directly into mechanical power:
such is the case in hydraulic energy, and had been for some time.
Almost every other form of energy is necessarily derived from caloric energy. This fact emphasizes the essential role played by energy converters, each possessing its own special qualities and a particular way of resupplying an energy source. But they also have their own specific uses. Energy is required for industrial production and is

===== PAGE 772 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
755 necessary for transports, distinguishing in the latter the railway from the ship, the classic ship from that which demands major autonomy in distance and time, and rockets which require special fuel types as the classic combustion engine cannot be used in space where there is no air.
We will try to divide these energy problems into two main groups:
A) The problem of energy sources, that is to say the improvement of techniques used in classic sources of energy and, on the other hand, the new sources or forms of energy.
B) The problem of energy converters, in other words not only engines but also the techniques involved in the use of caloric or chemical energy.
Production of energy As we have seen, the first type of energy used was hydraulic energy, and its role became almost exclusively to produce electricity. Attempts were made primarily to improve tribu tary techniques, particularly those in the design and construction of dams and pressure pipe-lines, as well as in the development of energy converters, turbines and electric engines. True, there were some spectacular developments, but these techniques were really almost totally saturated and any further improvement was virtually impos sible. The real problems lay in financing these ventures and the development of land space.
The use of tidal energy, which had been long known since the first tide mills cited in the 13th century, also received technicians' consider ation. In 1737, the French engineer, Belidor, developed the first industrial project using this form of energy, and between the two world wars, British and American technicians began work in this direction. It was only after the Second World War that a project to power a factory using the tides of the Rance, in France, came into being. The tech niques used here were similar to those employed for river dams and entailed the same problems.
Coal had become the main source of energy from the end of the 18th century, and it was in this domain that mining methods and the way in which coal was transported to the surface underwent the most pro found change, from the 1930's and particularly after 1944. These techniques were naturally determined by the aspect, slope and potential of the coal beds. Mechanical methods were extensively developed for coal beds where the gradient was less than 45 degrees.

===== PAGE 773 =====

756
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The pneumatic drill replaced the pick and henceforth gave way to cutting and planning machines. Two parallel galleries were dug through the coal bed, approximately 200 metres apart, giving access to the workforce, enabling coal to be brought out and making possible ventilation. The cutting machine worked gradually from one gallery to another, along the whole length of the coal face. This powerful machine dug a collection of picks into the coal and pulled out a layer of coal about 70 centimetres wide. The cutting machine was then placed approximately the same width away, and worked in the opposite direction until the site was exhausted. In order to avoid rock falls, the
"walking support" was now used, and jacks were linked to the cutting machine. The planning machine, a smaller machine which was haulled the length of the coal face by an endless chain, was used for soft coal.
Conveyor belts were now used to bring up the coal, replacing the old railways. All these new techniques enabled infinitely greater output than that recorded before 1940.
Here again we are probably confronting techniques which had reached their saturation point. The space required to store this fuel, the difficulties and the cost of its transportation, generally condemned it to be used in situ, either to be burnt and transformed into electricity, or to provide raw material for a chemical industry.
At the end of the 19th century, petroleum took the lead in the total energy consumption, and this percentage was to increase still in the coming years. Above all, techniques in prospecting and drilling were to advance, and were often completely replaced. Continuous seismic recording had been inaugurated in 1956, followed by gravimetric recording in 1957. Systematic magnetic, gravimetric and seismic surveys made possible known reserves to be increased considerably.
Although drilling equipment remained the same, the quality of the materials used and the automation of mechanical plants led to greater efficiency in production.
The most significant new technique was the introduction of drilling and extracting deposits from below the seabed. The first tentative attempts had been made in 1880 on the Californian coast where a few derricks were set up on wooden piers in order to find underwater extensions of inland deposits. In 1930, permanent plants were estab lished in lagoons in Venezuela and near the Louisiana coasts. In 1938, drilling went to a depth of five metres under the sea, and similar attempts were continued in 1939 on the shores of the Caspian Sea.

===== PAGE 774 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
757 One major new innovation lay in exploiting deep sea deposits some distance from the coast. The first steel platform began work in 1947, 30 kilometres from the coast and 17 metres under water. The first oil-drilling ship was put into operation in 1951, and progress in this technique was rapid up until 1960, whereupon advance continued at lightning speed until 1970. In 1968, the American ship, Glomar
Challenger, discovered traces of oil at a depth of 3580 metres in the Gulf of Mexico. The first auto-elevating platforms saw the light of day in 1953, followed by the semi-submarine platform in 1962. From 1971, all anchorage systems were dispensed with, and the floating supports were maintained above the drilling wells with the aid of transversal propellers controlled by a computer.
The most difficult problem was certainly in trying to support plat forms in bad weather: waves in the Persian Gulf can be up to 7 metres in height. At Ekofisk (a diagram of which we have reproduced) the secular wave average might go up to 24 metres, 28 metres at Forties, and 30 metres at Brent, all deposit areas in the North Sea. One of the platforms at the last deposit site mentioned, Sea Quest, set up at latitude 60 degrees north off the coast of Norway, is triangular in shape, dominated by a 45 metre derrick. Its legs rest on three laundry iron shaped floats, 30 metres in length, 15 metres wide, and submerged
20 metres underwater, while the platform towers 35 metres above the sea. Drilling must stop when the vertical movement of the platform reaches two metres. It has become possible to create true miniature floating towns, as has been done at Ekofisk (fig.1). New techniques, such as underwater concrete reservoirs, are being tested.
All these new techniques therefore enable the exploitation of deposits which had previously been inaccessible. But many of these techniques are not profitable, as the price of oil has increased out of all proportion. Today, we can drill to a depth of 800 metres (research carried out off the coast of Thailand), but we cannot exploit deposits over 150 or 170 metres below the sea. These off-shore deposits provided 9.8% of world oil production in 1960, 14.7% in 1970, 16 to
17% in 1975, and one was hoping that they would provide 25% in 1980.
At the beginning of 1976, there were 75 platforms in operation, and 52 in construction.
In 1937, methane was discovered and exploited in the south-east of the Po Plain, in Emilia. In 1952, the production of natural gas in Italy represented a fifth of its coal consumption. A vast "gas pipeline"

===== PAGE 775 =====

758
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 1. - The Ekofisk complex. 1, reserve supply of 160,000m3; 2, 3 driling platforms for new wells; 3, processing and accommodation platform; 4, wells currently being exploited; 5, loading bays; 6, living quarters; 7, pumping platform. (From a drawing by P. Ferran printed in
Le Monde)

===== PAGE 776 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
759 network was constructed for the distribution of this energy throughout the whole country; in 1971, 13 million cubic metres of natural gas had been distributed using a pipeline network of approximately 10,000 kilometres, and, at the same date, another 5000 kilometres of pipeline was in the process of being built or was in project. Discoveries of gas in
Lacq in France, and Groningen in the Netherlands, Russia and Algeria, have provided Europe with a rich new source of energy.
Although gas pipelines borrowed techniques used in distributing oil, they have on the other hand developed the techniques of liquefaction and the transport of methane by sea.
Natural gas, gas used in blast furnaces, and the gas used in coke works, are now supplied by extensive networks of gas pipelines. In
1946, France counted some 546 old type gasworks in its towns. Only 14 of these were left in 1963, just one in 1968 (that at Belfort), and none remain today. Radical change in the techniques used therefore took place.
Of the other energy sources (developed either recently or a while ago), some have seen the improvement of the techniques used to exploit them, while others are still waiting for their techniques to be developed. The Frenchman, Georges Claude, suggested using the thermal energy of the sea, produced by the temperature difference between the surface of the sea and that several fathoms below the surface or on the sea bed. Attempts have been made in the Caribbean
Sea where the temperature was 30°C on the surface and 7°C 500 metres below. This difference is relatively slight and subsequently the machine's output was mediocre, less than 10% . Furthermore, the operation was destroyed by a storm. Similarly, a project to construct a pilot factory with an approximate capacity of 7000 kW, at Abidjan
(Ivory Coast), also had to be abandoned suddenly. At the time of writing about these techniques, there does not seem to have been a real source of power.
Men have long dreamed of using solar power. The main difficulty stems from the low energy flow. At sea level, its annual average is in the nature of 0.1 to 0.2 kW per square metre, which would require a collecting surface of two hectares, in order to supply a working plant of
1000 kW operating with an output of 30% . The furnace at Montlouis, in the French Pyrenees, found that this energy could be concentrated on one point using mirrors. The harnessing of solar energy has been used, as we will see, in space engines. Silicon, solar cells which provide

===== PAGE 777 =====

760
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
electricity on board satellites are not very efficient, around 11 to 12% output, and are extremely expensive; cadmium sulphide based cells have a slightly higher efficiency, around 15% , but they are very fragile.
The improvement of solar pumps (air heated by the sun gives up its calories to another fluid, propane, for example, and the gas operates a motor), and the development of solar cells are undoubtedly avenues for future progress, particularly as the technique of using mirrors presupposes a limpid atmosphere.
Geothermal energy was first used by men cooking eggs on hot lava flows. During the Middle Ages, some houses in Chaudes-Aigues, in the province of Cantal (France), were heated with water which had a temperature of 81°C to 83°C degrees when it came out of the springs.
These dry steam deposits are in fact extremely rare, indeed only five are known to exist in the world; one in Italy, two in the United States, and two in Japan. Hot water springs are a far more frequent occurrence and usually lie deeper underground. The deposit used in
Melun (France), and which lies under the whole Paris basin, is at a depth of 1800 metres. It has been estimated that this deposit may be used for some thirty to forty years - the use of this source of water in fact reduces the temperature of the layer by 1°C every year - and it is possible to supply heat to 2000 houses by using 100 cubic metres per hour. Warm, dry rocks exist in regions with a high thermic pressure gradient, but their exploitation raises great difficulties and may lead to considerable seismic risks. Despite an extensive programme costing
600 million dollars, it is thought that the proportion of this source of energy would never contribute more than 4 % of the total energy production in the United States. Moreover, this energy cannot be stored or transported.
Bituminous schists have also been exploited for many years. Bring ing up oil has always entailed prohibitive costs, and research has continued into finding means of improving the techniques involved.
This sector will therefore probably remain a minor source of energy.
We will just mention in passing the burning of household waste used by some urban heating systems. Although there was concrete development of this technique from the end of the last war owing to the low cost price of the fuel, on the other hand the cost of installations and the loss of steam heat transported in the pipes are significant factors, and there does not seem to have been any room for technical progress.

===== PAGE 778 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
761 All things considered, these marginal sources of energy could only have been of secondary importance, and were undoubtedly temporary ones for the most part. Even with the development of these secondary energy sources, they could only ensure, at the very most, the transition towards the definitive development of other techniques.
The greatest hope lay in the advance of nuclear energy. We have seen that a whole century of research had led to the discovery of atomic fission, and subsequently to the liberation of a considerable source of energy. In 1938, three German physicists, Hahn, Strassmann, and Lise
Meitner discovered and explained a new phenomenon, this being uranium nucleus fission. Although the first nuclear bombes suddenly unleashed energy produced through fission, it was necessary to achieve chain reactions and to slow down the process in order to use this energy practically and peacefully. Several types of usable nuclear reactors were developed between 1944 and 1955. The French, who for military reasons required plutonium production, concentrated on a graphite gas network: natural uranium reactors, slowed down with graphite and cooled with carbonic gas, were developed at the beginning of the
1950's. Successive failures in these nuclear centres led to this technique being abandoned in the middle of 1968. The Americans designed two channels, both burning enriched uranium, where water was used as a regulator and a peddling fluid, which enabled a chain reaction to be produced and evacuated the calories formed by fission of the fuel
(fig.2). Pressurized water centres were also created (P.W.R. or P) where high temperature water is produced, but is prevented from boiling by being maintained under pressure (fig.3), as well as boiling water reactors (B. W.R. or B fig.4). A heat exchanger was necessary in the first type: primary water is raised to a high temperature through its passage in the core of the reactor, whereupon the heat is transferred to a secondary water supply which is transformed into steam for use in the turbines. Each of these channels has its advantages and disadvantages
(the cost of installation, the risk of radio-active waste, etc.). Their unit power is wholly comparable: from 1000 to 1200 megawatt.
The increased use of nuclear energy presented several problems of a technical or economic nature. Firstly, one must have the necessary fuel resources, and here again the questions of energy independence come into play, as uranium deposits are not as well distributed as large petroleum deposits. Consequently enrichment factories had to be built: this in turn entailed both technical problems - there are several

===== PAGE 779 =====

762
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Fuel rods Heat exchanger Mixer Figure 2. - Diagram of the nuclear power station at Hinkley Point, fueled with natural uranium. (From G. Parreins, !es Centrales nucleaires, Paris, 1967) methods of enriching uranium -and investment difficulties for countries with only mediocre economic power, particularly Europe where either
French or English technology had been favoured. In the meantime, this option has exerted a quasi-domination in the United States.
The generalization of power stations was not any easy process to implement, as there were several internal problems. The productivity of nuclear power stations was less than that of thermal power stations.
Nevertheless, the price per kWh of energy produced by nuclear power stations seems to have become very competitive with that produced by thermal power stations. In June, 1974, the cost price per nuclear kWh was estimated in France at 4.5 centimes against 8 to 8.5 centimes for one thermal kWh, but the cost of investment required for nuclear power stations was 50 to 60 % higher than that for thermal power stations.
Financing a programme which involved the replacement of a large number of thermal power stations with nuclear power stations seemed exorbitant, and essential technical progress was therefore staggered over a number of years. These nuclear power stations must be estab lished on very strict lines: a large quantity of water is required, indeed so much so that the possibility of building power stations in the sea has
