===== PAGE 782 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
765 been studied. Although it is not our aim here to discuss the "nuclear threat" - we will return to this subject later - some of the unknown effects of nuclear power have given rise to fears about pollution.
Firstly, pollution from thermal power stations: two thirds of the energy produced goes in heat, for water and air. Thus a power station of 1000 megawatt requires a removal of 40 cubic metres per second and heats the air and water by 10°C. It has been calculated that at a higher low-water mark, the temperature of the Rhine was increased by 3°C, that of the Rhone would increase by 5°C, and that of the Loire would increase by 7°C. Chemical pollution is linked to noxious products being released into the atmosphere and river ways. Finally, there is a risk of radio-active pollution together with the liberation of radio active elements, and that is without considering the question of waste.
There are undoubtedly more surprises in store as yet.
But the technical evolution is by no means complete. The greatest change will probably be in the development of different fuel types.
Studies have begun in developing what have been termed super generators, where heat is produced by the atomic fission of plutonium and not uranium 235 as before. This would certainly constitute a major advantage, for as the plutonium burns so new plutonium is formed, so to some extent, the power station produces more fuel than it actually burns. The quantity of heat given off per unit of volume is far greater than that produced in standard reactors and the heart is recooled by liquid sodium. So far, only the U.S.S.R. has managed to produce kilowatts from a reactor designed in the image of the super regenerator stations of the future. The Americans expressed the opinion that these new techniques were dangerous, as sodium is highly noxious. Similarly, the construction of the first power station of this type, at Oak-Ridge (Tennessee), was broken off in the middle of 1973, upon a judicial decision. In France, the super-regenerating power station, Phenix, at Marcoule, was coupled up to the national electric network in December, 1973. Indeed it was thought that the plutonium produced by power stations using enriched uranium could supply the new type of power station. But whatever the case, this was still only a demonstration station, working at reduced power (250 megawatt).
The British were to quickly follow this example by building a similar power plant in Scotland.
Meanwhile, hydrogen-using techniques were gradually being devel oped. Since the development of the first thermonuclear bomb in 1953,

===== PAGE 783 =====

766
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
physicists have dreamed of being able to control the reactions of atomic fusion. The idea of using hydrogen is not new, however, as town gas once contained approximately 50 % of hydrogen, and the first hydrogen powered engine dates from 1927. Hydrogen is the most common element in nature, and may be partly made from water. Its combustion in the air produces only water and very little nitrogen oxide, and like natural gas it is both transportable and easy to store: the
United States have developed advanced techniques in hydrogen lique faction and storage. At the moment, hydrogen is produced by the distillation of coal, although production is naturally limited, and by electrolysis ·of water which requires large amounts of electricity. The second process is therefore very expensive and only partialy efficient, giving around 60 % . The simplest method of producing hydrogen would be to split water molecules, but this has only taken place at
2500°C. Even the most advanced nuclear power stations with high temperature gas reactors (H. T. G. R.) can only obtain a temperature of
800°C. Thermo-chemical methods are currently being studied, but this technique is still far from being perfectly controlled.
Thus, research is still being carried out in ever more delicate areas.
Israel filed a patent on laser enrichment of uranium in March, 1974, and the Japanese have allocated considerable funds to research the development of an iron and steel manufacturing system, replacing classic blast furnaces using coke from coal, with nuclear reactors.
Moreover, one should note that evolutions have already taken place in this domain: it is hoped that steel may soon be produced directly from a blast furnace using fuel and oxygen. After long trial periods, the high temperature Dragon reactor in Britain, has succeeded in providing its cooling fluid, in this case helium, with temperatures between 1400°C to
1500°C. But change is still a long way from being wrought.
In fact, we have still not really entered the atomic age. Promising developments exist - as is so in the miniaturization of atomic reactors so they can be used in certain forms of transport - put there are many bridges to cross as yet.
Let us end by looking at one particular technique. Most of the engi̛1es in use today operate using air as a combustive, whereas space rockets cannot use air and must consequently carry a fuel supply with them. The new fuel therefore appears to be a source of thermochemi cal energy, the energy of which may result either from the action of a reducing element on an oxidizer, or from the catalytic decomposition

===== PAGE 784 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
767 of a body. Such an energy source is known as a propellant, made of two separate components. When two components are stocked in separate reservoirs (nitric acid and rocket fuel for example), they are known as liquid propellant. Solid propellants are kept in a single reservoir, as is the case with ethylene oxide and nitro-methane. Compound propel lants also exist. Such discoveries made in the field of modern chemistry have enabled the development of the all important fuel required to send rockets to the moon.
All these modifications have naturally been recorded by statistics which reveal the changes implemented (in percentages of world energy consumed):
1900 1938 1958 1966 1975 Coal 92 64.2 44.7 37.6 31.7 Lignite 3.9 6.7 7.6 5.8 31.7 Oil 3.5 18.6
28.7 33.6 44.7 Gas 1 .2 5.2 12.7 17. 1 20.9 Electricity 0.4 5.3 6.3 5.9 2.7 Millions of TEC
818 1904 4074 6309 7971 This provides some sort of numerical image of the technical revolu tion which is in process.
Energy converters In the domain of engines and energy converters, one becomes aware of the restrictions of technical imagination. This is obviously due to the limited number of possible combinations.
Of course, certain machines disappeared altogether: mill wheels are only used in some under developed countries, or are tourist attrac tions. This is also true of the reciprocating steam engine, the classic steam engine from the "first" industrial revolution. Here again, its preservation, particularly on some railways, is proof of a technological and economical halt. In advanced countries it has disappeared completely.
In a great many domains, especially in mobile machines and in those designed for use in industry, a three stage system is currently favoured, consisting of the production of energy, the transformation of this

===== PAGE 785 =====

768
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
energy into a mechanical form, and then the latter's transformation into electricity.
Electricity is an ideal intermediary because of its flexibility, and its capacity to rapidly adapt to variations of demand, even though it reveals significant losses in the course of these tranformations and the transportation of energy. But the ease with which it may be trans ported and used, although there was no way of storing it, ensured the success of electric power, as storing could be done at the level of the primary source of energy. We will look at a few examples to illustrate this.
We have seen that electric traction was an old idea used in the railways, and it continued to develop as current supplies were gene ralized. In recent years, the use of high voltage currents (25,000 volts) has been developed, which reduces any energy loss from overhead cables and, at the same time, locomotives could run on lines of different voltages. The only drawbacks of this technique lie in the massive investments required to establish the infrastructure. Electric diesel traction has virtually replaced steam traction in advanced countries. The electric petrol-traction torque had been introduced around the 1880's, but had not been developed any further owing to the bad performance of petrol engines during this period. Some railcars, however, enjoyed a solid reputation around the year 1914; it was in Sweden in 1913, that the possibility of replacing the petrol engine with the diesel engine was considered. The diesel engine had still to be perfected before it could be used properly: it was subsequen tly made lighter, and the speed at which it operated was increased. The electric diesel engine appeared on main American lines in 1930, and in
1934, a light and powerful diesel engine was launched by General Motors. In fact, in many countries, it was the existence, after the
Second World War, of collections of old fashioned locomotives that ensured the success of the electric diesel engine. We should add that this success was also due to easier fuel manipulation.
As for maritime transport, the diesel motor had mainly replaced the steam engine in merchant vessels which hardly required great speed, while faster ships preserved the turbine, having adopted the fuel used in steam production. Naturally, the transition was made from diesel to electric diesel engines which facilitated manoeuvring.
After the Second World War, the use of atomic energy to provide the necessary steam was envisaged. This technique presented many

===== PAGE 786 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
769 advantages in certain sectors, particularly in the military domain where it made possible the development of submarines with almost infinite range. Combined with atomic missiles, a virtually elusive force of perpetual movement was possible. As for the merchant navy, the process was undoubtedly less interesting. In fact, only four nuclear propelled ships were built. The first was the ice-breaker; the Lenin, which sailed in the Arctic from 1959. The second was the American cargo boat, the Savannah, which from 1962 to 1970 travelled more than 800,000 kilometres before she was laid up. The German ship, the
Otto-Hahn, with a 25,000 tonne capacity, was launched in 1969, but her reactor was replaced with a better one in 1972 after she had logged over 350,000 kilometres. From 1967, the Japanese began building the
Mutsu, but Japanese fishermen, aware of the atomic risk involved, immobilized the ship for nearly two years. When she entered circu lation again, there were indeed radio-active leaks. As fuel was low in price at this point, these nuclear ships were hardly worthwhile. Nev ertheless, research was restarted as soon as the cost of oil began to rise again. The Germans and the Japanese worked together on construc ting containers, developing from 80 to 240,000 horsepower (against
10,000 produced by current nuclear ships). Here again, the techniques involved had not yet reached the point of perfection.
Like the classic steam engine, the petrol engine appeared to be a saturated technique. In the domain of the automobile, detailed though often minor improvements were made, or were made in annexed instruments, in particular the gearbox.
The only new technique to be introduced was the rotary engine, or the rotary piston engine as it is known today (fig. 5). The reciprocating machine was now defunct: the petrol engine had progressed beyond the evolution undergone by the steam engine. The piston, or rotor, with a triangular and curvilinear shape, turned in the trochaical cavity, or stator, on an eccentric shaft to which it transmitted the movement.
Its edges were in contact with the inside walls of the stator, and by moving forwards and backwards from the walls of the trochaic, the sides of the piston delimited the volumes of the working chambers where the operations of the four stroke cycle were performed (admis sion, compression, explosion-release, and exhaust). The cycle is completed when the piston has gone only a third of its course, while the shaft completes its own cycle, owing to the ratio of the gears. Thus, a whole piston rotation corresponds to three cycles and three turns of the

===== PAGE 787 =====

770
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
driving shaft. The rotors in the Comotor engine, are made of a light alloy, with an interior covering of nickel-silicon, with segmented iron rotor sides of steel and the edges of the segments in a calcined material.
This less pollutant motor was more efficient than the classic engine owing to the elimination of the connecting rod and crank system.
Although this was adopted in some machines, its definitive improvement was probably not completed.
In the domain of electric engines, the use of the classic engine was still very widespread, with the inevitable improvements in detail or the use of new material. There were some new innovations in this field, however, one being the linear engine. It was composed of a metallic structure supporting coils of electric wire which passed through notches. The metallic structure and the coils encased a central metal rail fixed on a concrete line. An electric current was sent to the coils, thus creating a magnetic field between the coils in the air-gap in the rail. This magnetic field induced electric currents in the rail which tended to oppose the currents which were produced. A force was therefore created which moved the rail motor and furnished the thrust.
The engine was not totally perfected, as the metallic structures of the engine which supported the weight of the coils and received the stress of the thrust and the guides throughout the length of the rail needed to be made lighter. Problems also arose in the way in which the engine should be attached to the vehicle, and finally, the method by which the current was harnessed. The engine was supplied by a three-phase system which had to be furnished from the exterior by three trapezoi dal shaped aluminium rails, one of the sides of which was covered with a fine stainless steel sheet. The major problem was ensuring that the engine and the harnessing shaft did not suffer too much from the jolts and shakes of the vehicle.
It was this engine which equiped Gometz's experimental hovertrain, and was then tried in the prototypes of the "Urba" vehicle, designed for town transport. The engine was to be mounted on the experimental air cushion vehicle devised by the American firm, Rohr, the "Tracked
Air Cushion Vehicle" (T.A.C.V.), and could produce a speed of 250 kilometres per hour on 4 degree slopes and confronted to a wind speed of 80 kilometres per hour. Here again some disappointing results were had, but the Grenoble firm which had developed this technique had good reason to expect satisfactory results.
But it was undoubtedly in aircraft engines that the greatest progress was made. Until the eve of the Second World War, only the classic

===== PAGE 788 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
771 11111 1.2.3.4.
.. 5. 6.
Induction Compression 11111 1.8.9.
Power .
.. 10. 11. 12.13.
Exhaust Figure 5. - Principle of the operation of the rotary engine. (From Le Monde)

===== PAGE 789 =====

772
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
piston engine had been used, and the range of the apparatus, its speed and its working altitude were measured in the same way. The principle of the reaction was based on all the methods of fluid propulsion. This consisted of communicating to a portion of the fluid, using suitable instruments, a speed directed towards the back and thus to produce reactions in these instruments towards the front, which finally created the propulsion. Blades, propellers, or gas jets were applications same principle. But jet propulsion is the term used to describe propulsion where the fluid is ejected by the machine, having first been compressed and then heated by burning a fuel. There are three major types of jet engine which we will now discuss.
The turbojet combines jet propulsion with the gas turbine, two very old ideas (fig. 6 and 7). The first English research, undertaken by A.
A. Griffith, an engineer in the Royal Aircraft Establishment, was concentrated on a compressor whose output could drive a propeller.
Such a unit was devised and constructed in 1929, but was later abandoned. It was in 1920 that Franck Whittle first envisaged a jet propelled system for planes. He continued his studies after entering the R.A.F. Although in 1929 he published a work in which he mentioned rocket and gas turbine propulsion, he still applied this system to drive a propeller. But, at the same time, he also patented the idea of combining the turbine and the jet. Whittle continued his work with some financial support, perfecting his ideas and taking out successive patents for them. A prototype underwent trials in 1937, but he came up against the problem of materials. In 1940, a Shell engineer designed an adequate combustion chamber. A suitable material was developed so that the plane was capable of supporting such an engine.
The W.2.B. jet was mass produced from 1943, with a centrifugal compressor.
German research was carried out in a similar direction, with Hans von Ohain, Max Millier, and Helmut Schelp, working independently of each other around the years 1937-1939. A reactor designed by
Ohain was used in a plane and the first jet flight was made in August, 1939. This reactor was very similar to Whittle's, although both men were unaware of the other's findings. They both came up against the same difficulties, those of combustion and the strength of the material.
Rivalry was fierce between engine and aircraft constructors, and the official authorities were sceptical of abandoning the piston motor.
Although mass production began in 1943, it was only in 1944 that large

===== PAGE 790 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
773

===== PAGE 791 =====

774
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Figure 7. - Diagram of a turboreactor.
numbers of jet planes literally began to take-off. Lack of nickel had previously halted research and production.
Like the turboprop, the turbojet consisted of a gas turbine and its compressor and one or more combustion chambers. The turbine only used the operation of the compressor, and the latter was intended to establish the necessary pressure in the combustion chamber for the smooth working of the machine. The turbine is less powerful than the turboprop, and less risks were involved as the gas escaped at a much higher temperature. Their internal energy is transformed into kinetic energy in a propulsion nozzle where they reduced in pressure at the same time as they cooled, and it is their speed of ejection which generates the thrust which propels the apparatus. One major drawback lay in the fact that power of movement was insufficient during take- off: jet propulsion had poor efficiency at low speeds. It was therefore necessary to create a richer mixture without raising the temperature in the turbine which its blades could not withstand.
Displacement of a body or mass naturally produces air pressure proportional to the square of the speed. This pressure may be used to compress the air to be employed in the gas turbine. The use of such a source was not ignored in turbojets, which equally eased the compres sor. Air intake was an important consequence of this. Although speed was sufficient, one could, at a pinch, bypass the compressor and the turbine which drove it. Subsequently the ramjet was developed, an extremely simple machine as it does not possess a single mobile part.
Divergent air intake is achieved by a combustion chamber and a jet nozzle where the burnt gases reach their speed by being released. This apparatus was patented by the Frenchman, Lorin, and was also the subject of Guillaume's patent of 1920 for the turbojet. The first ramjet

===== PAGE 792 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
775 was built in 1944 for use in the German Vl's: their speed was insufficient for the production of adequate compression, so the engine was operated by propulsion, using a phenomenon of resonance and flow of exhaust gas. The ramjet cannot operate under static conditions as without thrust there cannot be any speed.
Finally, the last type of engine was the rocket engine. As in the other engines, propulsion developed by this engine is created by the high speed ejection of a fluid towards the back. Air is no longer required:
this fluid is entirely produced by chemical reaction inside the machine.
We have mentioned above this new "fuel" type. The Germans were the first to study propulsion in fighter planes, assisted flight take-off, radio-controlled bombs, the propulsion of underwater torpedoes, and the V2 missile. The rocket engine is characterized, apart from its exceptional power-weight ratio, by a extremely high consumption of energy, in the sense that it does not draw from its oxidant, but it has to furnish it with the fuel. It is most often used in the propulsion of air to air, air to ground, or ground to ground flying missiles, with immediate impact, and for satellite launching, the first of which took place on
October 4, 1957. In the last case, usually three engine sections are used, released successively so as not to overload the whole rocket. The first section or launching rocket corresponds to what we have just said.
We will return to this on the subject of satellites.
Although new results have been obtained in engine performance, they have not changed totally from the engines of the old system.
Moreover, it is all the more remarkable to state as we have here, that in most cases very old ideas become concrete reality. Tributary tech niques therefore contribute considerably to the development and use of new machines, particularly in the creation of new materials without which nothing would have been possible. This is also true of the phenomena of combustion: for example, in the ramjet where the flame in the combustion chambers stabilizes with mixtures varying richness in proportion from 1 to 30, depending upon the flying requirements.
Research should also be carried out on the fuel types used, but does an Institute of fuel combustion exist?
The New Materials This is probably one of the lesser known aspects of the contemporary technical revolution, but it nevertheless one of the most essential. In

===== PAGE 793 =====

776
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
many cases, material may provide an absolute restriction to progress being made. In other, new materials have succeeded in replacing traditional materials owing to their superior qualities.
On the eve of the Second World War, the history of materials is already an old one. Alloys, bronze, and brass date back to Antiquity.
In the second half of the 19th century, considerable progress was made, with the development of specialized steel from 1867 onwards, and the search for all kinds of metal alloys, such as duralumin on the eve of World War I. Synthetic materials were also introduced, and we know the part they were to play: celluloid (1870), Galalith (1900), and
Bakelite (1902). It has been suggested that half of the materials in use today were not known thirty years ago, and that almost all the materials which will be used at the end of our era have yet to be developed.
The actual transformation of ancient methods used in the produc tion of classic materials is also important. For the production of pig iron, one tended to adopt a semi-acid or acid process. But the greatest innovation in this domain was the use of super-oxygenated air, combined with a simultaneous injection of steam, hydrocarbons, or with an increase of the temperature. The importance played by hydrogen was recognized, the use of which facilitated balancing thermal exchanges. The whole accessory apparatus, electric or pneu matic plugging machines, burners and Cowpers' hiving, automatic regulation of air temperature, and electrostatic purification, had all been improved as well as the controls. The blast furnace now became an enormous aparatus, which meant that fewer were required. We should also mention the experimental kiln developed at Liege, which operated with an injection of !iquid fuel and an increase in the air temperature to 900°C. Fuel may be replaced by high methane content gas used as a reducer.
Similarly, direct ore reduction was also attempted, with the aim to liberate the process of coke production, but this did not eliminate the necessity of remelting the ferrous material on the furnace floor. The choice of reducing agent is essential. The Swedish engineer, Wiberg, employed warm gases from the carburettor which produces these gases from the mixture of carbon and steam. The Krupp-Renn process uses a mixture of low grade minerals and carbonated products in a revolving furnace which produces a pasty fusion, and from which a silicated slag containing globules of iron is obtained. The iron globules, extracted

===== PAGE 794 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
777 after cooling and crushing, are then introduced into the blast furnace.
The Norwegian process operates by reduction, by simple contact with iron oxide and carbon, without fusion in the revolving furnace heated by gas, fuel, or coal. The final product, called sponge iron, is cooled, crushed, and compressed into billets, and then passes through the
Martin furnace or the electric furnace.
As long as instruments were changed, so the methods of fabrication were greatly modified. The Ugine-Perrin method was definitively developed in 1934: this was a refining process involving contact of slag metal. Today, two liquid materials are prepared seperately, the slag is then poured into pockets and the metal is introduced in a fine trickle, thus there is close mixing and instantaneous reduction. Rich gases from coke furnaces, natural gas, or oil, are now used in classic furnaces, which enables the elimination of gas producers. Fuels no longer require preliminary reheating. The growing use of pure oxygen in steelworks remains the major post-war innovation. This enabled the acceleration of operations, growth in the hourly production rate, and visible reduction in fuel consumption. Research .at the Ijmuiden centre into flames has led to much shorter, hotter and thereby more efficient flames. In the Thomas furnace, the blast of air enriched either with pure oxygen or a mixture of oxygen with a neutral gas, means that high temperatures can be avoided. The Austrian LD process consists of blowing the oxygen at high speed onto the surface of the bath. In the Swedish Kaldo process, the oxygen is blown into a cylindrical apparatus turning at high speed, by means of a nozzle tangential to the level of the bath. The length of this operation, which took six and a half hours in a low hearth furnace, has been reduced to an hour and a half.
In many areas, progress is still at the research stage. By using a blast furnace, it was hoped to achieve direct production of steel with enriched, already reduced ores. In steel production, many more processes were to use oxygen. In any case, it seems that the role of coal in iron and steel making was declining.
In principle, forging processes remained unchanged. Continous trains were becoming much more important in rolling, but continuous production itself was an old idea beginning with the coninuous produc tion of paper first introduced by Robert in 1797, and which was eventually used for all types of processes, such as the continuous casting of sheets of glass. The first sheet metal continuous trains were

===== PAGE 795 =====

778
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
tested at Teplitz in 1892, and considerable improvements were made to these in the United States by 1916. These were break-dQwn and finishing mills and were used for military production. The progress made in automation has made them even more efficient: steel travels in ingots of 14 tons, and conveyor belts now run at a speed of 45 kmh. , which may eventually rise to 100 kmh. Servo-systems provide a far more precise check on the quality of products than formerly.
The history of industrial alloys still remains to be written, and would be instructive for many reasons. The industrial alloy was born from the need for certain materials possessing specific qualities which could be used in certain techniques: these qualities were strength, resistance to corrosion, elasticity, creep resistance, etc. These materials were needed to make steam turbines, and are also required for turbojets or the shields protecting satellites when they re-enter the earth's atmos phere. We also noted above the emergence of special steels in the laboratory of the Jacob Holtzer firm at the very end of the Second
Empire. Around 1902, ferro-nickels were produced which were chemically hypersensitive, and thus extremely useful. Research during the inter-war period concentrated mainly on thermal treatment and on the structural hardening of special steels. Steels with the alloy iron aluminium-nickel constitued a veritable revolution in the production of magnetites. In 1938 the Inland Steel Company developed the difficult steel-lead alloy.
After the last war the lack of certain metals brought with it the first alloys with a low grade of rare metals. In France the first alloys were produced with less than 1 .6% nickel, and this was supplemented by chromium, manganese and copper. Molybdenum was also becoming rare, so this too was reduced and chromium and vanadium added. In this way more and more binary or tertiary alloys were produced which brought out materials possessing particular properties from a mechan ical, chemical or thermic point of view. It was this proliferation of steel-based materials which made certain elements of technical pro gress possible in the field of engines, telecommunications and time keeping. There is no point in discussing all of these and their specific qualities in detail, but it is worth pointing out the imoprtance of the support that precision metallurgy provides to a number of technical sectors. It is also worth noting that the iron and steel sector was not the only one to contribute to this growth of new products, other types of metallurgy also contributed, with multiple alloys, to this effort. Again,

===== PAGE 796 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
779 we are forced to reiterate that we lack an adequate historical schema of this fairly continuous trend of progress, born of very conscious needs.
All aspects of metallurgy are directly linked to alloying, from auto-tempering to the forge, the indispensable complement to the fabrication of materials. In many cases it was also necessary to improve the measuring or examining apparatuses. The laboratory of an iron and steel enterprise was quickly transformed into a laboratory of fundamental science.
Who cannot be aware of the prodigious rise of plastic - or to use a more general term, synthetic materials (fig. 8)? At first, natural materials, such as cellulose or casein were used, which gave rise to the first new materials, celluloid and galalith. Bakelite, the last one of these new materials to be created, was in fact the first purely synthetic material. In 1907, Baekeland began with formol and phenol, and with an alkaline catalyst he obtained a condensation resin, with exactly the same properties as that of gumlacquer. This was to replace substances as diverse as wood and porcelain. It was at this point that the importance of these new materials were first realised, materials which were not derived as before from extracts of animal or vegetable products, or from natural minerals, but which could be artificially created by in the laboratory by shrewdly combining analytical and synthetic processes which the progress made in chemistry now made possible.
It is worth specifying a few points, here. Some research was done into the possibilities of replacing traditional materials difficult to obtain by these new, synthetic ones. This means that some of these materials are merely replacements: the Germans substituted rubber with synthetic rubber, for example. On the other hand, it also proved possible to create entirely new materials which not only replaced older ones, but could also be put to completely new uses. Bakelite may have replaced wood in some respects, but it was responsible for the emergence of the ink pen.
There are several types of elaboration of a plastic substance:
I - Cellulose, a macromolecular body is extracted from vegetable plants. After nitration, the cellulose is made plastic by another natural compound - this time with a relatively simple molecular formula - that of camphor. This is done in the presence of a solvent, alcohol, which is removed afterwards through evaporation.

===== PAGE 799 =====

782
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
II - Phenol and Formal, two bodies of not very complex molecular characters, are isolated from natural substances (or prepared synthetically). They are then combined by an appropriate heat treatment to produce a thermosetting plastic.
III - Several simple double-bonded molecules of the same compound (polyvinyl chloride) are accumulated to form one single chain of molecules.
IV - Numerous double-bonded molecules of related but different compounds (polyvinyl chloride and vinyl acetate) are accumulated in a single macromolecule.
The two essential operations are polymerisation (the suitable jux taposition of monomer molecules without any third body having to be eliminated) and polycondensation (the obtained product results from the generalised reaction of the original molecules, with the elimination of a third, simple molecular body, such as water). All this gave rise to three main families of plastics:
I - Plastics derived directly from vegetable (cellulose) or animal (casein) substances.
II - Synthetic thermosetting plastics obtained from simple molecular chemical types, by the process of polymerisation and polyconden sation.
III - Synthetic thermoplastic resins obtained by the polymerisation of simple molecules of a single type.
Cellulose plastics developed rapidly. Celluloid itself had one major drawback - its inflammability. Regenerated cellulose gave rise to matter in the form of pellicules or films, like cellophane which was developed in France around 1900. Then came the cellulose acetate plastics. The first synthetic thermosetting resin was bakelite; other phenoplastics have been industrialised from around 1925. Aminoplas tics are obtained by the reaction of polymerisation between formal and an organic amine body (urea for example): the Czech H. John discovered this in 1920, and the process was industrialised by the
Austrian F. Pollak. The subsitution of glycerine for formal gave rise to glycerophtalic resins - these were eventually to be used extensively as resins. Finally, indocoumaronic resins - part of the group of synthetic thermoplastic resins - were used by the Germans during the First

===== PAGE 800 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
783 World War. Vinyl resins are obtained by the polymerisation of slightly comised double-bonded molecules. The reaction of hydrochloric acid on acetylene gives vinyl chloride from which polyvinyl chloride can be produced through polymerisation. This substance is remarkable for its resistance to a large number of chemical agents. Styrene was prepared for the first time in 1831. But it was Staudinger in 1926 who showed that it was possible to obtain from polymerisation a whole range of products, from dimers to polymers consisting of enormous macro molecules, which serve as excellent insulating material.
Before the end of the Second World War, then, there was already a large number of products available whose properties were both exten sive and diverse. In many cases, the definitive developments took place during the 1930's, and the industrial production of many of these products had hardly begun. Throughout the entire the Second World
War, all the belligerent industrialised countries paid close attention to these new materials, perfected them and discovered new essential products.
Polythenes or polyethylenes are obtained by the polymerisation of ethylene, at first under considerable pressure. The major British company Imperial Chemical Industries began research into this around 1928. Their research was supplemented by help from the
Dutch scientist Michels who worked on hyperpressures in chemistry.
Using apparatuses devised by Michels in 1931, an experiment was conducted in 1933 to effect the polymerisation of ethylene. Something caused an abrupt fall in pressure, and as a result a small amount of white powder was discovered which was endowed with remarkable properties: it could be stretched when cold, it could be moulded, be transformed into fibres or leaves, and it was resistant to a large number of chemical agents. In 1937, a continuous running apparatus was set to work. The superiority of polyethylene over gutta percha was soon recognised, particularly for insulating cables. Its qualities also proved to be one of the essential materials needed for research on radar. In
1950, the German Ziegler discovered new ways of preparing polye thylene in normal temperature and pressure conditions: the patents, taken out in 1954, indicate that polymerisation should take place with a catalyst which was aluminium. The chemists at Philips Petroleum used chromium. Production began in 1956 in Germany and the United
States, and in 1960 in Britain. The mechanical properties of polye thylenes are sufficient, their electric properties are remarkable. And

===== PAGE 801 =====

784
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
the same is true of their chemical properties. They can weld together, mix with other substances, and produce synthetic rubbers.
Silicones are synthetic resins identical to the ones we have just discussed, except that the carbon is replaced, atom for atom, either by silicon or by oxygen. A reaction between two compounds was effected in a laboratory in 1904, but it was only in 1939 that two major
American companies, Corning Glass Works and the General Electric Company first became interested in the industrial use of these pro ducts, some of which were as transparent as glass, and which possessed important electrical properties. Corning Glass works began industrial production for military purposes in 1942, while GEC began production in 1946.
We saw earlier that the large firm Du Pont de Nemours decided to enter into fundamental research in 1927, and for this purpose they employed the services of a chemist, W.H. Carothers, who had already made a big reputation for himself in the field of polymerisation phenomena. He proposed studying the structure and synthesis of long-chain molecules and his first studies dealt with polyesters which he was the first to synthesise. In 1930, an observation took place on a polyester obtained by the action of suberic acid on ethylene glycol. It was noticed while trying to take a sample of polyester that this material could be stretched into long fibres, and what is more, once cooled, this fibre could be stretched even further to several times longer than its original length; furthermore, far from being harmful, this operation actually increased its solidity and elasticity. Its only drawback was that hot water made it pasty. So it was necessary to look down other avenues. The obstacles were many and Carothers decided to abandon the project. However he was persuaded to continue, and it was at this point that he turned his attention to polyamides, which have the same chemical arrangement as polyesters. The team succeeded in producing a polyamides known as "No 66," which gave an elastic resistant fibre capable of withstanding temperatures up to 260 degrees. The first threads, produced for an experimental batch of stockings, were pre pared in April 1936 and July 1938. In December 1939, industrial production began. Nylon was born.
The Germans had done similar research. but they started later than the Du Pont scientists. Their work resulted in the development of a very similar fibre called perlon. In general, polyamides were used at first almost exclusively as textiles: they are now also used as plastics.

===== PAGE 802 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
785 They are used notably in extrusion and grinding by injection or compression. Nylon is used today to make diverse utensils for the kitchen or toiletries. Rilsan, a polyamide produced in France, obtained from ricin and more resistant to water than nylon, is used extensively in packaging and conditioning.
Carother's results encouraged researchers to pursue further on the same line. It was as a result of this that J.R. Whinfield and J.T.
Dickson, both scientists at the British Calico Pinters Association, discovered terylene. They succeeded, where their precursor had failed, in producing a fibre form polyesters. They discovered this at a high fusion point, and during development they discovered by chance that this fibre had other remarkable qualities. In 1941 , this substance was finally developed in England. The first of the two required products, ethylene glycol, was already produced industrially, the second terephtalic acid was only a laboratory product. The solution to this problem was found after the war and industrial production began at the LC.I. factories in Wilton in 1955.
Research continued in the field of polyesters, polycarbonates and polymaleates. These have undergone considerable development since
1956: their main use is in the production of laminated mouldings, which can be made either rigid and very hard, or in a supple form.
In 1938, American chemists demonstrated that di- or poly isocyanates could react with poly-alcohols to give substances with plastic properties. Licences were taken out in Germany after 1939 in this field. This reaction led to the formation of polyurethanes with a linear structure, which could be used as thermoplastic resins.
The final series to be developed recently is that of fluoro-plastics. In monomers identical to vinyl chloride, but in which the hydrogen and chlorine is partially or totally replaced by fluoride, it is possible to produce polymers which can be used in technology, and which consti tute the group fluoro-plastics. The most important of these is teflon, a plastic with truly exceptional qualities, resistant to all acids, and which conserves its solidity and form at extremely high temperatures. It has even been used in mechanical parts. It is still an expensive commodity, however.
Carother's earliest work had been concerned with polyesters. But given their low melting point, they were abandoned infavour of polyamides which led to the discovery of Nylon. The two English scientists, Whinfield and Dickson discovered a polyester with a high

===== PAGE 803 =====

786
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
melting point. The first licence for the new textile fibre was granted in July 1941 . Because of the war, however, this was not followed up. The technical aspects of the discovery were published in July 1946. It was then that two firms, one British, Imperial Chemical Industries, and the other American, Du Pont, applied for licences and devoted a consider able amount of capital to the industrial development of what was eventually called Dacron.
The number of synthetic materials is now considerable, and we could multiply the examples. We shall mention only two more cases, whose importance is considerable.
It was Julius A. Nieuwland, a priest of Belgian origin who emigrated to the United States, who pursued the slow course of developing synthetic rubber. He began his study into acetylene in 1920, and at the end of many years of hard work he obtained an acetylene polymer, called divinylacetylene, formed by the union of three molecules. For his part, in 1925 Dr. E. K. Bolton, working at the research centre at Du
Pont, proposed exploring the synthesis of rubber from acetylene.
Involved in an erroneous line of research, he was able to correct it when he came into contact with Nieuwland's work, and with Caro ther's help, he was able to produce neophrene, a synthetic rubber whose qualities were far superior to those of its natural counterpart.
A Swiss chemical company specialising in the manufacture of synthetic colourants, began research on the production of moth repel lents and they were eventually successful in producing one of these.
The chemist Paul Muller continued this line of research to look for a general insecticide. He managed to find such a substance in 1939, by using the action of monochlorobenzene on chloral in the presence of sulphuric acid. This discovery was blocked by the war, but in 1942 it became known to the English legation in Switzerland, whereupon it was immediately adopted for industrial use both in Britain and the
United States. It was discovered later that it had been first produced in 1874 by the chemist Zeidler, who had not recognised all its qualities. It was this process which generated the manufacture of the product known as D.D.T.
We should be clear about all this however. These synthetic products are all made from natural ones. It is simply the origins of these industries which have been displaced. In former times, natural pro ducts such as wood, horn, wool and cotton, etc, were used directly, whereas today, most products are chemically produced, using a few

===== PAGE 804 =====

TOWARDS A CON1EMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
787 major natural products, of which petrol - although not the only one - has become the most important. Salts and pyrites which were widely used when the chemical industry first began, are still relatively important.
The Electronic Revolution It is widely thought that the computer is becoming one of the fundamental symbols of comtemporary techniques, and of the con temporary technical system. In fact, however, it is probably better to talk in terms of an electronic revolution, of which the computer is only one aspect. In many areas electronics is clearly one of the fundamental aspects of the new technical system.
The origins of electronics go back a long way. Ever since 1832, the great English physicist Faraday, had suspected the existence of electro-magnetic waves. In 1833, Faraday himself actually observed that the resistance of silver sulphate possessed a negativ̜ coefficient of temperature: he was thus on the way to discovering semi-conductors.
Every history of science teaches us that Maxwell provided theoretical proof of the existence of these waves, while Rudolph Hertz, in 1887, actually demonstrated their existence experimentally. We are also told that Branly invented the practical device used to detect these waves which was known as the coheror, and he also conceived the antenna. In
1895, Alexander Popov created the first system of wireless telegraphy, of which the first installation was set up by Marconi in 1896. By 1901, he had succeeded in sending long-distance signals.
In 1879, Edison created the first electric lamp using a carbon filament. In 1883, when Edison was trying to correct certain defects with the lamp, he noticed that in certain vacuum conditions and under certain voltages, a bluish light appeared inside the lamp: he discovered that an unexplained current passed between the two wires which fed the filament. J.-J. Thomson showed that the Edison effect was due to the passage of electrons from the negative electrode to the positive electrode. The theory of thermionic transmission was established in
1903 by O.W. Richardson. Shortly afterwards, Ambrose Fleming invented the diode, the first device putting freed electrons to work: the era of electronics was born (fig. 9). Branly's coherer was thus replaced by a radio-wave receiver. In 1900, Lee De Forest begun his research into finding a more effective detector of radio-electric signals. He

===== PAGE 805 =====

788
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
introduced a third electrode, shaped like a gridiron, between the cathode and the anode: the triode thus made its appearance in 1906
(fig. 10). It was thus possible to control the flux of electrons. The diode and the triode were both devised before the technique which was to make them both more effective, and which was evolved between 1910 and 1925. It had also proved necessary to perfect the method used to obtain a vacuum (the molecular vacuum pump devised by the German
Gaede in 1910), and find a suitable material for cathodes (metallic Evacuated glass tube Electrons flow from cathode
An ear1y Fleming diode Figure 9. - Early 2-electrode tube by Fleming. (From Handel, The Electronic Revolution,
London, 1967)

===== PAGE 806 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
789 Figure 10. - The triode. (From Handel, The Electronic Revolution, London, 1967) oxydes cathodes were introduced by Arnold in 1915). In 1927, A.C.
tubes were devised which were connected, via a tranformer, to the main supply. After this, the tetrode (1928), then the penthode (1929) both enabled radio signals to be amplified even further.
In the same year that Thomson announced the discovered of the electron (1897), Karl Ferdinand Braun, of the University of Stras bourg, created the first cathode-ray oscilloscope. Since it is possible to convert almost any physical phenomenon into variations of current or voltage the latter in their turn serve to vary the deflecting fields of cathode-ray tubes.
The photo-electric effect - the discharging of electrons from atoms by means of light photons - was known before Planck and Einstein provided the theoretical background. Hertz had discovered that under the action oflight, electrons are emitted by negatively charged alkaline metals, such as sodium or potassium. This gave rise to the the first photo-electric cell developed by Elster and Geitel in Germany in 1905.
After various improvements made by 1920, these became, like cathode-ray tubes, a basic element of electronics. Zworykin, a Russian emigre living in the United States worked on amplifiers which could be used to reproduce film sound tracks, and on ways of converting images

===== PAGE 807 =====

790
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
into electric currents. The latter research resulted in the iconoscope, first produced in 1928, which was the first television camera tube.
By the eve of the Second World War, then, electronics had already made a great deal of progress. The war was to speed up this process, by precipitating a sharp increase in the production of telecommunications equipment, radar installations, firing control and air defence appara tuses, etc. At first, the British were foremost in the field of research, whilst the Americans took the lead in development and industrial manufacture. All this required rapid, major improvements.and always in relation to other techniques (in particular for all that concerned materials, especially ultra-light ones).
The development of radar (Radio Detection And Ranging) proved to be a seminal development (fig. 11). In 1904, a professor of Pitts burgh University, R. Fessenden, made a depth detector which used the pulses of a high frequency oscillator. Sir Edward Appleton and the teams of researchers at the Carnegie foundation used radio waves to determine the height of the different layers of the ionosphere. Waves were relayed to the object to be detected; the waves were reflected off this object back to the starting point. If the speed of the waves was known, it proved easy to calculate the distance of the object. There was a great deal of work to be done between the principle and its practical application, and Appleton's research was continued after
1930 under the direction of Robert Watson-Watt. They also had to miniaturise the apparatuses so that they could be used in planes and ships, and also to make sure that they were capable of producing powerful pulses of high frequency waves. In 1939, in Britain, J.T.
Randall and H.A.H. Boot invented the magnetron, which was devel oped by the M.l.T.
All electric tubes use freely-moving electrons in as absolute a vacuum as possible. It had been easy to understand their action in magnetic and electric fields. For a long time, components of solid crystals had been in use, the main advantage of which was that they were better conductors of currents in one direction than in the other.
This was true of the famous galena receiver, composed of a crystal on w'1ich rested a fine metal point. This was effectively a kind of diode which rectified the current. These crystal valves, using silicon crystals, were much more efficient rectifiers for high-frequency radar signals than any other thermionic valve which was available before the war.
Even so, it was not clear how they worked. They were all built with

===== PAGE 808 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
791 Modulating pulse Figure 1 1 . - Diagram of radar. (From S. Handel)

===== PAGE 809 =====

792
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
what we call today "semi-conductors," positioned half-way between the conductors and insulators. They were used only as rectifiers, and never served as amplifiers.
The search for solid mechanisms for use in amplifiers had been going on for a long time, and this research was linked to pure research into the physics of solids. In 1931, Wilson created the quantum mechanics model of semi-conductor solids, and after 1942 work was undertaken on surface properties. The Bell Corporation undertook systematic research into a group of semi-conductors, two of which were ger manium and silicon. On 23rd December, 1947, three Americans,
Bardeen, Brattain and Schockley, passed an electric current between two gold electrodes placed on a plate of germanium: shortly after,
Pierce named this revolutionary invention "transistor," which is an abreviation of transfer resistor (fig. 12). The research for this had taken a long time, and since 1942 Schockley had been working on the problem in the Bell laboratories. The first results were insufficient, and later ones were contrary to those hoped for before the first pin-contact transistor was successfully produced. Soon afterwards, Schockley conceived of the junction transistor, which was quickly adopted
Variations of current flow in signal circuit appear as magnified variations in output J-C wafer of germanium
Figure 12. - Signals amplifier two-pin transistor. (From S. Handel)

===== PAGE 810 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
793 universally. This represented the embryo of a major revolution which was to have consequences in many different areas, from transmission to atomic bombs, from computers to artificial satellites.
Now that currents could be controlled, the data had to be recorded.
Magnetism solved this problem, in that certain materials, once magnetised, remain so. In an American journal of 1888, one Oberlin
Smith described a system of magnetic recording which was finally realised, ten years later, by Valdemar Poulsen. Smith advocated the use of a rope, a thread, a ribbon or a chain made entirely or partially of tempered steel. He even suggested that these could be made of
"silk or cotton whose fibres would be mixed with quenched steel filings." The main emphasis in research, particulary that concerning computers, was on materials and systems which would increase memory capacity and the speed of access to these memories. The most simple method was to use bars which were magnetised in one direction: the use of the binary system meant that negative and positive poles could be used. Obviously, it was not possible to stock a large number of these bars no matter how small they were. Each grain of matter constituting these bars itself acts as a bar. It was therefore possible, - and Smith had advocated this - to pulverise these bars and coat cylinders with the resultant substance: hence the magnetic drum. The next step was to produce magnetic tape and magnetic discs. It was hoped that this would increase the memory capacity and that the unwinding of tapes or disc rotation would provide more rapid access.
Eventually, static magnetic memories were produced, and usually, these were circular rings of magnetisable material, placed concentri cally to the magnetising current. These magnetic cores were compara ble to the bars mentioned above, and as such were compatible with the binary system. A matrix of cores is constituted by a lattice of n parallel and equidistant wires, and m wires, parallel to each other, and perpendicular to the former. Today, central memories are composed of magnetic cores and ferrites, the latter no doubt to be replaced by other materials.
It is clear that research in all areas has been constant, and in some cases it was begun a considerable time ago. But we must still await the time when all the elements become complementary and coherent before a more or less global technical mutation can take place.

===== PAGE 811 =====

794
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
A NEW WORLD TODAY
Everyone is aware of the extraordinary transformation taking place in the world as a result of new techniques. The introduction of the latter
"breaks the spatial equilibrium, modifies the framework of existence, which in geographical terminology constitutes both landscapes and lifestyles." It would take volumes - and there are already many of these - to analyse this evolution, the privileged and the deprived areas, the leading and the backward sectors, the imbalances and the compen sations. We shall therefore limit this discussion to some of the major areas, which are quite clearly dominant in the installation of the contemporary technical system.
"Chemicalisation" This term is not very elegant, but it does manage to express a reality which is apparent in all areas. From furniture to medecines, from clothes to cars or pleasure boats, everything, or nearly everything, comes from chemistry. The chemical industry sells 20% of its produc tion to the consumer directly. The rest goes to other industries in the form of intermediary or subsistence goods. A base of 100 for the year
1962, rose to 311 by 1973 for the chemical industry, as against 197 for industry as a whole. And using the same base, we have the following figures: organic chemistry, 502; mineral chemistry, 198; parachemistry
(pharmaceuticals, paint, varnish), 208.
A few base products were derived from some raw materials.
Sequences of chemical reactions resulted in a formidable branching out (fig. 13). At the top of this tree are several dozen base products, and at the bottom several thousand products. "The sequence of reactions tranforming a higher product into a lower one constitutes a channel of production. The ensemble of these different channels forms a network which illustrates at once the complexity of chemical produc tion and its logic, its flexibility and its rigidity." The capacity of chemistry to produce such diverse products has meant that its use is now all pervasive. The authors of the 6th French Plan were perfectly aware of this: "By the next generation, about 80% of the industries of developed countries will be chemically based."

TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Products of the chemical industry are used in nearly all types of manufacturing. There are hardly any objects which do not contain some element of plastic or some other chemical product, and this is not including of course those made entirely of these substances. The limitations - no doubt applying only to current products - of these new materials are well known: a school was razed to the ground because of its plasticised fittings, and bottles made of plastic are said to cause all kinds of misdeeds or damage. This movement, however, seems irreversible. The only difficulty is supplying the raw material, i.e. , oil:
oil crises and the exhaustion of resources are bound to present difficult problems for chemists in the generations to come.
The Computer The computer has become the symbol of modern civilisation. It is used everywhere, in administration, industry, in accounting, and space flights. It makes life easier for everyone, it solves all problems, it threatens public liberties, and according to a recent science-fiction novel, it will go so far as to mate with human beings.
In its totality, the computer represents a large number of diverse ideas and techniques, some of which - as the diagram in figure 14 shows
- date back as far as Antiquity. Problems of logic, calculation, and programming constitute the three lines of evolution, the conjunction of which was effectively to give birth to the computer, in essence at least. The technical means were electronical.
Programming is an ancient practice: the Greek engineers from Alexandria used programmes with the help of cams. Basile Bouchon,
Vaucanson (who also used cams in his automatons), and Jacquard used perforated rolls, and Hollerith created the punch card at the beginning of this century. But our admiration should really go to
Charles Babbage, from England, who, in his famous work, Analytical Engine, showed that a "programme" could also be used for intellec tual operations. With an extremely fine analysis, he was able to extract all the basic concepts of an automatic calculator: input and output, memory, programme and logic. A link had now been forged between something which up till then had been used only in mechanical organs and abstract logical operations. Moreover, he used an extended version of the perforated roll which had been developed by the
French.

===== PAGE 814 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM

===== PAGE 815 =====
