TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Calculating methods also date back to many centuries ago. Aba cuses are well-known. These eventually led via mathematics, to the creation of calculating machines: Schickard's and Pascal's hand operated adding machines (1624 and 1652), and Leibniz' four operation machine which was developed at the end of the 17th century.
A discussion of the varied and constant progress of the 19th century would take too long and is largely unneccessary. It culminated, however, in Hollerith's statistical machines (1890), which integrated electromechanics into calculating and gave rise to data processing.
The logical tradition is the final element. This began with Aristotle, who formulated the three fundamental principles of classical logic, enriched by the Arabs. The latter conceived of a machine for produc ing horoscopes, and Raymond Lulle surpassed this by devising a machine to demonstrate the existence of God. Let us pass on from
Leibniz and come to George Boole, who in two works published in 1847 and 1857, provided an algebra which could now be applied.
Progress from the second half of the 19th century to the eve of the Second World War was slow and barely perceptible. Lord Kelvin is worth mentioning: in 1876 he presented the framework of the first analogical machine for integrating differential equations with variable coefficients. Between 1937 and 1938, Shannon established that there was an isomorphism between Boole's algebra and contact algebra.
Turing invented his imaginary algorithmic machine, which was used to determine the provability of problems. And there was Von Neumann, who developed a synthetic machine combining the logical and arith metical tradition, and the recorded programme. By this time, all the elements were finally present, and with the emergence of electronics, the path was paved for the computer.
No doubt, the Z4, devised by the German Konrad Zuse should be mentioned first; this was destroyed in a bomb attack before completion. In 1944, the ASCC (Automatic Sequence Controlled
Calculator), also known as Mak I was devised at Harvard. This was an enormous calculating machine, the first non-specialised, universal machine of its kind, but which ultimately did not prove to have any notable innovations. Pennsylvania University produced ENJAC,
(Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer) , in 1946, which was the fruit of work done by J.P. Eckert and J.W. Mauchly. It contained
18,000 normal electronic valves and consumed 100 kw of power. These two apparatuses were veritable monsters, taking up the space of entire

===== PAGE 816 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
799 buildings, and consuming as much electricity as a small factory. They required enormous refrigeration installations, and the constant replacement of the valves meant that maintenance costs were inord inately high. These two apparatuses provided a testing ground. The choice had been between analogical computers, which tended to be imprecise, or numerical computers. They contained an input device, a memory, a calculating centre, and an output device. All of this based on the binary system, i.e. , a device which could be in one of two states,
0 and 1, a switch, a relay and a semi-conducting diode. The memory recorded the data and the results, and on the other hand, the program mes. The memories were also magnetised in two states. Every soluble problem would be divided up into a series of elementary logical operations. These were controlled by the programme which was either on punch cards, or magnetic imprints.
Despite its limitations, Mak 1 was extremely important regarding the development of this technique. From 1948 to 1960, Harvard and the M.l.T. developed a series of machines which were gradually perfected, particularly under the direction of Howard Aiken. ED VA C was created in 1948 by the same team which produced ENJAC. Then the National Standard Office in Washington developed SEAC in 1949.
Both of these computers used cups filled with mercury for the memory, each one of which was able to store about 400 binary elements. In fact,
EDVAC was the first true computer. All that was needed now was to simplify the machine and reduce its size. EDSAC, developed in
Cambridge in 1949, contained only 3000 valves and worked six times faster. Besides this, it was the first computer with internal program ming.
At this point, progress was still at the research stage, and principally academic research. It seemed, initially, that there was no commercial network which could be organised around these types of apparatus. In
1950, however, the ENIAC team founded a commercial company, UNIV AC, the only one until 1955, at least in the United States, to try and commercialise computers. We should note, however, that the first commercial computer was LEO, created from EDSAC, and acquired by Lyons of London in 1953. It was after this that IBM were tempted into this field and called upon Remington Rand to develop a marketa ble computer. It is curious to note that only the scientific and military openings were being considered, and that the problem of administ ration had been completely forgotton. These efforts resulted in the 701

===== PAGE 817 =====

800
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
of 1953, and the 650 of 1954, which proved to be unbelievably successful with over a thousand sold.
Research continued from 1951 to 1958. The British ACE, and the Harvard Mak series represented progressive developments. It was now that research began into creating other types of memories (19501954), notably electrostatic ones where the information was stored in the form of electric pulses on the surface of an insulator. The magnetic drum used in ADEC and Mak 3 was the principal example of this.
The computer of this first "generation" was still no more than an enormous calculator. It worked in milliseconds, was clumsy and therefore very slow, it was virtually blind and dumb, and with a somewhat short memory. There was hardly any input of data, or output of results. But there was still a potential future for computers, however; latent developments were being made, and a market was gradually forming. UNIVAC I , the first alphanumeric machine, was also the first to be industrially produced. As we mentioned earlier,
Lyons opened up the commercial sector.
The second generation, which began around the years 1958 to 1960, represented a major transformation. The machines' characteristics had completely changed: memory ferrite magnetic cores (around
1955), transistor logic, and a new organisation of information exchange by semi-conducting elements mark this stage. Rapid pro gress was made regarding the components, and new assembly tech niques led to an enormous reduction in size and weight. The IBM 709 memory had a capacity of more than a million binary elements. Two years later, the transistorised version, the 7090, proved to be four times as fast. Bull's Gamma 60, - as happens so often in France - was a technical success, and a major commercial failure. There were also major technical failures. The IBM 7030 was designed to perform a million logical operations per second, but it had to be abandoned, as well as Ferranti's project Atlas at Manchester University, which ran into serious difficulties. On the other hand, the simultaneity of oper ations was achieved around 1959-1960.
Computers were by now working in microseconds. Transistors and ferrite memories not only facilitated a reduction in size of the equip ment, but they also increased the machine's speed and complexity. It was now possible to effect many thousands of operations per second. A parallel development was the increase in speed of the printers. The perforated reading tapes were developed, as were magnetic tape

===== PAGE 818 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
801 heads. Their capacity for memorising and re-reading large amounts of data was still relatively limited however.
The third generation of computers made its appearance around 1964, and developed rapidly from 1965. The micromodule was now part of this technology. The classic chip was composed of a ceramic wafer of 2 square centimetres, supporting miniaturised components.
These wafers were layered on top of each other, and linked by vertical wires which served both as electrical connections and as a support.
Densities to the order of six components per cubic centimetre were now possible. Components were no longer manufactured in isolation:
they were produced en masse in the form of films on glass or ceramic, at the same time as the transistors and the connections which linked them. These "integrated circuits" were made using techniques such as diffusion, engraving, alloying and evaporation. "It was now possible,'' states the work from which we are borrowing these details, "to obtain densities to the order of sixty components per cubic centimetre, and with this a security of operation hitherto unobtain̝ble with earlier methods. " Microchips and integrated circuits are at the base of this third generation.
The structure of the apparatus had been developed and miniatu rised; it was now necessary to transform the way it worked. The passage was made from the cabled computer, i.e. , capable of perfor ming a series of operations in a given order, to the cabled command: it seemed sufficient at this time to cable elementary functions. This meant working at the most elementary levels, which limited electronic circuits to a few different types. A parallel result of this was the introduction of microprogrammes, which reduced the bulk of central memories. On the one hand, the number of programmes to be run simultaneously had to be reduced, and on the other, the programmers were requested to reduce the size of these programmes as much as possible. A new type of "fixed storage" memory, meant that the programme instructions available to the user could be separated from the internal structure of the central unit. If this was a "read only" fixed storage memory, it was purely and simply a matter of changing the physical support - in general a board of electronic circuits of a few squared decimetres.
Multiprogramming denotes the capacity of a computer to contain different programmes in its memory at the same time, programmes of a type such that they do not all use the same input and output units

===== PAGE 819 =====

802
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
simultaneously. Multiprogramming has become more extensive with the introduction of time sharing. This is a particular process for use with programmes which allocates to each an equal period of running time that is sufficiently brief to make it appear that they are all running
"simultaneously." It was possible to interweave the running of pro grammes so that they ended at the same time: it seems in this way as if several computers are contained within a single main one. With multiprocessing, the separate programme are run rigourously at the same time, as if several computers are being run together.
The performance of the new apparatuses needed to be improved, and the memory is an essential, but onerous part of this. Added to this is the increase in size of the central memory caused by the incorpor ation of multiprogramming and time sharing. The processing unit is used for logical operations and calculations. It works in conjunction with the central memory containing the instructions and the data relative to the programme being run, and in contact with all the elements of the calculator, and in particular with the entry and exit peripherals and auxiliary memories. The latter are slower, but with a far greater capacity. The power of a computer depends therefore on the functioning characteristics of the various units and their ouput, but also indirectly on the capacity of the central memory. Until this time, there had been only one data address, this was the real address. IBM added to this by creating the virtual memory, so that there were now two addresses that could be used. "Dynamic address translation"
(DAT), means that the passage from the address to the central memory can now be effected automatically. Today, large capacity auxiliary memories can be used.
Progress has also been made in two other areas thanks to these improvements: in remote data processing and in the networks of interconnected computers. A visualisation console or simply a tele phone means that the computer can be set to work from a distance and the appliance used for this is called the terminal. A multi-processing computer with time sharing can be used by several people at a time, therefore, which considerably reduces the costs of computer instal lation. The vast majority of computations by computers is done with ciata recorded on files or in memories. If someone wants to make certain calculations, then they address themselves to the computer which possesses the right data. The process is exactly the same when a given programme is required. Files, memories and programmes are no

===== PAGE 820 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
803 longer assigned to a given computer, and interconnections enable several computers to use a group of files and programmes. This means that it will be possible to use not only one computer, but a whole group of computers, which could also be of different makes. The most difficult problem is translating from one language into another, but this is not insoluble. The first computers network was established in the
United States in 1968.
The next arrivals were pocket calculators. These were made possible only by integrated circuits and elementary miniprogramming. It was possible to use betwen four (the four fundamental operations), and ten programmes, and as fallback, a small memory.
When IBM presented their new 360 line of computers on 7th april 1964, they opened up completely new channels. Before this, there was the maxi computer for universal use and more simple industrial computers with limited and fixed functions. After this, universal mini-computers were created: they were incorporated as components in industrial systems and could be given fixed allocations.
Of course, everyone knows [Transl. Note: written before 1977] that the evolution of computers is incomplete and that the laboratories are currently working on the fourth generation. The hallmarks of these future computers are said to be super integrated circuits with hundreds or even millions of transistors, large capacity optic memories, improved visualisation systems, and new programming methods. The future computer will be even smaller, faster and more effective. It will be easier to use and more varied in its functions, and cheaper. One can easily imagine the fourth generation performing super-multiprogram ming by generalising the interconnections, with each one being con sidered as a peripheral of the others, or with all of them working together. Direct access to these fourth generation computers would be a matter of course.
Is it not also possible to foresee the limits of computers? The computer will never be able to do everything: it uses an algorithmic method to solve problems, and Markov has shown that there are some
"classes" of problems which are insoluble in this way. And even if some problems are soluble by this method, no machine would ever be able to resolve them in practise. The problem of chess has been mentioned in this context. However, in August 1974, the first world championship of computer chess programmes took place in
Stockholm. "A limited intelligence which means that they are not

===== PAGE 821 =====

804
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
much higher than the level of a good amateur. But for all that, it is an intelligence nonetheless, this faculty of determining in a situation where the possibilities are almost endless, if not the best move, at least the most plausible, and of gradually outlining a winning strategy. "
Futile research no doubt, but programming research has been useful in other areas. In any case, the technical future of the computer is still open.
Automisation This is a relatively old practice, and one which developed rapidly after the Second World War, to the point that people are still unsure as to which terms to use. "Automatism," "automation," and "automi sation" are representative of a vocabulary which is not yet fully established. It has been said that automation is the integration of a number of automatisms. Others define automisation "as a means of replacing and expanding man's physical or intellectual labour with the labour of machines, by performing operations of analysis, organisation and control. One could say that automisation brings a new dimension to production. It is distinct from other forms of technical progress in that it is not actually a process of production, rather it improves and accelerates existing processes, thereby paving the way for new improvements."
A United Nations report distinguishes three periods in the evolution of automisation:
(The first, which began in the forties, was the) development of a pneumatic or electric switch in three positions (neutral, positive, negative), which was used to control various industrial processes; originally, they were of general use in automatic loop controls of temperature, pressure, levels, concentrations, and physical and chemical variables. At this stage, the different automatic loop controls worked independently according to a prearranged programme, so that several control operations and manual intervention were required for their coordination. In the second stage, automation was extended to these levels of control and coordination. This was achieved by developing control instruments and connecting them to detectors which were able to maintain constant checks on the working of the production operations. In other words, the different loop controls act on one another, and react automatically to unforeseen disruptions or changes in the production process so that the latter can work correctly according to the prescribed pattern. The first practical application of these controls in industrial proces ses and numerically controlled machine tools dates back to the period between 1955 and
1960. The third stage, which began during the sixties, represents the development of electronic computer control. The use of a computer enables the preestablished pattern of the production process to be changed according to management requirements and the production programme.

===== PAGE 822 =====

TOWARDS A CON1EMPORARY 1ECHNICAL SYS1EM
805 Automation has made considerable progress in industrial produc tion. Of course continuous machines were first produced centuries ago: they were used in the production of paper by the end of the 17th century, and the blast furnace, in use since the 15th century, is also a continuous machine. The same is true for the production of ice, but this is a more recent development. Hot or cold continous rolling mills were also used in manufacturing, and the same generalisation and extension was apparent in the field of machine-tools. These were first made automatic after 1840. The apparition of the electric engine greatly eased the expansion of automation. Engines, by means of mechanical controls, gears, racks, shafts and other mechanisms were used to perform the necessary tasks. The work could be done faster, products could be standardised more easily, and it became possible to manufacture much larger pieces. It was on this basis of automatism that automation was founded.
Let us take a production process, of any kind. It begins with the raw materials, and ends with the final product. The function of automi sation is to coordinate the various existing automatisms, and compen sate for any problems of automatism which might occur. Machines are thus linked to one another as if part of a chain, and they are regularly supplied with raw or semi-worked materials. From the first, three different aspects were apparent: handling (movement of goods within a factory), manipulation (presenting the pieces to the machine), and transfers (the movement of pieces from one machine to another).
Since the Second World War, most of the effort has been concentrated on these problems, thus consolidating those machines which were already automated. The most important elements of automatic hand ling installations are sensors for circulation, sensors for flow of materi als, gates for tele-controlled shunting, carriage conveyors, continuous and discontinuous indicators of level to control the filling of funnels, balanced feeding of conveyors and balanced loading , measures for the regulated discharge of funnels, as well as apparatus for acoustic and optical signals. Generally, all these installations can be controlled from a central control post. It is worth remembering that it was D.S.
Harder, one of the directors of Ford, who coined the term "auto mation", precisely with regard to automatic handling.
After this came the automisation of those elements which were already mechanised. Classical machine-tools were replaced by repro duction lathes or milling machines, which work by reproducing a

===== PAGE 823 =====

806
TECHNIQUES AND CIYILIZA TIONS
model, and by grinding machines, of which the grinding face is controlled automatically by optically enlarged profiles. Today, these machines are being replaced by numerically controlled machine-tools.
Since these machines can be automatically supplied, they can be set to work alongside each other, making continuous production possible:
transfer machines made their appearance at the end of the last war, and were used principally in car production. The difficulties involved in mechanising certain assembly operations makes it impossible, at least in mechanical construction, to conceive of the complete automi sation of this type of manufacture.
In the iron and steel industry, a great deal of progress has been made through the impetus of automisation (fig. 15). Minerals are prepared
(agglomeration) with automatic weighing and loading machines, the roasting is controlled. Today, the automisation of cast iron production in blast furnaces is under way. Automatisms control the regenerators, the flow and the temperature of hot air, the temperature of the molten iron and they automatically distribute the wind in the pipes. They control the level of the bath with a continuous probe or the isotope method, and the gas heating. They also control the loading of coke, dolomite, minerals and flux, as well as the apportioning of the mixture and bell control. On a more experimental level, it has been found possible to control the process by computer which automatically adjusts the characteristics to their nominal value. In completely automated rolling mill trains, the rolling mills are controlled using punch cards or electronic memories. Again on the experimental level, steelworks have been established which are run from an incorporated calculation center. The decisive innovation of automisation was the introduction of the closed circuit.
The Newstead and Ormonde collieries in England were automised in 1964-65. This made possible the extraction of four tonnes of coal per minute, with the moving support. The Pittston Company had already spent 30 million dollars by 1958-60 in order to mechanise a single shaft in West Virginia. This increased output to 30,000 tonnes of coal per day. We should keep in mind, however, that these installations depended upon particular natural conditions.
The transfer chains used for cutting the multiple cylinder pieces and engine blocks were first put into operation in Detroit in 1948. They were capable of performing 550 operations in 15 minutes. This tech nique was eventually used for engine blocks, for the casting of the

===== PAGE 824 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
807 Servo-motors acting on the parameters of control, output, humidity and wind temperature, load composition and speed of loading, etc.
Instructions Computer memory stores relations hetween parameters and variahles t Display & recording

temperature, etc.
Programme (desired composition, daily production) Figure 15. - Theoretical outline of a fully automised blast furnace. (From I.:Automatisme
Science et Vie, Special issue, 1964)

===== PAGE 825 =====

808
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
blocks, and the mounting of the engines. It used to take 400 people 40 minutes to create a engine block; today it takes 48 people 20 minutes.
Fifty years ago, it took 15,000 work hours to produce a car. In 1957, it took only 1000 work hours to produce a car which was infinitely more complicated. Forty machines, each one run by one worker, were able to produce 90% of the incandescent lamps' bulbs, used in all the radio and television receivers of the United Sates.
Automisation in petroleum refining developed rapidly. At the Esso refinery at Fawley in Great Britain, teams of six men are sufficient to refine 25 millions litres of petrol per day.
In manufacturing, the linear structure of older machines has given way to a circular one. The introduction of the closed circuit was the decisive innovation of automisation. G. Friedmann quite rightly noted that in this way fragmented work processes were organised into a coherent whole. "Technical progress, through its internal dialectic, tends to reconstitute in polyvalent automatic machines new norms in the unification of work processes, on a new level."
At the present time it is very difficult to determine the degree to which production has been automised. The more complex the pro ducts, the less automised the production. In France 60% of investment devoted to automisation comes from four different sectors: chemistry
30% ; petrol 15% ; metallurgy 7% ; electric energy 7% .
But the role of automisation in the industrial domain is not confined merely to production. It is also important in the field of measures and controls, which are adapted to automatic correction and adjusting systems.
Certain measures can be easily regulated by automatic recording systems: such is the case with dimensions and temperatures, etc. A higher degree of progress is dependent above all on the greater precision of measuring instruments, and indeed, the progress of this scientific technique is well known. Many people believe that precision is a unique quality, although in fact it incorporates a large number of different concepts, such as sensitivity, amplification, fidelity, etc.
Machines have been built which can classify high precision ball bearings: thirteen classes of 0.25 microns, at 5000 pieces per hour. The verification of spherical geometry is effected by repeating successive classifications of the same batch. The progress in mass production has necessarily meant that the tolerances of dimensions and weight have become smaller and smaller. The tolerances in the machining of parts

===== PAGE 826 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
809 are between 10 and 20 microns. In this way, all the parts can be regulated automatically. In the most simple cases, the parts are sorted out and grouped into three categories: good, bad, to be adjusted.
Continous rolling mills contain automatic sequence control which engage and extract the spools, as well as automatically adjusting them.
With billet shears, the optimal length is determined by an internal electronic computer which reduces the proportion of off-cuts to a minimum, the latter being collected in a circular conveyor belt. In cold rolling mills containing three, four or five continuous stands, the thickness of the plate is now automatically controlled. One X-Ray gauge is situated after the first gate and controls the thickness of this cage by releasing the mechanism controlling the tightness of the screw.
This rough-cutting action is completed by a second X-Ray gauge placed after the second gate. The degree of precision obtained is excellent: the desired thickness of the plate can be obtained to within a single micron.
The quality of products can also be controlled. Here again, the methods of observation have been vastly improved, and with the help of electronic circuits it is now easy to establish whether or not the qualities detected correspond to the production programme. These controls can sometimes be followed by automatic adjusting, and in this way automisation permits not only a growth in production, and a better use of the apparatuses of production, but also an improvement in the quality of the products.
One of the latest areas where automisation has been applied is that of transport. Again, the earliest attempts were made a relatively long time ago. Take for example, the automatic "block" for trains running on the same line, divided up into sections each of which is protected by a signal. Progress in this field was quite appreciable in the immediate post-war period. Points are now provided with certain automatic devices. Inasmuch as the points can now be controlled from a distance, it is now possible to automise the running of trains within a given sector according to pre-established programmes. In France, this method was first tried in the Dole-Vallorbe sector. It is now in general use in areas of heavy traffic, particularly in the suburbs of large towns.
Repeat signals were first adopted with the famous "crocodile," in France in 1872. This stage was superseded by the introduction of elec tric circuits and electronic transmissions, such that trains could now be run entirely in this way, as could the control centres on the lines.

===== PAGE 827 =====

810
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
It was now possible to conceive of trains being run without drivers entirely by remote control. The first attempts at this were made during the 1960's with electric locomotives, in well determined conditions.
There are still some problems to be solved. After the automatic underground network in San Francisco was put into operation, an accident occured which forced the authorities to put a driver back on board. However, Lille's future underground is to be completly automised and there will be no drivers. This type of automisation in overground trains is only possible on lines with no possible obstacles, no level crossings, no possibility of game crossing the line, etc. There have been only a few attempts at this.
The automisation of engines used in air and sea transport has been made possible thanks to radar which can provide information on position, direction and speed. Automisation in ships is internal, and therefore only partial. Even so it drastically reduces the size of the workforce. It is mainly the bigger ships which are automised, particu lary the medium and large oil tankers. Some instrm:nents, especially the gyrocompass, enable the ship to be navigated by light and astral observations inside a closed space. It was in this way that an American submarine was able to cross the Artie underneath the icecap.
The honour of having devised the first automatic pilot worthy of the name goes to Elmer A. Sperry. In 1914, Sperry flew to Europe at the controls of a Curtiss hydroplane. The plane was equipped with an electric system of gyroscopic stabilisation. This ancester of automatic pilots stabilised the plane around the roll and pitch axes, and itself drove the ailerons and the elevator. By the eve of the Second World
War, these now improved systems had been put into general use on airliners, at least in the United States. Hydro-pneumatics assured the tranmission of commands, but these were only developed after the advent of electronics. The first electronic autopilot was devised by
Honeywell in 1941 , and was used in several thousand bombers. In September 1947, the U.S. Air Force used a transport C-54 to go from
Newfoundland to Great Britain, and the flight was made entirely on automatic pilot, including take-off and landing. The successive improvements made to the equipment has meant that they are now considerably lighter, and as such can be used in smaller planes.
Today, the use of the automatic pilot is standard practice, as is the automatic landing with no visiblity (fig. 16). Operational minimums, vertical and horizontal visibility are being reduced to ever-decreasing

===== PAGE 828 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
811 levels. The same is true for air traffic control, which today is almost completely automised. Control networks cover almost the entire earth, except in those regions with little traffic, or high mountainous areas where radar, radio and television are largely useless. The operating conditions, as with all techniques used in transport, depend essentially on the infrastructure.
Control surface deflections Data of actual aircraft position and ATTITUDE Figure 16. - The principle of automatic piloting. (From S. Handel, The Electronic
Revolution, London. 196 7)

===== PAGE 829 =====

812
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Automisation is the key to space. Guidance, computers, altitude control, power sources, telecommunciations, instruments and structu ral sub-assemblies are all subject to unprecedented standards of extreme lightness, high efficiency, rigourous precision and reliability.
These missions are conducted automatically from take-off, the pilots, if any, having the option of taking control. Of course, the large majority of these spacecrafts navigate in space without pilots.
Everything within these crafts therefore, must be automised: the positioning of the television cameras and of the photoelectric cell plates providing the energy, and the launching of all operations. The real brain of satellites is on earth.
Transport The field of transport is currently undergoing a real revolution, as important, if not more so, than all preceding ones. New tecchniques and old ones which have been completely transformed are all investing transport with an entirely new image and structure. It is true that some tendencies were already manifest, but we are not unjustified in saying that the changes since the Second World War have been quite drastic.
The power of cars and lorries as against traditional forms of transport was well-known. The Pan Am company made the first commercial crossing of the North Atlantic between Marseille and Washington on the 28th June 1939, with a giant hydroplane, the Boeing 314 Dixie
Clipper, which carried 22 passengers and 12 crew members.
Two primary factors made progress necessary: quantity and speed.
The expansion of goods trading and the increased mobility of people hardly need explanation. And the same is true of the concern with speed, one of the fundamental hallmarks of our modern civilization.
And that goes for traditional modes of transport such as railways and ships as well as for the more recent areas of techniques like the plane.
There is one point which should be emphasised: the evolution of transport necessarily entailed a major transformation of infrastruc tures: but here the slow progress of certain techniques caused both oounting costs .and delays in execution. It is curious to note that the number of railways being built during the Second Empire was far greater than the number of motorways being built today.
Ships have certainly changed a great deal, but it seems now that naval technology has reached its saturation point. In any case, the

===== PAGE 830 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
813 current tendencies are clearly marked. The regular passenger services have almost completely been replaced by the airlines. It is therefore the merchant ship which attracts the technician's attention, and this from several angles.
Firstly, the types of transporters have grown more and more spe cialised. Ore tankers and liquified gas tankers (fig. 17), and banana boats have been added to the older petrol tankers, etc. In 1954 in the
United States, Malcolm Mac Lean ran articulated lorries between New York and Houston, crossing eight states whose road regulations were all different. In order to avoid this problem, he hit on the idea of carrying the trailers on adapted petrol tankers. The container ship was born. In the mid-sixties the system was adopted in Europe bringing with it important savings in handling, and thereby generating more sea-traffic. The time taken to load a ship weighing 10,000 tonnes fell from seven days to fifteen hours. From this point onwards, 80 to 90% of the goods transported between Europe and the United States were carried in containers, representing 61 % of New York harbour's traffic, and 23% of Le Havre's. The first ships travelled at a speed of 20 knots and carried 700 to 1000 containers. The third generation travels at a speed of 26 knots and they carry 2800 containers.
It is through partial and numerous developments, and by the use of more suitable materials that it has been possible to supercede limits which were originally thought to be insurmountable. The reduction in weight and bulk of the unit of installed power, and the reduction of the weight of the hull meant that the size of ships became almost limitless:
some oil tankers for example weigh more than 500,000 tonnes. It seems however that ships are no longer being built as large as they could be, partly because of the dangers of pollution in the event of the loss of a ship, and partly because of the blocking of certain passages due to the extension of territorial waters. The re-opening of the Suez
Canal has generated a rise of medium-sized tankers, weighing only 150,000 tonnes.
Despite the fact that the protection required for a nuclear engine makes it bulky and heavy, it is nevertheless admirably suited for powering ships. It has one principle advantage, which is that the refuelling is much less frequent. The attraction of autonomy and an almost unlimited field of action tempted the naval services to adopt this form of power, particularly in nuclear submarines. Half-way through 1975, some two hundred and fifty nuclear-powered ships were

===== PAGE 831 =====

2 9 3 m e t r e s Figure 17. - The independent spherical tank system used on the liquified natural gas carrier of Gotaas-Larsen longitudinal section.

===== PAGE 832 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
815 counted, (three of which were soviet ice-breakers). The problem is quite different for merchant ships: a nuclear reactor is only profitable for very fast or very large ships which consume large amounts of power. It was calculated in 1975 that a reactor was only economically competitive with ships capable of at least 45,000 horsepower, and in some cases this figure was as high as 60,000 to 90,000 depending on the price of petrol. This would be equivalent to petroleum tankers of
250,000 to 500,000 dead weight, travelling at speeds of 16 knots, or to container ships of at least 50,000 tonnes dead weight, travelling at a speed of 27 knots. The first nuclear powered ships, the American
Savannah and the German Otto Hahn did not produce any significant results. And most people are familiar with the problems encountered by the Japanese. People's general dislike of using nuclear apparatuses, and the decreasing consumption of petrol have compounded this already difficult problem. The transformation in this domain can only be envisaged as a long term one.
It seems that in the last few years, the railways have been in decline, unable to compete with cars and planes. In almost all countries, passenger routes are being closed, and in the United States the number of towns no longer served by railways continues to grow. In France, from August 1934 to the 1st April, 1968, the length of railway lines closed to passenger traffic went up to 13,475 kilometres. The same is currently true for the traffic of merchandise, and in 1971 in France, more freight was carried by road than by rail. At this time the total bulk of traffic was shared between the different modes of transport according to the following proportions: roads 38. 7% ; rail 37% ; pipe line 17.3% ; waterways 6.9% . And of course more and more freight is being carried in planes.
The evolution of techniques still leaves the railways some hope, however. Some of these techniques have yet to fulfil their potential, like the hovertrain for example, which can travel at 400 kmh. but which has yet to find a market and an engine. Progress has been much more obvious in other areas. The electric Diesel train, developed in 1924, has definitively replaced the steam engine in the most advanced countries since the Second World War. Electrification is also making great headway, and with ever higher voltages. On the 25th April, 1967, the French national railway company, SNCF for the first time, ran a turbotrain which is a self-propelling element whose propulsion is assured by a gas turbine originally developed for the aeronautical

===== PAGE 833 =====

816
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
industry. In this way, under independent traction a rapid and light vehicle was created which was capable of sustaining a much higher average speed thanks to its strong mechanical thrust. (a speed of 240 kmh. was obtained on the 21 February 1969; [Transl. Note: however, early in 1975, for economic reasons the SNCF decided to replace the gas turbine with a 6400 kW electrical group, which propels the present high speed train, the T. G. V. ] fig. 18).
But over average distances, i.e. , those distances for which the railways could reappropriate those passengers lost to road and sea traffic, these speeds have required new railway lines. There are several examples of this. The first express line was built between Florence and
Rome, with impressive results. The most spectacular line was the one built in Japan over a distance of 1000 kilometres. Several constructive works were necessary, 55% of the line is a tunnel, there are no level crossings. At first, when the line was not fully completed, 30 trains ran on it each way. Today, there is a flotilla of 2000 trains, 129 of which run
Figure 18. - High speed train T.G.V. (From an SNCF document)

===== PAGE 834 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
817 both ways each day, carrying 560,000 passengers daily. This line has obviously been very beneficial. The final example is that of the line between Paris and Lyon. The speed reached by the T. G. V. is 260 kmh.
This advanced project has incurred the ecologists' wrath, however, (it means encroaching on conservation areas, opening up 1 10 cuttings, and more noise etc).
On the other hand, less soil is needed for this than for a motorway, and it would no doubt have been necessary to enlarge existing roadways. Furthermore, it dispenses with the need for new airports.
The rise of the aviation industry is a widespread concern. There have been major increases in speed and the capacity to carry passengers and freight. We shall limit our discussion of engines to a couple of points. As with the car, the classic engine with pistons was part of a saturated technique in that beyond a speed of 500 kmh. the classic engine and its propeller gradually become less and less efficient. The output of the propeller falls to 75% at 550 kmh. , and to
42% at 1000 kmh. Air friction heats the frame and makes ventilation impossible. The highest possible speed with this engine was 750 kmh. , achieved by a German in 1939. History has clearly shown that it was military requirements which led to the various solutions, and that even if the principles were understood, there were still refined devel opments to be made on the technical side. Aviation was given a new dimension with the turbopropeller and the turbojet from which the commercial industry was to reap many benefits. It was now possible to travel at faster speeds, go for longer distances without refuelling and carry heavier loads. As well as all these advantages, the costs were getting lower and lower.
Lindberg took thirty three hours, twenty-nine minutes and thirty seconds to travel alone across the North Atlantic. In 1946, it took an
Air France DC-4 carrying 24 passengers twenty four hours and forty five minutes to make the same crossing, with two stops in Iceland and
Newfoundland. In February 1960, the four-engined jet Boeing 707 carrying 144 passengers crossed the Atlantic in seven and a half hours.
It took the Boeing 747 approximately the same time in 1970, but with 357 passengers. On the 26 September, 1973, Concorde linked Paris to
Washington in three hours and thirty three minutes. These figures are sufficient to indicate the type of progress achieved.
Over long distances, the plane certainly comes out on top over other forms of transport. The number of passengers crossing the North

===== PAGE 835 =====

818
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Atlantic by air in 1963 was 2,956,000; in 1969 it was 8,452,000; and by 1972 it had reached 13,040,000.
The proportion of those preferring to go by sea were as follows:
21.5% in 1963, 3.8% in 1969, and 0.8% in 1972.
The advantages of travelling by plane diminish as the distances get shorter. It is more expensive and airports are invariably more awkward to get to than railway stations. The new line between Paris and Lyon has shortened the distance time to two hours, which is on the way to competing with travelling by air. It is very difficult to establish the boundaries here, because assessing journeys simply in terms of time is not sufficient to assess the advantages and inconveniences. Going on a business trip is not the same thing as going away on holiday. Traffic jams on roads and long waits in airports (in France, a third of annual traffic takes place in three months, a third of weekly traffic takes place in three days) are persuading people who are tired of waiting to start using the railways again, especially now that these have been vastly improved (some trains now have sleepers and carry cars as well for example).
During the winter of 1948-49, about a hundred American DC-3's dispatched some 150,000 tonnes of freight to the isolated city of Berlin over 22,000 trips. This was a major first for air freight transport. From
1945 to 1967, the volume of air freight transport quadrupled. In 1966, the weight of goods transported over the North Atlantic by cargo planes exceeded the weight of passengers carried in airliners. And we should note that this freight consisted largely of small parcels. In the spring of 1972, Boeing 747 cargo planes were put into operation on the line between Frankfurt and New York. These planes carried at least
100 tonnes of freight, and they are also container planes. It is none theless true that most merchandise is still transported by sea, however.
It seems that the plane still has several difficult obstacles to surmount with regard to this. We are still in a relatively new field, therefore.
We are going to finish with two completely antithetical examples.
Urban transport is still one of the blockages of contemporary tech niques. In truth, solutions to this problem are hard to find, and the usual method is to apply measures of constraint (restricted parking, traffic lanes for different vehicles, car parks outside towns, etc).
There has been shortage of research or ideas, however. Usually, these are concerned with automatic systems of urban transport, consis ting of comfortable, fast vehicles, the size of which varied between that

===== PAGE 836 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
819 of an ordinary car and a bus, running on clean tracks and operating automatically with no drivers. Hundreds of such systems have been dreamed up since about 1969; a dozen or so have been developed and two are already in operation in the United States, not without growing pains however. The automatic underground system in San Francisco, the BART (Bay Area Rapid Transit), is around 130 kilometres long and passes through 34 stations. The system in use at Dallas-Fort Worth airport is based on the same techniques. On the other hand, the system in Morgantown (West Virginia) which was originally to link several university buildings, uses smaller vehicles. At first these carried four to six passengers, but the places now number from twelve to twenty.
Plans are currently being made to demolish existing works on this project, however. In France, research is being conducted into a system of small automatic vehicles (Aramis) and the automatic metro in Lille.
In fact, this research has brought about the modernisation and automi sation of older forms of transport, in the same way as the hovertrain prompted the rise of the the turbotrain.
The second example is infinitely more spectacular: transport in space. From the launching of Sputnik the first inhabited satellite, in
1957, to man's landing on the moon in 1969 and the even more distant explorations of today, progress has been steady and rapid.
It is not difficult to see that such enterprises require the coherence of a large number of very advanced techniques: materials with very specific heat and mechanical qualities, the rockets and fuel, computers for calculating the trajectories, televisions for the transmission of visual data, automation techniques and an infinity of details, each one as important as the other. Liquid propellents are generally used for the launch stages and the correction of the flight path. Solid propellents are used during the stages requiring less power. At the moment, research is being conducted into nuclear-powered engines derived from the Nerva model experiments in the United States. The essential element is the launch thrust. The Saturn 5 rocket used in the Apollo lunar mission had a thrust of 3400 tonnes, 140 times greater than the earlier german V2's. " The integration of the various stages of a rocket takes place in a special compartment situated at the tip of the final section. The equipment concentrated in this compartment maintains the coherence of the ensemble from firing until the separation of the various stages and the successive firing of the latter. Fifty-five cameras are placed in a ring 90 cm high and 2.65 metres in diameter, weighing

===== PAGE 837 =====

820
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
2 tonnes. It is here that the guidance system operates, that the problems of navigation are resolved, that the current source is to be found, and all the equipment necessary for protection against the surrounding environment and against drops in temperature.
The live load is composed of the satellite or of a special harness which must be led to a precise point in space. The vessel Apollo consisted of, starting from the top, the command module, (the manned
Apollo capsule), the service module (the propulsion system to assure the movement of the ensemble in space), and the lunar module. The whole of this load represented about 45 tonnes. Thus is it that man, although incapable of creating an adequate system of urban traffic, is able to navigate in space and arrive on the moon.
The Transmission of Thought The effect of printing, telegraphy, and the telephone and radio on our lives is of course fundamental, and in the second half of the century, all these forms of communication underwent several transformations.
Their importance in the world of techniques is widely acknowledged, probably because everyone is directly affected by them.
There are no doubt some sectors within which procedures may have been perfected, but they have kept their basic overall structure. This was so of printing, photography, the radio and telephone, and tele graphy. Their basic principles remained the same. Apart from a few major innovations which we shall discuss below, it seems that it was mainly existing techniques which were developed, although they did not reach saturation point. This phenomenon is also apparent in earlier periods. Coherence can be maintained between fundamental transformations and potential development.
Photography is a good example of this. Of course it is clear that the cumbersome, heavy concertina camera of 1920 was nothing like the slim compact camera of 1976. But apart from the reduction in size of both the camera and the film, there were many other changes: in winding on the film, in the automatic control of distance and aperture, etc. The only innovatory camera was the polaroia, with instantaneous developing, based on a principle discovered by Edwin H. Land in
1909, which was developed in 1947 and finally perfected in 1961. There was steady progress made in the type of film and paper used, and colour film was developed in 1935. However it was only in 1942 that

===== PAGE 838 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
821 these colours could also be printed on paper. In a sense, apart from these major elements of progress, the principal transformation was the creation of the modem camera made of moulded plastic; sometimes even the lens was made of plastic as well. The latest research has been concentrated on long distance transmission photographs, by the trans formation of variations in luminance into variations in electric current.
By using satellites, photographs can now cross space with perfect results. This is a far cry from the first apparatus produced by Belin
(1907).
Printing underwent much the same form of development. The principles remained the same, the machines were widely perfected.
But here again, there have been innovations which have still not been fully exploited. One cannot over-estimate the importance of the photocopier in administration. Readers are no doubt familiar with the progress made in the use of offset for printing, and reprinting ancient works. Telecopying was first thought of very soon after telegraphy - in
1860 it was already possible to reproduce a signature or picture at a distance. Some people believe this is the only fo"rm of telegraphy possible for those civilizations which use ideograms, like the Chinese, for example. There are some telecopiers of a limited size in existence today. Of course they all require a transmitter, and a receiver but this process could be made more automatic. If the apparatus could be equipped with an answering machine linked to the telecopier receiver, this would dispense with the need for any person to do the work. This form of communication is still in its research and development stages.
The use of electronics has profoundly changed newspaper editing (fig. 19). The editor, seated in front of a visualisation console similar to a computer terminal, types the text out using the keyboard. One by one the letters appear on the screen. By moving the cursor around with special controls, they can modify the text until they are completely satisfied with it. When they have finished, the computer tells them the exact length of the text. If it is too long, the editor can remove an entire part of the text, or a sentence or a few words. The remainder of the text automatically fills up the gaps. After having keyed in typographical instructions, the editor can then justify the text. The computer indi cates the exact size of the text, indexes it, and then stores it. If some new elements need to be added, and the making up has still not been done, the editor can bring the article up onto the screen and edit once more. The sub-editors responsible for the making up check the articles

===== PAGE 839 =====

822
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
on file as they go along. If for any reason they wish to change either the typeface or the justification, all they need to do is change the infor mation at the top of the text, and the computer will alter the latter accordingly. The make-up is also done by computer. It transmits the article electronically to a photocompositor which produces an offset plaque, for example, which is then ready for use on the rotary press.
Absolutely no paper has been used until this stage, except of course at the proof stage. Progress is advancing extremely rapidly in this field.

Figure 1 9. - The Electronic Newspaper. (From Le Monde)

===== PAGE 840 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
823 Forty-five per cent of the sixteen thousand American dailies now use word processing for printing and editing, and this has meant that the content and style of newspapers has profoundly changed.
We shall not dwell at length on the telephone and telegraphy. Once again, the principles did not change, but the apparatuses and instal lations were profoundly tranformed. Automatic telephones and the development of automised exchanges represented considerable pro gress. The main development of the telephone was the change from electro-mechanical switchboards to electronical ones. In France, this movement began only in 1960. American companies had already adopted electronic switchboards based on the principle of spatial commutation. This was a classic mode of transmission, the variations of the human voice being conveyed by variations in the intensity of the current. France adopted temporal commutation whereby the variations of the voice are measured and coded .in binary. Several conversations can therefore take place on the same pair of lines connected to an exchange. In the first case, the exchange connects two callers with mobile magnetic contacts. The latter are not used in the second type of transmission. In both cases, computers control the connections and they can also inform us of the state of the lines and junctions at all times, as well as all the signals coming into the exchange. The exchange has now become a complex data processing system, and the main difficulty today is to perfect the programming.
Modem exchanges can cater for up to 50 to 65,000 subscribers. There is also another problem, that of the connection with the old network.
The number of radio cables over large distances, particularly over seas, had doubled by this time, and they were now relayed by means of telecommunications satellites. These are able to relay radio signals across the world, and are thus extremely useful for the transmission of both the spoken word and the printed image. High frequency hertzian signals have an optical range: this is therefore limited by the curvature of the earth to around 150 km. But since these signals can be retrans mitted by satellites orbiting 50,000 km. above the earth, they can actually cover distances of up to 100,000 km. There are passive satellites, which simply retransmit signals, or satellites with a recording mechanism. The first telecommunication satellites were disappoint ing, from the Score (Signal Communication Orbit Relay Experiment), released in December 1958, to the Echos. The Te/star group were the first successful satellites to be used. Te/star 1 , which was launched in

===== PAGE 841 =====

824
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
July 1962, was a bowl measuring 87 cm in diameter, and weighing 77 kilograms. It has 72 facets, 60 of which are equipped with 3600 photo-electric cells for constant recharging. Three other facets carry mirrors for optical location. It contains 1064 transistors, 1464 diodes and 2528 semi-conductors. Giant antennae receive and transmit signals. It orbits between 953 and 5637 km above the earth, and revolves on its own axis three times per second, It can be used 9 times a day for 40 minutes. Te/star 2 was launched in May 1963, and works at between approximately 1000 and 10,000 km. The third generation of satellites, begun in 1965, was the Intelsats (INternational TELecom munications SATellites). The Intelsats 2, launched in 1966 and 1967 weighed 168 kg and had 240 telephonic channels. The Intelsats 3 were launched in 1968 and 1970 - these weighed 287 kg, and had 1200 telephonic channels. The group of Intelsats 4 was launched from 1971, and these weighed 1312 kg, and were equipped with 6000 telephonic channels. The entire telecommunications system had thus been completely transformed. Furthermore, one country managed to create a monopoly of this new system of communication. The French and the
Germans may have succeeded in launching one small communications satellite, but it was the U.S.A. which put the operation into practise.
We shall discuss this problem in Part 3 of this book.
There is still a great deal more to be gained from these techniques, none of which has yet fulfilled its potential. Current research is concentrating on direct broadcasting satellites, which will no longer require relaying, like the one located at Pleumeur-Bodou in France.
Ordinary television sets could therefore receive programmes directly.
One could hardly deny that the television, along with the computer and atomic energy, is one of the symbols of the new technical system.
Its history is already a fairly long one, but the definitive improvements and its sudden widespread expansion have in fact taken place only in the last thirty years. In 1877, Constantin Senlecq from France, elabo rated a quite feasible method of television. The first experimental television programme was broadcast in Britain by the B.B.C. in 1929, thanks to Baird's pioneering system. The wave-length was 261 metres, and they produced twelve images per second. The invention of the iconoscope and the dissector tube was one more important step. In
November 1936, the B.B.C. established the first official television studios, and a television receiver at one of the highest points in
London. In the same year, the American company R. C.A. , did exactly

===== PAGE 842 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
825 the same on the Empire State building. Most of the basic technical problems concerned with transmitting television pictures had been resolved by 1940. All that remained was to perfect these techniques.
The war interrupted this research, but it did encourage intense research in the field of radar, for example, which indirectly, was to bring about major improvements.
By the end of the Second World War, then, television was not a new concept, it was simply being developed and expanded. It expanded at a much greater pace than cinema, with which technically, it is closely linked. Colour television was the next step, and one wonders how soon it will be before we have television in 3-d. Furthermore, we need hardly point out the primary importance of television, not only as an instrument of information and cultural diffusion, but in many other fields as well, such as transport or industry.
Landscapes and Lifestyles The new technical revolution is radically transforming the physical world and all aspects of daily life, but no definitive statements can be made about these effects as yet, because this technical revolution is still not complete. Consequently, there is a lack of equilibrium both in the use of space, and in the organisation of daily life.
This profound transformation in the way of life is not always perceived to its full extent. No doubt, for example, it would be relatively easy to re-build a kitchen as it would have appeared, say, in
1929, with all the everyday household objects in use at that time. Such a collection would no doubt surprise many people, and not only those who were born after that year. It is likely that many people who do still remember this period would also be surprised by the objects they used to use and which they have probably completely forgotten. Some of these are no longer made, while others are rarely used: the pestle and mortar for making pureed potatoes, the antique coffee maker in two sections, the galvanised iron wash bowl, and the mushroom boiler for laundry. Then there are the old heavy sewing machines on cast iron supports and worked with treadles which made Singer so famous, or the magneto telephone for calling the switchboard, or of course the phonograph which also belongs in this category. Manufacturing catalogues, or those of major department stores are extremely useful for measuring the passage from one technical system to another in the context of everyday life.

===== PAGE 843 =====

826
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
After the Second World War, all these individual, everyday objects were replaced. Some objects or processes date back to the inter-war period, but nonetheless the post-war revolution was quite brutal - in the industrialised countries of course. Plastic, miniaturised electric motors, and even electronics all completely changed the nature of pre-war household equipment. Furthermore, the perfection of certain techniques and the use of new materials lowered prices and made these objects more readily available to the whole population. The more expansive use of heat sources, such as gas and electricity meant that hot water was now supplied straight to the sink. This meant that the old galvanised iron washbowl gave way to a totally new type of bowl made of plastic. The washing machine was introduced around this time, and in the fifties, the vegetable mill was devised. A further important development was the programming of washing machines and cookers.
The number of different types of household appliances also increased.
The following is a list of household appliances currently in use. The information is taken from the catalogue of a large trading firm:
coffee grinder (some are adjustable);
electric percolators;
toaster (some automatic);
electric mixer;
electric whisk;
citrus fruit squeezer;
fruit juice or vegetable centrifuge;
multi-purpose mixers;
electric slicing machine;
electric knife;
peeler;
combined tin-opener and knife sharpener;
plate warmer with thermostat;
hot-plate;
table tidy;
ice cream maker;
kettle;
immersion heaters;
electric boiling plates;
meat grill;
sterilizer;
electric deep fryer.

===== PAGE 844 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
827 The same was true of other types of objects: the portable transistor radio was developed, which replaced the cumbersome, stationary lamp radio. The biro was invented - which has not only destroyed the classical ink pen, but also the metal nib and lead pencil. The pocket calculator was also invented. The entire system of communications between people was profoundly changed.
It is noteworthy that the disparities between countries became much more marked at this time. J. Fourastie has demonstrated this with some telling statistics: in 1950, a small washing machine with no heating apparatus cost 35,000 francs in France, the equivalent of 500 hours of a cleaner's salary. In the United States it cost 50 dollars, or 50 hours of cleaner's salary, and in Egypt and India, the cost of such a machine was equal to 3 to 5000 hours of cleaner's salary. Since this time, in industrialised countries at least, prices have dropped consider ably. The same type of washing machine in France in 1962 cost the equivalent of 230 hourly salaries. In 1950, a 200 litre capacity refriger ator cost the equivalent of 1000 hourly salaries, by 1962 this figure had dropped to 420.
As J. Baudrillard wrote, "in an advanced industrial society, manufacturing products are not only different to what they were in the past, they also fulfil another function. " Social practises concer ning these objects, as well as the mythologies based upon them have been transformed. Giedion has provided us with a brief history of furniture, not of its style, but of its main functions. We lack many other such histories which would be invaluable to help us assess this progress.
The world outside the home was changing in similar ways, if not to the same degree. Working in offices had changed drastically since the
1930's, for example, but public transport had retained most of its inconveniences. Having a television enabled people to stay in all night, and both the television and the radio dispensed with the need to buy newspapers, or even books. On the other hand, the car, of which the cost was getting lower and lower as technology progressed, meant that people could now escape from an urban nightmare to the tranquility of the countryside.
The evolution of landscape - or to put it more generally, the organisation of space - was even more profound. In effect, distances have become much shorter. A map of equidistances would be extremely useful in this sense. It can take up to three or four hours to

===== PAGE 845 =====

828
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
cross Paris on a Friday afternoon, and the same time to go from Paris to New York. There are many, many more examples like this. It is getting more difficult to travel shorter distances, while the time taken over longer distances is constantly decreasing. The expansion of agricultu ral machinery is emptying the country of its inhabitants, and transfor ming property by the regrouping of lands. Industrial areas are not at all what they were fifty years ago. There have been changes in two major aspects. The first of these is the concentration of manufacturing areas.
The optimum productivity of a factory in relation to the highest returns seems to be closely linked to technical conditions. Steel production in one factory before the war was between 500,000 and 1 million tonnes.
In the early post-war period, this had risen to between 1 and 1.5 million. By 1960 this figure was 2 million, and according to most estimations, by 1968, 5.5 million. In 1970, production was at 8 million tonnes per annum, and this rose to 20 million in the next ten years. The
Soviet Union, the United States and Japan all developed approxi mately in the same way (fig. 20). The same progress is apparent in chemical industry. The enormous size of factories stems from technical progress which was best served by integrations.
The second point is no less spectacular in its manifestations. Not only had the units of production been increased, but they had also been displaced. This stemmed as much from the location of sources of raw materials as from technical progress. This was especially so after coal was no longer used, the transportation of which had been very expensive. Factories were no longer automatically built near coal fields, but closer to areas where raw materials were cheaper and more accessible. Take the iron and steel industry, for example. After several experiments, some of which date back to the Second Empire, this industry has now found its definitive place by the sea. This process began after 1950. The chemical industries installed themselves close to sources of petrol, and many such factories are close to refineries. The transport of petrol by pipelines has made the choice of location a much wider one, however.
It is clear that many of the balances of the technical system have been broken, and that it is difficult to restore such balances. This concerns peoples, individual daily lives as well as the industrial world.
Many people have dealt with these problems, and a few solutions have been found. There is still much to be done, however.
