TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
FEAR AND ANXIETY
The extent and rapidity of technical progress has created a sense of disquiet which is becoming more and more pervasive: the content of newpapers, television programmes and everyday conversations is clear evidence of this. There are various types of fear and opposition to technical progress. Between these often badly expressed sentiments, and the need for technical progress and all its advantages, is a sort of internal contradiction which has become a major element in our civilization. The continued growth of populations requires new technology, but these same populations are beginning to be character ised by a desire to escape from the noise and pollution of towns and machines, and to recapture nature, which represents the very negation of advanced techniques. The same people who curse new technology are those who are waiting for the latest model of a certain car to come on the market, or who could not possibly do without their gas central heating, or even more important, their television set.
There seems to be two types of opposition to technical progress, which in fact are not simply characteristic of this period. We have already cited some much older examples of the same attitudes. The first is concerned with the qualities of products produced by advanced techniques. When marketing products today, the manufacturer is obliged to include certain key phrases on the packaging and in advertising: "traditionally made," "made in the old fashioned way," etc. This is true with regard not only to food products, about which the public is particularly sensitive, but also of house-building and many other activities, which curiously enough, are not the areas where techniques have made the most progress. There is no question of abandoning the automatic turntable for the old phonograph, however.
The current fashion for handmade products also stems from the same attitude. This can be explained by the lack of industrial aestheticism:
an eggcup made of turned olive wood is much more attractive to look at than the rather odd-looking designs made of plastic painted with washed-out colours. And take for example the enamelled iron washbowl we mentioned above which was replaced by bowls made of plastic, when it became possible to supply hot water directly to the sink. These bowls can now be found in the shops once more, with a label prominently displaying "made entirely by hand." There is no

===== PAGE 848 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
831 need to cite any more examples of this, they can be found on the labels of all sorts of products.
In a very general sense, this attitude is linked to another, which concerns the quality of life - to use a common expression - and the fact that it is seriously deteriorating, largely due to technical progress. The latter is responsible in that it causes the expansion of industrialisation and the proliferation of harmful products. In part, this probably represents our version of the myth of the golden age. This attitude goes beyond the mere factors of environmental nuisances, which include pollution, and insofar as it is not based on concrete grievances, it is somewhat vague and ill-defined. It is supposed that there is a fundamental contradiction between technical progress and peoples' happiness. In a sense, technical progress is condemned because it represents a distancing from nature, because the artifical is assuming the place of the natural, and this is all the more important because the differences between them are sometime far less clear. The opposition to technical progress is based on other factors as well. We have already stated that there is a close relationship between tecl;mical and social structures, such that technical progress inevitably brings with it the evolution of society. Some see these changes in society - or from a more global point of view perhaps, the changes in social values that are implied by technical progress - as being the result of a one-way cause and effect relationship between techniques and society. and they believe that technical progress can have only a detrimental effect.
Both these attitudes question the value of technical progress, and much more, they actually warn us of its dangers. There is an anti scientific attitude which has been the subject of specific studies, and an anti-technical one. According to an enquiry held in 1972 by the
Delegation generale a la recherche scientifique et technique in France, the latter is more prevalent than the former. In the scale of social utility, the scientific researcher has a privileged place just below that of the doctor (which reveals an unconcious egoism since the doctor is more directly useful to oneself), but above that of the engineer. This enquiry revealed a bipolarisation of attitudes towards science which is considered implicitly as a major source of technical progress.
The person in the street tends to associate science and techniques with political, social and economic power. This vision of things means that there is less trust in those involved in scientific research when the latter are identified with authority, and as a result they experience the same distancing and mistrust as those in political authority.

===== PAGE 849 =====

832
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The same attitude is held by people who are highly trained, but in fields other than sciences, such as law or literature for example. Those who have had a scientific training, on the other hand, think the opposite: "science is a world quite distinct from politics."
Apart from these general fears, there are other, more precise con cerns. They are partial in the sense that they do not condemn technical progress out of hand. We shall consider a few precise examples.
The fear of nuclear power is now a familiar one. It has been propagated by campaigns in the U.S.A. and Europe for many years now. Every argument has been made against the harmful effects of nuclear radiation from nuclear power plants. But this of course is not the only side of the question: everyone knows that the idea of nuclear war must be considered; it cannot be ignored. In the event of a leak in a nuclear plant, everthing within a ten kilometre radius would be affected, from new-born babies, to fish and vegetation: asparagus would turn red - the colour of danger. A survey conducted in the area surrounding the nuclear plant at Chinon in France, revealed evidence of wild speculation, obvious or badly concealed panic, unverifiable attitudes, and organised protestation. The authors of this report concluded that fears of the bomb had been directed towards nuclear plants, and they also noticed that protests against the nuclear bombs were less violent and more sporadic than those against the peaceful utilisation of nuclear power. Fear of radiation is just as great as that of nuclear explosions. Even the clearest explanations about the plants' safety are plagued by the aura of mystery which surrounds all nuclear plants' operations. The real problem is not these plants, but the bomb, and beyond this, the political power of citizens and peoples.
Other surveys revealed that this fear was not quite so general as some had made out. One questionnaire carried the following question, with the corresponding distribution of answers: If the construction of a nuclear power station for the production of electricity was being considered, and if scientific experts affirmed that there would be no danger, would you:
trustthemcompletely? . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 19
58% tendtotrustthem? . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 39 tend not to trust them? . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 22
37% not trust them at all? . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 15 noopinionexpressed . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
5%

===== PAGE 850 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
833 What can we conclude from all this? The issue is a concrete one, nuclear power plants do exist. However, the mistrust and defiance, and the fear and anxiety are not so clear cut. They even become linked with other issues, which are quite different, at least superficially so in a technical sense. Thus, the fear and opposition to new techniques have been transformed into much vaguer notions of political power, and indeed, it is true that in some areas, techniques can give an enormous amount of power to the sectors which control it. Some countries are very much aware of this, and they have acted in a new spirit of freedom
- which goes beyond traditional criteria - and created what are now called balancing powers. The role of these arbiters - which were first established in the northern countries - in our rigid technical administ rations is becoming more and more important. Unfortunately, no-one has yet thought of writing a history of freedom, or of describing the evolution of this notion.
The solutions are not easy to find, or to apply. Take for example, the effect of television on a population where visual media have become more important than audio ones. In most cases, this instrument of unbounded power over peoples' minds is in the hands of governments.
There are some cases, however, where the problem could possibly be solved. In the United States, television is not controlled by the government, but it does therefore depend on private interests, and inevitably it has fallen into the same mould as the press. Commercial viability is the most important factor, to the detriment of culture, or even an honest information service. This has reached the stage where people are now attempting to start a channel which is completely independent, subsidised only by large foundations. In Britain, there is a fairly satisfactory balance between the political power, which funds one of the two main television companies, and the company itself, which is conscious of its duty and the importance of public opinion.
Here again, technical progress is clearly linked to structures which have still yet to be clearly defined.
"When industrialisation enabled every home to have a television," wrote a scientist recently, "one could see that society was no longer the same." And he added:
In spite of this disquieting power, television was installed in an experimental fashion, subject to tradition and circumstance ( . . ), television became a new tool in the arsenal of the executive power. It was neccessary to define the objectives before creating the structures. People today watch more and more television, and the televised image has

===== PAGE 851 =====

834
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
taken over from other cultural medium except in certain rare cases which we can link with the opposition to technical progress that we mentioned above. Some people believe that this "intellectual elite," which was able to propagate its thought by now outdated media, simply saw its monopoly pass into a new set of hands. And to its horror this new set of hands belonged to those in political power. Furthermore, people were trading in their freedom for comfort, solutions and certainty.
There is another field of techniques which generates the same type of fear and anxiety. A recent debate added fuel to these by bringing to light certain facts which had hitherto been unknown by the general public. According to Pierre Laroque:
In the past, machines have affected only the production, transmission and distribution of goods and services. It has not affected the exercising of political power or the freedom of individuals (even though this does require a certain amount of qualification). The emergence and development of computers is creating an entirely new situation, in that information can be collected and stored which concerns all aspects of public and private life, and collective and individual activities.
"Information, on this collective level, is an instrument of power." This is apparent at all levels: the power of a university, a firm, or a state, depends largely on the number of its computers, and their capacity. The embargo placed on the exportation of certain types of computers in the U.S.A. is clear evidence of this. A lot of capital is required to build as big an information system as possible, except for business computers. So this becomes a political problem. In order to prevent monopolies forming, some countries have nationalised the press agencies: the same question is being posed about public access to these information systems, and therefore to computers.
But there is more. "The computer enables information to be collec ted concerning all aspects of an individual's life, placing more and more restrictions every day on the autonomy and privacy of the latter." The computer, by means of this diverse collection of somewhat vague and unverifiable data, enables authorities to "synthesise social controls, and with all the infallibility which is attached to machines, it imposes a perpetual conformism." The system may enable one to cross-check, and thereby prevent the falsifying of information, but it encloses the individuals within their past, and within a past which is stereotyped by the language of programming. Ultimately, "the techni cal, economic and financial aspects of information systems is a legal and political problem, a problem of the exercising of power, and in particular, in the control of the latter." Once again, we find ourselves

===== PAGE 852 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
835 back with the problem of institutional structures whose evolution is necessarily linked to technical progress. The basis of most fears is wondering which side will win.
Of course the issue of individual freedom is paramount, but there are other questions of undeniable importance which are more closely connected to everyday material life. These are situated on various levels, sectional or general.
The replacement of people by machines is an obsessive fear which has existed before our time. The luddite movement which began in the first half of the nineteenth century in Britain, was a violent manifes tation of such fears held by the working classes. In the second half of the century, it seemed that this fear had abated somewhat. The enormous increase in production in contrast to the lesser development of mechanisation, and the emergence of relatively efficient compensa tory powers were probably the reasons for this. But during the inter-war period, and in particular after the Second World War, the acceleration not only of automatism, but of automation revived this opposition and disquiet. There were many ambiguities in this area of activity, and this might explain the sometimes contradictory positions assumed by successive groups. Automation unquestionably reduced the number of jobs in those areas where it was applied, or more accurately, perhaps, it transformed the distribution of tasks. After the
Second World War, American unions were extremely concerned about the effects of automation, particularly in the automobile industry. The British unions soon followed suit in opposing such moves. However, they were soon appeased by the fact that the number of those made redundant was not as high as they had thought.
However, there was, and still is an awkward transition to overcome.
It is true that the umemployment caused by automation is being compensated for by the expansion of the mechanical and electronic sectors. But any transfer of a workforce from one industry to another is an extremely delicate operation. It is difficult to turn a mechanician into an electronic engineer, especially beyond a certain age. In a study of labour at the Renault factories, it was shown that many of the trades no longer existed (there were far fewer joiners for example) but that others were emerging according to the development of new professio nal sectors.
Technical progress is completely transforming professional struc tures, and consequently, a major part of society. Some professions are

===== PAGE 853 =====

836
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
becoming obsolete, while others are emerging, but as well as this, the different types of trade are becoming more and more simplified, and the hierarchy of these trades is becoming more marked. Intermediary trades are tending to disappear, such that we seem to be progressing towards a kind of professional polarisation: there is the unskilled worker on the one hand, and the technicians on the other, who have been properly trained and therefore know everything about the sector in which they work. There is thus a polarisation in that the former cannot progress beyond a certain level, while the latter is obliged to seek ever higher levels of achievement.
The concern about the constraints imposed on social structures by technical progress is understandable. Or course, one could say that these sorts of constraints have always existed during such periods of adaptation. But insofar as progress is constant if of variable intensity, the problem is no less worrying. This permanent questioning of social and heriarchical structures cannot provide even the minimum of security to societies where change is so difficult.
The most potent image used against technical progress is that of pollution. It is no longer simply the freedom of individuals and the organisation of societies which is affected by technical progress and industrial growth, but life itself and the whole material environment which supports this life. Of course, pollution has existed - under various names - for a very long time. During the First Empire, it was the object of special legislation concerned with establishments which were considered a potential hazard to public health. This legislation advocated holding a public enquiry into the setting up of industrial establishments which might have a harmful effect on public health, and especially on the people living nearest the installation. As technical progress advanced, and as new industries were set up and old ones transformed, the list of such establishments was regularly. revised.
The extent of pollution is certainly due to technical progress, insofar as the latter creates new industries and encourages the development of others. Technical progress and industrial expansion are closely linked.
Population growth is another factor, and the concentration of industrial activity, or in other words, the rise of urbanism. The expansion of the large urban agglomerations is causing more pollution, and the latter is also exacerbated by the products of certain industries, such as oil-fired central heating and cars and planes, to name only the petroleum products for example. Then of course there is the acute

===== PAGE 854 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
837 problem of waste (nuclear waste, red mud etc.) The report issued by the Club of Rome was quite justified in making the following statement: "pollution is a complex function of population, industriali sation and technological development."
In actual fact, we do not know enough about certain aspects of this subject. There is the problem of direct pollution, but what about indirect pollution? Then there is immediate and long term pollution, such as that in the air, for example. Some types of pollution disappear quickly, others remain for a long time. "Our ignorance about the limits of the earth's capacity to absorb pollution" affirmed the Club of
Rome's report, "is sufficient reason for us to limit its sources." The models used can be illustrated by a few examples. The first concerns DDT, whose harmful effects, as well as its short term efficiency, have long been recognised. Over 100,000 tons of DDT is used in crop spraying every year. Part of this evaporates, and is carried by winds to land much further away, or to sea. In the latter, DDT is absorbed by plancton, then fish, and then by humans. At each stage of its cycle, DDT can either be degraded and transformed into harmless substances, or it can become concentrated in the tissues of living organisms, each stage representing a certain delay. The graph in figure
21 was part of the Club of Rome report. It was based on the hypothesis that the consumption of DDT had reached its maximum level, and that it would decrease to reach level zero by the year 2000. According to this graph, it will take another twenty-five years before the DDT content in fish organisms is the same as it was in 1970.
Other products have similar cycles: such is the case with two metals which are being increasingly used in industry. These are mecury and lead.
There are two other graphs in the same report which are just as telling. The first represents a forecast of the amount of nuclear waste which will be produced, as a function of the capacity of nuclear power plants (fig. 22). It is clear that the two curves match. The second curve represents the oxygen content of the Baltic Sea (fig. 23). The Baltic is a more or less closed area; there is no great movement of the waters.
Organic waste accumulates in this area, therefore, and it is likely that in certain depths there is no longer any oxygen at all.
There are many such examples of this; numerous works have been devoted to demonstrating the extent and the depth of that multi faceted problem which can no longer be ignored. Anti-pollution

But simply to recognize the problem is not sufficient. A few of the solutions offered to solve the problem of pollution are extreme:
some people believe that industry and technical progress should be halted to prevent further pollution, for example. Others believe that the quality of life should still be improved, but that pollution should be reduced. The former envisage a total impasse, while the latter feel that choices must be made.
Two systems have been devised to reduce pollution, which are not necessarily interchangeable: taxes or constraints. The difference is that constraints create what economists call annuities. Taxes go straight to governments, whereas constraints benefit the polluters and the land owners. The result is the same either way for the consumer, however.
All this is ultimately a matter of cost. The effects of anti-pollution measures are multiple, both directly and indirectly. They depend on

Models have been created in an attempt to examine the effects of anti-pollution measures in very precise sectors in Italy, the United States and Japan. The red mud tipped into the sea by an Italian company also had to be dealt with. The anti-pollution measures taken in this case caused an
18% rise in the price of titanium.
In its report of September 1973, the American Council for the Environment estimated that 274 billion dollars were spent on control ling pollution between 1972 and 1981. The break-down of these expenses is as follows:
atmospheric pollution . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 105.6 billion water pollution . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 121 .3 billion solid waste . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 41 .8 billion

===== PAGE 858 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
841 In 1968, the damage caused by atmospheric pollution alone cost 16.2 billion dollars to deal with. In 1977, the lack of established controls led to this figure being raised to 25 billion dollars. Some scientists even believe that the density of carbon dioxide layers resulting from the burning of hydrocarbons is gradually forming into a screen, which could eventually weaken the effect of the sun, and ultimately bring about considerable changes in the earth's climate.
Fears about pollution are so strong that some factories have had to close down. A paper mill near Gerardmer (Vosges, France) used 200 cubic metres of water for every tonne of paper produced. The used water contained cellulose fibres and starch, which destroys the flora in rivers and canals. It was estimated that the cost of refinement apparatuses would be 2.5 million francs. This was felt to be unprofit able, so the installation was abandoned. Some industrial projects have been rejected out of hand before they were even built. One remem bers the "wandering" refinery in Brest (Britanny), the one in the
Lyonnaise region, not to mention the nuclear power stations. Once industrial congestion and pollution are no longer tolerable, new areas of development will have to be found. "Come and pollute our country," became a catchphrase in Brazil, where it was clearly understood that France's iron and steel complex in Fos, on the
Mediterranean shore, was the last one that would ever be allowed to be built in the country.
Technical progress is in part responsible for this poisoning of nature.
It was the technical advances made in drilling and distillation which brought about the development of the petroleum industry, and techni cal advances which brought about the reduction in car prices, and consequently, the increase in the market, and the number of cars on the road. The same is true of all new materials, particularly plastic based ones. The accumulation of waste is striking evidence of this. As the novelist Chabrol once remarked during a television programme, the proportion of waste was once very small, today it is considerable.
In any case, the statistics show that the volume, if not the weight of urban waste is continuing to increase at an alarming rate.
There are no specialist studies of these different phenomena. We know very little about the effects of these various types of pollution on the environment, in what way the ecological system of the world might be changing as a result, or how these problems could be mitigated. We also need to know how this would effect other factors.

===== PAGE 859 =====

842
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
Pollution is not the only drawback of industrial growth. There is also the factor of environmental nuisances, or "nuisability." There are various types of nuisances, and some of them in fact are age-old problems. We shall consider only two examples which are character istic of our modern civilization.
A recent medical congress dealt with the problem of "urban sickness." Technical progress, industrialisation and especially the increase in communications have brought about rapid urban growth.
The problem is all the more pressing since actual urban techniques have not developed at the same pace. Atmospheric pollution, of course, is a major problem. Most factories were built near urban areas after the mid-19th century, then there is central heating, and vehicles, all of which are prime factors. The prevalence of carbon dioxide, nitrogen, sulphur dioxide, lead salts, hydrocarbons, ash, tar, and various gases have all resulted in above-average levels of morbidity and mortality. As well as this, it has been estimated that 14 million Americans are subjected to the noise of aeroplanes. 47 million planes passed over the United States in 1968, and 120 million in 1978.
Traffic, public highways, transmissions, and the vibrations of buildings built on the cheap to make them more profitable, are all factors of stress in a town-dweller's life. They are all the more dangerous for being constant, and because the city dweller's fragile nervous system makes them all the more vulnerable. It is not even safe to listen to jazz: the 125 decibels of a jazz orchestra is enough to burst an ear drum.
The symptoms of depression, anxiety, neurosis and madness are the concrete manifestations of life in our giant cities today. Promiscuity, noise, competition, crowds, drugs, alcohol, suicide: these are the most frequent hallmarks of urban life and modern technology. Research has confirmed this popular view, films have depicted it on screen, and books have denounced it. And since technical progress is considered as a prime factor of urbanism, it is also invoked as the essential cause of this malaise, being a central part of these nuisances: traffic congestion, noise, telephones, etc. etc.
The second example of such environmental nuisances is the car, which technical progress has made available to us all. At the end of
1971, there was one car in France for every 3.3 persons: nearly all those that professed a dislike to cars therefore probably had one. A news paper article recently highlighted the attacks made against cars.

===== PAGE 860 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
843 Autophobes are legion. And noisy. In the Holy of Holies, a car showroom in New York, leaflets are distributed bearing the provocative title "the car is anticity." The mayor of
New York, John Lindsay, joins a thousand cyclists on Fifth Avenue to protest against pollution; their example is followed in Paris, where on the 22nd April, 10,00 young cyclists - without the mayor, for they don't have one - demonstrate "contre la bagnole qui pue, qui tue, qui encombre, qui est moche, qui abrutit, (against the car which stinks, kills, congests, deadens the mind and spoils the view.)." (Charlie Hebda); on the 11th
May, 15,00 people demonstrated on the lawns at Rungstriidgarden, Stockholm, shouting, "We need trees, not cars, to survive!" In London, on the 3rd July, the Council suggested that people using cars in the city between 7 am and 6 pm should pay for the privilege; in Tokyo, on the 6th August, traffic between certain hours was forbidden in areas where schoolchildren had been affected by exhaust fumes; in Florence on the 8th september, the socialist mayor forbade the use of motor engines in the large park at
Cascine, and replaced these with municipal bicycles. Etc.
All that happened in only one year, 1973.
Autophiles are keeping a low profile. They are accused by all the intelligentsia of civilized countries of perpetuating these evils. They are being forced to apply draconian measures to prevent pollution and accidents as soon as possible. As the enemies of their workers in the factories, the car princes have finally succumbed to the guilty delights of sinistrosis. In the summer of 1970, Henry Ford II, the grandson of the man who invented the car, announced: "The golden age is over." On 24 February, 1972, Umberto Agnelli, the manager (sic) of Fiat, declared soberly to the Rotary Club of Turin: "Our real mistake was not looking ahead." Of course, not all the great car manufacturers agree with this fatalism. Some, such as Fiat, fight it with audacious advertising: "Now that the car creates more problems than it solves, where do we go from here?" And Volvo, the
Swedish firm, observes: "For many people, the car has become synonymous with envrionmental nuisances."
We could quite easily repeat the whole of the discussion on pollution in relation to the car. However, for the sake of brevity, we shall consider only certain aspects. In the first place, its manufacture:
The manufacturing of the piston alone requires both welding and machining operations.
The hourly rhythm of welding in 1920 was 40; today it is 450. The number of machines needed to produce 2000 of these has been reduced from 16 to 3, and the number of workers from 34 to 2. The number of machining operations has fallen from 14 to 5. The machining of a piston used to take 33 minutes; it now takes one and a half. The number of machines required has been reduced from 47 to 5. Even more important is the fact that it used to take 94 skilled workers to do this work; it now requires only 20 unskilled workers and 3 setters. Originally, the skilled workers used lathes and drills. Today, spf'cial machines for each operation devised by technicians and built by skilled workers are used by the unskilled workers, whilst the work is overseen by adjustors. All the components required by the production planning are manufactured in this way ( . . . ) Thus the limited number of skilled personnel still available in an economy do not produce the car anymore, but make the machines used by unskilled labour to produce the necessary parts. Unskilled workers can be trained in a few hours or a few days. It takes three to ten years to train a skilled worker.

===== PAGE 861 =====

844
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
In less than a decade, then, the social structures, the professional training, and the hierarchy of values in this sector of industry were completely tranformed. And these changes were not confined to the car industry alone. In 1950, it took three hours to make a shoe, for example, it now takes only 32 minutes. We could cite endless examples of this, but the car industry is the most important manifestation of what many believe to be detrimental changes.
Furthermore, the subsequent lowering of prices had a considerable impact on the family budget. On average, 27% of a family's budget in
France is spent on the car. The burden of such an expenditure is enormous, which many people believe will only get worse.
The car. is also a major factor in pollution, especially in the towns where it is thought that the vast majority of the population will be living in fifteen years time. Three toxic gases are emitted by cars:
carbon dioxide, hydrocarbons, and nitrogeneous oxide, and that is not including solid particles, notably lead. It was estimated that in the
U.S.A. in 1969, cars were responsible for 45% of hydrocarbon waste, 65% of carbon dioxide, and 37% of nitrigeneous oxide. Car pollution is worse when the vehicles are moving slowly, which is obviously the case in towns, and the above report stated that 80 to 95% of the carbon dioxide in towns was produced by this form of transport. The pollution is greater from multi-cylinder and older cars.
The car represents an environmental nuisance in another more concrete, and more dramatic sense. In towns, there is an increasing proliferation of cars which are often empty, and congestion in these areas is getting worse. We are all familiar with the problems of traffic jams - which make pollution even worse - the impatience and irascibi lity of car drivers, and pavements being taken over; they hardly need more discussion here. One very striking factor is the number of accidents occuring each year. As an indication, there are 200,000 cases of litigation per year in France as a result of car accidents, and there are not enough tribunals to deal with all the cases. This is not the most serious problem, obviously. Again, there is hardly any need to men tion the wholesale slaughter caused by cars. If we are not aware of it at first hand, then there are plenty of statistics to remind us of the problem. Every slogan, every picture has been used: "All the children born today will be involved in some way in a car accident; 2% will be killed, 25% will be slightly injured, and 15% will be injured seriously.
Can we call ourselves a 'society,' where one person out of two will be either killed or injured in a vehicle accident?"

===== PAGE 862 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
845 This of course is a choice. Apart from other car nuisances, there are also the changes in personality and the effects on the human body.
Perhaps it is worth noting at this point that the car is a necessary evil.
There can be no question of getting rid of cars: technical progress is irreversible and the car is too important a factor in our society and economy. In France, for example, it is estimated by some that the car represents 7% of all salaries. Others believe this figure is even higher, as much as 15% , if one includes all those who work in the car industry.
In 1960, the exportation of cars represented 16.58% of the total goods exported from France, in Germany the percentage was 12.06% , and in
Britain it was 10.81 % . Accidents are profitable for the car sector: there are 40,000 car dealers and repairers providing work for 245,000 people. Cars and tractors have caused the disappearance of other trades, as well. How many blacksmiths were there once? And how many are there today?
It is not difficult to list the advantages of cars, for getting to work easily, for drives into the country, and for avoiding the inadaquecies of public transport. The car does also have some psychological value. It is a mass phenomenon: 20% of french households had a car in 1953,
58.3% had one in 1972. A short while ago, a regional french newspaper reported that young people held the driving licence to be "a passport into society", more so than the baccalaureat. In an article judiciously entitled "Moteur et bonheur" (Engine and happiness), P. Viansson
Ponte quite rightly asked the question: "what can justify the damage to the environment and the slaughter?"
The car is often the substitute for a cramped, and unhealthy dwelling. It is an annex and an extension of this dwelling, which extends beyond the road, the workshop or the office. It recreates the privacy of a family. It restores the role of the father, it reinforces the faith in his competence and skill. It enables him to display his savoir-faire, and this can even be shared with the mother or the elder son, if they are allowed to take the wheel. The car represents escape, a change of scenery, adventure, freedom, and the do mini cal revenge against the monotony and colourlessness of daily life ( . . . ) Traffic jams give the illusion of equality.
Apart from the desire for independence, the car also represents a thirst for power, and the concretisation of social rank.
Car manufacturers are well aware of the different aspects of the situation. The Paris car exhibition of 1972 projected a rosier future for the car. One reviewer called it "a showroom of purity." The car is not responsible for urban congestion (already pointed out by Boileau in the 18th century), it is simply the towns which need adapting. And as

===== PAGE 863 =====

846
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
far as pollution is concerned, cars are beaten by heating. The car alleviates the suffering of man, enables them to escape and return to nature.
Measures to solve the problems created by cars had to be taken by public authorities. They were less radical than those proposed by car supporters, however. The two points of view held by these two camps were diametrically opposed. Car manufacturers and their supporters felt that related techniques should be made coherent within the techniques of the car industry. Neither the towns or the roads are suited to cars: that is where we should concentrate our efforts. The building of motorways has gone someway to solving the problem, but solutions within the towns are not so easy to find. In some fifteen years time, 85% of French people will be living in towns. Existing towns will be extended, and new ones will be designed which will be able to accomodate such large numbers of cars. As the president of Fiat declared in an interview, "It is the old towns which will be condemned.
They will be preserved as historical centres and museums. People will live in new towns, and the problems of traffic will be resolved from the very beginning."
Some groups believe that it would be better to act in a more direct way. For example, public transport should be improved, which would go a long way to reducing the number of cars on the road. Of course, there are many technical problems concerning public forms of trans port which have yet to be resolved. Such a transformation would also entail many changes in peoples' routine, and not only the routine of those who would use this transport (like shopkeepers for example).
Statistics show that such a transformation would have to take place over several years. The following is a list of statistics for Paris in 1972, with a base of 100 for 1962:
bus . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
75 train . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
115 metro . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
120 car . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
150 In the same report it was noted that current institutions were ill equipped to deal with such problems.
Of course, small cars are becoming much more popular, and recent research has concentrated on designing models from an aesthetic as

===== PAGE 864 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
847 well as from an economic viewpoint. But there are still several problems, some of which are psychological. The Fiat 500 was a useful car for a country with a relatively low standard of living. Eighty percent of those bought in France were to be used as a second car. The same was true of the Austin mini, despite its being considerably more expensive.
Steps are also taken to reduce the harmful effect of cars on the environment. The first problem to be considered was the pollution resulting from the burning of hydrocarbons. The United States has certainly taken some strict measures. The Federal Agency for the protection of the environment ruled in 1975 that the pollution from cars had to be reduced by 90% . Research was thus undertaken by both the petroleum industry and the car manufacturers. The former are dealing with the type of fuel rather than the engine; they are attempting to homogenise fuel mixtures and make them less flamma ble. The Japanese (Honda) are concentrating on engines with strati fied charge which is leading to the construction of entirely different engines. From the construction point of view, one needs to think in terms of a series of devices to be adapted to existing engines. But it would be difficult to perfect the various parts of engines: they have reached their saturation point. The cost of this research is enormous, and this will no doubt effect the prices of cars later on. It is thought that this rise could be as much as 15 to 20% , with an even greater proportion for small-engined cars.
Attempts have also been made to increase the safety of cars, and a large programme was launched to this effect in the U.S.A. called
E.S.V. (Experimental Safety Vehicles). But the schedules of condi tions in almost all countries have been very ambitious, and they entail vast sums of expenditure. Also, the norms were set on the basis of cars which were two or three times larger and more powerful than the existing American car. Again, the solutions adopted were little suited to medium or small sized cars. As R. Guillain wrote in Le Monde:
The original American concepts did not include the essential notion of "aggressiveness":
to ensure safety by increasing the size and rigidity of the vehicle means putting veritable tanks on the road, which are safe only for those inside - to everyone else they are extremely dangerous. The notion of compatibility should also be considered: cars are of varied size and strength, and the shock should be taken by both cars in the event of an accident. Furthermore, such measures should allow for the passengers, and not just the cars. There is only one conclusion: efforts should be made to strengthen smaller cars rather than the larger ones. The American system of simply strengthening the front of

===== PAGE 865 =====

848
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
every car is not sufficient. The E.S.V. programme did not take into account what happens in a reinforced vehicle after an accident. The air-bag method was expected to solve this problem, but the results of tests were disappointing and deceptive. The cost-benefit ratio was insufficiently taken into account, which means that prices could increase by as much as 40 to 50% .
What else could be done? The only possibility was to fall back on older solutions. The Federal Agency for the protection of the envir onment advocated tax measures to encourage the public to buy smaller cars. This was as much to reduce fuel consumption, a pressing problem in times of energy shortages, as to reduce pollution and the number of accidents. The other measure is now generally accepted: speed restric tions. In most countries, there are restrictions on both minor and major roads. This represents the opposite of what the car manufac turers have been striving towards ever since this industry began.
Some people doubt the effectiveness of such solutions. We have already noted the price increases caused by such conditions. Safety measures imposed increases of 28% for average-sized cars, and 40% for smaller ones. At any rate, the latter, which in fact represent the most satisfactory solution to the problems of urban traffic and pol lution are tending to disappear. In France, the proportion of small cars on the road was 27% , in 1972, this had gone down to 23% , and it is forecast that by 1980, this figure will have shrunk to 17% . Statistics have shown that eight many times as people are killed in accidents on motorways as in urban zones, and twice as many as on ordinary roads.
Electric cars are expensive, and the rotary piston can only bring marginal improvements. So does this leave us in a vicious circle? In
1955, there were 3,300,000 vehicles in France, in 1965, there were 9 million, there were 12,500,000 in 1970. In 1975, there were 16 million, and in 1980 there were 19,280,000. It is forecast that there will be
24,340,000 by 1990, and 29,100,000 by the year 2000.
Some figures are even more illuminating. In a period of five years, between 1962 and 1967, car production increased by a third. From
1967 to 1972, it rose by two-thirds. If this rythmn is maintained, by the end of the century, the rate of car production in France will be 40 million cars and lorries per annum. In the course of the last ten years, the number of vehicles on the road has doubled. If this trend conti nues, there will be 120 million lorries and cars on the road by the end of the century. From 1971 to 1973, the number of new road projects more than tripled, and similarly, if this rate of growth is sustained, then by

===== PAGE 866 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
849 the year 2000, there will be projects for motorways being set up for a total distance of 4 million kilometres. These figures show that this type of growth will not always be exponential.
It is possible, therefore, to define the waste created by cars in terms of pollution, of the dead and the wounded, and also in terms of occupied soil. To carry the same amount of traffic, the railways take up
215 square kilometres, while roads take up 3400 square kilometres, or sixteen times as much land. And this is not to mention the land taken up for motorways and car parks, etc. Or the absurd amount of energy consumed by cars in relation both to the amount of energy resources as a whole, and also in relation to other forms of transport.
The conclusion to such a state of affairs is given in the following paragraph:
The dangers of such an unlimited expansion urgently require counter-action: stabili sation. The first phase of this process would concern only the production of cars and lorries, and this would still enable the number of cars on the road in France to increase by
90,00 per annum. In the second phase, the number of cars on the road would actually be stabilised, but such a policy does not require either authoritarian measures or constraints with regard to consumers. All that is necessary is to reduce the demand for cars by making them more costly, and less neccessary.
So it is not merely a policy concerned with production and markets, but one concerned with development, and the competition of railways and public transport etc. , which will lead to success.
If one considers that the car problem is only one of the many caused by technical progress, even outside the transport sector which is such a sensitive subject, then one soon realises the extent of the problems to be resolved. The solutions can only be found by systematic, global research, and this will not be an easy task. If this is not done, however, then we run the risk - as the journalist author of the "Automobile, croissance zero" paper (Automobile, zero growth) affirmed - of being governed only by catastrophe.
The latest fears concerning technical progress and production in terms of the world as a whole have been concretised in the recently published [at the time of writing this book) famous Meadows report, which is the fruit of research conducted at M.I. T. at the request of the
Club of Rome. This report stirred a certain amount of controversy which has still not been resolved. Its conclusion is centred on the
"collective death" of the world at the beginning of the 21st century.

===== PAGE 867 =====

850
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The research was based on the exponential character of growth.
"Any consideration of problems concerned with human activities inevitably meets with phenomena of an exponential nature." The
M.l.T. team chose five major parameters: population, food produc tion, industrialisation, the use of non-renewable resources and pol lution.
I - The demographic considerations were based mainly on the schema provided by Malthus. The growth rate of the world population in the 17th century was 0.3% , which means that the population was doubling every 250 years. In 1970, this figure had risen to 2. 1 % , or a doubling of the population every 32 years. In 1970, the world popu lation was estimated to be 3.6 billion. So by 2000, this will have risen to over 7 billion.
II - Taking a base of 100 for 1963, the figures for industrialisation are as follows: 30 in 1930-40, 50 in 1950, 70 in 1958, and 140 in 1968; the rate of production per head is growing at a slower pace. A comparison between the two rates of growth: 7% for industry, and 2% for population is fairly hopeful, and in fact the major part of economic growth does not concern industrialised countries.
III - In 1970, there was an average of 0.4 hectares of arable land allowed to each individual, or in other words, a total of 13 billion hectares of land was cultivated. These figures are sufficient to prove this is not enough. In fact, 0.9 hectares per individual is necessary. It is therefore possible to calculate the required land surface for a growing population (fig. 24).
Until the beginning of the 20th century, the theoretical ceiling of cultivable land surface was 32 million square kilometres. This ceiling has lowered because of urbanisation, industrial installations, and its associated infrastructure. There is a threat of food shortages before the year 2000 therefore, and the same is true of the supply of soft water.
IV - Non-renewable resources, principally mining resources, are diminishing, while the demand for such resources is still growing.
Inevitably, then, there will be shortages. It is possible that there will be no silver, tin or uranium by 2000. The current known reserves of chromium are estimated at 775 million tonnes. The annual extraction of chromium is 1. 85 million tonnes per annum, which means that in 420 years time the reserves could be exhausted. But the annual consump-

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
853 tion of chromium is increasing by 2.6% per year, which means that these reserves are likely to be exhausted in only 95 years (fig. 25). The same calculations have been done for petrol, and the most optimistic estimates claim that the reserves of hydrocarbons will last for only another twenty years.
V - And finally there is pollution, the growth of which is also exponential. Carbonic acid gas from fossil fuels, heat energy and radio-active waste, which represent only three of the disruptive elements that men have introduced into their environment are still increasing, and this increase is exponential. The same is true of other types of industrial waste, such as pesticides, for example.
We live in a world of limited possiblilities. If all the factors are considered on a global scale, the conclusion is that growth cannot continue beyond the year 2100 (fig. 26). We cannot avoid catastrophe.
And what is more, technical progress can do nothing to remedy this.
Technology is not included in this model claims the report issued by the Club of Rome, as an independent variable. It is multiform in the sense that it can issue from a particular sector, or it can actually be an influence on it. Any element emerging as a result of technology (the contraceptive pill, high yield seeds, television, drilling platforms) has a distinct role in a given sector, and a precise part in the way a system works.
In fact, all the experts at M.l.T. are persuaded that technological progress can only postpone the final result.
Each hypothesis concerning almost unlimited energy resources (fig.
27), pollution control, and an increased agricultural yield and birth control fails to present a more optimistic outlook. The model conti nues to show the same results, it is simply a matter of time (fig. 28).
"The fundamental workings of the world ecosystem is defined by the exponential growth of population and investment, followed by a collapse." The authors of this report believe they have "demonstrated with their global model, that any attempts at solving with technology the problems of the exhaustion of resources, pollution or food shortages are bound to fail in terms of the essence of the problem:
exponential growth within a closed, complex system." According to the M.l.T. model, a stabilisation would require draco nian measures. The reduction in the birth rate, and production invest ment for consumer goods is seen to be "against nature" and "beyond

"Everything points to the fact that of the three possible hypotheses:
unlimited growth, the voluntary limitation of growth, or the natural limitation of growth, only the last two are plausible." Gradually, the definition of a state of equilibrium is reached, characterised by a constant global population and capital, the forces which tend to diminish or increase the latter being carefully balanced.
"Nevertheless, technical progress would be necessary and welcome in a stabilised world." Of course, this would have to be carefully controlled, so that progress took place in certain precise fields, and not in an arbitrary fashion, which could destroy the equilibrium. In fact, in the list of examples provided, it is merely a matter of progress bringing about indispensable changes to re-establish the equilibrium. There is no longer the growth of technical progress, just the control of detri-

(From Meadows et al) mental tendencies: (a) new methods of collecting waste to reduce pollution and facilitate recycling; ( b) more effective recycling methods to reduce the consumption of basic raw materials; (c) a different con ception of manufactured products, making them more durable and easier to repair, in order to reduce the rate at which capital depreciates;
(d) the domestication of solar energy, the least pollutive of all energy sources; (e) the more efficient means of neutralising harmful pesticides founded on a better understanding of ecological intercalation; (f) pro gress in medicine to reduce mortality; (g) effective contraceptive meth ods to bring the birth rate in line with the rate of mortality.
It is clear that this technical progress is very limited and rigorously directed.
There is thus widespread anxiety throughout the civilised world about the coming of the year 2000. And for some, this anxiety is very real. The vice-commissioner from the Holland at the E.E.C. Commis-

(From Meadows et al) sion voiced this fear: "Tommorow's society cannot be centred on growth, at least not in a material sense. " Recalling Tinbergen's notion of "gross national happiness", he proposed replacing the "gross national product" with "gross national utility." The primary objective of this was to safeguard ecological equilibrium and reserve for future generations sufficient energy resources. This picture of impending catastrophe clearly reflects the pessimistic attitudes to technical pro gress. The only thing lacking was a scientific formulation. This was provided by the Rome Club and M.I.T.
HOPE
In the face of doubt and anxiety, there is necessarily hope. Some of this hope of course is naive and simplistic. There is a belief in an indefinite

===== PAGE 874 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
857 technical progress, which in fact is a relatively recent phenomenon - with all due respect to Jules Verne - and furthermore, a belief that technical progress will bring with it human progress. This is simply faith, a kind of revealed truth which is not based on any concrete fact.
Besides these beliefs, there are other, more well-founded attitudes.
We have already said that technological forecasting has undergone a certain impetus and that some of its claims have already been substan tiated. It is possible that the problem has not been considered carefully enough, and the famous report of the Club of Rome, although partisan, has certainly stimulated reflection on this subject.
We have already raised some ambiguous points which we would do well to point out once more. The most important of these concerns the notion of technical progress itself. Is it true that any concrete inven tion, useful or not, profitable or not, or dangerous in some way (such as those which cause pollution for example) and which brings some positive element to a technical process, constitutes an element of technical progress? It does if it is a link in a chain of discoveries, but not otherwise.
Some recent examples which have been the subject of a great deal of discussion, reveal the limits of technical progress as such. Concorde is unquestionably an example of technical success: it has the advantage for commercial aviation of greatly reducing the length of flights. The flight from Paris to New York has been cut by three and a half hours thanks to this plane. But who wins? Those on business can go to more meetings: which means that they will live at a faster pace than ever. But is this a good thing? For someone involved in research, or going on holiday, this gain represents nothing at all. A reduction from 5 days to
8 hours was very significant, but is the same true of taking off another three and a half hours? The cost of this marginal advantage should also be considered. This apparatus is only marketable if it pays off the research and development costs and if companies are allowed to buy it on hire purchase with a governement guarantee. On the other hand, railway lines judged to be non-profitable are being phased out almost everywhere. The slow decline of the railways is a general process, and has reached large proportions in the United States. Those who oppose such a decline usually invoke the notion of public service, which should be assured "at any price." The authorities responsible have replied with the notion of "public interest," arguing that a national community should not have to bear the expensive brunt of such high costs which

===== PAGE 875 =====

858
1ECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
favour only a peripheral group - the public interest being identified here with the national one. In spite of their similarity, the two cases are in fact diametrically opposed: in the first case, technical progress has taken place, while in the second, there is a lack of technical progress which might recreate the profitability of a means of transport (it is also possible that the latter case is the effect of a reduced demand due to demographic changes brought about by changes in economic activity).
But as far as the general interest is concerned (one might almost, like Sicco Mansholt, call it national utility) the two cases are very similar, since the questions concern only restricted groups. Unless of course, one considers performance and prestige as elements of national utility.
These precisions reveal two fundamentally different types of atti tude among the optimists. Sometimes, the technician seeks only one element of what we have termed narrow technical progress. In a technological lineage which has not yet become saturated, possible future plans of action can be foreseen. Research is concentrated on certain areas, and science and technology are such today that technical developments appear to be possible. It was thus for the first satellites and so on until the first walk on the moon. Besides this, there are some people who have a wider outlook, whose optimism is more reasoned, and who take into account factors which are not necessarily technical.
It i« the latter that we shall consider, but while still bearing in mind the forecasts of the former.
The first basis for hope is having succeeded in dispelling most fears.
But it is not sufficient to calm anxiety with words, and this is not a responsible attitude. "With the help of wild extrapolations and fabri cated statistics," wrote a reader of a national newspaper, "the econo mists and engineers, the magi of our time, foresee all sorts of evils: take for example the ramblings issuing from the M.l.T. echoed recently on television by M. Mansholt."
A more interesting aspect of this opposition was the criticsm levelled at the method employed by the M.l.T. researchers. The discussion began around their statistical analysis (and cases have been cited, where factors intervened in the rate of mortality which were quantified in a particular way). Furthermore, it was noted that there was some confusion between the notions of simultaneity and causality. "For example, does the birth rate really depend only on income, industriali sation and hygiene?" The central focus of the critique was the choice of variables, however. Some factors would not have been relevant a few

===== PAGE 876 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
859 years ago, such as pollution, for example (fig. 29). Is it not then reasonable to suggest that in a few years time other variables will have to be taken into account?
Current methods of quantification lead to a mechanistic, deterministic vision of the world's future. The birth of ideologies and new attitudes cannot be foreseen, but they should be allowed for. The latter would create new curves within the model, and therefore a different vision of the world.
It is clear of course that the model is partial and not global, as some would have us believe. A global model cannot take into account local variables. It is also immutable, and cannot be adapted either in terms of techniques or otherwise. We can draw some conclusions from this.
Millions of tons of carbon monoxide 23 20 15 Non-depolluted \ car ЙЙ 1955 1911> 1915 1970 1975
1910 1115 1110 1 195 2000 Figure 29. - 'Optimistic' schema showing the reduction of pollution produced by cars.

===== PAGE 877 =====

860
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
The finite character of our world is certainly a given fact. But the fateful date remains a hypothesis. Th';! greatest contribution of the
M.I. T. report was probably to state that the effect of technical progress has hitherto been underestimated.
Other critiques were less concerned with theory than the actual data. Take for example the response of R. Barre, the then president of the European Community, to a letter sent by his vice-president. His judgement of the M.l.T. report was a harsh one. This particular criticism is aimed against the extrapolations concerning pollution.
It is not inconceivable that if in the future, 5 to 10% of the annual increase of the GNP was devoted to reducing levels of pollution, then not only would the destruction of the environment be checked, but significant improvements would be made.
And here, technical progress and raw materials have an essential role to play.
The development of fast (neutron) reactors made with currently known raw materials would enable the former to satisfy the same needs for a million years, those of 10 billion people having a consumer level which is double that currently attained in the United
States.
The spectre of over-population is certainly not a recent preoccu pation. The real problem, however, is the distribution of the popu lation in the different regions of the world. But there are still problems for over-populated and relatively poor regions, particularly in parts of
Asia. If the relationships between people and their environment can be mastered, then so must the relationships ·between people them selves. "It is at this level that the risks of explosion arising from political incompetence or bad social organisation are at their most acute." The president of the E.E.C. insisted on the social aspects of growth: the security of employment, the guarantee of a decent stand ard of life and the equal distribution of wealth which all favour a continuing rate of growth. But expansion should not be forced. In fact,
It would be difficult to pose and solve the problems of our societies in terms of rates of growth. It is the type of growth which is defined by the structures of demand and the conditions of production which should be adapted to meet the various competing objectives of our societies.
Societies would be changed, institutions transformed, and the price of raw materials would be greatly increased in order to increase the

===== PAGE 878 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
861 financial resources of under-developed countries (on condition that these resources were properly used).
In fact, - and several eminent scholars have taken great delight in emphasising this - the theory of exponential growth which is at the basis of the M.l.T. report, cannot be proved. As P. Uri wrote:
Can one really project over a hundred years without allowing for unforeseen factors?
Other forms of energy might be discovered, more powerful and more abundant, which might enable us to use the poorest minerals, such as clay alumina or titanium from the sea, for example; new plastics could be substituted for rare metals; and it is possible that chemistry might provide us with a new generation of pesticides which would not, unlike
DDT - even though it did save the lives of 500 million people - fall under suspicion.
It is clear that over-population could be stopped by raising the living standards of poorer countries. And furthermore, as Barre claimed, by the best possible distribution of resources.
In actual fact, the optimists used the picture of the year 2000 drawn up by a team from the Hudson Institute led by Hermann Kahn in 1968.
This team was similarly aware of the dangers of technical overheating and they constructed several pessimistic scenarios, notably in the perspective of world conflict. However, they lingered much longer on the optimistic elements.
The Kahn-Wiener report deals with the last third of the 20th century. It is claimed that by the year 2000, modern technology will have been mastered, and that the growth of the GNP per capita will have increased. One objection that could be made against this report is that certain parameters found in the M.I. T. report are not given any or enough weight in this model. On the subject of demography, for example, Kahn and Wiener estimate that in fifteen years or so: "birth control will have become a universal phenomenon, and will be far more effective than it is today." This is in striking comparison with the pessimistic view of demographic trends to be found among the M.l.T.
experts, who included the possibility of "complete birth control," but who then discovered that the pattern of the other curves changed as a result. Naturally, the Hudson Institute rejected the idea of technologi cal unemployment. "In the near future, automation and cybernetics, in contributing to the increase of productivity and the development of the economy, will probably create as many jobs as they have replaced."
These American optimists estimate that technology will be perfec ted, and they draw up impressive lists of probable inventions. They

===== PAGE 879 =====

862
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
make a finer analysis of six particular areas of technology where progress will be more marked: nuclear power, strategic warfare, electronics (calculators, data processing and automation), lasers, hol ography and biological engineering. In each case, they reach the opposite conclusions to those drawn in the M.l.T. report. Growth is not necessarily exponential, and our standard of living must necessa rily reach a peak, as it probably is in the United States at the moment.
On the other hand, a curve demonstrating rice yields (fig. 30), reveals that the optimists have more faith in agriculture than the researchers at the M.l.T. It is also possible that the rise in prices would enable us to exploit resources which otherwise would have been unprofitable.
The main idea of the Hudson Institute report is that this progress will necessarily bring about the birth of a post-industrial society.
The possibility of economic development, and the path this development is taking, our means of controlling both the interior and exterior environments, and the consequence of this, technological innovation, and finally the application and diffusion of these possibilities is increasing without any apparent limits. The question is, can our culture and institutions adapt themselves to such a fundamental and yet rapid change?
Keynes had already made this point. On a more detailed level, the partisans of the Hudson lnstitue did not fail to criticise some of the hypotheses used in the M.I. T. report. The treatment of pollution was one of the areas under attack. Despite the lack of knowledge about the effects of pollution, many people believe that it can be curbed. The subject of non-renewable resources was a different problem. Even with constant progress- it is thought that the findings of oil reserves are increasing twice as quickly as the product are being consumed - and even if technology can exploit these possibilities, it is no less true that at some stage there will be shortages in a certain number of essential areas. However, the techniques of recycling can still be called into play, which would give humanity an infinitely longer breathing space before the final catastrophe forecast by the experts at M. I. T. When oil is exhausted, there will be nuclear power, and after that probably solar energy (fig. 31). In other words, the world would be finite within a given technical system, but it might not within another - that is the basis for optimism.
Of course there are some sectors which are saturated, and within which no major invention can take place, but which can still be devel oped. Two examples of this are the fields of transport and traction.

All specialists are agreed during a car show held recently that there had been no new notable inventions. It was also agreed that fur thermore, it was unlikely that there would be any more at all, apart from the rotary piston which we mentioned above. The only new elements were developments made in the interiors, and the addition of gadgets, some of which were more useful than others. It does indeed seem that the fundamental technology of cars is now saturated.

===== PAGE 882 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
865 Progress is still possible in some areas: bodyworks are now being made of plastic, the designs and accessories can still be improved. These developments can be brought about only by using tributary tech niques. And this is all the more so now that component techniques - particularly those concerned with road networks and urban topog raphy - are no longer at the same degree of development.
One might have thought that the same was true of one of the most successful areas of technology in the 19th century, the railways.
However, the replacement of steam with electric traction and diesel, and the replacement of the latter with gas turbines introduced new elements. One could have invoked the impossibility of adapting the old superstructures, such as the rails, for instance. But it seems that this field of technology can be more easily adapted than others, and the railway has certainly not had its final word, especially when one considers its importance in everyday life. It is more reliable and safer - at the moment it is the least dangerous of all forms of transport - it has a greater capacity, and the costs are lower. It can even compete with planes in certain respects, especially the latter. Everyone is aware of the problems of getting to airports, while trains can run right into the centre of towns. Furthermore, the trains have been almost completely standardised. For these reasons, and because of the way in which the distribution of activities is evolving, the train of 2000 might still be an important factor in transport. It should be developed for use in certain specific areas: large-scale public transport; links with areas separated by long stretches of land; providing a service for suburbs which in fact are an extension of the urban transport network; and for heavy duty transport. The other side of this development is the construction of a new network which can be used by high-speed trains. This has already begun in some areas, there are now such lines between Florence and
Rome, and Paris and Lyons. The number of stations served by express trains will be reduced by half, which means that the road network must be linked with the rail network, as it is with the motorways. The older lines, which are no longer useful in this respect, could accomodate the transport of freight. The train takes two hours to get from Paris to
Lyon, and there is one every thirteen minutes. This certainly makes it competitive with the car, and even with the plane. It is fairly easy to calculate the limits of this competition. It is noteworthy, however, that the building and maintenance of airports is very expensive, and their use would soon be limited, the capacity of planes being much less than that of trains.

===== PAGE 883 =====

866
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
As far as urban transport is concerned, research is currently directed towards creating a network of automised engines. It is still early days yet, however. Take for example the Vrba system, or the programmed
Aramis by Matra transport system, to name but two of such projects.
These depend very much on developments in urbanisation and the continuity of older centres. Acceptable solutions can only be found if these two areas of research are linked. It is here that the notion of a technical system is most relevant, and the same is true with regard to the hovertrain and its use in a medium or even small sized network (the latter is particularly important for travelling to airports and satellite towns).
It is possible to forecast the saturation of certain sectors, and we shall consider two very different examples of this. The example of white light is characteristic. Progress was steady in this field, from
Edison's lamp to the fluorescent lamps of today. Each of the successive techniques employed is represented by an S-shaped curve, which are superposed on each other. The envelope curve has been simplified here and is represented by a staight line (fig. 32). It is clear that it could not pass the theoretical limit of white light.
The second curve represents the output of power stations (fig. 33).
They are at maximum output around the year 2000, and it would be Theoretical limit of white light --:"',

The diagram consists of a series of curves, each one representing a different technique, and which grow flatter as techniques are saturated. The ensemble of curves is repre1 0-1

870
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
sented by what is called an envelope curve, which indicates the general tendency. Would it be logical to extrapolate from this? Some people have done this, considering that it would be possible to substitute a saturated technique with a new one, since this has been happening now for centuries. This depends of course on the imagination of inventors, and the compatibility of tributary techniques. We shall consider this point further on.
It is not difficult to see how recent techniques could undergo spectacular developments. Take the computer, for example. There are two paths to follow, which have tributary elements. The computer can be considered as an autonomous object, especially in terms of its memory. But one should also consider the long-term development of data processing. The latter is of course linked to the development of the hardware. The French Commissariat general au plan recently published a study on data processing. A graph produced by the Delphi method (fig. 37) shows all the sectors where data processing will be used. These are the following: (a) traffic control in built up areas;
(b) teaching in schools; (c) the flying of civil planes; (d) medical diagnosis; (e) teaching at home; (f) as an alternative to libraries;
(g) generalisation of automatic motor vehicles; (h)'for use with tele phones and televisions.
The potential of this field is thus very wide, and we have still considered only a few of the areas where it can be applied.
There are two main conditions to the development of data proces sing: it must be possible to create programs for these different tasks, and secondly, the size of the computer memory must be increased. The latter is much more difficult to develop than improvements in compu tations. Figure 38 relates to the capacity of random access memories classed by access time. In contrast to the envelope curves, this curve is flattened. This progression is due to the "numerous radical changes in subjacent technology." The first computer was an electromechanical system, with no means of storing programs. The next models used contained mercury delay lines as a high speed memory and circuits of vacuum valves as logic circuits. Some of the latter incorporated electrostatic devices acting as memories (cathode ray tubes), which were soon replaced by magnetic ferrite cores. Transistors were used for most logical functions during the 1950's. The later stages are not represented in the parameters illustrated here.

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
873 The growth rate of energy provided by accelerators creates the same type of envelope curve as that which represents the progress made in terms of speed (figs. 39 and 40).
It is not sufficient merely to use envelope curves, however. In a sense, they are only significant within closed systems, which do not allow for exterior variables, whose effects can be fundamental. They serve as a basis for general extrapolation only.
From one industry to another, and from one technology to another, it was felt that technological forecasting on a global level still seemed possible, however, and indeed, this has been practised for longer than is generally thought. The modern era of technological forecasting
