(From J.M. Treille) began during the mid 1930's, when the National Resources Committe was founded by the National Research Council under the chairmanship of W.F. Ogburn, Professor of Sociology at Chicago
University. His report, Technological Trends and National Policy was published in 1937.
During a period of many changes, it is necessary that those at the helm of the ship of the state should anticipate future events. A study of inventions can reveal future social conditions and a nation's future problems. In fact, of the four most important factors regarding a nation's well-being, namely, invention, population, natural resources and economic organisation, it is the first which changes most frequently in the modern world, and which therefore is most often the cause of change.
The main preoccupations reflected in this passage are also found in the reports of the Hudson Institute and the M.l.T. , the latter dealing

===== PAGE 892 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
875 with technical progress only from certain angles. In fact the Ogburn report was unable to forecast atomic energy, radar, antibiotics and jet propulsion, even though the research for all these fields had begun in the 1920's.
Global planning, on the scale adopted in the U.S.A. does not exist, but it has still been found necessary to organise some form of fairly long-term technological forecasting. This has been done in the army, and in some universities, sometimes actually on behalf of the army.
Private industry concerned itself with the leading sectors where tech niques were evolving most rapidly: such was the case with the Lock heed Aircraft Corporation, or with the General Electric Tempo laboratory.
International organisations gradually began to follow the same path:
the O.E.C.D . , the constituent elements of the E.E.C. , etc. European countries, who are more in favour of forecasting and economic plann ing, were fairly slow in adopting the practice of technological forecas ting. This was all the more indispensable and restricting because of the limited financial resources which forced choices, apart from the ever present dilemma of fundamental or applied research.
Since there is a fundamental risk in research, noted the authors of the french 6th Plan, the state organisation of science would be better served by a greater understanding of the extent and the significance of this risk, and by a conscious acceptance of responsibility for it, with the aim of obtaining clearly defined cultural, social, economic and military objectives.
Or, similarly:
The first impulse of a nation which wants to stay ahead in those areas essential to its independence, is to try and understand the relationships between research and the economy, and to determine the criteria by which some scientific works are judged to generate expansion more than others.
The constraints are indicated here. If there is a plan, then there is also technological forecasting. The latter demands that choices be made, and the lack of financial, material and human resources means that the repercussions of these extend right back to pure research.
From this point, there is the possible and the desirable, which permits the distinction of two complementary types of forecasting.
Exploratory forecasting uses information from current science and technology, the main lines of which enable us to forecast fairly

===== PAGE 893 =====

876
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
long-term possible solutions to any technical or scientific problems which are known to exist. The information used in normative forecasting is based on needs and the tendencies which effect them.
This type of forecasting searches for means to supply such needs, and as such represents a quite different approach to the problem. Ideally, these two methods of research should be converged. Today, more than ever, technical innovation is due to precise normative concep tions and to various technological forecasts, a large part of which is normative.
We have already noted on several occasions the use of the envelope curve, or extrapolation, in exploratory forecasting, working from anterior technical data. One author has noted that the growth rate of some performance variables will be indefinitely asymptotic before the year 2000. Such is the case with the speed of vehicles. On the one hand, therefore, the exponential curve is not generalised, and on the other, the method has limits, in that different techniques are being compared.
In the case of a clearly determined technique, we saw that the curves were indefinitely asymptotic: this was the case with electrical power stations. But one could also use intersecting curves. From
1913, S.C. Gilfillan produced the above cited forecast for the size of steamships.
The extrapolation of temporal series (note that the period of extrapolation is no longer than the period over which the phenomena are observed) has given substance to attempts to formulate simple analytical models: this was so for Henry Adams in 1907, and then for the models created by Insenson, Hartman, Holton, and Putnam. The extrapolation of temporal series has also been done on a phenomeno logical basis: linear growth with saturation, S-curves, exponential curves.
The methods of normative technological forecasting have also been developed. We do not wish to describe, even summarily, from the matrix to the network method, from business management games to the games theory. We have considered relevant graphs, such as the
Pattern method, which are currently perhaps the most advanced.
The most spectacular results are probably those produced by the Delphi method, developed by the Rand Corporation, itself born of the forecasting department of American military aviation. This method is one of normative technological forecasting. It enables us to choose

===== PAGE 894 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
877 social or other objectives of a high level without prejudice, by using scenarios or operational models within social, military, political, economic and of course technical fields. As far as we are concerned, it is an enquiry conducted by different groups, each one concerned with a different problem, and each one composed of technicians and non-technicians. These groups are asked certain questions, and they must try to determine the chronological limits within which the answer can be given. The first study was published in 1965, under the direction of T.J. Gordon and Olaf Helmer, and it dealt with the foreseeable development of science and techniques in the next three decades. In fact, research was limited to a few possible scientific and technical breakthroughs in a given future. In the schema which we have included here (fig. 41), the length of each bar represents the average extent of the difference in the experts' estimations. In each case, a quarter of the experts put the date before the point at which the bar begins, and a quarter put the date after the point where the bar ends. In eight cases, indicated at the bottom of the graph, some experts put "never" as a date, while the dates forwarded by others are quite precise.
The result of this enquiry - whose limits are clear - is all the more interesting in that it is being assessed eight years after it was first published, so that some of its forecasts will have had a chance to become reality. Apart from the somewhat simplistic schema published by a French magazine to attract public attention to a little-known science, it is possible to draw up several annex tables showing these forecasts. The date printed in italics represents the opinion of the majority of the experts. The absence of a third date means that in the opinion of most of those consulted, it was considered impossible that the event should take place in the foreseeable future. Do not misunderstand us however: in effect, this is a straightforward list of possible progress, of progress which is likely within given chronologi cal limits. However simplistic this method might appear, its aim, within a context which is to be considered scientific, is to reveal the desires of certain technicians. In other words, we find ourselves confronted with a sketch of the future development of a technical system which is already evolving, with more difficulty, certainly, under our very eyes.
This is how, a few years ago, the experts of the Rand Corporation envisaged the future in four different fields:

1990 200 200 These, then, are the tables supplied by the Rand Corporation regarding scientific breakthroughs, the conquest of space, automi sation and military art. The reader is bound to be somewhat surprised by the results of the enquiry. Of course, this enquiry was not a systematic one, simply a selection of opinion polls concerning particu larly important areas, which no doubt will bring much influence to bear on the world of tomorrow. Some parts may make people smile - such as the control of gravity, or communication with extra-terrestrial beings. Other parts are more worrying, such as the use of mind reading for military purposes. But who has not already thought of these in reality?
What conclusions can we draw from all thisO The Hudson Institute's Year 2000, the tables of the Rand Corporation, and the works written about our civilization in the last twenty or thirty years all have common characteristics, even if their methods differ as fundamentally as their results. One of these is the necessity for global forecasting, within which technological forecasting holds an important place, if not the essential one. The primacy of techniques is apparent in all of these texts, to a greater or lesser degree, but it is always clear - even if the

===== PAGE 900 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
883 Hudson Institute did consider other sectors in its enquiry and analysis.
It is only natural, then, that such a great insistence has been placed on "leading techniques," symbolised in a way by electronic and nuclear techniques which are considered, rightly or wrongly, as the two major poles of technical progress. And it is around this entirely new technical system - that has almost nothing in common with the technical system created in the second half of the 19th century and which continued to develop until the eve of the Second World War - that all the other systems must organise themselves: the economic system, the social system, etc.
The graph in figure 42 naturally has only an indicative value. One can easily see that it has not been mathematically formulated. But it is helpful in that it provides a visual representation of that which has been observed and forecast, apart from possible corrections. The curve of scientific progress incorporates the progress of techniques, which f

===== PAGE 901 =====

884
TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
emerges from applied science. This curve is moderately exponential.
The curve representing social progress shows a much more feeble growth, even though the prospective part is on the upturn. The line representing institutions reveals a number of downward trends. It is easily demonstrable that as techniques progress, institutions wear out.
At irregular intervals, then, readjustments are necessary. To represent the relationships between institutions and scientific progress between
187 and 1945 by a straight line is no doubt a bit excessive.
It is not impossible that in the following decades, this graph may be able to offer more compatibility between the various elements of which it is composed. This is a work of the imagination.
At the end of these two short and necessarily fragmented analyses of hopes and fears, the reader may well be perplexed - but then he inhabits a perplexing reality. This perplexity was clearly expressed by a technician, M. Giuliani, in Le Monde in 1972. Since 1967 when the
Hudson Institute published its report, or even from 1965, when the report by the Rand Corporation was published, everything has been said for and against. Differences of opinion have remained, sometimes for surprising reasons. Communists and socialists are divided, while big business and some circles of economists understand each other perfectly. The authors of The Year 2000, and those of the M.l.T.
report belong to the same social class, the same country, the same universities. They use the same computers, have the same sources of information, use the same theoretical background and adhere, grosso modo, to the same ideology. It is quite legitimate, therefore, to consider the meaning of this fundamental contradiction.
We are living, writes M. Giuliani, in a world which is subject to three different types of logic. There is the rational logic of scientific and technical development; there is the logic of need which diverges from the former because of the irrationality of desire, but which approaches it because of the inability of the imagination to conceive of anything else other than that which techniques may offer it; finally, there is the logic of decisions and choices which inspires the contradictory will for power and desire for happiness. People living in the final part of this century are going to have to deal with an evolution which will depend as much on the autonomous mechanisms of the former, as on the obscure reactions and the apparently straightforward actions of the latter two, and it will be impossible to tell which is most important.
Either, as H. Kahn, the C.N.P.F. (Conseil National du Patronat Francais, the french industrial lobby), and the French C.P. believe, industrial society might possibly be able to maintain the objectives of more or less exponential growth. To do this would require an unprecedented technical revolution, a whole host of new inventions, and a complete change in methods and morals. Or, it is possible that this revolution might never take

===== PAGE 902 =====

TOWARDS A CONTEMPORARY TECHNICAL SYSTEM
885 place or be sufficient, in which case, as the M.I. T. and Sisco Maiisholt say, the objectives must be changed, which would bring about an entirely different type of revolution. Who knows which one would create the most upheaval. And what face would one or the other give to capitalism or socialism?
In any case, the question must be posed within as large a context as possible, and the authors of The Year 2000 understood this better than the researchers at the M.I. T. Technological forecasting by itself is not sufficient, it requires an understanding of the sort of world with which it might be compatible, and consequently, the transformations to be effected in a large number of areas. But is it not characteristic of humans to put the cart before the horse: the desire to understand matter is infinitely stronger than the need to understand oneself.
Attitudes of mind and established situations are all part of a certain comfort which people are reluctant to abandon, indeed, they often refuse to do so.

===== PAGE 903 =====

===== PAGE 904 =====

THE HISTORY
OF TECHNIQUES
Edited by Bertrand Gille Translated from the French Volume 1: Techniques and Civilizations
Volume 2: Techniques and Sciences Bringing together a wealth of previously scattered information, this classic work traces the history of techniques and analyzes its relationship with other areas of knowledge.-Parts 1 and 2 provide historical coverage, from prehistory to the twentieth century, of man's attempts to master his environment. Part 3 examines this endeavour from the point of view of other major disciplines: economics, geography, science, sociology, law and politics. Together they provide a unique reference tool that puts forward a stimulating new perspective on this increasingly important and interesting field.
About the editor At one time in charge of the private economic archives section of the French National Archives, Bertrand Gille went on to teach history at the
University of Clermont-Ferrand. He was later elected to the Ecole Pratique des Hautes Etudes (/Verne Section) and remained a major contributor to the literature on the history of banking, technology and industry until his death.
Related publications of interest HISTORY AND TECHNOLOGY An International Journal, Executive Editor,
Pietro Redondi.
THE SCIENCE OF HISTORY A Cybernetic Approach, by W illiamJ. Chandler.
THE SHAPING OF SOCIO-ECONOMIC SYSTEMS The Application of the Theory of Actor-System Dynamics to Conflict, Social Power, and
Institutional Innovation in Economic Life, by Thomas Baumgartner, Tom R. Burns and Philippe DeVille.
STATE AND STATISTICS IN FR ANCE 1789-1815, by Jean-Claude Perrot and Stuart J. Woolf.
